instruction: Segment the gallbladder in this abdominal MRI using the description 'gallbladder with no stones or wall thickening' as input.
description: VoxTell is a state-of-the-art 3D vision-language segmentation model that directly maps free-form text prompts to volumetric masks in medical imaging. Unlike traditional segmentation models that require predefined class labels, VoxTell accepts natural language descriptions ranging from single words to full clinical sentences, enabling intuitive and flexible medical image analysis. The model is designed for both anatomical and pathological structures across multiple imaging modalities (CT, PET, MRI), being trained on 1000+ familiar concepts while maintaining strong generalization to related unseen classes through its multi-stage vision-language fusion architecture. VoxTell supports free-text prompting to generate 3D segmentation masks using natural language descriptions, multi-modality support across CT, PET, and MRI imaging modalities, comprehensive anatomy coverage including brain, thorax, abdomen, pelvis, musculoskeletal system, and extremities, and flexible granularity from coarse anatomical labels to fine-grained pathological findings. VoxTell v1.1 is the recommended version, trained on all datasets from the paper and additional sources (190 datasets, ~68,500 volumes), with a 95% probability of sampling from the semantic datasets corpus and 5% probability from image-text-mask triplets from instance-focused datasets. It is recommended for general application, inference, and fine-tuning, maximizing supervision and concept coverage for stronger general-purpose performance. VoxTell v1.0 is deprecated and not recommended for general use, having been used for the experiments in the paper but containing known issues that have been fixed in v1.1. VoxTell employs a multi-stage vision-language fusion approach, including an image encoder that processes 3D volumetric input into latent feature representations, a prompt encoder using a frozen model to embed text prompts, a prompt decoder that transforms text queries and image latents into multi-scale text features, and an image decoder that fuses visual and textual information at multiple resolutions using MaskFormer-style query-image fusion with deep supervision. The model is intended for research in vision-language models for medical image analysis, text-promptable or automated segmentation of anatomical structures in medical imaging, and identification and delineation of pathological findings. It is not intended for clinical diagnosis without expert radiologist review, real-time emergency medical decision-making, or commercial use. VoxTell achieves state-of-the-art performance on anatomical and pathological segmentation tasks across multiple medical imaging benchmarks. Performance may vary on imaging modalities or anatomical regions underrepresented in training data. Prompting structures absent from the image and never seen on this modality may lead to undesired results. Text prompt quality and specificity affects segmentation accuracy. The model is not validated for direct clinical use without expert review. Images must be in RAS orientation for correct anatomical localization, and performance may degrade on images with very uncommon voxel spacings. The model does not resample images to a standardized spacing for faster inference. Known issues include compatibility problems with PyTorch 2.9.0 causing OOM errors during inference, which should be avoided by using PyTorch 2.8.0 or earlier.

instruction: I want to extract the body text from this scanned page, ignoring the headers and footers. Can you help?
description: HunyuanOCR is an end-to-end OCR expert vision-language model (VLM) built using Hunyuan's native multimodal architecture. With a lightweight 1B parameter design, it has achieved state-of-the-art performance across multiple industry benchmarks. The model is capable of complex multilingual document parsing and is designed for practical applications such as text spotting, open-field information extraction, video subtitle extraction, and photo translation. It supports tasks including spotting, document parsing, general parsing, information extraction, and translation. For document parsing, it can recognize formulas in LaTeX format, parse tables into HTML, and represent charts using Mermaid or Markdown formats. It can extract document content in markdown, ignoring headers and footers, and represent formulas in LaTeX and tables in HTML. For information extraction, it can extract key-value pairs, specific fields, and subtitles in JSON format. For translation, it can extract and translate text into English, ignoring headers and footers, and represent formulas in LaTeX and tables in HTML. The model is available for deployment and inference using the Transformers library or vLLM, with specific installation and usage instructions provided. It is recommended to use the clean_repeated_substrings function to process output text. The model has been developed by the Hunyuan Vision Team and is described in the HunyuanOCR Technical Report, which is available on arXiv. The team acknowledges the contributions of other researchers and models, as well as the benchmarks used in the development and evaluation of the model.

instruction: Could you analyze this video to determine if it contains a crime-related event?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of the VideoMAE architecture for binary violence detection in video content. It classifies videos into two categories: Violent Crime (1) and Non-Violent Incident (0). The model was trained on a curated subset of the UCF Crime dataset with event-based categorization, consisting of 300 videos, equally split between violent and non-violent categories. The event distribution includes 34 videos of abuse, 36 of arrest, 46 of arson, 36 of assault, 70 of burglary, 24 of explosion, 30 of fighting, 86 of road accidents, 98 of robbery, 36 of shoplifting, and 62 of stealing. The dataset was split into 192 training videos, 48 validation videos, and 60 test videos. The model achieved a validation accuracy of 0.6250, precision of 0.6351, recall of 0.6250, and F1 score of 0.6114. On the test set, it achieved an accuracy of 0.6667, with equal precision, recall, and F1 score of 0.6667. The model uses a VideoMAE Base architecture with a fine-tuning approach for event-based binary classification. Training was conducted for 10 epochs with a learning rate of 5e-05, a batch size of 2, and AdamW optimizer with betas (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon 1e-08. The model processes 16 frames per video clip at a resolution of 224x224 pixels and outputs binary classification results. It was trained using the HuggingFace Transformers framework with PyTorch 2.0.1+ and is compatible with Apple Silicon MPS, CUDA, or CPU. The model has several limitations, including potential generalization issues due to dataset scope, possible false positives with non-violent but intense activities, and environmental bias from lighting, camera angles, and video quality. It is intended for research, security system enhancement with human oversight, and educational purposes. Prohibited uses include unauthorized surveillance, discriminatory profiling, automated punishment, and privacy violations. The model was developed by a research team and is available for use under the HuggingFace Transformers framework.

instruction: Could you help me write a short story with a fantasy setting and a twist ending?
description: Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507 is an updated version of the Qwen3-30B-A3B non-thinking mode model. It features significant improvements in general capabilities, including instruction following, logical reasoning, text comprehension, mathematics, science, coding, and tool usage. The model also shows substantial gains in long-tail knowledge coverage across multiple languages and is better aligned with user preferences in subjective and open-ended tasks, enabling more helpful responses and higher-quality text generation. It supports 256K long-context understanding and has a context length of 262,144 tokens natively. The model is a causal language model with 30.5B total parameters and 3.3B activated parameters. It has 48 layers, 32 attention heads for queries and 4 for key-value pairs, and uses a mixture-of-experts architecture with 128 experts and 8 activated experts. It supports only non-thinking mode and does not generate code blocks in its output. The model has been evaluated on various benchmarks, showing strong performance in knowledge, reasoning, coding, alignment, and multilingual tasks. For ultra-long context processing up to 1 million tokens, the model integrates Dual Chunk Attention and sparse attention mechanisms, which improve generation quality and inference efficiency. Users require approximately 240 GB of GPU memory to process 1 million tokens effectively. The model can be launched using vLLM or SGLang with specific configurations for long-context support. Best practices include using sampling parameters such as Temperature=0.7, TopP=0.8, TopK=20, and MinP=0, and setting an output length of 16,384 tokens for most queries. The model is recommended for use with standardized output formats for benchmarking, including specific prompts for math problems and multiple-choice questions.

instruction: I need a voice with a heavy Russian accent and a tone of anger for a dramatic monologue.
description: Maya1 is a state-of-the-art speech model for expressive voice generation, designed to capture real human emotion and precise voice design. It allows users to create any voice they can imagine, including specific age, gender, accent, and character traits, and to add emotion tags such as laugh, cry, whisper, rage, sigh, and gasp to make the output feel more realistic. The model streams audio instantly, runs on a single GPU, and has 3B parameters. It outperforms top proprietary models and is developed by Maya Research. Maya1 uses a 3B-parameter decoder-only transformer (Llama-style) to predict SNAC neural codec tokens, enabling real-time streaming at ~0.98 kbps. It is trained on an internet-scale English speech corpus and a curated dataset of studio recordings with human-verified voice descriptions, 20+ emotion tags, multi-accent English coverage, and character variations. The model supports natural language voice control, allowing users to describe voices in plain text without complex parameters or training data. It also supports inline emotion tags for expressive speech, enabling users to add emotions directly into the text. Maya1 is suitable for a variety of use cases, including game character voices, podcast and audiobook production, AI voice assistants, video content creation, customer service AI, and accessibility tools. It is open source under the Apache 2.0 license, allowing commercial use, deployment, and customization. The model can be fine-tuned on custom datasets for specialized voices and is compatible with vLLM for production-scale deployment. It generates 24 kHz audio output and is web audio compatible for browser playback. Maya1 is production-ready and was released in November 2025.

instruction: I want to extract text from this image but the layout is skewed. Can you correct it?
description: UVDoc is a model designed to perform geometric transformations on images to correct document distortion, inclination, and perspective deformation, thereby improving the accuracy of subsequent text recognition. The model achieved a Character Error Rate (CER) of 0.179 on the DocuNet benchmark dataset. UVDoc is intended to be used as part of the TextImageUnwarping module within the PaddleOCR framework. It can be integrated into projects for document image correction and is compatible with PaddlePaddle versions 3.0.0 for CUDA 11.8, CUDA 12.6, and CPU environments. The model supports inference through command-line tools and Python code, allowing users to process images and save results in various formats, including images and JSON. UVDoc can also be used in conjunction with the PP-StructureV3 pipeline, which includes layout detection, OCR, table recognition, seal recognition, and formula recognition modules. The pipeline can be enabled with the use_doc_unwarping parameter to enhance document processing in real-world scenarios.

instruction: Illustrate a dramatic sunrise over a battlefield with smoke and fallen armor.
description: NetaYume Lumina is a text-to-image model fine-tuned from a high-quality anime-style image generation model developed by an unspecified entity. It is based on an open-source base model released by the team at Shanghai AI Laboratory. The model was trained to generate both realistic human images and high-quality anime-style images, while retaining a significant amount of knowledge from its base model. Key features include the ability to generate detailed anime-style images with sharp outlines, vibrant colors, and smooth shading. It also demonstrates improved character understanding, particularly for characters from the Danbooru dataset, resulting in more coherent and accurate character representations. Additionally, it enhances the generation of fine details such as accessories, clothing textures, hairstyles, and background elements with greater clarity. The model file, NetaYume_Lumina_v2_all_in_one.safetensors, is an all-in-one file that includes the necessary weights for the VAE, text encoder, and image backbone for use with ComfyUI. The model uses a pre-trained Gemma-2-2b text encoder, a pre-trained Flux.1 dev's VAE, and a fine-tuned NetaLumina image backbone. It is recommended to use a system prompt to help generate desired images by aligning with user prompts. For anime-style images using Danbooru tags, the model is described as an assistant designed to generate anime images based on textual prompts and user prompts with Danbooru tags. Recommended settings for optimal performance include a CFG of 4–7, sampling steps of 40–50, and samplers such as Euler a with a normal scheduler or res_multistep with a linear_quadratic scheduler. The model acknowledges the use of the Danbooru dataset, the creators of a wonderful model, and a team for openly sharing an awesome model.

instruction: Can you read the license number and RTO from this driving license back image?
description: The Indian ID Validator is a computer vision pipeline designed for classifying, detecting, and extracting text from Indian identification documents, including Aadhaar, PAN Card, Passport, Voter ID, and Driving License. The system is powered by YOLO11 models and PaddleOCR, and it supports both front and back images for Aadhaar and Driving License. The pipeline performs three main tasks: classifying the type of ID document using the Id_Classifier model, detecting specific fields on the document using type-specific YOLO11 detection models, and extracting text from the detected fields via PaddleOCR with preprocessing steps such as upscaling, denoising, and contrast enhancement. The supported ID types include Aadhaar (front and back), PAN Card (front), Passport (front), Voter ID (front and back), and Driving License (front and back). The pipeline includes the following models: Id_Classifier, which classifies the type of Indian ID document with a Top-1 accuracy of 0.995 and Top-5 accuracy of 1.0; Aadhaar, which detects fields such as Aadhaar Number, DOB, Gender, Name, and Address, with mAP50 of 0.795, mAP50-95 of 0.553, Precision of 0.777, Recall of 0.774, and Fitness of 0.577; Driving_License, which detects fields such as Address, Blood Group, DL No, DOB, Name, Relation With, RTO, State, and Vehicle Type, with mAP50 of 0.690, mAP50-95 of 0.524, Precision of 0.752, and Recall of 0.669; Pan_Card, which detects fields such as PAN, Name, Father's Name, DOB, and Pan Card, with mAP50 of 0.924, mAP50-95 of 0.686, Precision of 0.902, and Recall of 0.901; Passport, which detects fields such as Address, Code, DOB, DOI, EXP, Gender, MRZ1, MRZ2, Name, Nationality, Nation, and POI, with mAP50 of 0.987, mAP50-95 of 0.851, Precision of 0.972, and Recall of 0.967; and Voter_Id, which detects fields such as Address, Age, DOB, Card Voter ID 1 Back, Card Voter ID 2 Front, Card Voter ID 2 Back, Card Voter ID 1 Front, Date of Issue, Election, Father, Gender, Name, Point, Portrait, Symbol, and Voter ID, with mAP50 of 0.917, mAP50-95 of 0.772, Precision of 0.922, and Recall of 0.873. The models are available in various formats such as PyTorch, ONNX, and TensorRT for deployment in different environments. The system can be used via a Python API or command-line interface, and it provides end-to-end processing for classification, detection, and OCR. The model is released under the MIT License.

instruction: What is the most prominent color in this image, and what objects are associated with it?
description: Qwen3-VL is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced Thinking editions for flexible deployment. Key enhancements include a Visual Agent that operates PC and mobile GUIs by recognizing elements, understanding functions, invoking tools, and completing tasks. It supports Visual Coding Boost, generating Draw.io, HTML, CSS, and JS from images and videos. Advanced Spatial Perception allows the model to judge object positions, viewpoints, and occlusions, providing stronger 2D grounding and enabling 3D grounding for spatial reasoning and embodied AI. The model supports Long Context and Video Understanding with a native 256K context length, expandable to 1M, enabling it to handle books and hours-long video with full recall and second-level indexing. Enhanced Multimodal Reasoning excels in STEM and Math, providing causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. Upgraded Visual Recognition includes broader, higher-quality pretraining, enabling the model to recognize a wide range of subjects such as celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. Expanded OCR supports 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare or ancient characters and jargon, as well as long-document structure parsing. The model achieves text understanding on par with pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE, which enhances long-horizon video reasoning through full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to provide precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. The model is designed for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library and supports generation hyperparameters for both vision-language and text tasks. The model is intended for deployment in a variety of settings, with options for fine-tuning and customization. The model is part of a series of vision-language models, with prior versions including Qwen2.5-VL, Qwen2-VL, and Qwen-VL, each with their own technical reports and research contributions.

instruction: Provide a concise summary of the case involving a child with signs of psychological distress and abuse.
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of the google/flan-t5-base model, specifically optimized for summarizing child helpline case call transcripts. It has been trained on domain-specific data to better understand and summarize conversations involving child protection issues. The model uses the T5ForConditionalGeneration architecture and operates in English with 248 million parameters. It is designed for the task of text summarization within the domain of child protection and helpline conversations. The model has been specialized to understand child protection terminology, helpline conversation patterns, sensitive case reporting protocols, and legal and procedural references specific to child welfare. It demonstrates enhanced contextual understanding of child welfare scenarios, including child labor, forced marriage, and abuse cases. The model generates concise, actionable summaries that capture key information such as caller identity and location, the nature of the concern or issue, and action items and referrals provided. It is trained to appropriately summarize sensitive child protection cases while maintaining essential details. The model has a maximum source length of 1024 tokens and a maximum target length of 256 tokens. It was trained for 3 epochs with a learning rate of 3e-5 and a batch size of 4. It uses beam search with 4 beams, a length penalty of 2.0, and a no repeat n-gram size of 2. The model is intended for use by child protection organizations, helpline services, social workers, and researchers for automated summarization of case calls, quick generation of case summaries, efficient case documentation, and analysis of child protection case patterns and trends. The model has limitations, including domain specificity, language restriction to English, a context window limited to 1024 input tokens, and the need for human review of outputs for critical cases. Ethical considerations include the handling of sensitive information, the need for professional review of outputs, the maintenance of privacy and confidentiality protocols, and the model's role as an assistant rather than a replacement for human judgment in child protection cases. The model shows significant improvements over the base FLAN-T5 model, with 50-84% improvement across ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, and ROUGE-L metrics, 94% accuracy in using child protection terminology, 91% success rate in identifying key case details, 87% accuracy in identifying referrals and follow-up actions, and a 4.4/5 overall quality rating from child protection professionals. Performance highlights include better identification of key information in child protection contexts, more structured and actionable summaries, proper use of child protection terminology and procedures, more reliable output format across different case types, and summaries that meet standards for official case documentation. Evaluation results include a Rouge1 score of 0.5804, a Rouge2 score of 0.3623, a Rougel score of 0.5325, a train loss of 0.8403, and a val loss of 0.8031.

instruction: How can I use the features from this image to classify it into a specific category?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperform specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. These models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones that provide multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers applied on the class token, or a linear layer applied on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) is a curated dataset of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) is a dataset of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, image retrieval, 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification. Evaluation results show that the models outperform previous models on multiple benchmarks. For example, the ViT-7B model achieves 90.4 on IN-ReaL, 91.1 on IN-R, 91.1 on Obj.Net, 72.8 on Ox.-H, 55.9 on ADE20k, 0.309 on NYU, 79.7 on DAVIS, 64.4 on NAVI, and 58.7 on SPair. The ConvNeXt Large model achieves 88.9 on IN-ReaL, 89.4 on IN-R, 81.3 on Obj.Net, 82.4 on ADE20k, 59.3 on NYU, 65.2 on DAVIS, 47.8 on NAVI, and 0.403 on SPair. On satellite data, the ViT-7B model achieves 74.0 on m-BEnet, 97.2 on m-brick-kiln, 94.8 on m-eurosat, 62.3 on m-forestnet, 96.1 on m-pv4ger, 62.1 on m-so2sat, and 81.1 mean for classification tasks, and 94.1 on m-cashew, 76.6 on m-chesapeake, 62.6 on m-NeonTree, 83.4 on m-nz-cattle, 95.5 on m-pv4ger-seg, 37.6 on m-SA-crop, and 75.0 mean for segmentation tasks. The model architecture includes Vision Transformer models with varying parameters and ConvNeXt models with different sizes. The training of the ViT-7B model required 61,440 hours of compute time on Nvidia H100 GPUs, resulting in an estimated carbon emission of 18t CO2eq. The models were trained using PyTorch 2.7 on private infrastructure in the USA.

instruction: Could you parse the formulas in this math document and format them in LaTeX?
description: dots.ocr is a multilingual document layout parsing model that unifies layout detection and content recognition within a single vision-language model while maintaining good reading order. Built on a compact 1.7B-parameter LLM foundation, it achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance for text, tables, and reading order, with formula recognition results comparable to much larger models like Doubao-1.5 and Gemini2.5-Pro. It demonstrates robust parsing capabilities for low-resource languages, achieving decisive advantages across both layout detection and content recognition on in-house multilingual document benchmarks. By leveraging a single vision-language model, dots.ocr offers a significantly more streamlined architecture than conventional methods that rely on complex, multi-model pipelines. Switching between tasks is accomplished simply by altering the input prompt, proving that a VLM can achieve competitive detection results compared to traditional detection models like DocLayout-YOLO. Built upon a compact 1.7B LLM, dots.ocr provides faster inference speeds than many other high-performing models based on larger foundations. It supports a range of layout categories, including 'Caption', 'Footnote', 'Formula', 'List-item', 'Page-footer', 'Page-header', 'Picture', 'Section-header', 'Table', 'Text', and 'Title'. Text extraction and formatting rules specify that 'Picture' elements omit text, 'Formula' elements are formatted as LaTeX, 'Table' elements as HTML, and all others as Markdown. The output text must be the original text from the image, with no translation, and all layout elements must be sorted according to human reading order. The final output is a single JSON object. dots.ocr is not yet perfect for high-complexity tables and formula extraction, and pictures in documents are currently not parsed. The model may fail to parse under certain conditions, such as when the character-to-pixel ratio is excessively high or when continuous special characters are present. It is not yet optimized for high-throughput processing of large PDF volumes. Future work includes achieving more accurate table and formula parsing, enhancing OCR capabilities for broader generalization, and developing a more general-purpose perception model based on VLMs that integrates general detection, image captioning, and OCR tasks into a unified framework. Parsing the content of pictures in documents is also a key priority for future work.

instruction: Can you analyze this video clip to determine if it shows any violent behavior?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of the VideoMAE architecture for binary violence detection in video content. It classifies videos into two categories: Violent Crime (1) and Non-Violent Incident (0). The model was trained on a curated subset of the UCF Crime dataset with event-based categorization, consisting of 600 videos, equally split between violent and non-violent categories. The event distribution includes 28 videos of abuse, 18 of arrest, 16 of arson, 62 of assault, 120 of burglary, 54 of explosion, 48 of fighting, 58 of road accidents, 184 of robbery, 36 of shoplifting, 46 of stealing, and 72 of vandalism. The dataset was split into 384 training videos, 96 validation videos, and 120 test videos. The model uses a VideoMAE Base architecture with a fine-tuning approach for event-based binary classification. It processes 16 frames per video clip at an input resolution of 224x224 pixels per frame. The model was trained for 15 epochs with a learning rate of 5e-05, a batch size of 2, and the AdamW optimizer. On the validation set, it achieved a loss of 1.4159, accuracy of 0.7188, precision of 0.7207, recall of 0.7188, and F1 score of 0.7190. On the test set, it achieved a loss of 1.7586, accuracy of 0.6833, precision of 0.6963, recall of 0.6833, and F1 score of 0.6802. The model was trained in 69.5 minutes and achieved a best accuracy of 0.7188. The model is intended for use in research and development in video analysis, security system enhancement with human oversight, and educational purposes in computer vision and AI safety. It is not suitable for unauthorized surveillance, discriminatory profiling, automated punishment, or privacy violations. The model may have limitations in generalizing to all types of violence, may miss context in longer sequences due to its 16-frame clip approach, and may be affected by environmental factors such as lighting, camera angles, and video quality. It may also misclassify intense but non-violent activities. The model was developed using the HuggingFace Transformers framework and is compatible with Apple Silicon MPS, CUDA, or CPU. It is based on the MCG-NJU/videomae-base model and is available for use with the HuggingFace Transformers library. The model was trained on 2025-06-02 11:52:12 UTC and is intended for use by researchers, security professionals, and developers.

instruction: Analyze the relevance of this document to the question about the effects of meditation on mental health.
description: The Qwen3-Reranker-4B is a text reranking model designed for text retrieval tasks. It is part of the Qwen3 Embedding model series, which is specifically developed for text embedding and ranking. The model is built upon the dense foundational models of the Qwen3 series and inherits their multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills. The Qwen3 Embedding series includes various sizes of embedding and reranking models (0.6B, 4B, and 8B), offering a comprehensive range of options for different use cases. The Qwen3-Reranker-4B has 4 billion parameters and supports a context length of 32,000 tokens. It is instruction-aware, meaning it can accept user-defined instructions to enhance performance for specific tasks, languages, or scenarios. The model supports over 100 languages, including various programming languages, and provides robust multilingual, cross-lingual, and code retrieval capabilities. Evaluation results show that the Qwen3-Reranker-4B achieves a score of 69.76 on the MTEB-R benchmark, 75.94 on the CMTEB-R benchmark, 72.74 on the MMTEB-R benchmark, 69.97 on the MLDR benchmark, 81.20 on the MTEB-Code benchmark, and 14.84 on the FollowIR benchmark. These results are based on top-100 candidates retrieved by a dense embedding model. The model is recommended to be used with customized instructions, as this can improve performance by 1% to 5% in most retrieval scenarios. Developers are advised to write instructions in English, as most training instructions were originally in English. The model can be used with the Transformers library version 4.51.0 or higher, or with the vLLM library version 0.8.5 or higher. It is recommended to enable flash_attention_2 for better acceleration and memory saving. The model processes input pairs consisting of a query and a document, and outputs a score indicating the relevance of the document to the query. The model is evaluated on a range of benchmarks, including MTEB, CMTEB, MMTEB, and MTEB-Code, and demonstrates strong performance across these tasks.

instruction: Is there a shift in the market regime based on the latest technical signals for NIFTY 50?
description: This is a production-ready XGBoost model for predicting market regimes (RISK_ON/RISK_OFF) in Indian financial markets, specifically for the NIFTY 50 index. It is part of a complete MLOps pipeline that includes experiment tracking, data versioning, and automated deployment. The model performs binary classification to predict whether the market is in a RISK_ON or RISK_OFF regime. RISK_ON indicates favorable market conditions with lower volatility and bullish momentum, suitable for aggressive trading, while RISK_OFF indicates cautious conditions with higher volatility, where defensive positioning is advised. The model uses four key technical indicators as features: India VIX (a volatility index), RSI (14-day) for momentum, 50-Day MA for short-term trend, and 200-Day MA for long-term trend. It is trained using XGBoost (Extreme Gradient Boosting) with the binary logistic objective function and logloss as the evaluation metric. The model is evaluated using both ML metrics and finance metrics, with production thresholds requiring an F1 score of at least 0.65 and a Sharpe Ratio of at least 0.5. The model is integrated into an MLOps system that includes MLflow for experiment tracking and model registry, DVC for data version control, GitHub Actions for automated CI/CD pipeline, FastAPI for REST API serving, Docker for containerized deployment, and Hugging Face Spaces for cloud deployment. The training pipeline includes data preprocessing, feature engineering, train/test split with stratification, XGBoost training with hyperparameters, model evaluation, backtesting on historical data, and model registration in MLflow. The model is retrained weekly via GitHub Actions, with automatic promotion to production if performance thresholds are met. The model is version-controlled in MLflow Model Registry under the name market_regime_classifier, version 1, in the Production stage, with run ID 43544e1ed8eb4c2a9268948b6795bdf5. The dataset used is synthetic market data for NIFTY 50, with an 80/20 train/test split. The model can be used via MLflow or a REST API endpoint. This project demonstrates MLOps pipeline architecture, experiment tracking, model registry management, data versioning, CI/CD automation, model serving, financial metrics and backtesting, Docker containerization, and cloud deployment. It is licensed under the MIT License for free use in learning and projects.

instruction: How can I get a detailed breakdown of the body positions in this photo?
description: YOLO26-X-pose is a variant of the Ultralytics YOLO26 model, specifically optimized for human keypoint detection. It is the latest evolution in the YOLO series, designed from the ground up for edge and low-power devices. The model is a native end-to-end architecture that produces predictions directly without the need for non-maximum suppression (NMS), which eliminates a post-processing step and results in faster, lighter, and easier deployment in real-world systems. The end-to-end design simplifies integration, reduces latency, and enhances deployment robustness across diverse environments. YOLO26 introduces the MuSGD optimizer, a hybrid of SGD and Muon, inspired by Moonshot AI's Kimi K2 breakthroughs in LLM training, which provides enhanced stability and faster convergence. The model includes features such as Residual Log-Likelihood Estimation (RLE) for more accurate keypoint localization, optimized decoding for increased inference speed, and the removal of DFL to simplify inference and improve hardware compatibility. It achieves an mAP Pose of 71.6 (50-95, e2e) and 91.6 (50, e2e), with a CPU inference speed of 565.4 ms and a T4 TensorRT10 speed of 12.2 ms. The model has 57.6 million parameters and 201.7 billion FLOPs. It is optimized for edge computing, offering up to 43% faster CPU inference.

description: Ditto is a framework designed to address the scarcity of high-quality training data for instruction-based video editing. It introduces a novel data generation pipeline that combines the creative diversity of a leading image editor with an in-context video generator. The framework employs an efficient, distilled model architecture augmented by a temporal enhancer to reduce computational overhead and improve temporal coherence. An intelligent agent drives the entire pipeline, crafting diverse instructions and filtering output to ensure quality control at scale. Using this framework, the Ditto-1M dataset was created, consisting of one million high-fidelity video editing examples. The Editto model was trained on this dataset using a curriculum learning strategy, demonstrating superior instruction-following ability and establishing a new state-of-the-art in instruction-based video editing. Editto is a model designed for video editing based on textual instructions. It supports tasks such as applying editing instructions to input videos to generate output videos. The model can be used with the DiffSynth framework or the ComfyUI interface. When using ComfyUI, it is noted that due to the use of quantized and distilled models, there may be some quality degradation compared to full-precision models. The model requires specific model weights and dependencies to be downloaded and set up in designated directories. The model is licensed under the CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 and is provided for academic research purposes only.

instruction: Summarize the key events of the French Revolution in under 100 words.
description: Next-1B is a 1-billion parameter causal language model based on Gemma 3, designed for efficiency, low-resource deployment, and reasoning-focused natural language understanding. It is optimized for text reasoning, summarization, and creative generation. The model supports Turkish natively while remaining multilingual. It is open-source and transparent for research and applications. Next-1B is ideal for developers, students, and organizations needing fast, reliable, and low-resource text generation. The model features a lightweight architecture optimized for low VRAM usage, making it suitable for small GPUs or CPU deployment. It is capable of logical chain-of-thought for question-answering and problem-solving, and it provides reliable and reproducible results across multiple runs. The model was fine-tuned using instruction fine-tuning (SFT) with Turkish and multilingual datasets. It is quantization-ready for q8, f16, and f32 formats. The model supports a range of use cases, including text generation, summarization, question and answer, creative writing, and reasoning tasks. Next-1B has demonstrated strong performance on benchmarks, achieving 87.3% on MMLU (5-shot), 69.2% on MMLU-Pro, 90.5% on GSM8K, and 70.1% on MATH. The model is available under the MIT License, which allows for free use, modification, and distribution with attribution appreciated.

description: This is the fine-tuned version of the EAT-base model, further trained on the AS-2M dataset. Compared to the pre-trained model, this version provides enhanced audio representations and typically yields better performance in downstream audio understanding tasks such as classification and captioning. The model supports both frame-level (~50Hz) and utterance-level (CLS token) representations. It is designed for feature extraction and is intended for use in audio understanding tasks. The model is based on the EAT framework, which is described in the associated paper titled 'EAT: Self-supervised pre-training with efficient audio transformer' by Chen, Wenxi and Liang, Yuzhe and Ma, Ziyang and Zheng, Zhisheng and Chen, Xie, published as an arXiv preprint in 2024.

instruction: Can you describe the activity occurring near the red car in this photo?
description: The VLM-FO1_Qwen2.5-VL-3B-v01 model is an implementation of the VLM-FO1 framework built on the Qwen2.5-VL-3B base model. VLM-FO1 is a novel plug-and-play framework designed to bridge the gap between the high-level reasoning of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and the need for fine-grained visual perception. It endows pre-trained VLMs with superior fine-grained perception without compromising their inherent high-level reasoning and general understanding capabilities. The framework operates as a plug-and-play module that can be integrated with any existing VLM, preserving its original weights and capabilities. VLM-FO1 excels at a wide range of fine-grained perception tasks, including Object Grounding, Region Generative Understanding, and Visual Region Reasoning. The framework is designed as a set of enhancement modules that can be seamlessly integrated with any pre-trained VLM. A novel Dual-Vision Encoder architecture, called the Hybrid Region Encoder (HFRE), fuses semantic-rich features with perception-enhanced features, creating powerful region tokens that capture both high-level meaning and fine-grained spatial detail. VLM-FO1 achieves state-of-the-art results across a diverse suite of benchmarks. A two-stage training strategy ensures that fine-grained perception is gained without causing catastrophic forgetting of the base model's powerful general visual understanding abilities.

instruction: Can you analyze this image and determine the spatial relationships between the objects in the scene?
description: Qwen3-VL is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced Thinking editions for deployment flexibility. Key enhancements include the ability to operate PC and mobile GUIs by recognizing elements, understanding functions, invoking tools, and completing tasks. It can generate Draw.io, HTML, CSS, and JS from images and videos. The model supports advanced spatial perception, including object position, viewpoint, and occlusion judgment, with 2D and 3D grounding for spatial reasoning and embodied AI. It has a native 256K context length, expandable to 1M, enabling it to handle books and hours-long video with full recall and second-level indexing. The model excels in enhanced multimodal reasoning, particularly in STEM and Math, with causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. It features upgraded visual recognition with broader, higher-quality pretraining that can recognize a wide range of subjects, including celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. The model supports expanded OCR in 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare or ancient characters and jargon, as well as long-document structure parsing. It achieves text understanding comparable to pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE for full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings, enhancing long-horizon video reasoning. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. This is the weight repository for Qwen3-VL-32B-Instruct.

description: KAIROS-MM-Qwen2.5-VL-7B-SFT is a multimodal reasoning model based on Qwen2.5-VL, designed to enable robots and vision AI agents to reason about the real world using physics understanding, prior knowledge, and common sense. The model understands space, time, and fundamental physical principles, and is well suited for planning, decision making, and long horizon video reasoning in embodied and agentic systems. It serves as a planning and reasoning backbone for embodied agents, allowing them to infer what actions to take next based on visual observations, temporal context, and physical constraints.

instruction: What steps should I take to upscale a video while ensuring smooth transitions between frames?
description: ComfyUI-SeedVR2_VideoUpscaler is a high-quality video and image upscaling model designed for use with ComfyUI. The model is available in multiple variants, including 3B and 7B parameter models, with options for FP16, FP8, and GGUF quantization to accommodate different VRAM requirements. It features temporal consistency to maintain coherence across video frames, multi-format support for RGB and RGBA, and any video length compatibility. The model includes memory optimization techniques such as BlockSwap technology, VAE tiling, and intelligent offloading to reduce VRAM usage. Performance features include torch.compile integration for speed improvements, multi-GPU CLI support, and model caching for faster batch processing. Quality control options include advanced color correction methods and noise injection controls. The model can be installed via ComfyUI Manager or manually, with models automatically downloaded from HuggingFace. It supports a modular node architecture in ComfyUI, including dedicated nodes for DiT model, VAE model, torch.compile settings, and the main upscaler. The standalone CLI provides batch processing capabilities, multi-GPU support, and sophisticated optimization options. The model has specific limitations, including a required batch_size following the 4n+1 formula and potential VRAM bottlenecks during VAE encoding/decoding. Best practices include starting with debug mode enabled, using appropriate optimization settings for VRAM constraints, and testing with short clips before processing long videos.

instruction: What outputs can I expect from a set of images of a room, in terms of geometry and camera positions?
description: Pi-Cubed ($\pi^3$) is a feed-forward neural network designed for visual geometry reconstruction without the need for a fixed reference view. Traditional methods often rely on a designated reference frame, which can lead to instability if the reference is suboptimal. In contrast, $\pi^3$ uses a fully permutation-equivariant architecture to directly predict affine-invariant camera poses and scale-invariant local point maps from an unordered set of images. This design allows the model to be robust to input ordering and highly scalable. An emergent property of the model's simple, bias-free design is the learning of a dense and structured latent representation of the camera pose manifold. Without complex priors or training schemes, $\pi^3$ achieves state-of-the-art performance on tasks such as camera pose estimation, monocular and video depth estimation, and dense point map estimation. The model takes a tensor of images as input and outputs a dictionary containing the reconstructed geometry, including a global point cloud, per-view local point maps, confidence scores for local points, and camera-to-world transformation matrices. The model is implemented in PyTorch and can be run on a batch of images, with optional support for mixed precision inference on compatible GPUs. The model is available for academic use under a 2-clause BSD License, and commercial use requires contacting the authors.

instruction: What does the note 'recommended' indicate for certain quant types?
description: This model provides various quantized versions of a model, including imatrix and IQ quants, which are available for download. The quants are sorted by size, though not necessarily by quality, with IQ quants often being preferable to similar-sized non-IQ quants. The available quant types include i1-IQ1_S, i1-IQ1_M, i1-IQ2_XXS, i1-IQ2_XS, i1-IQ2_S, i1-IQ2_M, i1-Q2_K_S, i1-Q2_K, i1-IQ3_XXS, i1-IQ3_XS, i1-Q3_K_S, i1-IQ3_S, i1-IQ3_M, i1-Q3_K_M, i1-Q3_K_L, i1-IQ4_XS, i1-Q4_0, i1-IQ4_NL, i1-Q4_K_S, i1-Q4_K_M, i1-Q4_1, i1-Q5_K_S, i1-Q5_K_M, and i1-Q6_K. Each quant type has a specified size in gigabytes and associated notes, such as 'for the desperate', 'very low quality', or 'recommended'. An imatrix file is also provided for creating custom quants. Users are advised to refer to external resources for detailed instructions on using GGUF files and concatenating multi-part files. The model's quantization work was made possible through the use of company servers and a private supercomputer, which enabled the production of higher-quality imatrix quants.

instruction: Can you estimate the depth and orientation of objects in these images?
description: AnySplat is a feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting model designed to process input from unconstrained views. It is intended to reconstruct 3D scenes from a set of 2D images by inferring camera poses and generating Gaussian representations of the scene. The model supports inference tasks, including the estimation of extrinsic and intrinsic camera parameters and the generation of interpolated video outputs based on the reconstructed 3D data. The architecture is implemented in Python using PyTorch and is designed to be used in a pipeline that includes image preprocessing, model inference, and post-processing for video output. The model is trained to handle multiple input images, which are processed and stacked into a tensor for inference. During inference, the model outputs Gaussian representations of the scene along with predicted camera poses. The model is evaluated using standard practices in 3D reconstruction and is intended for use in applications requiring 3D scene understanding from arbitrary camera views. The model is provided with a license, and users are encouraged to cite the associated research paper for any use of the model or its outputs.

instruction: ຂ້ອຍຕ້ອງການຄໍາຄຳຖາມເພື່ອສຳຫຼວດຜູ້ໃຊ້ງານກ່ຽວກັບຜົນກະທົບຂອງດິຈິຕອນຕໍ່ສັງຄົມລາວ, ຂ້ອຍສາມາດໄດ້ຮັບຄໍາຄຳຖາມໄດ້ບໍ?
description: This is a Lao language summarization model fine-tuned on a dataset using the base model Gemma 3-12B Instruct. The model is designed to generate concise summaries from Lao language text. It is specifically adapted to understand and generate responses in Lao language. It was trained using a curated dataset of over 5,000 high-quality Lao input-output pairs, primarily focused on AIDC (Artificial Intelligence and Digital Content) topics. The model supports summarization, question answering, and general chat tasks. The fine-tuning was performed using LoRA with PEFT (Unsloth). The training dataset consists of 10,000 Laos supervised samples, with a sequence length of 2048 and a batch size of 2 (with gradient accumulation). The model was trained for 3–5 epochs with early stopping enabled. The model is available in GGUF format, with F16 and Q8_0 versions available. The model is based on Google's Gemma 3-12B Instruct model and is suitable for Lao language generation. The model uses the Apache 2.0 license. The evaluation metric used is the BLEU score, which assesses the quality of generated summaries against reference summaries in the dataset.

instruction: I need to convert this audio file into a written transcript for a report. Can you do that?
description: The LFM2.5-Audio-1.5B-bf16 model is a multimodal audio model designed for a range of audio-related tasks, including Text-to-Speech (TTS), Speech-to-Text (ASR), and Speech-to-Speech (STS) conversion. It supports interleaved generation of text and audio in a single turn and enables real-time, token-by-token generation for low-latency applications. The model was converted to MLX format using mlx-audio version 0.3.0. It is based on a 1.5B parameter Liquid Foundation Model (LFM) backbone, which is used for multimodal reasoning. The model architecture includes an audio encoder based on a Conformer, an audio decoder using a Depthformer, and a detokenizer that reconstructs audio waveforms using ISTFT from predicted spectrograms. The model also supports decoding via the Mimi neural codec as an alternative. The model can be used with the mlx-audio library, which provides tools for preprocessing, tokenization, and decoding of both text and audio. It allows for the generation of interleaved text and audio responses, where each audio token is a complete frame of shape (8,) containing all 8 codebook values. The model supports streaming generation, enabling real-time audio playback during generation. It includes a ChatState class for managing conversation turns and handling both text and audio inputs and outputs. The model is designed to work with 24kHz audio inputs and can generate audio outputs at the same sample rate. The model's generation process can be configured with parameters such as max_new_tokens, temperature, top_k, and top_p for text, and similar parameters for audio generation. The model is intended for use in applications requiring natural speech synthesis, audio transcription, and voice-based conversation systems. The model follows the license terms of the original LFM2.5-Audio model.

instruction: Can you help me understand how a person's posture changes in a series of images?
description: RTMPose-Body2d is a machine learning model designed for human pose estimation, detecting the location and confidence of 133 joints. It is optimized for mobile deployment and is part of the RTMPose series. The model has an input resolution of 256x192, contains 17.9 million parameters, and has a model size of 68.5 MB in float precision and 18.2 MB in w8a16 precision. It is intended to run on Qualcomm® devices, including various Snapdragon® and QCS chipsets, using different runtimes such as TFLITE, QNN_DLC, and ONNX. Performance metrics include inference times ranging from 0.75 ms to 176.198 ms and peak memory usage ranging from 0 MB to 189 MB, depending on the device and precision. The model is primarily executed on the NPU, with some CPU-based execution for specific configurations. The model can be compiled for on-device deployment using PyTorch's JIT tracing and Qualcomm® AI Hub APIs. It supports performance profiling, accuracy verification, and deployment to Android using TensorFlow Lite or QNN shared libraries. The model is available for use with an API token through the Qualcomm® AI Hub, and additional details on performance across devices can be found on the Qualcomm® AI Hub website. The original implementation of RTMPose-Body2d is licensed separately.

description: The VideoMAEv2-Base model is a base-sized model pre-trained for 800 epochs in a self-supervised manner on the UnlabeldHybrid-1M dataset. It was introduced in the paper 'VideoMAE V2: Scaling Video Masked Autoencoders with Dual Masking' by Wang et al. and first released in 2023. The model is intended for use in video feature extraction. It is designed to process video data and extract meaningful features from it. The model can be used with the Transformers library by loading the appropriate configuration, image processor, and model from the 'OpenGVLab/VideoMAEv2-Base' repository. The video input is expected to be in the form of a list of numpy arrays with dimensions (16, 3, 224, 24), representing 16 frames of 224x224 RGB images. The input is processed using the VideoMAEImageProcessor, and the model outputs are obtained by passing the processed inputs through the model in a no-grad context. The model is described in the following BibTeX entries: @InProceedings{wang2023videomaev2, author = {Wang, Limin and Huang, Bingkun and Zhao, Zhiyu and Tong, Zhan and He, Yinan and Wang, Yi and Wang, Yali and Qiao, Yu}, title = {VideoMAE V2: Scaling Video Masked Autoencoders With Dual Masking}, booktitle = {Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)}, month = {June}, year = {2023}, pages = {14549-14560}} and @misc{videomaev2, title={VideoMAE V2: Scaling Video Masked Autoencoders with Dual Masking}, author={Limin Wang and Bingkun Huang and Zhiyu Zhao and Zhan Tong and Yinan He and Yi Wang and Yali Wang and Yu Qiao}, year={2023}, eprint={2303.16727}, archivePrefix={arXiv}, primaryClass={cs.CV}}.

description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict fugacity-based environmental fate parameter C. This property is used in fugacity models for environmental distribution prediction. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. The model type is AttentiveFP (Graph Neural Network), and it performs a regression task. It accepts SMILES strings as input and produces a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and is based on an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model's hyperparameters include a hidden channel size of 64, two layers, two timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.2, a learning rate of 0.001, a weight decay of 1e-05, a batch size of 32, 50 epochs, and a patience of 10. The model was trained on the FBC dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

description: This is an xlm-roberta-large model that has been fine-tuned on multilingual training data in English, German, Hungarian, Spanish, and Slovakian, labeled with media codes. The model incorporates seven additional media codes: State and Local Government Administration (24), Weather and Natural Disaster (26), Fires (27), Sports and Recreation (29), Death Notices (30), Churches and Religion (31), and Other, Miscellaneous and Human Interest (99). The model is designed for text classification tasks and is intended to categorize input text into one of the defined media codes. It is based on the xlm-roberta-large architecture and is used with a sentencepiece tokenizer. The model achieves an overall accuracy of 74%, with a macro average precision of 0.76, recall of 0.74, and F1-score of 0.73. The weighted average precision, recall, and F1-score are also 0.76, 0.74, and 0.73, respectively. Per-class performance varies, with some classes achieving high precision and recall, such as Death Notices (precision 0.9565, recall 0.88) and others showing lower performance, such as Other, Miscellaneous and Human Interest (precision 0.3789, recall 0.72). The model is used by an open-source and free natural language processing tool designed to simplify and speed up projects for comparative research. Model performance can be improved by extending the training sets, and contributions of CAP-coded corpora in any domain and language are welcomed. The model requires a token for access, and users may need to pass the token parameter when loading the model. If a RuntimeError occurs when loading the model using the from_pretrained() method, adding ignore_mismatched_sizes=True should resolve the issue.

description: This repository contains a drop-in, Hugging Face–compatible checkpoint converted from an original model. It can be loaded using the same code as the original model, without requiring any additional conversion steps. The model is based on an architecture that includes an embedding layer (embed_tokens) and an output layer (lm_head), both of which may change in shape when the vocabulary size is expanded. However, the attention mechanism remains unaffected, as it operates on the hidden state dimensions, which stay consistent. Expanding the vocabulary increases the number of token embeddings but does not alter the attention calculation logic. The only change in the model is the addition of new token embeddings, which are initialized and used during the first forward pass. The output layer (lm_head) changes in shape but not in functional logic. The model supports tasks involving both image and text inputs, such as answering questions about medical images. It can be loaded using the LlavaForConditionalGeneration class from the transformers library and an AutoProcessor for input processing. Example inference involves constructing a prompt with an image and a text question, tokenizing the input, and generating a response using the model's generate method. The model is compatible with multi-GPU setups and uses the bfloat16 data type for efficient computation. It also supports the flash_attention_2 implementation for attention calculations.
