description: This model is a fine-tuned version of the wav2vec2-large-xlsr-53-russian model on the Common Voice 17.0 dataset for the Russian language. It is designed for automatic speech recognition tasks, specifically for converting audio input into transcribed text. The model was trained using the CTC (Connectionist Temporal Classification) approach and is based on the wav2vec2 architecture. It was trained for 5 epochs with a learning rate of 0.0001, a training batch size of 8, and an evaluation batch size of 4. The training used the ADAMW_TORCH_FUSED optimizer with betas (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon 1e-08, and a linear learning rate scheduler. Mixed precision training with Native AMP was enabled. The model was evaluated on the Common Voice 17.0 test set, achieving a final word error rate (WER) of 22.3989, a character error rate (CER) of 4.8036, and a sentence error rate (SER) of 75.4264. During training, the model's performance was monitored at regular intervals, with validation loss and error rates recorded at each step. The training process involved preprocessing audio data to a sampling rate of 16000 Hz, removing non-essential metadata columns, and using a custom data collator for padding and batching. The model was implemented using the Hugging Face Transformers library, PyTorch 2.6.0, and related dependencies including Datasets 3.6.0 and Tokenizers 0.21.1. The model is intended for speech recognition tasks in Russian but no specific limitations or caveats are explicitly stated in the provided text.

instruction: Could you generate a Python script that reads a CSV file and prints the sum of a specific column?
description: Maincoder is a code-focused language model optimized for code generation and completion tasks. It is designed to perform well on coding benchmarks while maintaining a compact size suitable for local deployment. The model is optimized for Python code completion and generation and features a modern transformer architecture with RoPE embeddings, grouped-query attention, QK normalization, and a high depth-to-width ratio. It is pre-trained and mid-trained on custom data mixes developed for high-performance coding and is fine-tuned using a specialized reinforcement learning policy optimization algorithm to improve training stability and accelerate convergence. Maincoder achieves state-of-the-art performance on Python coding benchmarks HumanEval, HumanEval+, and MBPP+, with scores of 0.7622, 0.7256, and 0.7090 respectively. The model has 1 billion parameters and includes features such as rotary position embeddings with a theta of 1,000,000, RMSNorm for pre-normalization, grouped query attention with a 4:1 ratio of query to key-value heads, QK normalization with RMSNorm applied to attention queries and keys, and SwiGLU MLP with SiLU activation. The model has a hidden size of 1536, 32 layers, 16 attention heads (4 key-value heads), a head dimension of 96, a vocabulary size of 151,936, a context length of 2,048 tokens, and uses bfloat16 precision. It supports code completion tasks such as function completion, class completion, and algorithm implementation. The model has a context length limit of 2,048 tokens and is primarily optimized for Python, with performance potentially varying on other languages. It may generate code with bugs or security issues, so generated code should always be reviewed. The model has not undergone any alignment or safety tuning, and outputs may be unsafe or biased. Appropriate safeguards and careful evaluation are recommended for use. The model is released under a license and can be cited as Maincoder-1B: A High-Performance 1B Parameter Coding Model by the Maincode Team in 2025.

instruction: Can you help me generate a compact representation of this long article for fast comparison with others?
description: The Qwen3-Embedding-4B is a text embedding model from the Qwen3 Embedding model series, designed for text embedding and ranking tasks. It is part of a comprehensive range of models in various sizes (0.6B, 4B, and 8B) that provide text embeddings and reranking capabilities. The model inherits the multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills of the Qwen3 series. It supports over 100 languages, including various programming languages, and offers robust multilingual, cross-lingual, and code retrieval capabilities. The model has 4 billion parameters, a context length of 32,000 tokens, and an embedding dimension of up to 2560, with support for user-defined output dimensions ranging from 32 to 2560. It is instruction-aware, allowing users to customize input instructions for specific tasks, languages, or scenarios, which can improve performance by 1% to 5% in most retrieval scenarios. The model is evaluated on multiple benchmarks, achieving a score of 69.45 on the MTEB multilingual leaderboard (as of May 24, 2025), and it supports flexible vector definitions and seamless integration with other modules. Developers are advised to write instructions in English for optimal performance in multilingual contexts. The model can be used with frameworks such as Sentence Transformers, Transformers, and vLLM, and it is compatible with deployment via Text Embeddings Inference (TEI) on both GPU and CPU devices.

instruction: What are the critical factors to consider when designing a secure AI governance framework?
description: ELISARCyberAIEdge7B-LoRA-GGUF is a LoRA-finetuned, GGUF-quantized version of the Mistral-7B backbone, tailored for edge deployment in cybersecurity and blue-team AI scenarios. It is designed for contextualized cybersecurity risk assessment using Retrieval-Augmented Generation and Agentic AI capabilities. The model is based on Mistral-7B-v0.3 (FP16 / BF16) and includes a LoRA adapter called `sallani/ELISARCyberAIEdge7B`. It has been converted to GGUF format and optionally quantized to Q4_K_M (4-bit) for efficient inference on resource-constrained devices such as NVIDIA T4 and desktop GPUs. The resulting model file, `elisar_merged.gguf`, is approximately 160 MiB in size and can be deployed offline using frameworks or through minimal Torch-based inference. The model supports real-world use cases including threat modeling (Blue ELISAR), offensive use-case generation (Red ELISAR), and GRC compliance automation (GRC ELISAR). It is intended for tasks such as ISO/IEC 42001 and NIS2 risk evaluation, threat scenario generation, AI audit preparation and reporting, and secure AI system design. The model is developed by Dr. Sabri Sallani, an expert in Artificial Intelligence and Cybersecurity. Users are advised to use the instruction format `### Instruction:` / `### Response:` and to include relevant logs or code in prompts. The model is not intended as a replacement for certified analysts. The model is part of the ELISAR framework, which is described in a paper titled 'ELISAR: An Adaptive Framework for Cybersecurity Risk Assessment Powered by GenAI' to be published by Springer on July 10, 2025.

instruction: Does the statement 'The capital of France is Paris' align with known facts?
description: This is an ONNX-optimized version of the DeBERTa-v3-large model for efficient inference. The repository contains a regular ONNX exported model and an INT8 dynamically quantized model for faster inference with minimal accuracy loss. The model is optimized for zero-shot classification tasks across multiple languages. The quantized model provides faster inference, with a speedup of approximately 2-3x compared to PyTorch, a smaller size due to INT8 quantization, and maintained accuracy with less than 1% loss compared to the original model. The base model is DeBERTa-v3-large, and it is designed for zero-shot classification and natural language inference tasks. The model was converted from PyTorch to ONNX format and uses dynamic quantization with INT8 weights. It is implemented using ONNX Runtime with Optimum. The model is licensed under the MIT License. If used, it should be cited as part of the original DeBERTa-v3-large Zero-Shot Classification model by Laurer, Atteveldt, Casas, and Welbers in 2022. This ONNX optimization was created for efficient deployment in production environments, with special thanks to the original model authors and the Hugging Face Optimum team.

instruction: What are the odds that a newly released Pikachu card will increase in price by 30% over half a year?
description: This model is a PyTorch-based Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) designed to analyze features of Pokémon cards to identify those with potential for a significant price increase. It is a tabular binary classification model that takes various card features—such as rarity, type, and historical price data—as input and outputs a single logit. A sigmoid function can be applied to this logit to obtain a probability score for the likelihood of a price rise. The model is implemented in PyTorch and is part of a custom dataset derived from the PokemonTCG/pokemon-tcg-data repository, augmented with pricing history. The model is trained to predict a 30% price increase over a 6-month period. It is important to note that the model is trained on historical data and may not accurately predict future trends, especially in a volatile market. Additionally, the model is not trained for other thresholds or timeframes, and the dataset may contain biases related to card popularity, set releases, or data collection artifacts. SHAP values were computed to provide model explainability, showing the average impact of each feature on the model's output magnitude.

instruction: Can you determine the dominant color distributions in this image for clustering?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. The models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones that provide multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers on the class token, or a linear layer on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) consists of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) consists of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, with the teacher being a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, image retrieval, 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification. Evaluation results show that the ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on web data achieve high scores on IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair. The ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web data also achieve strong performance on similar tasks. The ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on satellite data achieve high scores on GEO-Bench classification and segmentation tasks. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters), ViT-S+ (29M parameters), ViT-B (86M parameters), ViT-L (300M parameters), ViT-H+ (840M parameters), and ViT-7B (6716M parameters), all with a patch size of 16 and varying embedding dimensions, register tokens, heads, and FFN types. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The models were trained using Nvidia H100 GPUs on private infrastructure in the USA, with 61,440 hours of compute time and an estimated carbon emission of 18t CO2eq. The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: How can I search for domain-specific information in a set of images and text?
description: TomoroAI/tomoro-colqwen3-embed-4b is a state-of-the-art multimodal embedding model that maps text queries, visual documents (images, PDFs), or short videos into aligned multi-vector embeddings. It is built by merging components with a full vision stack, inheriting robust text retrieval capabilities. The model has been fine-tuned on a curated mixture of data sources and achieves state-of-the-art or competitive performance across ViDoRe V1-V3 in both English and multilingual settings. It offers a significantly reduced embedding footprint compared to other full-dimension Colpali model alternatives. The architecture is based on Qwen3-VL 4B (encoder-only variant) with a 320-dimension projection head. It uses a ColPali-style late interaction method with MaxSim scoring. The model supports up to 1,280 visual tokens per page or 5,120 visual tokens per video, with text prompts constrained by the base context window of 32k. It is capable of handling complex layouts, such as chart-rich PDFs and domain-specific documents, and supports end-to-end retrieval of unseen multimodal documents without requiring OCR or an intermediate vision LLM for summaries. It demonstrates strong performance on non-English document inputs and inherits strong text retrieval performance from the Qwen3-Embedding-8B model. The model generalizes to video retrieval but is not fine-tuned on large-scale video retrieval datasets, and improvements are planned. It is larger than single-vector baselines despite the smaller token dimension. The model is not currently fine-tuned with diverse system instructions, and future improvements are intended through synthetic datasets. It is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license, with upstream Qwen checkpoints retaining their community licenses. The training data includes ViDoRe/MTEB corpora and synthetic VisRAG assets. The model is applied to enhance Tomoro’s customized multimodal agentic RAG pipelines, enabling autonomous agents to parse, reason over, and retrieve from large-scale enterprise internal documentation. It supports Tomoro AI's mission to deliver high-quality enterprise multimodal solutions across high-stakes industries.

instruction: Is there a way to extract only the areas marked as danger zones in this image?
description: This model is a Mask2Former-Large (Swin-L backbone) model fine-tuned on the DLR SkyScapes aerial imagery dataset for 20-class semantic segmentation. It is designed for high-resolution urban and peri-urban environments and produces detailed semantic masks for roads, buildings, vegetation, vehicles, and more. The architecture is based on the Mask2Former framework with a Transformer-based Swin-L backbone. The base checkpoint used is `facebook/mask2former-swin-large-ade-semantic`, and the model is trained for the task of semantic segmentation in the domain of high-resolution aerial imagery. It supports 20 semantic classes from the DLR SkyScapes dataset, which is a subset of the original 31 classes. The model uses the CrossEntropyLoss function with an ignore index of 255 and is preprocessed using the Hugging Face `AutoImageProcessor`. For full reproducibility, it is recommended to use `use_fast=False`. The model weights are stored in `model.safetensors`. The DLR SkyScapes dataset is a high-resolution aerial dataset created by the German Aerospace Center (DLR), providing high-quality pixel-level annotations for detailed scene understanding in urban environments. The dataset contains 16 images with a resolution of 5616 × 3744 pixels and a ground sampling distance of 13 cm per pixel. It covers an aerial area of approximately 5.69 km², including both urban and rural regions, with 70,346 annotated instances. The 20 semantic classes used in this model include low vegetation, paved road, non paved road, paved parking, non paved parking, bikeways, sidewalks, entrance/exit, danger area, lane markings, building, car, trailer, van, truck, long truck, bus, clutter, impervious surface, and tree. These classes correspond to the class IDs used during training. The intended applications of this model include urban mapping and GIS, HD map generation, scene understanding for robotics, autonomous driving (aerial-based priors), environmental monitoring, and remote sensing research. However, it is not suitable for safety-critical deployment without human supervision.

instruction: Find the best-fitting product description for a given customer query.
description: The all-mpnet-base-v2 model maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space, enabling tasks such as clustering or semantic search. It is designed to function as a sentence and short paragraph encoder, producing vectors that capture semantic information for use in information retrieval, clustering, or sentence similarity tasks. Input text longer than 384 word pieces is truncated by default. The model was developed using a self-supervised contrastive learning objective, fine-tuned on a dataset of over 1 billion sentence pairs. The training process involved computing cosine similarity between all possible sentence pairs in a batch and applying cross-entropy loss based on true pairs. The model was fine-tuned for 100,000 steps on a TPU v3-8 with a batch size of 1024 (128 per TPU core), a learning rate of 2e-5, and a sequence length limit of 128 tokens. The training data was a combination of multiple datasets, including citation pairs, duplicate question pairs, question-answer pairs, and image captions, among others. The total number of training tuples across all datasets is 1,170,060,424. The model was developed during a project organized by Hugging Face, with support from Google's Flax, JAX, and Cloud teams. It is intended for use with the Sentence-Transformers library, HuggingFace Transformers, or the Text Embeddings Inference (TEI) system. When using the HuggingFace Transformers library, the model requires a pooling operation to be applied on top of the contextualized word embeddings. The model can be deployed via a Docker container for CPU or NVIDIA GPU environments, and embeddings can be generated by sending a request to the /v1/embeddings endpoint.

instruction: Can you extract the text from this scanned document and format it into a clean markdown file?
description: Chandra is an OCR model that outputs markdown, HTML, and JSON. It is highly accurate at extracting text from images and PDFs, while preserving layout information. Chandra supports converting documents to markdown, HTML, or JSON with detailed layout information. It has good handwriting support and can reconstruct forms accurately, including checkboxes. It also provides good support for tables, math, and complex layouts. The model extracts images and diagrams, including captions and structured data, and supports 40+ languages. Chandra has been evaluated using the olmocr benchmark, which is considered the most reliable current OCR benchmark in testing. In the benchmark, Chandra v0.1.0 achieved an overall score of 83.1 ± 0.9, with scores of 82.2 on ArXiv, 80.3 on Old Scans Math, 88.0 on Tables, 50.4 on Old Scans, 90.8 on Headers and Footers, 81.2 on Multi column, and 92.3 on Long tiny text. It outperformed several other models in multiple categories. Chandra can be used via a command-line interface, an interactive streamlit app, or through code using the HuggingFace or VLLM methods. The model is available for installation via pip and can be accessed through a hosted API or a free playground.

instruction: Where is the cat hiding in this picture?
description: OWL-ViT, short for Vision Transformer for Open-World Localization, is a zero-shot text-conditioned object detection model that allows querying an image with one or multiple text queries. It was proposed by Matthias Minderer, Alexey Gritsenko, Austin Stone, Maxim Neumann, Dirk Weissenborn, Alexey Dosovitskiy, Aravindh Mahendran, Anurag Arnab, Mostafa Dehghani, Zhuoran Shen, Xiao Wang, Xiaohua Zhai, Thomas Kipf, and Neil Houlsby. OWL-ViT uses CLIP as its multi-modal backbone, combining a ViT-like Transformer for visual features and a causal language model for text features. To enable detection, the final token pooling layer of the vision model is removed, and a lightweight classification and box head is attached to each transformer output token. Open-vocabulary classification is achieved by replacing the fixed classification layer weights with class-name embeddings from the text model. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned end-to-end with the classification and box heads on standard detection datasets using a bipartite matching loss. The model uses a CLIP backbone with a ViT-B/16 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned together with the box and class prediction heads with an object detection objective. The CLIP backbone of the model was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including crawling a handful of websites and using commonly-used pre-existing image datasets. A large portion of the data comes from crawling the internet, making it more representative of people and societies most connected to the internet. The prediction heads of OWL-ViT, along with the CLIP backbone, are fine-tuned on publicly available object detection datasets. The model is intended as a research output for research communities. It is primarily intended for use by AI researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. The model is designed to enable researchers to explore zero-shot, text-conditioned object detection and to support interdisciplinary studies of the potential impact of such models, especially in areas that commonly require identifying objects whose label is unavailable during training.

instruction: Is it possible to align this image with a textual description to verify their semantic consistency?
description: VTP is a unified visual tokenizer pre-training framework designed to improve the quality of latent spaces for generative models. It integrates image-text contrastive, self-supervised, and reconstruction learning to train visual tokenizers from scratch. The framework addresses the 'pre-training scaling problem,' where traditional reconstruction-based training leads to latent spaces biased toward low-level information, which does not translate to better generation performance. VTP introduces a new paradigm where understanding high-level semantics becomes a key driver for improving generation quality. The model supports multiple tasks, including zero-shot classification, linear probing classification, image reconstruction, and image generation. It demonstrates strong performance, achieving 78.2% zero-shot accuracy, 0.36 rFID for reconstruction, and 2.81 FID for generation. VTP shows significant scaling properties, with generative performance improving effectively with increased compute, parameters, and data. Compared to conventional autoencoders, VTP achieves a 65.8% improvement in FID for downstream generation when trained with the same DiT specifications. The model is available for use as an autoencoder, a feature extractor for self-supervised learning, and a CLIP-like image-text encoder. The authors caution that traditional autoencoders cannot be scaled up for diffusion generative models and emphasize the importance of representation learning for parameter, data, and training scalability.

instruction: What is the most likely label for this image based on its content?
description: The convnext_small.dinov3_lvd1689m is a DINOv3 ConvNeXt image feature model. It is designed for image classification and as a feature backbone. The model is pretrained on the LVD-1689M dataset using the self-supervised DINOv3 method and was distilled from a DINOv3 ViT-7B model. It has 49.5 million parameters, 8.7 GMACs, and 21.6 million activations. The model processes images of size 224 x 224. It supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. For image classification, the model can be used to generate output probabilities and class indices. For feature map extraction, the model can produce feature maps with varying spatial dimensions. For image embeddings, the model can generate embeddings either by removing the classifier or using forward features and head functions. The model is based on the ConvNeXt architecture described in the paper 'A ConvNet for the 2020s' and the DINOv3 method described in the paper 'DINOv3'. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Image Models library.

instruction: What are the best practices for maintaining good physical and mental health?
description: Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct is a next-generation foundation model designed to support increasingly powerful and agentic artificial intelligence through innovative model architecture. It is the first installment in the Qwen3-Next series and features a hybrid attention mechanism that combines Gated DeltaNet and Gated Attention to enable efficient context modeling for ultra-long context lengths. The model also incorporates a high-sparsity Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) design, which achieves an extreme low activation ratio in MoE layers, drastically reducing FLOPs per token while preserving model capacity. Additional stability optimizations include zero-centered and weight-decayed layernorm, and other enhancements for robust pre-training and post-training. The model also supports Multi-Token Prediction (MTP), which boosts pretraining performance and accelerates inference. Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct is a causal language model with 80B total parameters and 3B activated parameters, trained on 15T tokens. It has 48 layers with a hybrid layout of 12 repetitions of (3 repetitions of (Gated DeltaNet -> MoE) -> 1 repetition of (Gated Attention -> MoE)). The Gated Attention component has 16 Q heads and 2 KV heads, with a head dimension of 256 and a rotary position embedding dimension of 64. The Gated DeltaNet has 32 V heads and 16 QK heads, with a head dimension of 128. The MoE component includes 512 experts, with 10 activated experts and 1 shared expert, and an expert intermediate dimension of 512. The model natively supports a context length of 262,144 tokens and is extensible up to 1,010,000 tokens using RoPE scaling techniques. Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct outperforms Qwen3-32B-Base on downstream tasks with 10% of the training cost and 10 times the inference throughput for contexts over 32K tokens. It performs on par with Qwen3-235B-A22B-Instruct-2507 on certain benchmarks while demonstrating significant advantages in handling ultra-long-context tasks up to 256K tokens. The model supports instruct (non-thinking) mode and does not generate code blocks in its output. It is recommended to use dedicated inference frameworks such as SGLang or vLLM for optimal efficiency and throughput. The model is capable of handling a wide range of tasks, including knowledge-based reasoning, coding, alignment, agent-based interactions, and multilingualism. For ultra-long text processing, the model can be configured with YaRN to handle context lengths up to 1 million tokens. Best practices for using the model include setting sampling parameters such as Temperature=0.7, TopP=0.8, TopK=20, and MinP=0, using an output length of 16,384 tokens for most queries, and standardizing output formats for benchmarking. The model is available through Hugging Face Transformers and can be deployed using SGLang or vLLM to create an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint. It is important to note that the efficiency or throughput improvement depends highly on the implementation, and the use of a dedicated inference framework is recommended. Additionally, the default context length is 256K, and it may be necessary to reduce the context length if the server fails to start. The model is also recommended for agentic use with Qwen-Agent, which encapsulates tool-calling templates and parsers to reduce coding complexity.

instruction: Can you generate a radiology report for this X-ray based on the visible features?
description: MedGemma is a collection of multimodal and text-only models trained for performance on medical text and image comprehension. Developers can use MedGemma to accelerate the building of healthcare-based AI applications. MedGemma currently comes in three variants: a 4B multimodal version and 27B text-only and multimodal versions. Both MedGemma multimodal versions utilize an image encoder that has been specifically pre-trained on a variety of de-identified medical data, including chest X-rays, dermatology images, ophthalmology images, and histopathology slides. Their LLM components are trained on a diverse set of medical data, including medical text, medical question-answer pairs, FHIR-based electronic health record data (27B multimodal only), radiology images, histopathology patches, ophthalmology images, and dermatology images. MedGemma 4B is available in both pre-trained (suffix: `-pt`) and instruction-tuned (suffix `-it`) versions. The instruction-tuned version is a better starting point for most applications. The pre-trained version is available for those who want to experiment more deeply with the models. MedGemma 27B multimodal has pre-training on medical image, medical record and medical record comprehension tasks. MedGemma 27B text-only has been trained exclusively on medical text. Both models have been optimized for inference-time computation on medical reasoning. This means it has slightly higher performance on some text benchmarks than MedGemma 27B multimodal. Users who want to work with a single model for both medical text, medical record and medical image tasks are better suited for MedGemma 27B multimodal. Those that only need text use-cases may be better served with the text-only variant. Both MedGemma 27B variants are only available in instruction-tuned versions. MedGemma variants have been evaluated on a range of clinically relevant benchmarks to illustrate their baseline performance. These evaluations are based on both open benchmark datasets and curated datasets. Developers can fine-tune MedGemma variants for improved performance. MedGemma is optimized for medical applications that involve a text generation component. For medical image-based applications that do not involve text generation, such as data-efficient classification, zero-shot classification, or content-based or semantic image retrieval, the MedSigLIP is recommended. MedSigLIP is based on the same image encoder that powers MedGemma. The MedGemma model is built based on Gemma 3 and uses the same decoder-only transformer architecture as Gemma 3. The model type is a decoder-only Transformer architecture. The input modalities for 4B and 27B multimodal models are text and vision, while the 27B text-only model is text only. The output modality for all models is text only. The attention mechanism used is grouped-query attention (GQA). The context length supports long context, at least 128K tokens. The model was created on July 9, 2025, and the model version is 1.0.0. Inputs include text strings, such as a question or prompt, images normalized to 896 x 896 resolution and encoded to 256 tokens each, and a total input length of 128K tokens. Outputs include generated text in response to the input, such as an answer to a question, analysis of image content, or a summary of a document, with a total output length of 8192 tokens. MedGemma was evaluated across a range of different multimodal classification, report generation, visual question answering, and text-based tasks. The multimodal performance of MedGemma 4B and 27B multimodal was evaluated across a range of benchmarks, focusing on radiology, dermatology, histopathology, ophthalmology, and multimodal clinical reasoning. MedGemma 4B outperforms the base Gemma 3 4B model across all tested multimodal health benchmarks. MedGemma chest X-ray (CXR) report generation performance was evaluated using the MIMIC CXR dataset and the RadGraph F1 metric. The instruction-tuned versions of MedGemma 4B and MedGemma 27B achieve lower scores due to the differences in reporting style compared to the MIMIC ground truth reports. Further fine-tuning on MIMIC reports enables users to achieve improved performance. MedGemma 4B and text-only MedGemma 27B were evaluated across a range of text-only benchmarks for medical knowledge and reasoning. The MedGemma models outperform their respective base Gemma models across all tested text-only health benchmarks. All models were evaluated on a question answer dataset from synthetic FHIR data to answer questions about patient records. MedGemma 27B multimodal's FHIR-specific training gives it significant improvement over other MedGemma and Gemma models. The evaluation methods include structured evaluations and internal red-teaming testing of relevant content policies. Red-teaming was conducted by a number of different teams, each with different goals and human evaluation metrics. These models were evaluated against a number of different categories relevant to ethics and safety, including child safety, content safety, representational harms, and general medical harms. For all areas of safety testing, we saw safe levels of performance across the categories of child safety, content safety, and representational harms. All testing was conducted without safety filters to evaluate the model capabilities and behaviors. For text-to-text, image-to-text, and audio-to-text, and across both MedGemma model sizes, the model produced minimal policy violations. A limitation of our evaluations was that they included primarily English language prompts. The base Gemma models are pre-trained on a large corpus of text and code data. MedGemma multimodal variants utilize an image encoder that has been specifically pre-trained on a variety of de-identified medical data, including radiology images, histopathology images, ophthalmology images, and dermatology images. Their LLM component is trained on a diverse set of medical data, including medical text, medical question-answer pairs, FHIR-based electronic health record data (27B multimodal only), radiology images, histopathology patches, ophthalmology images, and dermatology images. MedGemma models have been evaluated on a comprehensive set of clinically relevant benchmarks, including over 22 datasets across 6 different tasks and 4 medical image modalities. These benchmarks include both open and internal datasets. MedGemma utilizes a combination of public and private datasets. This model was trained on diverse public datasets including MIMIC-CXR (chest X-rays and reports), ChestImaGenome (set of bounding boxes linking image findings with anatomical regions for MIMIC-CXR), SLAKE (multimodal medical images and questions), PAD-UFES-20 (skin lesion images and data), SCIN (dermatology images), TCGA (cancer genomics data), CAMELYON (lymph node histopathology images), PMC-OA (biomedical literature with images), and Mendeley Digital Knee X-Ray (knee X-rays). Additionally, multiple diverse proprietary datasets were licensed and incorporated. MedGemma is an open multimodal generative AI model intended to be used as a starting point that enables more efficient development of downstream healthcare applications involving medical text and images. MedGemma is intended for developers in the life sciences and healthcare space. Developers are responsible for training, adapting and making meaningful changes to MedGemma to accomplish their specific intended use. MedGemma models can be fine-tuned by developers using their own proprietary data for their specific tasks or solutions. MedGemma is based on Gemma 3 and has been further trained on medical images and text. MedGemma enables further development in any medical context (image and textual), however the model was pre-trained using chest X-ray, pathology, dermatology, and fundus images. Examples of tasks within MedGemma's training include visual question answering pertaining to medical images, such as radiographs, or providing answers to textual medical questions. MedGemma provides strong baseline medical image and text comprehension for models of its size. This strong performance makes it efficient to adapt for downstream healthcare-based use cases, compared to models of similar size without medical data pre-training. This adaptation may involve prompt engineering, grounding, agentic orchestration or fine-tuning depending on the use case, baseline validation requirements, and desired performance characteristics. MedGemma is not intended to be used without appropriate validation, adaptation and/or making meaningful modification by developers for their specific use case. The outputs generated by MedGemma are not intended to directly inform clinical diagnosis, patient management decisions, treatment recommendations, or any other direct clinical practice applications. Performance benchmarks highlight baseline capabilities on relevant benchmarks, but even for image and text domains that constitute a substantial portion of training data, inaccurate model output is possible. All outputs from MedGemma should be considered preliminary and require independent verification, clinical correlation, and further investigation through established research and development methodologies. MedGemma's multimodal capabilities have been primarily evaluated on single-image tasks. MedGemma has not been evaluated in use cases that involve comprehension of multiple images. MedGemma has not been evaluated or optimized for multi-turn applications. MedGemma's training may make it more sensitive to the specific prompt used than Gemma 3. When adapting MedGemma, developers should consider the following: bias in validation data, as with any research, developers should ensure that any downstream application is validated to understand performance using data that is appropriately representative of the intended use setting for the specific application (e.g., age, sex, gender, condition, imaging device, etc). Data contamination concerns, when evaluating the generalization capabilities of a large model like MedGemma in a medical context, there is a risk of data contamination, where the model might have inadvertently seen related medical information during its pre-training, potentially overestimating its true ability to generalize to novel medical concepts. Developers should validate MedGemma on datasets not publicly available or otherwise made available to non-institutional researchers to mitigate this risk.

instruction: Convert a technical manual written in French into its equivalent in Russian for a client in Moscow.
description: LMT-60 is a suite of Chinese-English-centric multilingual machine translation (MMT) models trained on 90B tokens, including both monolingual and bilingual data, covering 60 languages across 234 translation directions. The model achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance among models with similar language coverage. Both the CPT and SFT versions of LMT-60 are available in four sizes: 0.6B, 1.7B, 4B, and 8B. The supervised fine-tuning (SFT) data are publicly released. The model supports translation tasks across a wide range of languages, including 13 high-resource languages, 18 medium-resource languages, and 29 low-resource languages. The high-resource languages include Arabic, English, Spanish, German, French, Italian, Japanese, Dutch, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Turkish, and Chinese. The medium-resource languages include Bulgarian, Bengali, Czech, Danish, Modern Greek, Persian, Finnish, Hindi, Hungarian, Indonesian, Korean, Norwegian, Romanian, Slovak, Swedish, Thai, Ukrainian, and Vietnamese. The low-resource languages include Amharic, Azerbaijani, Tibetan, Modern Hebrew, Croatian, Armenian, Icelandic, Javanese, Georgian, Kazakh, Central Khmer, Kirghiz, Lao, Chinese Mongolian, Marathi, Malay, Burmese, Nepali, Pashto, Sinhala, Swahili, Tamil, Telugu, Tajik, Tagalog, Uighur, Urdu, Uzbek, and Yue Chinese. The model is designed to perform translation tasks and is available for use through the Hugging Face Transformers library. The model is described in a paper titled 'Beyond English: Toward Inclusive and Scalable Multilingual Machine Translation with LLMs,' authored by Yingfeng Luo, Ziqiang Xu, Yuxuan Ouyang, Murun Yang, Dingyang Lin, Kaiyan Chang, Tong Zheng, Bei Li, Peinan Feng, Quan Du, Tong Xiao, and Jingbo Zhu, and available on arXiv with the identifier 2511.07003.

instruction: I have a list of product descriptions; could you help me find the one that best matches this customer's question?
description: jina-reranker-v3 is a 0.6B parameter multilingual document reranker with a novel last but not late interaction architecture. Unlike ColBERT's separate encoding with multi-vector matching, this model performs causal self-attention between query and documents within the same context window, extracting contextual embeddings from the last token of each document. Built on Qwen3-0.6B with 28 transformer layers and a lightweight MLP projector (1024→512→256), it processes up to 64 documents simultaneously within 131K token context. The model achieves state-of-the-art BEIR performance with 61.94 nDCG@10 while being 10× smaller than generative listwise rerankers. It is designed to rerank documents based on their relevance to a given query, supporting multilingual retrieval tasks. The model can be used for reranking in applications such as information retrieval, document search, and question answering. The model is available for local inference using the transformers library and can also be accessed via Jina AI's API for faster integration. The rerank function takes a query and a list of documents, returning a list of documents sorted by relevance score. The model outputs include the document text, relevance score, and optionally, document embeddings. The model is licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0 for non-commercial use. For commercial usage inquiries, contact the appropriate licensing authority. The model is listed on AWS and Azure platforms.

instruction: Is there a way to modify the shape of a 3D object by changing code generated from its scan?
description: MeshCoder is a framework that converts 3D point clouds into editable Blender Python scripts, enabling programmatic reconstruction and editing of complex human-made objects. It addresses prior limitations by developing expressive APIs for modeling intricate geometries, building a large-scale dataset of 1 million object-code pairs across 41 categories, and training a multimodal LLM to generate accurate, part-segmented code from point clouds. The approach outperforms existing methods in reconstruction quality, supports intuitive shape and topology editing via code modifications, and enhances 3D reasoning capabilities in LLMs.

instruction: Make a video of a dancing robot in a sci-fi environment, including synthesized electronic music that syncs with the movements.
description: LTX-2 is a DiT-based audio-video foundation model designed to generate synchronized video and audio within a single model. It integrates the core building blocks of modern video generation, with open weights and a focus on practical, local execution. The model is developed by Lightricks and is based on a diffusion approach, supporting English language inputs. LTX-2 is intended for tasks involving the generation of audio-visual content, including image-to-video generation. The model is available in multiple versions, including a full model, quantized variants (fp8, nvfp4), a distilled version, and LoRA versions. Additional components include spatial and temporal upscalers for increasing resolution and frame rate in multi-stage pipelines. The model can be used locally with a direct use license, and is supported in the PyTorch framework and the Diffusers library. The codebase is compatible with Python 3.12 or higher, CUDA 12.7 or higher, and PyTorch 2.7. The base model is fully trainable, and training for specific aspects such as motion, style, or likeness can be completed in under an hour in many settings. The model has several limitations: it is not intended to provide factual information, may amplify societal biases, may not perfectly match prompts, is influenced by prompting style, may generate inappropriate or offensive content, and may produce lower quality audio when no speech is present.

instruction: I need to understand the spatial relationships between objects in this scene. Can you provide insights?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. These models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones that provide multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers on the class token, or a linear layer on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) consists of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) consists of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, image retrieval, 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on web data, the models achieve results such as 87.0 on IN-ReaL, 60.4 on IN-R, 50.9 on Obj.Net, 49.5 on Ox.-H, 47.0 on ADE20k, 0.403 on NYU, 72.7 on DAVIS, 56.3 on NAVI, and 50.4 on SPair for the ViT-S/16 model. For ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web data, the models achieve results such as 86.6 on IN-ReaL @256px, 87.7 on IN-ReaL @512px, 73.7 on Obj.Net @256px, 74.1 on Obj.Net @512px, 52.6 on ADE20k @256px, 58.7 on ADE20k @512px, 42.7 on NYU @256px, and 0.448 on NYU @512px for the ConvNeXt Tiny model. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on satellite data, the models achieve results such as 73.0 on m-BEnet, 96.5 on m-brick-kiln, 94.1 on m-eurosat, 60.6 on m-forestnet, 96.0 on m-pv4ger, 57.4 on m-so2sat, and 79.6 mean for the ViT-L/16 model. The models were trained using Nvidia H100 GPUs on private infrastructure in the USA, with 61,440 hours of compute time for the ViT-7B model, resulting in 18t CO2eq of carbon emissions. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters), ViT-S+ (29M parameters), ViT-B (86M parameters), ViT-L (300M parameters), ViT-H+ (840M parameters), and ViT-7B (6716M parameters), all with a patch size of 16 and varying embedding dimensions, register tokens, heads, and FFN types. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: Which of these headlines best captures the essence of the article?
description: The all-mpnet-base-v2 model maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space, enabling tasks such as clustering or semantic search. It is designed to function as a sentence and short paragraph encoder, producing vectors that capture semantic information for use in information retrieval, clustering, or sentence similarity tasks. Input text longer than 384 word pieces is truncated by default. The model was developed using a self-supervised contrastive learning objective, fine-tuned on a dataset of over 1 billion sentence pairs. The training process involved computing cosine similarity between all possible sentence pairs in a batch and applying cross-entropy loss based on true pairs. The model was fine-tuned for 100,000 steps on a TPU v3-8 with a batch size of 1024 (128 per TPU core), a learning rate of 2e-5, and a sequence length limit of 128 tokens. The training data was a combination of multiple datasets, including citation pairs, duplicate question pairs, question-answer pairs, and image captions, among others. The total number of training tuples across all datasets is 1,170,060,424. The model was developed during a project organized by Hugging Face, with support from Google's Flax, JAX, and Cloud teams. It is intended for use with the Sentence-Transformers library, HuggingFace Transformers, or the Text Embeddings Inference (TEI) system. When using the HuggingFace Transformers library, the model requires a pooling operation to be applied on top of the contextualized word embeddings. The model can be deployed via a Docker container for CPU or NVIDIA GPU environments, and embeddings can be generated by sending a request to the /v1/embeddings endpoint.

instruction: How do I build a recommendation system that uses both product descriptions and images?
description: The embedding model trained by Jina AI is a version of the Qwen2.5-VL model with a retrieval adapter merged into the base weights. This modification allows the model to be natively compatible with vLLM without the need for custom adapter-handling code. The model is designed for multimodal multilingual retrieval tasks, supporting both text and image inputs. It can encode text prompts and image prompts to generate embeddings. For text, prompts are structured with either a 'Query' or 'Passage' prefix. For images, a 'user' instruction is provided alongside the image data. The model processes these prompts and generates embeddings, which are pooled and normalized. Vision tokens are isolated for image prompts by identifying specific token IDs marking the start and end of the image content. The embeddings for text-only prompts are derived from all tokens. The model uses PyTorch for computation and supports float16 data type for efficiency. The model is intended for use in retrieval systems where both textual and visual information may be relevant.

instruction: Design a motion clip of someone bending down to pick up an object and then standing up again.
description: HY-Motion 1.0 is a series of text-to-3D human motion generation models based on Diffusion Transformer (DiT) and Flow Matching. It enables developers to generate skeleton-based 3D character animations from simple text prompts, which can be directly integrated into various 3D animation pipelines. This model series is the first to scale DiT-based text-to-motion models to the billion-parameter level, achieving significant improvements in instruction-following capabilities and motion quality over existing open-source models. The HY-Motion 1.0 series includes two models: HY-Motion-1.0, a standard text-to-motion model with 1.0 billion parameters, and HY-Motion-1.0-Lite, a lightweight version with 0.46 billion parameters. Both models require a minimum of 24GB GPU VRAM for operation. The models are trained using a three-stage process: large-scale pre-training on over 3,000 hours of diverse motion data to learn a broad motion prior, high-quality fine-tuning on 400 hours of curated, high-quality 3D motion data to enhance motion detail and smoothness, and reinforcement learning using human feedback and reward models to refine instruction-following and motion naturalness. HY-Motion 1.0 supports macOS, Windows, and Linux. It provides a command-line interface for batch inference and a Gradio-based web interface for interactive visualization. Users are advised to use English prompts under 60 words, focusing on action descriptions or detailed movements of the limbs and torso. The model does not support non-humanoid characters, subjective or visual attributes, environment or camera descriptions, multi-person interactions, or special modes such as seamless loops or in-place animations. Example prompts include descriptions of specific physical actions like squats, climbing, standing up, and walking. The model is evaluated for its state-of-the-art performance in instruction-following and motion quality, with the billion-scale models outperforming comparable open-source models. Users should note that the model requires specific settings to reduce GPU VRAM usage, such as limiting the number of seeds, using short text prompts, and generating motions under 5 seconds in length.

instruction: I have a song and want to create a version with only the background instruments—can you help?
description: Mini-BS-RoFormer-V2 is a model designed for the music source separation task. It has been improved upon previous versions. The model has a total of 46.8 million parameters and uses BF16 weight precision. On the validation set of the MUSDB18HQ dataset, the model achieves an SDR score of 10.03 for overall tracks, 9.68 for bass, 10.58 for drums, 8.99 for other, and 10.86 for vocals. The model uses time dimension downsampling to significantly reduce resource consumption. The computational cost for inference on 30 seconds of audio is 8343.55 GFLOPs. The model requires the transformers library version 4.55.4, as well as the soundfile, einops, and librosa libraries to function properly. The model can separate audio into bass, drums, other, and vocal tracks. If only vocals and instrumental tracks are desired, the bass, drums, and other tracks can be merged. The model was trained using the train and test sets of the MUSDB18HQ dataset. Training was conducted for 310k steps with a constant learning rate of 5e-4 and a batch size of 16. The Muon optimizer was used for training the transformer layers, while the AdamW optimizer was used for other network layers. Key improvements in Mini-BS-RoFormer-V2 compared to previous versions include training with bf16-true precision, using the Muon optimizer for faster convergence, changing the n_fft for the STFT operation from 2048 to 4096 for input audio, switching from 62 frequency bands to 80 mel-based frequency bands, time dimension downsampling with a step size of 4, and additional code modifications.

instruction: Can you describe the movement of the person in this video and their surroundings?
description: Molmo2 is a family of open vision-language models developed by the Allen Institute for AI (Ai2) that support image, video, and multi-image understanding and grounding. The models are trained on publicly available third-party datasets, which include highly-curated image-text and video-text pairs. Molmo2 models demonstrate state-of-the-art performance among multimodal models of similar size. The Molmo2-O-7B variant is based on a specific architecture and uses a specific vision backbone. It outperforms other open weight and data models on short videos, counting, and captioning, and is competitive on long-videos. Ai2 is committed to open science, and the Molmo2 datasets are available. All other artifacts used in creating Molmo2, including training code, evaluations, and intermediate checkpoints, will be made available at a later date to support open-source AI development and reproducibility. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is intended for research and educational use in accordance with Ai2’s policies. It is trained on third-party datasets that are subject to academic and non-commercial research use only. Users should review the sources to determine if the model is appropriate for their use case. Evaluations show that Molmo2-O-7B achieves an average score of 59.7 on 15 academic benchmarks, compared to other models such as GPT-5 (70.6), Gemini 3 Pro (70.0), and others. The model supports tasks such as general video question answering and pointing video question answering, where it can generate text responses and extract pointing coordinates as a list of (frame_id, x, y) triplets from model output text.

instruction: How can I extract the sky region from this image to improve the accuracy of depth estimation?
description: DA3 Nested is a model that combines the any-view Giant model with the metric Large model for metric-scale visual geometry reconstruction. It is designed to perform a range of visual geometry tasks, including relative depth estimation, pose estimation, pose conditioning, 3D Gaussian generation, metric depth estimation, and sky segmentation. The model is based on a plain transformer architecture with a unified depth-ray representation, and it uses a vision transformer for visual geometry. It was developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is trained exclusively on public academic datasets. The model has 1.40B parameters and is licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0, which restricts its use to non-commercial purposes. It significantly outperforms Depth Anything 2 for monocular depth estimation and VGGT for multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it may have limitations on domain-specific images and its performance can vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity. The model is available for use through the Hugging Face Hub and can be accessed via a Python API or command line interface for inference and export in various formats such as glb, npz, ply, mini_npz, gs_ply, and gs_video.

instruction: How does the surface-to-volume ratio impact the resin required for this model?
description: MiniRes is a small Python library and pretrained ensemble model designed to predict resin usage in grams for pre-supported 3D printed miniatures. It accepts tabular features exported from slicing and analysis tools such as UVtools, PrusaSlicer, and trimesh, and returns a single float per part representing the estimated resin usage. The model combines a Keras neural network with an XGBoost regressor. Model weights are hosted on Hugging Face and automatically downloaded and cached. The training data was generated from STL files provided by multiple miniature artists, which were sliced using PrusaSlicer with a consistent profile and a resin density of 1.1/g. UVtools was used to extract resin usage statistics, including the label of grams used, while trimesh extracted mesh-level features such as volume, surface area, implied mass, Euler characteristic, and bounding box sizes. Feature engineering added interaction and ratio features such as volume × mass, surface/volume, surface/mass, bounding-box/volume, and similar. MiniRes is trained on this tabular data to predict resin usage based on these engineered features. The model expects a fixed set of feature columns used during training, which can be inspected at runtime. Required features include kb, volume, surface_area, bbox_area, euler_number, scale, and surface_volume_ratio. The model raises an error if any required columns are missing. MiniRes includes two command-line scripts: stl-scanner.py, which scans STL files and exports geometric features to a CSV using trimesh, and mini-pricing.py, which calculates the total cost of each file based on resin usage and user-defined cost parameters. The model is distributed under the MIT License, which permits use, modification, and distribution of the software without restriction, provided the copyright and permission notices are included. The software is provided 'as is', without warranty of any kind, and the authors or copyright holders are not liable for any damages arising from its use.

instruction: Could you extract the text from this Russian voice memo and normalize it for clarity?
description: GigaAM-v3 is a Conformer-based foundation model with 220–240M parameters, pretrained on diverse Russian speech data using the HuBERT-CTC objective. It is the third generation of the GigaAM family and provides state-of-the-art performance on Russian ASR across a wide range of domains. GigaAM-v3 includes the following model variants: `ssl` — self-supervised HuBERT–CTC encoder pre-trained on 700,000 hours of Russian speech; `ctc` — ASR model fine-tuned with a CTC decoder; `rnnt` — ASR model fine-tuned with an RNN-T decoder; `e2e_ctc` — end-to-end CTC model with punctuation and text normalization; and `e2e_rnnt` — end-to-end RNN-T model with punctuation and text normalization. GigaAM-v3 training incorporates new internal datasets: callcenter conversations, speech with background music, natural speech, and speech with atypical characteristics. The models perform on average 30% better on these new domains, while maintaining the same quality as previous GigaAM generations on public benchmarks. The Word Error Rate (%) for GigaAM-v3 and other existing models over diverse domains is as follows: Open Datasets — V3_CTC 3.0, V3_RNNT 2.6, T-One + LM 5.7, Whisper 12.0; Golos Farfield — V3_CTC 4.5, V3_RNNT 3.9, T-One + LM 12.2, Whisper 16.7; Natural Speech — V3_CTC 7.8, V3_RNNT 6.9, T-One + LM 14.5, Whisper 13.6; Disordered Speech — V3_CTC 20.6, V3_RNNT 19.2, T-One + LM 51.0, Whisper 59.3; Callcenter — V3_CTC 10.3, V3_RNNT 9.5, T-One + LM 13.5, Whisper 23.9; the average Word Error Rate is 9.2 for V3_CTC and 8.4 for V3_RNNT, compared to 19.4 for T-One + LM and 25.1 for Whisper. The end-to-end ASR models (`e2e_ctc` and `e2e_rnnt`) produce punctuated, normalized text directly. In end-to-end ASR comparisons of `e2e_ctc` and `e2e_rnnt` against Whisper-large-v3, using Gemini 2.5 Pro as an LLM-as-a-judge, GigaAM-v3 models win by an average margin of 70:30.

instruction: How do I segment the part of the image that matches the phrase 'a man wearing a blue jacket standing on the street'?
description: OneRef is a unified one-tower model for expression grounding and segmentation, introduced in the paper 'OneRef: Unified One-tower Expression Grounding and Segmentation with Mask Referring Modeling' presented at NeurIPS 2024. The model is developed by Linhui Xiao, Xiaoshan Yang, Fang Peng, Yaowei Wang, and Changsheng Xu. OneRef introduces a novel paradigm called Mask Referring Modeling (MRefM), which effectively models the referential relationship between visual and language modalities. Unlike previous approaches, OneRef uses a minimalist one-tower framework that unifies visual and linguistic feature spaces, eliminating the need for modality interaction modules, fusion en-/decoders, and special grounding tokens. The model is validated across three referring tasks on five datasets, achieving state-of-the-art (SoTA) performance. OneRef supports both referring expression comprehension (REC) and referring expression segmentation (RES) tasks. The model is implemented in PyTorch and is compatible with Python 3.9.10 and PyTorch 2.0.1. It has been tested on multiple PyTorch versions and is easy to deploy in various environments. The model is trained using a referring-aware dynamic image masking strategy, which focuses on the referred region rather than using fixed ratios or random masking. The model is available in both Base and Large variants, with pre-trained checkpoints provided for single-dataset fine-tuning and dataset-mixed grounding pre-training settings. The MRefM pre-training for REC is conducted using a mixture of RefCOCO/+/g and ReferIt datasets, while for RES it is conducted using RefCOCO/+/g datasets. The model is built upon the BEiT-3 architecture and includes Sentencepiece tokenizer and VQKD model for pre-training. The model has been shown to achieve significant performance improvements in mixed pre-training settings, although the performance gains in single-dataset settings are less pronounced. The model is available for download from HuggingFace and Google Drive, with detailed training and evaluation scripts provided. The model has been tested on datasets such as RefCOCO, RefCOCO+, RefCOCOg, ReferItGame, and Flickr30K. The model's performance is evaluated using standard metrics such as mIoU and oIoU for segmentation tasks and standard accuracy metrics for comprehension tasks. The model is noted for its energy efficiency and computational cost advantages compared to existing approaches. The model's architecture and training methodology are described in detail in the paper, with visualizations and qualitative results provided for various datasets.

instruction: Design a picture of a dragon soaring over a misty valley with ancient ruins below.
description: HunyuanImage-3.0 is a native multimodal model that unifies multimodal understanding and generation within an autoregressive framework. It is designed for image generation and supports tasks such as text-to-image generation, prompt self-rewrite, and multi-turn interaction. The model employs a unified autoregressive architecture, moving beyond traditional DiT-based designs, and is the largest open-source image generation Mixture of Experts (MoE) model to date, with 64 experts and a total of 80 billion parameters, of which 13 billion are activated per token. It demonstrates superior image generation performance, achieving a balance between semantic accuracy and visual quality through advanced dataset curation and reinforcement learning post-training. The model also exhibits intelligent world-knowledge reasoning, allowing it to interpret user intent and elaborate on sparse prompts to produce detailed and contextually appropriate visual outputs. Evaluation results include structured semantic alignment scores (SSAE) and human evaluations (GSB), with HunyuanImage-3.0 showing strong performance in both. The model requires a Linux operating system, NVIDIA GPU with CUDA support, and at least 3×80GB GPU memory for optimal performance. It is available for download and use with specific dependencies and installation instructions provided. Performance optimizations such as FlashAttention and FlashInfer can significantly speed up inference. The model is currently available in a pretrain checkpoint and an Instruct checkpoint, with the latter supporting prompt rewriting and reasoning. Users are advised to consult the official documentation for best practices in prompt writing and system configuration.

instruction: Describe the key points from this audio recording of a scientific lecture.
description: Gemma 3n is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models developed by Google, built using the same research and technology as the Gemini models. These models are designed for efficient execution on low-resource devices and are capable of handling multimodal input, including text, image, video, and audio, to generate text outputs. They are available in pre-trained and instruction-tuned variants and were trained on data in over 140 spoken languages. Gemma 3n models use selective parameter activation technology to reduce resource requirements, allowing them to operate at an effective size of 2B and 4B parameters, which is lower than the total number of parameters they contain. The raw parameter count of the model is 8B, but the architecture design allows the model to be run with a memory footprint comparable to a traditional 4B model by offloading low-utilization matrices from the accelerator. The models use a MatFormer architecture that allows nesting sub-models within the E4B model. One sub-model is provided, or a spectrum of custom-sized models can be accessed. The models support a total input context of 32K tokens and can generate outputs up to 32K tokens, subtracting the request input tokens. Input images are normalized to 256x256, 512x512, or 768x768 resolution and encoded to 256 tokens each. Audio data is encoded to 6.25 tokens per second from a single channel. The models were trained on a dataset totaling approximately 11 trillion tokens, with a knowledge cutoff date of June 2024. The training data includes web documents, code, mathematics, images, and audio. Rigorous filtering methods were applied to the training data, including CSAM filtering, sensitive data filtering, and additional methods based on content quality and safety. The models were trained using TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e hardware. Training was done using JAX and ML Pathways, which allow for efficient training of large models. The models were evaluated at full precision (float32) against a large collection of different datasets and metrics to cover different aspects of content generation. Evaluation results show performance improvements over previous Gemma models in categories such as child safety, content safety, and representational harms. The models have certain limitations, including potential biases or gaps in the training data, challenges with open-ended or highly complex tasks, difficulties with language ambiguity and nuance, and the possibility of generating incorrect or outdated factual statements. Ethical considerations include the risk of perpetuating biases, generating harmful content, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. Developers are encouraged to implement appropriate content safety safeguards and adhere to privacy regulations. The models are intended for a wide range of applications, including content creation, research, and education. They provide superior performance compared to other, similarly sized open model alternatives.

instruction: Could you summarize the key events in this extended video?
description: The SAGE-MM-Qwen3-VL-4B-SFT_RL from allenai is a 4B-parameter reinforcement learning (RL) post-trained vision-language model, further refined from the SAGE-MM-Qwen3-VL-4B-SFT base via RL on top of Qwen/Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct. It serves as the core decision-maker in the SAGE (Smart Any-Horizon Agent) system for long video reasoning with improved performance, achieving a +4.1% improvement over SFT according to experiments. The model operates in two stages: Stage-1 analyzes initial frames and metadata to classify queries as single-turn or multi-turn, while Stage-2 iteratively invokes JSON-formatted tools such as web-search, transcribe-speech (ASR), ground-event (temporal localization), extract-video-parts (frames/subclips), and analyze for dynamic context updates until resolution. It is designed for extended video Q&A beyond fixed horizons, handling sports, narratives, and events. The model requires the SAGE GitHub runtime for tool parsing and execution and outputs action strings for robust, any-length video understanding. It is available under the Apache 2.0 license for research and educational use per Ai2 guidelines, with GGUF quantizations available.

instruction: Create a detailed image of a cat and a dog playing together in a sunlit backyard with a garden in the background.
description: Qwen-Image-2512 is the December update of Qwen-Image’s text-to-image foundational model. It is designed to generate high-quality images from text prompts, with a focus on enhancing realism, detail, and text integration. Compared to the base Qwen-Image model released in August, Qwen-Image-2512 features enhanced human realism, finer natural detail, and improved text rendering. The model significantly reduces the “AI-generated” look and substantially enhances overall image realism, especially for human subjects. It delivers more detailed rendering of landscapes, animal fur, and other natural elements. The model also improves the accuracy and quality of textual elements, achieving better layout and more faithful multimodal (text + image) composition. Over 10,000 rounds of blind model evaluations have been conducted, and the results show that Qwen-Image-2512 is currently the strongest open-source model and remains highly competitive among closed-source models. The model supports a variety of tasks, including generating images with specific aspect ratios, rendering detailed human portraits, landscapes, and complex infographics. It is capable of accurately capturing age cues, facial expressions, and environmental context, as well as rendering fine details such as hair strands, fur, and water flow. The model also supports the generation of text-rich images, including PPT slides, infographics, and educational posters, with improved accuracy, layout, and multimodal integration. The model is implemented using the diffusers library and can be loaded and used with Python code. It is available for use on platforms such as Hugging Face and ModelScope. The model is based on a diffusion pipeline and can be run on CUDA-enabled devices for faster inference. The model is suitable for a wide range of applications, including but not limited to, content creation, design, and educational materials. However, the model may still have limitations in certain scenarios, and users are encouraged to test and evaluate its performance for their specific use cases.

instruction: Can you help me create a low-poly 3D version of this robot from a single photo?
description: Stable Fast 3D (SF3D) is a large reconstruction model that takes a single image of an object and generates a textured UV-unwrapped 3D mesh asset. The model is a Transformer-based image-to-3D model designed to create a 3D model from a single image in under one second. The output asset is UV-unwrapped, textured, and has a relatively low polygon count. The model performs a delighting step, which facilitates easier use of the asset in downstream applications such as game engines or rendering work. It also predicts per-object material parameters, including roughness and metallic, which enhances reflective behaviors during rendering. The model expects an input size of 512x512 pixels. The model is trained using renders from a dataset available under the Open Data Commons Attribution License. An enhanced rendering method is used, which more closely replicates the distribution of images found in the real world, significantly improving the model's ability to generalize. Objects are filtered based on the review of licenses, and a subset is curated to meet training needs. Intended uses include the generation of artworks and use in design and other artistic processes, applications in educational or creative tools, and research on reconstruction models, including understanding the limitations of these models. The model was not trained to produce factual or true representations of people or events, and generating such content is out of scope for the model's capabilities. Individuals or organizations generating annual revenue of US $1,000,000 (or local currency equivalent) or more must obtain an enterprise commercial license directly from Stability AI before commercially using SF3D or any derivative work of SF3D or its outputs. Community License allows free use for research, non-commercial, and commercial use by organizations and individuals generating annual revenue of US $1M or less. Developers are encouraged to conduct their own testing and apply additional mitigations based on their specific use cases, as the model includes safety measures intended to reduce the risk of severe harms.
