instruction: What images are most similar to the text 'a vintage car parked on a rainy street'?
description: The CLIP model was developed by researchers at OpenAI to study what contributes to robustness in computer vision tasks and to test the ability of models to generalize to arbitrary image classification tasks in a zero-shot manner. It was not developed for general model deployment, and researchers are advised to carefully study its capabilities in relation to the specific context of deployment. The model was developed in January 2021 and uses a ViT-B/32 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The original implementation had two variants: one using a ResNet image encoder and the other using a Vision Transformer. This repository contains the variant with the Vision Transformer. The model is intended for research use by AI researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. Any deployed use case of the model, whether commercial or not, is currently out of scope. Non-deployed use cases such as image search in a constrained environment are also not recommended unless there is thorough in-domain testing of the model with a specific, fixed class taxonomy. This is because the model's performance varies significantly with different class taxonomies, and untested deployment may be harmful. Use cases related to surveillance and facial recognition are always out of scope, regardless of model performance, due to the lack of testing norms and checks to ensure fair use. The model has not been purposefully trained or evaluated on any languages other than English, so its use should be limited to English language use cases. The model was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including data crawled from the internet and pre-existing image datasets. The data is more representative of people and societies most connected to the internet, which tend to skew towards more developed nations and younger, male users. The goal of the dataset was to test robustness and generalizability in computer vision tasks, and the data was gathered in a mostly non-interventionist manner, with crawling limited to websites that had policies against excessively violent and adult images. The dataset is not intended for use as the basis for any commercial or deployed model and will not be released. The model has been evaluated on a wide range of benchmarks across various computer vision datasets, including Food101, CIFAR10, CIFAR100, Birdsnap, SUN397, Stanford Cars, FGVC Aircraft, VOC2007, DTD, Oxford-IIIT Pet dataset, Caltech101, Flowers102, MNIST, SVHN, IIIT5K, Hateful Memes, SST-2, UCF101, Kinetics700, Country211, CLEVR Counting, KITTI Distance, STL-10, RareAct, Flickr30, MSCOCO, ImageNet, ImageNet-A, ImageNet-R, ImageNet Sketch, ObjectNet (ImageNet Overlap), Youtube-BB, and ImageNet-Vid. The model has several limitations, including struggles with fine-grained classification and counting objects. It also exhibits issues with fairness and bias, with performance and biases depending significantly on class design and category choices. Testing revealed significant disparities with respect to race and gender when classifying images of people into crime-related and non-human animal categories, with disparities shifting based on class construction. Evaluations on the Fairface dataset showed accuracy greater than 96% across all races for gender classification, with 'Middle Eastern' having the highest accuracy (98.4%) and 'White' the lowest (96.5%). CLIP averaged approximately 93% for racial classification and approximately 63% for age classification. These evaluations are used to assess model performance across demographics and surface potential risks, not to endorse such tasks. The approach to testing CLIP also has a limitation: in many cases, linear probes were used to evaluate performance, and there is evidence that linear probes can underestimate model performance.

instruction: Is there a way to add more points to an existing mask for better precision?
description: EdgeTAM is an on-device executable variant of the SAM 2 model designed for promptable segmentation and tracking in videos. It runs 22× faster than SAM 2 and achieves 16 FPS on iPhone 15 Pro Max without quantization. EdgeTAM supports automatic mask generation to segment all objects in an image. It can be used for basic image segmentation through single point clicks, multiple points for refinement, and bounding box inputs. The model allows for segmentation of multiple objects simultaneously. It supports batch inference for multiple images and multiple objects per image, including complex batch scenarios with multiple points per object. EdgeTAM can use masks from previous predictions as input to refine segmentation. A key strength of EdgeTAM is its ability to track objects across video frames. It supports basic video tracking, multi-object video tracking, and refining video segmentation through additional clicks on any frame. For real-time applications, EdgeTAM supports streaming video inference, processing video frames as they arrive. It also supports video batch processing for multiple objects, allowing multiple objects to be tracked simultaneously in video by adding them all at once.

instruction: What makes the approach effective for recognizing objects in previously unseen contexts?
description: The VisionReasoner-7B model is part of the Seg-Zero framework, which is designed to demonstrate generalizability and derive explicit chain-of-thought reasoning through cognitive reinforcement for reasoning segmentation. The VisionReasoner-7B model uses a decoupled architecture that includes a reasoning model and a segmentation model. The reasoning model interprets user intentions, generates explicit reasoning chains, and produces positional prompts, which are then used by the segmentation model to generate precise pixel-level masks. The model is trained exclusively through reinforcement learning with GRPO and without the use of explicit reasoning data. This training approach enables Seg-Zero to achieve robust zero-shot generalization and to exhibit emergent test-time reasoning capabilities. Experimental results show that Seg-Zero-7B achieves a zero-shot performance of 57.5 on the ReasonSeg benchmark, which is an 18% improvement over the prior LISA-7B model. This improvement highlights the framework's ability to generalize across domains while presenting an explicit reasoning process.

instruction: What is the most probable movement pattern of the cyclist to my right?
description: HERMES is a unified Driving World Model designed for simultaneous 3D scene understanding and generation in autonomous driving scenarios. It integrates scene understanding, which involves interpreting and reasoning about the driving environment, with future scene evolution (generation). HERMES uses a Bird's-Eye View (BEV) representation to consolidate multi-view spatial information while preserving geometric relationships and interactions. The model introduces world queries that incorporate world knowledge into BEV features through causal attention in a Large Language Model (LLM), enabling contextual enrichment for both understanding and generation tasks. HERMES was evaluated on the nuScenes and OmniDrive-nuScenes datasets, achieving state-of-the-art performance with a 32.4% reduction in generation error and an 8.0% improvement in understanding metrics such as CIDEr. The model is based on prior works including BEVFormer v2, InternVL, UniPAD, OmniDrive, and DriveMonkey. HERMES is open-sourced, with code, pretrained weights, and processed data available. A demo has also been released.

instruction: How many objects in the image are located inside the large box shown?
description: SpaceThinker-Qwen2.5VL-3B is a multimodal vision-language model (VLM) designed to enhance spatial reasoning through test-time compute. It is a fine-tuned version of UCSC-VLAA/VLAA-Thinker-Qwen2.5VL-3B, using synthetic reasoning traces generated by a 3D scene reconstruction pipeline involving Molmo, VGGT, and SAM2. The model has 3.75B parameters in FP16 format and is based on the Qwen2.5-VL-3B architecture. It was fine-tuned using LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) with a rank of 128 and an alpha of 256, targeting modules q_proj and v_proj. The training used AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of 2e-5, a batch size of 1, and 3 epochs. The model is trained on a dataset of over 12K synthetic spatial reasoning traces, covering question types such as distances (with units), spatial relations (e.g., left-of, above, inside), and object properties. It is designed to support tasks involving spatial reasoning, including distance estimation, object relations, and scene interpretation. The model demonstrates strong performance on benchmarks such as SpatialScore, SpaCE-10, and QSpatial++, particularly in the SpatialScore-Hard split, where it achieves an overall score of 19.47. It also performs well in the SpaCE-10 benchmark, achieving an overall score of 32.72. On the QSpatial++ benchmark, it achieves a success rate of 55% and a sample completion rate of 99 out of 100. The model is intended for applications such as robotics and drones, where planning and navigation in 3D space are required. However, it has limitations, including potential performance degradation in cluttered environments or under certain camera perspectives, and it was trained on synthetic data over an internet image dataset. Multimodal biases from the base model may persist, and the model is not intended for safety-critical or legal decision-making. Users are advised to evaluate outputs critically and consider domain-specific fine-tuning for improved safety and performance. The model is licensed under Apache-2.0.

instruction: Can you convert a voice message into text for me to read later?
description: LFM2.5-Audio-1.5B is an updated end-to-end audio foundation model. Key improvements include a custom, LFM-based audio detokenizer, `llama.cpp` compatible GGUFs for CPU inference, and better ASR and TTS performance. LFM2.5-Audio is an end-to-end multimodal speech and text language model, and as such does not require separate ASR and TTS components. Designed with low latency and real-time conversation in mind, at only 1.5 billion parameters LFM2.5-Audio enables seamless conversational interaction, achieving capabilities on par with much larger models. Our model consists of a pretrained LFM2.5 model as its multimodal backbone, along with a FastConformer-based audio encoder to handle continuous audio inputs, and an RQ-transformer generating discrete tokens coupled with a lightweight audio detokenizer for audio output. LFM2.5-Audio supports two distinct generation routines, each suitable for a set of tasks. Interleaved generation enables real-time speech-to-speech conversational chatbot capabilities, where audio generation latency is key. Sequential generation is suited for non-conversational tasks such as ASR or TTS, and allows the model to switch generated modality on the fly. The model has 1.2 billion parameters for the language model only, an audio encoder with 115 million parameters, a hybrid convolution and attention-based backbone, an audio detokenizer compatible with 8 codebooks, a context length of 32,768 tokens, a vocabulary size of 65,536 for text and 2049*8 for audio, and uses bfloat16 precision. The model is licensed under the LFM Open License v1.0 and supports English. Performance on VoiceBench (audio input) includes scores of 3.76 on AlpacaEval, 3.53 on CommonEval, 3.15 on WildVoice, 30.92 on SD-QA, 33.04 on MMSU, 44.40 on OBQA, 49.54 on BBH, 28.48 on IFEval, 99.04 on Overall. For ASR, the model has a word error rate (WER) average of 7.53, with 15.63 on AMI, 14.56 on Earnings22, 10.47 on GigaSpeech, 1.95 on LibriSpeech-clean, 4.30 on LibriSpeech-other, 2.76 on SPGISpeech, 3.47 on TED-LIUM, and 7.13 on VoxPopuli. The model is intended for use with the `liquid-audio` package, which provides a lower-level interface for custom use cases, including multi-turn, multi-modal chat. The model is suitable for tasks such as multi-turn chat, ASR, and TTS. The model has some caveats, including that it is currently limited to English and that performance may vary depending on the specific use case and deployment environment.

instruction: I need a detailed breakdown of the visual tokens from this high-resolution landscape image.
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. The models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones that provide multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers on the class token, or a linear layer on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) consists of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) consists of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, with the teacher being a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, image retrieval, 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification. Evaluation results show that the ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on web data achieve high scores on IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair. The ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web data also achieve strong performance on similar tasks. The ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on satellite data achieve high scores on GEO-Bench classification and segmentation tasks. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters), ViT-S+ (29M parameters), ViT-B (86M parameters), ViT-L (300M parameters), ViT-H+ (840M parameters), and ViT-7B (6716M parameters), all with a patch size of 16 and varying embedding dimensions, register tokens, heads, and FFN types. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The models were trained using Nvidia H100 GPUs on private infrastructure in the USA, with 61,440 hours of compute time and an estimated carbon emission of 18t CO2eq. The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: What are the most relevant medical concepts associated with this image?
description: DermLIP is a vision-language model for dermatology, trained on the Derm1M dataset, which is the largest dermatological image-text corpus to date. The model variant PanDerm-base-w-PubMed-256 uses domain-specific pretraining to achieve superior performance compared to other DermLIP variants. It is a pretrained vision-language model with a CLIP-style architecture. The vision encoder is PanDerm-base, and the text encoder is PubMedBert-256. The model processes images at a resolution of 224×224 pixels. It was trained on 403,563 skin image-text pairs from the Derm1M dataset, which includes both dermoscopic and clinical images. The training objective was to minimize image-text contrastive loss, and the model was trained using an Nvidia H200 GPU with approximately 90GB of memory over a period of about 9.5 hours. The intended uses of the model include zero-shot classification, few-shot learning, cross-modal retrieval, and concept annotation or explanation. The model is distributed under the cc-by-nc-nd-4.0 license.

instruction: What are the top documents that explain the evolution of the internet over the past 30 years?
description: The mxbai-edge-colbert-v0-32m is a lightweight ColBERT model with 32 million parameters and a projection dimension of 64. It is built on top of ModernBERT, benefiting from its architectural efficiencies. Despite its small size, it is the best-performing 'edge-sized' retriever, outperforming ColBERTv2 and many models with over ten times more parameters. The model can create multi-vector representations for documents of up to 32,000 tokens and is fully compatible with the PyLate library. It is designed for end-to-end retrieval tasks and supports encoding documents and queries, indexing, and retrieving top-k matches. It is also well-suited for use as a re-ranker following a first-stage retrieval with more lightweight models. Evaluation results on the BEIR benchmark show that it achieves an average score of 0.521, with strong performance on tasks such as MS MARCO (0.450), SciFact (0.740), and TREC-COVID (0.775). On the LongEmbed benchmark, it achieves an average score of 0.849 for documents up to 32,000 tokens. The model is available under the Apache 2.0 license. If used, it should be cited using the associated tech report, and if its projection heads are specifically discussed, an additional report on projection variants should also be cited. Additionally, if PyLate is used in conjunction with the model, it should be cited separately.

instruction: Can you analyze a Japanese text for linguistic acceptability and flag any unnatural expressions?
description: ModernBERT-Ja-310M is a variant of the BERT model that combines local and global attention mechanisms, enabling it to handle long sequences while maintaining high computational efficiency. It incorporates modern architectural improvements. The model is trained on a high-quality corpus of Japanese and English text comprising 4.09T tokens, with a vocabulary size of 102,400 and a sequence length of 8,192 tokens. It is designed for masked language modeling and is intended to be fine-tuned for downstream tasks. It is not suitable for text generation, and users are advised to use a dedicated text generation model for such purposes. The model uses a unigram language model tokenizer with byte fallback, and does not apply pre-tokenization for Japanese, allowing raw sentences to be input directly. However, the token boundaries often do not align with morpheme boundaries, which may lead to poor performance in token classification tasks such as named entity recognition and span extraction. The model was constructed through a three-stage training process: pre-training with 3.51T tokens using a sequence length of 1,024 and a masking rate of 30%, followed by two phases of context length extension. Phase 1 used 430B tokens with a sequence length of 8,192 and a masking rate of 30%, and Phase 2 used 150B tokens with a sequence length of 8,192 and a reduced masking rate of 15%. The model's configuration includes a vocabulary size of 102,400, a head dimension of 64, and an activation function of GELU. The attention mechanism consists of one global attention layer and two local attention layers, with a sliding window attention window context size of 128. The global_rope_theta is set to 160,000 and the local_rope_theta to 10,000. The model was evaluated on 12 datasets, including JGLUE, across various tasks such as knowledge-based tasks, Japanese linguistic acceptability classification, natural language inference, semantic textual similarity, and various classification tasks. The evaluation used a maximum sequence length not exceeding 512, with settings aligned to existing models. Hyperparameters such as learning rate and number of epochs were explored, and the model was fine-tuned with a classification head consisting of a linear layer, a GELU activation function, and another linear layer. The embedding of the special token at the beginning of the sentence was used for classification, as the model does not perform next sentence prediction during pretraining. Evaluations were conducted using 5-fold cross-validation, with optimal hyperparameters determined based on validation set performance and final scores reported on test sets. For datasets without predefined splits, 10% of the data was set aside as a test set, and for datasets with only train and validation sets, the validation set was treated as the test set. The model achieved state-of-the-art performance across evaluation tasks, even when compared with much larger models. Despite being a long-context model capable of processing sequences of up to 8,192 tokens, it also exhibited strong performance in short-sequence evaluations. The model may produce representations that reflect biases, and when used for masked language modeling, it may generate biased or harmful expressions.

instruction: Describe the image and the audio as if you’re narrating a scene for someone who can’t see or hear it.
description: Phi-4-multimodal-instruct is a lightweight open multimodal foundation model that leverages the language, vision, and speech research and datasets used for Phi-3.5 and 4.0 models. The model processes text, image, and audio inputs, generating text outputs, and comes with 128K token context length. The model underwent an enhancement process, incorporating both supervised fine-tuning, direct preference optimization, and RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) to support precise instruction adherence and safety measures. The languages that each modal supports are the following: Text: Arabic, Chinese, Czech, Danish, Dutch, English, Finnish, French, German, Hebrew, Hungarian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Norwegian, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Spanish, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian; Vision: English; Audio: English, Chinese, German, French, Italian, Japanese, Spanish, Portuguese. The model is intended for broad multilingual and multimodal commercial and research use. The model provides uses for general purpose AI systems and applications which require memory/compute constrained environments, latency bound scenarios, strong reasoning (especially math and logic), function and tool calling, general image understanding, optical character recognition, chart and table understanding, multiple image comparison, multi-image or video clip summarization, speech recognition, speech translation, speech QA, and audio understanding. The model is designed to accelerate research on language and multimodal models, for use as a building block for generative AI powered features. The model is not specifically designed or evaluated for all downstream purposes. Developers should consider common limitations of language models and multimodal models, as well as performance difference across languages, as they select use cases, and evaluate and mitigate for accuracy, safety, and fairness before using within a specific downstream use case, particularly for high-risk scenarios. Developers should be aware of and adhere to applicable laws or regulations (including but not limited to privacy, trade compliance laws, etc.) that are relevant to their use case. This release of Phi-4-multimodal-instruct is based on valuable user feedback from the Phi-3 series. Previously, users could use a speech recognition model to talk to the Mini and Vision models. To achieve this, users needed to use a pipeline of two models: one model to transcribe the audio to text, and another model for the language or vision tasks. This pipeline means that the core model was not provided the full breadth of input information – e.g. cannot directly observe multiple speakers, background noises, jointly align speech, vision, language information at the same time on the same representation space. With Phi-4-multimodal-instruct, a single new open model has been trained across text, vision, and audio, meaning that all inputs and outputs are processed by the same neural network. The model employed new architecture, larger vocabulary for efficiency, multilingual, and multimodal support, and better post-training techniques were used for instruction following and function calling, as well as additional data leading to substantial gains on key multimodal capabilities. It is anticipated that Phi-4-multimodal-instruct will greatly benefit app developers and various use cases. The enthusiastic support for the Phi-4 series is greatly appreciated. Feedback on Phi-4 is welcomed and crucial to the model's evolution and improvement. The Phi-4-multimodal-instruct was observed as having strong automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech translation (ST) performance, surpassing expert ASR model WhisperV3 and ST models SeamlessM4T-v2-Large. It ranked number 1 on the leaderboard with word error rate 6.14% in comparison with the current best model 6.5% as of March 04, 2025. It is the first open-sourced model that can perform speech summarization, and the performance is close to GPT4o. It has a gap with close models, e.g. Gemini-1.5-Flash and GPT-4o-realtime-preview, on speech QA task. Work is being undertaken to improve this capability in the next iterations. Phi-4-multimodal-instruct is capable of processing both image and audio together, and achieves much stronger performance on multiple benchmarks compared to other existing state-of-the-art omni models that can enable audio and visual signal as input. The model has 5.6B parameters and is a multimodal transformer model. The model has the pretrained Phi-4-Mini-Instruct as the backbone language model, and the advanced encoders and adapters of vision and speech. The model's inputs are text, image, and audio. It is best suited for prompts using the chat format. The model's context length is 128K tokens. The model was trained on 512 A100-80G GPUs for 28 days. The training data includes 5 trillion text tokens, 2.3M speech hours, and 1.1T image-text tokens. The model's outputs are generated text in response to the input. The model was trained between December 2024 and January 2025. The model is a static model trained on offline datasets with the cutoff date of June 2024 for publicly available data. The model supports the following languages: Text: Arabic, Chinese, Czech, Danish, Dutch, English, Finnish, French, German, Hebrew, Hungarian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Norwegian, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Spanish, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian; Vision: English; Audio: English, Chinese, German, French, Italian, Japanese, Spanish, Portuguese. The model's release date is February 2025. The model's training data includes a wide variety of sources, totaling 5 trillion text tokens, and is a combination of publicly available documents filtered for quality, selected high-quality educational data, and code; newly created synthetic, “textbook-like” data for the purpose of teaching math, coding, common sense reasoning, general knowledge of the world (e.g., science, daily activities, theory of mind, etc.); high quality human labeled data in chat format; selected high-quality image-text interleave data; synthetic and publicly available image, multi-image, and video data; anonymized in-house speech-text pair data with strong/weak transcriptions; selected high-quality publicly available and anonymized in-house speech data with task-specific supervisions; selected synthetic speech data; and synthetic vision-speech data. Focus was placed on the quality of data that could potentially improve the reasoning ability for the model, and the publicly available documents were filtered to contain a preferred level of knowledge. As an example, the result of a game in premier league on a particular day might be good training data for large foundation models, but such information was removed for the Phi-4-multimodal-instruct to leave more model capacity for reasoning for the model's small size. The data collection process involved sourcing information from publicly available documents, with a focus on filtering out undesirable documents and images. To safeguard privacy, image and text data sources were filtered to remove or scrub potentially personal data from the training data. The decontamination process involved normalizing and tokenizing the dataset, then generating and comparing n-grams between the target dataset and benchmark datasets. Samples with matching n-grams above a threshold were flagged as contaminated and removed from the dataset. A detailed contamination report was generated, summarizing the matched text, matching ratio, and filtered results for further analysis. Like other language models, the Phi family of models can potentially behave in ways that are unfair, unreliable, or offensive. Some of the limiting behaviors to be aware of include: Quality of Service: The Phi models are trained primarily on English language content across text, speech, and visual inputs, with some additional multilingual coverage. Performance may vary significantly across different modalities and languages: Text: Languages other than English will experience reduced performance, with varying levels of degradation across different non-English languages. English language varieties with less representation in the training data may perform worse than standard American English. Speech: Speech recognition and processing shows similar language-based performance patterns, with optimal performance for standard American English accents and pronunciations. Other English accents, dialects, and non-English languages may experience lower recognition accuracy and response quality. Background noise, audio quality, and speaking speed can further impact performance. Vision: Visual processing capabilities may be influenced by cultural and geographical biases in the training data. The model may show reduced performance when analyzing images containing text in non-English languages or visual elements more commonly found in non-Western contexts. Image quality, lighting conditions, and composition can also affect processing accuracy. Multilingual performance and safety gaps: We believe it is important to make language models more widely available across different languages, but the Phi 4 models still exhibit challenges common across multilingual releases. As with any deployment of LLMs, developers will be better positioned to test for performance or safety gaps for their linguistic and cultural context and customize the model with additional fine-tuning and appropriate safeguards. Representation of Harms & Perpetuation of Stereotypes: These models can over- or under-represent groups of people, erase representation of some groups, or reinforce demeaning or negative stereotypes. Despite safety post-training, these limitations may still be present due to differing levels of representation of different groups, cultural contexts, or prevalence of examples of negative stereotypes in training data that reflect real-world patterns and societal biases. Inappropriate or Offensive Content: These models may produce other types of inappropriate or offensive content, which may make it inappropriate to deploy for sensitive contexts without additional mitigations that are specific to the case. Information Reliability: Language models can generate nonsensical content or fabricate content that might sound reasonable but is inaccurate or outdated. Limited Scope for Code: The majority of Phi 4 training data is based in Python and uses common packages such as

instruction: Please create a voice recording in Tamil for an IVR system that asks the caller to enter their account number.
description: svara-TTS is a multilingual text-to-speech (TTS) model designed for 19 languages, including 18 Indic languages and Indian English. It is built using an Orpheus-style discrete audio token approach, aiming to provide clarity, expressiveness, and low-latency performance on commodity GPUs and CPUs. The model supports lightweight emotion and style control through tags such as `` and `` and allows for simple speaker identities using the format `Language (Gender)`. It also enables zero-shot adaptation for new use cases. The model is trained on over 2000 hours of high-quality speech data from sources including SYSPIN, RASA, IndicTTS, and SPICOR, covering approximately 50 speakers across the 19 languages. The data was selected to support natural prosody, broad language coverage, and stable multilingual transfer. The model is intended for use in multilingual assistants, IVR systems, learning applications, reading aids, accessibility tools, and content localization for education, public information, and civic services. It is also suitable for research on Indic prosody, emotion control, and cross-lingual transfer. It is not intended for impersonation of individuals, deceptive content creation, or safety-critical deployments without human oversight. The model has limitations, including challenges with proper nouns and rare entities, very long sentences, varying emotion strength by language, and code-mixing that is not fully deterministic. These limitations can be improved with targeted LoRA finetuning and better preprocessing. Users are encouraged to follow applicable laws and ethical guidelines, avoid harmful uses such as impersonation or harassment, and disclose synthetic speech to end users where appropriate. The model is licensed under Apache-2.0 and is versioned as v1.0.0, marking its initial public release with support for 19 languages.

instruction: What are some effective strategies for studying for a standardized test like the SAT or GRE?
description: gpt-oss-120b-Distill-Phi-4-14B is a GRPO inference test model based on the microsoft/phi-4 (14B) base model. It is fine-tuned through a three-stage training pipeline—SFT (Cold Start) → RLHF (GRPO) → SFT—aiming to achieve excellent multi-step reasoning capabilities. The core methodology of this model is derived from three papers: DeepSeek-R1 (Nature, vol. 645), DeepSeekMath (arXiv:2402.03300v3), and Phi-4-reasoning (arXiv:2504.21318). Unlike the Microsoft paper, the reinforcement learning-guided reasoning part is moved to an earlier stage, using the SFT + GRPO + SFT method to generate explicit Chain-of-Thought (CoT), attempting to induce reasoning abilities in the Phi-4 Instruct model through reinforcement learning and supervised learning. This model uses a distilled dataset from the open-source model gpt-oss-120b as its fine-tuning knowledge base, thereby inheriting its profound reasoning style and knowledge system. The model's training pipeline includes three stages: Foundational Supervised Fine-Tuning (Cold Start), Reinforcement Learning with GRPO, and Post-RL SFT (Alignment and Capability Consolidation). The GRPO algorithm is an advanced RLHF algorithm that estimates the advantage function by comparing the relative merits of a group of candidate outputs, achieving more stable policy gradient updates. The reward system includes multiple weighted rewards and penalty signals to shape the model's behavior, covering core objective rewards, quality and alignment rewards, behavioral regularization and penalties, and exploration and diversity incentives. The model's performance includes the ability to perform logical reasoning, mathematical problem-solving, and role-playing debates, with demonstrated capabilities in general chat, knowledge Q&A, adversarial reasoning, and mathematical reasoning. Limitations include limited training data and resources, potential language mixing in reasoning processes, and reduced expressive richness in broader open-domain conversations due to domain-specific training. The model is intended as a reference test model and is not a fully optimized reasoning model.

instruction: How accurate is the depth estimation for small objects in this detailed image?
description: DA3 Nested is a model that combines the any-view Giant model with the metric Large model for metric-scale visual geometry reconstruction. It is the recommended model that integrates all available capabilities. The model belongs to the Nested series and contains 1.40B parameters. It is licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0, which restricts its use to non-commercial purposes. The model supports relative depth estimation, pose estimation, pose conditioning, 3D Gaussians, metric depth estimation, and sky segmentation. It is developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is based on a vision transformer architecture with a plain transformer backbone and a unified depth-ray representation. The model is trained exclusively on public academic datasets. It significantly outperforms Depth Anything 2 for monocular depth estimation and VGGT for multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it may have limitations when applied to domain-specific images, and its performance can vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity. The model is intended for non-commercial use only due to its license.

instruction: Can you extract the company names from this business proposal?
description: The bert-base-NER is a fine-tuned BERT model designed for Named Entity Recognition (NER) and achieves state-of-the-art performance for the NER task. It is trained to recognize four types of entities: location (LOC), organizations (ORG), person (PER), and Miscellaneous (MISC). Specifically, this model is a bert-base-cased model that was fine-tuned on the English version of the standard dataset. A larger BERT-large model fine-tuned on the same dataset is also available. The model can be used with the Transformers pipeline for NER. It is limited by its training dataset of entity-annotated news articles from a specific span of time, which may not generalize well for all use cases in different domains. Additionally, the model occasionally tags subword tokens as entities, and post-processing of results may be necessary to handle those cases. The model was fine-tuned on the English version of the standard dataset, which distinguishes between the beginning and continuation of an entity to allow the model to output where the second entity begins. Each token is classified as one of the following classes: O (Outside of a named entity), B-MISC (Beginning of a miscellaneous entity right after another miscellaneous entity), I-MISC (Miscellaneous entity), B-PER (Beginning of a person’s name right after another person’s name), I-PER (Person’s name), B-ORG (Beginning of an organization right after another organization), I-ORG (Organization), B-LOC (Beginning of a location right after another location), and I-LOC (Location). The training dataset was derived from the Reuters corpus, consisting of Reuters news stories. The dataset includes 7140 training examples for LOC, 3438 for MISC, 6321 for ORG, and 6600 for PER. The development set includes 1837 LOC, 922 MISC, 1341 ORG, and 1842 PER examples. The test set includes 1668 LOC, 702 MISC, 1661 ORG, and 1617 PER examples. The training dataset consists of 946 articles, 14,987 sentences, and 203,621 tokens. The development dataset includes 216 articles, 3,466 sentences, and 51,362 tokens. The test dataset includes 231 articles, 3,684 sentences, and 46,435 tokens. The model was trained on a single NVIDIA V100 GPU with recommended hyperparameters from the source that trained and evaluated the model on the CoNLL-2003 NER task. The model achieved an f1 score of 95.1 on the development set and 91.3 on the test set. The precision was 95.0 on the development set and 90.7 on the test set. The recall was 95.3 on the development set and 91.9 on the test set. The test metrics are slightly lower than the official Google BERT results, which encoded document context and experimented with CRF.

instruction: How can I convert a set of multilingual user comments into a uniform format for sentiment analysis?
description: The Qwen3 Embedding model series is the latest proprietary model of the Qwen family, specifically designed for text embedding and ranking tasks. Building upon the dense foundational models of the Qwen3 series, it provides a comprehensive range of text embeddings and reranking models in various sizes (0.6B, 4B, and 8B). This series inherits the exceptional multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills of its foundational model. The Qwen3 Embedding series represents significant advancements in multiple text embedding and ranking tasks, including text retrieval, code retrieval, text classification, text clustering, and bitext mining. The embedding model has achieved state-of-the-art performance across a wide range of downstream application evaluations. The 8B size embedding model ranks No.1 in the MTEB multilingual leaderboard (as of June 5, 2025, score 70.58), while the reranking model excels in various text retrieval scenarios. The Qwen3 Embedding series offers a full spectrum of sizes (from 0.6B to 8B) for both embedding and reranking models, catering to diverse use cases that prioritize efficiency and effectiveness. Developers can seamlessly combine these two modules. Additionally, the embedding model allows for flexible vector definitions across all dimensions, and both embedding and reranking models support user-defined instructions to enhance performance for specific tasks, languages, or scenarios. The Qwen3 Embedding series offers support for over 100 languages, thanks to the multilingual capabilities of Qwen3 models. This includes various programming languages, and provides robust multilingual, cross-lingual, and code retrieval capabilities. Qwen3-Embedding-8B is a text embedding model with 8B parameters, a context length of 32k, and an embedding dimension of up to 4096, supporting user-defined output dimensions ranging from 32 to 4096. Developers are advised to create tailored instructions specific to their tasks and scenarios, as using instructions typically yields an improvement of 1% to 5% compared to not using them. In multilingual contexts, it is also advised to write instructions in English, as most instructions used during the model training process were originally written in English.

instruction: What is the main idea conveyed in this infographic, and what data is presented?
description: Qwen3-VL is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced Thinking editions for flexible deployment. Key enhancements include the ability to operate PC and mobile GUIs by recognizing elements, understanding functions, invoking tools, and completing tasks. It can generate Draw.io, HTML, CSS, and JS from images and videos. The model supports advanced spatial perception, including judging object positions, viewpoints, and occlusions, with capabilities for 2D and 3D grounding. It handles long contexts and video understanding with a native 256K context length, expandable to 1M, enabling full recall and second-level indexing for books and hours-long video. The model excels in enhanced multimodal reasoning, particularly in STEM and Math, with causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. It features upgraded visual recognition with broader, higher-quality pretraining that can recognize a wide range of subjects, including celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. Expanded OCR supports 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare or ancient characters and jargon. The model achieves text understanding on par with pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE for full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings, enhancing long-horizon video reasoning. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. The model is designed for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library and ModelScope, with generation hyperparameters available for both visual and text tasks. The model is described in technical reports and preprints available on arXiv.

instruction: What are the best settings for running this on a system with an AMD GPU?
description: This model is a set of imatrix quantizations of the gemma-3n-E4B-it model by Google, using the llama.cpp release b5760 for quantization. The original model is quantized using the imatrix option with a dataset provided. The quantizations are intended to be run directly with llama.cpp or any other llama.cpp-based project. No chat template is specified, so a default prompt format is used, which may not be accurate and should be verified against the original model card. The quantizations vary in quality and file size, with options ranging from Q8_0 (7.35GB, extremely high quality) to IQ3_XS (3.17GB, lower quality). Some quantizations, such as Q3_K_XL and Q4_K_L, use Q8_0 for embed and output weights instead of the default quantization. The model supports online repacking for ARM and AVX CPU inference, particularly for the Q4_0 and IQ4_NL quantizations, which can improve performance on compatible hardware. The Q4_0 quantization has been deprecated in favor of the Q4_0 format with online repacking. The model can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool, with instructions provided for downloading specific files or multiple files if the model exceeds 50GB. Performance benchmarks on an AVX2 system (EPYC7702) show that the Q4_K_M quantization offers improved tokens per second compared to Q4_0 in most cases, while the Q4_0_8_8 quantization provides a performance boost for prompt processing and text generation. The choice of quantization depends on available hardware resources, with recommendations for maximizing speed or quality based on RAM and VRAM availability. I-quants (e.g., IQ3_M) are newer and offer better performance for their size on cuBLAS (Nvidia) or rocBLAS (AMD) systems, though they may be slower on CPU compared to K-quants (e.g., Q5_K_M). The model includes contributions from kalomaze and Dampf for the imatrix calibration dataset, ZeroWw for inspiration on embed/output quantization, and LM Studio for sponsorship.

instruction: Can you create a video where a young woman blows a bubblegum bubble that contains a tiny underwater scene with fish and plants?
description: This model is a controlnet module designed for the Wan2.2 model. It follows the same approach as the controlnet implementation for Wan2.2. The model is intended for use with ComfyUI and supports inference tasks for generating video content based on text prompts and control frames. The model architecture includes a custom controlnet module integrated with the Wan2.2-TI2V-5B-Diffusers base model. Inference is performed using a pipeline that incorporates a controlnet, a transformer, and an autoencoder. The model processes video frames with Gaussian blur and resizing to generate output video sequences. Performance is evaluated through inference examples, including a detailed prompt for generating a video of a young woman blowing a bubblegum bubble containing a miniature aquarium. The model includes parameters for guidance scale, controlnet weight, and frame resolution. The model is available on GitHub, and users are encouraged to raise issues directly in the repository. Professional support can be requested via email.

instruction: Is it possible to change the person’s accessories, like adding glasses or a necklace, while keeping the rest of the video intact?
description: Lucy Edit 1.1 Dev is an open-weight video editing model that performs instruction-guided edits on videos using free-text prompts. It supports a variety of edits, including clothing and accessory changes, character changes, object insertions, and scene replacements, while preserving the motion and composition of the video. Built on the Wan2.2 5B architecture, it inherits a high-compression VAE and DiT stack, making it easier to adapt existing scripts and workflows. The model preserves motion and composition perfectly, allowing for precise edits. It offers improved edit reliability compared to common inference time methods. It supports changing wardrobes and accessories, such as adding glasses, earrings, or hats. It can replace characters with monsters, animals, or known characters, for example, replacing a person with a polar bear. It can also transform scenes, such as turning a scene into a 2D cartoon. It uses pure text instructions without requiring finetuning or masks for common edits. It supports global edits to the background and style, performing scene-wide changes while preserving subjects. It provides seamless blending with cleaner boundaries and more coherent lighting across edits. It can insert general objects with improved spatial grounding. The model has approximately 5B parameters and is built on top of Wan2.2 5B. Version 1.1 includes better overall quality, higher fidelity details, stronger prompt adherence, and improved identity preservation. It offers more reliable global edits with better subject preservation and control. It provides better blending and seamless edits with cleaner boundaries, fewer artifacts, and improved color and lighting consistency across frames. It supports the addition of general objects with more reliable insertion of wearable, handheld, and general scene objects with improved spatial grounding, occlusion, and shadows. Lucy Edit 1.1 is designed for precise, realistic, and identity-preserving video edits. Prompts with 20–30 descriptive words work best, and using the right trigger words helps the model understand the intent. Supported edit types include clothing changes, human or character replacement, object replacement, color changes, adding objects, and global transformations. The model excels at identity conservation, edit precision, realism, and prompt adherence. Longer prompts describing style, appearance, and context improve results. Frame counts of 81 produce better temporal consistency than shorter clips. Version 1.1 improves boundary blending and temporal coherence across edits. The model is available as a hosted API with 5000 free credits. A Diffusers pipeline (LucyEditPipeline) is available, and ComfyUI integration is coming soon. The roadmap includes a public batch API, Diffusers pipeline, remote ComfyUI custom nodes, and a technical report. The model is based on the Wan2.2 (5B) foundation and acknowledges the open-source community, including diffusers and Hugging Face.

instruction: Can you summarize the lyrical content of this song in a few sentences?
description: Music Flamingo (MF) is a fully open, state-of-the-art Large Audio-Language Model (LALM) designed to advance music understanding in foundational audio models. It supports deep music understanding across songs and instrumentals, and is capable of generating rich, theory-aware captions and answering questions about harmony, structure, timbre, lyrics, and cultural context. The model is trained using reasoning-centric methods that include chain-of-thought and reinforcement learning with custom rewards for step-by-step reasoning. It is also capable of long-form song reasoning over full-length, multicultural audio with extended context. Evaluations have confirmed its effectiveness, setting new benchmarks on over 10 public music understanding and reasoning tasks. The model is based on a Transformer architecture and includes an AF-Whisper unified audio encoder from Audio Flamingo 3, an MLP-based audio adaptor, and a decoder-only LLM backbone (Qwen2.5-7B). It has 8 billion parameters. The model is intended for non-commercial research purposes only and is supported in HuggingFace Transformers. It processes audio in 30-second windows with a 10-minute total cap per sample, and longer inputs are truncated. The model accepts music (song or instrumental) and text as input, with audio in WAV/MP3/FLAC formats and text in UTF-8. It can handle up to 20 minutes of audio and 24,000 text tokens. The output is text in UTF-8 string format, with a maximum of 2048 tokens. The model is optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems (A100/H100) and is compatible with PyTorch and HuggingFace Transformers. It supports Linux-based operating systems and is intended for use in research and development for applications such as music question answering, long-context music comprehension, and interactive music design assistants. The model was trained on music data collected from various sources, with training data ranging from 10,000 to 1 million hours. The training data was collected using human methods, while labeling was a mix of human and automated methods. Testing was performed on datasets with human-collected data, with labeling also a mix of human and automated methods. The model is a preview version, and a better base and a thinking version are expected to be released in the near future. Ethical considerations include ensuring the model meets industry and use-case requirements and addressing potential misuse. The model is subject to NVIDIA's terms of service and policies on Trustworthy AI.

instruction: What are the main differences between the K-quants and the I-quants in this set?
description: This is a set of imatrix quantizations of the Voxtral-Small-24B-2507 model by MistralAI, using the llama.cpp release b6014 for quantization. The model is intended for use with llama.cpp or other llama.cpp-based projects. The quantizations are generated using the imatrix option and a dataset provided by the original model. The model supports various quantization types, each with different file sizes and quality levels. Some quantizations use Q8_0 for embedding and output weights to maintain higher quality. The available quantizations include BF16, Q8_0, Q6_K_L, Q6_K, Q5_K_L, Q5_K_M, Q5_K_S, Q4_1, Q4_K_L, Q4_K_M, Q4_K_S, Q4_0, IQ4_NL, Q3_K_XL, IQ4_XS, Q3_K_L, Q3_K_M, IQ3_M, Q3_K_S, IQ3_XS, Q2_K_L, IQ3_XXS, Q2_K, IQ2_M, IQ2_S, and IQ2_XS. Each quantization is described with its quality level and file size. The model can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool, with instructions provided for downloading specific files or multiple files if the model exceeds 50GB. Some quantizations, such as Q4_0, support online repacking for improved performance on ARM and AVX hardware. The model includes information on performance benchmarks on an AVX2 system, showing speed improvements for certain quantizations. Users are advised to consider their hardware capabilities when selecting a quantization, balancing speed, quality, and memory usage. I-quants (IQX_X) are recommended for lower quantization levels when using cuBLAS or rocBLAS, while K-quants (QX_K_X) are generally suitable for most use cases. The model includes credits to contributors for dataset creation, inspiration, and sponsorship.

instruction: Is there any indication of gene mutations in this slide that could affect treatment options?
description: Digepath is a self-supervised foundation model for intelligent gastrointestinal pathology image analysis. The model is a Vision Transformer Large/16 with DINO-V2 self-supervised pre-training on 353 million multi-scale images from 210,043 H&E-stained gastrointestinal related slides. Gastrointestinal (GI) diseases represent a clinically significant burden, necessitating precise diagnostic approaches to optimize patient outcomes. Conventional histopathological diagnosis suffers from limited reproducibility and diagnostic variability. To overcome these limitations, Digepath was developed as a specialized foundation model for GI pathology. The framework introduces a dual-phase iterative optimization strategy combining pretraining with fine-screening, specifically designed to address the detection of sparsely distributed lesion areas in whole-slide images. Digepath was initially pretrained on a large-scale dataset comprising over 353 million multi-scale images derived from 210,043 H&E-stained slides of GI diseases. It was subsequently fine-tuned on 471,443 carefully selected regions of interest (ROIs) in the second stage. It attains state-of-the-art performance on 32 out of 33 tasks related to GI pathology, including pathological diagnosis, protein expression status prediction, gene mutation prediction, and prognosis evaluation. Digepath demonstrates broad applicability across diverse clinical tasks, highlighting its potential for reliable deployment in real-world pathology workflows.

instruction: What are the named chemical compounds in this summary of a drug discovery project?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition in biomedical literature. It identifies and extracts chemical compounds and substances from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the BC4CHEMD dataset, a manually annotated corpus for chemical entity recognition from the BioCreative IV challenge. The BC4CHEMD dataset contains PubMed abstracts with chemical entities annotated according to ChEBI guidelines and is used as a benchmark for evaluating named entity recognition models. The model can identify and classify the following entity types: B-CHEM and I-CHEM. It achieves an F1 score of 0.92, precision of 0.91, recall of 0.93, and accuracy of 0.98. The model is based on the e5-small-v2 architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. If used in research or applications, it should be cited as described in the provided citation.

instruction: Which of these two options best matches the tone and intent of the original message?
description: This is a model finetuned from Alibaba-NLP/gte-multilingual-reranker-base using the Sentence Transformers library. It is a Cross Encoder model designed to compute scores for pairs of texts, which can be used for text reranking and semantic search. The model has a maximum sequence length of 512 tokens and produces one output label. It can be loaded and used with the Sentence Transformers library for inference, where it can calculate scores for text pairs or rank texts based on similarity to a single text. The model was trained on a dataset of 24,588 samples, with each sample containing two sentences and a label. The training used a non-default set of hyperparameters, including a batch size of 32 for both training and evaluation, 5 training epochs, and FP16 precision. During training, the model achieved a Spearman correlation of 0.8709 on the validation dataset. The training loss decreased over time, and the Spearman correlation on the validation set improved from 0.7581 at step 100 to 0.8709 at step 3200. The model was trained using PyTorch 2.8.0+cu128, Sentence Transformers 5.1.2, and Transformers 4.57.1. The training data included a variety of sentence pairs, with sentence lengths ranging from 3 to 998 characters for the first sentence and 73 to 352 characters for the second sentence. The labels ranged from 0.0 to 1.0, with a mean of 0.41. The model uses a linear learning rate scheduler with no warmup steps and a learning rate of 5e-05. The training process included logging of training loss and validation Spearman correlation at each step.

instruction: What are the most recent developments in machine learning techniques for natural language processing?
description: inf-retriever-v1-pro is a specialized retrieval component of the INF-X-Retriever framework, designed to distill the core retrieval intent from complex, verbose, or reasoning-intensive queries. It is built upon the inf-retriever-v1 foundation and further trained to serve as the retriever within a RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) system. The model transforms raw user queries into concise, search-optimized queries for dense retrieval systems. In experiments, a single canonical query-writing prompt was applied across all datasets to ensure consistency and reproducibility. The task is defined as 'Given a web search query, retrieve relevant passages that answer the query.' This model is a key enabler for INF-X-Retriever's state-of-the-art performance, currently holding the No. 1 position on the (as of Dec 17, 2025). The BRIGHT (Benchmark for Reasoning-Intensive Grounded HT) is a rigorous text retrieval benchmark designed to evaluate the capability of retrieval models in handling questions that require intensive reasoning and cross-document synthesis. It is collected from real-world sources such as StackExchange, competitive programming platforms, and mathematical competitions, and comprises complex queries spanning diverse domains like mathematics, coding, biology, economics, and robotics. On the BRIGHT benchmark, INF-X-Retriever achieves state-of-the-art results (as of Dec 20, 2025). For short document performance, INF-X-Retriever achieves an average of 63.4, with 68.3 on StackExchange, 55.3 on Coding, and 57.7 on Theorem-based categories. For detailed results across 12 datasets, INF-X-Retriever achieves an average of 63.4, with 79.8 in Bio., 70.9 in Earth., 69.9 in Econ., 73.3 in Psy., 57.7 in Rob., 64.3 in Stack., 61.9 in Sus., 56.1 in Leet., 54.5 in Pony, 51.9 in AoPS, 53.1 in TheoQ., and 67.9 in TheoT. For long document performance, INF-X-Retriever achieves an average of 54.6, with 73.2 in Bio., 59.6 in Earth., 69.3 in Econ., 12.1 in Pony, 74.3 in Psy., 55.9 in Rob., 27.8 in Stack., and 64.8 in Sus. The model is implemented using the transformers library version 4.51.0 and can be used with the SentenceTransformer or the Transformers library for encoding queries and documents and computing retrieval scores.

instruction: This video has a lot of compression artifacts. How can I reduce them?
description: SeedVR is a diffusion transformer model designed for generic video restoration. It addresses the limitations of conventional restoration models, which often perform poorly on real-world and AIGC (AI-generated content) video restoration due to limited generation ability. While recent diffusion-based models have improved performance by incorporating diffusion prior via ControlNet-like or adaptor-like architectures, these methods are constrained by the diffusion prior, leading to issues such as limited generation ability on small texts and faces, and fixed-resolution processing (e.g., 512 or 1024). These models also typically rely on patch-based sampling, which involves dividing the input video into overlapping spatial-temporal patches and fusing them using a Gaussian kernel at each diffusion step. The large overlap required for coherent output often results in slow inference speed, especially for long videos at high resolutions. SeedVR tackles the key challenge in diffusion-based restoration by enabling arbitrary-resolution restoration without relying on any pretrained diffusion prior. It introduces advanced video generation technologies suitable for video restoration and follows state-of-the-art video generation training pipelines. SeedVR is described as the largest-ever diffusion transformer model for generic video restoration and is intended to push the frontiers of advanced video restoration and inspire future research in large vision models for real-world video restoration. However, these are prototype models, and their performance may not perfectly align with the paper. The methods are sometimes not robust to heavy degradations and very large motions, and they share some failure cases with existing methods, such as failing to fully remove degradation or generating unpleasing details. Additionally, due to their strong generation ability, the methods may overly generate details on inputs with very light degradations, such as 720p AIGC videos, occasionally leading to oversharpened results.

instruction: Is there a way to determine if these two legal texts are related?
description: LEGAL-BERT is a family of BERT models designed for the legal domain, intended to support legal NLP research, computational law, and legal technology applications. The models were pre-trained on 12 GB of diverse English legal text from various fields, including legislation, court cases, and contracts, collected from publicly available resources. Different sub-domain variants, such as CONTRACTS-BERT-BASE, EURLEX-BERT-BASE, and ECHR-BERT-BASE, as well as the general LEGAL-BERT-BASE, perform better than using BERT out of the box for domain-specific tasks. LEGAL-BERT-SMALL is a lightweight version of BERT-BASE, pre-trained from scratch on legal data, which is 33% the size of BERT-BASE. It achieves comparable performance to larger models while being approximately four times faster and having a smaller environmental footprint. The pre-training corpora include 116,062 documents of EU legislation from EURLEX, 61,826 documents of UK legislation, 19,867 cases from the European Court of Justice, 12,554 cases from the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR), 164,141 cases from various US courts, and 76,366 US contracts from EDGAR. LEGAL-BERT-BASE, also known as LEGAL-BERT-SC, was trained from scratch using a newly created vocabulary by a sentence-piece tokenizer trained on the same legal corpora. LEGAL-BERT-FP models, which rely on the original BERT-BASE checkpoint, have been released in Archive.org and are considered secondary. The models were trained using the official Google BERT code, with a setup of 1 million training steps, batches of 256 sequences of length 512, and an initial learning rate of 1e-4. Training was conducted on a single Google Cloud TPU v3-8. LEGAL-BERT variants have been evaluated on downstream tasks and are described in the paper 'LEGAL-BERT: The Muppets straight out of Law School' by Chalkidis et al. (2020).

instruction: Can you adjust the perspective in this photo to make the subject appear closer?
description: This repository contains Nunchaku-quantized versions of an image-editing model based on advances in complex text rendering. The model is optimized for efficient inference while maintaining minimal loss in performance. It is an image-to-image model developed by the Nunchaku Team and is licensed under the Apache-2.0 license. The model is quantized from an unspecified base model. The data type for the model is INT4 for non-Blackwell GPUs (pre-50-series) and NVFP4 for Blackwell GPUs (50-series). Two rank configurations are available: r32 for faster inference and r128 for better quality but slower inference. Standard inference speed models are provided for general use. Additionally, 4-step distilled models are available, fused with unspecified components using LoRA strength = 1.0. These include models with data types INT4 and NVFP4, and ranks r32 and r128. Similarly, 8-step distilled models are available, also fused with unspecified components using LoRA strength = 1.0. The model sources include an unspecified inference engine, quantization library, paper, and demo. The model is described in the paper titled 'SVDQuant: Absorbing Outliers by Low-Rank Components for 4-Bit Diffusion Models,' presented at The Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations in 2025.

instruction: Can you explain what's happening in this snapshot of a sports event?
description: The Vision Transformer (ViT) is a transformer encoder model, similar to BERT, that was pre-trained on a large collection of images in a supervised manner using the ImageNet-21k dataset, which contains 14 million images across 21,843 classes. The model processes images at a resolution of 224x224 pixels by dividing them into fixed-size patches of 16x16 pixels, which are linearly embedded. A [CLS] token is added to the beginning of the sequence for classification tasks, and absolute position embeddings are included before the sequence is fed into the Transformer encoder layers. The model does not include any fine-tuned heads, as these were set to zero by the researchers, but it does retain the pre-trained pooler, which can be used for downstream tasks such as image classification. Through pre-training, the model learns an internal representation of images that can be used to extract features for downstream tasks, such as training a standard classifier by placing a linear layer on top of the pre-trained encoder. The [CLS] token's last hidden state is typically used as a representation of the entire image. The model was pre-trained on TPUv3 hardware using 8 cores, with a batch size of 4096 and a learning rate warmup of 10,000 steps. During training, images were resized and normalized to 224x224 resolution with mean (0.5, 0.5, 0.5) and standard deviation (0.5, 0.5, 0.5) across the RGB channels. Evaluation results on image classification benchmarks are detailed in the original paper, with the best fine-tuning results achieved at a higher resolution of 384x384. Larger model sizes are associated with better performance. The model can be used for image classification, and fine-tuned versions for specific tasks are available. The model was introduced in a paper by Dosovitskiy et al. and the weights were converted from JAX to PyTorch by Ross Wightman. The model card was written by the Hugging Face team, as the original team did not provide one.

instruction: Can I run this on a system without a dedicated graphics card?
description: MelBandRoformer-GGUF is a repository containing Mel-Band-Roformer models converted to the GGUF format, enabling high-performance, cross-platform local inference using a specific inference engine. The repository provides GGUF quantized versions of original models, including a model referred to as (voc_fv6). Various quantized versions are available to meet different hardware requirements. The q8_0 quantization type is recommended, offering 8-bit quantization that significantly reduces memory usage and bandwidth requirements while maintaining audio quality almost identical to FP32. The fp16 quantization type provides 16-bit floating point precision, suitable for scenarios requiring maximum precision. Lower-bit quantization types, including q4_0, q4_1, q5_0, and q5_1, are available for devices with low VRAM or RAM. Norm and Bias weights remain in FP32 to ensure numerical stability. The model is intended to be used with a specific command-line tool. Users are instructed to download the executable for their system or compile it themselves, download the desired .gguf file from the repository, and run inference using the provided command-line interface. Basic usage involves specifying the model file, input audio file, and output audio file. Advanced usage allows for adjusting chunk size and overlap to optimize quality.

instruction: Can you create a dreamlike underwater scene with vibrant coral reefs and exotic marine life?
description: Qwen-Image-2512-Turbo-LoRA is a 4 or 8-step turbo LoRA for Qwen Image 2512, trained by Wuli Team. This LoRA matches the original model's output quality but is over 20x faster, with 2x speed improvement from CFG-distillation and additional speed gains from a reduced number of inference steps. For users in the Chinese mainland, the model can be directly tried on the team's website, where four images with 2k resolution can be generated in just 5 seconds. The model has undergone several updates: on January 8, 2026, version 3.0 was released with further improved image color and details and is already ComfyUI compatible. On January 2, 2026, version 2.0 was released with improved image color and details and is also ComfyUI compatible. On December 31, 2025, version 1.0 and its ComfyUI compatible version were released, with plans to soon release version 2.0 with enhanced performance. The model can be used with the Diffsynth-Engine, which includes an example code for creating a pipeline, loading the turbo LoRA, changing the scheduler configuration, and sampling an image. One caveat is that generated image quality may degrade when handling extremely complex details, and users can increase the value of num_inference_steps to address this issue.

instruction: Based on this picture, what kind of story could be told?
description: BLIP-2 is a model that consists of three components: a CLIP-like image encoder, a Querying Transformer (Q-Former), and a large language model with 2.7 billion parameters. The image encoder and large language model are initialized from pre-trained checkpoints and remain frozen during training, while the Q-Former, a BERT-like Transformer encoder, is trained to map a set of 'query tokens' to query embeddings that bridge the embedding spaces of the image encoder and the language model. The model is designed to predict the next text token given the query embeddings and the previous text. It supports tasks such as image captioning, visual question answering (VQA), and chat-like conversations by providing the image and previous conversation as input. The model can be used for conditional text generation given an image and optional text. BLIP-2 uses an off-the-shelf OPT model as its language model, which inherits the same risks and limitations as described in Meta's model card, including potential biases, safety issues, and generation quality concerns. The model is fine-tuned on image-text datasets collected from the internet, which may result in the model generating inappropriate content or replicating biases present in the training data. It has not been tested in real-world applications and should not be directly deployed without careful evaluation of its safety and fairness in the intended context. This release is for research purposes only and is not designed or evaluated for all downstream applications. Users are advised to evaluate and address potential concerns related to accuracy, safety, and fairness before deployment. The model's memory requirements vary depending on the precision used, with options including float32, float16/bfloat16, int8, and int4. The model can be run on CPU or GPU, with different configurations for full, half, and 8-bit precision.

instruction: Is it better to punt or go for it on 4th and 9 from the 45-yard line with 3:45 left in the 4th and a 4-point lead?
description: The NFL 4th-Down Decision Model is a small multi-task MLP that recommends whether to go for it, kick a field goal, or punt on 4th down by predicting the change in win probability (ΔWP) for each action. It is trained on NFL play-by-play data from 2016 to 2024, with labels generated from historical probabilities. The model takes 23 input features, including quarter, game and half seconds remaining, yardline, yards-to-go, score differential, timeouts, weather, point spread, and one-hot encoded roof and surface indicators, along with derived features such as field goal distance and whether the field goal is in range. The model outputs ΔWP for the three actions (go, field goal, punt) as the main head, and also predicts auxiliary heads for field goal make probability and 4th-down conversion probability. The recommendation is to choose the action with the highest ΔWP. The model was trained using a split of 2016–2021 for training, 2022 for validation, and 2023–2024 for testing. The training objective combines Huber loss for ΔWP with 0.2 times the mean squared error for auxiliary heads. Training used the AdamW optimizer with BatchNorm, Dropout, and L2 regularization. The best model weights are saved as `mlp_dwp_plus_aux_best.keras`. On the 2023–2024 test set, the model achieved a policy agreement of 0.794, a mean regret (ΔWP) of 0.0023, a late and close (≤600 seconds, absolute score ≤8) agreement of 0.744, and a late and close mean regret of 0.0099. ΔWP is reported in win-probability points, with lower regret indicating better performance. The model is intended as an educational decision assistant for 4th-down scenarios and is not suitable for wagering or real-time coaching. It relies on historical probabilities and inherits assumptions from the nfl4th project. Simplifications in weather and wp_punt imputation may affect accuracy, and domain judgment should be applied when using the model's recommendations.

instruction: Which of these customer support responses is most appropriate for the given query?
description: The Qwen3-Reranker-8B is a text reranking model designed for text retrieval tasks. It is part of the Qwen3 Embedding model series, which is specifically developed for text embedding and ranking. The model is based on the Qwen3 series and offers a comprehensive range of text embeddings and reranking models in various sizes (0.6B, 4B, and 8B). It inherits the multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills of the foundational Qwen3 model.
