The Qwen3-Reranker-8B is optimized for reranking in text retrieval scenarios and supports over 100 languages, including various programming languages, providing robust multilingual, cross-lingual, and code retrieval capabilities. The model has 8 billion parameters and a context length of 32,000 tokens. It is instruction-aware, meaning it supports customizing input instructions according to different tasks, which can improve performance by 1% to 5% in most downstream tasks. Developers are advised to write instructions in English for optimal results, as most training instructions were in English. The model is evaluated on multiple benchmarks, including MTEB-R, CMTEB-R, MMTEB-R, MLDR, MTEB-Code, and FollowIR, where it achieves strong performance, particularly in multilingual and code-related tasks. The Qwen3-Reranker-8B is available for use with the Transformers library version 4.51.0 or higher and requires specific formatting for input queries and documents. It is recommended to use the model with flash_attention_2 for better acceleration and memory efficiency. The model is part of a series that includes both embedding and reranking models, allowing developers to combine these modules for diverse use cases.

instruction: Generate a clip of a time-traveling car appearing in the middle of a bustling 1920s street.
description: Self Forcing is a method for training autoregressive video diffusion models by simulating the inference process during training, using autoregressive rollout with KV caching. This approach addresses the train-test distribution mismatch and allows for real-time, streaming video generation on a single RTX 4090 while maintaining the quality of state-of-the-art diffusion models. The model is designed to generate high-quality video content and supports both GUI and CLI-based inference. It is trained using a data-free algorithm, meaning no video data is required, and instead uses text prompts and an ODE-initialized checkpoint. The model can be trained using multiple GPUs, with a training run of 600 iterations completing in under 2 hours using 64 H100 GPUs. Performance can be optimized by enabling `torch.compile` or using FP8 Linear layers, though these may affect quality. The model works best with long, detailed prompts, and it is recommended to use third-party LLMs like GPT-4o to extend prompts before input. The model supports chunk-wise autoregressive generation and is evaluated using prompts from the MovieGenVideoBench dataset. The codebase is based on open-source implementations from other repositories. The model is available on HuggingFace and requires an Nvidia GPU with at least 24 GB memory, a Linux operating system, and 64 GB RAM for optimal performance.

instruction: Please show me all the trees in this picture based on the phrase 'a tall green plant' and a sample leaf image.
description: SAM 3 is a unified foundation model for promptable segmentation in images and videos. It can detect, segment, and track objects using text or visual prompts such as points, boxes, and masks. Compared to its predecessor, SAM 3 introduces the ability to exhaustively segment all instances of an open-vocabulary concept specified by a short text phrase or exemplars. Unlike prior work, SAM 3 can handle a vastly larger set of open-vocabulary prompts. It achieves 75-80% of human performance on a new benchmark that contains 270K unique concepts, over 50 times more than existing benchmarks. SAM 3 supports promptable concept segmentation (PCS) for images and videos, as well as promptable visual segmentation (PVS) for images and videos. For image PCS, the model takes text and/or image exemplars as prompts and returns segmentation masks for all matching object instances in the image. It supports text-only prompts, single or multiple bounding box prompts (positive and negative), and combined prompts (text + negative box). It also supports batched inference with text prompts and batched mixed prompts. For video PCS, the model takes text as prompts and detects and tracks all matching object instances across video frames. It supports pre-loaded video inference and streaming video inference. For image PVS, the model takes interactive visual prompts (points, boxes, masks) to segment a specific object instance per prompt. It supports single point clicks, multiple points for refinement, bounding box input, and multiple objects segmentation. It also supports batch inference. For video PVS, the model takes interactive visual prompts (points, boxes, masks) to track a specific object instance per prompt across video frames. It supports basic video tracking, multi-object video tracking, and streaming video inference. SAM 3 also provides semantic segmentation alongside instance masks. The model is an updated version of SAM2 and SAM2 Video, maintaining the same API while providing improved performance, making it a drop-in replacement for SAM2 workflows. It is available through the Transformers library and supports both image and video processing. For best results, pre-loaded video inference is recommended when all frames are available, as streaming inference may result in more false positive detections and duplicate object tracks due to the disabling of hotstart heuristics.

instruction: Create a clip of a dancer performing in front of a vibrant mural.
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video generation models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video (TI2V) capabilities. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity without increasing computational cost. The A14B model series uses a two-expert design: a high-noise expert for early-stage layout and a low-noise expert for later-stage detail refinement, with each expert having approximately 14B parameters and a total of 27B parameters. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a defined threshold step for switching. The model is trained on significantly larger data, with +65.6% more images and +83.2% more videos compared to Wan2.1, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 also introduces a 5B model with a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE, achieving a compression ratio of 16×16×4, and supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps. This model can run on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090 and is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available. The T2V-A14B model supports generating 5-second videos at both 480P and 720P resolutions and delivers outstanding video generation quality. On the Wan-Bench 2.0 benchmark, the model surpasses leading commercial models across most key evaluation dimensions. The model is available for download via Hugging Face and ModelScope, and supports both single-GPU and multi-GPU inference using FSDP and DeepSpeed Ulysses. Prompt extension is available via Dashscope API or local Qwen models to enhance video quality. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license terms and applicable laws.

instruction: What kind of vehicle is featured in this 10-second sound sample?
description: Audio Flamingo 3 (AF3) is a fully open, state-of-the-art Large Audio-Language Model (LALM) that advances reasoning and understanding across speech, sounds, and music. AF3 builds on previous work with innovations in unified audio representation learning (speech, sound, music), flexible, on-demand chain-of-thought reasoning, long-context audio comprehension (up to 10 minutes), multi-turn, multi-audio conversational dialogue (AF3-Chat), and voice-to-voice interaction (AF3-Chat). Extensive evaluations confirm AF3’s effectiveness, setting new benchmarks on over 20 public audio understanding and reasoning tasks. This model is for non-commercial research purposes only. AF3 uses an AF-Whisper unified audio encoder, an MLP-based audio adaptor, a Decoder-only LLM backbone (Qwen2.5-7B), and a Streaming TTS module (AF3-Chat). Audio Flamingo 3 can take up to 10 minutes of audio inputs. The model is supported in 🤗 Transformers and processes audio in 30-second windows with a 10-minute total cap per sample. Longer inputs are truncated. AF3 supports single-turn audio and text instruction, multi-turn chat, batch multiple conversations, text-only and audio-only prompts, and transcription of local or remote files. It also supports think-mode reasoning with a PEFT adapter (AF-Think) and training or fine-tuning. AF3 can be optimized for inference using vLLM for up to 5-7x speedup, Flash Attention 2, Torch compile, or PyTorch SDPA. The model is designed to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems (A100/H100) and is compatible with PyTorch and HuggingFace Transformers. AF3 accepts audio in WAV, MP3, or FLAC formats and text in UTF-8, with a maximum audio length of 10 minutes and a maximum text length of 16000 tokens. It outputs text in UTF-8 string format, with a maximum text length of 1024 tokens, and can optionally produce speech in the form of a streaming TTS waveform. AF3 is trained entirely on open-source audio data, organized into four novel, large-scale collections. The data collection method for all training datasets is human, with labeling methods varying between automated and human. AF3 is evaluated on the test split of multiple datasets, with data collection methods also being human for most datasets, and labeling methods varying. The model is intended for researchers and developers to explore audio question answering and reasoning, long-context audio comprehension, interactive sound/music design assistants, and multi-turn (voice) chat. AF3 was released on 07/10/2025 via GitHub and HuggingFace. The model is released under a license, with portions of the dataset generation also subject to other licenses and OpenAI’s terms. NVIDIA encourages responsible use of the model and asks users to report any security vulnerabilities or AI concerns.

instruction: Can you help me determine which of these texts is most relevant to my research topic?
description: This is a model finetuned from a base model. It maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for semantic textual similarity, semantic search, paraphrase mining, text classification, clustering, and more. The model is a Sentence Transformer with a maximum sequence length of 8192 tokens and uses cosine similarity as the similarity function. It is designed for the English language and is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license. The model architecture consists of a Transformer with a ModernBertModel, a Pooling layer with mean token pooling, and a Normalize layer. The model was trained on 2,828 triplet samples with anchor, positive, and negative text examples. Training used a batch size of 16, a learning rate of 2e-05, and one training epoch with a warmup ratio of 0.1. The model was evaluated on 489 samples, achieving a cosine accuracy of 0.9959 on the dev dataset. The model is intended for use with the Sentence Transformers library and supports inference for generating embeddings and computing similarity scores between text inputs.

instruction: How can I find the closest match to this sentence in a multilingual document collection?
description: The Qwen3-Embedding-4B is a text embedding model from the Qwen3 Embedding model series, designed for text embedding and ranking tasks. It is part of a comprehensive range of models in various sizes (0.6B, 4B, and 8B) that provide text embeddings and reranking capabilities. The model inherits the multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills of the Qwen3 series. It supports over 100 languages, including various programming languages, and offers robust multilingual, cross-lingual, and code retrieval capabilities. The model has 4 billion parameters, a context length of 32,000 tokens, and an embedding dimension of up to 2560, with support for user-defined output dimensions ranging from 32 to 2560. It is instruction-aware, allowing users to customize input instructions for specific tasks, languages, or scenarios, which can improve performance by 1% to 5% in most retrieval scenarios. The model is evaluated on multiple benchmarks, achieving a score of 69.45 on the MTEB multilingual leaderboard (as of May 24, 2025), and it supports flexible vector definitions and seamless integration with other modules. Developers are advised to write instructions in English for optimal performance in multilingual contexts. The model can be used with frameworks such as Sentence Transformers, Transformers, and vLLM, and it is compatible with deployment via Text Embeddings Inference (TEI) on both GPU and CPU devices.

instruction: Could you scan this biomedical dataset and identify all the disease entities for me?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for disease entity recognition, specifically for disease entities from the BC5CDR dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the BC5CDR_DISEASE dataset, which contains 1,500 PubMed abstracts with 5,818 annotated disease entities. The model supports the identification and classification of two entity types: B-DISEASE and I-DISEASE. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the BiomedNLP-BiomedELECTRA-large-uncased-abstract architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It has been validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.90, precision of 0.89, recall of 0.91, and accuracy of 0.98. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical and research applications where accurate disease entity identification is crucial for medical informatics and healthcare analytics. If used in research or applications, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: List the anatomical structures referenced in this section of the pathology report.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for high-precision biomedical entity recognition in the domain of cancer genetics. It is optimized for identifying and extracting cancer-related genetic entities from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports a wide range of biomedical entity types, including but not limited to amino acids, anatomical systems, cancers, cells, cellular components, developing anatomical structures, genes or gene products, immaterial anatomical entities, multi-tissue structures, organs, organisms, organism subdivisions, organism substances, pathological formations, simple chemicals, and tissues. Both B- and I- tags are supported for these entity types. The model is trained on the BIONLP2013_CG dataset, a specialized corpus for cancer genetics entities and gene regulation in oncology research. It is based on the xlm-roberta-base architecture and is implemented using the Hugging Face Transformers framework. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.79, precision of 0.79, recall of 0.80, and accuracy of 0.89. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is production-ready and validated on clinical benchmarks, and it is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and academic settings. If used in research, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: I need a prediction of the environmental impact for this molecule—can you provide one?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict toxicity-related molecular properties. The model is designed to perform regression tasks, taking SMILES strings as input and using graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. It is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and features an architecture based on AttentiveFP with enhanced atom and bond features. The model uses 10-dimensional enhanced atom features and 6-dimensional enhanced bond features. The hyperparameters include 128 hidden channels, 3 layers, 3 timesteps, 0.1 dropout, 0.0005 learning rate, 0.0001 weight decay, 32 batch size, 50 epochs, and 10 patience. The model was trained on the TMP dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: I have a 1-minute clip in Polish from a local news broadcast. Can you transcribe it for me?
description: The parakeet-tdt-0.6b-v3 is an on-device multilingual automatic speech recognition (ASR) model converted to Core ML for Apple platforms. It powers FluidAudio’s batch ASR and is the same model used in the backend. The model supports 25 European languages and is optimized for low-latency, private, offline transcription. It is based on the Parakeet TDT v3 architecture, a Token Duration Transducer with 0.6 billion parameters. The model accepts 16 kHz, mono, Float32 PCM audio input in the range [-1, 1]. It is implemented in mixed precision optimized for Core ML execution on Apple Neural Engine (ANE) and CPU. The model achieves a real-time factor (RTF) of approximately 110× on M4 Pro in batch mode, meaning 1 minute of audio takes about 0.5 seconds to transcribe. Performance, including throughput and latency, varies depending on the device, input duration, and compute units used. The model is intended for batch transcription of complete audio files on macOS and iOS, local dictation and note-taking applications where privacy and latency are important, and embedded ASR in production apps via the FluidAudio Swift framework. It is supported on macOS 14+ (Apple Silicon recommended) and iOS 17+. The model is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license. It is primarily optimized for European languages, and performance may degrade for non-European languages.

instruction: Could you help me find the table in this document that lists the participants' demographics?
description: ColPali is a model based on a novel architecture and training strategy using Vision Language Models (VLMs) to efficiently index documents from their visual features. It generates multi-vector representations of text and images. The model was introduced in a paper and first released in a version trained with a batch size of 256 for 3 epochs on the same data as the original ColPali model. This version is trained with right padding for queries to fix unwanted tokens in the query encoding and stems from the fixed `vidore/colpaligemma-3b-pt-448-base` to guarantee deterministic projection layer initialization. It was trained for 5 epochs, with in-batch negatives and hard mined negatives, and a warmup of 1000 steps to help reduce non-English language collapse. The data used is the same as the ColPali data described in the paper. The model is built iteratively starting from an off-the-shelf model. It is fine-tuned to create and fed the patch-embeddings output by SigLIP to an LLM to create. One benefit of inputting image patch embeddings through a language model is that they are natively mapped to a latent space similar to textual input (query). This enables leveraging the strategy to compute interactions between text tokens and image patches, which enables a step-change improvement in performance compared to BiPali. The training dataset consists of 127,460 query-page pairs, including 63% from openly available academic datasets and 37% from a synthetic dataset made up of pages from web-crawled PDF documents augmented with VLM-generated (Claude-3 Sonnet) pseudo-questions. The training set is fully English by design, enabling the study of zero-shot generalization to non-English languages. No multi-page PDF document is used both in the training and validation sets to prevent evaluation contamination. A validation set is created with 2% of the samples to tune hyperparameters. Multilingual data is present in the pretraining corpus of the language model (Gemma-2B) and may occur during PaliGemma-3B's multimodal training. All models are trained for 1 epoch on the train set in bfloat16 format, using low-rank adapters with alpha=32 and r=32 on the transformer layers from the language model, as well as the final randomly initialized projection layer, and the paged_adamw_8bit optimizer. Training is done on an 8 GPU setup with data parallelism, a learning rate of 5e-5 with linear decay and 2.5% warmup steps, and a batch size of 32. The model primarily focuses on PDF-type documents and high-resource languages, potentially limiting its generalization to other document types or less represented languages. It relies on multi-vector retrieving derived from the ColBERT late interaction mechanism, which may require engineering efforts to adapt to widely used vector retrieval frameworks that lack native multi-vector support. The vision language backbone model (PaliGemma) is under the Gemma license, while the adapters attached to the model are under the MIT license.

instruction: I want to improve the audio quality of a voice message recorded on my smartwatch—can you help?
description: DPDFNet is a family of causal, single-channel speech enhancement models designed for real-time noise suppression in challenging everyday environments. It extends the DeepFilterNet2 enhancement framework by incorporating Dual-Path RNN (DPRNN) blocks into the encoder, which enhances long-range temporal and cross-band modeling while maintaining a compact, streaming-friendly design. The models are optimized for deployment on mobile and edge devices using TensorFlow Lite (TFLite). The 16kHz models include baseline, dpdfnet2, dpdfnet4, and dpdfnet8, while a 48kHz model, dpdfnet2_48khz_hr, is available for fullband enhancement. These models are characterized by their causal and low-latency design, suitable for streaming applications such as telephony, conferencing, and embedded devices. The Dual-Path RNN integration improves temporal context and frequency-domain interactions, enabling more robust enhancement in difficult noise conditions. The model family is scalable, allowing users to choose between baseline and dpdfnet2/4/8 to balance quality and computational requirements. The models are intended for real-time, single-channel speech enhancement and are targeted for deployment on mobile devices, embedded NPUs, and edge platforms. The 16kHz models process 16kHz mono noisy speech waveforms and output 16kHz mono enhanced speech waveforms, while the 48kHz model processes and outputs 48kHz mono waveforms. Typical applications include voice calls, video conferencing, always-on voice interfaces, and audio devices such as wearables and earbuds. The models were trained using a mixture of public speech and noise datasets, including DNS4 (downsampled), MLS, MUSAN, and FSD50K. The baseline model has 2.31 million parameters, 0.36 gigaflops of multiply-accumulate operations, and a TFLite size of 8.5 MB. DPDFNet-2 has 2.49 million parameters, 1.35 gigaflops, and a TFLite size of 10.7 MB. DPDFNet-4 has 2.84 million parameters, 2.36 gigaflops, and a TFLite size of 12.9 MB. DPDFNet-8 has 3.54 million parameters, 4.37 gigaflops, and a TFLite size of 17.2 MB. The 48kHz model, DPDFNet-2 HR, has 2.58 million parameters, 2.42 gigaflops, and a TFLite size of 11.6 MB. The models are distributed under the Apache-2.0 license.

instruction: Please segment the body parts and accessories in this fashion photo.
description: The FASHN Human Parser is a SegFormer-B4 model fine-tuned for human parsing with 18 semantic classes, optimized for fashion and virtual try-on applications. It segments human images into 18 semantic categories, including body parts such as face, hair, arms, hands, legs, feet, and torso; clothing items such as top, dress, skirt, pants, belt, and scarf; and accessories such as bag, hat, glasses, and jewelry. The model uses a SegFormer-B4 architecture, which includes an MIT-B4 encoder and an MLP decoder. It accepts input images of size 384 x 576 and produces 18-class semantic segmentation masks. The model was fine-tuned on a proprietary dataset curated and annotated by FASHN AI, specifically designed for virtual try-on applications. The 18-class label schema was developed to capture the semantic regions most relevant for clothing transfer and human body understanding in fashion contexts. The model is optimized for single-person images with clear visibility and performs best on fashion or e-commerce style photography. Input images are resized to 384x576, and very small subjects may lose detail. The label definitions include background, face, hair, top, dress, skirt, pants, belt, bag, hat, scarf, glasses, arms, hands, legs, feet, torso, and jewelry. For virtual try-on applications, the categories are mapped as follows: Tops (upper body) include top, dress, and scarf; Bottoms (lower body) include skirt, pants, and belt; and One-pieces (full body) include top, dress, scarf, skirt, pants, and belt. Labels typically preserved during virtual try-on include face, hair, jewelry, bag, glasses, and hat.

instruction: Can you create a summary of this government white paper, making sure every claim is supported by a reference to the original text?
description: sui-1 (Summarization with Unique Identifiers) is a specialized model for high-quality summarization of very long texts with built-in source grounding. Every claim in the summary can be traced back to its source sentence, enabling verification and reducing hallucination risk. The model handles up to 128k tokens natively, with a two-step iterative approach for documents up to 2 million tokens. The FP8 variant runs on a single A100 40GB or A6000 48GB GPU, and the iterative approach enables deployment on even more modest hardware. sui-1 significantly outperforms all tested open-weight baselines, including models with 3x more parameters. It is fine-tuned for English, German, Spanish, French, and Italian, and inherits 20+ additional languages from Mistral Small 3.2. The model is built using a sophisticated data generation pipeline that produced 22,000+ training examples from parliamentary documents, web sources, and Wikipedia using chain-of-thought reasoning with multi-stage verification. It features a built-in citation mechanism that links each claim to its source sentence for full traceability. The model follows a three-phase approach: a planning phase that analyzes the input and plans the summary structure, a reference selection phase that identifies the most important sentences to cite, and a grounded generation phase that produces a summary with inline citations to source sentences. Citations use XML tags assigned during preprocessing, enabling deterministic verification of each claim. The input text must be preprocessed with XML sentence tags, each sentence wrapped in a unique 8-character hexadecimal tag. The model outputs a JSON object with three keys: 'structure', 'xml_tags', and 'summary'. The 'structure' key contains internal reasoning about the content and planned summary structure, the 'xml_tags' key is a list of XML tags cited in the summary, and the 'summary' key is the actual summary with inline XML tag references. The model supports a 128k token context window natively and an iterative approach for longer documents. For documents exceeding the context limit, a two-step iterative approach is used that preserves grounding quality. The first step involves creating partial summaries of document chunks, and the second step merges these into a final summary. The iterative approach maintains source grounding through careful XML tag propagation, ensuring that every claim in the final summary traces back to a specific source sentence. The model provides two variants: bfloat16 (full precision, ~48GB weights) and FP8 (quantized, ~24GB weights). The FP8 version preserves high quality, scoring 81.05% overall on the benchmark, nearly identical to bfloat16. The bfloat16 variant requires ~55GB VRAM for 8k context, scaling to ~76GB for 128k. The FP8 variant requires ~38GB for 8k and ~50GB for 128k. The model is evaluated using an LLM-as-a-Judge methodology, where a strong judge model evaluates summary quality across multiple criteria, including factual accuracy, coverage and completeness, specificity and informativeness, format compliance, and custom instruction adherence. The evaluation was conducted on 100 diverse test samples covering multiple languages and document types, with scoring using binary pass/fail per criterion, aggregated to success rates. Grounding quality is validated using structural checks for tag uniqueness, tag validity, and tag usage. The model is a contribution to the open-source community and is released under the Apache 2.0 license. It has limitations, including the requirement for preprocessing of input text with XML tags, a maximum single-pass context of 128k tokens, and occasional JSON output parsing failures, for which retry logic is recommended in production use.

instruction: What biological species are referenced in this paragraph on infectious diseases?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for species entity recognition, specifically for identifying and extracting species and organism names from biomedical texts. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the LINNAEUS dataset, a specialized biomedical named entity recognition (NER) dataset focused on species name identification and organism recognition in scientific literature. The model is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. It supports the classification of two entity types: B-SPECIES and I-SPECIES. The model is based on the ModernBERT-base architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.85, precision of 0.89, recall of 0.81, and accuracy of 0.99. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and healthcare analytics contexts. The model is part of the OpenMed NER project, which provides open-source, domain-adapted state-of-the-art transformers for biomedical NER across multiple datasets.

instruction: Can you make the colors in this image more saturated and vivid?
description: Qwen-Image-Edit is an image editing model built upon the 20B Qwen-Image model. It extends Qwen-Image's text rendering capabilities to support image editing tasks, enabling precise text editing. The model simultaneously processes input images through Qwen2.5-VL for visual semantic control and the VAE Encoder for visual appearance control, allowing both semantic and appearance editing. Semantic editing includes tasks such as IP creation, object rotation, and style transfer, where the overall pixel content may change while maintaining semantic consistency. Appearance editing involves low-level modifications such as adding, removing, or modifying specific elements while keeping other regions unchanged. Qwen-Image-Edit supports bilingual (Chinese and English) text editing, allowing direct addition, deletion, and modification of text in images while preserving the original font, size, and style. Evaluations on multiple public benchmarks show that Qwen-Image-Edit achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in image editing tasks, making it a powerful foundation model for the field. The model is capable of tasks such as novel view synthesis, object rotation, style transfer, and detailed appearance modifications, including the addition of signboards with reflections and the removal of fine hair strands. It also supports chained editing approaches for progressive correction of errors in generated content, such as calligraphy artworks. Qwen-Image-Edit is licensed under Apache 2.0.

instruction: How closely does this blog post align with the query on healthy eating habits?
description: The Qwen3-Reranker-4B is a text reranking model designed for text retrieval tasks. It is part of the Qwen3 Embedding model series, which is specifically developed for text embedding and ranking. The model is built upon the dense foundational models of the Qwen3 series and inherits their multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills. The Qwen3 Embedding series includes various sizes of embedding and reranking models (0.6B, 4B, and 8B), offering a comprehensive range of options for different use cases. The Qwen3-Reranker-4B has 4 billion parameters and supports a context length of 32,000 tokens. It is instruction-aware, meaning it can accept user-defined instructions to enhance performance for specific tasks, languages, or scenarios. The model supports over 100 languages, including various programming languages, and provides robust multilingual, cross-lingual, and code retrieval capabilities. Evaluation results show that the Qwen3-Reranker-4B achieves a score of 69.76 on the MTEB-R benchmark, 75.94 on the CMTEB-R benchmark, 72.74 on the MMTEB-R benchmark, 69.97 on the MLDR benchmark, 81.20 on the MTEB-Code benchmark, and 14.84 on the FollowIR benchmark. These results are based on top-100 candidates retrieved by a dense embedding model. The model is recommended to be used with customized instructions, as this can improve performance by 1% to 5% in most retrieval scenarios. Developers are advised to write instructions in English, as most training instructions were originally in English. The model can be used with the Transformers library version 4.51.0 or higher, or with the vLLM library version 0.8.5 or higher. It is recommended to enable flash_attention_2 for better acceleration and memory saving. The model processes input pairs consisting of a query and a document, and outputs a score indicating the relevance of the document to the query. The model is evaluated on a range of benchmarks, including MTEB, CMTEB, MMTEB, and MTEB-Code, and demonstrates strong performance across these tasks.

instruction: Show a video of a volcanic eruption with defined lava flows and atmospheric details.
description: This model is a ControlNet module designed for the Wan2.2 text-to-video generation system, specifically tailored for canny edge detection. It follows the same approach as other ControlNet modules for Wan2.2. The model is intended to be used with the ComfyUI framework and requires the latest version of the diffusers library. It supports inference tasks for generating video content based on textual prompts and control signals derived from canny edge maps. The model architecture includes a custom WanControlnet component, which is integrated with the Wan2.2 base model. The model is evaluated through inference examples that demonstrate its ability to generate high-resolution video outputs with specific visual characteristics, such as close-up shots with soft lighting and detailed imagery. The model is provided with a Python-based implementation that includes loading the base model, setting up the pipeline, and processing video frames with canny detection. The model is available for use under the terms of the associated GitHub repository. The model has been developed by Karachev Denis and is published on GitHub. For professional support and recommendations, users are directed to contact welcomedenk@gmail.com.

instruction: Can you help me understand a document written in Swahili by translating its main ideas into English?
description: mmBERT is a modern multilingual encoder that significantly outperforms previous generation models like XLM-R on classification, embedding, and retrieval tasks. Built on the ModernBERT architecture with novel multilingual training innovations, mmBERT demonstrates that low-resource languages can be effectively learned during the decay phase of training. It is also significantly faster than any previous multilingual encoder. mmBERT represents the first significant advancement over XLM-R for massively multilingual encoder models. Key features include massive language coverage, trained on over 1800 languages with a progressive inclusion strategy. The model is built on the ModernBERT foundation with Flash Attention 2 and unpadding techniques. It introduces a novel training recipe with inverse mask scheduling and temperature sampling. The training data is publicly available, consisting of 3T+ tokens. The model uses bidirectional attention with masked language modeling objectives, optimized specifically for multilingual understanding and cross-lingual transfer. The training process includes a progressive language addition strategy, starting with 60 high-resource languages, expanding to 110 mid-resource languages, and including all 1833 languages in the decay phase. An inverse mask schedule reduces the mask ratio from 30% to 15% to 5% across training phases for progressively refined learning. Inverse temperature sampling adjusts multilingual sampling from high-resource bias (τ=0.7) to uniform sampling (τ=0.3). Model merging combines English-focused, high-resource, and all-language decay variants using TIES merging. The model family includes mmBERT-small and mmBERT-base, with mmBERT-small having 140M total parameters and 42M non-embed parameters, and mmBERT-base having 307M total parameters and 110M non-embed parameters. Both models support 1800+ languages and have a max sequence length of 8192. The model is trained on a carefully curated 3T+ token multilingual dataset, including 2.3T tokens for 60 languages in the pre-training phase, 600B tokens for 110 languages in the mid-training phase, and 100B tokens for 1833 languages in the decay phase. Data sources include Filtered DCLM (English), FineWeb2 (multilingual), FineWeb2-HQ (20 high-resource languages), Wikipedia (MegaWika), code repositories (StarCoder, ProLong), academic papers (ArXiv, PeS2o), and community discussions (StackExchange).

description: This model is a fine-tuned version of an unspecified base model on an unknown dataset. It is designed to perform document question-answering tasks. The model was trained using the following hyperparameters: a learning rate of 5e-05, a training batch size of 4, an evaluation batch size of 8, a random seed of 42, the Adam optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and an epsilon of 1e-08, a linear learning rate scheduler, and a total of 20 training epochs. During training, the model achieved a final training loss of 0.0053 at step 4500 and a final validation loss of 4.7623. The model was developed using the Transformers 4.44.2 library, PyTorch 2.5.0+cu121, Datasets 3.0.2, and Tokenizers 0.19.1. More information is needed regarding the model's intended uses, limitations, and the specific training and evaluation data used.

instruction: Can you identify the names of books or publications mentioned in this article?
description: This repository hosts the ONNX version of NuNerZero, a zero-shot named entity recognition (NER) model optimized for fast inference using ONNX Runtime. This conversion aims to provide efficient and production-ready performance while maintaining the original capabilities of the NuNerZero model. The model is part of Zink, a zero-shot anonymizer that currently uses the onnx NuNERZero model for anonymization. The repository includes the primary ONNX model file used for inference, configuration settings for the model, additional tokens required by the tokenizer, mapping for special tokens, tokenizer vocabulary and configuration files, and the SentencePiece model file used by the tokenizer. The model requires Python 3.7 or higher and the GLiNER package, with the onnx version created on 'gliner==0.2.3'. The model file is approximately 1.85 GB in size, so sufficient bandwidth and disk space are required for downloading. Leveraging the ONNX format can significantly accelerate inference compared to the original PyTorch implementation. Contributions, suggestions, or bug reports are welcome through opening an issue or submitting a pull request for improvements. The model is available under the citation 10.57967/hf/4902.

instruction: Can you pan the camera to the right to reveal what's behind the tree?
description: This model is trained on ModelScope without the use of code. It is designed to support camera control functions, including moving the camera up, down, left, and right, rotating it left and right, looking down or up, and switching to a wide-angle or close-up shot. The model does not require trigger words for these operations. It is based on the Qwen-Edit-2509 model, and a LoRa version is available for use. The model has been updated to address an instability and consistency issue by increasing the number of training iterations. Users are encouraged to try various camera control commands, as there are many possibilities. The model's performance is described as very amazing, and it is considered more powerful and intelligent compared to the original model, which already had certain capabilities. Users are invited to join a Discord group for communication and cooperation. If the resources are helpful or used for business purposes, users are encouraged to support the content creator through a PayPal donation.

instruction: Identify the most similar document in a dataset to the sentence 'Remote work has become the new norm in many companies'.
description: The gte-v1.5 series is an upgraded version of the gte embeddings, supporting a context length of up to 8192 while further enhancing model performance. These models are built upon the transformer++ encoder, which combines BERT, RoPE, and GLU components. The gte-v1.5 series achieves state-of-the-art scores on the MTEB benchmark within the same model size category and provides competitive results on the LoCo long-context retrieval tests. The models are developed by the Institute for Intelligent Computing, Alibaba Group, and are designed for text embeddings. The gte-v1.5 series includes multiple models with varying language support, model sizes, maximum sequence lengths, and embedding dimensions. For example, one model supports multiple languages with a model size of 7720 million parameters, a maximum sequence length of 32768, and an embedding dimension of 4096, achieving an MTEB-en score of 67.34 and a LoCo score of 87.57. Another model is specifically for English with a model size of 434 million parameters, a maximum sequence length of 8192, and an embedding dimension of 1024, achieving an MTEB-en score of 65.39 and a LoCo score of 86.71. A third English model has a model size of 137 million parameters, a maximum sequence length of 8192, and an embedding dimension of 768, achieving an MTEB-en score of 64.11 and a LoCo score of 87.44. The training process involves a multi-stage strategy to support the long context length of 8192. The model first undergoes masked language modeling (MLM) pre-training on the c4-en dataset, followed by weak-supervised contrastive pre-training (CPT) and supervised contrastive fine-tuning. The MLM pre-training includes stages with different learning rates, batch sizes, and sequence lengths. The model is evaluated on the MTEB and LoCo benchmarks, achieving high scores across various tasks. The gte-v1.5 model is available for use with the transformers and sentence-transformers libraries, and can be accessed via the model path 'Alibaba-NLP/gte-base-en-v1.5'. It is recommended to install xformers and enable unpadding for acceleration. The model can be used for tasks such as text embedding generation, similarity computation, and retrieval. The model is described as a SOTA instruction-tuned multi-lingual embedding model that ranked 2nd in MTEB and 1st in C-MTEB.

instruction: Can you walk me through how to solve this algebra problem step by step?
description: gpt-oss-120b-Distill-Phi-4-14B is a GRPO inference test model based on the microsoft/phi-4 (14B) base model. It is fine-tuned through a three-stage training pipeline—SFT (Cold Start) → RLHF (GRPO) → SFT—aiming to achieve excellent multi-step reasoning capabilities. The core methodology of this model is derived from three papers: DeepSeek-R1 (Nature, vol. 645), DeepSeekMath (arXiv:2402.03300v3), and Phi-4-reasoning (arXiv:2504.21318). Unlike the Microsoft paper, the reinforcement learning-guided reasoning part is moved to an earlier stage, using the SFT + GRPO + SFT method to generate explicit Chain-of-Thought (CoT), attempting to induce reasoning abilities in the Phi-4 Instruct model through reinforcement learning and supervised learning. This model uses a distilled dataset from the open-source model gpt-oss-120b as its fine-tuning knowledge base, thereby inheriting its profound reasoning style and knowledge system. The model's training pipeline includes three stages: Foundational Supervised Fine-Tuning (Cold Start), Reinforcement Learning with GRPO, and Post-RL SFT (Alignment and Capability Consolidation). The GRPO algorithm is an advanced RLHF algorithm that estimates the advantage function by comparing the relative merits of a group of candidate outputs, achieving more stable policy gradient updates. The reward system includes multiple weighted rewards and penalty signals to shape the model's behavior, covering core objective rewards, quality and alignment rewards, behavioral regularization and penalties, and exploration and diversity incentives. The model's performance includes the ability to perform logical reasoning, mathematical problem-solving, and role-playing debates, with demonstrated capabilities in general chat, knowledge Q&A, adversarial reasoning, and mathematical reasoning. Limitations include limited training data and resources, potential language mixing in reasoning processes, and reduced expressive richness in broader open-domain conversations due to domain-specific training. The model is intended as a reference test model and is not a fully optimized reasoning model.

instruction: Which quantizations are described as highly compressed while still maintaining reasonable quality?
description: This is a set of imatrix quantizations of the Voxtral-Mini-3B-2507 model by mistralai, using the llama.cpp release b6014 for quantization. The model is designed to be run using llama.cpp or any other llama.cpp-based project. The quantizations vary in file size, quality, and performance characteristics, with options ranging from full BF16 weights to highly compressed formats such as IQ2_M. Some quantizations use Q8_0 for embedding and output weights to maintain higher quality. The model supports a variety of quantization types, including Q8_0, Q6_K_L, Q6_K, Q5_K_L, Q5_K_M, Q5_K_S, Q4_K_L, Q4_1, Q3_K_XL, Q4_K_M, Q4_K_S, Q4_0, IQ4_NL, IQ4_XS, Q3_K_L, Q3_K_M, Q2_K_L, IQ3_M, Q3_K_S, IQ3_XS, IQ3_XXS, Q2_K, and IQ2_M. Each quantization is described with its quality level, file size, and whether it is recommended for use. Some quantizations, such as Q4_0, support online repacking for improved performance on ARM and AVX hardware. The model can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool, with instructions provided for downloading specific files or multiple files if the model exceeds 50GB in size. Performance benchmarks are available for an AVX2 system, showing the tokens per second for various quantizations and tasks. The model can be run on CPU or GPU, with recommendations for choosing a quantization based on available RAM and VRAM. I-quants (IQX_X) are newer and offer better performance for their size when used with cuBLAS or rocBLAS, but may be slower than K-quants on CPU. The model includes credits to contributors for assistance with the imatrix calibration dataset, inspiration for embedding/output experiments, and sponsorship support.

instruction: Can you give a breakdown potential estimate for the compound with SMILES C1COC(=O)C1?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict biodegradability potential, which classifies whether a compound is readily biodegradable in the environment. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. The model type is AttentiveFP, and it performs a regression task, producing a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and features an architecture based on AttentiveFP with enhanced atom and bond features. The model's hyperparameters include a hidden channel size of 64, two layers, two timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.2, a learning rate of 0.001, a weight decay of 1e-05, a batch size of 32, 50 epochs, and a patience of 10. The model was trained on the tbiodeg dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: Is it possible to track multiple people in a video using just a few initial prompts?
description: SAM 2 is a foundation model designed for promptable visual segmentation in images and videos, developed by FAIR. It supports a wide range of segmentation tasks, including automatic mask generation, image segmentation using point clicks, bounding box inputs, and multi-object segmentation. The model can process both single images and batches of images, allowing for efficient inference across multiple inputs. It also supports video segmentation and tracking, enabling the tracking of objects across video frames with the ability to refine segmentation through additional prompts. SAM 2 can use masks from previous predictions as input to improve segmentation accuracy. The model is built to handle complex scenarios, such as batched images with multiple objects and multiple points per object, as well as batched bounding boxes. It supports streaming video inference for real-time applications and can track multiple objects simultaneously in video by adding them all at once. The model is implemented using PyTorch and integrates with the Hugging Face Transformers library for ease of use. Performance examples include generating 39 masks for an image using automatic mask generation and tracking 2 objects through 180 frames in a video. The model is described in the paper titled 'SAM 2: Segment Anything in Images and Videos' by Ravi et al., published in 2024.

instruction: Generate a 5-second clip of a child blowing out birthday candles on a cake with a smile on their face.
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video (TI2V) generation. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity without increasing computational cost. Wan2.2 is trained on significantly larger data, with 65.6% more images and 83.2% more videos than its predecessor, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. The model includes a 5B version with a 16×16×4 compression ratio, supporting both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps, and is capable of running on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090. The T2V-A14B model supports generating 5-second videos at 480P and 720P resolutions. It delivers outstanding video generation quality and surpasses leading commercial models on the Wan-Bench 2.0 benchmark. The model is available for download via Hugging Face and ModelScope, and supports both single-GPU and multi-GPU inference using FSDP and DeepSpeed Ulysses. Prompt extension is available via Dashscope API or local Qwen models to enhance video quality. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license terms and applicable laws.

instruction: Can you determine the main type of motion in this recorded scene?
description: The model is a variant that has been fine-tuned for multi-label video classification, specifically for camera motion classification on an internal dataset. It is designed to predict 18 different camera motion classes, including 'arc_left', 'arc_right', 'dolly_in', 'dolly_out', 'pan_left', 'pan_right', 'pedestal_down', 'pedestal_up', 'roll_left', 'roll_right', 'static', 'tilt_down', 'tilt_up', 'truck_left', 'truck_right', 'undefined', 'zoom_in', and 'zoom_out', as well as 3 shot type classes: 'pov', 'shake', and 'track'. The model is trained to associate the entire video with camera motion labels, rather than frame-level motions, meaning it outputs one or more labels for the whole video. If a camera motion is not present throughout the entire video, the model should predict 'undefined'. The model processes 16 frames per input clip, extracted uniformly from the video, regardless of its original duration. For videos longer than 2 seconds, it is recommended to split the video into non-overlapping 2-second segments, run inference on each segment independently, and then post-process the results. The model achieves an accuracy of 75% on an internal test dataset of 2-second videos, and approximately 84% when excluding the 'pov', 'shake', and 'track' classes.

instruction: Could you detect and outline all the bottles on the shelf in this image?
description: The Grounding DINO model was proposed by Shilong Liu, Zhaoyang Zeng, Tianhe Ren, Feng Li, Hao Zhang, Jie Yang, Chunyuan Li, Jianwei Yang, Hang Su, Jun Zhu, and Lei Zhang. Grounding DINO extends a closed-set object detection model with a text encoder, enabling open-set object detection. The model achieves remarkable results, such as 52.5 AP on COCO zero-shot. You can use the raw model for zero-shot object detection, which is the task of detecting things in an image out-of-the-box without labeled data.

instruction: What kind of setting is this image taken in?
description: BLIP-2 is a model that consists of three components: a CLIP-like image encoder, a Querying Transformer (Q-Former), and a large language model with 2.7 billion parameters. The image encoder and large language model are initialized from pre-trained checkpoints and remain frozen during training, while the Q-Former, a BERT-like Transformer encoder, is trained to map a set of 'query tokens' to query embeddings that bridge the embedding spaces of the image encoder and the language model. The model is designed to predict the next text token given the query embeddings and the previous text. It supports tasks such as image captioning, visual question answering (VQA), and chat-like conversations by providing the image and previous conversation as input. The model can be used for conditional text generation given an image and optional text. BLIP-2 uses an off-the-shelf OPT model as its language model, which inherits the same risks and limitations as described in Meta's model card, including potential biases, safety issues, and generation quality concerns. The model is fine-tuned on image-text datasets collected from the internet, which may result in the model generating inappropriate content or replicating biases present in the training data. It has not been tested in real-world applications and should not be directly deployed without careful evaluation of its safety and fairness in the intended context. This release is for research purposes only and is not designed or evaluated for all downstream applications. Users are advised to evaluate and address potential concerns related to accuracy, safety, and fairness before deployment. The model's memory requirements vary depending on the precision used, with options including float32, float16/bfloat16, int8, and int4. The model can be run on CPU or GPU, with different configurations for full, half, and 8-bit precision.

instruction: I want to analyze the depth of a bridge in my photo—can you provide a detailed map?
description: DepthPro is a foundation model for zero-shot metric monocular depth estimation, designed to generate high-resolution depth maps with remarkable sharpness and fine-grained details. It employs a multi-scale Vision Transformer (ViT)-based architecture, where images are downsampled, divided into patches, and processed using a shared Dinov2 encoder. The extracted patch-level features are merged, upsampled, and refined using a DPT-like fusion stage, enabling precise depth estimation. The model synthesizes high-resolution depth maps with unparalleled sharpness and high-frequency details. The predictions are metric, with absolute scale, without relying on the availability of metadata such as camera intrinsics. The model is fast, producing a 2.25-megapixel depth map in 0.3 seconds on a standard GPU. These characteristics are enabled by an efficient multi-scale vision transformer for dense prediction, a training protocol that combines real and synthetic datasets to achieve high metric accuracy alongside fine boundary tracing, dedicated evaluation metrics for boundary accuracy in estimated depth maps, and state-of-the-art focal length estimation from a single image. The model was developed by Aleksei Bochkovskii, Amaël Delaunoy, Hugo Germain, Marcel Santos, Yichao Zhou, Stephan R. Richter, and Vladlen Koltun. It is licensed under the Apple-ASCL license. The model uses a DepthProEncoder for encoding the input image and a FeatureFusionStage for fusing the output features from the encoder. The DepthProEncoder further uses two encoders: a patch_encoder and an image_encoder. The input image is scaled with multiple ratios, split into smaller patches of size patch_size with overlapping areas, and processed by the patch_encoder. The input image is also rescaled to patch_size and processed by the image_encoder. Both encoders can be configured via patch_model_config and image_model_config, both of which are separate Dinov2Model by default. Outputs from both encoders (last_hidden_state) and selected intermediate states (hidden_states) from the patch_encoder are fused by a DPT-based FeatureFusionStage for depth estimation. The network is supplemented with a focal length estimation head. A small convolutional head ingests frozen features from the depth estimation network and task-specific features from a separate ViT image encoder to predict the horizontal angular field-of-view. Images are preprocessed by being rescaled by 1/225, normalized with mean=[0.5, 0.5, 0.5] and std=[0.5, 0.5, 0.5], and resized to 1536x1536 pixels.

instruction: Is it possible to reconstruct a 3D shape of a toy from a few reference images?
description: Hunyuan3D-2mv is a model finetuned to support multiview controlled shape generation. It is designed to create and manipulate 3D assets based on input images from multiple views, such as front, left, and back. The model uses a diffusion-based approach and is intended for high-resolution textured 3D asset generation. It is part of the Hunyuan3D 2.0 system, which scales diffusion models for this purpose. The model is described as a tool for realizing the imagination of creating and manipulating 3D assets. It is implemented using a pipeline that accepts multiple image inputs and generates a 3D mesh as output. The model is reported to be part of research detailed in an academic paper titled 'Hunyuan3D 2.0: Scaling Diffusion Models for High Resolution Textured 3D Assets Generation' and is associated with the Tencent Hunyuan3D Team.

instruction: Can you recognize and highlight all the birds in this backyard photo?
description: YOLOv13 is a real-time object detection model designed to improve detection performance in complex scenarios by capturing global multi-to-multi high-order correlations. It introduces a Hypergraph-based Adaptive Correlation Enhancement (HyperACE) mechanism that adaptively exploits latent high-order correlations, overcoming the limitations of previous methods that were restricted to pairwise correlation modeling. YOLOv13 also features a Full-Pipeline Aggregation-and-Distribution (FullPAD) paradigm, which distributes correlation-enhanced features across the entire network to achieve fine-grained information flow and representation synergy. Additionally, the model uses depthwise separable convolutions to replace large-kernel convolutions, significantly reducing parameters and computational complexity without sacrificing performance. YOLOv13 includes four variants: Nano, Small, Large, and X-Large. Experimental results on the MS COCO benchmark show that YOLOv13-N improves mAP by 3.0% over YOLO11-N and by 1.5% over YOLOv12-N. The model is available in multiple formats and supports training, validation, prediction, and export to ONNX or TensorRT. The code and models are open-sourced and can be accessed at the provided URL.

instruction: I want a summary of this technical specification document, with each statement traceable to its source for clarity.
description: sui-1 (Summarization with Unique Identifiers) is a specialized model for high-quality summarization of very long texts with built-in source grounding. Every claim in the summary can be traced back to its source sentence, enabling verification and reducing hallucination risk. The model handles up to 128k tokens natively, with a two-step iterative approach for documents up to 2 million tokens. The FP8 variant runs on a single A100 40GB or A6000 48GB GPU, and the iterative approach enables deployment on even more modest hardware. sui-1 significantly outperforms all tested open-weight baselines, including models with 3x more parameters. It is fine-tuned for English, German, Spanish, French, and Italian, and inherits 20+ additional languages from Mistral Small 3.2. The model is built using a sophisticated data generation pipeline that produced 22,000+ training examples from parliamentary documents, web sources, and Wikipedia using chain-of-thought reasoning with multi-stage verification. It features a built-in citation mechanism that links each claim to its source sentence for full traceability. The model follows a three-phase approach: a planning phase that analyzes the input and plans the summary structure, a reference selection phase that identifies the most important sentences to cite, and a grounded generation phase that produces a summary with inline citations to source sentences. Citations use XML tags assigned during preprocessing, enabling deterministic verification of each claim. The input text must be preprocessed with XML sentence tags, each sentence wrapped in a unique 8-character hexadecimal tag. The model outputs a JSON object with three keys: 'structure', 'xml_tags', and 'summary'. The 'structure' key contains internal reasoning about the content and planned summary structure, the 'xml_tags' key is a list of XML tags cited in the summary, and the 'summary' key is the actual summary with inline XML tag references. The model supports a 128k token context window natively and an iterative approach for longer documents. For documents exceeding the context limit, a two-step iterative approach is used that preserves grounding quality. The first step involves creating partial summaries of document chunks, and the second step merges these into a final summary. The iterative approach maintains source grounding through careful XML tag propagation, ensuring that every claim in the final summary traces back to a specific source sentence. The model provides two variants: bfloat16 (full precision, ~48GB weights) and FP8 (quantized, ~24GB weights). The FP8 version preserves high quality, scoring 81.05% overall on the benchmark, nearly identical to bfloat16. The bfloat16 variant requires ~55GB VRAM for 8k context, scaling to ~76GB for 128k. The FP8 variant requires ~38GB for 8k and ~50GB for 128k. The model is evaluated using an LLM-as-a-Judge methodology, where a strong judge model evaluates summary quality across multiple criteria, including factual accuracy, coverage and completeness, specificity and informativeness, format compliance, and custom instruction adherence. The evaluation was conducted on 100 diverse test samples covering multiple languages and document types, with scoring using binary pass/fail per criterion, aggregated to success rates. Grounding quality is validated using structural checks for tag uniqueness, tag validity, and tag usage. The model is a contribution to the open-source community and is released under the Apache 2.0 license. It has limitations, including the requirement for preprocessing of input text with XML tags, a maximum single-pass context of 128k tokens, and occasional JSON output parsing failures, for which retry logic is recommended in production use.

instruction: What does this image of a Japanese sign say? Please extract the text.
description: PP-OCRv5_server_rec is one of the PP-OCRv5_rec models, which are the latest generation text line recognition models developed by the PaddleOCR team. It is designed to efficiently and accurately support the recognition of four major languages—Simplified Chinese, Traditional Chinese, English, and Japanese—as well as complex text scenarios such as handwriting, vertical text, pinyin, and rare characters using a single model. The model achieves key accuracy metrics across various text recognition tasks, including 0.5807 for Handwritten Chinese, 0.5806 for Handwritten English, 0.9013 for Printed Chinese, 0.8679 for Printed English, 0.7472 for Traditional Chinese, 0.6039 for Ancient Text, 0.7372 for Japanese, 0.5946 for General Scenario, 0.8384 for Pinyin, 0.7435 for Rotation, 0.9314 for Distortion, 0.6397 for Artistic Text, and an overall average of 0.8401. These metrics are calculated by marking an entire line as incorrect if any character, including punctuation, is wrong, ensuring higher accuracy in practical applications. The model can be used for text recognition tasks through the PaddleOCR framework, either as a standalone module or as part of an OCR pipeline that includes document image orientation classification, text image unwarping, text line orientation classification, text detection, and text recognition modules. It supports integration into projects via Python code and command-line tools, and it can be used with GPU devices for model inference. The model is also compatible with the PP-StructureV3 pipeline for layout analysis, which includes layout detection, general OCR, document image preprocessing, table recognition, seal recognition, and formula recognition modules. The default model used in the PP-StructureV3 pipeline is PP-OCRv5_server_rec, and users can also specify a local model file if needed.

instruction: Can you enhance the clarity of this low-light photo of a person while reducing noise?
description: The model is a work in progress, with checkpoints being uploaded incrementally. The current best version uses LoRA A: qwen-edit-enhance_64-v3_000001000 and LoRA B: qwen-edit-enhance_000004250. The upcoming V4 candidates are LoRA A: /1000 and LoRA B: /4750. LoRA A and LoRA B are intended to be applied sequentially. The model is used in a Comfy workflow and includes a demonstration available on 🤗. The model is designed to enhance image quality and generate a detailed textual description of the scene, with more descriptive prompts leading to better upscale effects. All images shown are generated using an 8-step Lighting LoRA. The ModelSamplingAuraFlow is required, and the shift parameter must remain below 0.3; for higher resolutions, such as image 3, it can be set as low as 0.02. Recommended samplers include LCM (best), Euler_Ancestral, and Euler. All schedulers are compatible and produce varying results in terms of smoothness. The model was trained on a filtered subset of Unsplash-Lite and UltraHR-100K datasets at 2.4MP resolution. The training style focuses on photography, with subjects including landscapes, architecture, interiors, portraits, plants, vehicles, abstract photos, man-made objects, and food. The model is trained to recover from low-resolution images up to 16x, oversharpened images, noise up to 50%, Gaussian blur with a radius up to 3px, JPEG artifacts with quality as low as 5%, motion blur up to 64px, pixelation up to 16x, and color banding up to 3 bits. It is also trained to recover images that have been upscaled by other models up to 16x. The model is not trained on 2D images, illustrations, artworks, text, graphics, or CGI.

instruction: Can you generate a response based on a mix of text and audio input?
description: Gemma 3n is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models developed by Google, built using the same research and technology as the Gemini models. These models are designed for efficient execution on low-resource devices and are capable of handling multimodal input, including text, image, video, and audio, to generate text outputs. They are available in pre-trained and instruction-tuned variants and were trained on data in over 140 spoken languages. Gemma 3n models use selective parameter activation technology to reduce resource requirements, allowing them to operate at an effective size of 2B and 4B parameters, which is lower than the total number of parameters they contain. The models are available in two sizes, with the raw parameter count of this model being 6B, but the architecture design allows it to be run with a memory footprint comparable to a traditional 2B model by offloading low-utilization matrices from the accelerator. They use a MatFormer architecture that allows nesting sub-models within the model. The models support a total input context of 32K tokens and can generate outputs up to 32K tokens, subtracting the request input tokens. The training dataset includes a wide variety of sources totaling approximately 11 trillion tokens, with a knowledge cutoff date of June 2024. The dataset includes web documents, code, mathematics, images, and audio. Rigorous data filtering methods were applied, including CSAM filtering, sensitive data filtering, and additional methods based on content quality and safety. The models were trained using TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e hardware, leveraging JAX and ML Pathways software for efficient training. Evaluation results show that the models perform well across a range of benchmarks, including reasoning, factuality, multilingual tasks, STEM and code, and additional benchmarks. The models were evaluated for safety and ethics, including child safety, content safety, and representational harms. They showed safe levels of performance relative to previous Gemma models, with minimal policy violations and significant improvements in high severity violations. The models have certain limitations, including potential biases in training data, challenges with complex tasks, difficulties with language ambiguity and nuance, and potential factual inaccuracies. Ethical considerations include the risk of perpetuating biases, generating harmful content, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. The models are intended for a wide range of applications, including content creation, research, and education. They provide high-performance open generative model implementations designed for responsible AI development.

instruction: How might a robot determine the safest path to reach a target in a cluttered room?
description: KAIROS-MM-Qwen2.5-VL-7B-SFT is a multimodal reasoning model based on Qwen2.5-VL, designed to enable robots and vision AI agents to reason about the real world using physics understanding, prior knowledge, and common sense. The model understands space, time, and fundamental physical principles, and is well suited for planning, decision making, and long horizon video reasoning in embodied and agentic systems. It serves as a planning and reasoning backbone for embodied agents, allowing them to infer what actions to take next based on visual observations, temporal context, and physical constraints.

instruction: Create a multi-view sequence of a modern chair, making sure all angles match in design and texture.
description: MV-AR is a diffusion-based model designed for generating multi-view consistent images. It addresses the limitations of existing methods that rely on a specific reference view for predicting subsequent views, which can lead to poor image quality and inconsistency when there is minimal overlap between the reference and predicted views. MV-AR instead conditions on the preceding view with significant overlap, improving the quality and consistency of the generated multi-view images. The model supports tasks such as text-to-multi-view image generation, image-to-multi-view image generation, and text-plus-geometry-to-multi-view image generation. The model is based on prior work, and its implementation is available for reproduction with specific pretrained models and datasets. Generated images are saved in designated directories following execution of provided shell scripts. The model's development and methodology are described in an arXiv preprint titled 'Auto-Regressively Generating Multi-View Consistent Images' by Hu, JiaKui and others.

instruction: What could the linear learning rate scheduler imply for the training dynamics?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of an unspecified base model on an unknown dataset. It achieves a loss of 5.1124 on the evaluation set. The model was trained using the following hyperparameters: learning rate of 5e-05, training batch size of 4, evaluation batch size of 8, random seed of 42, Adam optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon of 1e-08, linear learning rate scheduler, and 20 training epochs. During training, the model's training loss and validation loss were recorded at various steps and epochs. The training process was implemented using Transformers 4.44.2, PyTorch 2.5.0+cu121, Datasets 3.1.0, and Tokenizers 0.19.1. More information is needed regarding the model's intended uses, limitations, and the training and evaluation data.

instruction: I have an image of a document. Can you analyze its layout and generate a Markdown version?
description: NuMarkdown-8B-Thinking is the first reasoning OCR vision-language model (VLM) designed to convert documents into clean Markdown files, particularly suitable for RAG applications. It generates thinking tokens to analyze the layout of a document before producing the final Markdown output. The model excels at handling documents with unusual layouts and complex tables. The number of thinking tokens can range from 20% to 500% of the final answer, depending on the complexity of the task. It is a fine-tune of Qwen 2.5-VL-7B, trained on synthetic examples of documents followed by a reinforcement learning phase (GRPO) using a layout-centric reward. NuMarkdown-8B-Thinking outperforms generic non-reasoning models like GPT-4o and specialized OCR models like OCRFlux, and is competitive with large reasoning closed-source models like Gemini 2.5. In an arena ranking using the trueskill-2 system with approximately 500 anonymized votes, it ranks second with a μ of 26.10 and σ of 0.79, just behind gemini-flash-reasoning. The model is particularly effective for complex document-to-markdown tasks and is intended for use in scenarios requiring structured document conversion. A planned markdown arena will provide a tool for evaluating different solutions in this domain. The training process includes a single epoch of supervised fine-tuning on synthetic reasoning traces from public PDFs, followed by a reinforcement learning phase using a layout-centric reward with challenging image examples. The model is designed to handle headers, bullet points, tables with merged cells, footers, and multi-line content within cells, ensuring accurate and well-structured Markdown output. It is intended for use in educational and administrative settings where document conversion is required, such as for student and family information sheets. The model supports both vLLM and Hugging Face Transformers for deployment, with specific instructions for serving and inference provided.

instruction: I need a few different snowflake images for a science fair display—can you generate them?
description: This model is an unconditional DDPM U-Net (EMA) trained from scratch to generate images of snowflakes. It operates on latent representations produced by a pre-trained Variational Autoencoder (VAE), which is not part of this model. The model learns the visual structure of real snowflakes from macro photography, enabling the generation of novel, synthetic snowflake images that resemble real crystalline forms. The input to the model is Gaussian noise, and the output consists of snowflake images after VAE decoding, with a resolution of 512×512. The model is used in the open-source application Snowflakeizer for snowflake image generation. It was trained on a dataset of macro photos of real snowflakes by Alexey Kljatov, which is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution (CC BY). The model weights are licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0). When using this model, proper attribution to the original dataset author is required. The model is intended for research in generative modeling, image synthesis experiments, artistic and creative applications, and educational purposes related to diffusion models. It has limitations, including being trained on a single-domain dataset of snowflake macro photography, and generated images may not correspond to physically accurate crystal growth processes. The model may also produce artifacts or unrealistic structures. The training data consists of non-sensitive macro photographs of natural objects, with no known privacy, biometric, or identity-related concerns. Generated images should not be presented as real scientific observations.

instruction: Is it possible to infer the camera's position based on a set of images taken from different angles of a building?
description: DA3 Large is a model for multi-view depth estimation and camera pose estimation, designed as a foundation model with a unified depth-ray representation. It is part of the Any-view Model series, contains 0.35B parameters, and is licensed under Apache 2.0. The model supports relative depth estimation, pose estimation, and pose conditioning. It is developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is based on a vision transformer architecture for visual geometry, using a plain transformer with a unified depth-ray representation.
