The model is trained exclusively on public academic datasets. A single plain transformer, such as a vanilla DINO encoder, is used as the backbone without architectural specialization. The depth-ray representation allows the model to perform without the need for complex multi-task learning. The model significantly outperforms Depth Anything 2 for monocular depth estimation and VGGT for multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it is trained on academic datasets and may have limitations when applied to domain-specific images. Performance can also vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity.

instruction: What quantizations are considered to be of the lowest quality and why?
description: This model provides various quantized versions of a neural network, optimized for different use cases and performance requirements. The available quantizations include IQ1_S, IQ1_M, IQ2_XXS, IQ2_XS, IQ2_S, IQ2_M, Q2_K_S, Q2_K, IQ3_XXS, IQ3_XS, Q3_K_S, IQ3_S, IQ3_M, Q3_K_M, Q3_K_L, IQ4_XS, Q4_0, IQ4_NL, Q4_K_S, Q4_K_M, Q4_1, Q5_K_S, Q5_K_M, and Q6_K. These quants vary in file size from 1.3 GB to 3.7 GB and are associated with different quality and performance characteristics. For example, the Q4_K_S quant is described as optimal in terms of size, speed, and quality, while the Q4_K_M is noted as fast and recommended. Some quants, such as IQ1_S and IQ1_M, are described as suitable only for desperate use cases, and others like Q2_K_S and IQ3_XXS are noted for their low quality. The model is intended for users who require lightweight and efficient inference, and it supports concatenation of multi-part files for use. The model quants are made available through a combination of company resources and private computing infrastructure provided by a collaborator.

instruction: Show me a video of a cat playing with a ball of yarn in a cozy living room.
description: The FastWan2.2-TI2V-5B-FullAttn-Diffusers model is part of the FastWan2.2 series, which is finetuned using a novel Sparse-distill strategy. This strategy combines DMD and VSA in a single training process, leveraging distillation to shorten diffusion steps and sparse attention to reduce attention computations, enabling faster video generation. The model is built upon Wan-AI/Wan2.2-TI2V-5B-Diffusers and supports efficient 3-step inference, producing high-quality videos at 121×704×1280 resolution. The model is trained using simulated forward for the generator model, making the training process data-free. The current FastWan2.2-TI2V-5B-FullAttn-Diffusers model is trained using only DMD. The model is trained on 121×704×1280 resolution but can generate videos with any resolution, although quality may degrade. Training was conducted on 8 nodes with 64 H200 GPUs in total, using a global batch size of 64, and training runs for 3000 steps, which takes approximately 12 hours. If the model is used for research, the associated papers should be cited.

instruction: Are there any known compatibility issues when using the IQ4_XS quant on AMD hardware?
description: This model card describes imatrix quantizations of the LLaMA-Mesh model, using the llama.cpp release b4132 for quantization. The model is available in various quantization formats, each with different file sizes, quality levels, and performance characteristics. The quantizations are generated using the imatrix option with a dataset provided by an unspecified source. Some quantizations use Q8_0 for embedding and output weights to maintain higher quality. The model supports a range of quant types, including f16, Q8_0, Q6_K_L, Q6_K, Q5_K_L, Q5_K_M, Q5_K_S, Q4_K_L, Q4_K_M, Q3_K_XL, Q4_K_S, Q4_0, Q4_0_8_8, Q4_0_4_8, Q4_0_4_4, IQ4_XS, Q3_K_L, Q3_K_M, IQ3_M, Q2_K_L, Q3_K_S, IQ3_XS, Q2_K, and IQ2_M. Each quant type is described with its file size, quality level, and specific recommendations or caveats. For example, Q6_K and Q6_K_L are described as very high quality and recommended, while Q4_0_8_8 is optimized for ARM and AVX inference and should not be used on Mac. The Q4_0_4_4 quant is optimized for ARM inference and should not be used with GPUs. The model can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool, with instructions provided for targeting specific files and handling large models split into multiple files. The Q4_0_X_X quantizations are not suitable for Metal (Apple) or GPU offloading and are intended for ARM chips and certain AVX2/AVX512 CPUs. On AVX2 systems, the Q4_0_8_8 quant offers improved performance for prompt processing and text generation compared to the standard Q4_0 quant. When choosing a quantization, users should consider their available RAM and VRAM, as well as their preference for speed versus quality. K-quants, such as Q5_K_M, are recommended for general use, while I-quants, such as IQ3_M, offer better performance for their size but may be slower on CPU and Apple Metal. I-quants are not compatible with Vulcan on AMD hardware. The model includes credits to kalomaze and Dampf for assistance in creating the imatrix calibration dataset and to ZeroWw for inspiration in experimenting with embedding and output weights.

instruction: Can you show the scene from a 45-degree left angle and a very close distance?
description: Qwen-Image-Edit-2511-Multiple-Angles-LoRA is a multi-angle camera control LoRA designed for the Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 model. It provides 96 precise camera positions, derived from 4 elevations, 8 azimuths, and 3 distances. The model was trained on over 3000 Gaussian Splatting renders, which are high-quality 3D-consistent images, to enable better spatial understanding and camera control. It supports low-angle camera positions as low as -30°, making it suitable for ground-level and low camera shots. The LoRA enhances the built-in viewpoint capabilities of Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 by offering exact control over camera position, with a large dataset ensuring maximum accuracy. The model is intended for use with the exact prompt format of [azimuth] [elevation] [distance], and it is recommended to use a LoRA strength between 0.8 and 1.0 for optimal results. The model was built by Lovis Odin at fal using synthetic 3D renders with precise camera control. For best results, clear input images with good lighting are recommended, and users are advised to follow the specified prompt order and experiment with low-angle shots, where the model excels.

instruction: What are the potential risks of using this system for content generation?
description: Gemma 3n is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models developed by Google, built using the same research and technology as the Gemini models. These models are designed for efficient execution on low-resource devices and are capable of handling multimodal input, including text, image, video, and audio, to generate text outputs. They are available in pre-trained and instruction-tuned variants and were trained on data in over 140 spoken languages. Gemma 3n models use selective parameter activation technology to reduce resource requirements, allowing them to operate at an effective size of 2B and 4B parameters, even though the raw parameter count of the model is 8B. This is achieved by offloading low-utilization matrices from the accelerator, resulting in a memory footprint comparable to a traditional 4B model. The models also use a MatFormer architecture that allows nesting sub-models within the E4B model. One sub-model is provided, or users can access a spectrum of custom-sized models. The models support a total input context of 32K tokens and can generate outputs up to 32K tokens in length. They are trained on a dataset totaling approximately 11 trillion tokens, with a knowledge cutoff date of June 2024. The training data includes web documents, code, mathematics, images, and audio, ensuring a broad range of linguistic styles, topics, and formats. Rigorous data filtering methods were applied, including CSAM filtering, sensitive data filtering, and additional content quality and safety filtering. Gemma 3n models were trained using TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e hardware, leveraging JAX and ML Pathways software for efficient training. Evaluation results show strong performance across a variety of benchmarks, including reasoning, factuality, multilingual tasks, STEM and code, and additional metrics. The models demonstrated safe levels of performance in child safety, content safety, and representational harms evaluations, with minimal policy violations and significant improvements over previous Gemma models. However, the models have limitations, including potential biases from training data, challenges with complex or open-ended tasks, difficulties with language ambiguity and nuance, and the risk of generating incorrect or outdated factual statements. Ethical considerations include the potential for bias, misinformation, misuse, and privacy violations. Developers are encouraged to implement content safety safeguards, adhere to privacy regulations, and use the models responsibly. The models are intended for a wide range of applications, including content creation, research, education, and data extraction. They offer superior performance compared to other similarly sized open models, making them a high-performance option for responsible AI development.

instruction: Can you translate this paragraph from English to Spanish while keeping the formatting intact?
description: The Hunyuan Translation Model Version 1.5 includes two models: HY-MT1.5-1.8B, a 1.8B parameter translation model, and HY-MT1.5-7B, a 7B parameter translation model. Both models support mutual translation across 33 languages and incorporate 5 ethnic and dialect variations. HY-MT1.5-7B is an upgraded version of the WMT25 championship model, optimized for explanatory translation and mixed-language scenarios, with added support for terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. Despite having less than one-third the parameters of HY-MT1.5-7B, HY-MT1.5-1.8B delivers translation performance comparable to its larger counterpart, achieving both high speed and high quality. After quantization, the 1.8B model can be deployed on edge devices and support real-time translation scenarios, making it widely applicable. HY-MT1.5-1.8B achieves industry-leading performance among models of the same size, surpassing most commercial translation APIs. HY-MT1.5-1.8B supports deployment on edge devices and real-time translation scenarios, offering broad applicability. HY-MT1.5-7B, compared to its September open-source version, has been optimized for annotated and mixed-language scenarios. Both models support terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. The models support translation between Chinese and other languages, as well as among multiple languages, including English, French, Portuguese, Spanish, Japanese, Turkish, Russian, Arabic, Korean, Thai, Italian, German, Vietnamese, Malay, Indonesian, Filipino, Hindi, Traditional Chinese, Polish, Czech, Dutch, Khmer, Burmese, Persian, Gujarati, Urdu, Telugu, Marathi, Hebrew, Bengali, Tamil, Ukrainian, Tibetan, Kazakh, Mongolian, Uyghur, and Cantonese. The models can be used with llama.cpp and ollama for inference, with recommended parameters including top_k, top_p, repetition_penalty, and temperature. The models are available in different quantization versions, including FP8 and Int4, and can be downloaded from Hugging Face.

instruction: What kind of environmental impact might this molecule have according to its structure?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict toxicity-related molecular properties. The model is designed to perform regression tasks, taking SMILES strings as input and using graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. It is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and features an architecture based on AttentiveFP with enhanced atom and bond features. The model uses 10-dimensional enhanced atom features and 6-dimensional enhanced bond features. The hyperparameters include 128 hidden channels, 3 layers, 3 timesteps, 0.1 dropout, 0.0005 learning rate, 0.0001 weight decay, 32 batch size, 50 epochs, and 10 patience. The model was trained on the TMP dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: Could you help me hear how this greeting will sound when spoken aloud? Please create an audio version.
description: Kyutai TTS is a streaming text-to-speech (TTS) model designed to generate audio as soon as the first few words of the input text are provided, rather than requiring the full text to produce audio. It is trained on a mix of public TTS datasets to enable fair comparisons with other methods. The model architecture is a hierarchical Transformer that processes tokenized text and generates audio tokenized by Mimi. The frame rate is 12.5 Hz, with each audio frame represented by 16 audio tokens, and the model cannot use fewer tokens during inference. The backbone model has 300M parameters, while the depth transformer has 450M parameters and uses partial weight sharing. The audio is shifted by 16 steps (1.28 seconds) relative to the text, and the model uses an acoustic/semantic delay of 2. Kyutai TTS is a decoder-only model for streaming speech-to-text, leveraging a multistream architecture to model the text stream based on the speech stream, with the text stream shifted relative to the audio stream to allow the model to predict text tokens based on the input audio. It supports streaming TTS generation and allows for voice cloning through prefixing, achieving a speaker similarity score of 74.9%, which is in line with some existing baselines like CSM. The model does not perform watermarking because watermarking can be easily deactivated for open source models, and early experiments show that all watermark systems used by existing TTS models are removed by simply encoding and decoding the audio with Mimi. The model is provided primarily for scientific comparisons on public benchmarks. It is developed by Kyutai, is a streaming text-to-speech model, supports English, and is licensed under CC-BY 4.0.

instruction: Create a vivid scene of a bustling market in an ancient city, including accurate depth information.
description: The LDM3D model was proposed in a 2023 paper authored by Gabriela Ben Melech Stan, Diana Wofk, Scottie Fox, Alex Redden, Will Saxton, Jean Yu, Estelle Aflalo, Shao-Yen Tseng, Fabio Nonato, Matthias Muller, and Vasudev Lal. LDM3D is a Latent Diffusion Model for 3D that generates both image and depth map data from a given text prompt, allowing users to generate RGBD images from text prompts. The model is fine-tuned on a dataset of tuples containing an RGB image, depth map, and caption, and validated through extensive experiments. The model was fine-tuned on a dataset constructed from a subset of the LAION-400M dataset, a large-scale image-caption dataset that contains over 400 million image-caption pairs. The fine-tuning process comprises two stages. In the first stage, an autoencoder is trained to generate a lower-dimensional, perceptually equivalent data representation. Subsequently, the diffusion model is fine-tuned using the frozen autoencoder. The model can be used to generate an RGB image and depth map given a text prompt. The LDM3D model is on par with the Stable Diffusion models with the same number of parameters (1.06B). On the 512 x 512-sized MS-COCO dataset with 50 DDIM steps, the model achieved a FID score of 27.82, an IS score of 28.79 ± 0.49, and a CLIP similarity score of 26.61 ± 2.92. In depth evaluation, LDM3D achieved an AbsRel of 0.0911 and an RMSE of 0.334, compared to DPT-Large, which achieved an AbsRel of 0.0779 and an RMSE of 0.297. The model has some limitations and biases, particularly in depth map generation, where the ground truth is produced using DPT-large, and other limitations and biases from that model are applicable. Users should be made aware of the risks, biases, and limitations of the model. The model is available for use with PyTorch on both CPU and GPU architectures. The license on this model does not constitute legal advice. Users are advised to consult an attorney before using this model for commercial purposes.

instruction: Can you search for an image that represents 'a crowded train during rush hour'?
description: The CLIP model was developed by researchers at OpenAI to study what contributes to robustness in computer vision tasks and to test the ability of models to generalize to arbitrary image classification tasks in a zero-shot manner. It was not developed for general model deployment, and researchers are advised to carefully study its capabilities in relation to the specific context of deployment. The model was developed in January 2021 and uses a ViT-B/32 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The original implementation had two variants: one using a ResNet image encoder and the other using a Vision Transformer. This repository contains the variant with the Vision Transformer. The model is intended for research use by AI researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. Any deployed use case of the model, whether commercial or not, is currently out of scope. Non-deployed use cases such as image search in a constrained environment are also not recommended unless there is thorough in-domain testing of the model with a specific, fixed class taxonomy. This is because the model's performance varies significantly with different class taxonomies, and untested deployment may be harmful. Use cases related to surveillance and facial recognition are always out of scope, regardless of model performance, due to the lack of testing norms and checks to ensure fair use. The model has not been purposefully trained or evaluated on any languages other than English, so its use should be limited to English language use cases. The model was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including data crawled from the internet and pre-existing image datasets. The data is more representative of people and societies most connected to the internet, which tend to skew towards more developed nations and younger, male users. The goal of the dataset was to test robustness and generalizability in computer vision tasks, and the data was gathered in a mostly non-interventionist manner, with crawling limited to websites that had policies against excessively violent and adult images. The dataset is not intended for use as the basis for any commercial or deployed model and will not be released. The model has been evaluated on a wide range of benchmarks across various computer vision datasets, including Food101, CIFAR10, CIFAR100, Birdsnap, SUN397, Stanford Cars, FGVC Aircraft, VOC2007, DTD, Oxford-IIIT Pet dataset, Caltech101, Flowers102, MNIST, SVHN, IIIT5K, Hateful Memes, SST-2, UCF101, Kinetics700, Country211, CLEVR Counting, KITTI Distance, STL-10, RareAct, Flickr30, MSCOCO, ImageNet, ImageNet-A, ImageNet-R, ImageNet Sketch, ObjectNet (ImageNet Overlap), Youtube-BB, and ImageNet-Vid. The model has several limitations, including struggles with fine-grained classification and counting objects. It also exhibits issues with fairness and bias, with performance and biases depending significantly on class design and category choices. Testing revealed significant disparities with respect to race and gender when classifying images of people into crime-related and non-human animal categories, with disparities shifting based on class construction. Evaluations on the Fairface dataset showed accuracy greater than 96% across all races for gender classification, with 'Middle Eastern' having the highest accuracy (98.4%) and 'White' the lowest (96.5%). CLIP averaged approximately 93% for racial classification and approximately 63% for age classification. These evaluations are used to assess model performance across demographics and surface potential risks, not to endorse such tasks. The approach to testing CLIP also has a limitation: in many cases, linear probes were used to evaluate performance, and there is evidence that linear probes can underestimate model performance.

instruction: Generate a realistic portrait of a person with a warm smile and a background of a summer garden.
description: Qwen-Image-2512-Turbo-LoRA is a 4 or 8-step turbo LoRA for Qwen Image 2512, trained by Wuli Team. This LoRA matches the original model's output quality but is over 20x faster, with 2x speed improvement from CFG-distillation and additional speed gains from a reduced number of inference steps. For users in the Chinese mainland, the model can be directly tried on the team's website, where four images with 2k resolution can be generated in just 5 seconds. The model has undergone several updates: on January 8, 2026, version 3.0 was released with further improved image color and details and is already ComfyUI compatible. On January 2, 2026, version 2.0 was released with improved image color and details and is also ComfyUI compatible. On December 31, 2025, version 1.0 and its ComfyUI compatible version were released, with plans to soon release version 2.0 with enhanced performance. The model can be used with the Diffsynth-Engine, which includes an example code for creating a pipeline, loading the turbo LoRA, changing the scheduler configuration, and sampling an image. One caveat is that generated image quality may degrade when handling extremely complex details, and users can increase the value of num_inference_steps to address this issue.

instruction: Animate this sketch of a spaceship to show it launching into space and passing through the atmosphere.
description: HunyuanVideo-1.5 is a lightweight video generation model with 8.3 billion parameters, designed to deliver state-of-the-art visual quality and motion coherence while enabling efficient inference on consumer-grade GPUs. It supports both text-to-video (T2V) and image-to-video (I2V) generation across multiple durations and resolutions. The model integrates an advanced DiT (Diffusion Transformer) architecture with a 3D causal VAE, achieving compression ratios of 16× in spatial dimensions and 4× along the temporal axis. A key innovation is the SSTA (Selective and Sliding Tile Attention) mechanism, which prunes redundant spatiotemporal key-value blocks, significantly reducing computational overhead for long video sequences and accelerating inference. HunyuanVideo-1.5 also features a video super-resolution network that enhances sharpness and corrects distortions, allowing outputs to be upscaled to 1080p. The model is trained using a multi-stage, progressive strategy that refines motion coherence, aesthetic quality, and human preference alignment. It supports various inference optimizations, including step-distilled models for faster generation (e.g., 8 or 12 steps for 480p I2V), sparse attention, and caching techniques like deepcache, teacache, and taylorcache. The model is available on Hugging Face Diffusers and supports integration with tools like ComfyUI and LightX2V. It is trained using the Muon optimizer, which accelerates convergence and improves training stability. Evaluation results show that HunyuanVideo-1.5 achieves professional-grade content generation and sets a new state-of-the-art among open-source models. The model is accessible for developers and creators, with code, weights, and training scripts open-sourced to lower the cost of video creation and research.

instruction: How accurate is the depth estimation for objects in the mid-ground of this image?
description: The DA3 Metric Large model is specialized for metric depth estimation in monocular settings, ideal for applications requiring real-world scale. It provides canonical metric depth, where multiplying by focal length gives metric depth. The model is part of the Monocular Metric Depth series, has 0.35B parameters, and is licensed under Apache 2.0. It supports relative depth estimation, metric depth estimation, and sky segmentation. The model is developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is based on a vision transformer for visual geometry. Its architecture uses a plain transformer with a unified depth-ray representation. The model is trained on public academic datasets only. A single plain transformer, such as a vanilla DINO encoder, is sufficient as a backbone without architectural specialization. A singular depth-ray representation eliminates the need for complex multi-task learning. The model significantly outperforms Depth Anything 2 for monocular depth estimation and VGGT for multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it is trained on academic datasets and may have limitations on certain domain-specific images. Performance may vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity.

instruction: Can you create an anime-style image of a futuristic city with glowing neon lights and flying vehicles?
description: NetaYume Lumina is a text-to-image model fine-tuned from a high-quality anime-style image generation model developed by an unspecified entity. It is based on an open-source base model released by the team at Shanghai AI Laboratory. The model was trained to generate both realistic human images and high-quality anime-style images, while retaining a significant amount of knowledge from its base model. Key features include the ability to generate detailed anime-style images with sharp outlines, vibrant colors, and smooth shading. It also demonstrates improved character understanding, particularly for characters from the Danbooru dataset, resulting in more coherent and accurate character representations. Additionally, it enhances the generation of fine details such as accessories, clothing textures, hairstyles, and background elements with greater clarity. The model file, NetaYume_Lumina_v2_all_in_one.safetensors, is an all-in-one file that includes the necessary weights for the VAE, text encoder, and image backbone for use with ComfyUI. The model uses a pre-trained Gemma-2-2b text encoder, a pre-trained Flux.1 dev's VAE, and a fine-tuned NetaLumina image backbone. It is recommended to use a system prompt to help generate desired images by aligning with user prompts. For anime-style images using Danbooru tags, the model is described as an assistant designed to generate anime images based on textual prompts and user prompts with Danbooru tags. Recommended settings for optimal performance include a CFG of 4–7, sampling steps of 40–50, and samplers such as Euler a with a normal scheduler or res_multistep with a linear_quadratic scheduler. The model acknowledges the use of the Danbooru dataset, the creators of a wonderful model, and a team for openly sharing an awesome model.

instruction: What are the safety measures in place to prevent harmful content generation with these models?
description: Gemma is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models from Google, built from the same research and technology used to create the Gemini models. Gemma 3 models are multimodal, handling text and image input and generating text output, with open weights for both pre-trained variants and instruction-tuned variants. Gemma 3 has a large, 128K context window, multilingual support in over 140 languages, and is available in more sizes than previous versions. Gemma 3 models are well-suited for a variety of text generation and image understanding tasks, including question answering, summarization, and reasoning. Their relatively small size makes it possible to deploy them in environments with limited resources such as laptops, desktops or your own cloud infrastructure, democratizing access to state of the art AI models and helping foster innovation for everyone. The input includes text strings such as questions, prompts, or documents to be summarized, and images normalized to 896 x 896 resolution and encoded to 256 tokens each. The total input context is 128K tokens for the 4B, 12B, and 27B sizes, and 32K tokens for the 1B size. The output includes generated text in response to the input, such as an answer to a question, analysis of image content, or a summary of a document, with a total output context of 8192 tokens. These models were trained on a dataset of text data that includes a wide variety of sources. The 27B model was trained with 14 trillion tokens, the 12B model with 12 trillion tokens, the 4B model with 4 trillion tokens, and the 1B model with 2 trillion tokens. The training dataset includes web documents, code, mathematics, and images. Web documents ensure the model is exposed to a broad range of linguistic styles, topics, and vocabulary, including content in over 140 languages. Code helps the model learn the syntax and patterns of programming languages, improving its ability to generate code and understand code-related questions. Mathematics helps the model learn logical reasoning, symbolic representation, and to address mathematical queries. Images enable the model to perform image analysis and visual data extraction tasks. Rigorous CSAM filtering was applied at multiple stages in the data preparation process to ensure the exclusion of harmful and illegal content. Automated techniques were used to filter out certain personal information and other sensitive data from training sets. Additional methods were applied for content quality and safety in line with safety policies. Gemma was trained using Tensor Processing Unit (TPU) hardware (TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e). TPUs are specifically designed to handle the massive computations involved in training vision-language models (VLMs), offering performance, memory, scalability, and cost-effectiveness advantages. Training was done using JAX and ML Pathways. JAX allows researchers to take advantage of the latest generation of hardware, including TPUs, for faster and more efficient training of large models. ML Pathways is Google's effort to build artificially intelligent systems capable of generalizing across multiple tasks, suitable for foundation models like these. These models were evaluated against a large collection of different datasets and metrics to cover different aspects of text generation. In reasoning and factuality, the models showed performance across various benchmarks such as HellaSwag, BoolQ, PIQA, SocialIQA, TriviaQA, Natural Questions, ARC-c, ARC-e, WinoGrande, BIG-Bench Hard, and DROP. In STEM and code, the models were evaluated on benchmarks such as MMLU, AGIEval, MATH, GSM8K, GPQA, MBPP, and HumanEval. In multilingual tasks, the models were evaluated on benchmarks such as MGSM, Global-MMLU-Lite, WMT24++, FloRes, XQuAD, ECLeKTic, and IndicGenBench. In multimodal tasks, the models were evaluated on benchmarks such as COCOcap, DocVQA, InfoVQA, MMMU, TextVQA, RealWorldQA, ReMI, AI2D, ChartQA, VQAv2, BLINK, OKVQA, TallyQA, SpatialSense VQA, and CountBenchQA. The evaluation methods included structured evaluations and internal red-teaming testing of relevant content policies. Red-teaming was conducted by different teams with different goals and human evaluation metrics. The models were evaluated against categories such as child safety, content safety, and representational harms. For all areas of safety testing, the models showed major improvements in the categories of child safety, content safety, and representational harms relative to previous Gemma models. All testing was conducted without safety filters to evaluate the model capabilities and behaviors. For both text-to-text and image-to-text, and across all model sizes, the model produced minimal policy violations and showed significant improvements over previous Gemma models' performance with respect to ungrounded inferences. A limitation of the evaluations was that they included only English language prompts. These models have certain limitations that users should be aware of. The quality and diversity of the training data significantly influence the model's capabilities. Biases or gaps in the training data can lead to limitations in the model's responses. The scope of the training dataset determines the subject areas the model can handle effectively. Models are better at tasks that can be framed with clear prompts and instructions. Open-ended or highly complex tasks might be challenging. A model's performance can be influenced by the amount of context provided (longer context generally leads to better outputs, up to a certain point). Natural language is inherently complex. Models might struggle to grasp subtle nuances, sarcasm, or figurative language. Models generate responses based on information they learned from their training datasets, but they are not knowledge bases. They may generate incorrect or outdated factual statements. Models rely on statistical patterns in language. They might lack the ability to apply common sense reasoning in certain situations. The development of vision-language models (VLMs) raises several ethical concerns. VLMs trained on large-scale, real-world text and image data can reflect socio-cultural biases embedded in the training material. These models underwent careful scrutiny, input data pre-processing, and posterior evaluations. VLMs can be misused to generate text that is false, misleading, or harmful. Guidelines are provided for responsible use with the model. Transparency and accountability are emphasized, with details on the models' architecture, capabilities, limitations, and evaluation processes summarized in this model card. A responsibly developed open model offers the opportunity to share innovation by making VLM technology accessible to developers and researchers across the AI ecosystem. Risks identified include the perpetuation of biases, generation of harmful content, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. It is encouraged to perform continuous monitoring and the exploration of de-biasing techniques during model training, fine-tuning, and other use cases. Mechanisms and guidelines for content safety are essential. Developers are encouraged to exercise caution and implement appropriate content safety safeguards based on their specific product policies and application use cases. Technical limitations and developer and end-user education can help mitigate against malicious applications of VLMs. Educational resources and reporting mechanisms for users to flag misuse are provided. Prohibited uses of Gemma models are outlined in the Gemma Prohibited Use Policy. Models were trained on data filtered for removal of certain personal information and other sensitive data. Developers are encouraged to adhere to privacy regulations with privacy-preserving techniques. At the time of release, this family of models provides high-performance open vision-language model implementations designed from the ground up for responsible AI development compared to similarly sized models. Using the benchmark evaluation metrics described in this document, these models have shown to provide superior performance to other, comparably-sized open model alternatives.

instruction: Is there a way to make the skin in this photo look less flat and more realistic?
description: The qwen-edit-skin is a finetuned Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) model designed to enhance the realism and detail of human skin in images. It is built on top of the Qwen/Qwen-Image-Edit-2509 base model, which is known for its advanced image editing capabilities, particularly in maintaining consistency in single-image edits and preserving personal identity in multi-image editing. The LoRA was trained for 5000 steps on an NVIDIA RTX 5090 GPU using the AI-Toolkit. The training dataset was curated by taking real images of subject portraits with exposed skin, captioning them as target images, and then editing them in Photoshop to reduce skin texture, tone, and pore visibility to create control images. The model was trained at resolutions of 512, 768, and 1024 pixels using bf16 precision. Key training parameters include a batch size of 1, gradient accumulation of 1, a learning rate of 1.0e-04, and the adamw8bit optimizer, which helps reduce memory usage during training. The noise scheduler used is flowmatch, which contributes to efficient training and high-quality image generation. The LoRA architecture features linear and convolutional ranks and alphas of 16, ensuring a balanced scaling of learned adaptations to prevent overfitting. To use the model, the base Qwen-Image-Edit-2509 model must be loaded, followed by the qwen-edit-skin LoRA weights. The recommended weight strength is between 1 and 1.5, though higher values can be used to observe the effect of the LoRA. The model is intended for creative and artistic purposes, such as enhancing the realism of human skin in digital images for digital artists, photographers, and AI art enthusiasts. It may carry inherent biases from the base model and the training dataset, which focused on improving skin details and may not represent all skin tones and types equally. Users are encouraged to use descriptive and inclusive language in their prompts and to use the model responsibly. The model is not intended for unethical or harmful use, and users are responsible for the content they generate. A trigger phrase to activate the model's effect is 'make the subjects skin details more prominent and natural.'

instruction: Is it possible to find the head, shoulders, and hips for each person in this image?
description: This model combines an object detector with a custom PyTorch keypoint head and is trained on a 500-person subset of the COCO dataset. The system detects people using DETR and then predicts 17 COCO-style keypoints using heatmap regression. The model is designed to perform keypoint estimation in a top-down approach, where person bounding boxes are first detected, and then individual keypoint heatmaps are generated for each detected person. The architecture processes an input image through DETR to detect person bounding boxes, crops and resizes the detected regions, and feeds them into a CNN keypoint head to produce 17 heatmaps corresponding to the COCO keypoints. The model is trained on 500 images from the COCO val2017 dataset that contain visible persons, with 17 annotated keypoints per person. The model files include trained PyTorch weights, a Colab notebook for training and inference, a configuration file, and a project description. For inference, the model can be loaded using PyTorch, and the DETR object detector can be used to extract person bounding boxes from an input image, which are then used to generate keypoint heatmaps. The model supports optional fine-tuning on custom datasets by converting the data to COCO format and using the provided notebook. The model is based on the DETR architecture for object detection and a custom CNN for keypoint estimation. The system is intended for use in applications requiring human pose estimation from images.

instruction: Can you check this image and let me know if it was created by someone or by an automated process?
description: This is a fine-tuned model trained on 60,000 AI-generated and 60,000 human images. The model is designed to classify images as either AI-generated or human-created. It supports image classification tasks and is based on the Siglip architecture. During training, the model was trained for 5 epochs with a total of 51,652,280,821 GFLOPs. The training loss was 0.0799, and the training process took 2 hours, 39 minutes, and 49.46 seconds, achieving a training speed of 69.053 samples per second and 4.316 steps per second. On the test set, the model achieved an evaluation accuracy of 0.9923 with an evaluation loss of 0.0551. The evaluation process took 2 minutes and 35.78 seconds, with a speed of 212.533 samples per second and 6.644 steps per second. The final test accuracy is 0.9923, with a macro F1 score of 0.9923 and a weighted F1 score of 0.9923. Some users have reported overfitting issues. The model can be used for inference by loading the model and processor, preprocessing the input image, and running inference to obtain predicted labels and confidence scores.

instruction: I need an audio recording of a debate between two people discussing climate change—can you generate that?
description: VibeVoice is a novel open-source text-to-speech model designed to generate expressive, long-form, multi-speaker conversational audio, such as podcasts, from text. It addresses challenges in traditional TTS systems, particularly in scalability, speaker consistency, and natural turn-taking. A core innovation is the use of continuous speech tokenizers (Acoustic and Semantic) operating at an ultra-low frame rate of 7.5 Hz, which preserve audio fidelity while improving computational efficiency for long sequences. VibeVoice employs a next-token diffusion framework, combining a Large Language Model (LLM) to understand textual context and dialogue flow with a diffusion head to generate high-fidelity acoustic details. The model can synthesize speech up to 90 minutes long with up to 4 distinct speakers, surpassing the typical 1-2 speaker limits of many prior models. The LLM used is Qwen2.5. The Acoustic Tokenizer is based on a σ-VAE variant with a mirror-symmetric encoder-decoder structure featuring 7 stages of modified Transformer blocks, achieving 3200x downsampling from 24kHz input. Encoder and decoder components are approximately 340M parameters each. The Semantic Tokenizer mirrors the Acoustic Tokenizer's architecture (without VAE components) and is trained with an ASR proxy task. The Diffusion Head is a lightweight module (4 layers, ~600M parameters) conditioned on LLM hidden states, predicting acoustic VAE features using a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM) process. It uses Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) and DPM-Solver during inference. The model is trained with a curriculum increasing up to 32,768 tokens. Training involves separate pre-training of the Acoustic and Semantic tokenizers, followed by training of the LLM and diffusion head with a curriculum learning strategy for input sequence length (4k -> 16K -> 32K). The model is intended for research purposes only and is not recommended for commercial or real-world applications without further testing and development. It is limited to English and Chinese data, and outputs in other languages may be unintelligible or offensive. The model does not handle background noise, music, or other sound effects, and does not generate overlapping speech segments in conversations. Potential risks include misuse for deepfakes, disinformation, or voice impersonation. To mitigate these risks, an audible disclaimer is embedded in every synthesized audio file, and an imperceptible watermark is added for verification. Inference requests are logged (hashed) for abuse pattern detection and publishing aggregated statistics quarterly. Users are responsible for sourcing datasets legally and ethically, including securing appropriate rights and anonymizing data prior to use with VibeVoice. The model is developed by members of Microsoft Research, and feedback or reports of issues can be sent to VibeVoice@microsoft.com.

instruction: Visualize a scene of a bustling medieval marketplace with merchants and knights in the background.
description: The FLUX.2 [klein] model family is the fastest image generation model to date, unifying generation and editing in a single compact architecture. It delivers state-of-the-art quality with end-to-end inference in as low as under a second. Designed for real-time image generation without sacrificing quality, it runs on consumer hardware with as little as 13GB VRAM. FLUX.2 [klein] 4B is a 4 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating images from text descriptions and supports multi-reference editing capabilities. It is fully open under Apache 2.0 and can run on consumer GPUs like the RTX 3090/4070. The model supports text-to-image, image editing, and multi-reference editing at quality that exceeds its size. It is suitable for local development, edge deployment, and production use. The model is available via the BFL API and can be used with the Diffusers Python library. Limitations include the inability to provide factual information, potential inaccuracies in rendered text, possible amplification of biases from training data, and the possibility of failing to generate output that matches prompts. Prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting style. The model and its derivatives may not be used in any way that violates applicable law, for the purpose of exploiting or harming minors, to generate deceptive or harmful content, to generate personal identifiable information that can be used to harm individuals, to harass or abuse individuals or groups, to create non-consensual intimate imagery or illegal pornographic content, or for fully automated decision making or high-risk applications that adversely impact legal rights. Black Forest Labs is committed to responsible development and has implemented pre-training and post-training mitigations to prevent the generation of unlawful content, including child sexual abuse material and nonconsensual intimate imagery. These include filtering pre-training data for NSFW and CSAM content, targeted fine-tuning to inhibit harmful behaviors, and ongoing internal and external evaluations. The final FLUX.2 [klein] checkpoints demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs and were released under an Apache 2.0 license. The model includes filters for NSFW and protected content in inputs and outputs, and implements pixel-layer watermarking and C2PA metadata for content provenance. Acceptable use is governed by policies including the FLUX Non-Commercial License, Developer Terms of Service, and Usage Policy. Black Forest Labs maintains a safety email address and reporting relationships with organizations to address emerging risks and develop mitigations.

instruction: This video is being shared widely—can you help determine if it’s real?
description: The SOTA Deepfake Detector - DFD is a spatiotemporal adaptation built on a frozen CLIP-ViT-Large backbone with lightweight spatiotemporal adapters inserted to learn temporal relationships across frames. The backbone parameters are frozen; only the adapters and final classification head are trainable. The model is designed to classify short video clips or frame sequences as either real or deepfake/manipulated. The input consists of 12 frames per video, each of size 384×384 pixels, and the model outputs logits for two classes: 0 = real, 1 = fake. The model uses a PyTorch framework with Hugging Face Transformers and was trained using BF16 mixed precision. The model has approximately 5,255,938 trainable parameters, which is about 1.7% of the total 308 million parameters. The model was trained on the DFD (Deep Fake Detection) dataset, which contains 3,431 videos, with 363 real videos (10.6%) and 3,068 fake videos (89.4%). A total of 41,172 frames were extracted from the videos. The training used a 90/10 stratified train/validation split and a WeightedRandomSampler to address class imbalance. The training configuration included a batch size of 16 (effective 32 with gradient accumulation), AdamW optimizer with weight decay of 0.05, a learning rate of 5e-6 with cosine decay and 10% warmup, and 12 training epochs. The loss function was cross-entropy with label smoothing of 0.1, and gradient clipping was applied with a max_grad_norm of 1.0. Training augmentations included random horizontal flip and brightness jitter. The model was trained on an NVIDIA L4 GPU with 24GB VRAM, and all random seeds were fixed for reproducibility. The best validation accuracy achieved was 84.88%, with real class detection ranging from approximately 47–55% and fake class detection ranging from approximately 64–93%. The training loss converged from 0.7097 to 0.6921 over 12 epochs. The model has several known limitations, including a highly imbalanced validation set (89% fake), a small number of real videos (363) that limits generalization to unseen real samples, and optimization for the DFD dataset, which may require fine-tuning for other deepfake types. The temporal context is limited to 12 frames (approximately 0.4–1s depending on FPS), so long-term artifacts may be missed. The model is intended for use in research on deepfake detection, benchmarking against other detectors, and integration into content-moderation pipelines with caution. It is not intended for use in medical, legal, or other high-stakes decisions without human review, or for use on domains/styles substantially different from the DFD dataset without further fine-tuning. The model is available on the Hugging Face Hub under the repository Arko007/deepfake-detector-dfd-sota and is licensed under the Apache-2.0 license. The model files include pytorch_model.bin (1.28GB) and config.json. Inference involves loading a checkpoint, initializing the model, preprocessing input frames using the CLIP image processor, and running a forward pass to obtain logits, which are then converted to softmax probabilities and a predicted class. The model expects input tensors of shape (batch_size, 12, 3, 384, 384) with dtype float32 or bf16 where supported. The model can be used with helper functions to extract frames from a video or folder, preprocess them, and perform inference. The model is not intended for production use without additional robustness, error handling, and optimization.

instruction: Create a 720P video from this image, adding a gentle wind blowing through the trees.
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video models, incorporating innovations such as an effective Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video and image-to-video generation. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity without increasing computational cost. Wan2.2 includes a high-noise expert for early denoising stages and a low-noise expert for later stages, each with about 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active per step. The model is trained on significantly larger data, with +65.6% more images and +83.2% more videos than its predecessor, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 also features a 5B model with a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE, achieving a compression ratio of 16×16×4, supporting both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps, and running on consumer-grade graphics cards like 4090. The model supports both 480P and 720P resolutions and is built with a Mixture-of-Experts architecture for more stable video synthesis with reduced unrealistic camera movements and enhanced support for diverse stylized scenes. The model is available in multiple versions, including T2V-A14B for text-to-video, I2V-A14B for image-to-video, and TI2V-5B for high-compression text-image-to-video. It is open-sourced under the Apache 2.0 License, with usage restrictions to ensure compliance with applicable laws and ethical standards. The model has been integrated into ComfyUI and Diffusers, and includes multi-GPU and single-GPU inference options, as well as support for prompt extension and negative prompts. Computational efficiency tests show that the model can generate a 5-second 720P video in under 9 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU, making it one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available. The model is evaluated on the Wan-Bench 2.0 benchmark, demonstrating superior performance compared to leading closed-source commercial models.

instruction: What trends can you identify from the line graph shown in this image?
description: This model is a specialized version of Qwen3-VL-2B-Thinking, fine-tuned using LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) on the ChartQA dataset for advanced chart understanding, data extraction, and visual reasoning. It is designed to perform 'System 2' thinking, generating step-by-step reasoning traces before providing the final answer, which makes the decision process regarding chart interpretation transparent and verifiable. The model uses tags to articulate the visual analysis process, such as identifying axes and comparing bars, before concluding. It is optimized for bar charts, line graphs, and pie charts found in the ChartQA dataset. The model is fine-tuned with LoRA, involving approximately 2% trainable parameters, while retaining the base model's general capabilities. It is trained to return answers in a strict JSON format {\

instruction: Can you rank these documents based on how well they match the question about renewable energy policies?
description: The granite-embedding-reranker-english-r2 is a 149M parameter dense cross-encoder model from the Granite Embeddings collection, designed to generate high-quality text embeddings. It produces embedding vectors of size 768 based on a context length of up to 8192 tokens. The model was trained using open-source relevance-pair datasets with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses, as well as IBM-collected and generated datasets. It uses a cross-encoder architecture to compute relevance scores between queries and documents by jointly encoding their text, enabling precise reranking based on contextual alignment. The model is trained with ranking-specific loss functions such as pListMLE and model merging techniques to enhance performance. It shows strong performance on standard information retrieval benchmarks (BEIR, MIRACL), long-document search benchmarks (MLDR), and enterprise use cases. The model is based on the ModernBERT architecture, with an embedding size of 768, 22 layers, 12 attention heads, an intermediate size of 1152, a GeGLU activation function, a vocabulary size of 50368, and a maximum sequence length of 8192. It is designed to compute relevance scores for query-document pairs, making it well-suited for reranking tasks in information retrieval and search applications. The model is compatible with the SentenceTransformer library and can be used to jointly encode pairs of text to compute relevance scores. It is also compatible with the Huggingface Transformers library and can be used to encode pairs of text. The model can be used in conjunction with a retriever to produce an end-to-end search system. The model is trained using data from four key sources: unsupervised title-body paired data scraped from the web, publicly available paired data with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses, IBM-internal paired data targeting specific technical domains, and IBM-generated synthetic data. Notably, the model does not use the MS-MARCO retrieval dataset due to its non-commercial license. The underlying encoder models use GneissWeb, an IBM-curated dataset composed exclusively of open, commercial-friendly sources. All data undergoes a data clearance process subject to technical, business, and governance review, capturing information about content description, ownership, intended use, data classification, licensing, usage restrictions, data acquisition, and an assessment of sensitive information. The model is trained using IBM's BlueVela Cluster, which is outfitted with NVIDIA H100 80gb GPUs. The data used to train the base language model was filtered to remove text containing hate, abuse, and profanity. The model is fine-tuned on English and has a context length of 8192 tokens (longer texts will be truncated to this size).

instruction: Generate an image of a train station with the destination 'Shanghai' written in large letters above the platform.
description: LongCat-Image is an open-source and bilingual (Chinese-English) foundation model for image generation, designed to address core challenges in multilingual text rendering, photorealism, deployment efficiency, and developer accessibility prevalent in current leading models. With 6B parameters, LongCat-Image surpasses numerous open-source models that are several times larger across multiple benchmarks, demonstrating the immense potential of efficient model design. The model demonstrates superior accuracy and stability in rendering common Chinese characters compared to existing state-of-the-art open-source models and achieves industry-leading coverage of the Chinese dictionary. Through an innovative data strategy and training framework, LongCat-Image achieves remarkable photorealism in generated images. For both Text-to-Image and Image Editing tasks involving text generation, the target text must be enclosed within single or double quotation marks (both English '...' / "..." and Chinese ‘...’ / “...” styles are supported). The model utilizes a specialized character-level encoding strategy specifically for quoted content. Failure to use explicit quotation marks prevents this mechanism from triggering, which will severely compromise the text rendering capability.

instruction: I need to understand who is speaking in each part of this recorded debate—can you help?
description: This model is a streaming version of the Sortformer diarizer, designed for speaker diarization in conversations. It is a novel end-to-end neural model trained with unconventional objectives compared to existing end-to-end diarization models. The model employs an Arrival-Order Speaker Cache (AOSC) to store frame-level acoustic embeddings of previously observed speakers. Sortformer resolves the permutation problem in diarization by following the arrival-time order of the speech segments from each speaker. The model can be used to recognize speakers in conversations. The streaming version of Sortformer employs a pre-encode layer in the Fast-Conformer to generate a speaker-cache, which is filtered at each step to retain only high-quality speaker cache vectors. Aside from the speaker-cache management part, the streaming Sortformer follows the architecture of the offline version of Sortformer. The offline version consists of an L-size (17 layers) encoder based on a Fast-Conformer, followed by an 18-layer Transformer encoder with a hidden size of 192, and two feedforward layers with 4 sigmoid outputs for each frame input at the top layer. The model is available for use in the NeMo Framework and can be used as a pre-trained checkpoint for inference or for fine-tuning on another dataset. The model accepts single-channel (mono) audio sampled at 16,000 Hz. The input tensor is a Ns x 1 matrix for each audio clip, where Ns is the number of samples in the time-series signal. The output of the model is an T x S matrix, where S is the maximum number of speakers (in this model, S = 4), and T is the total number of frames, including zero-padding. Each frame corresponds to a segment of 0.08 seconds of audio. Each element of the T x S matrix represents the speaker activity probability in the [0, 1] range. The model operates in a streaming mode (online mode) and can detect a maximum of 4 speakers; performance degrades on recordings with 5 and more speakers. While the model is designed for long-form audio and can handle recordings that are several hours long, performance may degrade on very long recordings. The model was trained on publicly available speech datasets, primarily in English, and performance may degrade on non-English speech and on out-of-domain data, such as recordings in noisy conditions. The model was trained on a combination of 2445 hours of real conversations and 5150 hours of simulated audio mixtures generated by a specific method. The datasets include Fisher English (LDC), AMI Meeting Corpus, VoxConverse-v0.3, ICSI, AISHELL-4, Third DIHARD Challenge Development (LDC), 2000 NIST Speaker Recognition Evaluation, split1 (LDC), DiPCo, AliMeeting, 2004-2010 NIST Speaker Recognition Evaluation (LDC), and Librispeech. The model was trained on 8 nodes of 8×NVIDIA Tesla V100 GPUs using 90-second long training samples and a batch size of 4. The model can be trained using specific tools and scripts. Inference can be performed with post-processing algorithms using inference tools. The model's performance is evaluated using the Diarization Error Rate (DER) on various datasets, including DIHARD III Eval, CALLHOME-part2, and CH109. Evaluation results show varying DER values depending on the latency and whether post-processing is applied. The model is deployed via NVIDIA RIVA ASR, an accelerated speech AI SDK deployable on-prem, in all clouds, multi-cloud, hybrid, on edge, and embedded. Riva provides world-class out-of-the-box accuracy for the most common languages, best-in-class accuracy with run-time word boosting, and customization of acoustic model, language model, and inverse text normalization. It also provides streaming speech recognition, Kubernetes compatible scaling, and enterprise-grade support. The model is licensed under the CC-BY-4.0 license.

instruction: Based on this dataset, can you generate a bar chart to visualize the distribution of product categories?
description: JT-DA-8B is a specialized large language model designed for complex table reasoning tasks across diverse real-world scenarios. It is built upon the open-sourced JT-Coder-8B model, an 8B-parameter decoder-only foundation model trained from scratch. The model architecture follows a mainstream Transformer decoder design, including RMS layer normalization, RoPE, and GQA mechanism. The model is trained on a comprehensive and diverse training corpus, which includes 29 public table QA datasets, over 300 domain-specific non-QA tables, and 1 million rule-based table reasoning QA pairs. A structured pipeline is used to generate realistic multi-step analytical tasks involving reasoning patterns like correlation, anomaly detection, and hypothesis testing. The training data is extensively cleaned and processed, including short answer filtering, repetitive thinking filtering, low information density filtering, and LLM-based scoring to ensure high-quality, table-centric data. The training data composition includes six core capabilities with 34 subtasks, covering natural language understanding, table understanding, table basic operation, table computational operation, data analysis, and advanced data analysis. The model is trained using a distributed parallel strategy on 96 NPUs, with a high-bandwidth, low-latency communication network and shared high-performance storage. Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) is conducted to enhance the model's capability in structured table reasoning, using a teacher-forcing approach to minimize the cross-entropy loss between predicted and ground-truth tokens. A four-stage table reasoning workflow is proposed, including table preprocessing, table sensing, tool-integrated reasoning, and prompt engineering, to improve model interpretability and execution accuracy. The model is evaluated on the TReB benchmark, which covers six core capabilities and 26 subtasks, using three inference modes: Textual Chain-of-Thought (TCoT), Program-of-Thought (PoT), and Interleaved Chain-of-Thought (ICoT). The evaluation results demonstrate that the model excels among models of the same size, showcasing outstanding performance in table understanding, querying, computation, and data analysis tasks. The model is capable of performing multi-step reasoning, handling inconsistent and noisy data types, and generating executable code for data analysis. It supports tasks such as table summarization, descriptive analysis, statistical metric calculation, variable relationship modeling, and result interpretation. The model also supports data visualization, including the generation of bar charts to illustrate data patterns. The model is distributed under a license and includes a disclaimer that it may still produce content that is inaccurate, biased, or inappropriate, and users are advised to exercise discretion when interpreting the model’s outputs and assume full responsibility for any outcomes arising from its use.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Can you create a video that shows a sunset over the ocean, following the detailed aesthetic I described?
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video generation models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and an efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video and image-to-video (TI2V) system. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity without increasing computational cost. The high-noise expert handles early-stage layout, while the low-noise expert refines details in later stages. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a threshold step defining the switch. The A14B model series, which uses this MoE design, has 27B total parameters but only 14B active parameters per step, maintaining low inference cost and GPU memory usage. Wan2.2 also incorporates meticulously curated aesthetic data with detailed labels for lighting, composition, contrast, and color tone, enabling precise and controllable cinematic style generation. Compared to Wan2.1, Wan2.2 is trained on 65.6% more images and 83.2% more videos, improving generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. The TI2V-5B model, built with the advanced Wan2.2-VAE, achieves a compression ratio of 16×16×4, enabling 720P video generation at 24fps on consumer-grade GPUs like the RTX 4090. It supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation and is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available. The model can run on a single GPU with at least 24GB VRAM and can be optimized for higher VRAM GPUs to improve speed. Wan2.2 is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their use complies with the license terms, including not generating harmful or illegal content. The model is open-sourced and supports both industrial and academic applications.

instruction: Rank these texts to show which ones are most aligned with the theme of sustainability.
description: This is a model finetuned using the Sentence Transformers library. It computes scores for pairs of texts, which can be used for text reranking and semantic search. The model is a Cross Encoder with a maximum sequence length of 1024 tokens and produces one output label. It is designed to work with multilingual text and is licensed under cc-by-nc-4.0. The model can be loaded using the Sentence Transformers library and supports tasks such as computing scores for text pairs and ranking texts based on similarity to a single text. It was trained on a dataset of 16,862 samples with query, document, and label columns, and evaluated on a dataset of 808 samples. The training used a batch size of 32 for both training and evaluation, a learning rate of 2e-05, and 2 training epochs with a warmup ratio of 0.1. The model was trained using bf16 precision. Evaluation results on the affiliation-val dataset show a mean average precision (map) of 0.9307, a mean reciprocal rank at 10 (mrr@10) of 0.9307, and a normalized discounted cumulative gain at 10 (ndcg@10) of 0.9502. The model was trained using the AdamW optimizer with a linear learning rate scheduler and a weight decay of 0.0. The training loss decreased from 0.577 at the first step to 0.2783 at step 1000, with a validation loss of 0.528 at the saved checkpoint. The model was developed using Python 3.12.12, Sentence Transformers 5.2.0, Transformers 4.57.3, PyTorch 2.9.1+cu128, Accelerate 1.12.0, Datasets 4.4.2, and Tokenizers 0.22.1.

instruction: What are the key chemical entities in this excerpt from a toxicology report?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition, specifically for identifying chemical entities from the BC5CDR dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated BC5CDR_CHEM dataset, which contains 1,500 PubMed abstracts with 4,409 annotated chemical entities. The model supports the classification of two entity types: `B-CHEM` and `I-CHEM`. It is intended for use in applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the xlm-roberta-base architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It has been validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.95, precision of 0.93, recall of 0.96, and accuracy of 0.99. It is ranked among the top models on the BC5CDR_CHEM dataset, with an F1 score of 0.9614, precision of 0.9520, recall of 0.9710, and accuracy of 0.9892. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and healthcare applications. It is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. The model can be used with the Hugging Face pipeline API, and supports various aggregation strategies for entity prediction. For efficient processing of large datasets, the model supports batch processing with adjustable batch sizes depending on hardware capabilities. The model is part of the OpenMed NER project, which provides open-source, domain-adapted state-of-the-art transformers for biomedical named entity recognition across multiple public datasets.

instruction: Can you spot any birds flying in this aerial view of a lake?
description: YOLOWorld is an SDK that enables efficient open-vocabulary object detection using YOLO-Worldv2 Large. It is optimized for Axera’s NPU-based SoC platforms, including the AX650 Series, AX630C Series, and AX8850 Series, as well as Axera's dedicated AI accelerator. The model supports inference on both host systems and M.2 accelerator cards, such as those used with Raspberry PI 5. It includes two main models: one for object detection (yolo_u16_ax650.axmodel and yolo_u16_ax630c.axmodel) and one for text encoding (clip_b1_u16_ax650.axmodel and clip_b1_u16_ax630c.axmodel). On the AX650 platform, the object detection model has an input shape of 1 x 640 x 640 x 3 and a latency of 9.522 ms, with a CMM usage of 21 MB, while the text encoder has an input shape of 1 x 77 and a latency of 2.997 ms, with a CMM usage of 137 MB. On the AX630C platform, the object detection model has a latency of 43.450 ms and a CMM usage of 31 MB, and the text encoder has a latency of 10.703 ms and a CMM usage of 134 MB. The model is designed to accept text inputs for object detection, such as 'person', 'dog', 'car', 'horse', 'man', 'shoes', 'ball', and others, and it processes images for inference. The SDK includes a Python interface with example scripts for running inference, and it requires specific dependencies installed via pip. The model is deployed using precompiled shared libraries and model files, and it supports both host and AXCL device types for execution. The system outputs detection results in multiple feature maps with varying spatial resolutions. The model is intended for use with Axera hardware and may not be compatible with other platforms.

instruction: Could you break down the fluency of this speech into 3-second segments and report any disfluencies?
description: This model implements a speech fluency classification system based on the Whisper Large v3 architecture, as described in the paper 'Vox-Profile: A Speech Foundation Model Benchmark for Characterizing Diverse Speaker and Speech Traits'. The model is designed to classify speech segments as either 'fluent' or 'disfluent' using a 3-second window with a 1-second step size. When disfluent speech is detected, the model further classifies the type of disfluency into one of the following categories: 'Block', 'Prolongation', 'Sound Repetition', 'Word Repetition', or 'Interjection'. The model is intended for use in characterizing speech traits and is not suitable for clinical or diagnostic applications, surveillance, privacy-invasive uses, or commercial purposes. The model is released under the Open RAIL license, and users are required to respect the privacy and consent of data subjects and comply with relevant laws and regulations in their jurisdictions.

instruction: Could you estimate the degradation rate in the atmosphere for the SMILES input 'C(C)(C)C'?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict the reaction rate constant with hydroxyl radicals (log kAOH). This property is related to atmospheric degradation and environmental persistence. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. The model type is AttentiveFP (Graph Neural Network), and it performs a regression task. It processes SMILES strings as input and produces a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and is based on an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model's hyperparameters include a hidden channel size of 128, three layers, three timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.1, a learning rate of 0.0005, a weight decay of 0.0001, a batch size of 32, and 50 training epochs with a patience of 10. The model was trained on the kAOH dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: What diseases are identified in this section of a clinical trial summary?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for disease entity recognition, specifically for disease entities from the BC5CDR dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the BC5CDR_DISEASE dataset, which contains 1,500 PubMed abstracts with 5,818 annotated disease entities. The model supports the identification and classification of two entity types: B-DISEASE and I-DISEASE. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem and is validated on clinical benchmarks for production-ready reliability. Performance metrics for the model include an F1 score of 0.87, precision of 0.85, recall of 0.88, and accuracy of 0.97. The model is based on the e5-base-v2 architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. If used in research or applications, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: What are the advantages of using a specific reward system for video generation tasks?
description: The Wan2.2-Fun-Reward-LoRAs are pre-trained models in the form of LoRAs (Low-Rank Adaptations) designed to enhance the alignment of generated videos with human preferences through the use of reward backpropagation techniques. These LoRAs can be used as plug-ins to improve the performance of the corresponding base model or to train custom reward LoRAs. The models include Wan2.2-Fun-A14B-InP-high-noise-HPS2.1, Wan2.2-Fun-A14B-InP-low-noise-HPS2.1, Wan2.2-Fun-A14B-InP-high-noise-MPS, and Wan2.2-Fun-A14B-InP-low-noise-MPS. Each LoRA is trained with a batch size of 8 for varying numbers of steps: 5,000 steps for the high-noise HPS2.1 and high-noise MPS models, 2,700 steps for the low-noise HPS2.1 model, and 4,500 steps for the low-noise MPS model. The HPS2.1 and MPS reward LoRAs have a rank of 128 and a network alpha of 64. It is noted that the MPS reward LoRA for the low-noise model converges significantly more slowly and may not deliver satisfactory results, so the HPSv2.1 reward LoRA is recommended for the low-noise model. The models are intended to be used in conjunction with the base model Wan2.2-Fun-A14B-InP. During training, the reward continues to increase after a certain point, but the quality of the generated videos does not improve further, as the model may learn to exploit shortcuts such as adding artifacts in the background. Additionally, there is a lack of suitable preference models for video generation, and using image preference models leads to a decrease in the dynamism of generated videos, although this can be partially mitigated by computing the reward using only the first frame of the decoded video.

instruction: Imagine a cyberpunk city with neon lights, holograms, and rain-soaked streets.
description: The FLUX.2 [klein] model family are the fastest image models to date, unifying generation and editing in a single compact architecture, delivering state-of-the-art quality with end-to-end inference in as low as under a second. Built for applications that require real-time image generation without sacrificing quality. FLUX.2 [klein] 9B is a 9 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating images from text descriptions and supports multi-reference editing capabilities. It is the flagship small model, defining the Pareto frontier for quality versus latency across text-to-image, single-reference editing, and multi-reference generation. It matches or exceeds models five times its size in under half a second. It is built on a 9B flow model with an 8B Qwen3 text embedder, step-distilled to 4 inference steps. It is a distilled model for sub-second image generation with outstanding quality. It supports text-to-image and image-to-image multi-reference editing in a single unified model. It is suitable for real-time generation and integration into applications. It is ideal for creative exploration with excellent prompt adherence and output diversity. It is available for non-commercial use. The model is not intended or able to provide factual information. While the model can output text, the text rendered may be inaccurate or subject to distortion. As a statistical model, this checkpoint may represent or amplify biases observed in the training data. The model may fail to generate output that matches the prompts. Prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting style.
