The model and its derivatives may not be used in any way that violates applicable law, for the purpose of exploiting, harming or attempting to exploit or harm minors in any way, including but not limited to the solicitation, creation, acquisition, or dissemination of child exploitative content, to generate or disseminate deceptive, fraudulent, misleading or otherwise harmful content, to generate or disseminate personal identifiable information that can be used to harm an individual, to harass, abuse, threaten, stalk, or bully individuals or groups of individuals, to create non-consensual intimate imagery or illegal pornographic content, or for fully automated decision making or high risk applications that adversely impact an individual's legal rights or otherwise create or modify a binding, enforceable obligation. The model fits in approximately 29GB VRAM and is accessible on NVIDIA RTX 4090 and above. Black Forest Labs is committed to the responsible development and deployment of models. Prior to releasing the FLUX.2 family of models, the company evaluated and mitigated a number of risks in model checkpoints and hosted services, including the generation of unlawful content, including child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and nonconsensual intimate imagery (NCII). Pre-training mitigation included filtering pre-training data for multiple categories of 'not safe for work' (NSFW) and known CSAM to help prevent a user generating unlawful content in response to text prompts or uploaded images. The company partnered with an independent nonprofit organization dedicated to preventing online abuse to filter known CSAM from the training data. Post-training mitigation included multiple rounds of targeted fine-tuning to provide additional mitigation against potential abuse, including both text-to-image (T2I) and image-to-image (I2I) attacks. By inhibiting certain behaviors and suppressing certain concepts in the trained model, these techniques can help to prevent a user generating synthetic CSAM or NCII from a text prompt, or transforming an uploaded image into synthetic CSAM or NCII. Ongoing evaluation included multiple internal and external third-party evaluations of model checkpoints to identify further opportunities for mitigation. External third-party evaluations focused on eliciting CSAM and NCII through adversarial testing with (i) text-only prompts, (ii) a single uploaded reference image with text prompts, and (iii) multiple uploaded reference images with text prompts. Based on this feedback, further safety fine-tuning was conducted to produce the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] models. The release decision included a final third-party evaluation of the proposed release checkpoints, focused on T2I and I2I generation of synthetic CSAM and NCII, including a comparison with other open-weight T2I and I2I models. The final FLUX.2 [klein] checkpoints demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs in complex generation and editing tasks, and demonstrated higher resilience than leading open-weight models across these risk categories. Based on these findings, the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models were approved for release under an Apache 2.0 license, and the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models were released under a non-commercial license to support third-party research and development. Inference filters for NSFW and protected content in inputs and outputs are included in the repository for the FLUX.2 [klein] models. Filters or manual review must be used with the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models under the terms of the FLUX Non-Commercial License, and deployers are encouraged to implement these mitigations when using the FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models. Where these features are implemented on hosted services, multiple filters may be applied to intercept text prompts, uploaded images, and output images. Both in-house and third-party filters are used to mitigate against harmful outputs, such as CSAM and NCII outputs, including filters provided by external organizations. Content provenance features can help users and platforms better identify, label, and interpret AI-generated content online. The inference code for FLUX.2 [klein] implements an example of pixel-layer watermarking. Additionally, the repository includes links to a standard for metadata. The API for FLUX.2 [klein] applies cryptographically-signed C2PA metadata to downloaded output content to indicate that images were produced with the model. Acceptable use of the models and access to the API are governed by policies set out in applicable documentation, including the FLUX Non-Commercial License (for non-commercial open-weight users), Developer Terms of Service, Self-Hosted Commercial License Terms, and Usage Policy (for commercial open-weight model users), and Developer Terms of Service, FLUX API Service Terms, and Usage Policy (for API users). These prohibit the generation of unlawful content or the use of generated content for unlawful, defamatory, or abusive purposes. Developers and users must consent to these conditions to access the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models on Hugging Face. Black Forest Labs takes model safety seriously and may monitor use to detect misuse or abuse of models and services. A dedicated email address (safety@blackforestlabs.ai) is provided to solicit feedback from the community. The company maintains a reporting relationship with organizations such as the and the and welcomes ongoing engagement with authorities, developers, and researchers to share intelligence about emerging risks and develop effective mitigations.

instruction: How can I improve the robustness of an object detection model in real-world, uncontrolled environments?
description: This repository contains models from the paper 'Mitigating Hallucinations in Object Detection Models: A Revisit to Out-of-Distribution Detection'. It provides YOLOv10 and Faster R-CNN models, including standard baseline versions and fine-tuned models designed to reduce false positive detections on out-of-distribution (OoD) data. The models are trained to address hallucinations, where object detectors produce high-confidence false predictions when encountering novel objects not seen during training. The approach involves identifying flaws in existing OoD benchmarks and introducing a fine-tuning strategy that teaches detectors to be less overconfident. This is achieved by training on 'proximal OoD' samples—objects semantically similar to training classes but not part of them. These samples are treated as background, helping the model suppress predictions for unfamiliar objects. The result is a model that is more conservative and less likely to hallucinate, with minimal impact on performance on the original task. The models are available for the BDD 100K dataset, used in autonomous driving, and the Pascal VOC dataset for general object detection. Both vanilla and fine-tuned versions are provided for each architecture. The fine-tuned models show significant reductions in hallucination errors, with an 88% reduction on the BDD-100K benchmark when combined with an OoD filter. For example, the YOLOv10 model on BDD-100K reduces hallucination counts from 708 in the vanilla version to 145 in the fine-tuned version, and to 78 when combined with a KNN filter. Similarly, the Faster R-CNN model on BDD-100K reduces hallucination counts from 2,595 in the vanilla version to 395 in the fine-tuned version, and to 270 with the KNN filter. The models are available in different configurations, including real-time YOLOv10 and high-accuracy Faster R-CNN. The repository also includes corrected benchmark datasets, 'Near-OoD' and 'Far-OoD', which address flaws in previous benchmarks. The models are released under the MIT License. If used in research, the models and methodology should be cited according to the provided references.

instruction: Find the chemical substances discussed in this article about new treatment options.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition, specifically for identifying chemical entities from the BC5CDR dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated BC5CDR_CHEM dataset, which contains 1,500 PubMed abstracts with 4,409 annotated chemical entities. The model supports the classification of two entity types: `B-CHEM` and `I-CHEM`. It is intended for use in applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the xlm-roberta-base architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It has been validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.95, precision of 0.93, recall of 0.96, and accuracy of 0.99. It is ranked among the top models on the BC5CDR_CHEM dataset, with an F1 score of 0.9614, precision of 0.9520, recall of 0.9710, and accuracy of 0.9892. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and healthcare applications. It is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. The model can be used with the Hugging Face pipeline API, and supports various aggregation strategies for entity prediction. For efficient processing of large datasets, the model supports batch processing with adjustable batch sizes depending on hardware capabilities. The model is part of the OpenMed NER project, which provides open-source, domain-adapted state-of-the-art transformers for biomedical named entity recognition across multiple public datasets.

instruction: I want to extract just the product from this e-commerce image — can you help?
description: BiRefNet is a model designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation (DIS). It is trained on the DIS-TR dataset and validated on DIS-TE and DIS-VD datasets. The model has achieved state-of-the-art performance on three tasks: DIS, HRSOD, and COD. BiRefNet is based on a bilateral reference approach, which is intended to improve segmentation accuracy and resolution. The model supports inference tasks and can be deployed locally or through an inference endpoint. It is implemented using PyTorch and can be loaded with weights from HuggingFace or local storage. The model includes functionality for image preprocessing, prediction, and post-processing, including alpha channel generation for object extraction. The model weights are available in the official repository, and the model can be used with or without the associated GitHub codebase. The model has been developed by researchers from Nankai University, Northwestern Polytechnical University, National University of Defense Technology, Aalto University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and University of Trento. The model is described in the paper 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation,' published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research in 2024. The model's implementation and weights are available on GitHub and HuggingFace, and online demos for inference are provided. The model's development has been supported by GPU resources from Freepik and Fal, as well as assistance with deployment from Not-Lain.

instruction: Create a scene where a person walks through a vibrant autumn forest with falling leaves.
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video generation models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video and image-to-video generation. The MoE architecture in Wan2.2 separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity while maintaining computational efficiency. The model is trained on significantly larger datasets, with 65.6% more images and 83.2% more videos than its predecessor, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 includes a 5B model with a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE, achieving a compression ratio of 16×16×4, and supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps. It can run on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090 and is among the fastest 720P@24fps models available. The model also includes the I2V-A14B model for image-to-video generation, supporting 480P and 720P resolutions, built with an MoE architecture for more stable video synthesis and enhanced support for diverse stylized scenes. The A14B model series uses a two-expert design: a high-noise expert for early denoising stages and a low-noise expert for later stages, with each expert having about 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active per step. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a threshold step defined for switching. Validation results show that the MoE-based Wan2.2 achieves the lowest validation loss, indicating superior convergence and video quality. The TI2V-5B model supports 720P video generation at 24 FPS and can generate a 5-second 720P video in under 9 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU. It is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models and supports both text-to-video and image-to-video tasks in a unified framework. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license terms, including not generating harmful or illegal content. The model has been integrated into ComfyUI and Diffusers, and inference code and model weights are available for download via Hugging Face and ModelScope. Computational efficiency tests have been conducted on different GPUs, with parameter settings and testing methodologies detailed in the documentation. The model has been compared to leading closed-source commercial models on the Wan-Bench 2.0 benchmark, demonstrating superior performance across multiple dimensions.

instruction: I have a list of job descriptions. How can I find which one matches the query 'remote marketing job with good benefits' the most?
description: This is a model that maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and was designed for semantic search. It has been trained on 215 million (question, answer) pairs from diverse sources. The model uses mean pooling to generate embeddings and produces normalized embeddings with a length of 1. It supports the use of dot-product, cosine-similarity, or euclidean distance as score functions. When loaded with sentence-transformers, dot-product is preferred as it is faster. The model is intended to encode queries and text paragraphs in a dense vector space to find relevant documents. It has a limit of 512 word pieces, and text longer than that will be truncated. The model was trained on input text up to 250 word pieces and may not work well for longer text. The training procedure involved fine-tuning the model using a contrastive learning objective on a concatenation of multiple datasets. The model was trained using mean-pooling, cosine-similarity as the similarity function, and a scale of 20. The training data includes duplicate question pairs from WikiAnswers, automatically generated (question, paragraph) pairs from Wikipedia, (title, body) and (title, answer) pairs from StackExchanges, triplets from Bing search engine, (query, answer) pairs from Google queries and featured snippets, (question, answer) pairs from Amazon product pages, Yahoo Answers, and other sources. The total number of training tuples is 214,988,242.

instruction: Generate a clip of a waterfall cascading into a deep pool surrounded by greenery.
description: The FastWan2.2-TI2V-5B-FullAttn-Diffusers model is part of the FastWan2.2 series, which is finetuned using a novel Sparse-distill strategy. This strategy combines DMD and VSA in a single training process, leveraging distillation to shorten diffusion steps and sparse attention to reduce attention computations, enabling faster video generation. The model is built upon Wan-AI/Wan2.2-TI2V-5B-Diffusers and supports efficient 3-step inference, producing high-quality videos at 121×704×1280 resolution. The model is trained using simulated forward for the generator model, making the training process data-free. The current FastWan2.2-TI2V-5B-FullAttn-Diffusers model is trained using only DMD. The model is trained on 121×704×1280 resolution but can generate videos with any resolution, although quality may degrade. Training was conducted on 8 nodes with 64 H200 GPUs in total, using a global batch size of 64, and training runs for 3000 steps, which takes approximately 12 hours. If the model is used for research, the associated papers should be cited.

instruction: How would you predict the plasma level volume for the molecule 'C1CC1'?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict plasma level volume or protein binding parameter, which may relate to pharmacokinetics or blood partitioning behavior. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. It is designed for a regression task, producing a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and features an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model has 128 hidden channels, 3 layers, 3 timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.1, a learning rate of 0.0005, a weight decay of 0.0001, a batch size of 32, and is trained for 50 epochs with a patience of 10. The model was trained on the PLV dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: Can you recommend a quant type that balances both speed and file size efficiently?
description: This is a vision model, and if applicable, mmproj files will be provided. Weighted imatrix quantizations are available. Static quantizations are also available for download. For users unfamiliar with GGUF files, guidance is available on how to use them, including instructions for concatenating multi-part files. A variety of quantized versions are provided, sorted by size but not necessarily by quality. IQ-quants are often considered preferable to similar-sized non-IQ quants. The available quant types include i1-IQ1_S, i1-IQ1_M, i1-IQ2_XXS, i1-IQ2_XS, i1-IQ2_S, i1-IQ2_M, i1-Q2_K_S, i1-IQ3_XXS, i1-Q2_K, i1-IQ3_XS, i1-IQ3_S, i1-Q3_K_S, i1-IQ3_M, i1-Q3_K_M, i1-Q3_K_L, i1-IQ4_XS, i1-IQ4_NL, i1-Q4_0, i1-Q4_K_S, i1-Q4_K_M, i1-Q4_1, i1-Q5_K_S, i1-Q5_K_M, and i1-Q6_K. Each quant type has a specified size in gigabytes and associated notes. For example, the i1-IQ1_S quant is 1.2 GB and is described as suitable for the desperate, while the i1-Q4_K_M quant is 2.6 GB and is described as fast and recommended. An imatrix file is also available for creating custom quants, and it is 0.1 GB in size. A graph by ikawrakow compares some lower-quality quant types, and Artefact2 has provided additional commentary on the topic. Users are directed to a resource for frequently asked questions and for requesting other models to be quantized. The model developer acknowledges their company for providing server access and workstation upgrades to support this work, as well as another individual for granting access to a private supercomputer, which enabled the production of higher-quality imatrix quants.

instruction: I have a voice note in Arabic—can you turn it into a written summary for me?
description: This is a fine-tuned XLSR-53 large model for speech recognition in Arabic. It was fine-tuned using the train and validation splits of unspecified datasets. When using this model, the speech input must be sampled at 16kHz. The model was fine-tuned with the support of GPU credits provided by an unspecified source. The model can be used directly for speech recognition without a language model. It is based on the Wav2Vec2 architecture and is designed to transcribe speech into text. Evaluation results on the Arabic test data of Common Voice show a Word Error Rate (WER) of 39.59% and a Character Error Rate (CER) of 18.18%. These results were obtained using a specific evaluation script. The model has been compared to other Arabic speech recognition models, with varying WER and CER values reported. The model can be accessed and used via the Hugging Face Transformers library. It is important to note that the evaluation results may differ slightly depending on the specific evaluation script used. The model is intended for use in speech recognition tasks involving Arabic speech input.

instruction: What insights can you provide about the spatial relationships in this aerial photograph?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. These models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained following the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. These models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones providing multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers applied on the class token, a linear layer applied on the class token and the average of the patch tokens, image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation using linear layers, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2. However, a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) is a curated dataset of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) is a dataset of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on various tasks. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on web (LVD-1689M), the results include 87.0 on IN-ReaL, 60.4 on IN-R, 50.9 on Obj.Net, 49.5 on Ox.-H, 47.0 on ADE20k, 0.403 on NYU↓, 72.7 on DAVIS, 56.3 on NAVI, and 50.4 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-S/16; 88.0 on IN-ReaL, 68.8 on IN-R, 54.6 on Obj.Net, 50.0 on Ox.-H, 48.8 on ADE20k, 0.399 on NYU↓, 75.5 on DAVIS, 57.1 on NAVI, and 55.2 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-S+/16; 89.3 on IN-ReaL, 76.7 on IN-R, 64.1 on Obj.Net, 58.5 on Ox.-H, 51.8 on ADE20k, 0.373 on NYU↓, 77.2 on DAVIS, 58.8 on NAVI, and 57.2 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-B/16; 90.2 on IN-ReaL, 88.1 on IN-R, 74.8 on Obj.Net, 63.1 on Ox.-H, 54.9 on ADE20k, 0.352 on NYU↓, 79.9 on DAVIS, 62.3 on NAVI, and 61.3 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-L/16; 90.3 on IN-ReaL, 90.0 on IN-R, 78.6 on Obj.Net, 64.5 on Ox.-H, 54.8 on ADE20k, 0.352 on NYU↓, 79.3 on DAVIS, 63.3 on NAVI, and 56.3 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-H+/16; and 90.4 on IN-ReaL, 91.1 on IN-R, 91.1 on Obj.Net, 72.8 on Ox.-H, 55.9 on ADE20k, 0.309 on NYU↓, 79.7 on DAVIS, 64.4 on NAVI, and 58.7 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-7B/16. For ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web (LVD-1689M), the results include 86.6 on IN-ReaL @256px, 87.7 on IN-ReaL @512px, 73.7 on Obj.Net @256px, 74.1 on Obj.Net @512px, 52.6 on ADE20k @256px, 58.7 on ADE20k @512px, 42.7 on NYU↓, and 0.448 on SPair for DINOv3 ConvNeXt Tiny; 87.9 on IN-ReaL @256px, 88.7 on IN-ReaL @512px, 73.7 on Obj.Net @256px, 74.1 on Obj.Net @512px, 52.6 on ADE20k @256px, 58.7 on ADE20k @512px, 44.8 on NYU↓, and 0.432 on SPair for DINOv3 ConvNeXt Small; 88.5 on IN-ReaL @256px, 89.2 on IN-ReaL @512px, 77.2 on Obj.Net @256px, 78.2 on Obj.Net @512px, 56.2 on ADE20k @256px, 61.3 on ADE20k @512px, 46.3 on NYU↓, and 0.420 on SPair for DINOv3 ConvNeXt Base; and 88.9 on IN-ReaL @256px, 89.4 on IN-ReaL @512px, 81.3 on Obj.Net @256px, 82.4 on Obj.Net @512px, 59.3 on ADE20k @256px, 65.2 on ADE20k @512px, 47.8 on NYU↓, and 0.403 on SPair for DINOv3 ConvNeXt Large. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on satellite (SAT-493M), the results include 73.0 on m-BEnet, 96.5 on m-brick-kiln, 94.1 on m-eurosat, 60.6 on m-forestnet, 96.0 on m-pv4ger, 57.4 on m-so2sat, and 79.6 mean for DINOv3 ViT-L/16; and 74.0 on m-BEnet, 97.2 on m-brick-kiln, 94.8 on m-eurosat, 62.3 on m-forestnet, 96.1 on m-pv4ger, 62.1 on m-so2sat, and 81.1 mean for DINOv3 ViT-7B/16. For segmentation tasks on satellite data, the results include 94.2 on m-cashew, 75.6 on m-chesapeake, 61.8 on m-NeonTree, 83.7 on m-nz-cattle, 95.2 on m-pv4ger-seg, 36.8 on m-SA-crop, and 74.5 mean for DINOv3 ViT-L/16; and 94.1 on m-cashew, 76.6 on m-chesapeake, 62.6 on m-NeonTree, 83.4 on m-nz-cattle, 95.5 on m-pv4ger-seg, 37.6 on m-SA-crop, and 75.0 mean for DINOv3 ViT-7B/16. The model training for the ViT-7B model used 61,440 hours of compute on Nvidia H100 GPUs in a private infrastructure in the USA, resulting in 18t CO2eq of carbon emissions. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 384, 4 register tokens, 6 heads, MLP FFN, RoPE; ViT-S+ (29M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 384, 4 register tokens, 6 heads, SwiGLU FFN, RoPE; ViT-B (86M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 768, 4 register tokens, 12 heads, MLP FFN, RoPE; ViT-L (300M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 1024, 4 register tokens, 16 heads, MLP FFN, RoPE; ViT-H+ (840M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 1280, 4 register tokens, 20 heads, SwiGLU FFN, RoPE; and ViT-7B (6716M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 4096, 4 register tokens, 32 heads, SwiGLU FFN, RoPE. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: Create an image of a bustling street market in an ancient Chinese city with colorful stalls.
description: Z-Image is a powerful and highly efficient image generation model with 6B parameters. It includes three variants: Z-Image-Turbo, Z-Image-Base, and Z-Image-Edit. Z-Image-Turbo is a distilled version that matches or exceeds leading competitors with only 8 NFEs (Number of Function Evaluations). It offers sub-second inference latency on enterprise-grade H800 GPUs and fits within 16G VRAM consumer devices. It excels in photorealistic image generation, bilingual text rendering (English and Chinese), and robust instruction adherence. Z-Image-Base is the non-distilled foundation model, intended for community-driven fine-tuning and custom development. Z-Image-Edit is a variant fine-tuned for image editing tasks, supporting creative image-to-image generation with strong instruction-following capabilities for precise edits based on natural language prompts. The model uses a Scalable Single-Stream DiT (S3-DiT) architecture, where text, visual semantic tokens, and image VAE tokens are concatenated at the sequence level to serve as a unified input stream, maximizing parameter efficiency compared to dual-stream approaches. According to Elo-based Human Preference Evaluation, Z-Image-Turbo shows highly competitive performance against other leading models and achieves state-of-the-art results among open-source models. The model is supported by the Decoupled-DMD algorithm, a core few-step distillation method that enables the 8-step Z-Image model. Decoupled-DMD is based on the insight that the success of existing DMD methods results from two independent mechanisms: CFG Augmentation (CA), which drives the distillation process, and Distribution Matching (DM), which ensures the stability and quality of the generated output. By decoupling these mechanisms, the model's distillation process is optimized for performance. Building on Decoupled-DMD, the model also incorporates DMDR, which fuses DMD with Reinforcement Learning (RL) during post-training. DMDR improves semantic alignment, aesthetic quality, and structural coherence, while producing images with richer high-frequency details. The model is available for download and use with the Hugging Face diffusers library, with support added via pull requests merged into the latest official release. Users are advised to install diffusers from source to access the latest features and Z-Image support.

instruction: Can you find all the red bicycles in this image?
description: OWL-ViT, short for Vision Transformer for Open-World Localization, is a zero-shot text-conditioned object detection model that allows querying an image with one or multiple text queries. It was proposed by Matthias Minderer, Alexey Gritsenko, Austin Stone, Maxim Neumann, Dirk Weissenborn, Alexey Dosovitskiy, Aravindh Mahendran, Anurag Arnab, Mostafa Dehghani, Zhuoran Shen, Xiao Wang, Xiaohua Zhai, Thomas Kipf, and Neil Houlsby. OWL-ViT uses CLIP as its multi-modal backbone, combining a ViT-like Transformer for visual features and a causal language model for text features. To enable detection, the final token pooling layer of the vision model is removed, and a lightweight classification and box head is attached to each transformer output token. Open-vocabulary classification is achieved by replacing the fixed classification layer weights with class-name embeddings from the text model. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned end-to-end with the classification and box heads on standard detection datasets using a bipartite matching loss. The model uses a CLIP backbone with a ViT-B/16 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned together with the box and class prediction heads with an object detection objective. The CLIP backbone of the model was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including crawling a handful of websites and using commonly-used pre-existing image datasets. A large portion of the data comes from crawling the internet, making it more representative of people and societies most connected to the internet. The prediction heads of OWL-ViT, along with the CLIP backbone, are fine-tuned on publicly available object detection datasets. The model is intended as a research output for research communities. It is primarily intended for use by AI researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. The model is designed to enable researchers to explore zero-shot, text-conditioned object detection and to support interdisciplinary studies of the potential impact of such models, especially in areas that commonly require identifying objects whose label is unavailable during training.

instruction: Could you help me remove the background from this image of a car for a car listing?
description: RMBG v2.0 is a state-of-the-art background removal model that significantly improves upon RMBG v1.4. It is designed to effectively separate foreground from background in a range of categories and image types. The model has been trained on a carefully selected dataset that includes general stock images, e-commerce, gaming, and advertising content, making it suitable for commercial use cases and enterprise content creation at scale. The accuracy, efficiency, and versatility of the model currently rival leading source-available models. It is ideal for applications where content safety, legally licensed datasets, and bias mitigation are paramount. Developed by BRIA AI, RMBG v2.0 is available as a source-available model for non-commercial use. Bria RMBG2.0 is available as source-code and weights, ComfyUI nodes, or API endpoints. Commercial use is subject to a commercial agreement with BRIA. The model is released under a CC BY-NC 4.0 license for non-commercial use. BRIA RMBG-2.0 is a dichotomous image segmentation model trained exclusively on a professional-grade dataset. The model output includes a single-channel 8-bit grayscale alpha matte, where each pixel value indicates the opacity level of the corresponding pixel in the original image. This non-binary output approach offers developers the flexibility to define custom thresholds for foreground-background separation, catering to varied use cases and enhancing integration into complex pipelines. The Bria-RMBG model was trained with over 15,000 high-quality, high-resolution, manually labeled (pixel-wise accuracy), fully licensed images. The training data includes balanced gender, balanced ethnicity, and people with different types of disabilities. The distribution of images is as follows: 45.11% objects only, 25.24% people with objects/animals, 17.35% people only, 8.52% people/objects/animals with text, 2.52% text only, and 1.89% animals only. The distribution of image types is 87.70% photorealistic and 12.30% non-photorealistic. The distribution of background types is 52.05% non-solid background and 47.95% solid background. The distribution of foreground content is 51.42% single main foreground object and 48.58% multiple objects in the foreground. RMBG-2.0 is developed on an enhanced architecture with a proprietary dataset and training scheme, which significantly improves the model’s accuracy and effectiveness for background-removal tasks. If you use this model in your research, please cite the article titled 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation' by Zheng, Peng and Gao, Dehong and Fan, Deng-Ping and Liu, Li and Laaksonen, Jorma and Ouyang, Wanli and Sebe, Nicu, published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research in 2024. The model requires the following libraries: torch, torchvision, pillow, kornia, and transformers. To use the model, load the model from the 'briaai/RMBG-2.0' repository, preprocess the input image by resizing it to 1024x1024, normalizing it, and converting it to a tensor. Perform inference using the model, apply a sigmoid function to the output, and convert the result to a PIL image. Resize the mask to the original image size and apply it as an alpha channel to the image. Save the resulting image with the background removed.

instruction: I need to isolate the object in this image that matches the phrase 'a wooden boat on water'. Can you help?
description: Talk2DINO is a hybrid model that combines the spatial accuracy of DINOv2 with the language understanding capabilities of CLIP to address the task of Open-Vocabulary Segmentation (OVS). OVS involves segmenting images based on free-form textual concepts without predefined training classes. While CLIP can generate segmentation masks using Vision Transformers, it struggles with spatial localization due to global alignment of image and text features. On the other hand, self-supervised models like DINO excel in fine-grained visual encoding but lack language integration. Talk2DINO bridges this gap by aligning CLIP's textual embeddings with DINOv2's patch-level features through a learned mapping function, without fine-tuning the underlying backbones. During training, the model uses DINOv2's attention maps to selectively align local visual patches with textual embeddings. This approach enhances the segmentation process by producing more natural and less noisy segmentations and effectively distinguishing foreground objects from the background. Experimental results show that Talk2DINO achieves state-of-the-art performance on several unsupervised OVS benchmarks.

instruction: I need this short poem to be read aloud with a poetic and expressive voice—can you help?
description: GLM-TTS is a high-quality text-to-speech (TTS) synthesis system based on large language models, supporting zero-shot voice cloning and streaming inference. The system uses a two-stage architecture that combines an LLM for speech token generation and a Flow Matching model for waveform synthesis. By introducing a Multi-Reward Reinforcement Learning framework, GLM-TTS significantly improves the expressiveness of generated speech, achieving more natural emotional control compared to traditional TTS systems. Key features include the ability to clone any speaker's voice with just 3-10 seconds of prompt audio, the use of a multi-reward reinforcement learning framework (GRPO) to optimize prosody and emotion, high-quality speech synthesis comparable to commercial systems with reduced Character Error Rate (CER), support for 'Hybrid Phoneme + Text' input for precise pronunciation control, real-time audio generation suitable for interactive applications, and optimization for Chinese and English mixed text. The system follows a two-stage design: Stage 1 uses a Llama-based model to convert input text into speech token sequences, and Stage 2 uses a Flow model to convert token sequences into high-quality mel-spectrograms, which are then turned into waveforms by a vocoder. To address flat emotional expression, GLM-TTS uses a Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) algorithm with multiple reward functions (Similarity, CER, Emotion, Laughter) to align the LLM's generation strategy. Evaluated on seed-tts-eval, GLM-TTS_RL achieves the lowest Character Error Rate (CER) while maintaining high speaker similarity. The model GLM-TTS_RL achieves a CER of 0.89 and a SIM score of 76.4, outperforming other models such as Seed-TTS (CER 1.12, SIM 79.6), CosyVoice2 (CER 1.38, SIM 75.7), and F5-TTS (CER 1.53, SIM 76.0). GLM-TTS is open-source, while Seed-TTS is not. The system is designed to be used for real-time applications and supports phoneme-level control for precise pronunciation. It is optimized for both Chinese and English mixed text and is intended for use in interactive applications requiring streaming inference.

instruction: What does the depth look like from different angles in this image?
description: DA3 Small is a model for multi-view depth estimation and camera pose estimation. It is an efficient foundation model that uses a unified depth-ray representation. The model is part of the Any-view Model series, has 0.08B parameters, and is licensed under Apache 2.0. It supports relative depth estimation, pose estimation, and pose conditioning. The model is developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is based on a Vision Transformer for Visual Geometry with a plain transformer architecture. It is trained using public academic datasets only. A single plain transformer, such as a vanilla DINO encoder, is used as the backbone without architectural specialization. The depth-ray representation allows the model to avoid complex multi-task learning. The model significantly outperforms Depth Anything 2 for monocular depth estimation and VGGT for multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it is trained on academic datasets and may have limitations on domain-specific images. Performance can vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity.

instruction: What visual features are most prominent in this image of a night sky?
description: This is a DINOv3 ViT model image feature encoder, distilled on the LVD-1689M dataset from the DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model is designed to extract image features and supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. It is based on a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture with a large patch size of 16x16. The model has 303.1 million parameters, 82.4 GMACs, and 90.6 million activations, and processes images of size 256 x 256. The original model weights had all QKV projection biases set to zero, and for the `timm` implementation, the QKV bias is disabled (`qkv_bias=False`) for these models. Some model sizes have variants with `qkvb` in the name that have the bias enabled (`qkv_bias=True`) but set to zero, to match the behavior of `transformers` and original models. The original models store RoPE periods as a persistent `bfloat16` buffer, while `timm` generates `float32` periods at initialization, which may result in some numerical differences but appears to work as well or slightly better for fine-tuning. The model is evaluated on various benchmarks, including IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair, with performance metrics such as accuracy and mean intersection over union (mIoU) reported for different model variants. The model is also evaluated on satellite datasets such as SAT-493M for classification and segmentation tasks, with performance metrics reported for models like DINOv3 ViT-L/16 and DINOv3 ViT-7B/16. The model is associated with the DINOv3 paper, as well as the papers on Vision Transformers and PyTorch Image Models.

instruction: I need a 3D scene of a luxury apartment with a view of the city skyline—can you create that?
description: SpatialGen is a multi-view, multi-modal diffusion model designed to generate 3D indoor scenes conditioned on either a reference image or a textual description, given a 3D semantic layout. The model supports tasks such as image-to-3D scene generation and text-to-3D scene generation. It is intended for use in 3D scene generation applications and is based on a diffusion model architecture. The model has been developed as part of a research project and is associated with a paper published in the International Conference on 3D Vision in 2026. The model is currently available for inference, with a dataset containing 48 rooms and 4.8K rendered images provided for multi-view diffusion inference. The model is derived from another project and is licensed under the same license as that project. The model is tested with Python 3.10, PyTorch 2.3.1, and CUDA Version 12.1. Training instructions and a full dataset release are planned for future updates.

instruction: Is there a way to know if this recorded incident is violent or not?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of the VideoMAE architecture for binary violence detection in video content. It classifies videos into two categories: Violent Crime (1) and Non-Violent Incident (0). The model was trained on a curated subset of the UCF Crime dataset with event-based categorization, consisting of 600 videos, equally split between violent and non-violent categories. The event distribution includes 28 videos of abuse, 18 of arrest, 16 of arson, 62 of assault, 120 of burglary, 54 of explosion, 48 of fighting, 58 of road accidents, 184 of robbery, 36 of shoplifting, 46 of stealing, and 72 of vandalism. The dataset was split into 384 training videos, 96 validation videos, and 120 test videos. The model uses a VideoMAE Base architecture with a fine-tuning approach for event-based binary classification. It processes 16 frames per video clip at an input resolution of 224x224 pixels per frame. The model was trained for 15 epochs with a learning rate of 5e-05, a batch size of 2, and the AdamW optimizer. On the validation set, it achieved a loss of 1.4159, accuracy of 0.7188, precision of 0.7207, recall of 0.7188, and F1 score of 0.7190. On the test set, it achieved a loss of 1.7586, accuracy of 0.6833, precision of 0.6963, recall of 0.6833, and F1 score of 0.6802. The model was trained in 69.5 minutes and achieved a best accuracy of 0.7188. The model is intended for use in research and development in video analysis, security system enhancement with human oversight, and educational purposes in computer vision and AI safety. It is not suitable for unauthorized surveillance, discriminatory profiling, automated punishment, or privacy violations. The model may have limitations in generalizing to all types of violence, may miss context in longer sequences due to its 16-frame clip approach, and may be affected by environmental factors such as lighting, camera angles, and video quality. It may also misclassify intense but non-violent activities. The model was developed using the HuggingFace Transformers framework and is compatible with Apple Silicon MPS, CUDA, or CPU. It is based on the MCG-NJU/videomae-base model and is available for use with the HuggingFace Transformers library. The model was trained on 2025-06-02 11:52:12 UTC and is intended for use by researchers, security professionals, and developers.

instruction: Translate this paragraph about climate change from English to Spanish, please.
description: Seed-X is a series of open-source multilingual translation language models, including an instruction model, a reinforcement learning model, and a reward model. It is designed to push the boundaries of translation capabilities within a 7 billion parameter scale. Seed-X is intended as an accessible, off-the-shelf tool to support the community in advancing translation research and applications. It exhibits state-of-the-art translation capabilities, on par with or outperforming ultra-large models like Gemini-2.5, Claude-3.5, and GPT-4, as validated by human evaluations and automatic metrics. With a compact 7B parameter count and mistral architecture, Seed-X offers outstanding translation performance in a lightweight and efficient package, ideal for deployment and inference. It excels on a highly challenging translation test set spanning diverse domains, including the internet, science and technology, office dialogues, e-commerce, biomedicine, finance, law, literature, and entertainment. The Seed-X-PPO model is a causal language model that has undergone pretraining and post-training. It supports multilingual translation among 28 languages, including Arabic, Czech, Danish, German, English, Spanish, Finnish, French, Hungarian, Indonesian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Dutch, Norwegian Bokmal, Polish, Portuguese, Romanian, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian, Vietnamese, and Chinese. The model is specialized in multilingual translation and is not expected to support other tasks. The language tags at the end of the prompt are necessary for translation, as used in PPO training. The model does not have a chat template, so users should avoid prompting it in a multi-round conversation format. The model is evaluated on a diverse set of translation benchmarks, including FLORES-200, WMT-25, and a publicly released benchmark, accompanied by human evaluations. The model is licensed under OpenMDW. Users are recommended to use the Seed-X-PPO model, as its translation performance is superior to Seed-X-Instruct. Unofficial quantized versions for local deployment are not recommended, as the team plans to release an official quantized model and develop a demo on Hugging Face Space.

instruction: Could you extract the names of all the signatories in this document?
description: This model was trained from scratch on an unknown dataset. It achieves a loss of 3.7004 on the evaluation set. The model was trained using the following hyperparameters: a learning rate of 5e-05, a training batch size of 4, an evaluation batch size of 8, a seed of 42, the ADAMW_TORCH optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon of 1e-08, a cosine learning rate scheduler, 10 training epochs, and native AMP for mixed precision training. During training, the training loss decreased from 4.4903 at step 100 to 0.2683 at step 2200, while the validation loss fluctuated, reaching a minimum of 2.3696 at step 800 and a maximum of 3.7012 at step 2200. The model was developed using Transformers 4.56.1, PyTorch 2.6.0+cu124, Datasets 3.6.0, and Tokenizers 0.22.0.

instruction: What is the main subject of the text in the middle of the image?
description: The H2OVL-Mississippi-800M is a compact yet powerful vision-language model developed by H2O.ai, featuring 0.8 billion parameters. Despite its relatively small size, it achieves state-of-the-art performance in text recognition, particularly excelling in the Text Recognition segment of OCRBench and outperforming larger models in this domain. The model is built upon the architecture of H2O-Danube language models, extending their capabilities by integrating vision and language tasks. It is trained on 19 million image-text pairs, with a focus on OCR, document comprehension, and the interpretation of charts, figures, and tables. The model is optimized for superior OCR performance and is suitable for OCR and document processing due to the balance it achieves between performance and efficiency. The model is designed to support a range of tasks, including text recognition, document comprehension, and the interpretation of visual data such as charts and tables. It can be used for both pure-text conversations and image-based queries. The model includes a disclaimer that acknowledges potential biases and limitations in the generated content, as well as ethical considerations for its use. Users are encouraged to use the model responsibly and to report any issues with the generated content.

instruction: I have a concept in mind for a scene—can you turn it into a dynamic video using just a single image?
description: The Wan2.2 Distilled LoRA Models are high-performance video generation models that enable 4-step inference using LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) techniques. These models are designed to provide excellent generation quality while supporting flexible deployment options. The LoRA weights are extracted from Wan2.2 distilled models and can be combined with base models either through offline merging or online loading during inference. The models support multiple frameworks, including LightX2V and ComfyUI. They offer dual noise control, allowing users to choose between high noise for more creative and diverse outputs or low noise for more stable and input-faithful results. The LoRA models are compact in size, with a rank of 64, and can be further optimized by combining them with quantization techniques for storage efficiency and easy sharing. The 4-step inference process ensures ultra-fast generation of high-quality videos while maintaining the benefits of distilled models. The available LoRA models include both high noise and low noise variants for image-to-video (I2V) tasks. These models must be used in conjunction with the Wan2.2 I2V base model and require additional components such as T5 text encoder, CLIP vision encoder, VAE encoder/decoder, and tokenizer for full functionality. When using 4-step inference, the recommended denoising step list is [1000, 750, 500, 250].

instruction: What is the expected environmental distribution value for the compound 'C1=CC=C(C=C1)C(=O)O'?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict fugacity-based environmental fate parameter C. This property is used in fugacity models for environmental distribution prediction. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. The model type is AttentiveFP (Graph Neural Network), and it performs a regression task. It accepts SMILES strings as input and produces a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and is based on an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model's hyperparameters include a hidden channel size of 64, two layers, two timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.2, a learning rate of 0.001, a weight decay of 1e-05, a batch size of 32, 50 epochs, and a patience of 10. The model was trained on the FBC dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: Generate a written version of this Japanese voice recording with accurate timestamps.
description: Canary-1b-v2 is a 1-billion parameter model designed for high-quality speech transcription and translation across 25 European languages. It supports automatic speech recognition (ASR) for 25 languages, speech translation (AST) from English to 24 languages, and speech translation from 24 languages to English. The model is built on an encoder-decoder architecture with a FastConformer Encoder and a Transformer Decoder, featuring 32 encoder layers and 8 decoder layers, totaling 978 million parameters. It uses a unified SentencePiece Tokenizer with a vocabulary of 16,384 tokens optimized across all 25 languages. Canary-1b-v2 is trained using a 3-stage procedure on a multilingual speech recognition and translation dataset combining Nvidia's Granary dataset and NeMo ASR Set 3.0, with a total of 1.7 million training hours. The model achieves state-of-the-art performance among models of similar size, with comparable quality to models three times larger while being up to 10 times faster. It provides automatic punctuation and capitalization, accurate word-level and segment-level timestamps, and segment-level timestamps for translated outputs. Evaluation results show a Word Error Rate (WER) of 8.40% on Fleurs-25 Langs, 8.85% on CoVoST-13 Langs, and 7.27% on MLS-6 Langs for ASR. For speech translation, it achieves a COMET score of 79.30 on Fleurs-24 Langs and 77.48 on CoVoST-13 Langs for X→English, and 84.56 on Fleurs-24 Langs and 80.29 on CoVoST-5 Langs for English→X. The model is robust to noise, with a WER of 2.18% at 100 dB SNR and 19.38% at -5 dB SNR. It also performs well on long-form transcription, achieving a WER of 13.78% on Earnings-22 and 9.87% on This American Life. Canary-1b-v2 is released under a permissive CC BY 4.0 license and is designed to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems for faster training and inference. It is intended for use by developers, researchers, academics, and industries building applications that require speech-to-text capabilities, including conversational AI, voice assistants, transcription services, subtitle generation, and voice analytics platforms. The model is available for use in the NeMo toolkit and can be used as a pre-trained checkpoint for inference or fine-tuning on another dataset. It supports input in 16kHz audio formats such as .wav and .flac, and outputs text with punctuation and capitalization included. The model is compatible with NVIDIA Ampere, Hopper, and Blackwell microarchitectures and requires at least 6GB RAM for model loading. Canary-1b-v2 is the first model from the NeMo team to leverage the full Nvidia Granary dataset, showcasing its multitask and multilingual capabilities. The model is verified to meet prescribed NVIDIA quality standards and is evaluated using metrics such as Word Error Rate (WER), BLEU score, and COMET score. Technical limitations include potential inaccuracies in transcripts and translations, which may vary based on source and target language and input audio characteristics. The model is not recommended for word-for-word or incomplete sentences as accuracy may vary based on context. Ethical considerations include ensuring the model meets requirements for the relevant industry and use case and addressing unforeseen product misuse. The model is not known to generate or reverse engineer personal data, and all datasets used in training have provenance. Data labeling complies with privacy laws, but data correction or removal is not possible with externally-sourced data. The model is not intended for life-critical applications and use is restricted by the license. Model and dataset restrictions include the application of the Principle of least privilege and adherence to dataset license constraints.

instruction: Can you translate this explanation of a scientific concept from English into Japanese, ensuring clarity and accuracy?
description: Hunyuan Translation Model Version 1.5 includes a 1.8B translation model, HY-MT1.5-1.8B, and a 7B translation model, HY-MT1.5-7B. Both models focus on supporting mutual translation across 33 languages and incorporating 5 ethnic and dialect variations. Among them, HY-MT1.5-7B is an upgraded version of the WMT25 championship model, optimized for explanatory translation and mixed-language scenarios, with newly added support for terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. Despite having less than one-third the parameters of HY-MT1.5-7B, HY-MT1.5-1.8B delivers translation performance comparable to its larger counterpart, achieving both high speed and high quality. After quantization, the 1.8B model can be deployed on edge devices and support real-time translation scenarios, making it widely applicable. HY-MT1.5-1.8B achieves the industry-leading performance among models of the same size, surpassing most commercial translation APIs. HY-MT1.5-1.8B supports deployment on edge devices and real-time translation scenarios, offering broad applicability. HY-MT1.5-7B, compared to its September open-source version, has been optimized for annotated and mixed-language scenarios. Both models support terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation.

instruction: Can you explain the musical structure of this song, including its verses and choruses?
description: Music Flamingo (MF) is a fully open, state-of-the-art Large Audio-Language Model (LALM) designed to advance music (including song) understanding in foundational audio models. It brings together innovations in deep music understanding across songs and instrumentals, rich, theory-aware captions and question answering (harmony, structure, timbre, lyrics, cultural context), reasoning-centric training using chain-of-thought and reinforcement learning with custom rewards for step-by-step reasoning, and long-form song reasoning over full-length, multicultural audio (extended context). Extensive evaluations confirm Music Flamingo's effectiveness, setting new benchmarks on over 10+ public music understanding and reasoning tasks. The model is intended for non-commercial research purposes only. Music Flamingo is supported in HuggingFace Transformers and processes audio in 30-second windows with a 10-minute total cap per sample. Longer inputs are truncated. It supports single-turn audio and text instruction, batch multiple conversations, text-only and audio-only prompts, and training or fine-tuning. The model is based on a Transformer architecture with a decoder-only LLM backbone (Qwen2.5-7B), an AF-Whisper unified audio encoder from Audio Flamingo 3, and an MLP-based audio adaptor. It has 8 billion parameters. The model accepts music (song or instrumental) and text as input, with audio in WAV/MP3/FLAC formats and text in UTF-8. The maximum audio length is 20 minutes, and the maximum text length is 24,000 tokens. The output is text (and optional speech) in UTF-8 string format, with a maximum text length of 2048 tokens. The model is designed and optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems (A100/H100), leveraging NVIDIA’s hardware and software frameworks for faster training and inference. It is compatible with PyTorch and HuggingFace Transformers and supports NVIDIA Ampere (A100) and Hopper (H100) hardware on Linux. The model was trained entirely on music data collected from various sources, with training data size ranging from 10,000 to 1 million hours. The data collection method for training data is human, with labeling methods including both human and automated. The model is evaluated on test splits of datasets with human data collection and labeling methods. The model version is v1.0. Ethical considerations include the belief in Trustworthy AI and the responsibility of developers to ensure the model meets requirements for the relevant industry and use case and addresses unforeseen product misuse. The model is built with Audio Flamingo 3, Qwen, NVILA, and the open audio-ML community.

instruction: Can you generate a 3D Gaussian representation of a moving object from a video sequence?
description: HunyuanWorld-Mirror is a versatile feed-forward model for comprehensive 3D geometric prediction. It integrates diverse geometric priors, including camera poses, calibrated intrinsics, and depth maps, and simultaneously generates various 3D representations, such as point clouds, multi-view depths, camera parameters, surface normals, and 3D Gaussians, in a single forward pass. The model consists of two key components: Multi-Modal Prior Prompting, which embeds diverse prior modalities into the feed-forward model using lightweight encoding layers to convert each modality into structured tokens, and Universal Geometric Prediction, a unified architecture capable of handling the full spectrum of 3D reconstruction tasks, including camera and depth estimation, point map regression, surface normal estimation, and novel view synthesis.

instruction: Please highlight all the proteins and their families mentioned in this scientific article.
description: This model is a state-of-the-art fine-tuned transformer designed to deliver enterprise-grade accuracy for biomedical entity recognition. It is specialized for identifying and extracting various biomedical entities from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is production-ready and suitable for clinical and research applications. The model can identify and classify biomedical entities including B-protein, B-protein_complex, B-protein_enum, B-protein_familiy_or_group, B-protein_variant, I-protein, I-protein_complex, I-protein_enum, I-protein_familiy_or_group, and I-protein_variant. It is trained on the FSU dataset, a biomedical NER dataset focused on protein interactions and molecular biology entities for systems biology research. The dataset includes annotations for proteins, protein complexes, protein families, protein variants, and molecular interaction entities relevant to systems biology and biochemistry research. The model is based on the deberta-v3-small architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling, and validated through cross-validation on a held-out test set. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.92, precision of 0.91, recall of 0.93, and accuracy of 0.97. It is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem and supports various aggregation strategies for entity prediction. The model is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0.

instruction: Please provide a ranked list of possible labels for this image along with their probabilities.
description: This is a DINOv3 ConvNeXt image feature model, designed for image classification and as a feature backbone. It is pretrained on the LVD-1689M dataset using the self-supervised DINOv3 method and distilled from a DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model has 27.8 million parameters, 4.5 GMACs, and 13.4 million activations. It processes images of size 224 x 224. The model supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. For image classification, it can be used to generate top 5 probabilities and class indices. For feature map extraction, it outputs feature maps with varying shapes, such as [1, 96, 56, 56], [1, 192, 28, 28], [1, 384, 14, 14], and [1, 768, 7, 7]. For image embeddings, it can produce a tensor of shape (batch_size, num_features) by removing the classifier or using forward features and forward head methods. The model is based on the research described in the papers 'DINOv3', 'A ConvNet for the 2020s', and 'PyTorch Image Models'.

instruction: Draw a fantasy village built into the side of a giant, ancient tree.
description: This is a GGUF quantized version of the FLUX.2 [dev] model, which uses a methodology for state-of-the-art performance, with important layers upcasted to higher precision. FLUX.2 [dev] is a 32 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating, editing, and combining images based on text instructions. It is designed for open text-to-image generation, single-reference editing, and multi-reference editing, allowing character, object, and style reference without the need for additional training. The model is trained using guidance distillation, making it more efficient. It has open weights to support scientific research and empower artists to develop innovative workflows. Generated outputs can be used for personal, scientific, and commercial purposes as described in the FLUX [dev] Non-Commercial License. A reference implementation and sampling code are provided for developers and creatives to build upon the model. FLUX.2 [dev] is available in both quantized and non-quantized versions. For local deployment on consumer-grade graphics cards, such as an RTX 4090 or RTX 5090, instructions are available on the GitHub page. Black Forest Labs has evaluated and mitigated a number of risks in the model, including the generation of unlawful content such as child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and nonconsensual intimate imagery (NCII). Pre-training mitigation included filtering pre-training data for NSFW and known CSAM content, with collaboration from the Internet Watch Foundation. Post-training mitigation involved targeted fine-tuning to inhibit certain behaviors and suppress concepts that could lead to synthetic CSAM or NCII. Ongoing evaluations, including internal and external third-party testing, were conducted to identify and address residual risks. The final FLUX.2 [dev] checkpoint demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs in complex generation and editing tasks and showed higher resilience than leading open-weight models. The model includes filters for NSFW and IP-infringing content at input and output, and developers must use these filters or manual review processes under the terms of the FLUX.2 [dev] Non-Commercial License. Inference filters are applied to the API for FLUX.2 Pro, using both in-house and third-party filters to prevent CSAM and NCII outputs. The model also includes content provenance features, such as pixel-layer watermarking and links to the C2PA standard for metadata. The FLUX.2 Pro API applies cryptographically-signed C2PA metadata to output content. Use of the model and access to the API are governed by the FLUX [dev] Non-Commercial License, Developer Terms of Service, and Usage Policy, which prohibit the generation of unlawful content or the use of generated content for unlawful, defamatory, or abusive purposes. The model is monitored for patterns of violative use after release, and takedown requests are issued to websites or businesses that misuse the model. Users or developers who repeatedly violate policies may be banned. A dedicated email address is provided for community feedback, and the company maintains reporting relationships with organizations such as the Internet Watch Foundation and the National Center for Missing and Exploited Children.

instruction: Can you generate a 3D Gaussian representation of the objects in this image?
description: DA3 Giant is a model for multi-view depth estimation, camera pose estimation, and 3D Gaussian estimation. It is the flagship foundation model that uses a unified depth-ray representation. The model is part of the Any-view Model series and contains 1.15 billion parameters. It is licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0, which restricts its use to non-commercial purposes. The model supports relative depth estimation, pose estimation, pose conditioning, and 3D Gaussian estimation. It is developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is based on a vision transformer architecture for visual geometry. The model uses a plain transformer with a unified depth-ray representation and is trained exclusively on public academic datasets. A single plain transformer, such as a vanilla DINO encoder, is used as the backbone without architectural specialization. The depth-ray representation allows the model to avoid complex multi-task learning. The model significantly outperforms Depth Anything 2 for monocular depth estimation and VGGT for multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it is trained on academic datasets and may have limitations when applied to domain-specific images. Performance can vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity. The model is intended for non-commercial use only due to its CC BY-NC 4.0 license.

instruction: Based on the provided context, what hardware was used for training?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of sberbank-ai/ruBert-large designed for the task of document question answering. It has been adapted specifically for extracting answers from a provided document context and fine-tuned on a custom JSON dataset containing context, question, and answer triples. The model was fine-tuned using the Transformers library with a custom training pipeline. A loader reads a JSON file containing context, question, and answer triples. The script tokenizes the document and question with a sliding window approach to handle long texts. The training process leverages mixed precision training and the AdamW optimizer to improve optimization. The training script evaluates model performance on a validation set, saves checkpoints, and employs early stopping based on validation loss. The model was trained for 20 epochs with a batch size of 4 per device, using 0.1 of total steps for warmup. FP16 training was enabled if CUDA was available. Training was performed on an 1xRTX 3070. The input is formed by concatenating the question and the document context, guiding the model to focus on the relevant segments. This model is ideal for interactive document question answering tasks, making it a powerful tool for applications such as customer support, document search, and automated Q&A systems. While primarily focused on Russian texts, the model also supports English language inputs. The model also supports English language, but its support was not tested.

instruction: Generate a video of a bustling city at night with vibrant lights, traffic, and animated crowds.
description: This model is a GGUF conversion of the Wan2.2 T2V A14B model integrated with VACE scopes, intended for experimental use only. It is based on the Wan2.2 T2V A14B model, which is licensed under Apache 2.0 and permits commercial use of generated content. The model is provided in two versions: a high-noise model for initial denoising steps and a low-noise model for detail refinement. Both models are in FP16 precision within GGUF container format, not quantized. The base model combines Wan2.2 T2V A14B with VACE scopes, and the model uses a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture. The high-noise expert is used for early denoising, focusing on layout and motion, while the low-noise expert is used for refining textures and details. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). The model incorporates VACE (Video Aesthetic Control Embedding), which enhances cinematic-level aesthetics and allows fine control over lighting, composition, contrast, and color tone. The model supports text-to-video generation at 480p and 720p resolutions, with high-definition motion generation at 720p@24fps on consumer GPUs. The model files are approximately 34.7 GB each and are intended to be used with the ComfyUI-GGUF extension. The model has known limitations, including color shifting issues similar to the original lym00 model, and is not recommended for production environments without thorough testing. Commercial use is allowed under the Apache 2.0 license, but users are responsible for ensuring compliance with laws and ethical use. The model is not quantized, and the FP16 format provides maximum quality at the cost of larger file sizes. The model is experimental, and output quality may vary. The VACE team is reportedly working on a fix for the color shifting issue, but until then, the model should be avoided for applications requiring color accuracy.

instruction: Can you determine if these two images depict the same scene?
description: The Perception Encoder (PE) is a state-of-the-art encoder for image and video understanding, trained using a simple vision-language learning approach. It was introduced by Meta and is part of a family of large-scale vision encoder models with strong performance on a wide range of vision tasks. PE leverages a robust contrastive pretraining recipe and is finetuned on synthetically aligned videos, enabling it to outperform existing models on classification and retrieval tasks. It also produces strong, general features that scale well for downstream applications. PE core is the base model, trained with a robust image pretraining schedule and finetuned on data generated by a synthetic video data engine. PE core currently comes in three sizes: PE core G is the main checkpoint, with L and B models distilled from it. The L and B models include a class token for global aggregation in addition to an attention pooling block with 8 heads on top of the vision tower. PE core achieves strong results on zero-shot image classification and retrieval, as well as zero-shot video classification and retrieval. It performs particularly well on challenging benchmarks such as ObjectNet and ImageNet-A. The model is designed for image and text feature extraction and supports tasks such as classification and retrieval. It is based on a contrastive pretraining approach and is compatible with the OpenCLIP framework. The model is available for use through the OpenCLIP library and can be accessed via the provided GitHub repositories. The model is described in detail in the associated technical report and research papers.
