instruction: Create a realistic photo of a red apple with a bite taken out of it, placed on a wooden table with soft lighting.
description: Qwen-Image is an image generation foundation model in the Qwen series that achieves significant advances in complex text rendering and precise image editing. It demonstrates strong general capabilities in both image generation and editing, with exceptional performance in text rendering, especially for Chinese. The model supports a wide range of artistic styles, from photorealistic scenes to impressionist paintings, anime aesthetics, and minimalist design. It enables advanced image editing operations such as style transfer, object insertion or removal, detail enhancement, text editing within images, and human pose manipulation. Qwen-Image also supports image understanding tasks, including object detection, semantic segmentation, depth and edge (Canny) estimation, novel view synthesis, and super-resolution. These capabilities are powered by deep visual comprehension, allowing the model to adapt fluidly to creative prompts and provide intuitive input with coherent output. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0.

instruction: I want a voice sample that sounds like it's from a popular anime show—can you make one from this text?
description: Anime-XCodec2-44.1kHz-v2 is a fine-tuned variant of NandemoGHS/Anime-XCodec2, designed to produce 44.1kHz high-fidelity Japanese speech, particularly in anime and game-style voices. It incorporates upsampling layers and RMS loss, inspired by Inworld TTS-1, and was trained on approximately 22,000 hours of Japanese speech. This version includes updates to the upsampler parameters, loss configurations, and a fix for a RoPE bug from the original XCodec2. Only the decoder was updated; the encoder and codebook remain frozen, ensuring speech tokens are identical to the original XCodec2. This makes the model a drop-in decoder for systems already using XCodec2 tokens, such as Llasa. The model accepts 16kHz input and outputs 44.1kHz audio, and can be used for decoding XCodec2 speech tokens into high-fidelity Japanese speech, upgrading existing pipelines to 44.1kHz output, or as a form of audio super-resolution, though the latter use is untested. The model modifies the original XCodec2 architecture and requires a custom xcodec2 library version (v0.1.7 or later) for inference, as older versions will not work. The model is optimized for Japanese and anime/game-style voices, and may perform poorly on other languages or domains. It is based on NandemoGHS/Anime-XCodec2 (16kHz), with modifications including the integration of an UpSamplerBlock into the decoder, the adoption of RMS Loss in addition to original losses, and the freezing of the encoder and codebook. Key updates in v2 include a RoPE bug fix, changes to upsampler settings, a switch in the perceptual loss model, and adjustments to the spectral discriminator for 44.1kHz audio. The model is licensed under CC-BY-NC 4.0, inherited from XCodec2 and Anime-XCodec2. It acknowledges HKUSTAudio/xcodec2 as the original model, Inworld AI for upsampler architecture and RMS Loss, imprt for the kushinada-hubert-large model used in perceptual loss, and the community for Japanese speech resources.

instruction: Look for the slide that includes a diagram of a computer network architecture.
description: ColQwen2.5-Omni is a model based on a novel architecture and training strategy derived from Omnimodal Language Models, designed to efficiently index documents using their visual features. It is an extension of Qwen2.5-Omni-3B that generates multi-vector representations of text and images. The model takes dynamic image resolutions as input without resizing, which may alter the aspect ratio, similar to ColPali. The maximum resolution is set to produce at most 1024 image patches, with experiments showing improvements in performance with more patches, though at the cost of increased memory requirements. This version is trained using colpali-engine version 0.3.11 and uses the same data as described in the ColPali paper. The training dataset consists of 127,460 query-page pairs, with 63% from openly available academic datasets and 37% from a synthetic dataset made of web-crawled PDF pages augmented with pseudo-questions generated by a Vision Language Model (VLM). The training set is entirely in English, allowing for the study of zero-shot generalization to non-English languages. No multi-page PDF document is used in both the training and evaluation sets to avoid contamination. A validation set of 2% of the samples is used for hyperparameter tuning. The audio retrieval capabilities are acquired in a zero-shot manner, as the training data is purely image-text matching, with the audio and vision towers frozen during training. The model supports tasks involving the retrieval of documents based on visual and audio features. It is intended to be used with the colpali-engine version 0.3.11 or higher. The model is evaluated using a dataset of 500 audio samples from the 'dailytalk-conversations-grouped' dataset, and retrieval results are obtained by scoring query embeddings against document embeddings. The model is available for use with specific installation and processing instructions, including the use of a processor for audio and query processing. The model is developed by Manuel Faysse and Antonio Loison, and its methodology is described in a paper available on arXiv. If used in research, the model should be cited according to the provided BibTeX entry.

instruction: Can you generate a short video where a woman's face is shown with a bubblegum bubble that looks like an underwater scene?
description: This model is a ControlNet module designed for the Wan2.2 A14B text-to-video generation system, specifically for depth-based control. It follows the same approach as other ControlNet implementations for Wan2.2. The model is intended to be used with the ComfyUI framework and requires the latest version of the diffusers library. It supports inference tasks for generating video content based on textual prompts and depth control inputs. The architecture includes a custom WanControlnet component, a transformer model, and an autoencoder. Performance is demonstrated through inference examples using a CLI and code, where the model processes a video input and generates a controlled video output based on a detailed prompt. The model is evaluated using a prompt that describes a complex visual scene involving a close-up shot of a young woman's face with a bubblegum bubble containing a miniature aquarium. The model is trained on unspecified data and is available for use with specific model paths. Caveats include the requirement for specific software versions and hardware, such as CUDA-compatible devices, and the need for manual installation of dependencies. The model is provided under a GitHub repository, and users are directed to raise issues or seek professional support through the repository or via email.

instruction: I need to match a video clip with the best corresponding paragraph from a script. How can I get a relevance score for each pair?
description: The Qwen3-VL-Reranker-2B is a multimodal reranking model designed for cross-modal understanding and information retrieval. It is part of the Qwen3-VL model series, which includes the Qwen3-VL-Embedding and Qwen3-VL-Reranker models. These models are built upon the Qwen3-VL foundation model and are specifically engineered to handle diverse input modalities, including text, images, screenshots, and videos, as well as combinations of these modalities. The Embedding model generates high-dimensional vectors for tasks like retrieval and clustering, while the Reranker model refines these results by taking a (query, document) pair as input and outputting a precise relevance score. The two models are typically used together in a two-stage pipeline, where the Embedding model performs initial recall and the Reranker model refines the results, significantly improving retrieval accuracy. The Qwen3-VL-Reranker-2B supports over 30 languages and is capable of handling arbitrary multimodal combinations. It has 2 billion parameters and a context length of 32,000 tokens. The model supports quantization post-processing, custom dimensions for the final embedding (MRL), and instruction-aware customization for specific tasks. Evaluation results show that the Qwen3-VL-Reranker-2B outperforms the base embedding model and baseline rerankers on various retrieval tasks, with performance metrics including an average score of 75.1 on the MMEB-v2 retrieval benchmark, 73.8 for image retrieval, 52.1 for video retrieval, 83.4 for visual document retrieval, and 70.0 on the MMTEB retrieval benchmark. The model is designed for practical use in real-world applications, offering flexibility in vector dimensions, strong performance with quantized embeddings, and the ability to integrate into existing pipelines. Developers are advised to use tailored instructions for specific tasks and to write instructions in English for optimal performance in multilingual contexts.

instruction: I need a photorealistic image from a normal map—can you ensure the orientation and depth are correct?
description: ControlNet v1.1 is a neural network structure designed to control diffusion models by adding extra conditions. It is the successor to an earlier version and was developed by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala. This checkpoint is a conversion into `diffusers` format and is intended to be used in combination with Stable Diffusion. The model is a diffusion-based text-to-image generation model and is primarily associated with the English language. The license is adapted from the work of others in the area of responsible AI licensing. The model is conditioned on normalbae images, which are images containing surface normal information, typically represented as color-coded images. The preprocessor in Normal 1.1 is trained to estimate normal maps using a more correct protocol (NYU-V2's visualization method), making it more reasonable and physically correct compared to the previous version. This improvement allows the model to interpret real normal maps from rendering engines, provided the colors are correct (blue is front, red is left, green is top). In testing, the model has shown robust performance and can achieve results similar to the depth model. The previous Normal 1.0 model was not frequently used due to its limitations, but Normal 1.1 has significant improvements and potential for more frequent use.

instruction: I have a chemical with the SMILES 'C1=CC=C(C=C1)S(=O)(=O)O'. What would be its acid dissociation constant in aqueous solution?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict the acid dissociation constant (pKa) for acidic groups. This property predicts the pH at which acidic functional groups donate protons, affecting ionization state and bioavailability. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. The model type is AttentiveFP (Graph Neural Network), and it performs a regression task. It accepts SMILES strings as input and produces a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and is based on an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model's hyperparameters include a hidden channel size of 128, three layers, three timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.1, a learning rate of 0.0005, a weight decay of 0.0001, a batch size of 32, 50 epochs, and a patience of 10. The model was trained on the pKa_acidic dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

description: 이 모델은 한국어 텍스트를 6가지 감정인 분노, 불안, 슬픔, 평온, 당황, 기쁨으로 분류하도록 설계되었습니다. `klue/roberta-base` 모델을 기반으로 파인튜닝되었습니다. 감정 분류를 수행하는 데 사용되며, 각 감정에 대해 정밀도, 재현율, F1 점수를 평가했습니다. 분노의 경우 정밀도 0.9801, 재현율 0.9788, F1 점수 0.9795를 기록했으며, 불안은 정밀도 0.9864, 재현율 0.9848, F1 점수 0.9856를 보였습니다. 슬픔의 정밀도는 0.9837, 재현율 0.9854, F1 점수 0.9845이며, 평온의 정밀도는 0.9782, 재현율 0.9750, F1 점수 0.9766입니다. 당황의 정밀도는 0.9607, 재현율 0.9668, F1 점수 0.9652이며, 기쁨의 정밀도는 0.9857, 재현율 0.9886, F1 점수 0.9872입니다. 전체 정확도는 0.9831이며, 매크로 평균 정밀도는 0.9791, 재현율은 0.9804, F1 점수는 0.9798입니다. 가중 평균 정밀도는 0.9831, 재현율은 0.9831, F1 점수는 0.9831입니다.

instruction: Create a sample image of a cat in RGB format from the CIFAR-10 categories.
description: This model is an EMA (Exponential Moving Average) version of the DDPM (Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models) trained on the CIFAR-10 dataset. It is based on the original model but uses exponential moving averages of model parameters for improved stability and quality. The model is designed for unconditional image generation and implements the DDPM approach described in the paper by Jonathan Ho, Ajay Jain, and Pieter Abbeel. The EMA version provides more stable training and often better sample quality by maintaining exponentially weighted averages of model parameters. The model generates high-quality 32×32 pixel images from all 10 CIFAR-10 categories: airplane, automobile, bird, cat, deer, dog, frog, horse, ship, and truck. It is fully compatible with the Hugging Face Diffusers library. The model uses a U-Net architecture and is trained on the CIFAR-10 dataset, which consists of 50,000 training images of 32×32 RGB pixels. The training procedure is an EMA version of standard DDPM training, with exponential moving averages applied to model weights. The training objective is the variational lower bound on negative log likelihood. The EMA version typically provides improved stability, better sample quality, and reduced mode collapse, often achieving better FID scores compared to non-EMA versions. Approximate performance metrics include FID scores of 4.5216 for 50K .png samples generated by the DDIM with 100 sampling steps, and 6.5398 for 10K .png samples generated by the DDIM with 100 sampling steps. The model generates diverse samples across all CIFAR-10 categories, with all generated images being 32×32 pixels in RGB format. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 License.

instruction: I have two images of a park from different times of day; can you find the matching points?
description: The LightGlue model was proposed by Philipp Lindenberger, Paul-Edouard Sarlin, and Marc Pollefeys. It is a deep neural network designed to match local features across images. The model matches two sets of interest points detected in an image and, when paired with a keypoint detector, can be used to match two images and estimate the pose between them. It is useful for tasks such as image matching, homography estimation, and other multiple-view geometry problems. LightGlue builds on the success of SuperGlue and introduces improvements that make it more efficient in terms of memory and computation, more accurate, and easier to train. One key property is that LightGlue is adaptive to the difficulty of the problem: inference is faster on image pairs that are easier to match, such as those with a larger visual overlap or limited appearance change. The model can stop inference at an early layer if all predictions are ready, and it discards points that are deemed not matchable early from further steps. This adaptability makes LightGlue faster, more accurate, and easier to train than SuperGlue. LightGlue is developed by ETH Zurich - Computer Vision and Geometry Lab and is categorized as an image matching model. It is licensed for academic or non-profit, non-commercial research use only, implied by the use of SuperPoint as its keypoint detector. The model is designed for feature matching and pose estimation tasks in computer vision and can handle challenging real-world indoor and outdoor environments. However, it may not perform well on tasks that require different types of visual understanding, such as object detection or image classification. LightGlue is trained on large annotated datasets for pose estimation, enabling it to learn priors for pose estimation and reason about the 3D scene. The training data consists of image pairs with ground truth correspondences and unmatched keypoints derived from ground truth poses and depth maps. LightGlue follows the supervised training setup of SuperGlue, first pre-trained with synthetic homographies sampled from 1 million images and then fine-tuned with the MegaDepth dataset, which includes 1 million crowd-sourced images of 196 tourism landmarks with camera calibration and poses recovered by SfM and dense depth by multi-view stereo. LightGlue is trained using the fp32 training regime. It is designed to be efficient and runs in real-time on a modern GPU, with a forward pass taking approximately 44 milliseconds (22 FPS) for an image pair. The model has 13.7 million parameters, making it relatively compact compared to some other deep learning models. Its inference speed is suitable for real-time applications and can be readily integrated into modern Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) or Structure-from-Motion (SfM) systems.

instruction: What is the content of this handwritten note, and can you transcribe it clearly?
description: Qwen3-VL is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced Thinking editions for flexible deployment. Key enhancements include the ability to operate PC and mobile GUIs by recognizing elements, understanding functions, invoking tools, and completing tasks. It can generate Draw.io, HTML, CSS, and JS from images and videos. The model supports advanced spatial perception, including judging object positions, viewpoints, and occlusions, with capabilities for 2D and 3D grounding. It has a native 256K context length, expandable to 1M, enabling it to handle books and hours-long video with full recall and second-level indexing. The model excels in enhanced multimodal reasoning, particularly in STEM and Math, with causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. It features upgraded visual recognition with broader, higher-quality pretraining that can recognize a wide range of subjects, including celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. The model supports expanded OCR in 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare or ancient characters and jargon. It also provides improved long-document structure parsing and text understanding comparable to pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE for full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings, enhancing long-horizon video reasoning. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. This is the weight repository for Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Instruct.

instruction: Could you process this image and highlight the important visual details?
description: This is a DINOv3 ViT model image feature encoder, distilled on the LVD-1689M dataset from the DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model is designed to extract image features and supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. It is based on a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture with a large patch size of 16x16. The model has 303.1 million parameters, 82.4 GMACs, and 90.6 million activations, and processes images of size 256 x 256. The original model weights had all QKV projection biases set to zero, and for the `timm` implementation, the QKV bias is disabled (`qkv_bias=False`) for these models. Some model sizes have variants with `qkvb` in the name that have the bias enabled (`qkv_bias=True`) but set to zero, to match the behavior of `transformers` and original models. The original models store RoPE periods as a persistent `bfloat16` buffer, while `timm` generates `float32` periods at initialization, which may result in some numerical differences but appears to work as well or slightly better for fine-tuning. The model is evaluated on various benchmarks, including IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair, with performance metrics such as accuracy and mean intersection over union (mIoU) reported for different model variants. The model is also evaluated on satellite datasets such as SAT-493M for classification and segmentation tasks, with performance metrics reported for models like DINOv3 ViT-L/16 and DINOv3 ViT-7B/16. The model is associated with the DINOv3 paper, as well as the papers on Vision Transformers and PyTorch Image Models.

instruction: What part of this presentation discusses the benefits of adopting cloud-based solutions?
description: TomoroAI/tomoro-colqwen3-embed-8b is a state-of-the-art multimodal embedding model that maps text queries, visual documents (images, PDFs), or short videos into aligned multi-vector embeddings. It is built by merging components with a full vision stack, inheriting robust text retrieval capabilities. The model has been fine-tuned on a curated mixture of data sources and achieves state-of-the-art or competitive performance across ViDoRe V1-V3 in both English and multilingual settings. It offers a significantly reduced embedding footprint compared to other full-dimension Colpali model alternatives. The model is based on a Qwen3-VL 8B architecture with an encoder-only variant and a 320-dimension projection head. It uses a ColPali-style late interaction methodology with MaxSim scoring. It supports up to 1,280 visual tokens per page or 5,120 visual tokens per video, with text prompts constrained by the base context window of 32,000 tokens. The model is capable of handling complex layouts, such as chart-rich PDFs and domain-specific documents, and supports end-to-end retrieval of unseen multimodal documents without requiring OCR or an intermediate vision LLM for summaries. It demonstrates strong performance on non-English document inputs and inherits strong text retrieval performance from the Qwen3-Embedding-8B model. However, it is not fine-tuned on large-scale video retrieval datasets, and its video support is based on preliminary findings. The model is larger than single-vector baselines despite having a smaller token dimension. It is not currently fine-tuned with diverse system instructions, and improvements are planned using more synthetic datasets. The model is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license, with upstream Qwen checkpoints retaining their community licenses. Training data includes ViDoRe/MTEB corpora and synthetic VisRAG assets. The model is applied to enhance Tomoro’s customized multimodal agentic RAG pipelines, enabling autonomous agents to parse, reason over, and retrieve from large-scale enterprise internal documentation. It supports Tomoro AI's mission to deliver high-quality enterprise multimodal solutions across high-stakes industries.

instruction: Generate a video of a dancer performing in front of a colorful abstract background.
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video generation models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video and image-to-video capabilities. The MoE architecture in Wan2.2 separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity while maintaining computational efficiency. The model includes a high-noise expert for early-stage layout and a low-noise expert for later-stage detail refinement, with a transition point determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). The A14B model series, part of Wan2.2, has two experts with approximately 14B parameters each, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active per step. Wan2.2 also features a 5B dense model, TI2V-5B, supported by a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE with a compression ratio of 4×16×16, increasing the overall compression rate to 64 while maintaining high-quality video reconstruction. TI2V-5B supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps and can run on consumer-grade GPUs like the 4090. The model is trained on significantly larger data compared to its predecessor, with +65.6% more images and +83.2% more videos, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. Wan2.2 achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models and is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available. The repository also includes the I2V-A14B model for image-to-video generation, supporting 480P and 720P resolutions with a MoE architecture for more stable video synthesis and enhanced support for diverse stylized scenes. The model is available for download via Hugging Face and ModelScope, with integration into ComfyUI and Diffusers. Computational efficiency tests show that Wan2.2 models perform well on different GPUs, with specific parameter settings for multi-GPU and single-GPU inference. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license terms and applicable laws.

instruction: Please determine the intent behind the words in this audio message.
description: SEW-D-tiny is a base model pretrained on 16kHz sampled speech audio. It is designed to be fine-tuned on downstream tasks such as Automatic Speech Recognition, Speaker Identification, Intent Classification, and Emotion Recognition. The model is part of the SEW (Squeezed and Efficient Wav2vec) architecture, which is optimized for performance and efficiency in pre-trained models for automatic speech recognition. SEW-D-tiny demonstrates significant improvements in both performance and efficiency compared to wav2vec 2.0. For example, under the 100h-960h semi-supervised setup on LibriSpeech, SEW achieves a 1.9x inference speedup compared to wav2vec 2.0, with a 13.5% relative reduction in word error rate. With a similar inference time, SEW reduces word error rate by 25-50% across different model sizes. When using the model, it is important to ensure that the speech input is also sampled at 16kHz. Evaluation results on LibriSpeech's 'clean' and 'other' test data show a word error rate (WER) of 10.47 and 22.73, respectively.

instruction: Создай 3D-объект, который будет похож на куб, но с цветом, напоминающим синюю гамму.
description: alpha3D-v1 — это экспериментальная легковесная нейросеть для генерации 3D-объектов по текстовому описанию. Модель преобразует текстовые эмбеддинги в цветные облака точек. Она обучена на синтетическом датасете геометрических примитивов. Модель поддерживает генерацию форм, таких как куб и сфера, а также цветов: красный, зеленый и синий. Она умеет смешивать формы и цвета, например, создавать фиолетовый кубо-шар. Вывод модели представляет собой облако из 1000 точек, каждая из которых содержит координаты XYZ и значения цвета RGB. Архитектура модели — это многослойный перцептрон (MLP), в котором входной one-hot вектор размерностью 5 (для цветов и форм) проходит через последовательность слоев: 128, 512, 1024 нейронов, и заканчивается выходным слоем размерностью 6000, соответствующим 1000 точкам с 6 значениями. Модель обучалась с использованием функции потерь MSE. Датасет для обучения был сгенерирован синтетически: сферы создавались по спирали Фибоначчи, а кубы — по равномерной сетке. Модель способна на морфинг между объектами, например, при смешивании входных векторов можно получить промежуточные формы, такие как раздутый куб или фиолетовый цвет. Модель распространяется под лицензией Apache 2.0, что позволяет использовать, модифицировать и распространять код, в том числе в коммерческих целях.

instruction: What part of this report provides the results of the user satisfaction survey?
description: ColQwen2 is a model based on a novel architecture and training strategy derived from Vision Language Models (VLMs), designed to efficiently index documents using their visual features. It generates multi-vector representations of text and images, extending the capabilities of VLMs. This version is an untrained base model that ensures deterministic initialization of the projection layer. The model accepts dynamic image resolutions without resizing, preserving the aspect ratio, with a maximum resolution that allows up to 768 image patches. Larger numbers of image patches improve performance but increase memory requirements. It was trained using colpali-engine version 0.3.1 and uses the same data as the ColPali dataset described in the associated paper. The training dataset consists of 127,460 query-page pairs, including 63% from openly available academic datasets and 37% from a synthetic dataset of web-crawled PDF pages augmented with pseudo-questions generated by a VLM (Claude-3 Sonnet). The training set is entirely in English, allowing for the study of zero-shot generalization to non-English languages. No multi-page PDF document is used in both the training and evaluation sets to avoid contamination. A validation set of 2% of the samples is used for hyperparameter tuning. All models are trained for one epoch using bfloat16 format, low-rank adapters with alpha=32 and r=32 on the language model's transformer layers and the final projection layer, and the paged_adamw_8bit optimizer. Training is conducted on an 8 GPU setup with data parallelism, a learning rate of 5e-5 with linear decay and 2.5% warmup steps, and a batch size of 32. The model is intended for use with the colpali-engine version 0.3.1 or higher and requires transformers version greater than 4.45.0. It supports processing of images and queries, generating embeddings for multi-vector retrieval. Limitations include a primary focus on PDF-type documents and high-resource languages, which may limit generalization to other document types or less represented languages. The model relies on multi-vector retrieval based on the ColBERT late interaction mechanism, which may require additional engineering to adapt to standard vector retrieval frameworks. The vision language backbone model (Qwen2-VL) is licensed under Apache 2.0, while the attached adapters are under the MIT license.

instruction: What types of trees can be seen in this outdoor scene?
description: The OWLv2 model, short for Open-World Localization, was proposed by Matthias Minderer, Alexey Gritsenko, and Neil Houlsby. It is a zero-shot text-conditioned object detection model that can be used to query an image with one or multiple text queries. The model uses CLIP as its multi-modal backbone, with a ViT-like Transformer to extract visual features and a causal language model to extract text features. To use CLIP for detection, OWL-ViT removes the final token pooling layer of the vision model and attaches a lightweight classification and box head to each transformer output token. Open-vocabulary classification is enabled by replacing the fixed classification layer weights with the class-name embeddings obtained from the text model. The authors first train CLIP from scratch and fine-tune it end-to-end with the classification and box heads on standard detection datasets using a bipartite matching loss. One or multiple text queries per image can be used to perform zero-shot text-conditioned object detection. The model uses a CLIP backbone with a ViT-L/14 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned together with the box and class prediction heads with an object detection objective. The model is intended as a research output for research communities. It is hoped that this model will enable researchers to better understand and explore zero-shot, text-conditioned object detection. It is also hoped it can be used for interdisciplinary studies of the potential impact of such models, especially in areas that commonly require identifying objects whose label is unavailable during training. The primary intended users of these models are AI researchers. The model is primarily imagined to be used by researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. The CLIP backbone of the model was trained on publicly available image-caption data. This was done through a combination of crawling a handful of websites and using commonly-used pre-existing image datasets. A large portion of the data comes from crawling the internet, which means that the data is more representative of people and societies most connected to the internet. The prediction heads of OWL-ViT, along with the CLIP backbone, are fine-tuned on publicly available object detection datasets.

instruction: Please compare these two headlines and tell me which one is more attention-grabbing.
description: Skywork-Reward-V2 is a series of eight reward models designed for versatility across a wide range of tasks. These models are trained on a mixture of 26 million carefully curated preference pairs. The Skywork-Reward-V2 series is based on the Bradley-Terry model, with improvements in training data scale and quality to achieve superior performance. Compared with the first generation of Skywork-Reward, the Skywork-Reward-V2 series is trained on a significantly larger and higher-quality preference data mixture, consisting of 26 million preference pairs curated via a large-scale human-LLM synergistic pipeline. The series offers state-of-the-art performance on seven major reward model benchmarks, including RewardBench v1, RewardBench v2, PPE Preference, PPE Correctness, RMB, RM-Bench, and JudgeBench. The series includes models of multiple sizes, with the smallest 0.6B variant, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-0.6B, nearly matching the average performance of the previous best model, Skywork-Reward-Gemma-2-27B-v0.2. The largest 8B version, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B, surpasses all existing reward models across all benchmarks on average. The top experimental model, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M, outperforms all existing reward models on every benchmark. The models are available in eight variants, including Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.2-1B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.2-3B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-0.6B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-1.7B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-4B, and Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-8B. The Skywork-Reward-V2 series outperforms models in both Bradley-Terry and Generative categories with much smaller model sizes. For most use cases, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B is recommended, with smaller variants suitable for low-resource settings. All models are trained on preference data with a maximum length of 16,384 tokens, and inference should be performed within this limit. System prompts should not be included when using chat templates. The Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M model is considered experimental, trained on the complete set of 40 million preference pairs, with about one third of the chosen-rejected pairs flipped. It is recommended for research or non-production purposes only. The model repository, including the model weights and code, is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License for models derived from Qwen3, and under the Meta License for models derived from Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct. The Skywork-Reward-V2 series supports commercial use and permits modifications and the creation of derivative works, provided that all conditions of the respective licenses are met and proper attribution is given.

instruction: Can you add realistic shadows and reflections to this product design to enhance its visual appeal?
description: Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 is an enhanced version of Qwen-Image-Edit-2509, featuring multiple improvements, including notably better consistency. Key enhancements include mitigating image drift, improved character consistency, integrated LoRA capabilities, enhanced industrial design generation, and strengthened geometric reasoning ability. The model is designed to perform imaginative edits based on an input portrait while preserving the identity and visual characteristics of the subject. It further enhances consistency in multi-person group photos, enabling high-fidelity fusion of two separate person images into a coherent group shot. Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 integrates selected popular LoRAs directly into the base model, unlocking their effects without extra tuning. For example, it supports realistic lighting control and generating new viewpoints directly with the base model. The model is also optimized for industrial design applications, such as batch industrial product design and material replacement for industrial components. Additionally, it introduces stronger geometric reasoning capability, such as directly generating auxiliary construction lines for design or annotation purposes. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0.

instruction: Could you look through this medical journal excerpt and find all species references?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for species entity recognition, specifically for identifying species names from the Species-800 dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated SPECIES800 dataset, which contains 800 manually annotated abstracts for organism mentions. The model supports the classification of two entity types: `B-SPECIES` and `I-SPECIES`. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.82, precision of 0.81, recall of 0.82, and accuracy of 0.96. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the Bio_Discharge_Summary_BERT architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and healthcare analytics contexts. If used in research, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: Design an image of a steampunk-style airship flying over a city with gears and steam.
description: The FLUX.2 [klein] model family is the fastest image generation model to date, unifying generation and editing in a single compact architecture. It delivers state-of-the-art quality with end-to-end inference in as low as under a second. Designed for real-time image generation without sacrificing quality, it runs on consumer hardware with as little as 13GB VRAM. FLUX.2 [klein] 4B is a 4 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating images from text descriptions and supports multi-reference editing capabilities. It is fully open under Apache 2.0 and can run on consumer GPUs like the RTX 3090/4070. The model supports text-to-image, image editing, and multi-reference editing at quality that exceeds its size. It is suitable for local development, edge deployment, and production use. The repository includes an FP8 version of FLUX.2 [klein] 4B, with the main repository containing full BF16 weights. A reference implementation and sampling code are provided for developers and creatives. The model is available via the BFL API and in other formats. It can be used with the Diffusers Python library by installing or upgrading diffusers and using the Flux2KleinPipeline. The model is not intended to provide factual information and may produce inaccurate or distorted text. As a statistical model, it may represent or amplify biases from the training data and may fail to generate output that matches prompts. Prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting style. The model and its derivatives may not be used in any way that violates applicable law, for the purpose of exploiting or harming minors, to generate deceptive or harmful content, to generate personal identifiable information that can be used to harm individuals, to harass or abuse individuals or groups, to create non-consensual intimate imagery or illegal pornographic content, or for fully automated decision making or high-risk applications that adversely impact legal rights. Black Forest Labs is committed to responsible development and has implemented pre-training and post-training mitigations to prevent the generation of unlawful content, including child sexual abuse material and nonconsensual intimate imagery. Pre-training mitigation included filtering pre-training data for NSFW and known CSAM content, with partnerships to filter known CSAM. Post-training mitigation involved targeted fine-tuning to inhibit harmful behaviors and suppress certain concepts. Ongoing evaluations were conducted with internal and external third-party assessments to identify and address risks. The final checkpoints demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs and were released under Apache 2.0 for the 4B model and a non-commercial license for the 9B model. Inference filters for NSFW and protected content are included in the repository, and deployers are encouraged to implement these mitigations. Content provenance features include pixel-layer watermarking and C2PA metadata to indicate AI-generated content. Policies govern acceptable use, including the FLUX Non-Commercial License, Developer Terms of Service, and Usage Policy. Black Forest Labs provides a dedicated email address for safety feedback and maintains relationships with organizations to address emerging risks.

instruction: Is there a way to generate a report combining text and visual data?
description: Gemma 3n is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models developed by Google, built using the same research and technology as the Gemini models. These models are designed for efficient execution on low-resource devices and are capable of handling multimodal input, including text, image, video, and audio, to generate text outputs. They are available in pre-trained and instruction-tuned variants and were trained on data in over 140 spoken languages. Gemma 3n models use selective parameter activation technology to reduce resource requirements, allowing them to operate at an effective size of 2B and 4B parameters, which is lower than the total number of parameters they contain. The models are available in two sizes, with the raw parameter count of this model being 6B, but the architecture design allows it to be run with a memory footprint comparable to a traditional 2B model by offloading low-utilization matrices from the accelerator. They use a MatFormer architecture that allows nesting sub-models within the model. The models support a total input context of 32K tokens and can generate outputs up to 32K tokens, subtracting the request input tokens. The training dataset includes a wide variety of sources totaling approximately 11 trillion tokens, with a knowledge cutoff date of June 2024. The dataset includes web documents, code, mathematics, images, and audio. Rigorous data filtering methods were applied, including CSAM filtering, sensitive data filtering, and additional methods based on content quality and safety. The models were trained using TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e hardware, leveraging JAX and ML Pathways software for efficient training. Evaluation results show that the models perform well across a range of benchmarks, including reasoning, factuality, multilingual tasks, STEM and code, and additional benchmarks. The models were evaluated for safety and ethics, including child safety, content safety, and representational harms. They showed safe levels of performance relative to previous Gemma models, with minimal policy violations and significant improvements in high severity violations. The models have certain limitations, including potential biases in training data, challenges with complex tasks, difficulties with language ambiguity and nuance, and potential factual inaccuracies. Ethical considerations include the risk of perpetuating biases, generating harmful content, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. The models are intended for a wide range of applications, including content creation, research, and education. They provide high-performance open generative model implementations designed for responsible AI development.

instruction: Could you detect the specific organisms discussed in this patient case report?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for species entity recognition, specifically for identifying species names from the Species-800 dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated SPECIES800 dataset, which contains 800 manually annotated abstracts for organism mentions. The model supports the classification of two entity types: `B-SPECIES` and `I-SPECIES`. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is based on the distilroberta-base architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It has an F1 score of 0.72, precision of 0.72, recall of 0.73, and accuracy of 0.95. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical and research settings. It is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. The model can be used with different aggregation strategies for entity prediction, including `none`, `simple`, `first`, `average`, and `max`. For large-scale processing, it supports batch and dataset-level operations with adjustable batch sizes depending on hardware capabilities.

instruction: Which team scored the most points in the final match?
description: This model is a TAPAS large model fine-tuned on WikiTable Questions (WTQ). It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_large_reset` checkpoint, and the other version is `no_reset`, corresponding to `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_large`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional step called intermediate pre-training, followed by fine-tuning on SQA, WikiSQL, and finally WTQ. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table. The non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised fashion. It was pre-trained with two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table using a balanced dataset of syntactically created training examples. This allows the model to learn a bidirectional representation of tables and associated texts. The model can be used for tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining whether a sentence is entailed or refuted by the contents of a table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head and aggregation head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training these with the base model. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 50,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 512. The optimizer used is Adam with a learning rate of 1.93581e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.128960. An inductive bias is added to ensure the model only selects cells of the same column. The preprocessing involves lowercasing and tokenizing the texts using WordPiece with a vocabulary size of 30,000. The input format is [CLS] Question [SEP] Flattened table [SEP]. The model's performance on the development set for the LARGE version with reset is 0.5097, and with noreset is 0.5062. The model is intended for answering questions related to tables, and users are directed to the HuggingFace documentation for code examples. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model; this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering
