description: Sarashina-Embedding-v2-1B is a Japanese text embedding model based on a Japanese large language model. It was trained using multi-stage contrastive learning and achieved a state-of-the-art average score across 28 datasets in the Japanese Massive Text Embedding Benchmark as of July 28, 2025. The model maps sentences and paragraphs into a 1792-dimensional dense vector space and supports applications such as semantic textual similarity, semantic search, paraphrase mining, text classification, and clustering. It is a Sentence Transformer model with a maximum sequence length of 8,192 tokens and uses cosine similarity as the similarity function. The model architecture consists of a Transformer with LlamaModel and a Pooling layer that uses the last token for pooling. The model is designed to work with Japanese text. For usage, it can be loaded using the sentence-transformers library version 4.0.2. To enhance performance, the model was created through a three-stage learning process: first, weakly-supervised learning using contrastive learning with web-crawled and open datasets; second, supervised fine-tuning with higher-quality data and multiple model variations; and third, model merging by linearly combining the weights of the top-performing models from the second stage. Evaluation results as of July 28, 2025, show an average score of 76.38 across all tasks, with 76.48 in Retrieval, 84.22 in STS, 77.14 in Classification, 86.28 in Reranking, and 52.56 in Clustering. The model is licensed under an unspecified license, and commercial use requires contacting the developers.

instruction: Is there a way to improve the mask by providing additional points after the initial selection?
description: SAM 2 is a foundation model developed by FAIR for solving promptable visual segmentation in images and videos. It is designed to perform segmentation based on various types of input prompts, including point clicks, bounding boxes, and masks from previous predictions. The model supports both image and video segmentation tasks, enabling users to segment objects in static images or track them across video frames. SAM 2 can be used for automatic mask generation to segment all objects in an image, as well as for refining segmentation through multiple points or additional clicks. It allows for batch processing of multiple images or objects within a single image, and supports multi-object segmentation in both images and videos. For video segmentation, SAM 2 can track objects across frames and refine tracking with additional input on later frames. The model is built to handle complex scenarios, including streaming video inference and batched processing of multiple objects. It is implemented using PyTorch and integrates with the Hugging Face Transformers library for ease of use. The model's performance includes the ability to generate multiple mask predictions per input and track objects through hundreds of video frames. Users are advised to refer to the official documentation for detailed usage instructions and examples.

instruction: I need a realistic image derived from a normal map generated by a 3D rendering engine—can you assist?
description: ControlNet v1.1 is a neural network structure designed to control diffusion models by adding extra conditions. It is the successor to an earlier version and was developed by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala. This checkpoint is a conversion into `diffusers` format and is intended to be used in combination with Stable Diffusion. The model is a diffusion-based text-to-image generation model and is primarily associated with the English language. The license is adapted from the work of others in the area of responsible AI licensing. The model is conditioned on normalbae images, which are images containing surface normal information, typically represented as color-coded images. The preprocessor in Normal 1.1 is trained to estimate normal maps using a more correct protocol (NYU-V2's visualization method), making it more reasonable and physically correct compared to the previous version. This improvement allows the model to interpret real normal maps from rendering engines, provided the colors are correct (blue is front, red is left, green is top). In testing, the model has shown robust performance and can achieve results similar to the depth model. The previous Normal 1.0 model was not frequently used due to its limitations, but Normal 1.1 has significant improvements and potential for more frequent use.

instruction: Can you turn these tokens into a voice that sounds natural for a Japanese anime character?
description: Anime‑XCodec2 is a fine‑tuned variant of HKUSTAudio/xcodec2, trained on approximately 25,000 hours of Japanese anime/game-style voices. Only the decoder was updated; the encoder and codebook remain frozen, so speech tokens are identical to the original XCodec2. This makes the model a drop-in decoder for downstream systems that already work with XCodec2 tokens, such as Llasa. The model is a neural speech codec or speech tokenizer model based on XCodec2 with a decoder fine-tuned for Japanese speech, particularly anime/game-style voices. The training scope involved decoder-only fine-tuning on approximately 25,000 hours of Japanese data, while the encoder and codebook are frozen. Because the encoder and codebook are unchanged, speech tokens produced at encode time are identical with the original XCodec2. Any downstream model expecting XCodec2 codes can use Anime‑XCodec2 as a drop-in decoder. The model operates at a sampling rate of 16 kHz. The intended use of the model is to decode XCodec2 speech tokens, such as from Llasa or other autoregressive token generators trained on XCodec2 codes, into Japanese speech with improved naturalness for anime/game-style voices. It is also intended for the reconstruction of Japanese speech from XCodec2 tokens when analyzing or building Japanese-focused speech pipelines. The model is optimized for Japanese, and performance on other languages may degrade compared to the baseline XCodec2. It operates at a sampling rate of 16 kHz, and inputs must be resampled to 16 kHz before encoding; decoding assumes 16 kHz. The model is tuned toward anime/game-style voices, and out-of-domain speech may not benefit. The training data consists of approximately 25,000 hours of Japanese speech, with a focus on anime/game-style voices, including acting and character voices. Data preparation included resampling to 16 kHz and standard loudness/peak checks where appropriate. The training procedure involved fine-tuning the components `generator.backbone`, `generator.head`, and `fc_post_a`, while all other non-listed components were frozen. The goal of the training was to preserve token compatibility with HKUSTAudio/xcodec2 while improving reconstruction quality for Japanese anime/game-style speech. The model is licensed under CC-BY-NC 4.0, the same as the original XCodec2 license. The model acknowledges the original HKUSTAudio/xcodec2 and thanks contributors and the community around Japanese speech resources.

instruction: Is there a way to find common elements between two photos of a forest?
description: LightGlue is a neural network designed to match two sets of local features by jointly finding correspondences and rejecting non-matchable points. It is a variant of the LightGlue model trained on DISK and is available under a commercially permissive Apache 2.0 license. The model requires the `kornia` library to be installed and is usable with the Transformers library. LightGlue was proposed by Philipp Lindenberger, Paul-Edouard Sarlin, and Marc Pollefeys. It is useful for tasks such as image matching, homography estimation, and pose estimation between images. The model is adaptive to the difficulty of the problem, with faster inference on image pairs that are easier to match. It is more efficient in terms of memory and computation, more accurate, and easier to train than the previous state-of-the-art model, SuperGlue. LightGlue can introspect the confidence of its predictions and adapts its depth and width during inference. It can stop at an early layer if all predictions are ready and discards non-matchable points early in the process. The model is developed by ETH Zurich - Computer Vision and Geometry Lab and is designed for feature matching and pose estimation tasks in computer vision. It can be applied to a variety of multiple-view geometry problems and can handle challenging real-world indoor and outdoor environments. However, it may not perform well on tasks that require different types of visual understanding, such as object detection or image classification. LightGlue is trained on large annotated datasets for pose estimation, including image pairs with ground truth correspondences and unmatched keypoints derived from ground truth poses and depth maps. It is first pre-trained with synthetic homographies sampled from 1 million images and then fine-tuned with the MegaDepth dataset, which includes 1 million crowd-sourced images of 196 tourism landmarks. The model is trained using a supervised setup and runs in real-time on a modern GPU, with a forward pass taking approximately 44 milliseconds (22 FPS) for an image pair. It has 13.7 million parameters and is suitable for integration into modern Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) or Structure-from-Motion (SfM) systems.

instruction: Can you detect any objects or structures in this image that might be relevant for a land-use analysis?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. These models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained following the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. These models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones providing multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers applied on the class token, a linear layer applied on the class token and the average of the patch tokens, image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation using linear layers, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2. However, a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) is a curated dataset of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) is a dataset of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on various tasks. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on web (LVD-1689M), the results include 87.0 on IN-ReaL, 60.4 on IN-R, 50.9 on Obj.Net, 49.5 on Ox.-H, 47.0 on ADE20k, 0.403 on NYU↓, 72.7 on DAVIS, 56.3 on NAVI, and 50.4 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-S/16; 88.0 on IN-ReaL, 68.8 on IN-R, 54.6 on Obj.Net, 50.0 on Ox.-H, 48.8 on ADE20k, 0.399 on NYU↓, 75.5 on DAVIS, 57.1 on NAVI, and 55.2 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-S+/16; 89.3 on IN-ReaL, 76.7 on IN-R, 64.1 on Obj.Net, 58.5 on Ox.-H, 51.8 on ADE20k, 0.373 on NYU↓, 77.2 on DAVIS, 58.8 on NAVI, and 57.2 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-B/16; 90.2 on IN-ReaL, 88.1 on IN-R, 74.8 on Obj.Net, 63.1 on Ox.-H, 54.9 on ADE20k, 0.352 on NYU↓, 79.9 on DAVIS, 62.3 on NAVI, and 61.3 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-L/16; 90.3 on IN-ReaL, 90.0 on IN-R, 78.6 on Obj.Net, 64.5 on Ox.-H, 54.8 on ADE20k, 0.352 on NYU↓, 79.3 on DAVIS, 63.3 on NAVI, and 56.3 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-H+/16; and 90.4 on IN-ReaL, 91.1 on IN-R, 91.1 on Obj.Net, 72.8 on Ox.-H, 55.9 on ADE20k, 0.309 on NYU↓, 79.7 on DAVIS, 64.4 on NAVI, and 58.7 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-7B/16. For ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web (LVD-1689M), the results include 86.6 on IN-ReaL @256px, 87.7 on IN-ReaL @512px, 73.7 on Obj.Net @256px, 74.1 on Obj.Net @512px, 52.6 on ADE20k @256px, 58.7 on ADE20k @512px, 42.7 on NYU↓, and 0.448 on SPair for DINOv3 ConvNeXt Tiny; 87.9 on IN-ReaL @256px, 88.7 on IN-ReaL @512px, 73.7 on Obj.Net @256px, 74.1 on Obj.Net @512px, 52.6 on ADE20k @256px, 58.7 on ADE20k @512px, 44.8 on NYU↓, and 0.432 on SPair for DINOv3 ConvNeXt Small; 88.5 on IN-ReaL @256px, 89.2 on IN-ReaL @512px, 77.2 on Obj.Net @256px, 78.2 on Obj.Net @512px, 56.2 on ADE20k @256px, 61.3 on ADE20k @512px, 46.3 on NYU↓, and 0.420 on SPair for DINOv3 ConvNeXt Base; and 88.9 on IN-ReaL @256px, 89.4 on IN-ReaL @512px, 81.3 on Obj.Net @256px, 82.4 on Obj.Net @512px, 59.3 on ADE20k @256px, 65.2 on ADE20k @512px, 47.8 on NYU↓, and 0.403 on SPair for DINOv3 ConvNeXt Large. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on satellite (SAT-493M), the results include 73.0 on m-BEnet, 96.5 on m-brick-kiln, 94.1 on m-eurosat, 60.6 on m-forestnet, 96.0 on m-pv4ger, 57.4 on m-so2sat, and 79.6 mean for DINOv3 ViT-L/16; and 74.0 on m-BEnet, 97.2 on m-brick-kiln, 94.8 on m-eurosat, 62.3 on m-forestnet, 96.1 on m-pv4ger, 62.1 on m-so2sat, and 81.1 mean for DINOv3 ViT-7B/16. For segmentation tasks on satellite data, the results include 94.2 on m-cashew, 75.6 on m-chesapeake, 61.8 on m-NeonTree, 83.7 on m-nz-cattle, 95.2 on m-pv4ger-seg, 36.8 on m-SA-crop, and 74.5 mean for DINOv3 ViT-L/16; and 94.1 on m-cashew, 76.6 on m-chesapeake, 62.6 on m-NeonTree, 83.4 on m-nz-cattle, 95.5 on m-pv4ger-seg, 37.6 on m-SA-crop, and 75.0 mean for DINOv3 ViT-7B/16. The model training for the ViT-7B model used 61,440 hours of compute on Nvidia H100 GPUs in a private infrastructure in the USA, resulting in 18t CO2eq of carbon emissions. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 384, 4 register tokens, 6 heads, MLP FFN, RoPE; ViT-S+ (29M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 384, 4 register tokens, 6 heads, SwiGLU FFN, RoPE; ViT-B (86M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 768, 4 register tokens, 12 heads, MLP FFN, RoPE; ViT-L (300M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 1024, 4 register tokens, 16 heads, MLP FFN, RoPE; ViT-H+ (840M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 1280, 4 register tokens, 20 heads, SwiGLU FFN, RoPE; and ViT-7B (6716M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 4096, 4 register tokens, 32 heads, SwiGLU FFN, RoPE. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: I need a spoken version of this French text for a language learning app—can you help?
description: Kyutai TTS is a streaming text-to-speech model designed to generate audio as soon as the first few words of the input text are provided, rather than requiring the full text to produce audio. The model has 1.8B parameters, despite the name suggesting 1.6B. It is a hierarchical Transformer architecture that processes tokenized text and generates audio tokenized by Mimi. The frame rate is 12.5 Hz, with each audio frame represented by 32 tokens, though fewer tokens can be used during inference for faster generation. The backbone model consists of 1B parameters, and the depth transformer has 600M parameters with partial weight sharing. The audio is shifted by 16 steps (1.28 seconds) relative to the text, and the model uses an acoustic/semantic delay of 2. Kyutai TTS is a decoder-only model that uses a multistream architecture to model the text stream based on the speech stream, with the text stream shifted relative to the audio stream to allow prediction of text tokens from input audio. It supports English and French languages and is licensed under CC-BY 4.0. The model can perform streaming text-to-speech generation, including for dialogues, and supports voice conditioning through cross-attention pre-computed embeddings provided in the repository. It does not support Classifier Free Guidance (CFG) directly but was trained using CFG distillation to improve speed without requiring a doubled batch size. The model is easy to batch and can achieve a throughput of 75x generated audio per compute unit of time. It does not perform watermarking, as watermarking can be easily deactivated in open source models and existing TTS watermark systems are removed by encoding and decoding the audio with Mimi. Instead, voice cloning is restricted to the use of pre-computed voice embeddings. The model was trained for 750k steps with a batch size of 64 and a segment duration of 120 seconds, followed by 24k updates of CFG distillation. The pretraining data consisted of 2.5 million hours of publicly available audio, with synthetic transcripts generated using `whisper-medium`. Pretraining was conducted using 32 H100 Nvidia GPUs, and CFG distillation was performed on 8 H100 Nvidia GPUs. The model was developed by Kyutai and authored by Neil Zeghidour, Eugene Kharitonov, Manu Orsini, Václav Volhejn, Gabriel de Marmiesse, Edouard Grave, Patrick Perez, Laurent Mazaré, and Alexandre Défossez.

instruction: Can you add a small dog following the main character in the video?
description: Lucy Edit Dev is an open-weight video editing model that performs instruction-guided edits on videos using free-text prompts. It supports a variety of editing tasks, including clothing and accessory changes, character changes, object insertions, and scene replacements, while preserving the motion and composition of the original video. The model is built on the Wan2.2 5B architecture, which includes a high-compression VAE and DiT stack, allowing for easy adaptation of existing scripts and workflows. It is designed to preserve motion and composition perfectly, enabling precise edits. Compared to common inference-time methods, Lucy Edit Dev offers more reliable editing results. It supports changes to wardrobe and accessories, such as adding glasses, earrings, or hats. It can also perform character changes, such as replacing a person with a monster, animal, or known character. Additionally, it supports scene transformations, such as changing a real-world scene into a 2D cartoon. The model operates using pure text instructions, requiring no finetuning or masks for common edits. The model has approximately 5 billion parameters and is built on top of the Wan2.2 5B architecture. Lucy Edit Dev is the first open-source instruction-guided video editing model. It is designed to be used with descriptive text prompts, with best results achieved using prompts of around 20–30 words. Trigger words such as 'change,' 'add,' 'replace,' and 'transform to' help the model understand the user's intent. The model excels at clothing changes, human or character replacement, and object replacement, with strong results for structure-preserving swaps. Color changes have mixed reliability and work best with precise descriptions. Adding objects is most effective for wearable or handheld props. Global transformations can alter the environment or style but may also affect the subject's identity. Lucy Edit Dev is particularly strong in identity conservation, edit precision, realism, and adherence to prompts. Longer, detailed prompts improve results, and 81-frame generations provide better temporal consistency than shorter clips. The model is available for use with the diffusers library and is integrated with the LucyEditPipeline. It is also available via a hosted API. Lucy Edit Dev is built on the Wan2.2 5B architecture and acknowledges the contributions of the open-source community, including diffusers and Hugging Face. The model was first released on 2025-09-18, with a diffusers integration merged on 2025-09-16. Future plans include local inference ComfyUI nodes, LoRA and fine-tuning scripts, and additional technical documentation.

instruction: Extract all the multi-tissue structures referenced in this pathology report.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for high-precision biomedical entity recognition in the domain of cancer genetics. It is optimized for identifying and extracting cancer-related genetic entities from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports a wide range of biomedical entity types, including but not limited to amino acids, anatomical systems, cancers, cells, cellular components, developing anatomical structures, genes or gene products, immaterial anatomical entities, multi-tissue structures, organs, organisms, organism subdivisions, organism substances, pathological formations, simple chemicals, and tissues. Both B- and I- tags are supported for these entity types. The model is trained on the BIONLP2013_CG dataset, a specialized corpus for cancer genetics entities and gene regulation in oncology research. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.88, precision of 0.87, recall of 0.88, and accuracy of 0.94. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the bge-m3 architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and academic settings. If used in research, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: Can you locate the signature area in this scanned invoice?
description: This model is a Conditional-DETR with a ResNet-50 backbone, fine-tuned for the task of detecting handwritten signatures in document images. It was selected as the best-performing model among various object detection architectures based on its highest mAP@0.5 score of 93.65% in a comprehensive evaluation. The model was trained on a dataset consisting of 2,819 document images, with 1,980 images used for training (70%), 420 for validation (15%), and 419 for testing (15%). The dataset was processed to a resolution of 640x640 pixels in COCO JSON format. During the training process, multiple models were evaluated for precision, recall, and inference time, with Conditional-DETR ResNet-50 achieving the best mAP@0.5 score. Hyperparameter tuning was performed on the YOLOv8s model using Optuna, with parameters including dropout, learning rate, box and class loss coefficients, and optimizer selection. After tuning, the best model achieved a precision of 94.74%, recall of 89.72%, mAP@50 of 94.50%, and mAP@50-95 of 67.35%. Inference times were 171.56 ms on CPU (ONNX Runtime) and 7.657 ms on GPU (TensorRT). The model can be used for inference via the Hugging Face Transformers library, and it supports post-processing for object detection results. A live demo is available for real-time inference, and the model can be deployed using the NVIDIA Triton Inference Server. The model was developed using Python 3.10.12, PyTorch 2.5.1, and related libraries, and trained on a Google Cloud Platform n1-standard-8 instance with an NVIDIA Tesla T4 GPU. The model weights, code, and training materials are licensed under the Apache 2.0 license.

instruction: How can I get a detailed alpha channel for this high-resolution image?
description: Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation is a model designed for image matting tasks, specifically for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation. The model is intended to predict alpha matte outputs from input images, enabling the separation of foreground and background elements. It is based on the BiRefNet architecture, which incorporates bilateral reference mechanisms to enhance segmentation accuracy. The model is optimized for use in web environments and is provided in ONNX format to facilitate deployment with WebML technologies. The model has been developed by researchers from Nankai University, Northwestern Polytechnical University, National University of Defense Technology, Aalto University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and the University of Trento. The model is available for use with the Transformers.js library, where it can be loaded, processed, and used to generate output masks from input images. The model's output is a mask that can be saved as an image file. The model is intended to be used as a temporary solution until WebML becomes more widely adopted. Users are encouraged to convert their models to ONNX format and structure their repositories similarly for web deployment.

instruction: Is it possible to identify body joints in this image for pose analysis?
description: YOLO26-N-pose is a variant of the Ultralytics YOLO26 model, specifically optimized for human keypoint detection. It is the latest evolution in the YOLO series, designed from the ground up for edge and low-power devices. The model has an input size of 640 pixels and achieves a mAP Pose (50-95, e2e) of 57.2 and a mAP Pose (50, e2e) of 83.3. It runs at 40.3 ms on CPU (ONNX) and 1.8 ms on T4 TensorRT10, with 2.9M parameters and 7.5B FLOPs. The architecture of YOLO26 is guided by three core principles: simplicity, deployment efficiency, and training innovation. It is a native end-to-end model that produces predictions directly without the need for non-maximum suppression (NMS), which eliminates a post-processing step and makes inference faster, lighter, and easier to deploy. The end-to-end design simplifies integration, reduces latency, and improves deployment robustness across diverse environments. YOLO26 introduces the MuSGD optimizer, a hybrid of SGD and Muon inspired by Moonshot AI's Kimi K2 breakthroughs in LLM training, which provides enhanced stability and faster convergence. The model includes features such as Residual Log-Likelihood Estimation (RLE) for more accurate keypoint localization, optimized decoding for increased inference speed, DFL removal for simplified inference and broader hardware compatibility, and up to 43% faster CPU inference for edge computing. The model can be installed via pip and used with the ultralytics library for inference on images.

instruction: Could you express the sentence 'If it rains, then the ground gets wet' in first-order logic?
description: This model is a fully fine-tuned version of the T5 architecture, developed by Vossel et al. at Osnabrück University and funded by the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (DFG, German Research Foundation) under grant number 456666331. It is designed to translate natural language (NL) statements into First-Order Logic (FOL) representations. The model is an encoder-decoder sequence-to-sequence model, supporting English and FOL. It was fine-tuned from the google/t5-base model and is released under the Apache 2.0 License. The model is intended for use in automated semantic parsing, explainable AI systems requiring symbolic reasoning, research in neurosymbolic AI, and integration into pipelines for natural language inference, question answering, or knowledge base population. It is not designed for general natural language generation and may struggle with ambiguous, highly figurative, or out-of-domain input. Outputs should be validated for correctness before use, especially in critical applications. The model should not be used as the sole basis for decisions in critical areas without expert review. It is specialized for English NL and FOL and may not generalize to other languages or logics. Human-in-the-loop workflows are recommended for sensitive tasks. The model is intended for research and prototyping, not for standalone critical systems. The model was fine-tuned on two datasets: WillowNLtoFOL, which contains over 16,000 NL-FOL pairs and is licensed under CC BY-NC-ND 4.0, and MALLS-v0, which contains 34,000 NL-FOL pairs generated by GPT-4 and syntactically checked, licensed under Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0, subject to OpenAI terms. The training procedure involved full fine-tuning from the google/t5-base model with prompt-based instruction tuning, single-GPU training in float32 precision, and preprocessing that replaced FOL quantifiers (e.g., ∀) with tokens like FORALL. The maximum input/output sequence length was 250 tokens. Training hyperparameters included a batch size of 8 per device, a learning rate of 0.001, 12 epochs, the AdamW optimizer with an Adam epsilon of 1e-8, a linear warmup scheduler with 500 steps, no gradient accumulation, a weight decay of 0.01, no LoRA (full fine-tuning), a task type of SEQ_2_SEQ_LM, early stopping with a patience of 4 epochs, an evaluation strategy per epoch, a save strategy per epoch, a save total limit of 5 checkpoints, and best model selection based on eval_loss.

instruction: Can you find the document in this collection that most directly addresses cybersecurity best practices?
description: jina-reranker-v3 is a 0.6B parameter multilingual document reranker with a novel last but not late interaction architecture. Unlike ColBERT's separate encoding with multi-vector matching, this model performs causal self-attention between query and documents within the same context window, extracting contextual embeddings from the last token of each document. Built on Qwen3-0.6B with 28 transformer layers and a lightweight MLP projector (1024→512→256), it processes up to 64 documents simultaneously within 131K token context. The model achieves state-of-the-art BEIR performance with 61.94 nDCG@10 while being 10× smaller than generative listwise rerankers. It is designed to rerank documents based on their relevance to a given query, supporting multilingual retrieval tasks. The model can be used for reranking in applications such as information retrieval, document search, and question answering. The model is available for local inference using the transformers library and can also be accessed via Jina AI's API for faster integration. The rerank function takes a query and a list of documents, returning a list of documents sorted by relevance score. The model outputs include the document text, relevance score, and optionally, document embeddings. The model is licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0 for non-commercial use. For commercial usage inquiries, contact the appropriate licensing authority. The model is listed on AWS and Azure platforms.

instruction: What are the odds that the next FIFA World Cup will be held in the Middle East by 2034?
description: OpenForecaster-8B is a specialized language model for open-ended forecasting and predicting future events. It is post-trained from Qwen3-8B using reinforcement learning. The model is trained to make calibrated predictions on open-ended questions about future events, including providing calibrated confidence estimates when asked, reasoning about uncertainty and future scenarios, and leveraging retrieved information in context to improve predictions. OpenForecaster-8B was trained on the OpenForesight dataset, which contains over 52,000 forecasting questions generated from global news events, using GRPO to optimize a joint reward function combining accuracy and brier score. The model's knowledge cutoff is at best until April 2025, with the base model's cutoff being around June 2024, meaning it has no knowledge of events occurring after that date. Therefore, if asked about events in 2026 or later without recent developments or relevant context, the model's answers may not be helpful or up to date. OpenForecaster-8B achieves competitive performance with larger models like DeepSeek-v3 and Qwen3-235B-A22B on forecasting benchmarks, with key improvements in accuracy, calibration of confidence estimates, and consistency in predictions. Performance on a benchmark in July-August 2025 on non-numeric questions (86 questions) showed that OpenForecaster-8B has much higher accuracy than 100B+ models, with a comparison limited to models released before April 2025 for a fair, equal knowledge cutoff. The model is released under the MIT License.

instruction: Identify the species names in this passage about wildlife conservation and health.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for species entity recognition, specifically for identifying and extracting species and organism names from biomedical texts. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the LINNAEUS dataset, a specialized biomedical named entity recognition (NER) dataset focused on species name identification and organism recognition in scientific literature. The model is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. It supports the classification of two entity types: B-SPECIES and I-SPECIES. The model is based on the ModernBERT-base architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.85, precision of 0.89, recall of 0.81, and accuracy of 0.99. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and healthcare analytics contexts. The model is part of the OpenMed NER project, which provides open-source, domain-adapted state-of-the-art transformers for biomedical NER across multiple datasets.

instruction: Can you turn this picture into a 5-second video with a gentle breeze moving the trees?
description: Wan2.1 is a comprehensive and open suite of video foundation models designed to push the boundaries of video generation. It consistently outperforms existing open-source models and state-of-the-art commercial solutions across multiple benchmarks. The T2V-1.3B model requires only 8.19 GB VRAM, making it compatible with most consumer-grade GPUs. It can generate a 5-second 480P video on an RTX 4090 in about 4 minutes, with performance comparable to some closed-source models. Wan2.1 supports multiple tasks, including Text-to-Video, Image-to-Video, Video Editing, Text-to-Image, and Video-to-Audio. It is the first video model capable of generating both Chinese and English text, enhancing its practical applications. The model features a powerful video VAE called Wan-VAE, which delivers exceptional efficiency and performance, encoding and decoding 1080P videos of any length while preserving temporal information. The repository includes the I2V-14B model, which is capable of generating 480P videos with fast generation and excellent quality. Wan2.1 is built on the diffusion transformer paradigm, incorporating a novel 3D causal VAE architecture, scalable training strategies, large-scale data construction, and automated evaluation metrics. The model uses a Flow Matching framework within the Diffusion Transformer paradigm, with a T5 Encoder to encode multilingual text input and cross-attention in each transformer block to embed the text. An MLP with a Linear layer and a SiLU layer processes the input time embeddings and predicts six modulation parameters individually. The model is trained on a curated and deduplicated dataset of image and video data, processed through a four-step data cleaning process focusing on fundamental dimensions, visual quality, and motion quality. Manual evaluations indicate that Wan2.1 outperforms both closed-source and open-source models. The model is available under the Apache 2.0 License, with usage restrictions to ensure compliance with applicable laws and ethical standards.

instruction: What are the key differences between IQ1_S and IQ1_M in terms of performance?
description: This model provides various quantized versions of a neural network, optimized for different use cases and performance requirements. The available quantizations include IQ1_S, IQ1_M, IQ2_XXS, IQ2_XS, IQ2_S, IQ2_M, Q2_K_S, Q2_K, IQ3_XXS, IQ3_XS, Q3_K_S, IQ3_S, IQ3_M, Q3_K_M, Q3_K_L, IQ4_XS, Q4_0, IQ4_NL, Q4_K_S, Q4_K_M, Q4_1, Q5_K_S, Q5_K_M, and Q6_K. These quants vary in file size from 1.3 GB to 3.7 GB and are associated with different quality and performance characteristics. For example, the Q4_K_S quant is described as optimal in terms of size, speed, and quality, while the Q4_K_M is noted as fast and recommended. Some quants, such as IQ1_S and IQ1_M, are described as suitable only for desperate use cases, and others like Q2_K_S and IQ3_XXS are noted for their low quality. The model is intended for users who require lightweight and efficient inference, and it supports concatenation of multi-part files for use. The model quants are made available through a combination of company resources and private computing infrastructure provided by a collaborator.

instruction: Provide a brief summary of the visual elements in this photo of a farmer in a field.
description: BLIP is a Vision-Language Pre-training (VLP) framework designed for both vision-language understanding and generation tasks. It is pretrained on the COCO dataset using a base architecture with a Vision Transformer (ViT) base backbone. The model supports conditional and unconditional image captioning, allowing users to generate captions based on an image or a given text prompt. BLIP is designed to transfer flexibly to a wide range of vision-language tasks, including image-text retrieval, image captioning, and visual question answering (VQA). It achieves state-of-the-art results, with improvements of +2.7% in average recall@1 for image-text retrieval, +2.8% in CIDEr for image captioning, and +1.6% in VQA score. Additionally, BLIP demonstrates strong generalization ability when applied to video-language tasks in a zero-shot manner. The model utilizes noisy web data by bootstrapping captions, where a captioner generates synthetic captions and a filter removes the noisy ones. This release is intended for research purposes in support of an academic paper. The model, datasets, and code are not specifically designed or evaluated for all downstream purposes. Users are advised to evaluate and address potential concerns related to accuracy, safety, and fairness before deployment. The model is released under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International license.

instruction: Produce a 720P video of a rainstorm over a city skyline with lightning flashes.
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video generation models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video (TI2V) capabilities. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity without increasing computational cost. The A14B model series uses a two-expert design: a high-noise expert for early-stage layout and a low-noise expert for later-stage detail refinement, with each expert having approximately 14B parameters and a total of 27B parameters. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a defined threshold step for switching. The model is trained on significantly larger data, with +65.6% more images and +83.2% more videos compared to Wan2.1, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 also introduces a 5B model with a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE, achieving a compression ratio of 16×16×4, and supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps. This model can run on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090 and is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available. The T2V-A14B model supports generating 5-second videos at both 480P and 720P resolutions and delivers outstanding video generation quality. On the Wan-Bench 2.0 benchmark, the model surpasses leading commercial models across most key evaluation dimensions. The model is available for download via Hugging Face and ModelScope, and supports both single-GPU and multi-GPU inference using FSDP and DeepSpeed Ulysses. Prompt extension is available via Dashscope API or local Qwen models to enhance video quality. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license terms and applicable laws.

instruction: What’s the fastest way to create a voice recording in English from a short text on my computer?
description: Supertonic is a lightning-fast, on-device text-to-speech system designed for extreme performance with minimal computational overhead. It runs entirely on the user's device using ONNX Runtime, eliminating the need for cloud services, API calls, or external dependencies, and ensuring privacy. Supertonic 2 extends the original system's capabilities by adding multilingual support while maintaining the same inference speed and efficiency. It supports English, Korean, Spanish, Portuguese, and French. Supertonic 2 delivers ultra-fast inference speeds, up to 167 times faster than real-time, with an efficient architecture of only 66 million parameters optimized for on-device deployment. All supported languages share the same model architecture and inference pipeline, ensuring cross-language consistency. Performance is evaluated using two metrics: Characters per Second, which measures throughput by dividing the number of input characters by the time required to generate audio, and Real-time Factor (RTF), which measures the time taken to synthesize audio relative to its duration. Supertonic achieves high throughput and low RTF across input texts of varying lengths—short (59 characters), mid (152 characters), and long (266 characters)—on different hardware configurations, including M4 Pro CPU, M4 Pro WebGPU, and RTX4090. For example, on M4 Pro WebGPU, it processes up to 2509 characters per second for long texts and achieves an RTF of 0.006. On RTX4090, it processes up to 12164 characters per second and achieves an RTF of 0.001. Additional performance data is available for 5-step inference, where throughput and RTF remain consistently high across all tested configurations. The project's sample code is released under the MIT License, the accompanying model under the OpenRAIL-M License, and the model was trained using PyTorch, which is licensed under the BSD 3-Clause License but is not redistributed with the project.

instruction: Is there a recommended quantization for systems with both low RAM and an Nvidia GPU?
description: This model is a quantized version of the Huihui-gemma-3n-E4B-it-abliterated model, processed using the imatrix quantization method with a dataset provided by an unspecified source. The quantizations were performed using the llama.cpp release b5856. The model is intended to be run using llama.cpp or any other llama.cpp-based project. The prompt format uses a default template since no chat template was specified, which may not be accurate and should be verified against the original model card. The model is available in various quantization types, each with different file sizes and quality levels. Some quantizations, such as Q3_K_XL and Q4_K_L, use Q8_0 for the embedding and output weights instead of the default quantization. The model supports online repacking for ARM and AVX CPU inference, which can improve performance on specific hardware. The Q4_0 quantization format is compatible with online repacking, and newer IQ4_NL and IQ4_XS formats offer similar benefits for ARM CPUs. The model can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool, with instructions provided for both single-file and multi-file downloads. Users are advised to consider their hardware limitations, such as RAM and VRAM availability, when selecting a quantization type. For maximum quality, users should aim for a quant with a file size 1-2GB smaller than the total available memory. K-quant formats, such as Q5_K_M, are recommended for general use, while I-quant formats, such as IQ3_M, may offer better performance for specific hardware like Nvidia or AMD GPUs. The model includes a variety of quantization options, ranging from high-quality Q6_K_L and Q5_K_L to lower-quality but more memory-efficient options like Q3_K_S and Q2_K. Performance benchmarks on an AVX2 system show that Q4_K_M and Q4_0_8_8 formats offer improvements in prompt processing and text generation compared to the standard Q4_0 format. The model was developed with assistance from kalomaze and Dampf for the imatrix calibration dataset, and inspired by ZeroWw's work on embedding and output weights. Support for the model's development was provided by LM Studio.

instruction: How do I replace the sky in this landscape with a stormy cloud formation?
description: This repository contains Nunchaku-quantized versions of a model capable of editing images based on text instructions. It is optimized for efficient inference while maintaining minimal loss in performance. The model is developed by the Nunchaku Team and is categorized as an image-to-image model. Two quantized versions are available: one is SVDQuant quantized INT4 FLUX.1-Kontext-dev model for users with non-Blackwell GPUs (pre-50-series), and the other is SVDQuant quantized NVFP4 FLUX.1-Kontext-dev model for users with Blackwell GPUs (50-series). The FLUX.1 [dev] Model is licensed by Black Forest Labs Inc. under the FLUX.1 [dev] Non-Commercial License. Copyright Black Forest Labs Inc. IN NO EVENT SHALL BLACK FOREST LABS INC. BE LIABLE FOR ANY CLAIM, DAMAGES OR OTHER LIABILITY, WHETHER IN AN ACTION OF CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE, ARISING FROM, OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH USE OF THIS MODEL.

instruction: Find and highlight the chemical compounds in this excerpt from a biomedical study.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition in biomedical literature. It identifies and extracts chemical compounds and substances from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the BC4CHEMD dataset, a manually annotated corpus for chemical entity recognition from the BioCreative IV challenge. The BC4CHEMD dataset contains PubMed abstracts with chemical entities annotated according to ChEBI guidelines and is used as a benchmark for evaluating named entity recognition models. The model can identify and classify the following entity types: B-CHEM and I-CHEM. It achieves an F1 score of 0.92, precision of 0.91, recall of 0.93, and accuracy of 0.98. The model is based on the e5-small-v2 architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. If used in research or applications, it should be cited as described in the provided citation.

instruction: I’m working on a voice-based project and need to classify the gender of each speaker in the dataset.
description: Common-Voice-Gender-Detection is a fine-tuned version of `facebook/wav2vec2-base-960h` for binary audio classification, specifically trained to detect speaker gender as female or male. This model uses the `Wav2Vec2ForSequenceClassification` architecture for efficient and accurate voice-based gender classification. The model achieves a precision of 0.9705 for female and 0.9943 for male, with a recall of 0.9916 for female and 0.9799 for male. The F1-scores are 0.9809 for female and 0.9870 for male, with an overall accuracy of 0.9846 across 6545 samples. The model is intended for use in speech analytics, conversational AI personalization, voice dataset curation, research applications, and multimedia content tagging. It is designed to assist in analyzing speaker demographics in call centers or customer service recordings, adjust tone or dialogue in voice assistants based on gender detection, automatically tag or filter voice datasets by speaker gender, enable linguistic and acoustic research involving gender-specific speech patterns, and automate metadata generation for gender identification in podcasts, interviews, or video content.

instruction: Can you convert this technical explanation into a spoken format for accessibility?
description: VibeVoice-Realtime is a lightweight real-time text-to-speech model that supports streaming text input and robust long-form speech generation. It is designed to enable real-time TTS services, narrate live data streams, and allow different LLMs to start producing speech from their first tokens before a full answer is generated. The model produces initial audible speech in approximately 300 milliseconds, depending on hardware. Although primarily built for English, it exhibits some level of multilingual capability and performs reasonably well in additional languages including German, French, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Dutch, Polish, Portuguese, and Spanish. The model uses an interleaved, windowed design that incrementally encodes incoming text chunks while continuing diffusion-based acoustic latent generation from prior context. It relies solely on an efficient acoustic tokenizer operating at an ultra-low frame rate of 7.5 Hz. The model has a parameter size of 0.5B and is deployment-friendly. It supports only a single speaker and is currently intended for English speech only; other languages may produce unpredictable results. The model integrates a transformer-based LLM with a specialized acoustic tokenizer and a diffusion-based decoding head. The acoustic tokenizer is based on a σ-VAE variant with a mirror-symmetric encoder-decoder structure featuring 7 stages of modified Transformer blocks and achieves 3200x downsampling from 24kHz input. The diffusion head is a lightweight module with 4 layers and approximately 40M parameters, conditioned on LLM hidden states, and uses a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM) process with Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) and DPM-Solver during inference. The model is trained with a curriculum increasing up to 8,192 tokens. The acoustic tokenizer is pre-trained, and during VibeVoice training, only the LLM and diffusion head parameters are trained using a curriculum learning strategy for input sequence length (4k to 8K). The model achieves satisfactory performance on short-sentence benchmarks and is focused on long-form speech generation. On the LibriSpeech test-clean set, it achieves a WER of 2.00% and a speaker similarity of 0.695. On the SEED test-en set, it achieves a WER of 2.05% and a speaker similarity of 0.633. The model is intended for research purposes exploring real-time highly realistic audio generation. It is not intended for use in any manner that violates applicable laws or regulations, including trade compliance laws, or for use in any other way that is prohibited by the MIT License. It is not intended for generating text transcripts or for use in scenarios such as voice impersonation, disinformation, real-time voice conversion, circumventing technical safeguards, unsupported languages, or generating background ambience, Foley, or music. The model may inherit any biases, errors, or omissions from its base model, Qwen2.5 0.5b. It may produce unexpected, biased, or inaccurate outputs. High-quality synthetic speech can be misused for impersonation, fraud, or disinformation. Users must ensure transcripts are reliable, check content accuracy, and avoid using generated content in misleading ways. The model does not support reading code, mathematical formulas, or uncommon symbols. It does not handle overlapping speech segments or non-speech audio. We do not recommend using VibeVoice in commercial or real-world applications without further testing and development. We recommend disclosing to end users that they are listening to AI-generated content. The model is intended for research and development purposes only. To mitigate misuse, the acoustic tokenizer has been removed to prevent users from creating embeddings, an audible disclaimer is automatically embedded into every synthesized audio file, and an imperceptible watermark is added to generated audio for verification. Users are responsible for sourcing datasets legally and being mindful of data privacy concerns.

instruction: Please evaluate the tissue for signs of lymphocytic infiltration or other immune responses.
description: Digepath is a self-supervised foundation model for intelligent gastrointestinal pathology image analysis. The model is a Vision Transformer Large/16 with DINO-V2 self-supervised pre-training on 353 million multi-scale images from 210,043 H&E-stained gastrointestinal related slides. Gastrointestinal (GI) diseases represent a clinically significant burden, necessitating precise diagnostic approaches to optimize patient outcomes. Conventional histopathological diagnosis suffers from limited reproducibility and diagnostic variability. To overcome these limitations, Digepath was developed as a specialized foundation model for GI pathology. The framework introduces a dual-phase iterative optimization strategy combining pretraining with fine-screening, specifically designed to address the detection of sparsely distributed lesion areas in whole-slide images. Digepath was initially pretrained on a large-scale dataset comprising over 353 million multi-scale images derived from 210,043 H&E-stained slides of GI diseases. It was subsequently fine-tuned on 471,443 carefully selected regions of interest (ROIs) in the second stage. It attains state-of-the-art performance on 32 out of 33 tasks related to GI pathology, including pathological diagnosis, protein expression status prediction, gene mutation prediction, and prognosis evaluation. Digepath demonstrates broad applicability across diverse clinical tasks, highlighting its potential for reliable deployment in real-world pathology workflows.

instruction: Can you suggest a few engaging topics for a blog post about personal finance for young adults?
description: The gpt-oss series is designed for powerful reasoning, agentic tasks, and versatile developer use cases. Two open models are available: gpt-oss-120b and gpt-oss-20b. The gpt-oss-120b is intended for production, general-purpose, and high reasoning use cases that fit into a single 80GB GPU (like NVIDIA H100 or AMD MI300X), with 117B parameters and 5.1B active parameters. The gpt-oss-20b is intended for lower latency, local, or specialized use cases, with 21B parameters and 3.6B active parameters. Both models were trained on OpenAI's data and should only be used with the harmony format, as they will not work correctly otherwise. The gpt-oss models are released under a permissive Apache 2.0 license, allowing for free use without copyleft restrictions or patent risk, making them ideal for experimentation, customization, and commercial deployment. Users can adjust the reasoning effort (low, medium, high) based on their specific use case and latency needs. The models provide full access to the reasoning process, facilitating easier debugging and increased trust in outputs, though this is not intended to be shown to end users. The models are fine-tunable, allowing for full customization to specific use cases through parameter fine-tuning. They support agentic capabilities, including function calling and structured outputs. The models were post-trained with MXFP4 quantization of the MoE weights, enabling gpt-oss-120b to run on a single 80GB GPU and gpt-oss-20b to run within 16GB of memory. All evaluations were performed using the same MXFP4 quantization. The gpt-oss-20b model can be fine-tuned on consumer hardware, while the gpt-oss-120b model can be fine-tuned on a single H100 node. The models support web browsing using built-in tools, function calling with defined schemas, and agentic operations like browser tasks. The reasoning level can be set in the system prompts to low, medium, or high, depending on the task requirements. The gpt-oss models can be used with various frameworks and tools, including Transformers, vLLM, PyTorch / Triton, Ollama, and LM Studio. Model weights can be downloaded directly from Hugging Face CLI.

instruction: Extract all named gene entities from this paragraph discussing molecular pathways.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for gene entity recognition, specifically for identifying and extracting gene-related entities from biomedical texts. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated GELLUS dataset, which is focused on gene recognition and genetics entities for genomics and molecular biology applications. The model supports the classification of biomedical entities such as 'B-Cell-line-name' and 'I-Cell-line-name'. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is based on the xlm-roberta-large architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model has achieved an F1 score of 1.00, with precision, recall, and accuracy also at 1.00. Comparative performance on the GELLUS dataset shows that it ranks among the top models, with an F1 score of 0.9976, precision of 0.9977, recall of 0.9975, and accuracy of 0.9989. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical and research applications. It is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. The model is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with the AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling, and it is validated using cross-validation on a held-out test set.

instruction: Illustrate a park with a notice that says 'Keep the Park Clean' in both English and Chinese.
description: LongCat-Image is an open-source and bilingual (Chinese-English) foundation model for image generation, designed to address core challenges in multilingual text rendering, photorealism, deployment efficiency, and developer accessibility prevalent in current leading models. With 6B parameters, LongCat-Image surpasses numerous open-source models that are several times larger across multiple benchmarks, demonstrating the immense potential of efficient model design. The model demonstrates superior accuracy and stability in rendering common Chinese characters compared to existing state-of-the-art open-source models and achieves industry-leading coverage of the Chinese dictionary. Through an innovative data strategy and training framework, LongCat-Image achieves remarkable photorealism in generated images. For both Text-to-Image and Image Editing tasks involving text generation, the target text must be enclosed within single or double quotation marks (both English '...' / "..." and Chinese ‘...’ / “...” styles are supported). The model utilizes a specialized character-level encoding strategy specifically for quoted content. Failure to use explicit quotation marks prevents this mechanism from triggering, which will severely compromise the text rendering capability.

instruction: Could you help me translate this Persian paragraph into English?
description: The Hunyuan Translation Model includes a translation model, Hunyuan-MT-7B, and an ensemble model, Hunyuan-MT-Chimera. The translation model is used to translate source text into the target language, while the ensemble model integrates multiple translation outputs to produce a higher-quality result. It primarily supports mutual translation among 33 languages, including five ethnic minority languages in China. In the WMT25 competition, the model achieved first place in 30 out of the 31 language categories it participated in. Hunyuan-MT-7B achieves industry-leading performance among models of comparable scale. Hunyuan-MT-Chimera-7B is the industry’s first open-source translation ensemble model, elevating translation quality to a new level. A comprehensive training framework for translation models has been proposed, spanning from pretrain → cross-lingual pretraining (CPT) → supervised fine-tuning (SFT) → translation enhancement → ensemble refinement, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) results for models of similar size. The model supports translation between Chinese and other languages, as well as among multiple non-Chinese languages. Specific supported languages include Chinese, English, French, Portuguese, Spanish, Japanese, Turkish, Russian, Arabic, Korean, Thai, Italian, German, Vietnamese, Malay, Indonesian, Filipino, Hindi, Traditional Chinese, Polish, Czech, Dutch, Khmer, Burmese, Persian, Gujarati, Urdu, Telugu, Marathi, Hebrew, Bengali, Tamil, Ukrainian, Tibetan, Kazakh, Mongolian, Uyghur, and Cantonese. The model can be used with the transformers library, and specific instructions are provided for loading and applying the model, including adjustments for fp8 quantized versions. The model is available for download on Hugging Face, and users are encouraged to use version v4.56.0 of the transformers library. For inference, a set of recommended parameters is provided, including top_k, top_p, repetition_penalty, and temperature. The model does not have a default system_prompt. The model was open-sourced in September 2025.

instruction: Based on the features provided, what is the probability of this loan being approved?
description: sap-rpt-1-oss is a deep learning model designed for tabular in-context learning (ICL) that combines semantic understanding and alignment within a table-native ICL framework. The model is intended to perform classification and regression tasks on tabular data. It accepts input in the form of a pandas DataFrame or a NumPy array, with no preprocessing required. Column names and cell values are automatically embedded using a background large language model, and missing values are handled appropriately. The model is trained on large-scale real-world tabular data and is competitive with state-of-the-art models across a broad set of benchmarks, particularly excelling on the semantically rich CARTE benchmark. It employs specialized embeddings for different data modalities to better leverage the semantics and world knowledge in real-world data. For optimal performance, it is recommended to use a GPU with at least 80 GB of memory, a context size of 8192, and a bagging factor of 8. For lower resource requirements, the context size can be reduced to 2048 and the bagging factor to 1. The model checkpoints are available for download from Hugging Face and require login access. The model is licensed under the Apache Software License, version 2.0, and is intended for research purposes only. The model checkpoints inherit restrictions from the training data, which is a subset of another dataset. The model was previously known as ConTextTab, but the code and repository have been updated to reflect the new name, while the model checkpoint and functionality remain unchanged.

instruction: How does the system differentiate between someone earning just under and just over 50K?
description: This model is a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) that uses Categorical Embeddings to classify income levels as either greater than 50K or less than or equal to 50K based on census data. It is a more complex and robust approach compared to models that use standard one-hot encoding. The architecture includes specialized Embedding layers for categorical features and has 13.1k parameters. It contains two hidden layers with dimensions 128 and 64, both using ReLU activation. A key feature of the model is its use of dense representations (embeddings) for categorical variables instead of one-hot encoding. The preprocessing pipeline includes Log-transformation for numerical stability and Standard Scaling. The model achieves an accuracy of 0.806 and an F1-Score of 0.768. While slightly more conservative in raw scores compared to base models, it demonstrates excellent generalization. The Embedding architecture is noted for its training stability, as the evaluation loss curves show that the model remains flatter and more controlled compared to base models, which tend to overfit more aggressively.

instruction: What is the result of simplifying the fraction 24/60?
description: This model is a continuously trained Qwen3-0.6B fine-tuned using the Gensyn RL-Swarm framework with GRPO (Generalized Reward Policy Optimization) to enhance reasoning and mathematical capabilities. The model is identified by the Agent ID 'tall_tame_panther' and is currently in active training, with updates occurring every 5-10 minutes. It supports GGUF (llama.cpp) for edge deployment and local inference and is compatible with Text Generation Inference for production use. The model is trained on a composite dataset of 1,000 samples, with a weighted sampling strategy across multiple domains including propositional logic, calendar arithmetic, decimal arithmetic, base conversion, fraction simplification, and basic arithmetic. Training is conducted in bfloat16 precision and involves distributed reinforcement learning across a swarm network. The model has 0.6 billion parameters, with an architecture including 28 layers, 16 attention heads, 8 key-value heads, a hidden size of 1024, and an intermediate size of 3072. It supports a context length of 40,960 tokens and a vocabulary of 151,936 tokens. The model excels in logical reasoning, mathematical operations, number system conversions, date/time calculations, step-by-step problem solving, and conversational tutoring. It is optimized for math and reasoning tasks and may underperform on creative writing. The model is suitable for edge deployment but is not state-of-the-art for complex reasoning. Training is experimental and decentralized, which may lead to less predictable behavior compared to supervised models. The model is compatible with chat formats and supports system instructions, multi-turn dialogue, tool calling, and reasoning mode. It is trained using a reward system that evaluates model outputs via a judge API and updates the policy using GRPO. The model is part of a decentralized swarm with fault tolerance, diverse exploration, and collective intelligence. Training and model updates are verified on-chain. The model is available in multiple quantized formats (F16, Q3_K_M, Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M) and can be deployed using various methods including standard Transformers, TGI, GGUF with llama.cpp, and Ollama. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is developed by 0xgrey. Reproducibility requires specific configurations, including the same seed, dataset, and swarm state. Formal evaluation benchmarks are planned for future inclusion as training progresses.

instruction: Extract the speech from the audio and present it as a readable text summary.
description: NVIDIA NeMo Canary-Qwen-2.5B is an English speech recognition model that achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple English speech benchmarks. With 2.5 billion parameters and running at 418 RTFx, Canary-Qwen-2.5B supports automatic speech-to-text recognition (ASR) in English with punctuation and capitalization (PnC). The model works in two modes: as a transcription tool (ASR mode) and as an LLM (LLM mode). In ASR mode, the model is only capable of transcribing the speech into text, but does not retain any LLM-specific skills such as reasoning. In LLM mode, the model retains all of the original LLM capabilities, which can be used to post-process the transcript, e.g. summarize it or answer questions about it. In LLM mode, the model does not 'understand' the raw audio anymore - only its transcript. This model is ready for commercial use. Canary-Qwen is a Speech-Augmented Language Model (SALM) with FastConformer Encoder and Transformer Decoder. It is built using two base models: `nvidia/canary-1b-flash` and `Qwen/Qwen3-1.7B`, a linear projection, and low-rank adaptation (LoRA) applied to the LLM. The audio encoder computes audio representation that is mapped to the LLM embedding space via a linear projection, and concatenated with the embeddings of text tokens. The model is prompted with 'Transcribe the following: ', using Qwen's chat template. The model is intended for users requiring speech-to-text transcription capabilities for English speech, and/or transcript post-processing capabilities enabled by prompting the underlying LLMs. Typical use-cases: transcription, summarization, answering user questions about the transcript. The model was trained using the NVIDIA NeMo toolkit for a total of 90k steps on 32 NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs. LLM parameters were kept frozen. Speech encoder, projection, and LoRA parameters were trainable. The encoder's output frame rate is 80ms, or 12.5 tokens per second. The model was trained on approximately 1.3B tokens in total (this number includes the speech encoder output frames, text response tokens, prompt tokens, and chat template tokens). The tokenizer was inherited from `Qwen/Qwen3-1.7B`. The model can be trained using this and . The model is available for use in the NVIDIA NeMo toolkit, and can be used as a pre-trained checkpoint for inference or for fine-tuning on another dataset. Input to Canary-Qwen-2.5B is a batch of prompts that include audio. Input Type(s): Audio, text prompt. Input Format(s): Audio: .wav or .flac files. Text prompt string for ASR mode: 'Transcribe the following: '. Input Parameters(s): Audio: Two-Dimensional (batch, audio-samples); Text: One-Dimensional (string). Other Properties Related to Input: 16000 Hz Mono-channel Audio, Pre-Processing Not Needed. Output Type(s): Text. Output Format: Text transcript as a sequence of token IDs or a string. Output Parameters: One-Dimensional text string. Other Properties Related to Output: May Need Inverse Text Normalization. Our AI models are designed and/or optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems. By leveraging NVIDIA’s hardware (e.g. GPU cores) and software frameworks (e.g., CUDA libraries), the model achieves faster training and inference times compared to CPU-only solutions. Runtime Engine(s): NeMo - 2.5.0 or higher. Supported Hardware Microarchitecture Compatibility: NVIDIA Ampere, NVIDIA Blackwell, NVIDIA Jetson, NVIDIA Hopper, NVIDIA Lovelace, NVIDIA Pascal, NVIDIA Turing, NVIDIA Volta. Preferred/Supported Operating System(s): Linux, Linux 4 Tegra, Windows. Model Version(s): Canary-Qwen-2.5B. Canary-Qwen-2.5B was trained on a total of 234K hrs of publicly available speech data. The datasets include conversations, videos from the web and audiobook recordings. The majority of the training data comes from the English portion of the Granary dataset: YouTube-Commons (109.5k hours), YODAS2 (77k hours), LibriLight (13.6k hours). In addition, the following datasets were used: Librispeech 960 hours, Fisher Corpus, Switchboard-1 Dataset, WSJ-0 and WSJ-1, National Speech Corpus (Part 1, Part 6), VCTK, VoxPopuli (EN), Europarl-ASR (EN), Multilingual Librispeech (MLS EN), Mozilla Common Voice (v11.0), Mozilla Common Voice (v7.0), Mozilla Common Voice (v4.0), AMI, FLEURS. AMI was oversampled during model training to constitute about 15% of the total data observed. This skewed the model towards predicting verbatim transcripts that include conversational speech disfluencies such as repetitions. The training transcripts contained punctuation and capitalization. The ASR performance is measured with word error rate (WER), and we process the groundtruth and predicted text with version 0.1.12. WER on: Version 2.5.0, Model Canary-Qwen-2.5B, RTFx 418, Mean 5.63, AMI 10.18, GigaSpeech 9.41, LS Clean 1.60, LS Other 3.10, Earnings22 10.42, SPGISpech 1.90, Tedlium 2.72, Voxpopuli 5.66. The ASR predictions were generated using greedy decoding. Number of characters per minute on 48 hrs eval set (`max_new_tokens=50` following `nvidia/canary-1b-flash` evaluation): Version 2.5.0, Model Canary-Qwen-2.5B, 138.1. WER on at different SNR (signal to noise ratio) levels of additive white noise: Version 2.5.0, Model Canary-Qwen-2.5B, SNR 10 2.41%, SNR 5 4.08%, SNR 0 9.83%, SNR -5 30.60%. As outlined in the paper 'Towards Measuring Fairness in AI: the Casual Conversations Dataset', we assessed the Canary-Qwen-2.5B model for fairness. The model was evaluated on the CasualConversations-v1 dataset with inference done on non-overlapping 40s chunks, and the results are reported as follows: Gender Bias: Gender Male, Female, N/A, Other. Num utterances 18471, 23378, 880, 18. % WER 16.71, 13.85, 17.71, 29.46. Age Bias: Age Group (18-30), (31-45), (46-85), (1-100). Num utterances 15058, 13984, 12810, 41852. % WER 15.73, 15.3, 14.14, 15.11. (Error rates for fairness evaluation are determined by normalizing both the reference and predicted text, similar to the methods used in the evaluations found at ). The model has the following limitations: Input length. The maximum audio duration in training was 40s, and the maximum token sequence length was 1024 tokens (including prompt, audio, and response). The model may technically be able to process longer sequences, but its accuracy may be degraded. Exclusively ASR oriented capabilities. The model is not expected to preserve any of the underlying LLM's capabilities into speech modality. English-only language support. The model was trained using English data only. It may be able to spuriously transcribe other languages as the underlying encoder was pretrained using German, French, and Spanish speech in addition to English, but it's unlikely to be reliable as a multilingual model. NVIDIA believes Trustworthy AI is a shared responsibility and we have established policies and practices to enable development for a wide array of AI applications. When downloaded or used in accordance with our terms of service, developers should work with their internal model team to ensure this model meets requirements for the relevant industry and use case and addresses unforeseen product misuse. For more detailed information on ethical considerations for this model, please see the Model Card++ Explainability, Bias, Safety & Security, and Privacy Subcards. Please report security vulnerabilities or NVIDIA AI Concerns.

instruction: Transform a modern city skyline into a steampunk-style version with gears and steam.
description: FLUX.2 [dev] is a 32 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating, editing, and combining images based on text instructions. It is designed to perform state-of-the-art open text-to-image generation, single-reference editing, and multi-reference editing. The model supports character, object, and style reference without the need for additional training. It was trained using guidance distillation, which makes it more efficient. The model is available for personal, scientific, and commercial use under the FLUX [dev] Non-Commercial License. Developers and creatives can use a reference implementation and sampling code provided in a dedicated repository. For local deployment on consumer-grade graphics cards, such as an RTX 4090 or RTX 5090, users can follow instructions on the GitHub page. The model includes filters for NSFW and IP-infringing content at input and output, and these filters or manual review must be used under the terms of the license. Black Forest Labs has implemented multiple pre-training and post-training mitigations to prevent the generation of unlawful content such as child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and nonconsensual intimate imagery (NCII). These include filtering pre-training data for NSFW and known CSAM, targeted fine-tuning to suppress harmful content, and ongoing internal and external evaluations. The final FLUX.2 [dev] checkpoint demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs in complex generation and editing tasks and showed higher resilience than leading open-weight models. The model includes content provenance features such as pixel-layer watermarking and links to the Coalition for Content Provenance and Authenticity (C2PA) standard for metadata. The API for FLUX.2 Pro applies cryptographically-signed C2PA metadata to output content. Use of the model is governed by the FLUX [dev] Non-Commercial License and other policies that prohibit the generation of unlawful content or the use of generated content for unlawful, defamatory, or abusive purposes. Black Forest Labs is monitoring for patterns of violative use after release and may issue takedown requests or ban users who repeatedly violate policies. A dedicated email address is provided for community feedback, and the company maintains reporting relationships with organizations such as the Internet Watch Foundation and the National Center for Missing and Exploited Children.

instruction: Summarize the key information from this 22-minute English technical presentation.
