description: Voxtral-Small-24B-2507-FP8-dynamic is a model based on the VoxtralForConditionalGeneration architecture, designed to process audio-text inputs and generate text outputs. It is optimized through FP8 quantization for both weights and activations, reducing GPU memory requirements by approximately 50% and increasing matrix-multiply compute throughput by approximately 2x. The model is intended for audio understanding tasks, including transcription, question answering, summarization, and function calling based on spoken input. It supports a pure speech transcription mode with automatic language detection and transcribes audio in multiple languages, including English, Spanish, French, Portuguese, Hindi, German, Dutch, and Italian. Voxtral can handle audios up to 30 minutes for transcription or 40 minutes for understanding, with a 32k token context length. It retains the text understanding capabilities of its language model backbone, Ministral-3B. The model was evaluated on the Fleurs transcription task, with performance measured by word error rate (WER) recovery. For English, the WER is 3.43% with 100.0% recovery; for French, 3.96% with 100.0% recovery; for Spanish, 2.84% with 100.1% recovery; for German, 3.36% with 100.1% recovery; for Italian, 2.54% with 99.7% recovery; for Portuguese, 3.57% with 100.0% recovery; and for Dutch, 5.29% with 100.1% recovery. The model was developed by Red Hat and released on 08/21/2025, with version 1.0. It was created by quantizing the MLP operators within transformer blocks of the language model using a symmetric static per-channel scheme for weights and a symmetric dynamic per-token scheme for activations. The model can be deployed using the vLLM framework and supports both transcription and audio instruct use cases.

instruction: I need a Russian audio file converted to text for a project—can you assist?
description: This is a fine-tuned XLSR-53 large model for speech recognition in Russian. It was fine-tuned using the train and validation splits of unspecified datasets. When using this model, the speech input must be sampled at 16kHz. The model was fine-tuned with the support of GPU credits provided by an unspecified source. The model can be used directly for speech recognition without a language model. It is based on the Wav2Vec2 architecture and is designed to transcribe Russian speech into text. Evaluation can be performed on the mozilla-foundation/common_voice_6_0 dataset with the test split, and on the speech-recognition-community-v2/dev_data dataset with the validation split. The model has been tested on a set of Russian speech samples, and some reference and predicted transcriptions are provided, showing varying degrees of accuracy. The model can be accessed and used via the Hugging Face Transformers library. It is important to note that the model may not always produce perfect transcriptions, as demonstrated by the discrepancies between reference and predicted sentences in the evaluation examples.

instruction: What is the speaker discussing in this audio clip from a science podcast?
description: Audio Flamingo 3 (AF3) is a fully open, state-of-the-art Large Audio-Language Model (LALM) that advances reasoning and understanding across speech, sounds, and music. AF3 builds on previous work with innovations in unified audio representation learning (speech, sound, music), flexible, on-demand chain-of-thought reasoning, long-context audio comprehension (up to 10 minutes), multi-turn, multi-audio conversational dialogue (AF3-Chat), and voice-to-voice interaction (AF3-Chat). Extensive evaluations confirm AF3’s effectiveness, setting new benchmarks on over 20 public audio understanding and reasoning tasks. This model is for non-commercial research purposes only. AF3 uses an AF-Whisper unified audio encoder, an MLP-based audio adaptor, a Decoder-only LLM backbone (Qwen2.5-7B), and a Streaming TTS module (AF3-Chat). Audio Flamingo 3 can take up to 10 minutes of audio inputs. The model is supported in 🤗 Transformers and processes audio in 30-second windows with a 10-minute total cap per sample. Longer inputs are truncated. AF3 supports single-turn audio and text instruction, multi-turn chat, batch multiple conversations, text-only and audio-only prompts, and transcription of local or remote files. It also supports think-mode reasoning with a PEFT adapter (AF-Think) and training or fine-tuning. AF3 can be optimized for inference using vLLM for up to 5-7x speedup, Flash Attention 2, Torch compile, or PyTorch SDPA. The model is designed to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems (A100/H100) and is compatible with PyTorch and HuggingFace Transformers. AF3 accepts audio in WAV, MP3, or FLAC formats and text in UTF-8, with a maximum audio length of 10 minutes and a maximum text length of 16000 tokens. It outputs text in UTF-8 string format, with a maximum text length of 1024 tokens, and can optionally produce speech in the form of a streaming TTS waveform. AF3 is trained entirely on open-source audio data, organized into four novel, large-scale collections. The data collection method for all training datasets is human, with labeling methods varying between automated and human. AF3 is evaluated on the test split of multiple datasets, with data collection methods also being human for most datasets, and labeling methods varying. The model is intended for researchers and developers to explore audio question answering and reasoning, long-context audio comprehension, interactive sound/music design assistants, and multi-turn (voice) chat. AF3 was released on 07/10/2025 via GitHub and HuggingFace. The model is released under a license, with portions of the dataset generation also subject to other licenses and OpenAI’s terms. NVIDIA encourages responsible use of the model and asks users to report any security vulnerabilities or AI concerns.

instruction: What is the expected solubility of the compound with the SMILES code 'C1=CC=CC=C1C(=O)OC(C)C'?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict aqueous solubility (log SW). This property affects environmental fate, bioavailability, and exposure potential. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. The model type is AttentiveFP (Graph Neural Network), and it performs a regression task. It accepts SMILES strings as input and produces a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and is based on an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model's hyperparameters include a hidden channel size of 128, three layers, three timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.1, a learning rate of 0.0005, a weight decay of 0.0001, a batch size of 32, and 50 training epochs with a patience of 10. The model is trained on the SW dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: How can I get a clean matte of the person in this picture for a video overlay?
description: BiRefNet is a bilateral reference network designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation, particularly for matting tasks. The model is adaptable to arbitrary input shapes, ranging from 256x256 to 2304x2304, and demonstrates robust performance on inputs of any shape. It was trained on the DIS-TR dataset and validated on DIS-TE and DIS-VD datasets. The model has achieved state-of-the-art performance on three tasks: DIS (Dichotomous Image Segmentation), HRSOD (High-Resolution Salient Object Detection), and COD (Complementary Object Detection). The architecture of BiRefNet is not explicitly detailed in the text, but it supports inference on arbitrary resolutions without the need for resizing. The model can be loaded using the HuggingFace Transformers library or via local code and weights. It is implemented in PyTorch and supports GPU inference. The model is associated with the research paper 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation' by Peng Zheng, Dehong Gao, Deng-Ping Fan, Li Liu, Jorma Laaksonen, Wanli Ouyang, and Nicu Sebe, published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research in 2024. The model is available through a GitHub repository and HuggingFace, and online inference demos are provided. The authors acknowledge support from Freepik and Fal for GPU resources and from Not-lain for deployment assistance on HuggingFace.

instruction: Can you determine how the camera moved between these two images?
description: DA3 Large is a model for multi-view depth estimation and camera pose estimation, designed as a foundation model with a unified depth-ray representation. It is part of the Any-view Model series, contains 0.35 billion parameters, and is licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0. The model supports relative depth estimation, pose estimation, and pose conditioning. It is developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is based on a Vision Transformer for Visual Geometry with a plain transformer architecture. The model is trained using public academic datasets only. A single plain transformer, such as a vanilla DINO encoder, is used as the backbone without architectural specialization. The depth-ray representation allows for a simplified approach without complex multi-task learning. The model significantly outperforms Depth Anything 2 for monocular depth estimation and VGGT for multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it is trained on academic datasets and may have limitations on domain-specific images. Performance can vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity.

instruction: Please find the percentage of tax applied in this invoice.
description: This model was trained from scratch on an unknown dataset. It achieves a loss of 3.7004 on the evaluation set. The model was trained using the following hyperparameters: a learning rate of 5e-05, a training batch size of 4, an evaluation batch size of 8, a seed of 42, the ADAMW_TORCH optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon of 1e-08, a cosine learning rate scheduler, 10 training epochs, and native AMP for mixed precision training. During training, the training loss decreased from 4.4903 at step 100 to 0.2683 at step 2200, while the validation loss fluctuated, reaching a minimum of 2.3696 at step 800 and a maximum of 3.7012 at step 2200. The model was developed using Transformers 4.56.1, PyTorch 2.6.0+cu124, Datasets 3.6.0, and Tokenizers 0.22.0.

instruction: Does the phrase 'She is a talented singer' entail that 'She can sing well'?
description: This multilingual model is designed to perform natural language inference (NLI) on 100 languages, making it suitable for multilingual zero-shot classification. It is based on the mDeBERTa-v3-base model, which was pre-trained by Microsoft on 100 languages. The model was fine-tuned on the multilingual-nli-26lang-2mil7 dataset and the XNLI validation dataset. The multilingual-nli-26lang-2mil7 dataset contains 2,730,000 NLI hypothesis-premise pairs in 26 languages spoken by more than 4 billion people, with 105,000 text pairs per language. The dataset was created using the latest open-source machine translation models and is based on English datasets. The languages included are: ar, bn, de, es, fa, fr, he, hi, id, it, ja, ko, mr, nl, pl, ps, pt, ru, sv, sw, ta, tr, uk, ur, vi, zh. An additional 105,000 text pairs for English were added, bringing the total to 27 languages. The dataset also includes a random set of 10% of the hypothesis-premise pairs where an English hypothesis is paired with a premise in another language, and vice versa. This mix of languages enables users to formulate a hypothesis in English for a target text in another language. The XNLI validation set consists of 2490 professionally translated texts from English to 14 other languages, totaling 37,350 texts. The XNLI training set was excluded due to quality issues with the 2018 machine translations. For evaluation, three languages—bg, el, th—were excluded from the XNLI training data and included only in the test data to assess the model's performance on languages not seen during NLI fine-tuning but pre-trained on. The total training dataset had 3,287,280 hypothesis-premise pairs. The model was trained using the Hugging Face trainer with the following hyperparameters: 3 training epochs, a learning rate of 2e-05, a per-device train batch size of 32, gradient accumulation steps of 2, a warmup ratio of 0.06, a weight decay of 0.01, and fp16 disabled. The model was evaluated on the XNLI test set in 15 languages (5010 texts per language, 75150 in total) and the English test sets of MNLI, ANLI, FEVER, LING, and WANLI. The model's performance on the XNLI test set in 15 languages achieved an average accuracy of 0.822, with individual language accuracies ranging from 0.744 to 0.871. On English datasets, the model achieved an average accuracy of 0.794. The model's speed varied by language, with the highest speed of 1887 texts per second on a ZH language and the lowest of 374 texts per second on the FEVER test set. The model is capable of cross-lingual transfer, meaning it can perform NLI on languages it has not seen during NLI fine-tuning, but performance is likely lower than for languages seen during fine-tuning. The performance on the languages bg, el, and th is an indicator of this cross-lingual transfer. The model has limitations due to the use of machine-translated data, which may reduce data quality for complex tasks like NLI. Users are advised to consult the original DeBERTa-V3 paper and literature on NLI datasets for potential biases. The model may have issues with older versions of the Hugging Face Transformers library, and using Transformers==4.13 or higher is recommended. The model currently does not support FP16.

instruction: Can you help me sign up for a free trial of a language-learning app with the best user ratings?
description: Fara-7B is Microsoft's first agentic small language model (SLM) designed specifically for computer use. With 7 billion parameters, it is an ultra-compact Computer Use Agent (CUA) that achieves state-of-the-art performance within its size class and is competitive with larger, more resource-intensive agentic systems. Fara-7B is a multimodal decoder-only language model that takes an image (screenshot) and text context as input and directly predicts thoughts and actions with grounded arguments. It is based on Qwen 2.5-VL (7B). The model accepts user goals (text), current screenshots, and a history of previous outputs (thoughts and actions) as input. It has a context length of 128k and produces generated text in response to the input, with a chain-of-thought block followed by a tool call block to indicate the action. Fara-7B was trained using 64 H100 GPUs over 2.5 days. It is a static model trained on public and private data, with training occurring between October 26th and October 29th, 2025. It was released on November 24th, 2025, under an MIT license. Fara-7B is specialized for taking actions on the web to accomplish high-level user tasks, such as booking restaurants, applying for jobs, planning trips, and buying shopping lists. It perceives browser inputs via screenshots, while internal reasoning and state history are recorded textually. Based on recent screenshots and a full history of actions, it predicts the next action with necessary arguments, such as coordinates for clicks. Fara-7B uses a robust post-training safety approach leveraging open-source and in-house synthetic datasets. It incorporates critical point recognition—situations requiring user permission or sensitive information—to safely halt actions. The model is trained to refuse harmful tasks and undergoes automated red teaming to assess risks, including grounding, jailbreaks, harmful content, and copyright violations. Fara-7B is trained to refuse tasks in categories that violate usage policy, including illegal activities, deceptive tasks, high-risk or regulated domains, harassment, exploitation, hate, unsafe technical use, misinformation, and sexual content. Critical points where the agent stops include entering personal information, completing purchases, making calls, sending emails, submitting applications, and signing into accounts. Fara-7B can be used to automate web tasks such as shopping, booking travel, restaurant reservations, information-seeking, or account workflows. It performs actions step-by-step using multimodal understanding from browser screenshots. On-device execution provides privacy guarantees and lower latency. It is not evaluated for all downstream purposes, and users should consider the limitations of LLMs for accuracy, safety, and fairness. It must adhere to applicable laws and regulations and is currently limited to English-only support. Fara-7B is available through Hugging Face and Azure AI Foundry. It requires specific input formats, including the use of the ChatML template with a system prompt for inference. The system prompt instructs the model to stop execution at critical points, such as checkout, booking, purchasing, calling, emailing, or ordering. It provides function signatures for all actions, including key presses, typing, mouse movements, left clicks, scrolling, visiting URLs, web searches, browser history navigation, memorizing facts, waiting, and terminating tasks. Fara-7B has technical requirements including Python packages such as torch, transformers, and vllm, and is tested on NVIDIA A6000, A100, and H100 GPUs. It is recommended to use vLLM server with bf16 precision and is provided via Magentic-UI in a Docker sandbox for safe web execution. Responsible AI considerations include its English-only support, potential for stereotype reinforcement or inappropriate content, the need to verify outputs in high-stakes or regulated domains, and the risk of misuse for fraud, spam, or malware generation. Users are advised to use safety services like Azure AI Content Safety, implement human-in-the-loop monitoring, sandboxing, access control, and output verification. Fara-7B is trained using a multi-agent data generation pipeline that produces synthetic trajectories from seed URLs and open-source tasks. It records screenshots, thoughts, action traces, and verification via verifier agents. The training data includes high-quality public datasets with image and text modalities, as well as specialized data for grounding, UI understanding (VQA, captioning, OCR), and safety/refusal datasets. Fara-7B has been evaluated on benchmarks such as WebVoyager, Online-Mind2Web, DeepShop, and WebTailBench, achieving a task completion success rate of 73.5 on WebVoyager, 34.1 on Online-Mind2Web, 26.2 on DeepShop, and 38.4 on WebTailBench. These scores are averaged over three runs. It has undergone safety evaluation and red-teaming on Azure, focusing on grounding, jailbreaks, harmful content, and copyright. Guidelines for safe use include human-in-the-loop monitoring, not sharing sensitive data, running in sandboxed environments, limiting internet access via allow-lists or block-lists, and avoiding use in commercial, high-stakes, or regulated domains. Security considerations include the model's ability to automate interactions across websites, apps, and operating systems, which requires strict access control, sandboxing, and monitoring. Fara-7B is based on Qwen 2.5 VL, which has an Apache 2.0 license. Fara-7B is released under an MIT license, and both licenses are compatible.

instruction: Find the most relevant academic sources that discuss the ethical implications of AI in healthcare.
description: The Diver Retriever 4B model is a reasoning-intensive model designed to tackle the challenge of reasonIR and rader. It is a text embedding model that supports bilingual (Chinese and English) processing with a context length of 40,000 tokens and contains 4 billion parameters. The model was trained using data from the fields of mathematics, coding, and healthcare, with careful attention to the difficulty level of the samples and the construction of unique negative samples for each field. As a result, the model performs very well on the Bright LeaderBoard and the Mteb-Medical Benchmark. Evaluation results show that the model achieves a BRIGHT score of 28.9, outperforming several other models in various categories including biology, earth science, economics, psychology, robotics, stack overflow, sustainability, leetcode, pony, AoPS, theoretical questions, and theoretical topics. The model can be used for inference tasks such as encoding queries and documents to compute cosine similarity, and it supports both Sentence Transformers and Transformers libraries for implementation. For finetuning, the model can be trained using the infonce loss with appropriate configuration and dependencies. The model is available for download from HuggingFace and ModelScope.

instruction: Please assess whether the voice in this sample is flowing or punctuated.
description: This model implements voice (sounding) quality classification based on the Vox-Profile framework, which is designed to characterize diverse speaker and speech traits. It is specifically trained to classify speech samples into a set of predefined voice quality attributes. The model evaluates voice characteristics across five categories: pitch, texture, volume, clarity, and rhythm. The classification labels include 'shrill', 'nasal', 'deep', 'silky', 'husky', 'raspy', 'guttural', 'vocal-fry', 'booming', 'authoritative', 'loud', 'hushed', 'soft', 'crisp', 'slurred', 'lisp', 'stammering', 'singsong', 'pitchy', 'flowing', 'monotone', 'staccato', 'punctuated', 'enunciated', and 'hesitant'. The model is based on the Whisper Large v3 architecture and is fine-tuned for voice quality classification. Performance is evaluated using speaker-level Macro-F1 scores, calculated by averaging the Macro-F1 scores across speakers. The speaker-level score is derived from five randomly sampled utterances per speaker, with the stratification process repeated 20 times. The final performance metric is the unweighted average of speaker-level Macro-F1 scores between the VoxCeleb and Expresso datasets. EARS is excluded from ParaSpeechCaps due to the limited number of samples in the holdout set. The model is intended for use with audio inputs that are no shorter than 3 seconds and no longer than 15 seconds, sampled at 16kHz and in mono channel format. Predictions are made using a sigmoid function on the model's output logits, with a threshold of 0.7 used to determine the presence of each voice quality label. The model is not intended for clinical or diagnostic applications, surveillance, privacy-invasive applications, or commercial use.

instruction: Highlight the cell lines mentioned in this report on cancer cell behavior.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for biomedical entity recognition, specifically for identifying proteins, DNA, RNA, cell lines, and cell types. It is optimized for high precision and is trained on the JNLPBA dataset, a curated corpus for biomedical named entity recognition. The model is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem, enabling easy integration into various applications. It supports the classification of biomedical entities such as B-DNA, B-RNA, B-cell_line, B-cell_type, B-protein, I-DNA, I-RNA, I-cell_line, I-cell_type, and I-protein. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.81, with a precision of 0.75, recall of 0.86, and accuracy of 0.93. It is suitable for applications including drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the deberta-v3-base architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical and research settings. If used in research, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: How can I generate a fantasy creature based on a human skeleton pose?
description: ControlNet v1.1 is a neural network structure designed to control diffusion models by adding extra conditions. It is the successor to an earlier version and was developed by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala. This checkpoint is a conversion into diffusers format and can be used in combination with Stable Diffusion. The model is a diffusion-based text-to-image generation model that supports additional input conditions. It is primarily used with openpose images, which represent human poses as a set of keypoints or skeletons. The model is trained to learn task-specific conditions in an end-to-end manner, and the learning process is robust even with small training datasets. The model supports a variety of conditions, including canny edge detection, pixel to pixel instruction, image inpainting, multi-level line segment detection, depth estimation, surface normal estimation, image segmentation, line art generation, anime line art generation, human pose estimation, scribble-based image generation, soft edge image generation, and image shuffling. Improvements in Openpose 1.1 include a more accurate implementation of OpenPose, especially for hands, and support for more inputs such as hand and face. The training dataset for the previous version had several issues, including duplicated grayscale human images, low-quality or blurry images with JPEG artifacts, and incorrect paired prompts due to data processing errors. These issues have been resolved in the new model, making it more reliable in many cases.

instruction: Can you generate a depth visualization for this image of a street corner with cars and buildings?
description: This is a model card for the `marigold-depth-v1-1` model for monocular depth estimation from a single image. The model is fine-tuned from the `stable-diffusion-2` as described in the papers titled 'Repurposing Diffusion-Based Image Generators for Monocular Depth Estimation' and 'Marigold: Affordable Adaptation of Diffusion-Based Image Generators for Image Analysis'. The model can be used to generate an estimated depth map of an input image. Even though any resolution can be processed, the model inherits the base diffusion model's effective resolution of roughly 768 pixels. This means that for optimal predictions, any larger input image should be resized to make the longer side 768 pixels before feeding it into the model. This model was designed for usage with the DDIM scheduler and between 1 and 50 denoising steps. The predicted values are between 0 and 1, interpolating between the near and far planes of the model's choice. An uncertainty map is produced only when multiple predictions are ensembled with ensemble size larger than 2.

instruction: Could you help me create a weekly restocking list based on predicted unit sales?
description: The Smart Inventory Advisor for Retail Sales (Invense) is a machine learning model trained to predict daily sales for retail products and provide actionable inventory recommendations. The model is a Random Forest Regressor implemented using scikit-learn, designed to forecast the number of units sold for a given product based on a variety of features. It generates a 'Store Owner's Action Plan' that identifies products at risk of stocking out and recommends a reorder quantity. The model is intended for use as a decision-support tool for small to medium-sized retail business owners, helping them determine which products to restock and how many units to order. It is not suitable for real-time stock market prediction or long-term financial forecasting, as its predictions are based on historical patterns and may not account for drastic changes in market conditions. The model's performance is dependent on the quality and patterns of the training data, and it was trained on a synthetic dataset containing over 73,000 daily records across multiple stores and products. The final model achieved a Testing R² score of 0.34, indicating that about 66% of the variance in daily sales is due to unpredictable factors not captured in the data. The model was trained using a preprocessing pipeline that included feature engineering, label encoding of categorical features, and standard scaling of numerical features. Hyperparameters were tuned using RandomizedSearchCV, with the best settings including 200 estimators, a minimum of 10 samples to split, a minimum of 4 samples per leaf, 'sqrt' for max features, and a max depth of 20. The model was evaluated on a 20% holdout set using R-squared, Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), and Mean Absolute Error (MAE) as metrics. The Testing R² was 0.3402, RMSE was 88.39, and MAE was 69.27. Users are advised to treat the model's recommendations as data-driven suggestions and to apply their own domain knowledge, particularly when considering external factors not included in the dataset. The model should not be used for automated financial decisions without human oversight. The model is provided under the MIT license and was developed by jerewy (NexusCrew).

instruction: Можно ли получить читаемый текст из этого голосового файла с сохранением запятых и точек?
description: Borealis - это первая аудио-модель с поддержкой ASR для русского языка. Модель обучалась на примерно 7000 часах аудио на русском языке. Важной особенностью модели является поддержка пунктуации в распознанных аудио. Архитектура модели во многом вдохновлена другими моделями, но имеет отличия в некоторых аспектах. Модель демонстрирует следующие метрики: средний WER 6.33%, RuLS 6.39%, CV 22.0 2.67%, Books 5.28%, Speak 1.95%, Sova 15.37%. Модель предназначена для распознавания речи и поддерживает генерацию текста с сохранением пунктуации.

instruction: I want this Indonesian product manual translated into English for international distribution.
description: Seed-X is a series of open-source multilingual translation language models, including an instruction model, a reinforcement learning model, and a reward model, designed to push the boundaries of translation capabilities within a 7 billion parameter scale. Seed-X exhibits state-of-the-art translation capabilities, on par with or outperforming ultra-large models like Gemini-2.5, Claude-3.5, and GPT-4, as validated by human evaluations and automatic metrics. With a compact 7B parameter count and mistral architecture, Seed-X offers outstanding translation performance in a lightweight and efficient package, ideal for deployment and inference. It excels on a highly challenging translation test set spanning diverse domains, including the internet, science and technology, office dialogues, e-commerce, biomedicine, finance, law, literature, and entertainment. The Seed-X-PPO-7B-GPTQ-Int8 model is a causal language model that supports multilingual translation among 28 languages, including Arabic, Czech, Danish, German, English, Spanish, Finnish, French, Hungarian, Indonesian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Norwegian Bokmal, Dutch, Polish, Portuguese, Romanian, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian, Vietnamese, and Chinese. It is trained through pretraining and post-training stages and is quantized using GPTQ 8-bit. The model is specialized in multilingual translation and is not expected to support other tasks. The language tags at the end of the prompt are necessary for PPO training. There is no chat template, so the model should not be prompted in a multi-round conversation format. The model is evaluated on a diverse set of translation benchmarks, including FLORES-200, WMT-25, and a publicly released benchmark, accompanied by human evaluations. The model is recommended to be used with vllm version 0.8.0 and transformers version 4.51.3. The model is licensed under OpenMDW. It is recommended to use the Seed-X-PPO model, as its translation performance is superior to Seed-X-Instruct. Unofficial quantized versions for local deployment are not recommended. An official quantized model and a demo on Hugging Face Space are planned for release.

instruction: Could you point out the signature in this document scan?
description: This model is a Conditional-DETR with a ResNet-50 backbone, fine-tuned for the task of detecting handwritten signatures in document images. It was selected as the best-performing model among various object detection architectures based on its highest mAP@0.5 score of 93.65% in a comprehensive evaluation. The model was trained on a dataset consisting of 2,819 document images, with 1,980 images used for training (70%), 420 for validation (15%), and 419 for testing (15%). The dataset was processed to a resolution of 640x640 pixels in COCO JSON format. During the training process, multiple models were evaluated for precision, recall, and inference time, with Conditional-DETR ResNet-50 achieving the best mAP@0.5 score. Hyperparameter tuning was performed on the YOLOv8s model using Optuna, with parameters including dropout, learning rate, box and class loss coefficients, and optimizer selection. After tuning, the best model achieved a precision of 94.74%, recall of 89.72%, mAP@50 of 94.50%, and mAP@50-95 of 67.35%. Inference times were 171.56 ms on CPU (ONNX Runtime) and 7.657 ms on GPU (TensorRT). The model can be used for inference via the Hugging Face Transformers library, and it supports post-processing for object detection results. A live demo is available for real-time inference, and the model can be deployed using the NVIDIA Triton Inference Server. The model was developed using Python 3.10.12, PyTorch 2.5.1, and related libraries, and trained on a Google Cloud Platform n1-standard-8 instance with an NVIDIA Tesla T4 GPU. The model weights, code, and training materials are licensed under the Apache 2.0 license.

instruction: What details can be extracted about the geometry of the environment from these images?
description: DA3 Giant is a model for multi-view depth estimation, camera pose estimation, and 3D Gaussian estimation. It is the flagship foundation model that uses a unified depth-ray representation. The model is part of the Any-view Model series and contains 1.15 billion parameters. It is licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0, which restricts its use to non-commercial purposes. The model supports relative depth estimation, pose estimation, pose conditioning, and 3D Gaussian estimation. It is developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is based on a vision transformer architecture for visual geometry. The model uses a plain transformer with a unified depth-ray representation and is trained exclusively on public academic datasets. A single plain transformer, such as a vanilla DINO encoder, is used as the backbone without architectural specialization. The depth-ray representation allows the model to avoid complex multi-task learning. The model significantly outperforms Depth Anything 2 for monocular depth estimation and VGGT for multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it is trained on academic datasets and may have limitations when applied to domain-specific images. Performance can vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity. The model is intended for non-commercial use only due to its CC BY-NC 4.0 license.

instruction: How can I compare the meaning of two sentences even if they are phrased differently?
description: EmbeddingGemma is a 300M parameter open embedding model developed by Google DeepMind, built from Gemma 3 with T5Gemma initialization and the same research and technology used to create Gemini models. It produces vector representations of text, making it suitable for search and retrieval tasks, including classification, clustering, and semantic similarity search. The model was trained on data in over 100 spoken languages, and its small size and on-device focus allow it to be deployed in environments with limited resources such as mobile phones, laptops, or desktops. EmbeddingGemma supports input text strings with a maximum context length of 2048 tokens and outputs numerical vector representations with a default embedding dimension size of 768. Smaller embedding sizes (512, 256, or 128) are available via Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL), which allows users to truncate the output embedding and re-normalize it for efficient and accurate representation. The model was trained on a dataset of approximately 320 billion tokens, including web documents, code and technical documents, and synthetic and task-specific data. Rigorous filtering methods were applied to exclude harmful and sensitive content, including CSAM and personal information. Training was conducted using the latest generation of TPUv5e hardware. The model was evaluated on a range of benchmarks, including MTEB (Multilingual, English, and Code versions), with performance metrics varying by embedding dimension and quantization configuration. EmbeddingGemma can be used for various tasks such as semantic similarity, classification, clustering, retrieval (document and query), question answering, fact verification, and code retrieval. Users are advised to be aware of potential limitations, including biases or gaps in training data, and the model's potential challenges in understanding language ambiguity and nuance. Ethical considerations include the risk of bias perpetuation, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. Developers are encouraged to use de-biasing techniques, adhere to privacy regulations, and monitor for misuse. EmbeddingGemma is intended for responsible AI development and has demonstrated superior performance compared to similarly sized open models.

instruction: How does this image of a budget spreadsheet relate to the question about annual spending trends?
description: The `llama-nemotron-rerank-vl-1b-v2` is a multimodal reranking model developed by NVIDIA for question-answering retrieval. It is designed to provide a logit score that represents the relevance of a document page to a given query. The model can process documents in the form of image, text, or a combination of both. The expected images are screenshots of document pages or slides, and the model supports images containing text, tables, charts, and infographics. The reranking model is a key component of a multimodal retrieval system, such as a vision RAG pipeline, where it helps improve overall accuracy by reranking top candidate documents retrieved by embedding models. The model is ready for commercial use and is suitable for users who want to build a multimodal question-and-answer application over a large corpus, leveraging the latest dense retrieval technologies. The model is based on the Eagle VLM architecture with a SigLIP 2 400M vision encoder and the Llama 3.2 1B language model. It is a cross-encoder model with approximately 1.7B parameters, fine-tuned for the ranking task. The final embedding is aggregated using a mean pooling strategy, and a binary classification head is used to produce the logit output. The model was fine-tuned exclusively on image data with a maximum of 4 input tiles and a maximum context length of 2048 tokens. For evaluation, it was tested on image-only, image+text, and text-only inputs with a maximum of 6 input tiles and a maximum context length of 10240 tokens. Inputs exceeding the maximum length are truncated. The model outputs a list of floats, where each value corresponds to a raw logit. Users can apply a Sigmoid activation function to convert the logits into probabilities. The model is optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems, leveraging NVIDIA’s hardware and software frameworks for faster training and inference. The model requires transformers version 4.47.1 or higher and flash-attention 2.6.3 or higher. It is compatible with NVIDIA Ampere, Hopper, Lovelace, and Blackwell microarchitectures and is supported on Linux. The model was fine-tuned using publicly available image datasets and synthetic queries generated from proprietary models. The training data includes a mix of automated, human, and synthetic data collection and labeling methods. The model was evaluated on the ViDoRe V1, V2, and V3 benchmarks, as well as two internal visual retrieval datasets: DigitalCorpora-10k and Earnings V2. On these benchmarks, the model achieved an average Recall@5 improvement of approximately 7.2% for text modality, 6.9% for image modality, and 6% for image+text modality compared to the VLM embedding baseline. The model also demonstrated competitive performance on text retrieval benchmarks, comparable to NVIDIA's text-only reranking model. The model is designed to be used in conjunction with the `llama-nemotron-embed-vl-1b-v2` embedding model. The model is intended for use in document reranking for retrieval, where user queries can be text and documents can be text, document page images, charts, tables, and infographics. The model does not generate or reverse engineer personal data, and no personal data was used to create the model. The dataset is reviewed upon addition and as needed. The model has been verified to meet prescribed NVIDIA quality standards and is intended for use by generative AI developers working with conversational AI models. The model has technical limitations, including a maximum sequence length of 10240 tokens. Longer text inputs should be truncated. The model does not always guarantee to provide a meaningful ranking of documents for a given query. The use of the model is governed by specific terms of use, and the post-processing scripts are licensed under a separate license. The model is built with Llama.

instruction: Create an image of a woman in a lab coat holding a beaker with a glowing liquid, set in a modern science lab.
description: Qwen-Image-2512 is the December update of Qwen-Image’s text-to-image foundational model. It is designed to generate high-quality images from text prompts, with a focus on enhancing realism, detail, and text integration. Compared to the base Qwen-Image model released in August, Qwen-Image-2512 features enhanced human realism, finer natural detail, and improved text rendering. The model significantly reduces the “AI-generated” look and substantially enhances overall image realism, especially for human subjects. It delivers more detailed rendering of landscapes, animal fur, and other natural elements. The model also improves the accuracy and quality of textual elements, achieving better layout and more faithful multimodal (text + image) composition. Over 10,000 rounds of blind model evaluations have been conducted, and the results show that Qwen-Image-2512 is currently the strongest open-source model and remains highly competitive among closed-source models. The model supports a variety of tasks, including generating images with specific aspect ratios, rendering detailed human portraits, landscapes, and complex infographics. It is capable of accurately capturing age cues, facial expressions, and environmental context, as well as rendering fine details such as hair strands, fur, and water flow. The model also supports the generation of text-rich images, including PPT slides, infographics, and educational posters, with improved accuracy, layout, and multimodal integration. The model is implemented using the diffusers library and can be loaded and used with Python code. It is available for use on platforms such as Hugging Face and ModelScope. The model is based on a diffusion pipeline and can be run on CUDA-enabled devices for faster inference. The model is suitable for a wide range of applications, including but not limited to, content creation, design, and educational materials. However, the model may still have limitations in certain scenarios, and users are encouraged to test and evaluate its performance for their specific use cases.

instruction: Can you transfer the yoga pose from this reference photo to the image of the dancer?
description: AnyPose LoRAs are designed to enable pose transfer between images without the need for control nets or openpose. The model allows users to copy the pose from a reference image onto another image by using a detailed prompt. It is optimized for fast inference and works with a single reference image as a pose guide. The model supports both simple and complex poses, including yoga poses, and is intended to be used with Qwen Image Edit 2511. It uses a 4-step lightning LoRA setup with both base and helper weights set to 0.7 for optimal performance. The model is trained primarily on 3D poses generated via Blender, which allows for precise control over character positioning and movement. However, it has limitations when applied to 2D or flat art styles such as cel-shaded or pixel art, where performance may be poor. It also struggles with highly technical poses and images containing more than one character. The model may sometimes replace the background of the reference image with the background of the target image, which can be corrected by explicitly prompting for the desired background. Users are advised to provide full-body images with visible environments to improve consistency. The model may hallucinate or incorrectly fill in missing areas, but these issues can often be addressed with additional prompting. A version 2 of the model is planned to address these limitations and improve support for non-humanoid characters and overall consistency.

instruction: Can you design a custom wardrobe with specific dimensions of 200cm in height and 100cm in width?
description: This is a 3D furniture generation model based on a Transformer architecture. It is designed to generate 3D furniture structural data based on text prompts such as 'wardrobe' or 'cabinet'. The model directly produces custom JSON structures containing panel size, position, rotation, and type information, which can be rendered directly in front-end frameworks such as Three.js. The model supports structured output in the form of JSON structures that can be directly parsed. It allows parametric control by supporting the input of furniture type, size, and other parameters. It achieves millimeter-level accuracy in coordinates through normalization training. The model is highly extensible and can be fine-tuned with a user's own furniture dataset. It is compatible with Three.js, enabling the generated furniture models to be rendered directly in the browser. The model is open-sourced on Hugging Face Model Hub and can be downloaded, tested, and fine-tuned. For collaboration or technical inquiries, contact chengyuhan1030@gmail.com.

instruction: Could you help me identify which of these product descriptions best matches a customer's query?
description: The Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B is a text reranking model part of the Qwen3 Embedding series, designed for text embedding and ranking tasks. It is a modified version of a foundational model, adapted as a sequence classification model. The Qwen3 Embedding series builds upon the dense foundational models of the Qwen3 series, offering a comprehensive range of text embeddings and reranking models in various sizes (0.6B, 4B, and 8B). This series inherits the multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills of its foundational model. The Qwen3 Embedding series has achieved state-of-the-art performance in multiple text embedding and ranking tasks, including text retrieval, code retrieval, text classification, text clustering, and bitext mining. The 8B size embedding model ranks No.1 in the MTEB multilingual leaderboard (as of June 5, 2025, score 70.58), while the reranking model excels in various text retrieval scenarios. The Qwen3 Embedding series offers a full spectrum of sizes (from 0.6B to 8B) for both embedding and reranking models, catering to diverse use cases that prioritize efficiency and effectiveness. Developers can combine these two modules seamlessly. The embedding model allows for flexible vector definitions across all dimensions, and both embedding and reranking models support user-defined instructions to enhance performance for specific tasks, languages, or scenarios. The Qwen3 Embedding series supports over 100 languages, including various programming languages, and provides robust multilingual, cross-lingual, and code retrieval capabilities. The Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B has 0.6B parameters, a context length of 32k, and supports 100+ languages. Evaluation results show that the Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B achieves a score of 65.80 on MTEB-R, 71.31 on CMTEB-R, 66.36 on MMTEB-R, 67.28 on MLDR, 73.42 on MTEB-Code, and 5.41 on FollowIR. Developers are advised to customize the input instructions according to their specific tasks and scenarios, as using instructions typically yields an improvement of 1% to 5% in performance. In multilingual contexts, it is recommended to write instructions in English, as most training instructions were originally in English.

instruction: How do I transform this video into a night scene with realistic lighting and shadows?
description: Lucy Edit 1.1 Dev is an open-weight video editing model that performs instruction-guided edits on videos using free-text prompts. It supports a variety of edits, including clothing and accessory changes, character changes, object insertions, and scene replacements, while preserving the motion and composition of the video. Built on the Wan2.2 5B architecture, it inherits a high-compression VAE and DiT stack, making it easier to adapt existing scripts and workflows. The model preserves motion and composition perfectly, allowing for precise edits. It offers improved edit reliability compared to common inference time methods. It supports changing wardrobes and accessories, such as adding glasses, earrings, or hats. It can replace characters with monsters, animals, or known characters, for example, replacing a person with a polar bear. It can also transform scenes, such as turning a scene into a 2D cartoon. It uses pure text instructions without requiring finetuning or masks for common edits. It supports global edits to the background and style, performing scene-wide changes while preserving subjects. It provides seamless blending with cleaner boundaries and more coherent lighting across edits. It can insert general objects with improved spatial grounding. The model has approximately 5B parameters and is built on top of Wan2.2 5B. Version 1.1 includes better overall quality, higher fidelity details, stronger prompt adherence, and improved identity preservation. It offers more reliable global edits with better subject preservation and control. It provides better blending and seamless edits with cleaner boundaries, fewer artifacts, and improved color and lighting consistency across frames. It supports the addition of general objects with more reliable insertion of wearable, handheld, and general scene objects with improved spatial grounding, occlusion, and shadows. Lucy Edit 1.1 is designed for precise, realistic, and identity-preserving video edits. Prompts with 20–30 descriptive words work best, and using the right trigger words helps the model understand the intent. Supported edit types include clothing changes, human or character replacement, object replacement, color changes, adding objects, and global transformations. The model excels at identity conservation, edit precision, realism, and prompt adherence. Longer prompts describing style, appearance, and context improve results. Frame counts of 81 produce better temporal consistency than shorter clips. Version 1.1 improves boundary blending and temporal coherence across edits. The model is available as a hosted API with 5000 free credits. A Diffusers pipeline (LucyEditPipeline) is available, and ComfyUI integration is coming soon. The roadmap includes a public batch API, Diffusers pipeline, remote ComfyUI custom nodes, and a technical report. The model is based on the Wan2.2 (5B) foundation and acknowledges the open-source community, including diffusers and Hugging Face.

instruction: Add a glowing aura around the person in this clip for a magical effect while preserving the motion flow.
description: Lucy Edit 1.1 Dev is an open-weight video editing model that performs instruction-guided edits on videos using free-text prompts. It supports a variety of edits, such as clothing and accessory changes, character changes, object insertions, and scene replacements, while preserving the motion and composition of the original video. Built on the Wan2.2 5B architecture, it inherits a high-compression VAE and DiT stack, making it easier to adapt existing scripts and workflows. The model preserves motion and composition perfectly, allowing for precise edits. It offers improved edit reliability compared to common inference time methods. It supports wardrobe and accessory changes, such as changing outfits and adding items like glasses, earrings, and hats. It can also perform character changes, replacing characters with monsters, animals, or known characters. It supports scene replacements, such as transforming a scene into a 2D cartoon. It accepts pure text instructions without requiring finetuning or masks for common edits. It can perform global edits to the background and style, maintaining subject identity. It provides seamless blending with cleaner boundaries and more coherent lighting across edits. It supports the insertion of general objects with improved spatial grounding. The model has approximately 5 billion parameters and is built on top of Wan2.2 5B. Version 1.1 includes better overall quality, higher fidelity details, stronger prompt adherence, and improved identity preservation. It offers more reliable global edits with better subject preservation and control. It provides better blending and seamless edits with cleaner boundaries, fewer artifacts, and improved color and lighting consistency across frames. It supports the addition of general objects, including wearable, handheld, and general scene objects, with improved spatial grounding, occlusion, and shadows. Lucy Edit 1.1 is designed for precise, realistic, and identity-preserving video edits. Prompts with 20–30 descriptive words work best, and using the right trigger words helps the model understand the intent. Supported edit types include clothing changes, human or character replacement, object replacement, color changes, adding objects, and global transformations. The model excels at identity conservation, edit precision, realism, and prompt adherence. Longer prompts describing style, appearance, and context improve results. Frame counts of 81 produce better temporal consistency than shorter clips. Version 1.1 improves boundary blending and temporal coherence across edits. The model is available via a hosted API and is compatible with the Diffusers pipeline. Future integrations include ComfyUI custom nodes and local inference nodes. The model is based on the Wan2.2 5B architecture and acknowledges contributions from the open-source community, including diffusers and Hugging Face.

instruction: I have an audio file and need a written summary of what was said. Can you assist?
description: The LFM2.5-Audio-1.5B-bf16 model is a multimodal audio model designed for a range of audio-related tasks, including Text-to-Speech (TTS), Speech-to-Text (ASR), and Speech-to-Speech (STS) conversion. It supports interleaved generation of text and audio in a single turn and enables real-time, token-by-token generation for low-latency applications. The model was converted to MLX format using mlx-audio version 0.3.0. It is based on a 1.5B parameter Liquid Foundation Model (LFM) backbone, which is used for multimodal reasoning. The model architecture includes an audio encoder based on a Conformer, an audio decoder using a Depthformer, and a detokenizer that reconstructs audio waveforms using ISTFT from predicted spectrograms. The model also supports decoding via the Mimi neural codec as an alternative. The model can be used with the mlx-audio library, which provides tools for preprocessing, tokenization, and decoding of both text and audio. It allows for the generation of interleaved text and audio responses, where each audio token is a complete frame of shape (8,) containing all 8 codebook values. The model supports streaming generation, enabling real-time audio playback during generation. It includes a ChatState class for managing conversation turns and handling both text and audio inputs and outputs. The model is designed to work with 24kHz audio inputs and can generate audio outputs at the same sample rate. The model's generation process can be configured with parameters such as max_new_tokens, temperature, top_k, and top_p for text, and similar parameters for audio generation. The model is intended for use in applications requiring natural speech synthesis, audio transcription, and voice-based conversation systems. The model follows the license terms of the original LFM2.5-Audio model.

instruction: Which of these two versions of a user guide is more user-friendly?
description: Skywork-Reward-V2 is a series of eight reward models designed for versatility across a wide range of tasks. These models are trained on a mixture of 26 million carefully curated preference pairs. The Skywork-Reward-V2 series is based on the Bradley-Terry model, with improvements in training data scale and quality to achieve superior performance. Compared with the first generation of Skywork-Reward, the Skywork-Reward-V2 series is trained on a significantly larger and higher-quality preference data mixture, consisting of 26 million preference pairs curated via a large-scale human-LLM synergistic pipeline. The series offers state-of-the-art performance on seven major reward model benchmarks, including RewardBench v1, RewardBench v2, PPE Preference, PPE Correctness, RMB, RM-Bench, and JudgeBench. The series includes models of multiple sizes, with the smallest 0.6B variant, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-0.6B, nearly matching the average performance of the previous best model, Skywork-Reward-Gemma-2-27B-v0.2. The largest 8B version, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B, surpasses all existing reward models across all benchmarks on average. The top experimental model, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M, outperforms all existing reward models on every benchmark. The models are available in eight variants, including Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.2-1B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.2-3B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-0.6B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-1.7B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-4B, and Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-8B. The Skywork-Reward-V2 series outperforms models in both Bradley-Terry and Generative categories with much smaller model sizes. For most use cases, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B is recommended, with smaller variants suggested for low-resource settings. All models are trained on preference data with a maximum length of 16,384 tokens, and inference should be performed within this limit. System prompts should not be included when using chat templates. The model Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M is considered an experimental variant, trained on the complete set of 40 million preference pairs, with about one third of the chosen-rejected pairs flipped. It is recommended for research or non-production purposes only. The models are licensed under the Apache 2.0 License for commercial use, with conditions for attribution and modification. Models derived from Qwen3 follow the Qwen3 license, while those derived from Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct follow the Llama license.

instruction: How do I simplify this legal document without losing its meaning?
description: This model is a set of imatrix quantizations of the INTELLECT-3 model by PrimeIntellect, using the llama.cpp release b7127. The quantizations are based on a dataset from a combined source, including a subset of combined_all_small.parquet from Ed Addario. The model can be run directly with llama.cpp or any other llama.cpp-based project. The prompt format includes a system prompt, user input, and assistant response. The model is available in various quantization types, each with different file sizes and quality levels. Some quantizations use Q8_0 for embedding and output weights to maintain higher quality. The model supports online repacking for ARM and AVX CPU inference, which can improve performance on specific hardware. For models larger than 50GB, the files are split and can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool. The Q4_0 quantization format has been deprecated in favor of online repacking. The I-quants (IQX_X) are newer and offer better performance for their size, especially when using cuBLAS (Nvidia) or rocBLAS (AMD), but may be slower on CPU compared to K-quants. The choice of quantization depends on available hardware resources, desired speed, and quality. The model has been benchmarked on an AVX2 system, showing performance improvements for certain quantizations. Credits are given to several contributors for assistance with dataset creation, inspiration, and sponsorship.

instruction: Can you help me write a Python script that calculates Fibonacci numbers up to 1000?
description: Qwen2.5 is the latest series of Qwen large language models. The Qwen2.5 series includes a range of base language models and instruction-tuned language models with parameter sizes ranging from 0.5 to 72 billion. This model introduces several improvements over Qwen2, including significantly more knowledge and enhanced capabilities in coding and mathematics, achieved through specialized expert models in these domains. It also shows significant improvements in instruction following, generating long texts (over 8K tokens), understanding structured data such as tables, and generating structured outputs, especially JSON. The model is more resilient to the diversity of system prompts, which enhances role-play implementation and condition-setting for chatbots. It supports long-context processing up to 128K tokens and can generate up to 8K tokens. The model supports over 29 languages, including Chinese, English, French, Spanish, Portuguese, German, Italian, Russian, Japanese, Korean, Vietnamese, Thai, and Arabic. The instruction-tuned 7B Qwen2.5 model is a causal language model that has undergone pretraining and post-training. It uses a transformer architecture with RoPE, SwiGLU, RMSNorm, and Attention QKV bias. It has 7.61 billion parameters, with 6.53 billion non-embedding parameters. The model has 28 layers and uses 28 attention heads for queries and 4 for keys and values. It supports a full context length of 131,072 tokens and can generate up to 8192 tokens. The model requires the latest version of the Hugging Face `transformers` library for compatibility. Older versions of `transformers` (before 4.37.0) may result in a KeyError. The model can be loaded and used with the `AutoModelForCausalLM` and `AutoTokenizer` classes from the `transformers` library. For processing long texts beyond 32,768 tokens, a technique called YaRN is used to enhance model length extrapolation. This can be enabled by adding a specific configuration to the `config.json` file. For deployment, vLLM is recommended, although it currently only supports static YARN, which may impact performance on shorter texts. The model's evaluation results are detailed in a separate document, and information on GPU memory requirements and throughput is also available. If the model is used, it should be cited using the provided references.

instruction: I recorded a lecture—can you convert it into a written document with timestamps for each section?
description: LFM2.5-Audio-1.5B is an updated end-to-end audio foundation model. Key improvements include a custom, LFM-based audio detokenizer, `llama.cpp` compatible GGUFs for CPU inference, and better ASR and TTS performance. LFM2.5-Audio is an end-to-end multimodal speech and text language model, and as such does not require separate ASR and TTS components. Designed with low latency and real-time conversation in mind, at only 1.5 billion parameters LFM2.5-Audio enables seamless conversational interaction, achieving capabilities on par with much larger models. Our model consists of a pretrained LFM2.5 model as its multimodal backbone, along with a FastConformer-based audio encoder to handle continuous audio inputs, and an RQ-transformer generating discrete tokens coupled with a lightweight audio detokenizer for audio output. LFM2.5-Audio supports two distinct generation routines, each suitable for a set of tasks. Interleaved generation enables real-time speech-to-speech conversational chatbot capabilities, where audio generation latency is key. Sequential generation is suited for non-conversational tasks such as ASR or TTS, and allows the model to switch generated modality on the fly. The model has 1.2 billion parameters for the language model only, an audio encoder with 115 million parameters, a hybrid convolution and attention-based backbone, an audio detokenizer compatible with 8 codebooks, a context length of 32,768 tokens, a vocabulary size of 65,536 for text and 2049*8 for audio, and uses bfloat16 precision. The model is licensed under the LFM Open License v1.0 and supports English. Performance on VoiceBench (audio input) includes scores of 3.76 on AlpacaEval, 3.53 on CommonEval, 3.15 on WildVoice, 30.92 on SD-QA, 33.04 on MMSU, 44.40 on OBQA, 49.54 on BBH, 28.48 on IFEval, 99.04 on Overall. For ASR, the model has a word error rate (WER) average of 7.53, with 15.63 on AMI, 14.56 on Earnings22, 10.47 on GigaSpeech, 1.95 on LibriSpeech-clean, 4.30 on LibriSpeech-other, 2.76 on SPGISpeech, 3.47 on TED-LIUM, and 7.13 on VoxPopuli. The model is intended for use with the `liquid-audio` package, which provides a lower-level interface for custom use cases, including multi-turn, multi-modal chat. The model is suitable for tasks such as multi-turn chat, ASR, and TTS. The model has some caveats, including that it is currently limited to English and that performance may vary depending on the specific use case and deployment environment.

instruction: Is there a way to make a 3D sculpture from a text prompt describing a fantasy creature?
description: AIEXX GenAI Image to 3D is a professional, GPU-accelerated AI system for generating high-quality 3D models from text prompts or 2D images. It is built on cutting-edge deep learning technologies and delivers production-ready 3D assets in seconds. The system supports both text-to-3D generation and image-to-3D conversion. For text-to-3D, it uses natural language prompts to generate 3D models, integrating Stable Diffusion 1.5 and supporting advanced negative prompting. This process takes 20-60 seconds per model. For image-to-3D, it converts a single image into a full 3D mesh, with support for multi-view reconstruction and automated texture generation, taking 15-30 seconds to process. The system is optimized for NVIDIA RTX 5060 GPUs, using CUDA 12.8 and cuDNN for acceleration, and supports BF16 mixed precision training and Flash Attention v2. It is implemented in Python 3.11.9 with PyTorch 2.7.0 and includes TripoSR for 3D reconstruction. The system provides professional output in GLB/GLTF format with PBR material support and configurable mesh resolution, compatible with Blender, Unity, and Unreal. It supports local, offline processing with no compilation required, and includes a drag-and-drop interface. The system requires Windows 10/11 (64-bit), an NVIDIA RTX 5060 GPU with 8GB VRAM, 16GB minimum RAM (32GB recommended), and ~55GB of free storage. It offers three workflows: a basic TripoSR pipeline for quick prototypes, an intermediate prompt-to-3D pipeline using Stable Diffusion 1.5, and an advanced InstantMesh pipeline for high-quality output. Performance benchmarks indicate processing times of 15-30 seconds for image-to-3D, 25-40 seconds for prompt-to-3D with TripoSR, and 45-90 seconds for prompt-to-3D with InstantMesh. The system is built upon open-source projects including ComfyUI, Stable Diffusion, TripoSR, PyTorch, and torch_scatter. It is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License.

instruction: Could you generate a mask that outlines all the lane markings in this image?
description: This model is a Mask2Former-Large (Swin-L backbone) model fine-tuned on the DLR SkyScapes aerial imagery dataset for 20-class semantic segmentation. It is designed for high-resolution urban and peri-urban environments and produces detailed semantic masks for roads, buildings, vegetation, vehicles, and more. The architecture is based on the Mask2Former framework with a Transformer-based Swin-L backbone. The base checkpoint used is `facebook/mask2former-swin-large-ade-semantic`, and the model is trained for the task of semantic segmentation in the domain of high-resolution aerial imagery. It supports 20 semantic classes from the DLR SkyScapes dataset, which is a subset of the original 31 classes. The model uses the CrossEntropyLoss function with an ignore index of 255 and is preprocessed using the Hugging Face `AutoImageProcessor`. For full reproducibility, it is recommended to use `use_fast=False`. The model weights are stored in `model.safetensors`. The DLR SkyScapes dataset is a high-resolution aerial dataset created by the German Aerospace Center (DLR), providing high-quality pixel-level annotations for detailed scene understanding in urban environments. The dataset contains 16 images with a resolution of 5616 × 3744 pixels and a ground sampling distance of 13 cm per pixel. It covers an aerial area of approximately 5.69 km², including both urban and rural regions, with 70,346 annotated instances. The 20 semantic classes used in this model include low vegetation, paved road, non paved road, paved parking, non paved parking, bikeways, sidewalks, entrance/exit, danger area, lane markings, building, car, trailer, van, truck, long truck, bus, clutter, impervious surface, and tree. These classes correspond to the class IDs used during training. The intended applications of this model include urban mapping and GIS, HD map generation, scene understanding for robotics, autonomous driving (aerial-based priors), environmental monitoring, and remote sensing research. However, it is not suitable for safety-critical deployment without human supervision.

instruction: Could you extract the long, tiny text from this document and ensure it's not missed in the output?
description: Chandra is an OCR model that outputs markdown, HTML, and JSON. It is highly accurate at extracting text from images and PDFs, while preserving layout information. Chandra supports converting documents to markdown, HTML, or JSON with detailed layout information. It has good handwriting support and can reconstruct forms accurately, including checkboxes. It also provides good support for tables, math, and complex layouts. The model extracts images and diagrams, including captions and structured data, and supports 40+ languages. Chandra has been evaluated using the olmocr benchmark, which is considered the most reliable current OCR benchmark in testing. In the benchmark, Chandra v0.1.0 achieved an overall score of 83.1 ± 0.9, with scores of 82.2 on ArXiv, 80.3 on Old Scans Math, 88.0 on Tables, 50.4 on Old Scans, 90.8 on Headers and Footers, 81.2 on Multi column, and 92.3 on Long tiny text. It outperformed several other models in multiple categories. Chandra can be used via a command-line interface, an interactive streamlit app, or through code using the HuggingFace or VLLM methods. The model is available for installation via pip and can be accessed through a hosted API or a free playground.

instruction: Generate a scene of a farmer harvesting a golden wheat field under a setting sun.
description: Self Forcing is a method for training autoregressive video diffusion models by simulating the inference process during training, using autoregressive rollout with KV caching. This approach addresses the train-test distribution mismatch and allows for real-time, streaming video generation on a single RTX 4090 while maintaining the quality of state-of-the-art diffusion models. The model is designed to generate high-quality video content and supports both GUI and CLI-based inference. It is trained using a data-free algorithm, meaning no video data is required, and instead uses text prompts and an ODE-initialized checkpoint. The model can be trained using multiple GPUs, with a training run of 600 iterations completing in under 2 hours using 64 H100 GPUs. Performance can be optimized by enabling `torch.compile` or using FP8 Linear layers, though these may affect quality. The model works best with long, detailed prompts, and it is recommended to use third-party LLMs like GPT-4o to extend prompts before input. The model supports chunk-wise autoregressive generation and is evaluated using prompts from the MovieGenVideoBench dataset. The codebase is based on open-source implementations from other repositories. The model is available on HuggingFace and requires an Nvidia GPU with at least 24 GB memory, a Linux operating system, and 64 GB RAM for optimal performance.

instruction: Is there a way to determine the distance between objects in this image?
description: The DPT (Dense Prediction Transformer) model with DINOv2 backbone is a depth estimation model proposed by Oquab et al. It is designed to demonstrate that using the DPT framework with DINOv2 as the backbone results in a powerful depth estimator. The model supports depth estimation tasks and is intended for use in applications requiring accurate depth prediction from images. The architecture combines the DPT framework with the DINOv2 backbone, which is known for learning robust visual features without supervision. The model can be used with the Transformers library by loading the appropriate image processor and model from the 'facebook/dpt-dinov2-base-kitti' repository. The model processes images, generates depth predictions, and allows for visualization of the depth output. The model is based on research published in the paper titled 'DINOv2: Learning Robust Visual Features without Supervision' available on arXiv with the identifier 2304.07193.

description: This model is a fine-tuned version of an unspecified base model on an unknown dataset. It is designed to perform a task related to layout processing, though specific intended uses and limitations are not provided. The model was trained for 10 epochs with a learning rate of 5e-05, a training batch size of 4, and an evaluation batch size of 8. The optimizer used is ADAMW_TORCH with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and an epsilon of 1e-08. The learning rate scheduler type is reduce_lr_on_plateau with a warmup ratio of 0.06. The random seed used during training is 42. During training, the training loss decreased from 3.2231 at epoch 0.7692 to 0.0001 at epoch 10. The validation loss also decreased from 0.7135 at epoch 0.7692 to 0.0001 at epoch 10. The model was developed using Transformers 4.48.0, Pytorch 2.4.1.post100, Datasets 3.2.0, and Tokenizers 0.21.0.
