description: This is a GGUF quantized version of the FLUX.2 [dev] model, which uses a methodology for state-of-the-art performance, with important layers upcasted to higher precision. FLUX.2 [dev] is a 32 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating, editing, and combining images based on text instructions. It is designed for open text-to-image generation, single-reference editing, and multi-reference editing, allowing character, object, and style reference without the need for additional training. The model is trained using guidance distillation, making it more efficient. It has open weights to support scientific research and empower artists to develop innovative workflows. Generated outputs can be used for personal, scientific, and commercial purposes as described in the FLUX [dev] Non-Commercial License. A reference implementation and sampling code are provided for developers and creatives to build upon the model. FLUX.2 [dev] is available in both quantized and non-quantized versions. For local deployment on consumer-grade graphics cards, such as an RTX 4090 or RTX 5090, instructions are available on the GitHub page. Black Forest Labs has evaluated and mitigated a number of risks in the model, including the generation of unlawful content such as child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and nonconsensual intimate imagery (NCII). Pre-training mitigation included filtering pre-training data for NSFW and known CSAM content, with collaboration from the Internet Watch Foundation. Post-training mitigation involved targeted fine-tuning to inhibit certain behaviors and suppress concepts that could lead to synthetic CSAM or NCII. Ongoing evaluations, including internal and external third-party testing, were conducted to identify and address residual risks. The final FLUX.2 [dev] checkpoint demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs in complex generation and editing tasks and showed higher resilience than leading open-weight models. The model includes filters for NSFW and IP-infringing content at input and output, and developers must use these filters or manual review processes under the terms of the FLUX.2 [dev] Non-Commercial License. Inference filters are applied to the API for FLUX.2 Pro, using both in-house and third-party filters to prevent CSAM and NCII outputs. The model also includes content provenance features, such as pixel-layer watermarking and links to the C2PA standard for metadata. The FLUX.2 Pro API applies cryptographically-signed C2PA metadata to output content. Use of the model and access to the API are governed by the FLUX [dev] Non-Commercial License, Developer Terms of Service, and Usage Policy, which prohibit the generation of unlawful content or the use of generated content for unlawful, defamatory, or abusive purposes. The model is monitored for patterns of violative use after release, and takedown requests are issued to websites or businesses that misuse the model. Users or developers who repeatedly violate policies may be banned. A dedicated email address is provided for community feedback, and the company maintains reporting relationships with organizations such as the Internet Watch Foundation and the National Center for Missing and Exploited Children.

instruction: How can I create a visually appealing social media post that encourages people to recycle?
description: The gpt-oss series is designed for powerful reasoning, agentic tasks, and versatile developer use cases. Two open models are available: gpt-oss-120b and gpt-oss-20b. The gpt-oss-120b is intended for production, general-purpose, and high reasoning use cases that fit into a single 80GB GPU (like NVIDIA H100 or AMD MI300X), with 117B parameters and 5.1B active parameters. The gpt-oss-20b is intended for lower latency, local, or specialized use cases, with 21B parameters and 3.6B active parameters. Both models were trained on OpenAI's data and should only be used with the harmony format, as they will not work correctly otherwise. The gpt-oss models are released under a permissive Apache 2.0 license, allowing for free use without copyleft restrictions or patent risk, making them ideal for experimentation, customization, and commercial deployment. Users can adjust the reasoning effort (low, medium, high) based on their specific use case and latency needs. The models provide full access to the reasoning process, facilitating easier debugging and increased trust in outputs, though this is not intended to be shown to end users. The models are fine-tunable, allowing for full customization to specific use cases through parameter fine-tuning. They support agentic capabilities, including function calling and structured outputs. The models were post-trained with MXFP4 quantization of the MoE weights, enabling gpt-oss-120b to run on a single 80GB GPU and gpt-oss-20b to run within 16GB of memory. All evaluations were performed using the same MXFP4 quantization. The gpt-oss-20b model can be fine-tuned on consumer hardware, while the gpt-oss-120b model can be fine-tuned on a single H100 node. The models support web browsing using built-in tools, function calling with defined schemas, and agentic operations like browser tasks. The reasoning level can be set in the system prompts to low, medium, or high, depending on the task requirements. The gpt-oss models can be used with various frameworks and tools, including Transformers, vLLM, PyTorch / Triton, Ollama, and LM Studio. Model weights can be downloaded directly from Hugging Face CLI.

instruction: How accurate is the joint detection in this image with current technology?
description: YOLO26-M-pose is a variant of the Ultralytics YOLO26 model, specifically optimized for human keypoint detection. It is the latest evolution in the YOLO series, designed from the ground up for edge and low-power devices. The model operates with an input size of 640 pixels and achieves a mean average precision (mAP) of 68.8 for pose estimation across the 50-95 confidence range and 89.6 for the 50 confidence threshold. On CPU using ONNX, inference takes 218.0 milliseconds, while on T4 TensorRT10, it runs at 5.0 milliseconds. The model contains 21.5 million parameters and 73.1 billion FLOPs. YOLO26 is built on three core principles: simplicity, deployment efficiency, and training innovation. It is a native end-to-end model that eliminates the need for non-maximum suppression (NMS), resulting in faster and lighter inference. The end-to-end design reduces pipeline complexity, lowers latency, and improves deployment robustness. The model introduces the MuSGD optimizer, a hybrid of SGD and Muon, inspired by Moonshot AI's Kimi K2 breakthroughs in LLM training, which provides enhanced stability and faster convergence. YOLO26-M-pose includes features such as Residual Log-Likelihood Estimation (RLE) for more accurate keypoint localization, optimized decoding for increased inference speed, DFL removal for simplified inference and broader hardware compatibility, and up to 43% faster CPU inference for edge computing.

instruction: Can you convert these speech tokens into a more natural-sounding Japanese voice, especially for an anime-style character?
description: Anime‑XCodec2 is a fine‑tuned variant of HKUSTAudio/xcodec2, trained on approximately 25,000 hours of Japanese anime/game-style voices. Only the decoder was updated; the encoder and codebook remain frozen, so speech tokens are identical to the original XCodec2. This makes the model a drop-in decoder for downstream systems that already work with XCodec2 tokens, such as Llasa. The model is a neural speech codec or speech tokenizer model based on XCodec2 with a decoder fine-tuned for Japanese speech, particularly anime/game-style voices. The training scope involved decoder-only fine-tuning on approximately 25,000 hours of Japanese data, while the encoder and codebook are frozen. Because the encoder and codebook are unchanged, speech tokens produced at encode time are identical with the original XCodec2. Any downstream model expecting XCodec2 codes can use Anime‑XCodec2 as a drop-in decoder. The model operates at a sampling rate of 16 kHz. The intended use of the model is to decode XCodec2 speech tokens, such as from Llasa or other autoregressive token generators trained on XCodec2 codes, into Japanese speech with improved naturalness for anime/game-style voices. It is also intended for the reconstruction of Japanese speech from XCodec2 tokens when analyzing or building Japanese-focused speech pipelines. The model is optimized for Japanese, and performance on other languages may degrade compared to the baseline XCodec2. It operates at a sampling rate of 16 kHz, and inputs must be resampled to 16 kHz before encoding; decoding assumes 16 kHz. The model is tuned toward anime/game-style voices, and out-of-domain speech may not benefit. The training data consists of approximately 25,000 hours of Japanese speech, with a focus on anime/game-style voices, including acting and character voices. Data preparation included resampling to 16 kHz and standard loudness/peak checks where appropriate. The training procedure involved fine-tuning the components `generator.backbone`, `generator.head`, and `fc_post_a`, while all other non-listed components were frozen. The goal of the training was to preserve token compatibility with HKUSTAudio/xcodec2 while improving reconstruction quality for Japanese anime/game-style speech. The model is licensed under CC-BY-NC 4.0, the same as the original XCodec2 license. The model acknowledges the original HKUSTAudio/xcodec2 and thanks contributors and the community around Japanese speech resources.

instruction: How can I enhance a search engine to understand context and meaning rather than just keywords?
description: The Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B is a text embedding model from the Qwen3 Embedding series, designed for tasks such as text retrieval, code retrieval, text classification, text clustering, and bitext mining. It is part of a series that includes models of various sizes (0.6B, 4B, and 8B) and provides both embedding and reranking capabilities. The model is built upon the dense foundational models of the Qwen3 series and inherits their multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills. It supports over 100 languages, including various programming languages, and offers robust multilingual, cross-lingual, and code retrieval capabilities. The Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B has 0.6 billion parameters, a context length of 32,000 tokens, and supports embedding dimensions up to 1024, with user-defined output dimensions ranging from 32 to 1024. The model is instruction-aware, meaning it supports customizing input instructions to enhance performance for specific tasks, languages, or scenarios. Evaluation results show that the 8B size embedding model ranks No.1 in the MTEB multilingual leaderboard with a score of 70.58 (as of June 5, 2025), and the Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B achieves a mean score of 64.33 on the MTEB multilingual benchmark. Developers are advised to use tailored instructions for their tasks and to write instructions in English for optimal performance, as most training instructions were in English. The model can be used with frameworks such as Sentence Transformers, Transformers, and vLLM, and supports deployment via Text Embeddings Inference (TEI) on both GPU and CPU devices. The model is available for use with specific versions of libraries and requires attention to compatibility with older versions of Transformers. The Qwen3 Embedding series is described as offering exceptional versatility and comprehensive flexibility, with a full spectrum of sizes and features to cater to diverse use cases.

instruction: What species are being referred to in this part of the medical literature?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for species entity recognition, specifically for identifying species names from the Species-800 dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated SPECIES800 dataset, which contains 800 manually annotated abstracts for organism mentions. The model supports the classification of two entity types: `B-SPECIES` and `I-SPECIES`. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is based on the distilroberta-base architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It has an F1 score of 0.72, precision of 0.72, recall of 0.73, and accuracy of 0.95. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical and research settings. It is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. The model can be used with different aggregation strategies for entity prediction, including `none`, `simple`, `first`, `average`, and `max`. For large-scale processing, it supports batch and dataset-level operations with adjustable batch sizes depending on hardware capabilities.

instruction: Could you prioritize these web pages to help me find the best resources on quantum computing?
description: jina-reranker-v3-GGUF is a 0.6B parameter multilingual listwise reranker designed for efficient inference, using GGUF quantizations with llama.cpp. It is intended to rerank documents based on their relevance to a given query. The model supports reranking tasks and includes a rerank function that accepts a query, a list of documents, and optional parameters such as the number of top results to return and whether to include embeddings in the output. The model architecture includes quantized model weights in BF16 format and an MLP projector for additional processing. The model is implemented in Python and requires Python 3.8+, llama.cpp binaries, and specific dependencies such as numpy and safetensors. The model is available under a CC BY-NC 4.0 license, and commercial usage requires separate inquiries. The model is described in a research paper titled 'jina-reranker-v3: Last but Not Late Interaction for Document Reranking' by Feng Wang, Yuqing Li, and Han Xiao, available on arXiv.

instruction: How do I compare the semantic similarity between a Chinese and a German sentence?
description: mmBERT is a modern multilingual encoder that significantly outperforms previous generation models like XLM-R on classification, embedding, and retrieval tasks. Built on the ModernBERT architecture with novel multilingual training innovations, mmBERT demonstrates that low-resource languages can be effectively learned during the decay phase of training. It is also significantly faster than any previous multilingual encoder. The model uses bidirectional attention with masked language modeling objectives, optimized specifically for multilingual understanding and cross-lingual transfer. Key features include massive language coverage, with training on over 1800 languages using a progressive inclusion strategy. The model is built on the ModernBERT foundation with Flash Attention 2 and unpadding techniques. It introduces novel training methods such as inverse mask scheduling and temperature sampling. The training data is publicly available, consisting of over 3T tokens. The model demonstrates effective learning of low-resource languages in the final training phase. The training data is composed of 2.3T tokens in the first pre-training phase with 60 languages, followed by an extension phase and a final pre-training phase. The mid-training phase includes 600B tokens across 110 languages, and the decay phase includes 100B tokens across 1833 languages. Data sources include Filtered DCLM, FineWeb2, FineWeb2-HQ, code repositories, academic papers, Wikipedia, and community discussions. The model family includes mmBERT-small and mmBERT-base, with varying parameter counts and language coverage. mmBERT supports tasks such as masked language modeling, cross-lingual embeddings, dense retrieval, cross-lingual classification, and multilingual reranking. The model is designed for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library and can be fine-tuned for specific downstream tasks. The model is publicly available for download and use under an open license. If used in research, it should be cited with the provided BibTeX entry.

instruction: Can you create a scene of a snowy village with glowing windows and a red-roofed church?
description: The FLUX.2 [klein] model family are the fastest image models to date. FLUX.2 [klein] unifies generation and editing in a single compact architecture, delivering state-of-the-art quality with end-to-end inference in as low as under a second. It is built for applications that require real-time image generation without sacrificing quality and can run on consumer hardware with as little as 13GB VRAM. FLUX.2 [klein] 4B Base is a 4 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating images from text descriptions and supports multi-reference editing capabilities. It is a full-capacity foundation model, undistilled, preserving complete training signal for maximum flexibility. It is ideal for fine-tuning, LoRA training, research, and custom pipelines where control matters more than speed. It offers higher output diversity than the distilled models. This repository holds an FP8 version of FLUX.2 [klein] Base 4B. The main repository of this model (full BF16 weights) can be found separately. The model has an exceptional speed and quality-to-size ratio and is ideal for local deployment and fine-tuning on limited hardware. It was trained without step or guidance distillation, making it more efficient and flexible. It has open weights for customization and fine-tuning to drive science, research, and empower artists to iterate with speed. Its outputs can be used for commercial purposes as described in the relevant documentation. A reference implementation of FLUX.2 [klein] 4B Base, as well as sampling code, is provided in a dedicated repository. Developers and creatives looking to build on top of FLUX.2 [klein] 4B Base are encouraged to use this as a starting point. The model is also available in both FP8 and BF16 versions. The FLUX.2 [klein] 4B Base model fits in approximately 13GB VRAM and is accessible on NVIDIA RTX 3090/4070 and above. The model is not intended or able to provide factual information. While the model can output text, the text rendered may be inaccurate or subject to distortion. As a statistical model, this checkpoint may represent or amplify biases observed in the training data. The model may fail to generate output that matches the prompts. Prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting style. The model and its derivatives may not be used in any way that violates applicable law, for the purpose of exploiting, harming or attempting to exploit or harm minors in any way, including but not limited to the solicitation, creation, acquisition, or dissemination of child exploitative content, to generate or disseminate deceptive, fraudulent, misleading or otherwise harmful content, to generate or disseminate personal identifiable information that can be used to harm an individual, to harass, abuse, threaten, stalk, or bully individuals or groups of individuals, to create non-consensual intimate imagery or illegal pornographic content, or for fully automated decision making or high risk applications that adversely impact an individual's legal rights or otherwise create or modify a binding, enforceable obligation. Nothing contained in this Model Card should be interpreted as or deemed a restriction or modification to the license the model is released under. Black Forest Labs is committed to the responsible development and deployment of its models. Prior to releasing the FLUX.2 family of models, the company evaluated and mitigated a number of risks in its model checkpoints and hosted services, including the generation of unlawful content, including child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and nonconsensual intimate imagery (NCII). Pre-training mitigation included filtering pre-training data for multiple categories of 'not safe for work' (NSFW) and known CSAM to help prevent a user from generating unlawful content in response to text prompts or uploaded images. The company partnered with an independent nonprofit organization dedicated to preventing online abuse to filter known CSAM from the training data. Post-training mitigation included multiple rounds of targeted fine-tuning to provide additional mitigation against potential abuse, including both text-to-image (T2I) and image-to-image (I2I) attacks. By inhibiting certain behaviors and suppressing certain concepts in the trained model, these techniques can help to prevent a user from generating synthetic CSAM or NCII from a text prompt, or transforming an uploaded image into synthetic CSAM or NCII. Ongoing evaluation was conducted throughout the process, including multiple internal and external third-party evaluations of model checkpoints to identify further opportunities for mitigation. External third-party evaluations focused on eliciting CSAM and NCII through adversarial testing with (i) text-only prompts, (ii) a single uploaded reference image with text prompts, and (iii) multiple uploaded reference images with text prompts. Based on this feedback, further safety fine-tuning was conducted to produce the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] models. The release decision was made after safety fine-tuning and prior to release, a final third-party evaluation of the proposed release checkpoints was conducted, focused on T2I and I2I generation of synthetic CSAM and NCII, including a comparison with other open-weight T2I and I2I models. The final FLUX.2 [klein] checkpoints demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs in complex generation and editing tasks and demonstrated higher resilience than leading open-weight models across these risk categories. Based on these findings, the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models were approved for release under an Apache 2.0 license and the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models under a non-commercial license to support third-party research and development. Inference filters for NSFW and protected content in inputs and outputs are included in the repository for the FLUX.2 [klein] models. Filters or manual review must be used with the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models under the terms of the FLUX Non-Commercial License, and deployers are encouraged to implement these mitigations when using the FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models. Where these features are implemented on the company's hosted services, multiple filters may be applied to intercept text prompts, uploaded images, and output images. Both in-house and third-party filters are used to mitigate against harmful outputs, such as CSAM and NCII outputs, including filters provided by external organizations. Content provenance features can help users and platforms better identify, label, and interpret AI-generated content online. The inference code for FLUX.2 [klein] implements an example of pixel-layer watermarking. Additionally, the repository includes links to a standard for metadata. The API for FLUX.2 [klein] applies cryptographically-signed C2PA metadata to downloaded output content to indicate that images were produced with the model. Acceptable use of the models and access to the API are governed by policies set out in applicable documentation, including the FLUX Non-Commercial License (for non-commercial open-weight users), Developer Terms of Service, Self-Hosted Commercial License Terms, and Usage Policy (for commercial open-weight model users), and Developer Terms of Service, FLUX API Service Terms, and Usage Policy (for API users). These prohibit the generation of unlawful content or the use of generated content for unlawful, defamatory, or abusive purposes. Developers and users must consent to these conditions to access the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models on Hugging Face. Black Forest Labs takes model safety seriously and provides a dedicated email address (safety@blackforestlabs.ai) to solicit feedback from the community. The company maintains a reporting relationship with organizations such as the and the and welcomes ongoing engagement with authorities, developers, and researchers to share intelligence about emerging risks and develop effective mitigations. The model is licensed under the specified license. This project may contain trademarks or logos for projects, products, or services. Use of Black Forest Labs and FLUX trademarks or logos in modified versions of this project must not cause confusion or imply sponsorship or endorsement. Any use of third-party trademarks, intellectual property or logos is subject to those third-party's policies.

instruction: Could you detect and highlight the presence of any bicycles in this street view?
description: YOLOS is a Vision Transformer (ViT) trained using the DETR loss. It is designed for object detection and is capable of achieving 42 AP on COCO validation 2017, similar to DETR and more complex frameworks such as Faster R-CNN. The model is trained using a 'bipartite matching loss,' where the predicted classes and bounding boxes of each of the N = 100 object queries are compared to the ground truth annotations, padded up to the same length N. The Hungarian matching algorithm is used to create an optimal one-to-one mapping between each of the N queries and each of the N annotations. Standard cross-entropy is used for the classes, and a linear combination of the L1 and generalized IoU loss is used for the bounding boxes to optimize the parameters of the model. The YOLOS model was pre-trained for 300 epochs on ImageNet-1k and fine-tuned for 300 epochs on COCO, a dataset consisting of 118k/5k annotated images for training/validation respectively. This model achieves an AP (average precision) of 28.7 on COCO 2017 validation. The model is intended for use in object detection tasks and supports PyTorch for both the feature extractor and model. The team releasing YOLOS did not write a model card for this model, so this model card has been written by the Hugging Face team.

instruction: How do I take a 3D scan of a furniture piece and make it editable in Blender?
description: MeshCoder is a framework that converts 3D point clouds into editable Blender Python scripts, enabling programmatic reconstruction and editing of complex human-made objects. It addresses prior limitations by developing expressive APIs for modeling intricate geometries, building a large-scale dataset of 1 million object-code pairs across 41 categories, and training a multimodal LLM to generate accurate, part-segmented code from point clouds. The approach outperforms existing methods in reconstruction quality, supports intuitive shape and topology editing via code modifications, and enhances 3D reasoning capabilities in LLMs.

instruction: Could you detect and estimate the positions of body parts in this image with multiple people?
description: The BBox-Mask-Pose (BMP) method integrates detection, pose estimation, and segmentation into a self-improving loop by conditioning these tasks on each other. This approach enhances all three tasks simultaneously. Using segmentation masks instead of bounding boxes improves performance in crowded scenarios, making top-down methods competitive with bottom-up approaches. The method includes MaskPose, a pose estimation model conditioned by segmentation masks instead of bounding boxes, which boosts performance in dense scenes without adding parameters. The BBox-MaskPose (BMP) method links bounding boxes, segmentation masks, and poses to simultaneously address multi-body detection, segmentation, and pose estimation. A fine-tuned RTMDet is adapted for iterative detection, ignoring 'holes'. The system supports multi-dataset training of ViTPose, previously implemented in the official ViTPose repository but absent in MMPose. The ViTPose-b multi-dataset model uses a ViT-b backbone with a multi-layer decoder, takes RGB images (192x256) as input, and outputs keypoint coordinates (48x64 heatmap for each keypoint, 21 keypoints). It is a vision model not dependent on language, licensed under GPL-3.0, and implemented in the MMPose framework. It was trained for 210 epochs with a batch size of 64 and a learning rate of 5e-5 using 4x NVIDIA A-100 hardware. The model was trained on multiple datasets and performs better in multi-body and crowded scenarios than COCO-trained ViTPose. The MaskPose-b model also uses a ViT-b backbone with a multi-layer decoder, takes RGB images (192x256) and estimated instance segmentation as input, and outputs keypoint coordinates (48x64 heatmap for each keypoint, 21 keypoints). It is a vision model not dependent on language, licensed under GPL-3.0, and implemented in the MMPose framework. It was trained for 210 epochs with a batch size of 64 and a learning rate of 5e-5 using 4x NVIDIA A-100 hardware. It uses training data including SAM-estimated instance masks. Compared to ViTPose, MaskPose takes instance segmentation as input and is better at distinguishing instances in multi-body scenes without computational overhead. The fine-tuned RTMDet-l model uses a CSPNeXt-P5 backbone, CSPNeXtPAFPN neck, and RTMDetInsSepBN head. It takes RGB images as input and outputs detected instances, including bounding boxes, instance masks, and class for each. It is a vision model not dependent on language, licensed under GPL-3.0, and implemented in the MMDetection framework. It was trained for 10 epochs with a batch size of 16 and a learning rate of 2e-2 using 4x NVIDIA A-100 hardware. It was trained on data with randomly masked-out instances and is designed for iterative detection, especially effective in multi-body scenes where background would not be detected otherwise.

instruction: Can you remove the person from this street scene while keeping the buildings and lighting consistent?
description: LongCat-Image-Edit is the image editing version of Longcat-Image. It supports bilingual (Chinese-English) editing and achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source image editing models, delivering leading instruction-following and image quality with superior visual consistency. LongCat-Image-Edit supports various editing tasks, such as global editing, local editing, text modification, and reference-guided editing. It has strong semantic understanding capabilities and can perform precise editing according to instructions. The model has strong consistency preservation capabilities, specifically scrutinizing whether attributes in non-edited regions, such as layout, texture, color tone, and subject identity, remain invariant unless targeted by the instruction, which is well demonstrated in multi-turn editing. LongCat-Image-Edit achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in image editing tasks while significantly improving model inference efficiency, especially among open-source image editing models. For both Text-to-Image and Image Editing tasks involving text generation, you must enclose the target text within single or double quotation marks (both English '...' / "..." and Chinese ‘...’ / “...” styles are supported). The model utilizes a specialized character-level encoding strategy specifically for quoted content. Failure to use explicit quotation marks prevents this mechanism from triggering, which will severely compromise the text rendering capability.

instruction: Provide a transcription of this audio file of a podcast with timestamps for each major section.
description: Whisper is a state-of-the-art model for automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech translation, proposed in a paper by Alec Radford et al. from OpenAI. It is trained on over 5 million hours of labeled data and demonstrates a strong ability to generalize to many datasets and domains in a zero-shot setting. Whisper large-v3-turbo is a finetuned version of a pruned model. It is the exact same model, except that the number of decoding layers has been reduced from 32 to 4. As a result, the model is significantly faster, at the expense of a minor quality degradation. Whisper is a Transformer-based encoder-decoder model, also referred to as a sequence-to-sequence model. There are two flavors of Whisper model: English-only and multilingual. The English-only models were trained on the task of English speech recognition. The multilingual models were trained simultaneously on multilingual speech recognition and speech translation. For speech recognition, the model predicts transcriptions in the same language as the audio. For speech translation, the model predicts transcriptions to a different language from the audio. Whisper checkpoints come in five configurations of varying model sizes. The smallest four are available as English-only and multilingual. The largest checkpoints are multilingual only. All ten of the pre-trained checkpoints are available on the Hugging Face Hub. The pre-trained Whisper model demonstrates a strong ability to generalize to different datasets and domains. However, its predictive capabilities can be improved further for certain languages and tasks through fine-tuning. Whisper is supported in Hugging Face Transformers. The model can be used with the pipeline class to transcribe audios of arbitrary length. It can transcribe local audio files by passing the path to the audio file when calling the pipeline. Multiple audio files can be transcribed in parallel by specifying them as a list and setting the batch_size parameter. Transformers is compatible with all Whisper decoding strategies, such as temperature fallback and condition on previous tokens. Whisper predicts the language of the source audio automatically. If the source audio language is known a-priori, it can be passed as an argument to the pipeline. By default, Whisper performs the task of speech transcription, where the source audio language is the same as the target text language. To perform speech translation, where the target text is in English, set the task to 'translate'. The model can be made to predict timestamps. For sentence-level timestamps, pass the return_timestamps argument. And for word-level timestamps, pass return_timestamps='word'. The above arguments can be used in isolation or in combination. For example, to perform the task of speech transcription where the source audio is in French, and we want to return sentence-level timestamps, the following can be used. For more control over the generation parameters, use the model + processor API directly. Whisper has a receptive field of 30 seconds. To transcribe audios longer than this, one of two long-form algorithms are required: sequential or chunked. The sequential long-form algorithm should be used in either of the following scenarios: when transcription accuracy is the most important factor, and speed is less of a consideration, or when transcribing batches of long audio files, in which case the latency of sequential is comparable to chunked, while being up to 0.5% WER more accurate. Conversely, the chunked algorithm should be used when transcription speed is the most important factor or when transcribing a single long audio file. By default, Transformers uses the sequential algorithm. To enable the chunked algorithm, pass the chunk_length_s parameter to the pipeline. The Whisper forward pass is compatible with torch.compile for 4.5x speed-ups. Note that torch.compile is currently not compatible with the chunked long-form algorithm or Flash Attention 2. We recommend using Flash Attention 2 if your GPU supports it and you are not using torch.compile. To do so, first install flash-attn. Then pass attn_implementation='flash_attention_2' to from_pretrained. If your GPU does not support Flash Attention, we recommend making use of PyTorch's scale-product-attention (SDPA). This attention implementation is activated by default for PyTorch versions 2.1.1 or greater. Once a valid PyTorch version is installed, SDPA is activated by default. It can also be set explicitly by specifying attn_implementation='sdpa'. The pre-trained Whisper model demonstrates a strong ability to generalize to different datasets and domains. However, its predictive capabilities can be improved further for certain languages and tasks through fine-tuning. The primary intended users of these models are AI researchers studying robustness, generalization, capabilities, biases, and constraints of the current model. However, Whisper is also potentially quite useful as an ASR solution for developers, especially for English speech recognition. We recognize that once models are released, it is impossible to restrict access to only 'intended' uses or to draw reasonable guidelines around what is or is not research. The models are primarily trained and evaluated on ASR and speech translation to English tasks. They show strong ASR results in approximately 10 languages. They may exhibit additional capabilities, particularly if fine-tuned on certain tasks like voice activity detection, speaker classification, or speaker diarization but have not been robustly evaluated in these areas. We strongly recommend that users perform robust evaluations of the models in a particular context and domain before deploying them. In particular, we caution against using Whisper models to transcribe recordings of individuals taken without their consent or purporting to use these models for any kind of subjective classification. We recommend against use in high-risk domains like decision-making contexts, where flaws in accuracy can lead to pronounced flaws in outcomes. The models are intended to transcribe and translate speech, use of the model for classification is not only not evaluated but also not appropriate, particularly to infer human attributes. Our studies show that, over many existing ASR systems, the models exhibit improved robustness to accents, background noise, technical language, as well as zero shot translation from multiple languages into English; and that accuracy on speech recognition and translation is near the state-of-the-art level. However, because the models are trained in a weakly supervised manner using large-scale noisy data, the predictions may include texts that are not actually spoken in the audio input (i.e. hallucination). We hypothesize that this happens because, given their general knowledge of language, the models combine trying to predict the next word in audio with trying to transcribe the audio itself. Our models perform unevenly across languages, and we observe lower accuracy on low-resource and/or low-discoverability languages or languages where we have less training data. The models also exhibit disparate performance on different accents and dialects of particular languages, which may include higher word error rate across speakers of different genders, races, ages, or other demographic criteria. The models also exhibit disparate performance on different accents and dialects of particular languages, which may include higher word error rate across speakers of different genders, races, ages, or other demographic criteria. The sequence-to-sequence architecture of the model makes it prone to generating repetitive texts, which can be mitigated to some degree by beam search and temperature scheduling but not perfectly. Further analysis on these limitations are provided in the paper. It is likely that this behavior and hallucinations may be worse on lower-resource and/or lower-discoverability languages. We anticipate that Whisper models’ transcription capabilities may be used for improving accessibility tools. While Whisper models cannot be used for real-time transcription out of the box – their speed and size suggest that others may be able to build applications on top of them that allow for near-real-time speech recognition and translation. The real value of beneficial applications built on top of Whisper models suggests that the disparate performance of these models may have real economic implications. There are also potential dual use concerns that come with releasing Whisper. While we hope the technology will be used primarily for beneficial purposes, making ASR technology more accessible could enable more actors to build capable surveillance technologies or scale up existing surveillance efforts, as the speed and accuracy allow for affordable automatic transcription and translation of large volumes of audio communication. Moreover, these models may have some capabilities to recognize specific individuals out of the box, which in turn presents safety concerns related both to dual use and disparate performance. In practice, we expect that the cost of transcription is not the limiting factor of scaling up surveillance projects.

instruction: Can you help me write a Python function to calculate the Fibonacci sequence up to the nth term?
description: Maincoder is a code-focused language model optimized for code generation and completion tasks. It is designed to perform well on coding benchmarks while maintaining a compact size suitable for local deployment. The model is optimized for Python code completion and generation and features a modern transformer architecture with RoPE embeddings, grouped-query attention, QK normalization, and a high depth-to-width ratio. It is pre-trained and mid-trained on custom data mixes developed for high-performance coding and is fine-tuned using a specialized reinforcement learning policy optimization algorithm to improve training stability and accelerate convergence. Maincoder achieves state-of-the-art performance on Python coding benchmarks HumanEval, HumanEval+, and MBPP+, with scores of 0.7622, 0.7256, and 0.7090 respectively. The model has 1 billion parameters and includes features such as rotary position embeddings with a theta of 1,000,000, RMSNorm for pre-normalization, grouped query attention with a 4:1 ratio of query to key-value heads, QK normalization with RMSNorm applied to attention queries and keys, and SwiGLU MLP with SiLU activation. The model has a hidden size of 1536, 32 layers, 16 attention heads (4 key-value heads), a head dimension of 96, a vocabulary size of 151,936, a context length of 2,048 tokens, and uses bfloat16 precision. It supports code completion tasks such as function completion, class completion, and algorithm implementation. The model has a context length limit of 2,048 tokens and is primarily optimized for Python, with performance potentially varying on other languages. It may generate code with bugs or security issues, so generated code should always be reviewed. The model has not undergone any alignment or safety tuning, and outputs may be unsafe or biased. Appropriate safeguards and careful evaluation are recommended for use. The model is released under a license and can be cited as Maincoder-1B: A High-Performance 1B Parameter Coding Model by the Maincode Team in 2025.

instruction: How would a futuristic underwater city appear in 3D if it had transparent domes and coral structures?
description: MV-RAG is a text-to-3D generation method that retrieves 2D reference images to guide a multiview diffusion model. By conditioning on both text and multiple real-world 2D images, MV-RAG improves realism and consistency for rare or out-of-distribution objects. The model is designed to produce 3D-consistent outputs by first retrieving relevant 2D images from a large in-the-wild 2D database and then conditioning a multiview diffusion model on these images to synthesize consistent and accurate multiview outputs. Training is achieved via a hybrid strategy that bridges structured multiview data and diverse 2D image collections. This involves training on multiview data using augmented conditioning views that simulate retrieval variance for view-specific reconstruction, alongside training on sets of retrieved real-world 2D images using a held-out view prediction objective, where the model predicts the held-out view from the other views to infer 3D consistency from 2D data. To facilitate a rigorous out-of-distribution evaluation, a new collection of challenging out-of-distribution prompts has been introduced. Experiments show that the approach significantly improves 3D consistency, photorealism, and text adherence for out-of-distribution or rare concepts, while maintaining competitive performance on standard benchmarks.

instruction: I'm working on adverse event monitoring. Could you detect any genetic factors mentioned here?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for gene entity recognition, specifically for identifying and extracting gene-related entities from biomedical texts. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated GELLUS dataset, which is focused on gene recognition and genetics entities for genomics and molecular biology applications. The model supports the classification of biomedical entities such as 'B-Cell-line-name' and 'I-Cell-line-name'. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the BiomedNLP-BiomedBERT-base-uncased-abstract-fulltext architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It achieves an F1 score of 1.00, precision of 0.99, recall of 1.00, and accuracy of 1.00. Comparative performance on the GELLUS dataset shows it ranks first with an F1 score of 0.9976, precision of 0.9977, recall of 0.9975, and accuracy of 0.9989. The model is useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in research and applications, with proper citation of the associated paper.

instruction: Can you transcribe a 30-second audio clip of a French conversation recorded on my iPhone?
description: The parakeet-tdt-0.6b-v3 is an on-device multilingual automatic speech recognition (ASR) model converted to Core ML for Apple platforms. It powers FluidAudio’s batch ASR and is the same model used in the backend. The model supports 25 European languages and is optimized for low-latency, private, offline transcription. It is based on the Parakeet TDT v3 architecture, a Token Duration Transducer with 0.6 billion parameters. The model accepts 16 kHz, mono, Float32 PCM audio input in the range [-1, 1]. It is implemented in mixed precision optimized for Core ML execution on Apple Neural Engine (ANE) and CPU. The model achieves a real-time factor (RTF) of approximately 110× on M4 Pro in batch mode, meaning 1 minute of audio takes about 0.5 seconds to transcribe. Performance, including throughput and latency, varies depending on the device, input duration, and compute units used. The model is intended for batch transcription of complete audio files on macOS and iOS, local dictation and note-taking applications where privacy and latency are important, and embedded ASR in production apps via the FluidAudio Swift framework. It is supported on macOS 14+ (Apple Silicon recommended) and iOS 17+. The model is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license. It is primarily optimized for European languages, and performance may degrade for non-European languages.

instruction: Could you extract the text from this Chinese business card and show it to me?
description: POINTS-Reader is a vision-language model designed for end-to-end document conversion. It is a streamlined model that follows the structure of POINTS1.5, with the Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct replaced by Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct. The input to POINTS-Reader consists of a fixed prompt and a document image, and the output is a string of text extracted from the document image, delivered directly to the user without any post-processing. The model supports extraction from both Chinese and English documents, achieving scores of 0.133 for English and 0.212 for Chinese on OmniDocBench. It is optimized for high throughput by using a ViT with a moderate number of parameters (600M NaViT) and supports deployment with SGLang. The model's development includes a two-stage data augmentation strategy: the first stage uses automated data to provide basic document extraction capabilities, and the second stage employs continuous self-evolution to improve the quality of generated data. POINTS-Reader has been accepted for presentation at the EMNLP 2025 Main Conference and is available on Hugging Face Spaces. It can struggle with complex layouts, such as newspapers, and may produce repeated or missing content. It also has difficulty handling handwritten inputs, such as receipts or notes, which can lead to recognition errors or omissions. Currently, it supports only English and Chinese, limiting its effectiveness on other languages.

instruction: Can you create a bedroom scene that looks like a serene Chinese garden with traditional furniture and soft lighting?
description: FLUX.1-Layout-ControlNet is a ControlNet conditioned by a semantic image. It is capable of generating a 2D image based on a text description while following the semantic image layout. This ControlNet is applicable to FLUX.1 [dev] and is leveraged within the SpatialGen framework for 3D scene synthesis. It supports generating images of bedrooms with specific styles, such as serene Chinese-inspired, elegant European-style, whimsical cartoon, and futuristic cyberpunk. The model is designed to be used with the diffusers Python library, requiring installation or upgrade of diffusers and peft. It can be used with the FluxControlNetPipeline, which loads the model and processes input images and prompts to generate output images. The model uses a control image to guide the layout of the generated image and allows for customization of parameters such as conditioning scale, number of inference steps, guidance scale, and image dimensions. The model is trained to follow the layout of the control image while incorporating the stylistic and descriptive elements from the text prompt.

instruction: What is the position of the car in this street scene?
description: OWL-ViT, short for Vision Transformer for Open-World Localization, is a zero-shot text-conditioned object detection model that allows querying an image with one or multiple text queries. It was proposed by Matthias Minderer, Alexey Gritsenko, Austin Stone, Maxim Neumann, Dirk Weissenborn, Alexey Dosovitskiy, Aravindh Mahendran, Anurag Arnab, Mostafa Dehghani, Zhuoran Shen, Xiao Wang, Xiaohua Zhai, Thomas Kipf, and Neil Houlsby. OWL-ViT uses CLIP as its multi-modal backbone, combining a ViT-like Transformer for visual features and a causal language model for text features. To enable detection, the final token pooling layer of the vision model is removed, and a lightweight classification and box head is attached to each transformer output token. Open-vocabulary classification is achieved by replacing the fixed classification layer weights with class-name embeddings from the text model. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned end-to-end with the classification and box heads on standard detection datasets using a bipartite matching loss. The model uses a CLIP backbone with a ViT-B/32 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned together with the box and class prediction heads with an object detection objective. The CLIP backbone of the model was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including crawling a handful of websites and using commonly-used pre-existing image datasets. A large portion of the data comes from crawling the internet, making it more representative of people and societies most connected to the internet. The prediction heads of OWL-ViT, along with the CLIP backbone, are fine-tuned on publicly available object detection datasets. The model is intended as a research output for research communities. It is primarily intended for use by AI researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. The model is designed to enable researchers to explore zero-shot, text-conditioned object detection and to support interdisciplinary studies of the potential impact of such models, especially in areas that commonly require identifying objects whose label is unavailable during training.

instruction: I need to sort a set of customer reviews by their relevance to a product feature. Can you assist?
description: The Qwen3-Reranker-4B is a text reranking model designed for text retrieval tasks. It is part of the Qwen3 Embedding model series, which is specifically developed for text embedding and ranking. The model is based on the Qwen3 series and provides a comprehensive range of text embeddings and reranking models in various sizes (0.6B, 4B, and 8B). It inherits the multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills of the foundational Qwen3 model. The Qwen3 Embedding series has achieved state-of-the-art performance in multiple text embedding and ranking tasks, including text retrieval, code retrieval, text classification, text clustering, and bitext mining. The 8B size embedding model ranks No.1 in the MTEB multilingual leaderboard (as of June 5, 2025, score 70.58), while the reranking model excels in various text retrieval scenarios. The Qwen3 Embedding series offers a full spectrum of sizes (from 0.6B to 8B) for both embedding and reranking models, catering to diverse use cases that prioritize efficiency and effectiveness. Developers can combine these modules seamlessly. The embedding model allows for flexible vector definitions across all dimensions, and both embedding and reranking models support user-defined instructions to enhance performance for specific tasks, languages, or scenarios. The Qwen3-Reranker-4B has 4B parameters, a context length of 32k, and supports over 100 languages. Evaluation results show that the Qwen3-Reranker-4B achieves a score of 69.76 on MTEB-R, 75.94 on CMTEB-R, 72.74 on MMTEB-R, 69.97 on MLDR, 81.20 on MTEB-Code, and 14.84 on FollowIR. The model supports instruction-aware reranking, and using instructions typically improves performance by 1% to 5% compared to not using them. Developers are advised to create tailored instructions specific to their tasks and scenarios. In multilingual contexts, instructions should be written in English, as most training instructions were originally in English.

instruction: Can you generate a side profile of this person based on their front-facing portrait?
description: Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 is an enhanced version of Qwen-Image-Edit-2509, featuring multiple improvements, including notably better consistency. Key enhancements include mitigating image drift, improved character consistency, integrated LoRA capabilities, enhanced industrial design generation, and strengthened geometric reasoning ability. The model is designed to perform imaginative edits based on an input portrait while preserving the identity and visual characteristics of the subject. It further enhances consistency in multi-person group photos, enabling high-fidelity fusion of two separate person images into a coherent group shot. Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 integrates selected popular LoRAs directly into the base model, unlocking their effects without extra tuning. For example, it supports realistic lighting control and generating new viewpoints directly with the base model. The model is also optimized for industrial design applications, such as batch industrial product design and material replacement for industrial components. Additionally, it introduces stronger geometric reasoning capability, such as directly generating auxiliary construction lines for design or annotation purposes. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0.

instruction: I need a clean segmentation mask for this image of a flower to use in a graphic project.
description: U-2-Netp is a lightweight version of the U2Net model designed for efficient and effective image segmentation tasks, especially for generating masks. It retains the core architectural design of U2Net while being optimized for faster inference times and reduced memory usage. The model is based on a simplified version of the original U2Net architecture, designed to be more lightweight while still achieving high performance in segmentation tasks. The model consists of several stages with down-sampling and up-sampling paths, using Residual U-blocks (RSU) for enhanced feature representation. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 to match the original U-2-Net model.

instruction: Can you retrieve the most similar technical reports on cloud computing security?
description: The `llama-embed-nemotron-8b` is a text embedding model developed by NVIDIA, optimized for tasks such as retrieval, reranking, semantic similarity, and classification. It is designed for multilingual and cross-lingual text retrieval and serves as a foundational component in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems. The model is based on the Llama-3.1-8B architecture with a bidirectional attention mechanism and consists of 32 hidden layers and an embedding size of 4096. It was trained on a combination of public and synthetically generated datasets, with a total of 16.4 million query-passage pairs. The model achieves state-of-the-art performance on the MTEB (Multilingual, v2) benchmark as of October 21, 2025, with a Borda rank of 1 and 39,573 Borda votes, along with a mean task performance of 69.46. The model is instruction-aware, meaning it supports custom instructions to improve performance for specific use cases. For retrieval tasks, queries must be accompanied by a task instruction in the format `Instruct: {task_instruction}\nQuery: {query}`, while documents do not require special handling. The model outputs embedding vectors of dimension 4096 for each text input and supports input sequences up to 32768 tokens in length. It is compatible with NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems, including architectures such as Ampere, Hopper, Lovelace, Pascal, Turing, and Volta, and is optimized for use with software frameworks like TensorRT and Triton. The model is intended for non-commercial and research use only and is available on Hugging Face as of October 21, 2025. It is recommended to use the model with transformers version 4.51.0 and flash-attention for GPU processing. The model can be integrated using libraries such as Sentence Transformers or Hugging Face Transformers. NVIDIA encourages users to ensure the model meets industry and use-case requirements and to report any concerns regarding model quality, risk, or security vulnerabilities.

instruction: There’s some text in this image that looks like it was printed on a curved surface. Can you still read it?
description: PP-OCRv5_server_rec is one of the PP-OCRv5_rec models, which are the latest generation text line recognition models developed by the PaddleOCR team. It is designed to efficiently and accurately support the recognition of four major languages—Simplified Chinese, Traditional Chinese, English, and Japanese—as well as complex text scenarios such as handwriting, vertical text, pinyin, and rare characters using a single model. The model achieves key accuracy metrics across various text recognition tasks, including 0.5807 for Handwritten Chinese, 0.5806 for Handwritten English, 0.9013 for Printed Chinese, 0.8679 for Printed English, 0.7472 for Traditional Chinese, 0.6039 for Ancient Text, 0.7372 for Japanese, 0.5946 for General Scenario, 0.8384 for Pinyin, 0.7435 for Rotation, 0.9314 for Distortion, 0.6397 for Artistic Text, and an overall average of 0.8401. These metrics are calculated by marking an entire line as incorrect if any character, including punctuation, is wrong, ensuring higher accuracy in practical applications. The model can be used for text recognition tasks through the PaddleOCR framework, either as a standalone module or as part of an OCR pipeline that includes document image orientation classification, text image unwarping, text line orientation classification, text detection, and text recognition modules. It supports integration into projects via Python code and command-line tools, and it can be used with GPU devices for model inference. The model is also compatible with the PP-StructureV3 pipeline for layout analysis, which includes layout detection, general OCR, document image preprocessing, table recognition, seal recognition, and formula recognition modules. The default model used in the PP-StructureV3 pipeline is PP-OCRv5_server_rec, and users can also specify a local model file if needed.

instruction: Create a video that transitions from a quiet village at dawn to a bustling market in the afternoon, using my written notes.
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video generation models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and an efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video and image-to-video (TI2V) system. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity without increasing computational cost. The high-noise expert handles early-stage layout, while the low-noise expert refines details in later stages. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a threshold step defining the switch. The A14B model series, which uses this MoE design, has 27B total parameters but only 14B active parameters per step, maintaining low inference cost and GPU memory usage. Wan2.2 also incorporates meticulously curated aesthetic data with detailed labels for lighting, composition, contrast, and color tone, enabling precise and controllable cinematic style generation. Compared to Wan2.1, Wan2.2 is trained on 65.6% more images and 83.2% more videos, improving generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. The TI2V-5B model, built with the advanced Wan2.2-VAE, achieves a compression ratio of 16×16×4, enabling 720P video generation at 24fps on consumer-grade GPUs like the RTX 4090. It supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation and is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available. The model can run on a single GPU with at least 24GB VRAM and can be optimized for higher VRAM GPUs to improve speed. Wan2.2 is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their use complies with the license terms, including not generating harmful or illegal content. The model is open-sourced and supports both industrial and academic applications.

instruction: Create a short animation of a car speeding through a desert at sunset.
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video generation models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video and image-to-video capabilities. The MoE architecture in Wan2.2 separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity while maintaining computational efficiency. The model includes a high-noise expert for early-stage layout and a low-noise expert for later-stage detail refinement, with a transition point determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). The A14B model series, part of Wan2.2, has two experts with approximately 14B parameters each, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active per step. Wan2.2 also features a 5B dense model, TI2V-5B, supported by a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE with a compression ratio of 4×16×16, increasing the overall compression rate to 64 while maintaining high-quality video reconstruction. TI2V-5B supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps and can run on consumer-grade GPUs like the 4090. The model is trained on significantly larger data compared to its predecessor, with +65.6% more images and +83.2% more videos, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. Wan2.2 achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models and is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available. The repository also includes the I2V-A14B model for image-to-video generation, supporting 480P and 720P resolutions with a MoE architecture for more stable video synthesis and enhanced support for diverse stylized scenes. The model is available for download via Hugging Face and ModelScope, with integration into ComfyUI and Diffusers. Computational efficiency tests show that Wan2.2 models perform well on different GPUs, with specific parameter settings for multi-GPU and single-GPU inference. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license terms and applicable laws.

instruction: Can you analyze this image and tell me what kind of object is in the center?
description: The convnext_large.dinov3_lvd1689m is a DINOv3 ConvNeXt image feature model. It is designed for image classification and as a feature backbone. The model is pretrained on the LVD-1689M dataset using the self-supervised DINOv3 method and is distilled from a DINOv3 ViT-7B model. It has 196.2 million parameters, 34.4 GMACs, and 43.1 million activations. The model processes images of size 224 x 224. It supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. The model is based on the ConvNeXt architecture described in the paper 'A ConvNet for the 2020s' and is implemented using the PyTorch Image Models library. The model is associated with the DINOv3 research, as detailed in the arXiv preprint 'DINOv3'.

instruction: Can you help me identify the rotation of this image so I can fix it before processing?
description: The Document Image Orientation Classification Module is designed to identify the orientation of document images and correct them through post-processing. It is used to determine the orientation of documents or IDs containing text regions before OCR processing, which helps improve OCR accuracy. The model is based on PP-LCNet_x1_0 and classifies document images into four orientation categories: 0°, 90°, 180°, and 270°. It achieves an average recognition accuracy of 99.06% and has a model storage size of 7 MB. The model can be used as part of a larger pipeline that includes document orientation classification and geometric distortion correction. The pipeline supports document preprocessing for OCR, digital document management, and scenarios requiring improved document image quality. It offers flexible deployment options across multiple hardware platforms and supports secondary development through fine-tuning on custom datasets. The model can be integrated into projects using the PaddleOCR library and is compatible with both command-line and programmatic usage. The model is optional within the pipeline and can be enabled or disabled as needed.

instruction: What is happening in this picture, and what emotions might the people in it be feeling?
description: Next 12B is a 12-billion parameter multimodal Vision-Language Model (VLM) based on Gemma 3, fine-tuned to deliver exceptional performance in both text and image understanding. It is designed for superior understanding and generation of text and image descriptions, advanced reasoning and context-aware multimodal outputs, professional-grade Turkish support with extensive multilingual capabilities, and enterprise-ready deployment with optimized quantization options. The model is ideal for enterprises, researchers, and organizations who need a state-of-the-art multimodal AI capable of complex visual understanding, advanced reasoning, and creative generation. Next 12B approaches frontier model performance while maintaining efficiency, with 91.8% on MMLU benchmark, 81.2% on MATH benchmark, 94.3% on GSM8K, and 78.4% on MMLU-Pro. It supports multiple quantization formats including Q8_0, Q4_K_M, F16, and F32 for flexible deployment options. The model is built on a Transformer-based architecture with a causal LLM and an enhanced vision encoder, and it is fine-tuned using advanced instruction and multimodal fine-tuning (SFT) on curated Turkish and multilingual datasets. It supports both text and image modalities and is suitable for use cases such as advanced image captioning, multimodal question answering, text generation, complex reasoning, creative storytelling, and enterprise applications. The model is open-source and licensed under the MIT License, allowing free use, modification, and distribution for commercial and non-commercial purposes.

instruction: Can you improve the clarity of this audio file so it's suitable for a voice-activated assistant in a smart home?
description: DPDFNet is a family of causal, single-channel speech enhancement models designed for real-time noise suppression in challenging everyday environments. It extends the DeepFilterNet2 enhancement framework by incorporating Dual-Path RNN (DPRNN) blocks into the encoder, which enhances long-range temporal and cross-band modeling while maintaining a compact, streaming-friendly design. The models are optimized for deployment on mobile and edge devices using TensorFlow Lite (TFLite). The 16kHz models include baseline, dpdfnet2, dpdfnet4, and dpdfnet8, while a 48kHz model, dpdfnet2_48khz_hr, is available for fullband enhancement. These models are characterized by their causal and low-latency design, suitable for streaming applications such as telephony, conferencing, and embedded devices. The Dual-Path RNN integration improves temporal context and frequency-domain interactions, enabling more robust enhancement in difficult noise conditions. The model family is scalable, allowing users to choose between baseline and dpdfnet2/4/8 to balance quality and computational requirements. The models are intended for real-time, single-channel speech enhancement and are targeted for deployment on mobile devices, embedded NPUs, and edge platforms. The 16kHz models process 16kHz mono noisy speech waveforms and output 16kHz mono enhanced speech waveforms, while the 48kHz model processes and outputs 48kHz mono waveforms. Typical applications include voice calls, video conferencing, always-on voice interfaces, and audio devices such as wearables and earbuds. The models were trained using a mixture of public speech and noise datasets, including DNS4 (downsampled), MLS, MUSAN, and FSD50K. The baseline model has 2.31 million parameters, 0.36 gigaflops of multiply-accumulate operations, and a TFLite size of 8.5 MB. DPDFNet-2 has 2.49 million parameters, 1.35 gigaflops, and a TFLite size of 10.7 MB. DPDFNet-4 has 2.84 million parameters, 2.36 gigaflops, and a TFLite size of 12.9 MB. DPDFNet-8 has 3.54 million parameters, 4.37 gigaflops, and a TFLite size of 17.2 MB. The 48kHz model, DPDFNet-2 HR, has 2.58 million parameters, 2.42 gigaflops, and a TFLite size of 11.6 MB. The models are distributed under the Apache-2.0 license.

instruction: Which code repositories are most similar to the one I described using a natural language query?
description: ModernBERT is a modernized bidirectional encoder-only Transformer model (BERT-style) pre-trained on 2 trillion tokens of English and code data with a native context length of up to 8,192 tokens. It incorporates architectural improvements such as Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE) for long-context support, Local-Global Alternating Attention for efficiency on long inputs, and Unpadding and Flash Attention for efficient inference. ModernBERT is designed for tasks requiring processing of long documents, such as retrieval, classification, and semantic search within large corpora. It is suitable for a wide range of downstream tasks, including code retrieval and hybrid (text + code) semantic search. The model is available in two sizes: one with 22 layers and 149 million parameters, and another with 28 layers and 395 million parameters. ModernBERT was evaluated across a range of tasks, including natural language understanding (GLUE), general retrieval (BEIR), long-context retrieval (MLDR), and code retrieval (CodeSearchNet and StackQA). On GLUE, ModernBERT-base surpasses other similarly-sized encoder models, and ModernBERT-large is second only to Deberta-v3-large. For general retrieval tasks, ModernBERT performs well on BEIR in both single-vector (DPR-style) and multi-vector (ColBERT-style) settings. Thanks to the inclusion of code data in its training mixture, ModernBERT achieves new state-of-the-art code retrieval results on CodeSearchNet and StackQA. ModernBERT’s strong results, coupled with its efficient runtime on long-context inputs, demonstrate that encoder-only models can be significantly improved through modern architectural choices and extensive pretraining on diversified data sources. The model uses an encoder-only, Pre-Norm Transformer with GeGLU activations. It was pre-trained up to 1,024 tokens and then extended to 8,192 tokens. The training data consists of 2 trillion tokens of English text and code. The model was trained using the StableAdamW optimizer with trapezoidal LR scheduling and 1-sqrt decay, on 8x H100 GPUs. ModernBERT’s training data is primarily English and code, so performance may be lower for other languages. While it can handle long sequences efficiently, using the full 8,192 tokens window may be slower than short-context inference. Like any large language model, ModernBERT may produce representations that reflect biases present in its training data. Verify critical or sensitive outputs before relying on them. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 license.

instruction: Make a 5-second animation of a character riding a bicycle down a quiet street.
description: Wan2.2-Anime-Unlimited (v1.0 Stable Toy) is the first truly open-source, unrestricted anime video generation model. It is based on the Wan2.2-5B architecture and can run on 6GB VRAM. The model is uncensored and designed to generate anime-style video content. The v1.0 version is described as a 'Qualified Toy' and is fully stable, serving as a proof of concept that a 5B model, when fine-tuned with 531 high-quality polished clips, can produce impressive anime content. It specializes in character interactions and basic movements. The model is currently in a stable state and is intended to be a fun and usable tool for generating anime videos. The development roadmap includes future versions such as v2.x, which will focus on battle scenes and slice-of-life content with support for 8s and 10s video generation, and v3.0, which will expand the range of anime concepts and art styles. The v5.0 to v6.0 versions will introduce a new audio model, transforming the system into a multimodal audio-video model with sound alignment and unrestricted creativity. The model is being developed with the support of the open-source community and is intended to surpass the performance of the 27B base model in the anime domain. The model is currently being trained on 4,000+ high-quality videos for the v2.x series, and further development depends on community support and funding.

instruction: Can you identify who is speaking at each moment in this recorded meeting?
description: This model is a streaming version of the Sortformer diarizer, designed for speaker diarization in conversations. It is a novel end-to-end neural model trained with unconventional objectives compared to existing end-to-end diarization models. The model employs an Arrival-Order Speaker Cache (AOSC) to store frame-level acoustic embeddings of previously observed speakers. Sortformer resolves the permutation problem in diarization by following the arrival-time order of the speech segments from each speaker. The model can be used to recognize speakers in conversations. The streaming version of Sortformer employs a pre-encode layer in the Fast-Conformer to generate a speaker-cache, which is filtered at each step to retain only high-quality speaker cache vectors. Aside from the speaker-cache management part, the streaming Sortformer follows the architecture of the offline version of Sortformer. The offline version consists of an L-size (17 layers) encoder based on a Fast-Conformer, followed by an 18-layer Transformer encoder with a hidden size of 192, and two feedforward layers with 4 sigmoid outputs for each frame input at the top layer. The model is available for use in the NeMo Framework and can be used as a pre-trained checkpoint for inference or for fine-tuning on another dataset. The model accepts single-channel (mono) audio sampled at 16,000 Hz. The input tensor is a Ns x 1 matrix for each audio clip, where Ns is the number of samples in the time-series signal. The output of the model is an T x S matrix, where S is the maximum number of speakers (in this model, S = 4), and T is the total number of frames, including zero-padding. Each frame corresponds to a segment of 0.08 seconds of audio. Each element of the T x S matrix represents the speaker activity probability in the [0, 1] range. The model operates in a streaming mode (online mode) and can detect a maximum of 4 speakers; performance degrades on recordings with 5 and more speakers. While the model is designed for long-form audio and can handle recordings that are several hours long, performance may degrade on very long recordings. The model was trained on publicly available speech datasets, primarily in English, and performance may degrade on non-English speech and on out-of-domain data, such as recordings in noisy conditions. The model was trained on a combination of 2445 hours of real conversations and 5150 hours of simulated audio mixtures generated by a specific method. The datasets include Fisher English (LDC), AMI Meeting Corpus, VoxConverse-v0.3, ICSI, AISHELL-4, Third DIHARD Challenge Development (LDC), 2000 NIST Speaker Recognition Evaluation, split1 (LDC), DiPCo, AliMeeting, 2004-2010 NIST Speaker Recognition Evaluation (LDC), and Librispeech. The model was trained on 8 nodes of 8×NVIDIA Tesla V100 GPUs using 90-second long training samples and a batch size of 4. The model can be trained using specific tools and scripts. Inference can be performed with post-processing algorithms using inference tools. The model's performance is evaluated using the Diarization Error Rate (DER) on various datasets, including DIHARD III Eval, CALLHOME-part2, and CH109. Evaluation results show varying DER values depending on the latency and whether post-processing is applied. The model is deployed via NVIDIA RIVA ASR, an accelerated speech AI SDK deployable on-prem, in all clouds, multi-cloud, hybrid, on edge, and embedded. Riva provides world-class out-of-the-box accuracy for the most common languages, best-in-class accuracy with run-time word boosting, and customization of acoustic model, language model, and inverse text normalization. It also provides streaming speech recognition, Kubernetes compatible scaling, and enterprise-grade support. The model is licensed under the CC-BY-4.0 license.
