Each quantization is described with its quality level, file size, and whether it is recommended for use. Some quantizations, such as Q4_0, support online repacking for improved performance on ARM and AVX hardware. The model can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool, with instructions provided for downloading specific files or multiple files if the model exceeds 50GB in size. Performance benchmarks are available for an AVX2 system, showing the tokens per second for various quantizations and tasks. The model can be run on CPU or GPU, with recommendations for choosing a quantization based on available RAM and VRAM. I-quants (IQX_X) are newer and offer better performance for their size when used with cuBLAS or rocBLAS, but may be slower than K-quants on CPU. The model includes credits to contributors for assistance with the imatrix calibration dataset, inspiration for embedding/output experiments, and sponsorship support.

instruction: Which category had the highest number of complaints last year?
description: This model is a TAPAS large model fine-tuned on WikiTable Questions (WTQ). It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_large_reset` checkpoint, and the other version is `no_reset`, corresponding to `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_large`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional step called intermediate pre-training, followed by fine-tuning on SQA, WikiSQL, and finally WTQ. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table. The non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised fashion. It was pre-trained with two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table using a balanced dataset of syntactically created training examples. This allows the model to learn a bidirectional representation of tables and associated texts. The model can be used for tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining whether a sentence is entailed or refuted by the contents of a table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head and aggregation head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training these with the base model. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 50,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 512. The optimizer used is Adam with a learning rate of 1.93581e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.128960. An inductive bias is added to ensure the model only selects cells of the same column. The preprocessing involves lowercasing and tokenizing the texts using WordPiece with a vocabulary size of 30,000. The input format is [CLS] Question [SEP] Flattened table [SEP]. The model's performance on the development set for the LARGE version with reset is 0.5097, and with noreset is 0.5062. The model is intended for answering questions related to tables, and users are directed to the HuggingFace documentation for code examples. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model; this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Can you extract the content from this image of a document that includes mathematical expressions?
description: olmOCR-2-7B-1025-FP8 is a quantized version of the olmOCR model, fine-tuned from Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct using a specific dataset. It has been further fine-tuned using GRPO RL training to improve its performance on math equations, tables, and other challenging OCR cases. The model is designed to process document images and extract relevant text and metadata. It is intended to be used via the olmOCR toolkit, which provides an efficient inference setup using VLLM to handle millions of documents at scale. The model expects a single document image as input, with the longest dimension rendered to 1288 pixels. The prompt must include additional metadata from the document, which can be generated using the methods provided by the olmOCR toolkit. The model scores 83.0 on ArXiv, 82.3 on Old Scans Math, 84.9 on Tables, 47.7 on Old Scans, 96.1 on Headers and Footers, 83.7 on Multi column, 81.9 on Long tiny text, and 99.7 on Base, with an overall score of 82.4 ± 1.1 on the olmOCR-Bench when used with the olmOCR pipeline v0.4.0. This model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is intended for research and educational use in accordance with Ai2's policies.

instruction: How long does it take to initialize the system for a 256-word input using the CPU?
description: This model provides several variants of the embedding model published in the document, which are optimized for deployment on Android or iOS using the specified tools or frameworks. The Gecko embedding model can be used on Android through the provided SDK, which is available for download or can be installed directly from Maven. A sample application is also available for demonstration. The model uses the sentencepiece model as its tokenizer. Performance benchmarks are provided for a Samsung S23 Ultra device, with measurements taken for different backends, sequence lengths, initialization times, inference times, memory usage, and model sizes. The model size is measured by the size of the .tflite flatbuffer, which is the serialization format for LiteRT models. Memory usage is an indicator of peak RAM consumption. Inference on the CPU is accelerated using the LiteRT delegate with 4 threads, while inference on the GPU is accelerated using the LiteRT GPU delegate. The benchmarks assume that the XNNPACK cache is enabled. The dynamic_int8 variant refers to a quantized model with int8 weights and float activations. For a sequence length of 256, the CPU backend has an initialization time of 11.03 ms, an inference time of 147.6 ms, and uses 126.3 MB of memory, with a model size of 114 MB. For the same sequence length, the GPU backend has an initialization time of 1306.06 ms, an inference time of 76.2 ms, and uses 604.5 MB of memory, with a model size of 114 MB. For a sequence length of 512, the CPU backend has an initialization time of 30.04 ms, an inference time of 353.1 ms, and uses 225.6 MB of memory, with a model size of 120 MB. For the same sequence length, the GPU backend has an initialization time of 1363.38 ms, an inference time of 173.2 ms, and uses 604.6 MB of memory, with a model size of 120 MB. For a sequence length of 1024, the CPU backend has an initialization time of 79.17 ms, an inference time of 954 ms, and uses 619.5 MB of memory, with a model size of 145 MB. For the same sequence length, the GPU backend has an initialization time of 1419.87 ms, an inference time of 397 ms, and uses 871.1 MB of memory, with a model size of 145 MB.

instruction: Can you produce a high-quality image from a low-quality input while enhancing the visual details?
description: VTP is a unified visual tokenizer pre-training framework designed to improve the quality of latent spaces for generative models. It integrates image-text contrastive, self-supervised, and reconstruction learning to train visual tokenizers from scratch. The framework addresses the 'pre-training scaling problem,' where traditional reconstruction-based training leads to latent spaces biased toward low-level information, which does not translate to better generation performance. VTP introduces a new paradigm where understanding high-level semantics becomes a key driver for improving generation quality. The model supports multiple tasks, including zero-shot classification, linear probing classification, image reconstruction, and image generation. It demonstrates strong performance, achieving 78.2% zero-shot accuracy, 0.36 rFID for reconstruction, and 2.81 FID for generation. VTP shows significant scaling properties, with generative performance improving effectively with increased compute, parameters, and data. Compared to conventional autoencoders, VTP achieves a 65.8% improvement in FID for downstream generation when trained with the same DiT specifications. The model is available for use as an autoencoder, a feature extractor for self-supervised learning, and a CLIP-like image-text encoder. The authors caution that traditional autoencoders cannot be scaled up for diffusion generative models and emphasize the importance of representation learning for parameter, data, and training scalability.

instruction: What are the recommended settings for downloading this model if it's over 50GB?
description: This is a set of imatrix quantizations of the Voxtral-Small-24B-2507 model by MistralAI, using the llama.cpp release b6014 for quantization. The model is intended for use with llama.cpp or other llama.cpp-based projects. The quantizations are generated using the imatrix option and a dataset provided by the original model. The model supports various quantization types, each with different file sizes and quality levels. Some quantizations use Q8_0 for embedding and output weights to maintain higher quality. The available quantizations include BF16, Q8_0, Q6_K_L, Q6_K, Q5_K_L, Q5_K_M, Q5_K_S, Q4_1, Q4_K_L, Q4_K_M, Q4_K_S, Q4_0, IQ4_NL, Q3_K_XL, IQ4_XS, Q3_K_L, Q3_K_M, IQ3_M, Q3_K_S, IQ3_XS, Q2_K_L, IQ3_XXS, Q2_K, IQ2_M, IQ2_S, and IQ2_XS. Each quantization is described with its quality level and file size. The model can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool, with instructions provided for downloading specific files or multiple files if the model exceeds 50GB. Some quantizations, such as Q4_0, support online repacking for improved performance on ARM and AVX hardware. The model includes information on performance benchmarks on an AVX2 system, showing speed improvements for certain quantizations. Users are advised to consider their hardware capabilities when selecting a quantization, balancing speed, quality, and memory usage. I-quants (IQX_X) are recommended for lower quantization levels when using cuBLAS or rocBLAS, while K-quants (QX_K_X) are generally suitable for most use cases. The model includes credits to contributors for dataset creation, inspiration, and sponsorship.

instruction: I want to be sure this voice is genuine. Can you analyze it for signs of AI generation?
description: This is a fine-tuned Wav2Vec2 transformer model for binary audio classification, designed to distinguish between real human speech and AI-generated speech from text-to-speech and voice cloning software. The model is trained to detect audio generated by platforms including ElevenLabs, Amazon Polly, Hexgrad Kokoro, Hume AI, Speechify, and Luvvoice. It is based on a pre-trained Wav2Vec2-XLSR model that was already fine-tuned for deepfake audio detection, and it uses transfer learning with selective layer freezing. The feature extractor and bottom 12 transformer encoder layers are frozen, while the top 12 transformer encoder layers and a classification head are trained. The model has approximately 160 million trainable parameters. It is optimized for 2.5-13 second audio clips, resampled to 16kHz and converted to mono. The model outputs logits for two classes: real (human) audio and fake (AI-generated) audio, which can be converted to probabilities using softmax. The model achieves high performance on the validation set, with an accuracy of 97.9%, a ROC-AUC of 0.998, and a balanced accuracy of 97.9%. However, it is important to note that the high performance is due to the base model's existing deepfake detection capabilities and the similarity of the training data to the validation set. The model may not generalize well to novel TTS engines, advanced voice cloning systems, real-time voice manipulation, or low-quality recordings. The model is intended for use on audio files in WAV, MP3, or FLAC formats, and it is recommended to test it on TTS engines not included in the training data for true generalization assessment.

instruction: Can you guide me through the process of generating a high-quality video using minimal system resources?
description: This repository contains quantized models for HunyuanVideo-1.5, optimized for use with the LightX2V framework. The quantized models are designed to significantly reduce memory usage while maintaining high-quality video generation performance. The available models include two Diffusion Transformer (DIT) models for 720p video generation: one for image-to-video (I2V) and one for text-to-video (T2V) tasks, as well as a quantized text encoder based on the Qwen2.5-VL LLM architecture. These models use FP8-E4M3 quantization with the SGL (SGLang) kernel scheme, which provides up to 50% reduction in VRAM usage, minimal quality degradation compared to full precision models, and faster inference through optimized kernels. The quantized models are intended to be used in conjunction with the original HunyuanVideo-1.5 model weights and require specific configurations to be applied before the generator is created. Performance benefits include lower VRAM requirements, enabling use on GPUs with less memory, faster inference due to optimized kernels, and preserved visual quality. The models support both T2V and I2V tasks with specific parameters such as frame count, guidance scale, and aspect ratio. The original HunyuanVideo-1.5 model weights are still required for operation. For best performance, SageAttention 2 is recommended as the attention mode. The models are released under the Apache 2.0 License.

instruction: Please produce a video of a child riding a bicycle through a sunny park with trees and flowers.
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video models, incorporating innovations such as an effective Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video (TI2V) capabilities. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity without increasing computational cost. The model is trained on significantly larger data, with 65.6% more images and 83.2% more videos than its predecessor, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 includes a 5B model with a 16×16×4 compression ratio, supporting both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps, and can run on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090. The T2V-A14B model supports generating 5-second videos at 480P and 720P resolutions. It delivers outstanding video generation quality and surpasses leading commercial models on the Wan-Bench 2.0 benchmark. The model is built with a two-expert MoE design: a high-noise expert for early denoising stages and a low-noise expert for later stages, each with about 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active per step. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a threshold step defined for switching. The model also features a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE with a T×H×W compression ratio of 4×16×16, increasing the overall compression rate to 64 while maintaining high-quality video reconstruction. The TI2V-5B model supports 720P video generation at 24 FPS and can generate a 5-second 720P video in under 9 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU. It natively supports both text-to-video and image-to-video tasks within a single framework. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license provisions, including not generating harmful or illegal content.

instruction: Please extract all the genetic markers from this excerpt of a genomics study.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for gene entity recognition, specifically for identifying and extracting gene-related entities from biomedical texts. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated GELLUS dataset, which is focused on gene recognition and genetics entities for genomics and molecular biology applications. The model supports the classification of biomedical entities such as 'B-Cell-line-name' and 'I-Cell-line-name'. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the BiomedNLP-BiomedBERT-base-uncased-abstract-fulltext architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It achieves an F1 score of 1.00, precision of 0.99, recall of 1.00, and accuracy of 1.00. Comparative performance on the GELLUS dataset shows it ranks first with an F1 score of 0.9976, precision of 0.9977, recall of 0.9975, and accuracy of 0.9989. The model is useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in research and applications, with proper citation of the associated paper.

instruction: Can you create a realistic version of a medieval castle from a line-art blueprint?
description: ControlNet v1.1 is a neural network structure designed to control diffusion models by adding extra conditions. It is the successor model to ControlNet v1.0 and was developed by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala. This checkpoint is a conversion into `diffusers` format and is specifically conditioned on MLSD images, which are images with annotated line segments. ControlNet is used in combination with Stable Diffusion to support additional input conditions in text-to-image generation. The model is based on a diffusion-based architecture and is primarily used in English. The license is adapted from the work of Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala in the area of responsible AI licensing. The model was trained using a dataset that previously had issues such as duplicated grayscale human images, low-quality images, and incorrect paired prompts. These issues were resolved in the new version. The training dataset was expanded by adding 300K more images containing more than 16 straight lines, and data augmentations such as random left-right flipping were applied. The model was resumed from MLSD 1.0 and continued training for 200 GPU hours using A100 80G. ControlNet supports various conditions including canny edge detection, pixel to pixel instruction, image inpainting, multi-level line segment detection, depth estimation, surface normal estimation, image segmentation, line art generation, anime line art generation, human pose estimation, scribble-based image generation, soft edge image generation, image shuffling, and image tiling. Each condition is associated with a specific type of image input, such as monochrome images with white edges on a black background for canny edge detection, or color-coded images for surface normal estimation. The model is intended to learn task-specific conditions in an end-to-end manner, and the learning process is robust even with small training datasets.

instruction: Based on this image of a room, can you suggest a layout for furniture placement?
description: Qwen3-VL is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced Thinking editions for deployment flexibility. Key enhancements include the ability to operate PC and mobile GUIs by recognizing elements, understanding functions, invoking tools, and completing tasks. It can generate Draw.io, HTML, CSS, and JS from images and videos. The model supports advanced spatial perception, including object position, viewpoint, and occlusion judgment, with 2D and 3D grounding for spatial reasoning and embodied AI. It has a native 256K context length, expandable to 1M, enabling it to handle books and hours-long video with full recall and second-level indexing. The model excels in enhanced multimodal reasoning, particularly in STEM and Math, with causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. It features upgraded visual recognition with broader, higher-quality pretraining that can recognize a wide range of subjects, including celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. The model supports expanded OCR in 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare or ancient characters and jargon, as well as long-document structure parsing. It achieves text understanding comparable to pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE for full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings, enhancing long-horizon video reasoning. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. This is the weight repository for Qwen3-VL-32B-Instruct.

instruction: Can you generate a voice clip that matches the emotion in this text?
description: OuteTTS Version 1.0 is a compact, high-performance text-to-speech (TTS) model designed for speech synthesis and voice cloning. It supports a wide range of languages and features automatic word alignment, native multilingual text processing, and enhanced metadata integration for improved synthesis quality. The model uses a DAC audio encoder with two codebooks for high-fidelity audio reconstruction and includes special tokens for audio codebooks. It supports one-shot voice cloning with approximately 10 seconds of reference audio and can align raw text at the word level, even for languages without clear word boundaries. The model is trained on approximately 60,000 hours of audio and supports a maximum context window of 8,192 tokens. It is optimized for use with a speaker reference to maintain vocal characteristics, emotion, and accent. For optimal performance, a temperature of 0.4 is recommended, along with a repetition penalty of 1.1 applied to a 64-token recent window. The model is built on a base Llama3.2 architecture and has undergone continued pre-training and fine-tuning. It is intended for ethical use in applications that enhance accessibility, creativity, and communication, with prohibitions on impersonation, misleading content, harassment, and unauthorized voice cloning. The model is available through the outetts library, which automatically sets up necessary samplers and patches for all backends.

instruction: Produce a CIFAR10-style image of a ship and save it for future use.
description: Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM) are a class of latent variable models inspired by considerations from nonequilibrium thermodynamics. These models are designed for high-quality image synthesis. The training process is based on a weighted variational bound derived from a novel connection between diffusion probabilistic models and denoising score matching with Langevin dynamics. The models naturally support a progressive lossy decompression scheme that can be interpreted as a generalization of autoregressive decoding. On the unconditional CIFAR10 dataset, the model achieves an Inception score of 9.46 and a state-of-the-art FID score of 3.17. On 256x256 LSUN, the sample quality is comparable to that of ProgressiveGAN. For inference, DDPM models can use discrete noise schedulers such as ddpm, ddim, or pndm. While the ddpm scheduler provides the highest quality, it is also the slowest. The ddim or pndm schedulers offer a better trade-off between quality and inference speed. The model can be loaded using the DDPMPipeline, DDIMPipeline, or PNDMPipeline from the diffusers library. A generated image can be saved as a file for later use.

instruction: What is the depth difference between the closest and farthest visible objects in this image?
description: The DA3 Metric Large model is specialized for metric depth estimation in monocular settings, ideal for applications requiring real-world scale. It provides canonical metric depth, where multiplying by focal length gives metric depth. The model is part of the Monocular Metric Depth series, has 0.35B parameters, and is licensed under Apache 2.0. It supports relative depth estimation, metric depth estimation, and sky segmentation. The model is developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is based on a vision transformer for visual geometry. Its architecture uses a plain transformer with a unified depth-ray representation. The model is trained on public academic datasets only. A single plain transformer, such as a vanilla DINO encoder, is sufficient as a backbone without architectural specialization. A singular depth-ray representation eliminates the need for complex multi-task learning. The model significantly outperforms Depth Anything 2 for monocular depth estimation and VGGT for multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it is trained on academic datasets and may have limitations on certain domain-specific images. Performance may vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity.

instruction: How can I add a vintage filter to this image while keeping the colors vibrant?
description: This repository contains Nunchaku-quantized versions of a model capable of editing images based on text instructions. It is optimized for efficient inference while maintaining minimal loss in performance. The model is developed by the Nunchaku Team and is categorized as an image-to-image model. Two quantized versions are available: one is SVDQuant quantized INT4 FLUX.1-Kontext-dev model for users with non-Blackwell GPUs (pre-50-series), and the other is SVDQuant quantized NVFP4 FLUX.1-Kontext-dev model for users with Blackwell GPUs (50-series). The FLUX.1 [dev] Model is licensed by Black Forest Labs Inc. under the FLUX.1 [dev] Non-Commercial License. Copyright Black Forest Labs Inc. IN NO EVENT SHALL BLACK FOREST LABS INC. BE LIABLE FOR ANY CLAIM, DAMAGES OR OTHER LIABILITY, WHETHER IN AN ACTION OF CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE, ARISING FROM, OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH USE OF THIS MODEL.

instruction: Find the closest match to this description: 'a black and white photo of a rainy cityscape' in the given dataset.
description: The Perception Encoder (PE) is a state-of-the-art encoder for image and video understanding, trained using a simple vision-language learning approach. It was introduced by Meta and is part of a family of large-scale vision encoder models with strong performance on a wide range of vision tasks. PE uses a robust contrastive pretraining recipe and is finetuned on synthetically aligned videos, enabling it to outperform existing models on classification and retrieval tasks. It also produces strong, general features that scale for downstream tasks, allowing large-scale contrastive pretraining to transfer effectively with alignment tuning. PE Core is the base model, trained with a robust image pretraining schedule and finetuned on data generated by a synthetic video data engine. PE Core comes in three sizes: B, L, and G, with G being the main checkpoint and B and L models distilled from it. The G/14 variant has 1.88 billion parameters for the vision tower, a width of 1536, a depth of 50, an MLP size of 8960, 16 attention heads, a CLIP dimension of 1280, and a resolution of 448px. The text tower for G/14 has 0.47 billion parameters, a width of 1280, a depth of 24, an MLP size of 5120, 20 attention heads, and a CLIP dimension of 1280, with a context length of 72 tokens. All PE Core models use an attention pooling block with 8 heads on top of the vision tower, and the B and L models include an additional class token for global aggregation. PE Core achieves strong results on zero-shot image and video classification and retrieval tasks, with the G/14 variant achieving 85.4% accuracy on ImageNet-1k, 80.2% on ImageNet-v2, 92.6% on ImageNet-A, 88.2% on ObjectNet, 58.1% on COCO-T2I, 76.9% on Kinetics-400, and 51.2% on VTT-T2I. PE Core performs particularly well on challenging benchmarks such as ObjectNet and ImageNet-A. The model is designed for image and text feature extraction and supports tasks such as classification and retrieval. It is based on a vision-language architecture and is compatible with the OpenCLIP framework. The model can be used for encoding images and text, computing similarity scores, and generating label probabilities. The model is available through the OpenCLIP and original GitHub repositories. The model is described in detail in the associated research paper.

instruction: I want to extract a person from a complex background — is that possible with this system?
description: BiRefNet is a model designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation (DIS). It is trained on the DIS-TR dataset and validated on DIS-TE and DIS-VD datasets. The model has achieved state-of-the-art performance on three tasks: DIS, HRSOD, and COD. BiRefNet is based on a bilateral reference approach, which is intended to improve segmentation accuracy and resolution. The model supports inference tasks and can be deployed locally or through an inference endpoint. It is implemented using PyTorch and can be loaded with weights from HuggingFace or local storage. The model includes functionality for image preprocessing, prediction, and post-processing, including alpha channel generation for object extraction. The model weights are available in the official repository, and the model can be used with or without the associated GitHub codebase. The model has been developed by researchers from Nankai University, Northwestern Polytechnical University, National University of Defense Technology, Aalto University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and University of Trento. The model is described in the paper 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation,' published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research in 2024. The model's implementation and weights are available on GitHub and HuggingFace, and online demos for inference are provided. The model's development has been supported by GPU resources from Freepik and Fal, as well as assistance with deployment from Not-Lain.

instruction: Can you assess whether the claim 'Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius at sea level' is correct?
description: This is an ONNX-optimized version of the DeBERTa-v3-large model for efficient inference. The repository contains a regular ONNX exported model and an INT8 dynamically quantized model for faster inference with minimal accuracy loss. The model is optimized for zero-shot classification tasks across multiple languages. The quantized model provides faster inference, with a speedup of approximately 2-3x compared to PyTorch, a smaller size due to INT8 quantization, and maintained accuracy with less than 1% loss compared to the original model. The base model is DeBERTa-v3-large, and it is designed for zero-shot classification and natural language inference tasks. The model was converted from PyTorch to ONNX format and uses dynamic quantization with INT8 weights. It is implemented using ONNX Runtime with Optimum. The model is licensed under the MIT License. If used, it should be cited as part of the original DeBERTa-v3-large Zero-Shot Classification model by Laurer, Atteveldt, Casas, and Welbers in 2022. This ONNX optimization was created for efficient deployment in production environments, with special thanks to the original model authors and the Hugging Face Optimum team.

instruction: Find the image that best matches the phrase 'a quiet mountain lake at sunrise'.
description: This is a CLIP model with an extra text depth and a vision MLP head, trained on the LAION-2B English subset. The model uses the ConvNeXt-Large architecture as the image tower, with a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) head in the vision tower instead of a single projection. The text tower has the same width but includes four additional layers, resulting in a depth of 16 and an embedding dimension of 768. The 320x320 resolution model is a weight-averaged combination (model soup) of three fine-tunes from the original 256x256 training run. Each fine-tune used a different learning rate (1e-4, 6e-5, 5e-5) and a different number of additional samples (3.2B, 2B, 2.5B). At 320x320 resolution, the ConvNext-Large-D model is more efficient than the L/14 model at 336x336 resolution, with 2.5x fewer GMACs, 2.8x fewer activations, and 1.22x fewer parameters. The model achieves between 75.9% and 76.9% top-1 zero-shot accuracy on ImageNet-1k. It supports tasks such as zero-shot image classification, image and text retrieval, image classification fine-tuning, linear probe image classification, and image generation guiding and conditioning. The model is intended for research purposes and is not recommended for deployment in any use case without thorough in-domain testing. It is not suitable for use in surveillance or facial recognition due to the lack of testing norms and checks for fair use. The model has not been trained or evaluated on languages other than English and is therefore limited to English language use cases. The model was trained using the LAION-2B dataset, which is a 2 billion sample English subset of LAION-5B. This dataset is uncurated and may contain disturbing content. It is recommended to use a safety filter to reduce the risk of encountering harmful content. The model was trained by Ross Wightman on a cluster using a global batch size of 131072 for 10-16 checkpoint intervals, with a total of approximately 2-3 billion samples seen over the fine-tune. The training procedure involved a slurm script on 64 8-GPU (A100 40GB) nodes with specific training parameters. The model was evaluated using a combination of VTAB+ datasets for classification and COCO and Flickr for retrieval.

instruction: What features are extracted from a large image that isn't a multiple of 16 in width or height?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. The models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones that provide multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers on the class token, or a linear layer applied on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) consists of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) consists of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, image retrieval, 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on web data, the models achieve results such as 87.0 on IN-ReaL, 60.4 on IN-R, 50.9 on Obj.Net, 49.5 on Ox.-H, 47.0 on ADE20k, 0.403 on NYU, 72.7 on DAVIS, 56.3 on NAVI, and 50.4 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-S/16. For ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web data, the models achieve results such as 86.6 on IN-ReaL @256px, 87.7 on IN-ReaL @512px, 73.7 on Obj.Net @256px, 74.1 on Obj.Net @512px, 52.6 on ADE20k @256px, 58.7 on ADE20k @512px, 42.7 on NYU @256px, and 0.448 on NYU @512px for DINOv3 ConvNeXt Tiny. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on satellite data, the models achieve results such as 73.0 on m-BEnet, 96.5 on m-brick-kiln, 94.1 on m-eurosat, 60.6 on m-forestnet, 96.0 on m-pv4ger, 57.4 on m-so2sat, and 79.6 mean for DINOv3 ViT-L/16. The models were trained using Nvidia H100 GPUs on private infrastructure in the USA, with 61,440 hours of compute time for the ViT-7B model, resulting in an estimated carbon emission of 18t CO2eq. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters), ViT-S+ (29M parameters), ViT-B (86M parameters), ViT-L (300M parameters), ViT-H+ (840M parameters), and ViT-7B (6716M parameters), all with a patch size of 16 and varying embedding dimensions, register tokens, heads, and FFN types. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: What is the best classification for the image based on its visual characteristics?
description: LSE-DINOv2 is a DINOv2 Vision Transformer enhanced with a Deep Equilibrium Model (DEM) based local scale adaptation mechanism to improve scale equivariance, ensuring local scale consistency. The model learns to adaptively scale different image regions based on their content using fixed-point iteration to find optimal deformation parameters. This adaptation is applied at multiple transformer layers, and the model is fine-tuned on the ImageNet-1K dataset for image classification. The base architecture is a ViT-Base model with a 14×14 patch size, 224×224 image size, 768 embedding dimension, 12 transformer blocks, 12 attention heads, and 4 register tokens. The DEM adapter includes 3 phi layers, local scaling grids of [16x16, 8x8, 8x8], 2 DEQ layers, CNN channels of [3, 64, 128], 5 DEQ iterations, and is applied at the input, Layer 4, and Layer 8. The model was trained on ImageNet-1K for 20 epochs with a batch size of 200, using the AdamW optimizer with a backbone learning rate of 5e-5 and a DEM learning rate of 1e-5. The model introduces some computational overhead due to DEQ iterations and requires higher memory usage compared to standard DINOv2. It also depends on the `timm` library and optionally the `torchdeq` library. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 license and is described in the paper titled 'Local Scale Equivariance with Latent Deep Equilibrium Canonicalizer' by Rahman et al., published in the Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision in 2025.

instruction: I want to confirm the authenticity of this video. Can you help with that?
description: This model is a powerful deepfake video detection system that combines a ResNext50 network for spatial feature extraction with an LSTM network for temporal analysis. It is designed to classify video authenticity by identifying visual artifacts and temporal inconsistencies. The model achieves 87% accuracy on internal test datasets and is built using PyTorch. It is trained on a custom dataset of real and deepfake videos and provides a binary classification (real/fake) along with a confidence score. The architecture is optimized for video clips of 20 frames and performs best on human face-focused content. Its performance may be affected by video quality, such as heavy compression or low resolution. The model is intended for ethical use in combating misinformation and verifying media authenticity. It is integrated with the Hugging Face pipeline for easy inference and is available as a Docker-ready deployment with a Hugging Face Space.

instruction: Can you simulate the effect of a cloudy sky over a video with bright sunlight?
description: TC-Light is a one-shot model designed to manipulate the illumination distribution of video and achieve realistic world transfer. It is particularly suitable for high-dynamic videos, such as those with motion-rich actions and frequent switching of foreground and background objects. The model is distinguished by its outstanding temporal consistency on highly dynamic scenarios and superior computational efficiency, enabling long video processing, such as 300 frames at a resolution of 1280x720 on a 40G A100 GPU. These features make it valuable for sim2real and real2real augmentation for embodied agents or for preparing video pairs to train stronger video relighting models. TC-Light is a novel generative renderer that addresses the limitations of existing models, which are often constrained by the domain of training data or face bottlenecks in temporal consistency and computational efficiency, especially with complex dynamics and long durations. The model operates in two stages: the first stage optimizes appearance embedding to align global illumination, and the second stage optimizes the Unique Video Tensor (UVT), a canonical video representation, to align fine-grained texture and lighting. To evaluate performance, a long and highly dynamic video benchmark was established. Extensive experiments show that TC-Light enables physically plausible re-rendering results with superior temporal coherence and low computational cost. The model tokenizes input latents in the xy plane and yt plane separately, and the predicted noises are combined for denoising. The output undergoes the two-stage optimization process. The model works better on video resolutions over 512x512, as this is the minimum resolution used to train the related IC-Light model. Higher resolutions help maintain the consistency of image intrinsic properties. It performs relatively better on realistic scenes than on synthetic ones, in terms of both temporal consistency and physical plausibility. However, it struggles to drastically change the illumination of night scenarios or hard shadows, as demonstrated in IC-Light.

instruction: What emotion is conveyed in the sentence 'I can't believe how unfair this situation is!'?
description: The Emotion English DistilRoBERTa-base is a model designed to classify emotions in English text data. It predicts seven emotion classes based on Ekman's 6 basic emotions plus a neutral class: anger, disgust, fear, joy, neutral, sadness, and surprise. The model was trained on a balanced subset of six diverse English text datasets, including data from Twitter, Reddit, student self-reports, and TV dialogues. These datasets collectively provided 2,811 observations per emotion, totaling nearly 20,000 observations. The model was trained on 80% of this data and evaluated on the remaining 20%, achieving an evaluation accuracy of 66%, which is significantly higher than the random-chance baseline of 14%. The model is a fine-tuned checkpoint of a base model, and for a non-distilled version, users are directed to the model card of the original version. The model is intended for use in applications requiring emotion classification, such as analyzing sentiment in social media, news, or other text sources. It can be applied to single text examples or larger datasets, including .csv files, using Hugging Face's pipeline command. The model has been cited in scientific research, including studies on rumor detection, music-to-text synesthesia, and longitudinal analysis of news media sentiment. Users are encouraged to cite the model in their work, with a working paper and BibTeX citation provided for reference. The model's development was supported by Samuel Domdey and others, and users are invited to contact the model's creator for questions or feedback.

instruction: How can I check if this voice clip is a deepfake or a real human voice?
description: The DF Arena 1B is a universal antispoofing model trained on traditional speech antispoofing datasets as well as singing and environmental deepfake data. It is designed to detect audio deepfakes and determine whether an audio sample is spoofed or bonafide. The model supports antispoofing tasks and can be used to classify audio inputs into spoof or bonafide categories. It is based on a neural network architecture optimized for audio deepfake detection. The model has been evaluated on multiple datasets, achieving an Equal Error Rate (EER) of 0.00% on the dfadd dataset, 11.54% on add_2023_round_2, 8.37% on codecfake, 4.66% on asvspoof_2021_la, 0.91% on in_the_wild, 1.14% on asvspoof_2019, 22.21% on add_2022_track_1, 2.92% on fake_or_real, 17.25% on asvspoof_2024, 2.20% on add_2022_track_3, 5.08% on add_2023_round_1, 0.15% on librisevoc, 1.75% on asvspoof_2021_df, and 1.09% on sonar. The average EER across all datasets is 5.919%, and the pooled EER is 9.52%. In terms of F1-score, the model achieves 0.9993 on dfadd, 0.9188 on add_2023_round_2, 0.8695 on codecfake, 0.8037 on asvspoof_2021_la, 0.9928 on in_the_wild, 0.9473 on asvspoof_2019, 0.6678 on add_2022_track_1, 0.9711 on fake_or_real, 0.6615 on asvspoof_2024, 0.9357 on add_2022_track_3, 0.9639 on add_2023_round_1, 0.9958 on librisevoc, 0.7577 on asvspoof_2021_df, and 0.9903 on sonar. The average F1-score is 0.8863, and the pooled F1-score is 0.81. For accuracy, the model achieves 99.97% on dfadd, 88.46% on add_2023_round_2, 91.63% on codecfake, 95.34% on asvspoof_2021_la, 99.10% on in_the_wild, 98.86% on asvspoof_2019, 77.79% on add_2022_track_1, 97.11% on fake_or_real, 82.75% on asvspoof_2024, 97.80% on add_2022_track_3, 94.92% on add_2023_round_1, 99.84% on librisevoc, 98.25% on asvspoof_2021_df, and 98.89% on sonar. The average accuracy is 94.079%, and the pooled accuracy is 90.47%. The model is distributed under a non-commercial license. For questions or issues, users are encouraged to open an issue on the model repository or contact the developers via email. The model can be cited using the provided BibTeX entry.

instruction: Please summarize the key events in this short video in one sentence.
description: Molmo2 is a family of open vision-language models developed by the Allen Institute for AI (Ai2) that support image, video, and multi-image understanding and grounding. Molmo2 models are trained on publicly available third-party datasets, a collection of datasets with highly-curated image-text and video-text pairs. It has state-of-the-art performance among multimodal models with a similar size. Molmo2-4B is based on a vision backbone and outperforms others in the class of open weight and data models on short videos, counting, and captioning, and is competitive on long-videos. Ai2 is committed to open science. The Molmo2 datasets are available. All other artifacts used in creating Molmo2 (training code, evaluations, intermediate checkpoints) will be made available at a later date, furthering the commitment to open-source AI development and reproducibility. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is intended for research and educational use in accordance with Ai2’s policies. The model is trained on third-party datasets that are subject to academic and non-commercial research use only. Users should review the sources to determine if the model is appropriate for their use case. The Molmo2-4B model achieves an average score of 62.8 on 15 academic benchmarks.

instruction: Is it possible to see how close the tree is to the house in this image?
description: DPT-Large, also known as MiDaS 3.0, is a Dense Prediction Transformer (DPT) model trained on 1.4 million images for monocular depth estimation. It was introduced in the paper by Ranftl et al. (2021) and first released in March 2022. DPT uses the Vision Transformer (ViT) as a backbone and adds a neck and head on top for monocular depth estimation. The model is designed for zero-shot monocular depth estimation and can be used by anyone performing such tasks. It is typically intended to be fine-tuned for specific applications and should not be used to intentionally create hostile or alienating environments for people. The model was trained on a dataset called MIX 6, which contains around 1.4 million images, and was initialized with ImageNet-pretrained weights. During training, images were resized such that the longer side was 384 pixels, and random square crops of size 384 were used, with random horizontal flips for data augmentation. The model was evaluated on several benchmarks, including DIW WHDR, ETH3D AbsRel, Sintel AbsRel, KITTI δ>1.25, NYU δ>1.25, and TUM δ>1.25, achieving results of 10.82, 0.089, 0.270, 8.46, 8.32, and 9.97, respectively. The model is available under the Apache 2.0 license. The training and inference were conducted on an Intel Xeon Platinum 8280 CPU with 8 physical cores and an NVIDIA RTX 2080 GPU. Users should be made aware of the risks, biases, and limitations of the model. The extent of the risks involved by using the model remains unknown, and no additional risk mitigation strategies were considered during model development. The model is not intended to inform decisions central to human life or flourishing.

instruction: What is the context and meaning of this social media post and its associated image?
description: InternVL3.5 is a new family of open-source multimodal models that significantly advances versatility, reasoning capability, and inference efficiency in the InternVL series. A key innovation is the Cascade Reinforcement Learning (Cascade RL) framework, which enhances reasoning through a two-stage process: offline RL for stable convergence and online RL for refined alignment. This coarse-to-fine training strategy leads to substantial improvements on downstream reasoning tasks, such as MMMU and MathVista. To optimize efficiency, the Visual Resolution Router (ViR) dynamically adjusts the resolution of visual tokens without compromising performance. Coupled with ViR, the Decoupled Vision-Language Deployment (DvD) strategy separates the vision encoder and language model across different GPUs, effectively balancing computational load. These contributions collectively enable InternVL3.5 to achieve up to a +16.0% gain in overall reasoning performance and a 4.05 × inference speedup compared to its predecessor, InternVL3. In addition, InternVL3.5 supports novel capabilities such as GUI interaction and embodied agency. The largest model, InternVL3.5-241B-A28B, attains state-of-the-art results among open-source MLLMs across general multimodal, reasoning, text, and agentic tasks, narrowing the performance gap with leading commercial models like GPT-5. InternVL3.5 follows the 'ViT–MLP–LLM' paradigm, initializing the language model using the Qwen3 series and GPT-OSS, and the vision encoder using InternViT-300M and InternViT-6B. The Dynamic High Resolution strategy from InternVL1.5 is also retained. The Flash version of InternVL3.5 further integrates ViR, enabling efficient variants suitable for resource-constrained scenarios. InternVL3.5-Flash compresses visual tokens down to 64 tokens per patch using a higher compression rate pixel shuffle module, determined by a patch router assessing semantic richness. This patch-aware compression mechanism reduces the number of visual tokens by 50% while maintaining nearly 100% of the performance of InternVL3.5. The training pipeline comprises four stages: Multimodal Continual Pre-Training (CPT), Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), and Cascade Reinforcement Learning (CascadeRL). In CascadeRL, the model is first fine-tuned using Mixed Preference Optimization (MPO) under an offline RL setting, followed by GSPO under an online RL setting. For the Flash version, an additional training stage called Visual Consistency Learning (ViCO) is introduced to integrate ViR and reduce inference cost. InternVL3.5-Flash is the result of this process. The model supports a Thinking mode, which encourages step-by-step reasoning for complex problems, and a Best-of-N (BoN) strategy for parallel thinking, improving reasoning depth and breadth. The Decoupled Vision-Language Deployment (DvD) strategy separates vision and language processing, with the vision subsystem batching and processing images to produce compact feature embeddings, which are then transmitted to the language subsystem for fusion with the text context prior to decoding. This separation alleviates blocking and brings multimodal prefilling performance closer to that of pure language models. InternVL3.5 is evaluated on a wide range of tasks, including multimodal reasoning and mathematics, OCR, chart and document understanding, multi-image understanding, real-world comprehension, comprehensive multimodal understanding, multimodal hallucination evaluation, visual grounding, multimodal multilingual understanding, video understanding, GUI tasks, embodied tasks, and SVG tasks. The model is also evaluated on language capability. InternVL3.5 is available in various sizes, including 1B, 2B, 4B, 8B, 14B, 38B, 20B-A4B, 30B-A3B, and 241B-A28B, with both GitHub and HuggingFace formats. The model is publicly released under the Apache-2.0 License.

instruction: Can you render this Marathi text into English for a clearer understanding?
description: The Hunyuan Translation Model includes a translation model, Hunyuan-MT-7B, and an ensemble model, Hunyuan-MT-Chimera. The translation model is used to translate source text into the target language, while the ensemble model integrates multiple translation outputs to produce a higher-quality result. It primarily supports mutual translation among 33 languages, including five ethnic minority languages in China. In the WMT25 competition, the model achieved first place in 30 out of the 31 language categories it participated in. Hunyuan-MT-7B achieves industry-leading performance among models of comparable scale. Hunyuan-MT-Chimera-7B is the industry’s first open-source translation ensemble model, elevating translation quality to a new level. A comprehensive training framework for translation models has been proposed, spanning from pretrain → cross-lingual pretraining (CPT) → supervised fine-tuning (SFT) → translation enhancement → ensemble refinement, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) results for models of similar size. The model supports translation between Chinese and other languages, as well as among multiple non-Chinese languages. Specific supported languages include Chinese, English, French, Portuguese, Spanish, Japanese, Turkish, Russian, Arabic, Korean, Thai, Italian, German, Vietnamese, Malay, Indonesian, Filipino, Hindi, Traditional Chinese, Polish, Czech, Dutch, Khmer, Burmese, Persian, Gujarati, Urdu, Telugu, Marathi, Hebrew, Bengali, Tamil, Ukrainian, Tibetan, Kazakh, Mongolian, Uyghur, and Cantonese. The model can be used with the transformers library, and specific instructions are provided for loading and applying the model, including adjustments for fp8 quantized versions. The model is available for download on Hugging Face, and users are encouraged to use version v4.56.0 of the transformers library. For inference, a set of recommended parameters is provided, including top_k, top_p, repetition_penalty, and temperature. The model does not have a default system_prompt. The model was open-sourced in September 2025.

instruction: What genes are being discussed in this part of the biomedical literature?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for gene and protein entity recognition in biomedical texts. It is optimized for high-precision identification of gene and protein mentions in clinical documents, research papers, and healthcare records. The model supports tasks such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is trained on the BC2GM dataset, a curated corpus of MEDLINE abstracts with manually annotated gene and protein mentions. The model uses a ModernBERT-large architecture and is implemented using the Hugging Face Transformers framework. It achieves an F1 score of 0.85, precision of 0.85, recall of 0.86, and accuracy of 0.96. The model is validated on clinical benchmarks and is production-ready for integration into clinical and research workflows. It supports the entity types B-GENE/PROTEIN and I-GENE/PROTEIN. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is available for use in applications such as clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. It is compatible with Hugging Face's pipeline API and supports various aggregation strategies for entity prediction. The model is trained using the AdamW optimizer with learning rate scheduling and validated using cross-validation on a held-out test set. It is designed for efficient batch processing and can be tuned for performance based on hardware capabilities, including CPU and GPU configurations.

instruction: Could you predict how the vehicles around me will move in the next few moments?
description: HERMES is a unified Driving World Model designed for simultaneous 3D scene understanding and generation in autonomous driving scenarios. It integrates scene understanding, which involves interpreting and reasoning about the driving environment, with future scene evolution (generation). HERMES uses a Bird's-Eye View (BEV) representation to consolidate multi-view spatial information while preserving geometric relationships and interactions. The model introduces world queries that incorporate world knowledge into BEV features through causal attention in a Large Language Model (LLM), enabling contextual enrichment for both understanding and generation tasks. HERMES was evaluated on the nuScenes and OmniDrive-nuScenes datasets, achieving state-of-the-art performance with a 32.4% reduction in generation error and an 8.0% improvement in understanding metrics such as CIDEr. The model is based on prior works including BEVFormer v2, InternVL, UniPAD, OmniDrive, and DriveMonkey. HERMES is open-sourced, with code, pretrained weights, and processed data available. A demo has also been released.

instruction: I’m looking for a way to make audio encoding faster and less resource-intensive. Can you help?
description: Distill-NeuCodec is a version of NeuCodec that features a compatible, distilled encoder. The distilled encoder is 10 times smaller in parameter count and uses approximately 7.5 times fewer MACs at inference time. The distilled model includes the following adjustments: the notoriously slow acoustic encoder is replaced with a new acoustic encoder, reducing the parameter count from 70 million to 36 million; the semantic encoder is replaced, reducing the parameter count from 600 million to 21 million. The model is developed by Neuphonic and is categorized as a Neural Audio Codec. It is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license. The model was trained using the same data as the full model, with an additional distillation loss that computes the mean squared error between the outputs of the distilled and original encoders.

instruction: I have a .wav file and I want to encode it into a more compact format. How should I proceed?
description: NanoCodec for Apple Silicon is an MLX implementation of a lightweight neural audio codec. The model is designed for audio encoding and decoding tasks. It supports input and output in .wav file formats, with 22050 Hz mono-channel audio as the standard for both. The architecture consists of a fully convolutional generator neural network and three discriminators. The generator includes an encoder, followed by vector quantization, and a decoder. The model operates at a sample rate of 22.05 kHz and is built using the MLX framework. It contains 105 million parameters. The model is intended to be used for encoding and decoding audio signals, preserving the audio quality while reducing the data size. Users are required to comply with the Apache License 2.0 for the code and the original NVIDIA NeMo NanoCodec license for the model weights and architecture. When using this project, both licenses must be adhered to. The original NVIDIA NeMo NanoCodec model weights and architecture are developed by NVIDIA and are subject to their respective licensing terms.

instruction: Can you recognize the protein family names in this biochemistry textbook excerpt?
description: This model is a state-of-the-art fine-tuned transformer designed to deliver enterprise-grade accuracy for biomedical entity recognition. It is specialized for identifying and extracting various biomedical entities from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is production-ready and suitable for clinical and research applications. The model can identify and classify biomedical entities including B-protein, B-protein_complex, B-protein_enum, B-protein_familiy_or_group, B-protein_variant, I-protein, I-protein_complex, I-protein_enum, I-protein_familiy_or_group, and I-protein_variant. It is trained on the FSU dataset, a biomedical NER dataset focused on protein interactions and molecular biology entities for systems biology research. The dataset includes annotations for proteins, protein complexes, protein families, protein variants, and molecular interaction entities relevant to systems biology and biochemistry research. The model is based on the deberta-v3-small architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling, and validated through cross-validation on a held-out test set. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.92, precision of 0.91, recall of 0.93, and accuracy of 0.97. It is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem and supports various aggregation strategies for entity prediction. The model is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0.

instruction: Is it possible to generate a video from this image where the person moves smoothly and coherently?
description: SteadyDancer is a model designed for harmonized and coherent human image animation with first-frame preservation. It is intended to animate human images while maintaining the structure and appearance of the first frame. The model is associated with the Multimedia Computing Group at Nanjing University and the Platform and Content Group (PCG) at Tencent. This repository provides checkpoints for the model described in the paper titled 'SteadyDancer: Harmonized and Coherent Human Image Animation with First-Frame Preservation.' The model offers lower computational cost compared to alternatives. For users encountering a 'RuntimeError: expected scalar type Float but found BFloat16' issue, it is recommended to pull the latest code of Kijai ComfyUI. For each quantized version of the model, two sets of weights are provided: one derived from fp16 format and another from fp32 format. Both sets of weights are available and yield largely consistent results. The team is currently working to determine the final version of the model to be retained.

instruction: How can I find the best matching answer to the question 'What are the symptoms of a broken arm?' in a medical database?
description: This is a model that maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and was designed for semantic search. It has been trained on 215 million (question, answer) pairs from diverse sources. The model uses mean pooling to generate embeddings and produces normalized embeddings with a length of 1. It supports the use of dot-product, cosine-similarity, or euclidean distance as score functions. When loaded with sentence-transformers, dot-product is preferred as it is faster. The model is intended to encode queries and text paragraphs in a dense vector space to find relevant documents. It has a limit of 512 word pieces, and text longer than that will be truncated. The model was trained on input text up to 250 word pieces and may not work well for longer text. The training procedure involved fine-tuning the model using a contrastive learning objective on a concatenation of multiple datasets. The model was trained using mean-pooling, cosine-similarity as the similarity function, and a scale of 20. The training data includes duplicate question pairs from WikiAnswers, automatically generated (question, paragraph) pairs from Wikipedia, (title, body) and (title, answer) pairs from StackExchanges, triplets from Bing search engine, (query, answer) pairs from Google queries and featured snippets, (question, answer) pairs from Amazon product pages, Yahoo Answers, and other sources. The total number of training tuples is 214,988,242.

instruction: Can you help me categorize this text into one of the following classes: 'positive', 'neutral', or 'negative'?
description: This is an ONNX-optimized version of a RoBERTa-base model for efficient inference. The repository contains a regular ONNX exported model and an INT8 dynamically quantized model for faster inference with minimal accuracy loss. The model is optimized for zero-shot classification tasks across multiple languages. The quantized model provides faster inference, with a speedup of approximately 2-3x compared to PyTorch, a smaller size due to INT8 quantization, and maintained accuracy with less than 1% loss compared to the original model. The base model is RoBERTa-base, and it is designed for zero-shot classification or natural language inference tasks. The model was converted from PyTorch to ONNX format and uses dynamic quantization with INT8 weights. It is implemented using ONNX Runtime with Optimum. The model is licensed under the MIT License. If used, it should be cited as part of the original RoBERTa Base Zero-Shot Classification model by Laurer, Atteveldt, Casas, and Welbers in 2022. This ONNX optimization was created for efficient deployment in production environments, with special thanks to the original model authors and the Hugging Face Optimum team.

instruction: Could you inspect this image to determine its origin—real or artificial?
description: AIRealNet is a binary image classifier designed to distinguish AI-generated images from real human photographs. It is optimized for detecting conventional AI-generated content while adhering to strict privacy standards, avoiding the use of personal or sensitive images. The model leverages the SwinV2 Tiny architecture as its backbone, achieving high accuracy while maintaining a lightweight design suitable for practical deployment. AIRealNet was fine-tuned on a 14k-image dataset from the `Parveshiiii/AI-vs-Real` repository, which is balanced between AI-generated and real images (60% AI-generated, 40% real human images). The training data was sourced from public datasets and AI generation tools, with intentional exclusion of edited personal photos. The model demonstrates high accuracy and robustness on public datasets, though it has limitations in detecting subtle edits, such as nano-scale modifications, and cannot identify AI-modified real-person images due to privacy and ethical guidelines. Performance may also vary on images from unseen AI generators or unconventional content. AIRealNet is intended for use in detecting AI-generated imagery on social media, research publications, and digital media platforms, assisting content moderators, researchers, and fact-checkers in identifying synthetic media. It is not intended for legal verification without human corroboration. The model preprocesses images to 256x256 resolution, extracts features using the SwinV2 Tiny vision transformer, and outputs binary classification probabilities for AI-generated versus real human images. Ethical considerations include a privacy-first approach, responsible deployment with human review, and transparency in communicating limitations and dataset design. Future work aims to improve detection of subtle AI-generated edits, expand training data with diverse AI generators, and explore multi-modal detection capabilities.

instruction: Could you help me reduce the noise in this in-car conversation video?
description: Dolphin is an efficient audio-visual speech separation model that extracts target speech from noisy environments by combining acoustic and visual (lip movement) cues. It achieves state-of-the-art performance while being 6× faster and using 50% fewer parameters than previous methods. The model is designed to separate speech using both audio and video inputs, where the video input consists of grayscale lip region frames. It supports tasks such as hearing aid enhancement, video conferencing noise suppression, in-car speech extraction, AR/VR communication, and edge device deployment. The model architecture includes a DP-LipCoder video encoder, an audio encoder, a global-local attention separator, and an audio decoder. The DP-LipCoder uses a dual-path architecture with visual compression and semantic encoding, vector quantization for discrete lip semantic tokens, and knowledge distillation from AV-HuBERT, with only 8.5M parameters. The audio encoder extracts multi-scale acoustic features, and the global-local attention separator uses a single-pass TDANet-based architecture with global attention for long-range dependencies and local attention for noise suppression. The model is trained on the VoxCeleb2 dataset with 2-speaker mixtures at 0dB SNR, using a SI-SNR loss function. On the VoxCeleb2 benchmark, it achieves a SI-SNRi of 16.1 dB, SDRi of 16.3 dB, PESQ of 3.45, ESTOI of 0.93, with 51.3M parameters, 417G MACs, and an inference speed of 0.015s per 4s-clip. The model requires frontal or near-frontal face views for optimal performance, works best with 25fps video input, and is trained on English speech, which may require fine-tuning for other languages. Performance degrades with severe occlusions or low lighting.

instruction: I need a realistic illustration of a forest with glowing mushrooms and a waterfall at night.
description: The FLUX.2 [klein] model family is the fastest image generation model to date, unifying generation and editing in a single compact architecture. It delivers state-of-the-art quality with end-to-end inference in as low as under a second. Designed for real-time image generation without sacrificing quality, it runs on consumer hardware with as little as 13GB VRAM. FLUX.2 [klein] 4B is a 4 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating images from text descriptions and supports multi-reference editing capabilities. It is fully open under Apache 2.0 and can run on consumer GPUs like the RTX 3090/4070. The model supports text-to-image, image editing, and multi-reference editing at quality that exceeds its size. It is suitable for local development, edge deployment, and production use. The repository includes an FP8 version of FLUX.2 [klein] 4B, with the main repository containing full BF16 weights. A reference implementation and sampling code are provided for developers and creatives. The model is available via the BFL API and in other formats. It can be used with the Diffusers Python library by installing or upgrading diffusers and using the Flux2KleinPipeline. The model is not intended to provide factual information and may produce inaccurate or distorted text. As a statistical model, it may represent or amplify biases from the training data and may fail to generate output that matches prompts. Prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting style. The model and its derivatives may not be used in any way that violates applicable law, for the purpose of exploiting or harming minors, to generate deceptive or harmful content, to generate personal identifiable information that can be used to harm individuals, to harass or abuse individuals or groups, to create non-consensual intimate imagery or illegal pornographic content, or for fully automated decision making or high-risk applications that adversely impact legal rights. Black Forest Labs is committed to responsible development and has implemented pre-training and post-training mitigations to prevent the generation of unlawful content, including child sexual abuse material and nonconsensual intimate imagery. Pre-training mitigation included filtering pre-training data for NSFW and known CSAM content, with partnerships to filter known CSAM. Post-training mitigation involved targeted fine-tuning to inhibit harmful behaviors and suppress certain concepts. Ongoing evaluations were conducted with internal and external third-party assessments to identify and address risks. The final checkpoints demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs and were released under Apache 2.0 for the 4B model and a non-commercial license for the 9B model. Inference filters for NSFW and protected content are included in the repository, and deployers are encouraged to implement these mitigations. Content provenance features include pixel-layer watermarking and C2PA metadata to indicate AI-generated content. Policies govern acceptable use, including the FLUX Non-Commercial License, Developer Terms of Service, and Usage Policy. Black Forest Labs provides a dedicated email address for safety feedback and maintains relationships with organizations to address emerging risks.

instruction: What is the most important message in this video clip, and how do the visuals support it?
description: Phi-4-multimodal-instruct is a lightweight open multimodal foundation model that leverages the language, vision, and speech research and datasets used for Phi-3.5 and 4.0 models. The model processes text, image, and audio inputs, generating text outputs, and comes with 128K token context length. The model underwent an enhancement process, incorporating both supervised fine-tuning, direct preference optimization, and RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) to support precise instruction adherence and safety measures. The languages that each modal supports are the following: Text: Arabic, Chinese, Czech, Danish, Dutch, English, Finnish, French, German, Hebrew, Hungarian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Norwegian, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Spanish, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian; Vision: English; Audio: English, Chinese, German, French, Italian, Japanese, Spanish, Portuguese. The model is intended for broad multilingual and multimodal commercial and research use. It provides uses for general purpose AI systems and applications which require memory/compute constrained environments, latency bound scenarios, strong reasoning (especially math and logic), function and tool calling, general image understanding, optical character recognition, chart and table understanding, multiple image comparison, multi-image or video clip summarization, speech recognition, speech translation, speech QA, and audio understanding. The model is designed to accelerate research on language and multimodal models, for use as a building block for generative AI powered features. Developers should consider common limitations of language models and multimodal models, as well as performance difference across languages, as they select use cases, and evaluate and mitigate for accuracy, safety, and fairness before using within a specific downstream use case, particularly for high-risk scenarios. Developers should be aware of and adhere to applicable laws or regulations (including but not limited to privacy, trade compliance laws, etc.) that are relevant to their use case. This release of Phi-4-multimodal-instruct is based on valuable user feedback from the Phi-3 series. Previously, users could use a speech recognition model to talk to the Mini and Vision models. To achieve this, users needed to use a pipeline of two models: one model to transcribe the audio to text, and another model for the language or vision tasks. This pipeline means that the core model was not provided the full breadth of input information – e.g. cannot directly observe multiple speakers, background noises, jointly align speech, vision, language information at the same time on the same representation space. With Phi-4-multimodal-instruct, a single new open model has been trained across text, vision, and audio, meaning that all inputs and outputs are processed by the same neural network. The model employed new architecture, larger vocabulary for efficiency, multilingual, and multimodal support, and better post-training techniques were used for instruction following and function calling, as well as additional data leading to substantial gains on key multimodal capabilities. It is anticipated that Phi-4-multimodal-instruct will greatly benefit app developers and various use cases. The enthusiastic support for the Phi-4 series is greatly appreciated.
