Feedback on Phi-4 is welcomed and crucial to the model's evolution and improvement. The model has 5.6B parameters and is a multimodal transformer model. The model has the pretrained Phi-4-Mini-Instruct as the backbone language model, and the advanced encoders and adapters of vision and speech. The model's inputs include text, image, and audio. It is best suited for prompts using the chat format. The model's context length is 128K tokens. The model was trained on 512 A100-80G GPUs for 28 days. The training data includes 5 trillion text tokens, 2.3M speech hours, and 1.1T image-text tokens. The model's outputs are generated text in response to the input. The model was trained between December 2024 and January 2025. The model is a static model trained on offline datasets with the cutoff date of June 2024 for publicly available data. The model supports the following languages: Text: Arabic, Chinese, Czech, Danish, Dutch, English, Finnish, French, German, Hebrew, Hungarian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Norwegian, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Spanish, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian; Vision: English; Audio: English, Chinese, German, French, Italian, Japanese, Spanish, Portuguese. The model was released in February 2025. The model's training data includes a wide variety of sources, totaling 5 trillion text tokens, and is a combination of publicly available documents filtered for quality, selected high-quality educational data, and code; newly created synthetic, “textbook-like” data for the purpose of teaching math, coding, common sense reasoning, general knowledge of the world (e.g., science, daily activities, theory of mind, etc.); high quality human labeled data in chat format; selected high-quality image-text interleave data; synthetic and publicly available image, multi-image, and video data; anonymized in-house speech-text pair data with strong/weak transcriptions; selected high-quality publicly available and anonymized in-house speech data with task-specific supervisions; selected synthetic speech data; and synthetic vision-speech data. Focus was placed on the quality of data that could potentially improve the reasoning ability for the model, and the publicly available documents were filtered to contain a preferred level of knowledge. As an example, the result of a game in premier league on a particular day might be good training data for large foundation models, but such information was removed for the Phi-4-multimodal-instruct to leave more model capacity for reasoning for the model's small size. The data collection process involved sourcing information from publicly available documents, with a focus on filtering out undesirable documents and images. To safeguard privacy, image and text data sources were filtered to remove or scrub potentially personal data from the training data. The decontamination process involved normalizing and tokenizing the dataset, then generating and comparing n-grams between the target dataset and benchmark datasets. Samples with matching n-grams above a threshold were flagged as contaminated and removed from the dataset. A detailed contamination report was generated, summarizing the matched text, matching ratio, and filtered results for further analysis. The model is licensed under the . The model is not specifically designed or evaluated for all downstream purposes. Developers should consider common limitations of language models and multimodal models, as well as performance difference across languages, as they select use cases, and evaluate and mitigate for accuracy, safety, and fairness before using within a specific downstream use case, particularly for high-risk scenarios. Developers should be aware of and adhere to applicable laws or regulations (including but not limited to privacy, trade compliance laws, etc.) that are relevant to their use case. Nothing contained in this Model Card should be interpreted as or deemed a restriction or modification to the license the model is released under.

instruction: Which of these resources is most suitable for answering a query about the latest developments in solar panel technology?
description: The granite-embedding-reranker-english-r2 is a 149M parameter dense cross-encoder model from the Granite Embeddings collection, designed to generate high-quality text embeddings. It produces embedding vectors of size 768 based on a context length of up to 8192 tokens. The model was trained using open-source relevance-pair datasets with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses, as well as IBM-collected and generated datasets. It uses a cross-encoder architecture to compute relevance scores between queries and documents by jointly encoding their text, enabling precise reranking based on contextual alignment. The model is trained with ranking-specific loss functions such as pListMLE and model merging techniques to enhance performance. It shows strong performance on standard information retrieval benchmarks (BEIR, MIRACL), long-document search benchmarks (MLDR), and enterprise use cases. The model is based on the ModernBERT architecture, with an embedding size of 768, 22 layers, 12 attention heads, an intermediate size of 1152, a GeGLU activation function, a vocabulary size of 50368, and a maximum sequence length of 8192. It is designed to compute relevance scores for query-document pairs, making it well-suited for reranking tasks in information retrieval and search applications. The model is compatible with the SentenceTransformer library and can be used to jointly encode pairs of text to compute relevance scores. It is also compatible with the Huggingface Transformers library and can be used to encode pairs of text. The model can be used in conjunction with a retriever to produce an end-to-end search system. The model is trained using data from four key sources: unsupervised title-body paired data scraped from the web, publicly available paired data with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses, IBM-internal paired data targeting specific technical domains, and IBM-generated synthetic data. Notably, the model does not use the MS-MARCO retrieval dataset due to its non-commercial license. The underlying encoder models use GneissWeb, an IBM-curated dataset composed exclusively of open, commercial-friendly sources. All data undergoes a data clearance process subject to technical, business, and governance review, capturing information about content description, ownership, intended use, data classification, licensing, usage restrictions, data acquisition, and an assessment of sensitive information. The model is trained using IBM's BlueVela Cluster, which is outfitted with NVIDIA H100 80gb GPUs. The data used to train the base language model was filtered to remove text containing hate, abuse, and profanity. The model is fine-tuned on English and has a context length of 8192 tokens (longer texts will be truncated to this size).

instruction: How do I get a clean mask of this object without including the background?
description: SAM 2 is a foundation model developed by FAIR for solving promptable visual segmentation in images and videos. It is designed to perform segmentation based on various types of input prompts, including point clicks, bounding boxes, and masks from previous predictions. The model supports both image and video segmentation tasks, enabling users to segment objects in static images or track them across video frames. SAM 2 can be used for automatic mask generation to segment all objects in an image, as well as for refining segmentation through multiple points or additional clicks. It allows for batch processing of multiple images or objects within a single image, and supports multi-object segmentation in both images and videos. For video segmentation, SAM 2 can track objects across frames and refine tracking with additional input on later frames. The model is built to handle complex scenarios, including streaming video inference and batched processing of multiple objects. It is implemented using PyTorch and integrates with the Hugging Face Transformers library for ease of use. The model's performance includes the ability to generate multiple mask predictions per input and track objects through hundreds of video frames. Users are advised to refer to the official documentation for detailed usage instructions and examples.

instruction: Is it possible to classify these medical records into risk categories using the data I have?
description: TabPFN-2.5 is a transformer-based foundation model that uses in-context-learning to solve tabular prediction problems in a forward pass. It is designed for regression and classification tasks with up to 50,000 samples and 2000 features in structured tabular format. The model is not suitable for unstructured data such as text or images, and it has not been tested for use with more than 50,000 samples or 2000 features. The architecture consists of a transformer with TabPFNv2-like alternating attention and 18-24 layers. TabPFN-2.5 is trained purely on synthetic tabular tasks, while Real-TabPFN-2.5 is a version that has been continued pre-trained on real-world datasets. The model has been evaluated on proprietary benchmark collections, including TabArena and RealCause, and it achieves new state-of-the-art results in each. It is free from dataset leakage from the pretraining stage due to its training on synthetic data, but users should ensure that the labeled data is free of biases when applied to high-risk use cases. Performance can degrade when applied to more than 50,000 data points and/or 2000 features. The model weights are released under the tabpfn-2.5-license-v1.0, which allows for research and limited internal evaluation but prohibits use for commercial or production purposes. A Commercial Enterprise License is available for production use cases, providing access to a high-speed inference engine, dedicated support, integration tooling, and other internal models. The model was developed by Prior Labs and is available in version v1.0.

instruction: How do I convert the hexadecimal number '1A3' to decimal?
description: This model is a continuously trained Qwen3-0.6B fine-tuned using the Gensyn RL-Swarm framework with GRPO (Generalized Reward Policy Optimization) to enhance reasoning and mathematical capabilities. The model is identified by the Agent ID 'tall_tame_panther' and is currently in active training, with updates occurring every 5-10 minutes. It supports GGUF (llama.cpp) for edge deployment and local inference and is compatible with Text Generation Inference for production use. The model is trained on a composite dataset of 1,000 samples, with a weighted sampling strategy across multiple domains including propositional logic, calendar arithmetic, decimal arithmetic, base conversion, fraction simplification, and basic arithmetic. Training is conducted in bfloat16 precision and involves distributed reinforcement learning across a swarm network. The model has 0.6 billion parameters, with an architecture including 28 layers, 16 attention heads, 8 key-value heads, a hidden size of 1024, and an intermediate size of 3072. It supports a context length of 40,960 tokens and a vocabulary of 151,936 tokens. The model excels in logical reasoning, mathematical operations, number system conversions, date/time calculations, step-by-step problem solving, and conversational tutoring. It is optimized for math and reasoning tasks and may underperform on creative writing. The model is suitable for edge deployment but is not state-of-the-art for complex reasoning. Training is experimental and decentralized, which may lead to less predictable behavior compared to supervised models. The model is compatible with chat formats and supports system instructions, multi-turn dialogue, tool calling, and reasoning mode. It is trained using a reward system that evaluates model outputs via a judge API and updates the policy using GRPO. The model is part of a decentralized swarm with fault tolerance, diverse exploration, and collective intelligence. Training and model updates are verified on-chain. The model is available in multiple quantized formats (F16, Q3_K_M, Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M) and can be deployed using various methods including standard Transformers, TGI, GGUF with llama.cpp, and Ollama. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is developed by 0xgrey. Reproducibility requires specific configurations, including the same seed, dataset, and swarm state. Formal evaluation benchmarks are planned for future inclusion as training progresses.

instruction: Can I get a voice recording from a text input in under a minute on my computer?
description: Pocket TTS is a lightweight text-to-speech (TTS) application designed to run efficiently on CPUs. It eliminates the need for GPUs or web APIs by allowing audio generation through a simple pip install and function call. The model supports Python versions 3.10 through 3.14 and requires PyTorch 2.5 or higher, but does not require the GPU version of PyTorch. It is a CPU-based model with a small size of 100 million parameters. It supports audio streaming with low latency, approximately 200 milliseconds to produce the first audio chunk, and operates at about six times real-time speed on the CPU of a MacBook Air M4. It uses only two CPU cores and provides both a Python API and a command-line interface (CLI). The model supports voice cloning and can handle infinitely long text inputs. It is currently limited to English language support. Users can try the model through a web interface without installation, or use it via the CLI or as a Python library. The model can be run as a local server to generate audio via HTTP requests, keeping the model in memory between requests for faster performance. The model is not supported for GPU execution, as no speed improvements were observed compared to CPU execution due to the use of a batch size of 1 and a small model size. Contributions are welcome, and development instructions are available in the relevant documentation. The model must be used in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations, and its use must not involve illegal, harmful, deceptive, fraudulent, or unauthorized activities. Prohibited uses include voice impersonation or cloning without explicit and lawful consent, misinformation or disinformation, and the generation of unlawful, harmful, or privacy-invasive content.

instruction: What are the performance differences between using full attention and window attention?
description: STream3R presents a novel approach to 3D reconstruction by reformulating pointmap prediction as a decoder-only Transformer problem. It introduces a streaming framework that processes image sequences efficiently using causal attention, inspired by advances in modern language modeling. By learning geometric priors from large-scale 3D datasets, STream3R generalizes well to diverse and challenging scenarios, including dynamic scenes where traditional methods often fail. The model reformulates dense 3D reconstruction into a sequential registration task with causal attention. It supports multiple attention mechanisms, including FlashAttention, KV Cache, Causal Attention, Sliding Window Attention, and Full Attention. STream3R is inherently compatible with LLM-style training infrastructure, enabling efficient large-scale pretraining and fine-tuning for various downstream 3D tasks. Extensive experiments show that the method consistently outperforms prior work across both static and dynamic scene benchmarks. The model can be used in real-time or low-latency applications, such as real-time robotics, autonomous navigation, online 3D understanding, and SLAM. The model supports inference modes such as causal, window (with a default window size of 5), and full attention. A KV cache version is available to enable streaming input using StreamSession, which processes sequential input one by one. On the NRGBD dataset, STream3R achieves an accuracy mean of 0.057, an accuracy median of 0.014, a completeness mean of 0.028, a completeness median of 0.013, a non-completeness mean of 0.910, and a non-completeness median of 0.993, outperforming other methods. The peak GPU memory usage (VRAM) and runtime for the model are reported for different numbers of frames. For causal attention, VRAM usage increases with the number of frames, reaching 45.41 GB for 200 frames, while runtime increases to 1.1703 seconds. For sliding window attention, VRAM usage remains constant at 6.53 GB, and runtime stabilizes at around 0.1463 seconds for 200 frames. The model is trained on preprocessed datasets following standard configurations. Evaluation and training code are not yet released, and a metric-scale version is also pending. The project is licensed under an unspecified license, and redistribution and use should follow this license. If you use the code or paper, you are encouraged to cite the provided BibTeX entry.

instruction: Can you determine the jurisdiction mentioned in this Greek legal notice?
description: GEM-ELECTRA Legal is an improved ELECTRA-base model pre-trained from scratch on a 17GB corpus of Greek legal, parliamentary, and governmental text. This second version includes refined training hyperparameters for enhanced performance and stability. It is designed to understand the complex vocabulary and context of the legal domain in Greece and the European Union. The model was developed as part of a research project and optimized for downstream tasks such as Named Entity Recognition (NER), Text Classification, and Question Answering within the legal field. The ELECTRA architecture enables more efficient pre-training compared to masked language models like BERT by using a generator-discriminator approach. The training data was compiled from various legal and governmental sources, including the FEK - Greek Government Gazette (11.0 GB), Greek Parliament Proceedings (2.9 GB), Political Reports of the Supreme Court (1.2 GB), Eur-Lex (Greek Content, 0.92 GB), Europarl (Greek Content, 0.38 GB), and Raptarchis Legal Dictionary (0.35 GB), totaling approximately 16.75 GB. The text was tokenized using a custom ByteLevelBPE tokenizer trained from scratch on the Greek legal corpus, with an uncased vocabulary of 50,264 tokens. The data was processed into fixed-size chunks of 512 tokens, respecting document boundaries to ensure contextual coherence. The model was pre-trained for 200,000 steps on 8x NVIDIA A100 40GB GPUs using BFloat16 mixed-precision. Key hyperparameters included a learning rate of 1e-4 with a linear warmup of 12,000 steps, an effective batch size of 3,840, AdamW optimizer with beta1=0.9, beta2=0.98, and epsilon=1e-6, weight decay of 0.01, max sequence length of 512, and equal loss weights for the generator and discriminator of 50.0. The model achieved a final training loss of 0.0056 and a final evaluation loss of 0.0054.

instruction: I have a video and I want to track a specific object using a point I click on. How can I do that?
description: SAM 3 is a unified foundation model for promptable segmentation in images and videos. It can detect, segment, and track objects using text or visual prompts such as points, boxes, and masks. Compared to its predecessor, SAM 3 introduces the ability to exhaustively segment all instances of an open-vocabulary concept specified by a short text phrase or exemplars. Unlike prior work, SAM 3 can handle a vastly larger set of open-vocabulary prompts. It achieves 75-80% of human performance on a new benchmark that contains 270K unique concepts, over 50 times more than existing benchmarks. SAM 3 supports promptable concept segmentation (PCS) for images, taking text and/or image exemplars as prompts and returning segmentation masks for all matching object instances in the image. It also supports semantic segmentation alongside instance masks. For video, SAM 3 Video performs promptable concept segmentation (PCS) on videos, taking text as prompts and detecting and tracking all matching object instances across video frames. It supports both pre-loaded and streaming video inference. SAM 3 Tracker performs promptable visual segmentation (PVS) on images, taking interactive visual prompts (points, boxes, masks) to segment a specific object instance per prompt. It is an updated version of SAM2 that maintains the same API while providing improved performance. SAM 3 Tracker Video performs promptable visual segmentation (PVS) on videos, taking interactive visual prompts (points, boxes, masks) to track a specific object instance per prompt across video frames. It is an updated version of SAM2 Video that maintains the same API while providing improved performance. For streaming inference, it is noted that this mode disables hotstart heuristics that remove unmatched and duplicate objects, which may result in more false positive detections and duplicate object tracks compared to pre-loaded video inference. For best results, pre-loaded video inference is recommended when all frames are available.

instruction: Which of the following sentences is most similar in meaning to 'The software needs an update to fix the bug'?
description: This is a model finetuned from distilbert/distilbert-base-uncased. It maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for semantic textual similarity, semantic search, paraphrase mining, text classification, clustering, and more. The model is a Sentence Transformer with a maximum sequence length of 512 tokens and an output dimensionality of 768 dimensions. The similarity function used is Cosine Similarity. The full model architecture includes a Transformer with a DistilBertModel architecture and a Pooling layer with a word embedding dimension of 768. The pooling configuration includes pooling_mode_mean_tokens set to True and all other pooling modes set to False. The model was trained using Python 3.12.8, Sentence Transformers 5.1.0, Transformers 4.55.4, PyTorch 2.8.0, Accelerate 1.5.2, Datasets 3.5.0, and Tokenizers 0.21.1.

instruction: I need to match user queries with the most relevant FAQs—can you assist?
description: The all-MiniLM-L12-v2 is a model that maps sentences and paragraphs to a 384-dimensional dense vector space. It is designed for tasks such as clustering and semantic search. The model can be used with the Sentence-Transformers library by installing it via pip and encoding sentences using the SentenceTransformer class. Alternatively, it can be used with the HuggingFace Transformers library by applying a mean pooling operation on the contextualized word embeddings and normalizing the resulting sentence embeddings. The model was developed using a self-supervised contrastive learning objective, where it was fine-tuned on a dataset of over 1 billion sentence pairs. The model was trained on a TPU v3-8 for 100,000 steps with a batch size of 1024, a learning rate of 2e-5, and a sequence length of 128 tokens. The training data was a combination of multiple datasets, including citation pairs, duplicate question pairs, question-answer pairs, and image captions, among others. The model is intended to be used as a sentence and short paragraph encoder, outputting a vector that captures semantic information for tasks such as information retrieval, clustering, or sentence similarity. Input text longer than 256 word pieces is truncated by default.

instruction: What is the predicted label for the input (0.3, -0.5)?
description: This is a 2-qubit Quantum Neural Network (QNN) designed for binary classification tasks, specifically trained on the Two Moons dataset to demonstrate quantum machine learning on real quantum hardware. The model uses a hardware-efficient ansatz and has been tested on IBM Quantum's `ibm_fez` backend. It is a pure quantum model that uses quantum circuits for feature encoding and classification, optimized for NISQ-era quantum devices. The QNN has a total of 6 parameters, including 2 input parameters and 4 trainable parameters, with a circuit depth of 4 layers. The circuit includes 6 Ry gates and 1 CNOT gate, arranged in a linear entanglement topology. The model architecture consists of an encoding layer, two variational layers, and an entanglement layer, followed by a parity measurement for classification. The model was trained using the COBYLA optimizer and cross-entropy loss function on 8 training samples and tested on 4 test samples, achieving a train accuracy of approximately 87.5% and a test accuracy ranging from 0% to 75%. The low test accuracy is attributed to the small dataset size, quantum noise, and the limitations of NISQ devices. The model is intended as a proof-of-concept and educational tool, not for production use. It includes a parity measurement strategy to create a nonlinear decision boundary in feature space. The model is provided with pre-trained weights and a quantum circuit in Qiskit QPY format, along with helper functions for inference. It requires access to IBM Quantum hardware for execution and is distributed under the Apache License 2.0. The model has several limitations, including a small dataset, low capacity, binary classification only, and the need for quantum access. It is not suitable for multi-class problems or large-scale applications. The model is intended for educational, research, and development purposes, serving as a template for quantum machine learning projects and a benchmark for comparing quantum and classical models. Future improvements include increasing the dataset size, testing on multiple backends, and implementing error mitigation techniques.

instruction: I need a written version of this audio file for a presentation. Can you help?
description: Whisper is a pre-trained model for automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech translation. Trained on 680,000 hours of labelled speech data using large-scale weak supervision, Whisper models demonstrate strong generalization capabilities across many datasets and domains without the need for fine-tuning. Whisper is a Transformer-based encoder-decoder model, also referred to as a sequence-to-sequence model. The models were trained on either English-only data or multilingual data. English-only models were trained for speech recognition, while multilingual models were trained for both speech recognition and speech translation. For speech recognition, the model predicts transcriptions in the same language as the audio. For speech translation, the model predicts transcriptions in a different language. Whisper checkpoints come in five configurations of varying model sizes. The smallest four are trained on either English-only or multilingual data, while the largest checkpoints are multilingual only. The model is used in conjunction with a WhisperProcessor to pre-process audio inputs into log-Mel spectrograms and post-process model outputs into text. The model is informed of the task to perform (transcription or translation) by passing context tokens to the decoder. These context tokens include a transcription token, a language token, a task token, and optionally a timestamp prediction token. The task token can be set to either transcribe or translate. If the tokens are forced, the model is made to predict each token at each position, allowing control over the output language and task. If un-forced, the model predicts the output language and task automatically. Whisper can be used for English to English transcription, French to French transcription, and French to English translation by setting the appropriate context tokens. The model is evaluated using datasets such as LibriSpeech, and performance is measured using metrics like word error rate (WER). Whisper Tiny, for example, achieves a WER of approximately 7.55% on the LibriSpeech test clean dataset. The model is designed to work on audio samples up to 30 seconds in duration, but can be extended to handle longer audio through a chunking algorithm. Whisper can also predict sequence-level timestamps by enabling the return_timestamps option. While the pre-trained model generalizes well, its performance can be improved through fine-tuning, especially for certain languages and tasks. The models are primarily trained and evaluated on ASR and speech translation to English tasks and show strong results in approximately 10 languages. They may exhibit additional capabilities if fine-tuned for tasks like voice activity detection, speaker classification, or speaker diarization, but have not been robustly evaluated in these areas. Users are advised to perform thorough evaluations before deployment. The models are trained on 680,000 hours of audio data collected from the internet, with 65% in English, 18% in non-English audio with English transcripts, and 17% in non-English audio with corresponding non-English transcripts. The non-English data represents 98 different languages. Performance in a given language is correlated with the amount of training data available for that language. Whisper models exhibit improved robustness to accents, background noise, technical language, and zero-shot translation from multiple languages into English. However, due to training on noisy, weakly supervised data, the models may generate hallucinations—texts that are not actually spoken in the audio. Performance is uneven across languages, with lower accuracy observed in low-resource or low-discoverability languages and across different accents and dialects. The models may also exhibit higher word error rates for speakers of different genders, races, or ages. The sequence-to-sequence architecture makes the model prone to generating repetitive texts, which can be partially mitigated by beam search and temperature scheduling. The models are not recommended for use in high-risk domains or for subjective classification tasks. The broader implications of Whisper include potential use in accessibility tools and real-time speech recognition applications. However, the release of Whisper also raises concerns about dual use, including the potential for surveillance technologies. The models may have some capabilities to recognize specific individuals, which could present safety concerns. The cost of transcription is not expected to be a limiting factor in scaling up surveillance projects.

instruction: How is the urban area segmented in this image—roads, buildings, and open spaces?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of facebook/mask2former-swin-base-ade-semantic for semantic segmentation of satellite and aerial imagery. It uses the Mask2Former architecture with a Swin Transformer backbone and is designed for semantic segmentation, specifically land cover classification. The model classifies pixels into nine land cover categories: Background, Bareland, Grass, Pavement, Road, Tree, Water, Cropland, and Building. It was trained for 17 epochs and achieved a best mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) of 0.5202 and a validation loss of 44.21. The model is intended for land cover classification from satellite and aerial imagery, with applications in urban planning, environmental monitoring, geographic information systems (GIS), and remote sensing research. However, it has several limitations: it exhibits geographic bias due to being primarily trained on imagery from specific regions in the OpenEarthMap dataset, performs best on imagery with resolution similar to the training data, may require fine-tuning for different satellite sensors or aerial platforms, and its performance may vary with seasonal or weather conditions.

instruction: How can I generate a scene with a specific mood, like a mysterious forest at night with fog and low light?
description: OmniX is a family of flow matching generative models designed for unified panoramic perception, generation, and completion. It enables the creation of graphics-ready 3D scenes suitable for physically based rendering, relighting, and simulation. OmniX repurposes 2D generative models for panoramic perception of geometry, textures, and PBR materials, offering a versatile and unified framework. It is based on a lightweight and efficient cross-modal adapter structure that reuses 2D generative priors for a broad range of panoramic vision tasks. OmniX supports tasks such as panoramic image generation from text or image prompts, panoramic perception of geometric, intrinsic, and semantic properties, and the combination of generation and perception to produce panoramic images with multiple property annotations. The model is evaluated using a large-scale synthetic panorama dataset containing high-quality multimodal panoramas from diverse indoor and outdoor scenes. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of OmniX in panoramic visual perception and graphics-ready 3D scene generation. The model is still under development for certain functionalities, such as graphics-ready scene reconstruction and generation, which are noted as being in progress.

instruction: I want to extract the part of a document where the methodology for the study is described. Can you assist?
description: ColQwen2.5 is a model based on a novel architecture and training strategy using Vision Language Models (VLMs) to efficiently index documents from their visual features. It generates multi-vector representations of text and images. The model takes dynamic image resolutions as input without resizing, which may change their aspect ratio, similar to ColPali. The maximum resolution is set to create at most 768 image patches, with experiments showing improvements with more patches at the cost of increased memory requirements. This version is trained with colpali-engine version 0.3.7 and uses the same data as the ColPali dataset described in the paper. The training dataset consists of 127,460 query-page pairs, including 63% from openly available academic datasets and 37% from a synthetic dataset made of web-crawled PDF pages augmented with VLM-generated pseudo-questions. The training set is entirely in English, allowing for the study of zero-shot generalization to non-English languages. No multi-page PDF document is used in both the training and evaluation sets to prevent contamination. A validation set of 2% of the samples is used for hyperparameter tuning. Multilingual data may be present in the pretraining corpus of the language model and in the multimodal training. All models are trained for one epoch on the training set using bfloat16 format, low-rank adapters with alpha=32 and r=32 on the transformer layers from the language model and the final projection layer, and the paged_adamw_8bit optimizer. Training is conducted on an 8 GPU setup with data parallelism, a learning rate of 5e-5 with linear decay and 2.5% warmup steps, and a batch size of 32. The model is intended for use with colpali-engine version 0.3.1 or higher and transformers version greater than 4.45.0. The model primarily focuses on PDF-type documents and high-resource languages, which may limit its generalization to other document types or less represented languages. It relies on multi-vector retrieval derived from the ColBERT late interaction mechanism, which may require engineering efforts to adapt to widely used vector retrieval frameworks that lack native multi-vector support. The vision language backbone model (Qwen2.5-VL) is licensed under the Qwen RESEARCH LICENSE AGREEMENT, while the adapters are under the MIT license.

instruction: I want to identify and segment all the cars in this street scene—can you do that?
description: SAM 3 is a unified foundation model for promptable segmentation in images and videos. It can detect, segment, and track objects using text or visual prompts such as points, boxes, and masks. Compared to its predecessor, SAM 3 introduces the ability to exhaustively segment all instances of an open-vocabulary concept specified by a short text phrase or exemplars. Unlike prior work, SAM 3 can handle a vastly larger set of open-vocabulary prompts. It achieves 75-80% of human performance on a new benchmark that contains 270K unique concepts, over 50 times more than existing benchmarks. SAM 3 supports promptable concept segmentation (PCS) for images and videos, as well as promptable visual segmentation (PVS) for images and videos. For image PCS, the model takes text and/or image exemplars as prompts and returns segmentation masks for all matching object instances in the image. It also provides semantic segmentation alongside instance masks. For video PCS, the model detects and tracks all matching object instances across video frames using text prompts. It supports both pre-loaded and streaming video inference, with the latter being suitable for real-time applications. However, streaming inference may result in more false positive detections and duplicate object tracks compared to pre-loaded inference. For image PVS, the model takes interactive visual prompts (points, boxes, masks) to segment a specific object instance per prompt. It is an updated version of SAM2 that maintains the same API while providing improved performance. For video PVS, the model tracks a specific object instance per prompt across video frames using interactive visual prompts. It is an updated version of SAM2 Video that maintains the same API while providing improved performance. The model supports batched inference with text prompts and mixed prompts for different images in the same batch. It also supports multi-object video tracking, allowing multiple objects to be tracked simultaneously across video frames. The model can be used with the 🤗 Transformers library and provides various helper functions for post-processing results, such as overlaying masks on images.

instruction: This email has a lot of formatting and emojis. Can you still summarize the core message clearly?
description: This is a fine-tuned T5-small model specialized for email summarization. The model can generate both brief (one-line) and detailed (comprehensive) summaries of emails, and is robust to messy, informal inputs with typos and abbreviations. The base model is T5-small, and it was fine-tuned by the Wordcab Team. It is a T5 (Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer) model designed for English language processing and is released under the Apache 2.0 License. The model supports dual-mode summarization through the use of `summarize_brief:` and `summarize_full:` prefixes. It is optimized for fast inference and real-world email processing. The intended uses include summarizing professional and casual emails, email inbox management and prioritization, meeting invitation processing, customer support email triage, and newsletter summarization. Limitations include a maximum input length of 512 tokens (~2500 characters), support for English language only, potential difficulty in capturing nuanced context in very long email threads, and unsuitability for legal or medical document summarization. The model was fine-tuned on a dataset containing 364,000 email-summary pairs, including professional business emails, casual informal communications, meeting invitations and updates, technical discussions, and customer service interactions. The training data was augmented with subtle typos and misspellings in 50% of examples, common abbreviations, and various formatting styles. Training was conducted for one epoch with a batch size of 64 and a learning rate of 3e-4, using a validation split of 1%. The model was trained on an NVIDIA GPU with mixed precision (fp16) using the HuggingFace Transformers 4.36.0 framework. Emails were formatted as `Subject: [subject]. Body: [content]`, and two training examples were created per email (brief and full summaries). Data augmentation was applied to 50% of examples. The model achieved a ROUGE-L score of 0.42, an average inference time of 0.63 seconds for brief summaries and 0.71 seconds for full summaries on a T4 GPU, and a coherence score of 80% on messy inputs. It successfully differentiates between brief and full summaries, with a 2.5x length difference, and is robust in handling informal text and typos. The model is based on the T5 architecture, which includes an encoder and decoder with multiple T5 blocks, embedding layers, and a linear language model head. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 License and is based on the T5 model. It was fine-tuned on a dataset and trained using HuggingFace Transformers. Special thanks are given to the open-source community.

instruction: Is it possible to get better audio quality from a video with poor lighting conditions?
description: Dolphin is an efficient audio-visual speech separation model that extracts target speech from noisy environments by combining acoustic and visual (lip movement) cues. It achieves state-of-the-art performance while being 6× faster and using 50% fewer parameters than previous methods. The model is designed to separate speech using both audio and video inputs, where the video input consists of grayscale lip region frames. It supports tasks such as hearing aid enhancement, video conferencing noise suppression, in-car speech extraction, AR/VR communication, and edge device deployment. The model architecture includes a DP-LipCoder video encoder, an audio encoder, a global-local attention separator, and an audio decoder. The DP-LipCoder uses a dual-path architecture with visual compression and semantic encoding, vector quantization for discrete lip semantic tokens, and knowledge distillation from AV-HuBERT, with only 8.5M parameters. The audio encoder extracts multi-scale acoustic features, and the global-local attention separator uses a single-pass TDANet-based architecture with global attention for long-range dependencies and local attention for noise suppression. The model is trained on the VoxCeleb2 dataset with 2-speaker mixtures at 0dB SNR, using a SI-SNR loss function. On the VoxCeleb2 benchmark, it achieves a SI-SNRi of 16.1 dB, SDRi of 16.3 dB, PESQ of 3.45, ESTOI of 0.93, with 51.3M parameters, 417G MACs, and an inference speed of 0.015s per 4s-clip. The model requires frontal or near-frontal face views for optimal performance, works best with 25fps video input, and is trained on English speech, which may require fine-tuning for other languages. Performance degrades with severe occlusions or low lighting.

instruction: I have a Telugu poem and a reference voice—can you create a spoken version that follows the same rhythm and tone?
description: IndicF5 is a high-quality, near-human polyglot Text-to-Speech (TTS) model trained on 1417 hours of high-quality speech data. It supports 11 Indian languages: Assamese, Bengali, Gujarati, Hindi, Kannada, Malayalam, Marathi, Odia, Punjabi, Tamil, and Telugu. The model is designed to synthesize speech based on three inputs: the text to be spoken, a reference prompt audio that guides the model's prosody and speaker characteristics, and the transcript of the reference prompt audio. IndicF5 is intended for use with explicit permission for voice cloning, and unauthorized use is strictly prohibited. Users are responsible for any misuse of the model. The model is based on prior work by other researchers in the field of text-to-speech synthesis. If used in research or projects, it should be cited appropriately.

instruction: Could you summarize the visual content of this image in a structured format?
description: This is a DINOv3 ViT model image feature encoder, distilled on the LVD-1689M dataset from the DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model is designed to extract image features and supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. It is based on a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture with a base configuration and a patch size of 16. The model has 85.6 million parameters, 23.6 GMACs, and 34.1 million activations, and processes images of size 256 x 256. The original model weights had all QKV projection biases set to zero, and for the `timm` implementation, the QKV bias is disabled (`qkv_bias=False`) for these models. Some model sizes have variants with `qkvb` in the name that have the bias enabled (`qkv_bias=True`), but set to zero, to match the behavior of `transformers` and original models. The original models keep RoPE periods as a persistent `bfloat16` buffer, while `timm` generates `float32` periods at initialization, which may result in some numerical differences but appears to work as well or slightly better for fine-tuning. The model is evaluated on various benchmarks, including IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair, with performance metrics such as accuracy and mean intersection over union (mIoU). For ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on web data (LVD-1689M), the model achieves 89.3% accuracy on IN-ReaL, 76.7% on IN-R, 64.1% on Obj.Net, 58.5% on Ox.-H, 51.8% on ADE20k, 0.373 on NYU, 77.2% on DAVIS, 58.8% on NAVI, and 57.2% on SPair. For ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on satellite data (SAT-493M), the model achieves 73.0% mean accuracy on m-BEnet, 96.5% on m-brick-kiln, 94.1% on m-eurosat, 60.6% on m-forestnet, 96.0% on m-pv4ger, 57.4% on m-so2sat, and 79.6% mean accuracy. For segmentation tasks on satellite data, the model achieves 94.2% mean accuracy on m-cashew, 75.6% on m-chesapeake, 61.8% on m-NeonTree, 83.7% on m-nz-cattle, 95.2% on m-pv4ger-seg, 36.8% on m-SA-crop, and 74.5% mean accuracy. The model is associated with the DINOv3 paper, the paper on Vision Transformers for image recognition at scale, and the PyTorch Image Models repository. The model is available through the `timm` library and can be used for image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. For image classification, the model can be used with the `timm.create_model` function and the `eval` method, along with model-specific transforms. For feature map extraction, the model can be used with the `features_only=True` argument. For image embeddings, the model can be used with the `num_classes=0` argument to remove the classifier, or with the `forward_features` and `forward_head` methods to obtain the embeddings. The model has some caveats, including the handling of QKV biases and RoPE periods, which may affect numerical stability and compatibility with certain hardware.

instruction: What are the most important visual elements in this short-form video?
description: Kwai Keye-VL is an 8-billion-parameter multimodal foundation model designed for leading-edge performance in short-video understanding while maintaining robust general-purpose vision-language abilities. It is engineered to bridge the gap between static image comprehension and dynamic, information-dense short-form videos, which are a dominant medium in today's digital landscape. The model is built on a massive, high-quality dataset exceeding 600 billion tokens with a strong emphasis on video, and an innovative training recipe. This recipe includes a four-stage pre-training process for solid vision-language alignment, followed by a meticulous two-phase post-training process. The first post-training stage enhances foundational capabilities like instruction following, while the second phase focuses on stimulating advanced reasoning. A key innovation in the second phase is the five-mode ``cold-start'' data mixture, which includes ``thinking'', ``non-thinking'', ``auto-think'', ``think with image'', and high-quality video data. This mixture teaches the model to decide when and how to reason. Subsequent reinforcement learning (RL) and alignment steps further enhance these reasoning capabilities and correct abnormal model behaviors, such as repetitive outputs. Kwai Keye-VL achieves state-of-the-art results on public video benchmarks and remains highly competitive on general image-based tasks. Additionally, it shows a significant advantage on the newly developed KC-MMBench, a benchmark tailored for real-world short-video scenarios. The model architecture is based on the Qwen3-8B language model and incorporates a vision encoder initialized from the open-source SigLIP. It supports native dynamic resolution, preserving the original aspect ratio of images by dividing each into a 14x14 patch sequence. A simple MLP layer then maps and merges the visual tokens. The model uses 3D RoPE for unified processing of text, image, and video information, establishing a one-to-one correspondence between position encoding and absolute time to ensure precise perception of temporal changes in video information. The pre-training pipeline features a four-stage progressive strategy: Image-Text Matching, ViT-LLM Alignment, Multi-task Pre-training, and Annealing with model merging. The pre-training data is diverse, including image-text pairs, videos, pure text, and tasks such as fine-grained description, OCR, Q&A, and localization. The data is filtered using CLIP scores and VLM discriminators, and MinHASH is used for deduplication to prevent data leakage. High-quality internal datasets are specifically constructed, especially for detailed captions and Chinese OCR, to compensate for the shortcomings of open-source data. The training process includes four stages: Visual Pre-training, Cross-Modal Alignment, Multi-Task Pre-training, and Annealing Training. The post-training phase is designed into two phases with five stages, aiming to comprehensively enhance the model's performance, especially its reasoning ability in complex tasks. The first phase focuses on the model's basic performance and stability in non-reasoning scenarios, including Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Mixed Preference Optimization (MPO). The second phase is the highlight and major contribution of the training process, introducing a mix-mode Chain of Thought (CoT) and multi-thinking mode reinforcement learning (RL) mechanisms to significantly enhance the model's multimodal perception, reasoning, and think-with-image capabilities. The model is evaluated on a comprehensive suite of authoritative public video benchmarks, including Video-MME, Video-MMMU, TempCompass, LongVideoBench, and MMVU, where it significantly surpasses other top-tier models of a comparable size. It also demonstrates strong performance in evaluation sets that require complex logical reasoning and mathematical problem-solving, such as WeMath, MathVerse, and LogicVista, highlighting its advanced capacity for logical deduction and solving complex quantitative problems.

instruction: Identify the types of cells involved in the metastasis process described in this text.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for high-precision biomedical entity recognition in the domain of cancer genetics. It is optimized for identifying and extracting cancer-related genetic entities from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports a wide range of biomedical entity types, including but not limited to amino acids, anatomical systems, cancers, cells, cellular components, developing anatomical structures, genes or gene products, immaterial anatomical entities, multi-tissue structures, organs, organisms, organism subdivisions, organism substances, pathological formations, simple chemicals, and tissues. Both B- and I- tags are supported for these entity types. The model is trained on the BIONLP2013_CG dataset, a specialized corpus for cancer genetics entities and gene regulation in oncology research. It is based on the xlm-roberta-base architecture and is implemented using the Hugging Face Transformers framework. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.79, precision of 0.79, recall of 0.80, and accuracy of 0.89. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is production-ready and validated on clinical benchmarks, and it is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and academic settings. If used in research, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: How do I rank these product descriptions to highlight the best matches for a customer's query?
description: The Qwen3-Reranker-4B is a text reranking model designed for text retrieval tasks. It is part of the Qwen3 Embedding model series, which is specifically developed for text embedding and ranking. The model is based on the Qwen3 series and provides a comprehensive range of text embeddings and reranking models in various sizes (0.6B, 4B, and 8B). It inherits the multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills of the foundational Qwen3 model. The Qwen3 Embedding series has achieved state-of-the-art performance in multiple text embedding and ranking tasks, including text retrieval, code retrieval, text classification, text clustering, and bitext mining. The 8B size embedding model ranks No.1 in the MTEB multilingual leaderboard (as of June 5, 2025, score 70.58), while the reranking model excels in various text retrieval scenarios. The Qwen3 Embedding series offers a full spectrum of sizes (from 0.6B to 8B) for both embedding and reranking models, catering to diverse use cases that prioritize efficiency and effectiveness. Developers can combine these modules seamlessly. The embedding model allows for flexible vector definitions across all dimensions, and both embedding and reranking models support user-defined instructions to enhance performance for specific tasks, languages, or scenarios. The Qwen3-Reranker-4B has 4B parameters, a context length of 32k, and supports over 100 languages. Evaluation results show that the Qwen3-Reranker-4B achieves a score of 69.76 on MTEB-R, 75.94 on CMTEB-R, 72.74 on MMTEB-R, 69.97 on MLDR, 81.20 on MTEB-Code, and 14.84 on FollowIR. The model supports instruction-aware reranking, and using instructions typically improves performance by 1% to 5% compared to not using them. Developers are advised to create tailored instructions specific to their tasks and scenarios. In multilingual contexts, instructions should be written in English, as most training instructions were originally in English.

instruction: I have a collection of academic papers; how can I rank them by relevance to a particular research question?
description: The Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B is a text reranking model part of the Qwen3 Embedding series, designed for text embedding and ranking tasks. It is a modified version of a foundational model, adapted as a sequence classification model. The Qwen3 Embedding series builds upon the dense foundational models of the Qwen3 series, offering a comprehensive range of text embeddings and reranking models in various sizes (0.6B, 4B, and 8B). This series inherits the multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills of its foundational model. The Qwen3 Embedding series has achieved state-of-the-art performance in multiple text embedding and ranking tasks, including text retrieval, code retrieval, text classification, text clustering, and bitext mining. The 8B size embedding model ranks No.1 in the MTEB multilingual leaderboard (as of June 5, 2025, score 70.58), while the reranking model excels in various text retrieval scenarios. The Qwen3 Embedding series offers a full spectrum of sizes (from 0.6B to 8B) for both embedding and reranking models, catering to diverse use cases that prioritize efficiency and effectiveness. Developers can combine these two modules seamlessly. The embedding model allows for flexible vector definitions across all dimensions, and both embedding and reranking models support user-defined instructions to enhance performance for specific tasks, languages, or scenarios. The Qwen3 Embedding series supports over 100 languages, including various programming languages, and provides robust multilingual, cross-lingual, and code retrieval capabilities. The Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B has 0.6B parameters, a context length of 32k, and supports 100+ languages. Evaluation results show that the Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B achieves a score of 65.80 on MTEB-R, 71.31 on CMTEB-R, 66.36 on MMTEB-R, 67.28 on MLDR, 73.42 on MTEB-Code, and 5.41 on FollowIR. Developers are advised to customize the input instructions according to their specific tasks and scenarios, as using instructions typically yields an improvement of 1% to 5% in performance. In multilingual contexts, it is recommended to write instructions in English, as most training instructions were originally in English.

instruction: Can you identify and count the number of cars passing through this intersection?
description: YOLOv13 is a real-time object detection model designed to improve detection performance in complex scenarios by capturing global multi-to-multi high-order correlations. It introduces a Hypergraph-based Adaptive Correlation Enhancement (HyperACE) mechanism that adaptively exploits latent high-order correlations, overcoming the limitations of previous methods that were restricted to pairwise correlation modeling. YOLOv13 also features a Full-Pipeline Aggregation-and-Distribution (FullPAD) paradigm, which distributes correlation-enhanced features across the entire network to achieve fine-grained information flow and representation synergy. Additionally, the model uses depthwise separable convolutions to replace large-kernel convolutions, significantly reducing parameters and computational complexity without sacrificing performance. YOLOv13 includes four variants: Nano, Small, Large, and X-Large. Experimental results on the MS COCO benchmark show that YOLOv13-N improves mAP by 3.0% over YOLO11-N and by 1.5% over YOLOv12-N. The model is available in multiple formats and supports training, validation, prediction, and export to ONNX or TensorRT. The code and models are open-sourced and can be accessed at the provided URL.

instruction: Can you create a 10-second audio of someone laughing naturally while sharing a funny memory?
description: Higgs Audio v2 is a powerful audio foundation model pretrained on over 10 million hours of audio data and a diverse set of text data. It is designed for expressive audio generation and excels in this area despite having no post-training or fine-tuning. The model demonstrates strong performance in expressive audio generation due to its deep language and acoustic understanding. On a specific evaluation, it achieves win rates of 75.7% and 55.7% over 'gpt-4o-mini-tts' on the 'Emotions' and 'Questions' categories, respectively. It also obtains state-of-the-art performance on traditional TTS benchmarks like Seed-TTS Eval and Emotional Speech Dataset (ESD). The model demonstrates capabilities such as automatic prosody adaptation during narration, zero-shot generation of natural multi-speaker dialogues in multiple languages, melodic humming with the cloned voice, and simultaneous generation of speech and background music. Higgs Audio v2 adopts a 'generation variant' architecture. Its strong performance is driven by three key technical innovations: an automated annotation pipeline that leverages multiple ASR models, sound event classification models, and an in-house audio understanding model, which was finetuned on top of Higgs Audio v1 Understanding; a unified audio tokenizer trained from scratch that captures both semantic and acoustic features; and the DualFFN architecture, which enhances the LLM’s ability to model audio tokens with minimal computational overhead. The audio tokenizer runs at 25 frames per second while maintaining or improving audio quality compared to tokenizers with twice the bitrate. It is the first to train on 24 kHz data covering speech, music, and sound events in one unified system. It uses a simple non-diffusion encoder/decoder for fast, batch inference and achieves state-of-the-art performance in semantic and acoustic evaluations. The DualFFN architecture acts as an audio-specific expert, boosting the LLM's performance with minimal computational overhead. The implementation preserves 91% of the original LLM’s training speed with the inclusion of DualFFN, which has 2.2B parameters. The total number of parameters for Higgs Audio v2 is 3.6B (LLM) + 2.2B (Audio Dual FFN), and it has the same training / inference FLOPs as Llama-3.2-3B. Ablation studies show that the model equipped with DualFFN consistently outperforms its counterpart in terms of word error rate (WER) and speaker similarity. Higgs Audio v2 is evaluated on four benchmarks: Seed-TTS Eval, ESD, EmergentTTS-Eval, and a custom Multi-speaker Eval. On Seed-TTS Eval and ESD, it achieves a WER of 2.44 and a SIM of 67.70 on SeedTTS-Eval, and a WER of 1.78 and a SIM (emo2vec) of 86.13 on ESD. On the EmergentTTS-Eval, it achieves win rates of 75.71% and 55.71% on the 'Emotions' and 'Questions' categories, respectively. On the Multi-speaker Eval, it achieves a WER of 18.88 and a mean Sim & Dis-sim of 51.95 on the 'two-speaker-conversation' subset, a WER of 11.89 and a mean Sim & Dis-sim of 67.92 on the 'small talk (ref)' subset, and a WER of 14.65 and a mean Sim & Dis-sim of 55.28 on the 'small talk (no ref)' subset. The model is open-sourced and can be used for tasks such as text-to-speech generation, multi-speaker dialogue generation, and audio generation with specific emotional or prosodic characteristics. It is available through an open-source repository and can be installed and run using provided code snippets. The model is licensed under a specific license, which can be found in the repository.

instruction: How would you describe the visual structure of this image using extracted features?
description: This is a DINOv3 Vision Transformer (ViT) model image feature encoder, distilled on the LVD-1689M dataset from the DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model is designed for image feature extraction and supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. It is based on a Vision Transformer architecture with a patch size of 16x16. The model has 303.1 million parameters, 82.4 GMACs, and 90.6 million activations, and processes images of size 256 x 256. The original model weights had all QKV projection biases set to zero, and for the `timm` implementation, the QKV bias is disabled (`qkv_bias=False`) for most models, except for variants with `qkvb` in the name, which have the bias enabled but set to zero. The original models store RoPE periods as a persistent `bfloat16` buffer, while `timm` generates `float32` periods at initialization, which may result in some numerical differences but is less problematic on devices without bfloat16 support and appears to work as well or slightly better for fine-tuning. The model is evaluated on various benchmarks, including IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair, with performance metrics such as 90.2% on IN-ReaL and 0.352 on NYU for the ViT-L/16 variant. It is also evaluated on satellite-related tasks such as classification and segmentation, with performance metrics such as 79.6% mean accuracy on GEO-Bench classification and 74.5% mean accuracy on GEO-Bench segmentation for the ViT-L/16 variant. The model is associated with the DINOv3 paper and the Vision Transformer paper, and is implemented using the PyTorch Image Models library.

instruction: Write a short story about a robot that discovers the meaning of creativity.
description: The gpt-oss series is designed for powerful reasoning, agentic tasks, and versatile developer use cases. Two models are available: gpt-oss-120b, intended for production, general-purpose, and high reasoning use cases that fit into a single 80GB GPU (like NVIDIA H100 or AMD MI300X), with 117B parameters and 5.1B active parameters; and gpt-oss-20b, intended for lower latency, local, or specialized use cases, with 21B parameters and 3.6B active parameters. Both models were trained on OpenAI's data and require the harmony format to function correctly. The gpt-oss-120b model is covered by this model card. The models are released under a permissive Apache 2.0 license, allowing for free use without copyleft restrictions or patent risk, making them suitable for experimentation, customization, and commercial deployment. Users can adjust the reasoning effort (low, medium, high) based on their specific use case and latency needs. The models provide full access to the reasoning process, which is useful for debugging and increasing trust in outputs, though it is not intended for end users. They are fine-tunable for specific use cases through parameter fine-tuning and support agentic capabilities such as function calling and structured outputs. The models were post-trained with MXFP4 quantization of the MoE weights, enabling gpt-oss-120b to run on a single 80GB GPU and gpt-oss-20b to run within 16GB of memory. All evaluations were performed using the same MXFP4 quantization. The models can be used with Transformers, vLLM, PyTorch / Triton, Ollama, and LM Studio. They can be downloaded directly from Hugging Face CLI. The larger model, gpt-oss-120b, can be fine-tuned on a single H100 node, while the smaller model can be fine-tuned on consumer hardware. The models are suitable for web browsing, function calling with defined schemas, and agentic operations like browser tasks.

instruction: What are the distinguishing features of this skin lesion in the image?
description: DermLIP is a vision-language model for dermatology, trained on the Derm1M dataset, which is the largest dermatological image-text corpus to date. The model is a pretrained vision-language model with a CLIP-style architecture. It includes a ViT-B16 vision encoder and a GPT2 text encoder, operating on images with a resolution of 224×224 pixels. The model was trained on 403,563 skin image-text pairs from the Derm1M dataset, which includes both dermoscopic and clinical images. The training objective was based on image-text contrastive loss, and the model was trained using an Nvidia H200 GPU with approximately 40GB memory usage, requiring about 5 hours of training time. DermLIP is intended for use in zero-shot classification, few-shot learning, cross-modal retrieval, and concept annotation or explanation. The model is licensed under the cc-by-nc-nd-4.0 license.

instruction: Can you help me blend two images together to create a composite picture?
description: Qwen-Image-Edit-2511-Lightning is a collection of optimized models designed for image editing tasks, utilizing step distillation and quantization techniques to achieve high-efficiency inference performance. The model suite includes three core model files, each with distinct characteristics. One model, named Qwen-Image-Edit-2511-Lightning-4steps-V1.0-bf16.safetensors, is a 4-step distilled LoRA model with BF16 precision, offering a lightweight and fast inference option. Another model, Qwen-Image-Edit-2511-Lightning-4steps-V1.0-fp32.safetensors, is also a 4-step distilled LoRA model but with FP32 precision, providing high accuracy. The third model, qwen_image_edit_2511_fp8_e4m3fn_scaled_lightning.safetensors, is an FP8 quantized model with e4m3fn scaled precision, fused with a 4-step distilled LoRA and optimized for low-memory deployment. The models support two mainstream usage frameworks: the Qwen-Image-Lightning framework and the LightX2V lightweight video/image generation inference framework. The LoRA models are optimized through step distillation, reducing the original inference steps to just 4 steps, which results in a speedup of approximately 10 times faster than standard 40-step inference while maintaining image editing quality. Additionally, the FP8 quantized model balances performance and resource efficiency, reducing GPU memory usage by about 50% compared to FP32 while preserving editing fidelity. For technical support, users can open issues in the respective repositories for Qwen framework-specific questions or LightX2V integration questions.

instruction: This high-resolution image contains a complex scene. Could you break it down into its main components and describe them?
description: This is an updated version of a previous model, offering improved abilities in multimodal reasoning, visual perception, video understanding, and high-resolution image processing. The 2506 version achieves better accuracy on multimodal reasoning benchmarks, including 56.9 on MathVision (+20.1), 80.1 on MathVista (+8.4), 46.3 on MMMU-Pro (+3.3), and 64.0 on MMMU (+2.1), while requiring 20% fewer tokens for thinking. It also demonstrates strong performance on general visual perception and understanding tasks, such as MMBench-EN-v1.1 (84.4), MMStar (70.4), RealWorldQA (70.0), and MMVet (78.4), matching or surpassing the abilities of non-thinking models. The model extends to video scenarios, achieving state-of-the-art results on VideoMMMU (65.2) and maintaining strong general video understanding on Video-MME (71.9). It supports 3.2 million total pixels in a single image, four times the resolution of the previous version, leading to improvements on high-resolution perception and OS-agent grounding benchmarks such as V* Benchmark (83.2), ScreenSpot-Pro (52.8), and OSWorld-G (52.5). The model is designed for long-decode inference, generating up to 32K tokens, and is recommended to be used with VLLM for efficient performance. It is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers library and requires specific versions of Python, PyTorch, and Transformers for optimal use. The model is evaluated on a wide range of benchmarks, including MMLU (82.0), MMLU-Pro (68.5), MATH (91.8), and GPQA-Diamond (42.3), and is compared with other large open-source models such as Qwen2.5-VL-32B, Qwen2.5-VL-72B, and Gemma3-27B-IT. The model is available for use with specific installation instructions and inference code examples provided for both VLLM and Hugging Face Transformers. The model is described in a technical report and is available for citation through the arXiv repository.

instruction: I have a video that looks a bit washed out. Can you improve the colors and sharpness?
description: SeedVR2 is a one-step diffusion-based video restoration model designed to perform adversarial video restoration training against real data. It aims to address the challenges of high-resolution video restoration in real-world settings, where existing distillation-based approaches for one-step image restoration have not been effectively extended. SeedVR2 introduces several architectural and training enhancements to handle high-resolution video restoration within a single step. These include an adaptive window attention mechanism, where the window size is dynamically adjusted to fit the output resolution, preventing window inconsistency observed in high-resolution video restoration using fixed window sizes. Additionally, a series of losses are verified for their effectiveness in stabilizing and improving adversarial post-training, including a proposed feature matching loss that does not significantly sacrifice training efficiency. Extensive experiments show that SeedVR2 can achieve comparable or better performance than existing video restoration approaches in a single step. However, the model has some limitations: it may not be perfectly aligned with the paper due to being a prototype, and it is not always robust to heavy degradations or very large motions. It shares some failure cases with existing methods, such as failing to fully remove degradation or generating unpleasing details. Moreover, due to its strong generation ability, it may occasionally oversharpen results on inputs with very light degradations, such as 720p AIGC videos.

instruction: Could you describe the scene in the area where the two objects are closest together?
description: The VLM-FO1_Qwen2.5-VL-3B-v01 model is an implementation of the VLM-FO1 framework built on the Qwen2.5-VL-3B base model. VLM-FO1 is a novel plug-and-play framework designed to bridge the gap between the high-level reasoning of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and the need for fine-grained visual perception. It endows pre-trained VLMs with superior fine-grained perception without compromising their inherent high-level reasoning and general understanding capabilities. The framework operates as a plug-and-play module that can be integrated with any existing VLM, preserving its original weights and capabilities. VLM-FO1 excels at a wide range of fine-grained perception tasks, including Object Grounding, Region Generative Understanding, and Visual Region Reasoning. The framework is designed as a set of enhancement modules that can be seamlessly integrated with any pre-trained VLM. A novel Dual-Vision Encoder architecture, called the Hybrid Region Encoder (HFRE), fuses semantic-rich features with perception-enhanced features, creating powerful region tokens that capture both high-level meaning and fine-grained spatial detail. VLM-FO1 achieves state-of-the-art results across a diverse suite of benchmarks. A two-stage training strategy ensures that fine-grained perception is gained without causing catastrophic forgetting of the base model's powerful general visual understanding abilities.

instruction: What are some effective ways to reduce stress in daily life?
description: Next-1B is a 1-billion parameter causal language model based on Gemma 3, designed for efficiency, low-resource deployment, and reasoning-focused natural language understanding. It is optimized for text reasoning, summarization, and creative generation. The model supports Turkish natively while remaining multilingual. It is open-source and transparent for research and applications. Next-1B is ideal for developers, students, and organizations needing fast, reliable, and low-resource text generation. The model features a lightweight architecture optimized for low VRAM usage, making it suitable for small GPUs or CPU deployment. It is capable of logical chain-of-thought for question-answering and problem-solving, and it provides reliable and reproducible results across multiple runs. The model was fine-tuned using instruction fine-tuning (SFT) with Turkish and multilingual datasets. It is quantization-ready for q8, f16, and f32 formats. The model supports a range of use cases, including text generation, summarization, question and answer, creative writing, and reasoning tasks. Next-1B has demonstrated strong performance on benchmarks, achieving 87.3% on MMLU (5-shot), 69.2% on MMLU-Pro, 90.5% on GSM8K, and 70.1% on MATH. The model is available under the MIT License, which allows for free use, modification, and distribution with attribution appreciated.

instruction: Generate a realistic walking animation of a character turning their head to look over their shoulder.
description: HY-Motion 1.0 is a series of text-to-3D human motion generation models based on Diffusion Transformer (DiT) and Flow Matching. It enables developers to generate skeleton-based 3D character animations from simple text prompts, which can be directly integrated into various 3D animation pipelines. This model series is the first to scale DiT-based text-to-motion models to the billion-parameter level, achieving significant improvements in instruction-following capabilities and motion quality over existing open-source models. The HY-Motion 1.0 series includes two models: HY-Motion-1.0, a standard text-to-motion model with 1.0 billion parameters, and HY-Motion-1.0-Lite, a lightweight version with 0.46 billion parameters. Both models require a minimum of 24GB GPU VRAM for operation. The models are trained using a three-stage process: large-scale pre-training on over 3,000 hours of diverse motion data to learn a broad motion prior, high-quality fine-tuning on 400 hours of curated, high-quality 3D motion data to enhance motion detail and smoothness, and reinforcement learning using human feedback and reward models to refine instruction-following and motion naturalness. HY-Motion 1.0 supports macOS, Windows, and Linux. It provides a command-line interface for batch inference and a Gradio-based web interface for interactive visualization. Users are advised to use English prompts under 60 words, focusing on action descriptions or detailed movements of the limbs and torso. The model does not support non-humanoid characters, subjective or visual attributes, environment or camera descriptions, multi-person interactions, or special modes such as seamless loops or in-place animations. Example prompts include descriptions of specific physical actions like squats, climbing, standing up, and walking. The model is evaluated for its state-of-the-art performance in instruction-following and motion quality, with the billion-scale models outperforming comparable open-source models. Users should note that the model requires specific settings to reduce GPU VRAM usage, such as limiting the number of seeds, using short text prompts, and generating motions under 5 seconds in length.

instruction: I want to animate a cartoon character performing a magic trick. How can I describe it to get the best results?
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to the foundational video generation models, incorporating several key innovations to enhance generation quality and model capability. It introduces a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture into the video diffusion model, which separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models. This design increases the overall model capacity while maintaining the same computational cost. The A14B model series in Wan2.2 uses a two-expert design: a high-noise expert for the early stages of denoising, focusing on overall layout, and a low-noise expert for the later stages, refining video details. Each expert has about 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters, but only 14B active parameters per step, keeping inference computation and GPU memory nearly unchanged. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a threshold step defined to switch from the high-noise to the low-noise expert. Validation results show that the MoE architecture in Wan2.2 achieves the lowest validation loss, indicating superior convergence and video quality compared to other configurations. In addition to the MoE models, Wan2.2 also includes a 5B dense model, TI2V-5B, supported by a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE that achieves a compression ratio of 16×16×4, increasing the overall compression rate to 64 while maintaining high-quality video reconstruction. With an additional patchification layer, the total compression ratio of TI2V-5B reaches 4×32×32. TI2V-5B can generate a 5-second 720P video in under 9 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU, making it one of the fastest 720P@24fps video generation models. It supports both text-to-video and image-to-video tasks within a unified framework. Wan2.2 also incorporates meticulously curated aesthetic data, including detailed labels for lighting, composition, contrast, and color tone, allowing for more precise and controllable cinematic style generation. Compared to Wan2.1, Wan2.2 is trained on a significantly larger dataset, with +65.6% more images and +83.2% more videos, enhancing its generalization across multiple dimensions such as motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among all open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 open-sources a 5B model that supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps and can run on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090. It also supports character animation and replacement, where the model can generate a video of a character image that mimics the human motion in an input video (animation mode) or replace the character image with the input video (replacement mode).
