Wan2.2 has been integrated into ComfyUI and Diffusers, and its inference code and model weights have been released. It also supports text-to-speech synthesis for the Speech-to-Video generation task. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license provisions, including not generating harmful or illegal content.

instruction: What would a scene look like if a person's face was the center of a surreal underwater world inside a bubble?
description: This model is a ControlNet module designed for the Wan2.2 A14B text-to-video generation system, specifically for depth-based control. It follows the same approach as other ControlNet implementations for Wan2.2. The model is intended to be used with the ComfyUI framework and requires the latest version of the diffusers library. It supports inference tasks for generating video content based on textual prompts and depth control inputs. The architecture includes a custom WanControlnet component, a transformer model, and an autoencoder. Performance is demonstrated through inference examples using a CLI and code, where the model processes a video input and generates a controlled video output based on a detailed prompt. The model is evaluated using a prompt that describes a complex visual scene involving a close-up shot of a young woman's face with a bubblegum bubble containing a miniature aquarium. The model is trained on unspecified data and is available for use with specific model paths. Caveats include the requirement for specific software versions and hardware, such as CUDA-compatible devices, and the need for manual installation of dependencies. The model is provided under a GitHub repository, and users are directed to raise issues or seek professional support through the repository or via email.

instruction: Could you explain the events that lead to the climax in this video?
description: The SAGE-MM-Qwen3-VL-4B-SFT_RL from allenai is a 4B-parameter reinforcement learning (RL) post-trained vision-language model, further refined from the SAGE-MM-Qwen3-VL-4B-SFT base via RL on top of Qwen/Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct. It serves as the core decision-maker in the SAGE (Smart Any-Horizon Agent) system for long video reasoning with improved performance, achieving a +4.1% improvement over SFT according to experiments. The model operates in two stages: Stage-1 analyzes initial frames and metadata to classify queries as single-turn or multi-turn, while Stage-2 iteratively invokes JSON-formatted tools such as web-search, transcribe-speech (ASR), ground-event (temporal localization), extract-video-parts (frames/subclips), and analyze for dynamic context updates until resolution. It is designed for extended video Q&A beyond fixed horizons, handling sports, narratives, and events. The model requires the SAGE GitHub runtime for tool parsing and execution and outputs action strings for robust, any-length video understanding. It is available under the Apache 2.0 license for research and educational use per Ai2 guidelines, with GGUF quantizations available.

instruction: Please determine the intent behind this legislative proposal.
description: GEM-ModernBERT HQ Legal is a ModernBERT-base model pre-trained from scratch on a 21GB corpus of Greek legal, parliamentary, and governmental text. The model incorporates ModernBERT's architectural innovations, including Flash Attention 2, StableAdamW optimizer, 1024-token context length, and advanced memory optimization techniques. It is designed for superior performance on Greek legal document understanding tasks. The model uses a quality-based data repetition strategy, with 30% masking probability, trapezoidal learning rate scheduling, and optimized batch sizing to enhance convergence and performance. It supports extended 1024-token context windows for handling longer legal documents while maintaining computational efficiency. The model is optimized for downstream tasks such as Named Entity Recognition (NER), Text Classification, and Question Answering in the legal domain. The training data includes a 21.12GB corpus, expanded from an original 16.75GB through strategic repetition of higher-quality legal content. The model uses 1024-token sequences to process complete legal articles, full court decisions, complex legislative texts, and parliamentary debates. The model architecture includes a hidden size of 768, 12 attention heads, 12 hidden layers, and approximately 139 million parameters. It has a vocabulary size of 50,373 tokens and supports 1024 max position embeddings. The model was pre-trained for 150,000 steps on 8x NVIDIA H100 80GB GPUs using BFloat16 mixed-precision. Training took 97 hours and 9 minutes to complete. Key training optimizations include a per-device batch size of 16, gradient accumulation steps of 8, and an effective batch size of 1,024. The StableAdamW optimizer was configured with a learning rate of 0.0002, weight decay of 0.1, and trapezoidal learning rate scheduling with 9,000 warmup steps and square-root decay. The model achieved a final training loss of 0.7648 and a final evaluation loss of 0.7751. Training used a 90%/10% train/validation split and 21.12GB of effective training data. Advanced training infrastructure included Flash Attention 2 optimizations, memory optimization settings, and distributed training with NCCL backend and BFloat16 mixed precision. The model supports downstream tasks with extended context and is compatible with the ModernBERT tokenizer.

instruction: Can I process multiple video clips at once, and if so, how does that affect performance?
description: ComfyUI-SeedVR2_VideoUpscaler is an official release for ComfyUI that enables video and image upscaling. It provides high-quality upscaling and is suitable for any video length when the appropriate settings are used. The model supports both 3B and 7B versions, with FP8 and FP16 precision options. It requires a GPU with substantial VRAM, with at least 18GB recommended for the 3B version and more than 24GB for optimal performance with the 7B version. The model automatically downloads into the `models/SEEDVR2` directory. Users must configure parameters such as model version, seed, new resolution, batch size, and VRAM preservation settings. Temporal consistency requires a batch size of at least 5, and higher batch sizes improve results but increase VRAM usage. Input and output resolutions impact VRAM consumption, and users may need to adjust these if they encounter out-of-memory errors. The model has been benchmarked on various hardware, including NVIDIA H100 and RTX4090 GPUs, with performance metrics showing improvements in speed and VRAM efficiency over time. The 7B FP8 model has quality issues and is recommended to be used in FP16 if possible. The model has undergone several updates to improve speed, reduce VRAM usage, fix memory leaks, and allow process interruption. It is compatible with the latest ComfyUI version and Python 3.12.9. The model has high VRAM requirements and processing speed depends on GPU capabilities. Contributions are welcome, and users are encouraged to submit pull requests for improvements. The code is released under the MIT license.

instruction: Generate a clip of a forest at dawn with mist rising from the ground and birds starting to sing.
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video (TI2V) generation. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity without increasing computational cost. Wan2.2 is trained on significantly larger data, with 65.6% more images and 83.2% more videos than its predecessor, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. The model includes a 5B version with a 16×16×4 compression ratio, supporting both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps, and is capable of running on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090. The T2V-A14B model supports generating 5-second videos at 480P and 720P resolutions. It delivers outstanding video generation quality and surpasses leading commercial models on the Wan-Bench 2.0 benchmark. The model is available for download via Hugging Face and ModelScope, and supports both single-GPU and multi-GPU inference using FSDP and DeepSpeed Ulysses. Prompt extension is available via Dashscope API or local Qwen models to enhance video quality. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license terms and applicable laws.

instruction: Show me a video of a car driving at night with the hum of the engine and soft music.
description: This is a GGUF quantized version of the LTX-2 model. The unsloth/LTX-2-GGUF model uses a methodology for state-of-the-art performance, with important layers upcasted to higher precision. The model uses tooling from city96. The LTX-2 model is a DiT-based audio-video foundation model designed to generate synchronized video and audio within a single model. It integrates the core building blocks of modern video generation, with open weights and a focus on practical, local execution. The model is developed by Lightricks and is a diffusion-based audio-video foundation model that supports English. The model is available in several versions, including a full model, a full model in fp8 quantization, a full model in nvfp4 quantization, a distilled version of the full model, a LoRA version of the distilled model applicable to the full model, an x2 spatial upscaler for the ltx-2 latents used in multi-stage pipelines for higher resolution, and an x2 temporal upscaler for the ltx-2 latents used in multi-stage pipelines for higher FPS. The model can be accessed via online demos and is available for local execution. A direct use license allows the use of the models, including full, distilled, upscalers, and any derivatives, for specific purposes. The model can be used with ComfyUI through built-in LTXVideo nodes or manually installed as per provided instructions. The PyTorch codebase includes model definitions, pipelines, and training capabilities, and was tested with Python 3.12 or higher, CUDA version 12.7 or higher, and PyTorch 2.7. The model is supported in the Diffusers library for image-to-video generation. General usage tips include ensuring width and height settings are divisible by 32 and frame count is divisible by 8 + 1. If resolution or frame count is not divisible by these values, input should be padded with -1 and then cropped to the desired dimensions. The model has several limitations: it is not intended to provide factual information, may amplify existing societal biases, may fail to generate videos that perfectly match prompts, prompt following is heavily influenced by prompting-style, may generate inappropriate or offensive content, and when generating audio without speech, the audio may be of lower quality. The base (dev) model is fully trainable, and it is easy to reproduce the LoRAs and IC-LoRAs published with the model by following provided instructions. Training for motion, style, or likeness (sound+appearance) can take less than an hour in many settings.

instruction: What command should I use to check disk usage in real time?
description: OZGURLUK-GPT-LinuxGeneral is a fine-tuned language model developed specifically to assist with Linux system administration, network management, and database troubleshooting. It was trained using a custom dataset focused on technical tasks, enabling it to interpret Linux commands and provide useful solutions. The model is suitable for tasks such as server troubleshooting, network configuration, and database management, offering step-by-step guidance. It is available in three formats: 4-bit, 8-bit, and 16-bit. The 8-bit version balances performance and memory usage, while the 16-bit version provides higher accuracy and precision for more demanding tasks. The 4-bit version is a lightweight alternative optimized for low-resource environments. The model also supports the HUGG format (GGUF), which reduces memory footprint and enables fast load times. The dataset used for fine-tuning was developed internally, gathering knowledge from various sources and internet databases. It contains 56,466 question-answer pairs covering a broad range of technical domains, including Kubernetes, Linux, PostgreSQL, Docker, and others. The dataset is organized under different 'ticker' headings, grouping similar questions and answers within each domain. This structure allows the model to better understand the nuances of technical problem-solving and provide relevant, tailored responses. The model is optimized for Linux, trained with a dataset of Linux system commands, troubleshooting solutions, and network configurations. It supports multiple formats, allowing users to choose between 4-bit, 8-bit, 16-bit, or HUGG based on their hardware. It was trained using Unsloth and TRL, achieving faster training and efficient inference. The base model used for fine-tuning is meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, and the fine-tuning method is LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation). The training was conducted on Google Colab with A100 GPUs.

instruction: Produce a realistic image of a desert oasis with palm trees and a clear blue sky, along with the depth data.
description: The LDM3D model was proposed in a 2023 paper authored by Gabriela Ben Melech Stan, Diana Wofk, Scottie Fox, Alex Redden, Will Saxton, Jean Yu, Estelle Aflalo, Shao-Yen Tseng, Fabio Nonato, Matthias Muller, and Vasudev Lal. LDM3D is a Latent Diffusion Model for 3D that generates both image and depth map data from a given text prompt, allowing users to generate RGBD images from text prompts. The model is fine-tuned on a dataset of tuples containing an RGB image, depth map, and caption, and validated through extensive experiments. The model was fine-tuned on a dataset constructed from a subset of the LAION-400M dataset, a large-scale image-caption dataset that contains over 400 million image-caption pairs. The fine-tuning process comprises two stages. In the first stage, an autoencoder is trained to generate a lower-dimensional, perceptually equivalent data representation. Subsequently, the diffusion model is fine-tuned using the frozen autoencoder. The model can be used to generate an RGB image and depth map given a text prompt. The LDM3D model is on par with the Stable Diffusion models with the same number of parameters (1.06B). On the 512 x 512-sized MS-COCO dataset with 50 DDIM steps, the model achieved a FID score of 27.82, an IS score of 28.79 ± 0.49, and a CLIP similarity score of 26.61 ± 2.92. In depth evaluation, LDM3D achieved an AbsRel of 0.0911 and an RMSE of 0.334, compared to DPT-Large, which achieved an AbsRel of 0.0779 and an RMSE of 0.297. The model has some limitations and biases, particularly in depth map generation, where the ground truth is produced using DPT-large, and other limitations and biases from that model are applicable. Users should be made aware of the risks, biases, and limitations of the model. The model is available for use with PyTorch on both CPU and GPU architectures. The license on this model does not constitute legal advice. Users are advised to consult an attorney before using this model for commercial purposes.

instruction: I have an openpose image—can you create a realistic scene around the pose?
description: ControlNet v1.1 is a neural network structure designed to control diffusion models by adding extra conditions. It is the successor to an earlier version and was developed by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala. This checkpoint is a conversion into diffusers format and can be used in combination with Stable Diffusion. The model is a diffusion-based text-to-image generation model that supports additional input conditions. It is primarily used with openpose images, which represent human poses as a set of keypoints or skeletons. The model is trained to learn task-specific conditions in an end-to-end manner, and the learning process is robust even with small training datasets. The model supports a variety of conditions, including canny edge detection, pixel to pixel instruction, image inpainting, multi-level line segment detection, depth estimation, surface normal estimation, image segmentation, line art generation, anime line art generation, human pose estimation, scribble-based image generation, soft edge image generation, and image shuffling. Improvements in Openpose 1.1 include a more accurate implementation of OpenPose, especially for hands, and support for more inputs such as hand and face. The training dataset for the previous version had several issues, including duplicated grayscale human images, low-quality or blurry images with JPEG artifacts, and incorrect paired prompts due to data processing errors. These issues have been resolved in the new model, making it more reliable in many cases.

instruction: Process this image and return a depth map with minimal artifacts and high precision.
description: This ONNX-based DepthPro model generates high-quality depth maps with minimal overhead. Depth values are encoded such that near points are bright and far points are dark, making the output directly usable for stereo and disparity-based applications without additional inversion or preprocessing. The model is optimized for efficient inference on standard hardware. It features a depth-only ONNX export, which significantly reduces model size while preserving full depth quality. It skips field-of-view calibration, outputting raw predicted depth values without post-processing, thereby avoiding normalization artifacts and computational overhead. The output is disparity-ready and compatible with stereo/disparity workflows without conversion. The model uses FP16 weights, optimized for GPU acceleration via DirectML for faster inference. It is configured for batch size 1, as benchmarks show single-image batches deliver optimal throughput, while larger batches are slower. The model uses Opset 21 for broader runtime optimization support and includes aggressive graph optimization to simplify the model graph, reduce computation, and speed up loading. It achieves fast inference with a minimal memory footprint and rapid depth map generation. The input shape is (1, 3, 1536, 1536) in NCHW format, with an input data type of float16 and an input range of [-1.0, 1.0] (normalized RGB). The output shape is (1, 1536, 1536), with an output data type of float16 and an output range of relative depth (higher values indicate closer objects). The model requires approximately 5.2 GB of VRAM, ONNX Runtime version 1.19.0 or higher, and Python version 3.8 or higher. Benchmarks on an AMD Radeon RX 7900 XTX using ONNX Runtime v1.23.0 with DirectML show a throughput of 75.7 images per minute and a model size of 1.2 GB. The model is designed without post-processing, which can cause severe artifacts such as crushed contrast and inconsistent results due to extreme outlier depth values and unpredictable normalization effects. The model is licensed under the Apple Machine Learning Research Model License, which restricts use to non-commercial scientific research and academic development. Redistribution is allowed only with the license included. Use of Apple's trademarks, logos, or name to promote derivative models is prohibited. Commercial use, product integration, or service deployment is not allowed.

instruction: Could you tell me if this audio contains any speech or just noise?
description: MiDashengLM is an efficient audio-language model that achieves holistic audio understanding through caption-based alignment. It is designed to perform a range of audio understanding tasks, including audio captioning, audio and paralinguistic classification, speech recognition, and question answering. The model is optimized for high inference efficiency, delivering a 3.2× throughput speedup and supporting batch sizes up to 512. MiDashengLM is based on a 7B parameter architecture and is available in both FP32 and BF16 versions, with the BF16 version recommended for most applications to achieve optimal performance and efficiency. The model has been evaluated on multiple audio understanding benchmarks and has demonstrated state-of-the-art performance. For example, on the MusicCaps dataset, it achieves an FENSE score of 59.71, outperforming Qwen2.5-Omni-7B and Kimi-Audio-Instruct. On the VoxCeleb1 dataset, it achieves an accuracy of 92.36, significantly higher than the other models tested. In speech recognition tasks, it achieves a word error rate of 3.7 on the LibriSpeech test-clean dataset and 6.2 on the test-other dataset. The model is available under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in both research and business applications. Users are encouraged to cite the model in their work.

instruction: I want to fix an old photo of a car that has JPEG compression artifacts—can you help?
description: The model is a work in progress, with checkpoints being uploaded incrementally. The current best version uses LoRA A: qwen-edit-enhance_64-v3_000001000 and LoRA B: qwen-edit-enhance_000004250. The upcoming V4 candidates are LoRA A: /1000 and LoRA B: /4750. LoRA A and LoRA B are intended to be applied sequentially. The model is used in a Comfy workflow and includes a demonstration available on 🤗. The model is designed to enhance image quality and generate a detailed textual description of the scene, with more descriptive prompts leading to better upscale effects. All images shown are generated using an 8-step Lighting LoRA. The ModelSamplingAuraFlow is required, and the shift parameter must remain below 0.3; for higher resolutions, such as image 3, it can be set as low as 0.02. Recommended samplers include LCM (best), Euler_Ancestral, and Euler. All schedulers are compatible and produce varying results in terms of smoothness. The model was trained on a filtered subset of Unsplash-Lite and UltraHR-100K datasets at 2.4MP resolution. The training style focuses on photography, with subjects including landscapes, architecture, interiors, portraits, plants, vehicles, abstract photos, man-made objects, and food. The model is trained to recover from low-resolution images up to 16x, oversharpened images, noise up to 50%, Gaussian blur with a radius up to 3px, JPEG artifacts with quality as low as 5%, motion blur up to 64px, pixelation up to 16x, and color banding up to 3 bits. It is also trained to recover images that have been upscaled by other models up to 16x. The model is not trained on 2D images, illustrations, artworks, text, graphics, or CGI.

instruction: Can you assess whether the sentence 'He was born in 1990' supports the claim 'He is over 30 years old'?
description: This multilingual model is designed to perform natural language inference (NLI) on 100 languages, making it suitable for multilingual zero-shot classification. It is based on the mDeBERTa-v3-base model, which was pre-trained by Microsoft on 100 languages. The model was fine-tuned on the multilingual-nli-26lang-2mil7 dataset and the XNLI validation dataset. The multilingual-nli-26lang-2mil7 dataset contains 2,730,000 NLI hypothesis-premise pairs in 26 languages spoken by more than 4 billion people, with 105,000 text pairs per language. The dataset was created using the latest open-source machine translation models and is based on English datasets. The languages included are: ar, bn, de, es, fa, fr, he, hi, id, it, ja, ko, mr, nl, pl, ps, pt, ru, sv, sw, ta, tr, uk, ur, vi, zh. An additional 105,000 text pairs for English were added, bringing the total to 27 languages. The dataset also includes a random set of 10% of the hypothesis-premise pairs where an English hypothesis is paired with a premise in another language, and vice versa. This mix of languages enables users to formulate a hypothesis in English for a target text in another language. The XNLI validation set consists of 2490 professionally translated texts from English to 14 other languages, totaling 37,350 texts. The XNLI training set was excluded due to quality issues with the 2018 machine translations. For evaluation, three languages—bg, el, th—were excluded from the XNLI training data and included only in the test data to assess the model's performance on languages not seen during NLI fine-tuning but pre-trained on. The total training dataset had 3,287,280 hypothesis-premise pairs. The model was trained using the Hugging Face trainer with the following hyperparameters: 3 training epochs, a learning rate of 2e-05, a per-device train batch size of 32, gradient accumulation steps of 2, a warmup ratio of 0.06, a weight decay of 0.01, and fp16 disabled. The model was evaluated on the XNLI test set in 15 languages (5010 texts per language, 75150 in total) and the English test sets of MNLI, ANLI, FEVER, LING, and WANLI. The model's performance on the XNLI test set in 15 languages achieved an average accuracy of 0.822, with individual language accuracies ranging from 0.744 to 0.871. On English datasets, the model achieved an average accuracy of 0.794. The model's speed varied by language, with the highest speed of 1887 texts per second on a ZH language and the lowest of 374 texts per second on the FEVER test set. The model is capable of cross-lingual transfer, meaning it can perform NLI on languages it has not seen during NLI fine-tuning, but performance is likely lower than for languages seen during fine-tuning. The performance on the languages bg, el, and th is an indicator of this cross-lingual transfer. The model has limitations due to the use of machine-translated data, which may reduce data quality for complex tasks like NLI. Users are advised to consult the original DeBERTa-V3 paper and literature on NLI datasets for potential biases. The model may have issues with older versions of the Hugging Face Transformers library, and using Transformers==4.13 or higher is recommended. The model currently does not support FP16.

instruction: The document I took a picture of is a bit slanted. Can you rotate it to make it upright?
description: UVDoc is a model designed to perform geometric transformations on images to correct document distortion, inclination, and perspective deformation, thereby improving the accuracy of subsequent text recognition. The model achieved a Character Error Rate (CER) of 0.179 on the DocuNet benchmark dataset. UVDoc is intended to be used as part of the TextImageUnwarping module within the PaddleOCR framework. It can be integrated into projects for document image correction and is compatible with PaddlePaddle versions 3.0.0 for CUDA 11.8, CUDA 12.6, and CPU environments. The model supports inference through command-line tools and Python code, allowing users to process images and save results in various formats, including images and JSON. UVDoc can also be used in conjunction with the PP-StructureV3 pipeline, which includes layout detection, OCR, table recognition, seal recognition, and formula recognition modules. The pipeline can be enabled with the use_doc_unwarping parameter to enhance document processing in real-world scenarios.

instruction: I have an audio file of a customer service call—can you transcribe it and highlight the issue raised?
description: Voxtral Small 1.0 (24B) is an enhancement of Mistral Small 3, incorporating state-of-the-art audio input capabilities while retaining best-in-class text performance. It is designed to excel at speech transcription, translation, and audio understanding. Voxtral can operate in a pure speech transcription mode to maximize performance, automatically predicting the source audio language and transcribing the text accordingly. It supports long-form context with a 32k token context length, handling audios up to 30 minutes for transcription or 40 minutes for understanding. The model supports built-in Q&A and summarization, allowing users to ask questions directly through audio and generate structured summaries without the need for separate ASR and language models. It is natively multilingual, with automatic language detection and state-of-the-art performance in widely used languages including English, Spanish, French, Portuguese, Hindi, German, Dutch, and Italian. Voxtral enables function-calling straight from voice, allowing direct triggering of backend functions, workflows, or API calls based on spoken user intents. It retains the text understanding capabilities of its language model backbone, Mistral Small 3.1. The model can be used with frameworks such as vLLM and Transformers. For vLLM, it is recommended to use version 0.10.0 or higher. The model supports multiple audios per message, multiple user turns with audio, and function calling, though system prompts are not yet supported. Running Voxtral-Small-24B-2507 on GPU requires approximately 55 GB of GPU RAM in bf16 or fp16. The model can be used for audio instruct, transcription, and function calling. It supports text-only, audio-only, and multi-modal inputs, as well as batched inference. The model can be run in a server/client setting using vLLM or natively with Transformers version 4.54.0 or higher. It requires mistral-common version 1.8.1 or higher with audio dependencies installed.

instruction: What track is likely to be assigned to a northbound train arriving at South Station at 7:30 AM?
description: This model predicts which MBTA commuter rail track or platform a train will use. It is a small tabular neural-network ensemble trained on historical track assignments. The model performs multi-class classification across 13 track classes. It is implemented using Keras with a TensorFlow backend and consists of six diverse dense neural networks with embeddings and cyclical time features. At inference, the softmax outputs of the six models are averaged to produce the final prediction. The model accepts several inputs, including categorical features such as `station_id`, `route_id`, and `direction_id`, as well as cyclical time features like `hour_sin`, `hour_cos`, `minute_sin`, `minute_cos`, `day_sin`, and `day_cos`, and a continuous feature `scheduled_timestamp` (normalized in-model). The output is a probability distribution over the 13 track labels. The model is licensed under the MIT license. The ensemble includes six individual models, each saved as a `.keras` file, and best checkpoints from training are also available. Training metrics include an average validation loss of 1.2251 and an average validation accuracy of 0.5957, with individual model accuracies ranging from 0.5812 to 0.6049. The ensemble accuracy has a standard deviation of 0.0087. The training dataset contains 24,832 records, with 310 training steps and 77 validation steps per epoch. The model expects integer indices for `station_id` and `route_id`, and consistent vocabulary mappings are required for accurate inference. The training pipeline uses a dataset loader to define the exact feature mapping, and users must ensure the same vocabulary is used during inference. The model is intended for assisting real-time track/platform assignment predictions for MBTA commuter rail but is not a safety system and should not replace official dispatch or operations. It is sensitive to concept drift and unseen stations or routes. Consistent categorical index mapping between training and inference is required for reliable performance.

instruction: This image shows a mathematical equation with a diagram. Can you explain how the diagram supports the equation?
description: This is an updated version of a previous model, offering improved abilities in multimodal reasoning, visual perception, video understanding, and high-resolution image processing. The 2506 version achieves better accuracy on multimodal reasoning benchmarks, including 56.9 on MathVision (+20.1), 80.1 on MathVista (+8.4), 46.3 on MMMU-Pro (+3.3), and 64.0 on MMMU (+2.1), while requiring 20% fewer tokens for thinking. It also demonstrates strong performance on general visual perception and understanding tasks, such as MMBench-EN-v1.1 (84.4), MMStar (70.4), RealWorldQA (70.0), and MMVet (78.4), matching or surpassing the abilities of non-thinking models. The model extends to video scenarios, achieving state-of-the-art results on VideoMMMU (65.2) and maintaining strong general video understanding on Video-MME (71.9). It supports 3.2 million total pixels in a single image, four times the resolution of the previous version, leading to improvements on high-resolution perception and OS-agent grounding benchmarks such as V* Benchmark (83.2), ScreenSpot-Pro (52.8), and OSWorld-G (52.5). The model is designed for long-decode inference, generating up to 32K tokens, and is recommended to be used with VLLM for efficient performance. It is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers library and requires specific versions of Python, PyTorch, and Transformers for optimal use. The model is evaluated on a wide range of benchmarks, including MMLU (82.0), MMLU-Pro (68.5), MATH (91.8), and GPQA-Diamond (42.3), and is compared with other large open-source models such as Qwen2.5-VL-32B, Qwen2.5-VL-72B, and Gemma3-27B-IT. The model is available for use with specific installation instructions and inference code examples provided for both VLLM and Hugging Face Transformers. The model is described in a technical report and is available for citation through the arXiv repository.

instruction: Extract any DNA sequences or related terms from this medical journal excerpt.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for biomedical entity recognition, specifically for identifying proteins, DNA, RNA, cell lines, and cell types. It is optimized for high precision and is trained on the JNLPBA dataset, a curated corpus for biomedical named entity recognition. The model is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem, enabling easy integration into production environments. It supports the classification of biomedical entities such as B-DNA, B-RNA, B-cell_line, B-cell_type, B-protein, I-DNA, I-RNA, I-cell_line, I-cell_type, and I-protein. The model is suitable for applications including drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It achieves an F1 score of 0.80, with a precision of 0.76, recall of 0.86, and accuracy of 0.93. The JNLPBA dataset, derived from the GENIA corpus, contains annotations for five entity types and is widely used as a benchmark for evaluating biomedical NER systems. The model is based on the roberta-base architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It performs token classification for named entity recognition, taking tokenized biomedical text as input and producing BIO-tagged entity predictions as output. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. If used in research or applications, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: I want to generate a 3D scene from a written description—can you help?
description: AIEXX GenAI Image to 3D is a professional, GPU-accelerated AI system for generating high-quality 3D models from text prompts or 2D images. It is built on cutting-edge deep learning technologies and delivers production-ready 3D assets in seconds. The system supports both text-to-3D generation and image-to-3D conversion. For text-to-3D, it uses natural language prompts to generate 3D models, integrating Stable Diffusion 1.5 and supporting advanced negative prompting. This process takes 20-60 seconds per model. For image-to-3D, it converts a single image into a full 3D mesh, with support for multi-view reconstruction and automated texture generation, taking 15-30 seconds to process. The system is optimized for NVIDIA RTX 5060 GPUs, using CUDA 12.8 and cuDNN for acceleration, and supports BF16 mixed precision training and Flash Attention v2. It is implemented in Python 3.11.9 with PyTorch 2.7.0 and includes TripoSR for 3D reconstruction. The system provides professional output in GLB/GLTF format with PBR material support and configurable mesh resolution, compatible with Blender, Unity, and Unreal. It supports local, offline processing with no compilation required, and includes a drag-and-drop interface. The system requires Windows 10/11 (64-bit), an NVIDIA RTX 5060 GPU with 8GB VRAM, 16GB minimum RAM (32GB recommended), and ~55GB of free storage. It offers three workflows: a basic TripoSR pipeline for quick prototypes, an intermediate prompt-to-3D pipeline using Stable Diffusion 1.5, and an advanced InstantMesh pipeline for high-quality output. Performance benchmarks indicate processing times of 15-30 seconds for image-to-3D, 25-40 seconds for prompt-to-3D with TripoSR, and 45-90 seconds for prompt-to-3D with InstantMesh. The system is built upon open-source projects including ComfyUI, Stable Diffusion, TripoSR, PyTorch, and torch_scatter. It is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License.

instruction: Identify the species of this plant in the photo and provide a brief description.
description: Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Instruct is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced editions for flexible deployment. Key enhancements include a Visual Agent that can operate PC/mobile GUIs, recognize elements, understand functions, and invoke tools to complete tasks. It supports visual coding by generating Draw.io/HTML/CSS/JS from images and videos. The model features advanced spatial perception, capable of judging object positions, viewpoints, and occlusions, with support for 2D and 3D grounding for spatial reasoning and embodied AI. It has a long context and video understanding capability, with a native 256K context expandable to 1M, enabling it to handle books and hours-long video with full recall and second-level indexing. The model excels in enhanced multimodal reasoning, particularly in STEM and Math, with causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. It has upgraded visual recognition with broader, higher-quality pretraining, enabling it to recognize a wide range of subjects including celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. The model also features expanded OCR, supporting 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare/ancient characters and jargon, as well as long-document structure parsing. The model's text understanding is on par with pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE for full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings, enhancing long-horizon video reasoning. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. The model is designed for deployment using the vLLM framework with a recommended context length of 32768. It is available for download and can be run with a vLLM startup command that specifies GPU memory utilization, tensor parallel size, and other parameters. The model file size is 17GB, last updated on 2025-10-04. The model can be used for chat with the Transformers library, requiring specific installation and setup steps. The model is described in technical reports and academic articles published in 2023, 2024, and 2025.

instruction: How can I encode this audio so it works well on low-end devices without sacrificing clarity?
description: Distill-NeuCodec is a version of NeuCodec that features a compatible, distilled encoder. The distilled encoder is 10 times smaller in parameter count and uses approximately 7.5 times fewer MACs at inference time. The distilled model includes the following adjustments: the notoriously slow acoustic encoder is replaced with a new acoustic encoder, reducing the parameter count from 70 million to 36 million; the semantic encoder is replaced, reducing the parameter count from 600 million to 21 million. The model is developed by Neuphonic and is categorized as a Neural Audio Codec. It is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license. The model was trained using the same data as the full model, with an additional distillation loss that computes the mean squared error between the outputs of the distilled and original encoders.

instruction: Can you help me modify the lighting and shadows in a product photo to look more natural?
description: Qwen-Image-Edit-2509 is the monthly iteration of Qwen-Image-Edit, introduced in September. It is designed to support image editing tasks, with improvements over the previous version released in August. The model supports multi-image editing, allowing combinations such as 'person + person,' 'person + product,' and 'person + scene,' with optimal performance observed when using 1 to 3 input images. For single-image inputs, it significantly improves editing consistency, particularly in preserving facial identity for person editing, product identity for product editing, and text content, fonts, colors, and materials for text editing. The model also includes native support for ControlNet, enabling the use of depth maps, edge maps, keypoint maps, and other related features. It is built upon the Qwen-Image-Edit architecture and further trained via image concatenation to enable multi-image editing. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0.

instruction: Can you find the population of Kolkata from the provided table in Bengali?
description: The model is a M2M100ForConditionalGeneration model fine-tuned for the task of table question answering in low-resource Indic languages, specifically Hindi and Bengali. It is designed to answer questions over structured tabular data, returning either individual cells or entire tables as output. The model supports tasks involving structured information retrieval and processing in languages with limited annotated data and neural models. It is based on the M2M100 architecture, which is a multilingual machine translation model. The model has been trained on large-scale datasets generated through a fully automatic data generation process, which is particularly useful for low-resource languages with limited budgets. Evaluation results show that models trained on these datasets outperform state-of-the-art large language models. The model is intended for use in environments where Indic language support is required, and it includes functionality for processing and linearizing tables, translating between English and Hindi digits, and generating input-output sequences for training and inference. The model is part of a broader research effort focused on scalable data generation and evaluation procedures for low-resource table question answering. The work introduces the first tableQA datasets and models for Hindi and Bengali, and the proposed data generation method can be applied to any low-resource language with a web presence. The model and associated datasets, along with code, are publicly available for research and development purposes.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Please translate this dialogue between two characters from Japanese to Korean, preserving the natural tone.
description: The Hunyuan Translation Model Version 1.5 includes two models: HY-MT1.5-1.8B, a 1.8B parameter translation model, and HY-MT1.5-7B, a 7B parameter translation model. Both models support mutual translation across 33 languages and incorporate 5 ethnic and dialect variations. HY-MT1.5-7B is an upgraded version of the WMT25 championship model, optimized for explanatory translation and mixed-language scenarios, with added support for terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. Despite having less than one-third the parameters of HY-MT1.5-7B, HY-MT1.5-1.8B delivers translation performance comparable to its larger counterpart, achieving both high speed and high quality. After quantization, the 1.8B model can be deployed on edge devices and support real-time translation scenarios, making it widely applicable. HY-MT1.5-1.8B achieves industry-leading performance among models of the same size, surpassing most commercial translation APIs. HY-MT1.5-1.8B supports deployment on edge devices and real-time translation scenarios, offering broad applicability. HY-MT1.5-7B, compared to its September open-source version, has been optimized for annotated and mixed-language scenarios. Both models support terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. The models support translation between Chinese and other languages, as well as among multiple languages, including English, French, Portuguese, Spanish, Japanese, Turkish, Russian, Arabic, Korean, Thai, Italian, German, Vietnamese, Malay, Indonesian, Filipino, Hindi, Traditional Chinese, Polish, Czech, Dutch, Khmer, Burmese, Persian, Gujarati, Urdu, Telugu, Marathi, Hebrew, Bengali, Tamil, Ukrainian, Tibetan, Kazakh, Mongolian, Uyghur, and Cantonese. The models can be used with llama.cpp and ollama for inference, with recommended parameters including top_k, top_p, repetition_penalty, and temperature. The models are available in different quantization versions, including FP8 and Int4, and can be downloaded from Hugging Face.

instruction: Can you group these research abstracts by topic?
description: Granite-embedding-small-english-r2 is a 47M parameter dense biencoder embedding model from the Granite Embeddings collection, designed to generate high-quality text embeddings. It produces embedding vectors of size 384 based on a context length of up to 8192 tokens. Compared to most other open-source models, this model was trained using open-source relevance-pair datasets with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses, as well as IBM-collected and generated datasets. The r2 models show strong performance across standard and IBM-built information retrieval benchmarks (BEIR, ClapNQ), code retrieval (COIR), long-document search benchmarks (MLDR, LongEmbed), conversational multi-turn (MTRAG), table retrieval (NQTables, OTT-QA, AIT-QA, MultiHierTT, OpenWikiTables), and on many enterprise use cases. These models use a bi-encoder architecture to generate high-quality embeddings from text inputs such as queries, passages, and documents, enabling seamless comparison through cosine similarity. Built using retrieval-oriented pretraining, contrastive finetuning, knowledge distillation, and model merging, granite-embedding-small-english-r2 is optimized to ensure strong alignment between query and passage embeddings. The latest granite embedding r2 release introduces two English embedding models, both based on the ModernBERT architecture: granite-embedding-english-r2 (149M parameters) with an output embedding size of 768, replacing granite-embedding-125m-english; and granite-embedding-small-english-r2 (47M parameters), a reduced-size model with 8192 context length support, fewer layers, and a smaller output embedding size (384), replacing granite-embedding-30m-english. The model is designed to produce fixed-length vector representations for a given text, which can be used for text similarity, retrieval, and search applications. For efficient decoding, these models use Flash Attention 2. Installing it is optional but can lead to faster inference. The model is compatible with the SentenceTransformer library and is very easy to use. It can also be used with the Huggingface Transformers library and PyTorch. The granite embedding r2 models show strong performance across diverse tasks, including MTEB Retrieval (i.e., BEIR), MTEB-v2, code retrieval (CoIR), long-document search benchmarks (MLDR, LongEmbed), conversational multi-turn (MTRAG), and table retrieval (NQTables, OTT-QA, AIT-QA, MultiHierTT, OpenWikiTables). The average speed to encode documents on a single H100 GPU using a sliding window with 512 context length chunks is also reported. Nearing encoding speed of 200 documents per second, granite-embedding-small-english-r2 demonstrates speed and efficiency while maintaining competitive performance. The model has 47 million parameters, an embedding size of 384, 12 layers, 12 attention heads, an intermediate size of 1536, uses the GeGLU activation function, has a vocabulary size of 50368, a maximum sequence length of 8192, and is based on the ModernBERT architecture. The granite embedding r2 models incorporate key enhancements from the ModernBERT architecture, including alternating attention lengths to accelerate processing, rotary position embeddings for extended sequence length, a newly trained tokenizer optimized with code and text data, Flash Attention 2.0 for improved efficiency, and streamlined parameters, eliminating unnecessary bias terms. The models are trained using data from four key sources: unsupervised title-body paired data scraped from the web, publicly available paired data with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses, IBM-internal paired data targeting specific technical domains, and IBM-generated synthetic data. Notably, the popular MS-MARCO retrieval dataset is not used in the training corpus due to its non-commercial license. The underlying encoder models use GneissWeb, an IBM-curated dataset composed exclusively of open, commercial-friendly sources. All data undergoes a data clearance process subject to technical, business, and governance review, capturing critical information about the data, including content description, ownership, intended use, data classification, licensing information, usage restrictions, how the data will be acquired, and an assessment of sensitive information (i.e., personal information). The models were trained using IBM's computing cluster, BlueVela Cluster, outfitted with NVIDIA H100 80GB GPUs. Granite-embedding-small-english-r2 leverages both permissively licensed open-source and select proprietary data for enhanced performance. The training data for the base language model was filtered to remove text containing hate, abuse, and profanity. The model is trained only for English texts and has a context length of 8192 tokens (longer texts will be truncated to this size).

instruction: I have a rotated ID image—can you tell me how it's positioned so I can adjust it?
description: The Document Image Orientation Classification Module is designed to identify the orientation of document images and correct them through post-processing. It is used to determine the orientation of documents or IDs containing text regions before OCR processing, which helps improve OCR accuracy. The model is based on PP-LCNet_x1_0 and classifies document images into four orientation categories: 0°, 90°, 180°, and 270°. It achieves an average recognition accuracy of 99.06% and has a model storage size of 7 MB. The model can be used as part of a larger pipeline that includes document orientation classification and geometric distortion correction. The pipeline supports document preprocessing for OCR, digital document management, and scenarios requiring improved document image quality. It offers flexible deployment options across multiple hardware platforms and supports secondary development through fine-tuning on custom datasets. The model can be integrated into projects using the PaddleOCR library and is compatible with both command-line and programmatic usage. The model is optional within the pipeline and can be enabled or disabled as needed.

instruction: I need a voice line that includes a sneeze and a sniff—can you place them naturally in the dialogue?
description: Dia is a 1.6B parameter text-to-speech model developed by Nari Labs. It directly generates highly realistic dialogue from a transcript and allows conditioning the output on audio, enabling control over emotion and tone. The model can also produce nonverbal communications such as laughter, coughing, and clearing the throat. To support research, the model provides access to pretrained checkpoints and inference code. The model weights are hosted on a platform, and currently, the model only supports English generation. A comparison of the model to other models is also provided. The model was not fine-tuned on a specific voice, so different voices are generated with each run. Speaker consistency can be maintained by adding an audio prompt or fixing the seed. The model supports dialogue generation using [S1] and [S2] tags, and it can recognize non-verbal tags like (laughs), (clears throat), (sighs), (gasps), (coughs), (singing), (sings), (mumbles), (beep), (groans), (sniffs), (claps), (screams), (inhales), (exhales), (applause), (burps), (humming), (sneezes), (chuckle), and (whistles). Voice cloning is supported, and more information is available. The model can be used as a Python library or as part of the transformers library. It has been tested on GPUs with PyTorch 2.0+ and CUDA 12.6, and CPU support is planned. On enterprise GPUs, the model can generate audio in real-time, while on older GPUs, inference is slower. On an A4000 GPU, the model generates approximately 40 tokens per second, with 86 tokens corresponding to one second of audio. The full version requires around 10GB of VRAM, and a quantized version is planned for the future. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0. It is intended for research and educational use, and the following uses are strictly forbidden: identity misuse, deceptive content, and illegal or malicious use. The developers are not responsible for any misuse and oppose unethical usage. Future work includes Docker support, inference speed optimization, and memory-efficient quantization. The project is maintained by a small team and welcomes contributions. The developers acknowledge the support of computation resources, inspiration from other works, and the HuggingFace ZeroGPU Grant. The name 'Nari' is a Korean word for lily, and Jason Y. is thanked for assistance with data filtering.

instruction: This image has a handwritten note. Can you recognize the text and format it into a readable document?
description: HunyuanOCR is an end-to-end OCR expert vision-language model (VLM) built using Hunyuan's native multimodal architecture. With a lightweight 1B parameter design, it has achieved state-of-the-art performance across multiple industry benchmarks. The model is capable of complex multilingual document parsing and is designed for practical applications such as text spotting, open-field information extraction, video subtitle extraction, and photo translation. It supports tasks including spotting, document parsing, general parsing, information extraction, and translation. For document parsing, it can recognize formulas in LaTeX format, parse tables into HTML, and represent charts using Mermaid or Markdown formats. It can extract document content in markdown, ignoring headers and footers, and represent formulas in LaTeX and tables in HTML. For information extraction, it can extract key-value pairs, specific fields, and subtitles in JSON format. For translation, it can extract and translate text into English, ignoring headers and footers, and represent formulas in LaTeX and tables in HTML. The model is available for deployment and inference using the Transformers library or vLLM, with specific installation and usage instructions provided. It is recommended to use the clean_repeated_substrings function to process output text. The model has been developed by the Hunyuan Vision Team and is described in the HunyuanOCR Technical Report, which is available on arXiv. The team acknowledges the contributions of other researchers and models, as well as the benchmarks used in the development and evaluation of the model.

instruction: Come riesci a riconoscere gli occhi in un video registrato con una webcam?
description: Il modello implementa una rete neurale convoluzionale per la detection degli occhi basata su YOLOv12, ottimizzata per single-class detection, real-time processing, alta precisione e recall, e robusta performance in diverse condizioni di illuminazione. L'architettura utilizza YOLOv12n, una versione nano per performance ottimali, con input size configurabile a 640x640 pixel e output in forma di bounding boxes con confidence scores. Il modello è addestrato su un dataset custom di high-quality disponibile su HuggingFace, con una pipeline completa di training, validazione e test. Le metriche di performance includono una precisione superiore a 0.85, un recall superiore a 0.80, un F1 Score superiore a 0.82, un mAP@0.5 superiore a 0.80 e un mAP@0.5:0.95 superiore a 0.70. Il modello supporta predizioni su immagini, video e webcam in tempo reale, con configurazione flessibile tramite file YAML e monitoraggio avanzato con integrazione WandB. Il training include data augmentation con variazioni HSV, trasformazioni geometriche, mosaic augmentation e flipping orizzontale. Il modello è progettato per applicazioni come medical imaging, human-computer interaction, security systems, research applications e quality control. Tuttavia, presenta limitazioni in caso di posizioni non naturali degli occhi, ad esempio volto ruotato di 90°.

instruction: Can you turn this audio of a Czech lecture into a properly formatted text document?
description: parakeet-tdt-0.6b-v3 is a 600-million-parameter multilingual automatic speech recognition (ASR) model designed for high-throughput speech-to-text transcription. It extends a previous model by expanding language support from English to 25 European languages. The model automatically detects the language of the audio and transcribes it without requiring additional prompting. It is part of a series of models that leverage a multilingual corpus as their primary training dataset. The supported languages include Bulgarian, Croatian, Czech, Danish, Dutch, English, Estonian, Finnish, French, German, Greek, Hungarian, Italian, Latvian, Lithuanian, Maltese, Polish, Portuguese, Romanian, Slovak, Slovenian, Spanish, Swedish, Russian, and Ukrainian. The model is ready for commercial and non-commercial use. Key features include automatic punctuation and capitalization, accurate word-level and segment-level timestamps, long audio transcription supporting up to 24 minutes with full attention or up to 3 hours with local attention, and a permissive CC BY 4.0 license. The model is based on the FastConformer-TDT architecture and has 600 million parameters. It accepts 16kHz audio in .wav and .flac formats and produces text output with punctuation and capitalization included. The model is optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems, achieving faster training and inference times compared to CPU-only solutions. It was trained using the NeMo toolkit, initialized from a CTC multilingual checkpoint pretrained on the Granary dataset, and further trained for 150,000 steps on 128 A100 GPUs. Stage 2 fine-tuning was performed for 5,000 steps on 4 A100 GPUs using approximately 7,500 hours of high-quality, human-transcribed data from NeMo ASR Set 3.0. A unified SentencePiece Tokenizer with a vocabulary of 8,192 tokens was used during training. The model was trained on a combination of the Granary dataset and the NeMo ASR Set 3.0, which includes 10,000 hours of human-transcribed data and 660,000 hours of pseudo-labeled data. Evaluation datasets include Fleurs, MLS, CoVoST, and the Hugging Face Open ASR Leaderboard. The model's multilingual ASR performance is summarized by word error rate (WER) across different datasets, with an average WER of 11.97% on Fleurs, 7.83% on MLS, and 11.98% on CoVoST. On the Hugging Face Open-ASR-Leaderboard, the model achieves an average WER of 6.34%. Performance is evaluated across different Signal-to-Noise Ratios (SNR), with increasing WER as SNR decreases. The model is intended for use by developers, researchers, academics, and industries building applications that require speech-to-text capabilities, including conversational AI, voice assistants, transcription services, subtitle generation, and voice analytics platforms. Ethical considerations include ensuring the model meets requirements for the relevant industry and use case and addressing unforeseen product misuse. The model has been verified to meet prescribed NVIDIA quality standards and is licensed under a permissive CC BY 4.0 license. Technical limitations include potential inaccuracies in transcripts, with accuracy varying based on language and input audio characteristics. The model is not recommended for word-for-word or incomplete sentences as accuracy may vary based on the context of the input text.

instruction: How do the file sizes affect the overall performance and load times?
description: This model is a set of imatrix quantizations of the gemma-3n-E4B-it model by Google, using the llama.cpp release b5760 for quantization. The original model is quantized using the imatrix option with a dataset provided. The quantizations are intended to be run directly with llama.cpp or any other llama.cpp-based project. No chat template is specified, so a default prompt format is used, which may not be accurate and should be verified against the original model card. The quantizations vary in quality and file size, with options ranging from Q8_0 (7.35GB, extremely high quality) to IQ3_XS (3.17GB, lower quality). Some quantizations, such as Q3_K_XL and Q4_K_L, use Q8_0 for embed and output weights instead of the default quantization. The model supports online repacking for ARM and AVX CPU inference, particularly for the Q4_0 and IQ4_NL quantizations, which can improve performance on compatible hardware. The Q4_0 quantization has been deprecated in favor of the Q4_0 format with online repacking. The model can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool, with instructions provided for downloading specific files or multiple files if the model exceeds 50GB. Performance benchmarks on an AVX2 system (EPYC7702) show that the Q4_K_M quantization offers improved tokens per second compared to Q4_0 in most cases, while the Q4_0_8_8 quantization provides a performance boost for prompt processing and text generation. The choice of quantization depends on available hardware resources, with recommendations for maximizing speed or quality based on RAM and VRAM availability. I-quants (e.g., IQ3_M) are newer and offer better performance for their size on cuBLAS (Nvidia) or rocBLAS (AMD) systems, though they may be slower on CPU compared to K-quants (e.g., Q5_K_M). The model includes contributions from kalomaze and Dampf for the imatrix calibration dataset, ZeroWw for inspiration on embed/output quantization, and LM Studio for sponsorship.

instruction: Can you analyze this short video clip and tell me what action is being performed?
description: V-JEPA 2 is a video understanding model developed by FAIR at Meta, extending the pretraining objectives to achieve state-of-the-art video understanding capabilities, leveraging large-scale data and model sizes. The model is based on a ViT-L 256 architecture with a video classification head and is pretrained on the Something-Something-V2 dataset. It is designed to support video classification tasks and is intended for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library. The model processes video inputs by sampling frames according to its configuration, preprocessing them, and running inference to produce classification logits. Performance is demonstrated through an example where the model predicts top 5 class labels with corresponding probabilities. The model is available for use with the latest version of the Transformers library and requires a compatible environment for execution. The model's development and evaluation are detailed in a technical report published by FAIR at Meta in 2025.

instruction: How would you classify the primary subject in this picture?
description: The convnext_large.dinov3_lvd1689m is a DINOv3 ConvNeXt image feature model. It is designed for image classification and as a feature backbone. The model is pretrained on the LVD-1689M dataset using the self-supervised DINOv3 method and is distilled from a DINOv3 ViT-7B model. It has 196.2 million parameters, 34.4 GMACs, and 43.1 million activations. The model processes images of size 224 x 224. It supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. The model is based on the ConvNeXt architecture described in the paper 'A ConvNet for the 2020s' and is implemented using the PyTorch Image Models library. The model is associated with the DINOv3 research, as detailed in the arXiv preprint 'DINOv3'.

It is part of the Community Model Program, which highlights new and noteworthy models created by the community. The model is provided by a third party and is not created, owned, or endorsed by LM Studio. instruction: What is the first-order logic version of 'There exists a solution to this equation'?
description: This model is a fully fine-tuned version of the T5 architecture, developed by Vossel et al. at Osnabrück University and funded by the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (DFG, German Research Foundation) under grant number 456666331. It is designed to translate natural language (NL) statements into First-Order Logic (FOL) representations. The model is an encoder-decoder sequence-to-sequence model, supporting English and FOL. It was fine-tuned from the google/t5-base model and is released under the Apache 2.0 License. The model is intended for use in automated semantic parsing, explainable AI systems requiring symbolic reasoning, research in neurosymbolic AI, and integration into pipelines for natural language inference, question answering, or knowledge base population. It is not designed for general natural language generation and may struggle with ambiguous, highly figurative, or out-of-domain input. Outputs should be validated for correctness before use, especially in critical applications. The model should not be used as the sole basis for decisions in critical areas without expert review. It is specialized for English NL and FOL and may not generalize to other languages or logics. Human-in-the-loop workflows are recommended for sensitive tasks. The model is intended for research and prototyping, not for standalone critical systems. The model was fine-tuned on two datasets: WillowNLtoFOL, which contains over 16,000 NL-FOL pairs and is licensed under CC BY-NC-ND 4.0, and MALLS-v0, which contains 34,000 NL-FOL pairs generated by GPT-4 and syntactically checked, licensed under Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0, subject to OpenAI terms. The training procedure involved full fine-tuning from the google/t5-base model with prompt-based instruction tuning, single-GPU training in float32 precision, and preprocessing that replaced FOL quantifiers (e.g., ∀) with tokens like FORALL. The maximum input/output sequence length was 250 tokens. Training hyperparameters included a batch size of 8 per device, a learning rate of 0.001, 12 epochs, the AdamW optimizer with an Adam epsilon of 1e-8, a linear warmup scheduler with 500 steps, no gradient accumulation, a weight decay of 0.01, no LoRA (full fine-tuning), a task type of SEQ_2_SEQ_LM, early stopping with a patience of 4 epochs, an evaluation strategy per epoch, a save strategy per epoch, a save total limit of 5 checkpoints, and best model selection based on eval_loss.

instruction: Find a piece of music that evokes the feeling of walking through a forest at sunrise.
description: MuQ is a large music foundation model pre-trained via Self-Supervised Learning (SSL), achieving state-of-the-art performance in various MIR (Music Information Retrieval) tasks. MuQ-MuLan is a music-text joint embedding model trained via contrastive learning, supporting both English and Chinese texts. MuQ is based on a self-supervised learning approach using Mel Residual Vector Quantization and is designed to extract music audio features. MuQ-MuLan is designed to extract both music and text embeddings and calculate their similarity. MuQ has approximately 300 million parameters and was trained on the Million Song Dataset. MuQ-MuLan has approximately 700 million parameters and was trained on music-text pairs. It is important to note that the open-sourced MuQ model may not achieve the same level of performance as reported in the paper due to differences in dataset size.

instruction: Could you describe the composition and layout of the elements in this picture?
description: MobileCLIP2-S3-OpenCLIP is a variant of the MobileCLIP2 model, adapted from the original Apple model and configured using OpenCLIP settings and weight naming conventions. MobileCLIP2 was introduced in a publication featured in TMLR August 2025 by Fartash Faghri, Pavan Kumar Anasosalu Vasu, Cem Koc, Vaishaal Shankar, Alexander T Toshev, Oncel Tuzel, and Hadi Pouransari. The MobileCLIP2-S3 checkpoint is included in this repository. MobileCLIP2-S4 matches the accuracy of SigLIP-SO400M/14 with 2x fewer parameters and surpasses DFN ViT-L/14 at 2.5x lower latency measured on iPhone12 Pro Max. MobileCLIP-S3 and S4 are new architectures trained on the MobileCLIP training dataset, DataCompDR-1B. The smallest variant, MobileCLIP-S0, achieves similar zero-shot performance as ViT-B/16 while being 4.8x faster and 2.8x smaller. MobileCLIP-S2 achieves better average zero-shot performance than ViT-B/16 while being 2.3x faster and 2.1x smaller, and was trained with 3x fewer seen samples. MobileCLIP-B (LT) attains a zero-shot ImageNet performance of 77.2%, which is significantly better than recent works with similar architectures or even others. The model is designed for tasks involving image and text processing, including zero-shot classification and cross-modal feature encoding. It is based on a vision-language architecture and is optimized for performance on mobile devices. The model's performance is evaluated on the ImageNet-1k dataset and 38 other datasets, with results including zero-shot top-1 accuracy and average performance metrics. The model supports inference and model exporting, with optional reparameterization for improved performance. It uses a tokenizer and preprocessing steps for image and text inputs, and outputs label probabilities based on encoded features.

instruction: Could you help identify the key points of a person's legs in this image?
description: The Person Pose Detection 256x480 model is a lightweight quantized tflite model developed by Synaptics for the SR100 processor in the Synaptics Astra™ SR MCU Series. It is designed to generate bounding boxes and identify 17 keypoints for each person, corresponding to various body parts. Each keypoint is accompanied by a confidence score that indicates the reliability of the detection, enabling accurate estimation of the person's pose. The model is optimized to fit in Flash and is also designed to fit in SRAM. It accepts an input size of 256x480 and outputs, for each detected person, bounding box coordinates, 17 keypoint locations, and confidence scores. The model is a single-class model, specifically designed for pose estimation of people. It is fully quantized in INT8 and is intended for real-time person pose estimation on embedded, resource-constrained edge devices. Typical use cases include human activity recognition and gesture control, fitness and wellness tracking, smart home automation based on body posture, and interactive applications and user interfaces. The model can be optimized for the Astra™ SR100 MCU using the Astra™ Machina Micro Dev Kit. Input images must be resized and quantized to match model requirements. The model is distributed under a license that allows flexible use, modification, and distribution. Evaluation and testing can be performed in a hosted environment optimized for the Synaptics SR110 MCU, which provides a seamless sandbox for model evaluation using hardware-specific quantization and runtime settings.

instruction: How would you summarize the results of this dermatology image analysis?
description: MedGemma is a collection of multimodal and text-only models trained for performance on medical text and image comprehension. Developers can use MedGemma to accelerate the building of healthcare-based AI applications. MedGemma currently comes in three variants: a 4B multimodal version and 27B text-only and multimodal versions. Both MedGemma multimodal versions utilize an image encoder that has been specifically pre-trained on a variety of de-identified medical data, including chest X-rays, dermatology images, ophthalmology images, and histopathology slides. Their LLM components are trained on a diverse set of medical data, including medical text, medical question-answer pairs, FHIR-based electronic health record data (27B multimodal only), radiology images, histopathology patches, ophthalmology images, and dermatology images. MedGemma 4B is available in both pre-trained (suffix: `-pt`) and instruction-tuned (suffix `-it`) versions. The instruction-tuned version is a better starting point for most applications. The pre-trained version is available for those who want to experiment more deeply with the models. MedGemma 27B multimodal has pre-training on medical image, medical record and medical record comprehension tasks. MedGemma 27B text-only has been trained exclusively on medical text. Both models have been optimized for inference-time computation on medical reasoning. This means it has slightly higher performance on some text benchmarks than MedGemma 27B multimodal. Users who want to work with a single model for both medical text, medical record and medical image tasks are better suited for MedGemma 27B multimodal. Those that only need text use-cases may be better served with the text-only variant. Both MedGemma 27B variants are only available in instruction-tuned versions. MedGemma variants have been evaluated on a range of clinically relevant benchmarks to illustrate their baseline performance. These evaluations are based on both open benchmark datasets and curated datasets. Developers can fine-tune MedGemma variants for improved performance. MedGemma is optimized for medical applications that involve a text generation component. For medical image-based applications that do not involve text generation, such as data-efficient classification, zero-shot classification, or content-based or semantic image retrieval, the MedSigLIP is recommended. MedSigLIP is based on the same image encoder that powers MedGemma. The MedGemma model is built based on Gemma 3 and uses the same decoder-only transformer architecture as Gemma 3. The model type is a decoder-only Transformer architecture. The input modalities for 4B and 27B multimodal models are text and vision, while the 27B text-only model is text only. The output modality for all models is text only. The attention mechanism used is grouped-query attention (GQA). The context length supports long context, at least 128K tokens. The model was created on July 9, 2025, and the model version is 1.0.0. Inputs include text strings, such as a question or prompt, images normalized to 896 x 896 resolution and encoded to 256 tokens each, and a total input length of 128K tokens. Outputs include generated text in response to the input, such as an answer to a question, analysis of image content, or a summary of a document, with a total output length of 8192 tokens. MedGemma was evaluated across a range of different multimodal classification, report generation, visual question answering, and text-based tasks. The multimodal performance of MedGemma 4B and 27B multimodal was evaluated across a range of benchmarks, focusing on radiology, dermatology, histopathology, ophthalmology, and multimodal clinical reasoning. MedGemma 4B outperforms the base Gemma 3 4B model across all tested multimodal health benchmarks. MedGemma chest X-ray (CXR) report generation performance was evaluated using the MIMIC CXR dataset and the RadGraph F1 metric. The instruction-tuned versions of MedGemma 4B and MedGemma 27B achieve lower scores due to the differences in reporting style compared to the MIMIC ground truth reports. Further fine-tuning on MIMIC reports enables users to achieve improved performance. MedGemma 4B and text-only MedGemma 27B were evaluated across a range of text-only benchmarks for medical knowledge and reasoning. The MedGemma models outperform their respective base Gemma models across all tested text-only health benchmarks. All models were evaluated on a question answer dataset from synthetic FHIR data to answer questions about patient records. MedGemma 27B multimodal's FHIR-specific training gives it significant improvement over other MedGemma and Gemma models. The evaluation methods include structured evaluations and internal red-teaming testing of relevant content policies. Red-teaming was conducted by a number of different teams, each with different goals and human evaluation metrics. These models were evaluated against a number of different categories relevant to ethics and safety, including child safety, content safety, representational harms, and general medical harms. For all areas of safety testing, we saw safe levels of performance across the categories of child safety, content safety, and representational harms. All testing was conducted without safety filters to evaluate the model capabilities and behaviors. For text-to-text, image-to-text, and audio-to-text, and across both MedGemma model sizes, the model produced minimal policy violations. A limitation of our evaluations was that they included primarily English language prompts. The base Gemma models are pre-trained on a large corpus of text and code data. MedGemma multimodal variants utilize an image encoder that has been specifically pre-trained on a variety of de-identified medical data, including radiology images, histopathology images, ophthalmology images, and dermatology images. Their LLM component is trained on a diverse set of medical data, including medical text, medical question-answer pairs, FHIR-based electronic health record data (27B multimodal only), radiology images, histopathology patches, ophthalmology images, and dermatology images. MedGemma models have been evaluated on a comprehensive set of clinically relevant benchmarks, including over 22 datasets across 6 different tasks and 4 medical image modalities. These benchmarks include both open and internal datasets. MedGemma utilizes a combination of public and private datasets. This model was trained on diverse public datasets including MIMIC-CXR (chest X-rays and reports), ChestImaGenome (set of bounding boxes linking image findings with anatomical regions for MIMIC-CXR), SLAKE (multimodal medical images and questions), PAD-UFES-20 (skin lesion images and data), SCIN (dermatology images), TCGA (cancer genomics data), CAMELYON (lymph node histopathology images), PMC-OA (biomedical literature with images), and Mendeley Digital Knee X-Ray (knee X-rays). Additionally, multiple diverse proprietary datasets were licensed and incorporated.
