It is limited to English and Chinese data, and outputs in other languages may be unintelligible or offensive. The model does not handle background noise, music, or other sound effects and does not generate overlapping speech segments in conversations. It may inherit biases, errors, or omissions from its base model (Qwen2.5 1.5b). High-quality synthetic speech can be misused for impersonation, fraud, or disinformation. Users are expected to use the model lawfully and to disclose the use of AI when sharing AI-generated content. An audible disclaimer is embedded in every synthesized audio file, and an imperceptible watermark is added for provenance verification. Inference requests are logged (hashed) for abuse pattern detection and to publish aggregated statistics quarterly. Users are responsible for sourcing datasets legally and ethically, including securing appropriate rights and anonymizing data prior to use. The model is developed by members of Microsoft Research, and feedback or reports of issues can be sent to VibeVoice@microsoft.com.

instruction: How do I determine which of these two screenshots is more closely related to this article's content?
description: The Qwen3-VL-Reranker-2B is a multimodal reranking model designed for cross-modal understanding and information retrieval. It is part of the Qwen3-VL model series, which includes the Qwen3-VL-Embedding and Qwen3-VL-Reranker models. These models are built upon the Qwen3-VL foundation model and are specifically engineered to handle diverse input modalities, including text, images, screenshots, and videos, as well as combinations of these modalities. The Embedding model generates high-dimensional vectors for tasks like retrieval and clustering, while the Reranker model refines these results by taking a (query, document) pair as input and outputting a precise relevance score. The two models are typically used together in a two-stage pipeline, where the Embedding model performs initial recall and the Reranker model refines the results, significantly improving retrieval accuracy. The Qwen3-VL-Reranker-2B supports over 30 languages and is capable of handling arbitrary multimodal combinations. It has 2 billion parameters and a context length of 32,000 tokens. The model supports quantization post-processing, custom dimensions for the final embedding (MRL), and instruction-aware customization for specific tasks. Evaluation results show that the Qwen3-VL-Reranker-2B outperforms the base embedding model and baseline rerankers on various retrieval tasks, with performance metrics including an average score of 75.1 on the MMEB-v2 retrieval benchmark, 73.8 for image retrieval, 52.1 for video retrieval, 83.4 for visual document retrieval, and 70.0 on the MMTEB retrieval benchmark. The model is designed for practical use in real-world applications, offering flexibility in vector dimensions, strong performance with quantized embeddings, and the ability to integrate into existing pipelines. Developers are advised to use tailored instructions for specific tasks and to write instructions in English for optimal performance in multilingual contexts.

instruction: Can you test this audio file to see if it's been artificially generated?
description: The DF Arena 1B is a universal antispoofing model trained on traditional speech antispoofing datasets as well as singing and environmental deepfake data. It is designed to detect audio deepfakes and determine whether an audio sample is spoofed or bonafide. The model supports antispoofing tasks and can be used to classify audio inputs into spoof or bonafide categories. It is based on a neural network architecture optimized for audio deepfake detection. The model has been evaluated on multiple datasets, achieving an Equal Error Rate (EER) of 0.00% on the dfadd dataset, 11.54% on add_2023_round_2, 8.37% on codecfake, 4.66% on asvspoof_2021_la, 0.91% on in_the_wild, 1.14% on asvspoof_2019, 22.21% on add_2022_track_1, 2.92% on fake_or_real, 17.25% on asvspoof_2024, 2.20% on add_2022_track_3, 5.08% on add_2023_round_1, 0.15% on librisevoc, 1.75% on asvspoof_2021_df, and 1.09% on sonar. The average EER across all datasets is 5.919%, and the pooled EER is 9.52%. In terms of F1-score, the model achieves 0.9993 on dfadd, 0.9188 on add_2023_round_2, 0.8695 on codecfake, 0.8037 on asvspoof_2021_la, 0.9928 on in_the_wild, 0.9473 on asvspoof_2019, 0.6678 on add_2022_track_1, 0.9711 on fake_or_real, 0.6615 on asvspoof_2024, 0.9357 on add_2022_track_3, 0.9639 on add_2023_round_1, 0.9958 on librisevoc, 0.7577 on asvspoof_2021_df, and 0.9903 on sonar. The average F1-score is 0.8863, and the pooled F1-score is 0.81. For accuracy, the model achieves 99.97% on dfadd, 88.46% on add_2023_round_2, 91.63% on codecfake, 95.34% on asvspoof_2021_la, 99.10% on in_the_wild, 98.86% on asvspoof_2019, 77.79% on add_2022_track_1, 97.11% on fake_or_real, 82.75% on asvspoof_2024, 97.80% on add_2022_track_3, 94.92% on add_2023_round_1, 99.84% on librisevoc, 98.25% on asvspoof_2021_df, and 98.89% on sonar. The average accuracy is 94.079%, and the pooled accuracy is 90.47%. The model is distributed under a non-commercial license. For questions or issues, users are encouraged to open an issue on the model repository or contact the developers via email. The model can be cited using the provided BibTeX entry.

instruction: Can you detect and outline all the text in this image of a handwritten note?
description: PP-OCRv5_server_det is a text detection model from the PP-OCRv5_det series, developed by the PaddleOCR team. It is designed for high-performance applications and supports the detection of text in diverse scenarios, including handwriting, vertical, rotated, and curved text, across multiple languages such as Simplified Chinese, Traditional Chinese, English, and Japanese. The model is capable of handling complex layouts, varying text sizes, and challenging backgrounds, making it suitable for practical applications like document analysis, license plate recognition, and scene text detection. The model achieves key accuracy metrics, including 0.803 for Handwritten Chinese, 0.841 for Handwritten English, 0.945 for Printed Chinese, 0.917 for Printed English, 0.815 for Traditional Chinese, 0.676 for Ancient Text, 0.772 for Japanese, 0.797 for General Scenario, 0.671 for Pinyin, 0.8 for Rotation, 0.876 for Distortion, 0.673 for Artistic Text, and an overall average of 0.827. The model can be used as part of an OCR pipeline that includes modules for document image orientation classification, text image unwarping, text line orientation classification, text detection, and text recognition. It is also part of the PP-StructureV3 pipeline for layout analysis, which includes layout detection, general OCR, document image preprocessing, table recognition, seal recognition, and formula recognition. The model is implemented using the PaddlePaddle deep learning framework and can be run on GPU or CPU devices. It outputs detection results in the form of text polygons and scores, and supports saving results in image, JSON, and Markdown formats. The model is intended for integration into projects and pipelines for real-world text detection and document analysis tasks.

instruction: How can I get a detailed mask of the object described in this text within the given image?
description: Talk2DINO is a hybrid model that combines the spatial accuracy of DINOv2 with the language understanding capabilities of CLIP to address the task of Open-Vocabulary Segmentation (OVS). OVS involves segmenting images based on free-form textual concepts without predefined training classes. While CLIP can generate segmentation masks using Vision Transformers, it struggles with spatial localization due to global alignment of image and text features. On the other hand, self-supervised models like DINO excel in fine-grained visual encoding but lack language integration. Talk2DINO bridges this gap by aligning CLIP's textual embeddings with DINOv2's patch-level features through a learned mapping function, without fine-tuning the underlying backbones. During training, the model uses DINOv2's attention maps to selectively align local visual patches with textual embeddings. This approach enhances the segmentation process by producing more natural and less noisy segmentations and effectively distinguishing foreground objects from the background. Experimental results show that Talk2DINO achieves state-of-the-art performance on several unsupervised OVS benchmarks.

instruction: I’m working on a winter fashion pattern—can you generate a snowflake image to use as inspiration?
description: This model is an unconditional DDPM U-Net (EMA) trained from scratch to generate images of snowflakes. It operates on latent representations produced by a pre-trained Variational Autoencoder (VAE), which is not part of this model. The model learns the visual structure of real snowflakes from macro photography, enabling the generation of novel, synthetic snowflake images that resemble real crystalline forms. The input to the model is Gaussian noise, and the output consists of snowflake images after VAE decoding, with a resolution of 512×512. The model is used in the open-source application Snowflakeizer for snowflake image generation. It was trained on a dataset of macro photos of real snowflakes by Alexey Kljatov, which is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution (CC BY). The model weights are licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0). When using this model, proper attribution to the original dataset author is required. The model is intended for research in generative modeling, image synthesis experiments, artistic and creative applications, and educational purposes related to diffusion models. It has limitations, including being trained on a single-domain dataset of snowflake macro photography, and generated images may not correspond to physically accurate crystal growth processes. The model may also produce artifacts or unrealistic structures. The training data consists of non-sensitive macro photographs of natural objects, with no known privacy, biometric, or identity-related concerns. Generated images should not be presented as real scientific observations.

instruction: How can I detect all the footnotes in this PDF file?
description: This repository hosts three YOLOv11 models (nano, small, and medium) fine-tuned for high-performance Document Layout Analysis. The models are designed to accurately detect and classify key layout elements in a document, such as text, tables, figures, and titles. This is a fundamental task for document understanding and information extraction pipelines. The models are trained on the DocLayNet dataset to recognize 11 distinct layout types: Text, Title, Section-header, Table, Picture, Caption, List-item, Formula, Page-header, Page-footer, and Footnote. The recommended model is the `yolo11n` (nano) model, which offers an exceptional balance of speed and accuracy, making it ideal for production environments. The nano, small, and medium variants allow users to choose the best model for their use case. The nano model provides the best trade-off between speed and accuracy, while the small model is slightly more accurate, and the medium model provides the highest accuracy with a corresponding increase in computational cost. Performance gains are marginal when moving from the small to the medium model, making the nano and small variants the most practical choices. A deeper analysis revealed that the `train4` nano model stands out for its superior localization quality. It delivered significantly more accurate bounding boxes, with a +9.0% precision improvement for the `title` class and strong gains for `section-header` and `table`. It also achieved a +2.4% mAP50 and +2.05% mAP50-95 improvement for the difficult `footnote` class. The models were trained at 1280x1280 resolution, as initial tests at the default 640x640 resulted in a significant performance drop, especially for smaller elements like `footnote` and `caption`.

instruction: Can you help me identify the most relevant academic papers for a literature review in neuroscience?
description: ZeroEntropy has developed zerank-2, a state-of-the-art reranker designed to improve the accuracy of retrieval systems in search engines. This model outperforms closed-source competitors such as cohere-rerank-v3.5 and gemini-2.5-flash across various domains, including finance, legal, code, STEM, medical, and conversational data. The model uses an innovative multi-stage pipeline that models query-document relevance scores as adjusted. The model is currently released under a non-commercial license, and commercial license inquiries can be directed to founders@zeroentropy.dev. For evaluation purposes, OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small is used as an initial retriever for the Top 100 candidate documents. Across multiple domains, zerank-2 achieves NDCG@10 scores of 0.6346 in the Web domain, 0.6140 in Conversational, 0.6521 in STEM & Logic, 0.6528 in Code, 0.6644 in Legal, 0.7217 in Biomedical, and 0.7600 in Finance, compared to an average score of 0.6714. These scores outperform or are competitive with other models such as zerank-1, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Cohere rerank-3.5. The model can be used via the sentence_transformers library as a CrossEncoder and is also available for inference through ZeroEntropy's endpoint.

instruction: Can you help me create a multilingual chatbot that understands and responds in different languages?
description: mmBERT is a modern multilingual encoder that significantly outperforms previous generation models like XLM-R on classification, embedding, and retrieval tasks. Built on the ModernBERT architecture with novel multilingual training innovations, mmBERT demonstrates that low-resource languages can be effectively learned during the decay phase of training. It is also significantly faster than any previous multilingual encoder. The model uses bidirectional attention with masked language modeling objectives, optimized specifically for multilingual understanding and cross-lingual transfer. Key features include massive language coverage, with training on over 1800 languages using a progressive inclusion strategy. The model is built on the ModernBERT foundation with Flash Attention 2 and unpadding techniques. It introduces novel training methods such as inverse mask scheduling and temperature sampling. The training data is publicly available, consisting of over 3T tokens. The model demonstrates effective learning of low-resource languages in the final training phase. The training data is composed of 2.3T tokens in the first pre-training phase with 60 languages, followed by an extension phase and a final pre-training phase. The mid-training phase includes 600B tokens across 110 languages, and the decay phase includes 100B tokens across 1833 languages. Data sources include Filtered DCLM, FineWeb2, FineWeb2-HQ, code repositories, academic papers, Wikipedia, and community discussions. The model family includes mmBERT-small and mmBERT-base, with varying parameter counts and language coverage. mmBERT supports tasks such as masked language modeling, cross-lingual embeddings, dense retrieval, cross-lingual classification, and multilingual reranking. The model is designed for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library and can be fine-tuned for specific downstream tasks. The model is publicly available for download and use under an open license. If used in research, it should be cited with the provided BibTeX entry.

instruction: Create an image showing a serene beach at dawn with palm trees and calm waves.
description: Quantized GGUF versions of the model by Tongyi-Mai. The available models include Z-Image Turbo GGUF and Qwen3-4B (Text Encoder). The model can be used with tools provided by city96. The model is designed for image generation tasks and supports detailed text-to-image synthesis. It utilizes a transformer-based architecture and can be loaded using the Diffusers library with specific quantization configurations. The model can be run on CUDA-enabled devices with options for attention backend selection, model compilation, and CPU offloading to optimize performance and memory usage. The model is evaluated for efficiency and inference speed, particularly with the Turbo variant requiring zero guidance and a reduced number of inference steps. Users are advised to consult the original model card for detailed information. The repository follows the same license as the original model.

instruction: Find and tag all the chemical names in this pharmacology textbook excerpt.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition, specifically for identifying chemical entities from the BC5CDR dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated BC5CDR_CHEM dataset, which contains 1,500 PubMed abstracts with 4,409 annotated chemical entities. The model supports the classification of two entity types: B-CHEM and I-CHEM. It is intended for use in applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the DeBERTa-v3-large architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It has been validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.96, precision of 0.95, recall of 0.97, and accuracy of 0.99. It is ranked among the top-performing models on the BC5CDR_CHEM dataset, with an F1 score of 0.9614. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and academic settings. It is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and supporting biomedical NLP research. If used in research or applications, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: Are there any i1 versions that are not recommended due to poor performance?
description: This is a vision model, and if applicable, mmproj files will be provided. The model is available in various quantized versions, with different types and sizes. The available quant types include i1-IQ1_S (1.5 GB), i1-IQ1_M (1.5 GB), i1-IQ2_XXS (1.6 GB), i1-IQ2_XS (1.7 GB), i1-IQ2_S (1.8 GB), i1-IQ2_M (1.9 GB), i1-Q2_K_S (2.0 GB, very low quality), i1-Q2_K (2.0 GB, IQ3_XXS probably better), i1-IQ3_XXS (2.1 GB, lower quality), i1-IQ3_XS (2.3 GB), i1-IQ3_S (2.3 GB, beats Q3_K*), i1-Q3_K_S (2.3 GB, IQ3_XS probably better), i1-IQ3_M (2.4 GB), i1-Q3_K_M (2.5 GB, IQ3_S probably better), i1-Q3_K_L (2.6 GB, IQ3_M probably better), i1-IQ4_XS (2.7 GB), i1-IQ4_NL (2.8 GB, prefer IQ4_XS), i1-Q4_0 (2.8 GB, fast, low quality), i1-Q4_K_S (2.9 GB, optimal size/speed/quality), i1-Q4_K_M (3.0 GB, fast, recommended), i1-Q4_1 (3.1 GB), i1-Q5_K_S (3.3 GB), i1-Q5_K_M (3.4 GB), and i1-Q6_K (3.8 GB, practically like static Q6_K). The quants are sorted by size, not necessarily by quality, and IQ-quants are often preferable over similar-sized non-IQ quants. For a convenient overview and download list, users are directed to a specific page. If users are unsure how to use GGUF files, they are advised to refer to additional resources for details, including instructions on how to concatenate multi-part files. A graph by ikawrakow is available for comparing some lower-quality quant types, and Artefact2 has provided additional thoughts on the matter. Users are directed to a separate page for frequently asked questions and model request information. The model developer thanks their company for providing server access and workstation upgrades to support this work in their free time, and also thanks another individual for granting access to a private supercomputer, which enabled the production of many more imatrix quants at higher quality.

instruction: Can you summarize the plot of *The Great Gatsby* and highlight its major symbols?
description: Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507 is an updated version of the Qwen3-30B-A3B non-thinking mode model. It features significant improvements in general capabilities, including instruction following, logical reasoning, text comprehension, mathematics, science, coding, and tool usage. The model also shows substantial gains in long-tail knowledge coverage across multiple languages and is better aligned with user preferences in subjective and open-ended tasks, enabling more helpful responses and higher-quality text generation. It supports 256K long-context understanding and has a context length of 262,144 tokens natively. The model is a causal language model with 30.5B total parameters and 3.3B activated parameters. It has 48 layers, 32 attention heads for queries and 4 for key-value pairs, and uses a mixture-of-experts architecture with 128 experts and 8 activated experts. It supports only non-thinking mode and does not generate code blocks in its output. The model has been evaluated on various benchmarks, showing strong performance in knowledge, reasoning, coding, alignment, and multilingual tasks. For ultra-long context processing up to 1 million tokens, the model integrates Dual Chunk Attention and sparse attention mechanisms, which improve generation quality and inference efficiency. Users require approximately 240 GB of GPU memory to process 1 million tokens effectively. The model can be launched using vLLM or SGLang with specific configurations for long-context support. Best practices include using sampling parameters such as Temperature=0.7, TopP=0.8, TopK=20, and MinP=0, and setting an output length of 16,384 tokens for most queries. The model is recommended for use with standardized output formats for benchmarking, including specific prompts for math problems and multiple-choice questions.

instruction: Can you make an audio version of this summary that's clear and easy to understand?
description: Soprano is an ultra-lightweight, on-device text-to-speech (TTS) model designed for expressive, high-fidelity speech synthesis at unprecedented speed. It supports up to 2000x real-time generation on GPU and 20x real-time on CPU. The model enables lossless streaming and is designed for use in various environments, including WebUI, CLI, Python scripts, and OpenAI-compatible endpoints. Soprano allows for basic inference, batched inference, and streaming inference with customizable sampling parameters such as temperature, top_p, and repetition_penalty. It can be configured with different cache sizes and decoder batch sizes to optimize inference speed, though this may increase memory usage. The model is currently English-only and does not support voice cloning. It was trained on 1,000 hours of audio, which is approximately 100 times less than other TTS models, and as a result, mispronunciation of uncommon words may occur. This limitation is expected to improve with additional training data. Users are advised that Soprano is now outdated and should use a newer version instead. On Windows with CUDA, users must reinstall PyTorch explicitly with the correct CUDA wheel after installing Soprano to ensure CUDA support. If Soprano produces unsatisfactory results, users can regenerate the output for a new generation or adjust sampling settings for more varied results. Improper grammar, such as the lack of contractions or multiple spaces, should be avoided for optimal performance.

instruction: What species are referenced in this section on fungal infections in immunocompromised patients?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for species entity recognition, specifically for identifying and extracting species and organism names from biomedical texts. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the LINNAEUS dataset, a curated biomedical named entity recognition (NER) dataset focused on species name identification and organism recognition in scientific literature. The model supports the classification of two entity types: `B-SPECIES` and `I-SPECIES`. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is based on the e5-base-v2 architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.78, precision of 0.72, recall of 0.84, and accuracy of 0.98. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and healthcare analytics contexts. The model is part of the OpenMed NER project, which provides open-source, domain-adapted state-of-the-art transformers for biomedical NER across multiple datasets.

instruction: Which of these product descriptions best matches the customer’s request for eco-friendly materials?
description: The Qwen3-Reranker-8B is a text reranking model designed for text retrieval tasks. It is part of the Qwen3 Embedding model series, which is specifically developed for text embedding and ranking. The model is based on the Qwen3 series and offers a comprehensive range of text embeddings and reranking models in various sizes (0.6B, 4B, and 8B). It inherits the multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills of the foundational Qwen3 model. The Qwen3-Reranker-8B is optimized for reranking in text retrieval scenarios and supports over 100 languages, including various programming languages, providing robust multilingual, cross-lingual, and code retrieval capabilities. The model has 8 billion parameters and a context length of 32,000 tokens. It is instruction-aware, meaning it supports customizing input instructions according to different tasks, which can improve performance by 1% to 5% in most downstream tasks. Developers are advised to write instructions in English for optimal results, as most training instructions were in English. The model is evaluated on multiple benchmarks, including MTEB-R, CMTEB-R, MMTEB-R, MLDR, MTEB-Code, and FollowIR, where it achieves strong performance, particularly in multilingual and code-related tasks. The Qwen3-Reranker-8B is available for use with the Transformers library version 4.51.0 or higher and requires specific formatting for input queries and documents. It is recommended to use the model with flash_attention_2 for better acceleration and memory efficiency. The model is part of a series that includes both embedding and reranking models, allowing developers to combine these modules for diverse use cases.

instruction: What is the area in this image that the sentence 'a large tree with brown bark and green leaves' refers to?
description: OneRef is a unified one-tower model for expression grounding and segmentation, introduced in the paper 'OneRef: Unified One-tower Expression Grounding and Segmentation with Mask Referring Modeling' presented at NeurIPS 2024. The model is developed by Linhui Xiao, Xiaoshan Yang, Fang Peng, Yaowei Wang, and Changsheng Xu. OneRef introduces a novel paradigm called Mask Referring Modeling (MRefM), which effectively models the referential relationship between visual and language modalities. Unlike previous approaches, OneRef uses a minimalist one-tower framework that unifies visual and linguistic feature spaces, eliminating the need for modality interaction modules, fusion en-/decoders, and special grounding tokens. The model is validated across three referring tasks on five datasets, achieving state-of-the-art (SoTA) performance. OneRef supports both referring expression comprehension (REC) and referring expression segmentation (RES) tasks. The model is implemented in PyTorch and is compatible with Python 3.9.10 and PyTorch 2.0.1. It has been tested on multiple PyTorch versions and is easy to deploy in various environments. The model is trained using a referring-aware dynamic image masking strategy, which focuses on the referred region rather than using fixed ratios or random masking. The model is available in both Base and Large variants, with pre-trained checkpoints provided for single-dataset fine-tuning and dataset-mixed grounding pre-training settings. The MRefM pre-training for REC is conducted using a mixture of RefCOCO/+/g and ReferIt datasets, while for RES it is conducted using RefCOCO/+/g datasets. The model is built upon the BEiT-3 architecture and includes Sentencepiece tokenizer and VQKD model for pre-training. The model has been shown to achieve significant performance improvements in mixed pre-training settings, although the performance gains in single-dataset settings are less pronounced. The model is available for download from HuggingFace and Google Drive, with detailed training and evaluation scripts provided. The model has been tested on datasets such as RefCOCO, RefCOCO+, RefCOCOg, ReferItGame, and Flickr30K. The model's performance is evaluated using standard metrics such as mIoU and oIoU for segmentation tasks and standard accuracy metrics for comprehension tasks. The model is noted for its energy efficiency and computational cost advantages compared to existing approaches. The model's architecture and training methodology are described in detail in the paper, with visualizations and qualitative results provided for various datasets.

instruction: How do I isolate the object in the center of this image for editing?
description: BEN (Background Erase Network) is a model designed for foreground segmentation using a Confidence Guided Matting (CGM) pipeline. The architecture includes a refiner network that processes pixels where the base model has lower confidence, leading to more accurate matting results. BEN2, an enhanced version, is publicly available and was trained on the DIS5k dataset and a proprietary 22K segmentation dataset. It supports tasks such as hair matting, 4K processing, object segmentation, and edge refinement. The base model is available for free commercial use on HuggingFace. The model generates both a binary mask and a foreground image from input images and supports CUDA for GPU acceleration. Performance metrics for the BEN_Base model on the Disk 5k evaluation include a MAE of 0.0309, DICE of 0.8806, IOU of 0.8371, BER of 0.0516, and ACC of 0.9718. The BEN_Base combined with the commercial refiner model achieves a MAE of 0.0270, DICE of 0.8989, IOU of 0.8506, BER of 0.0496, and ACC of 0.9740. The model is implemented in Python and can be integrated using a simple API. Users are required to clone the repository and install the dependencies to use the model.

instruction: Can you convert this 30-second audio clip into written text for me?
description: Whisper is a pre-trained model for automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech translation. Trained on 680,000 hours of labelled speech data using large-scale weak supervision, Whisper models demonstrate strong generalization capabilities across many datasets and domains without the need for fine-tuning. Whisper is a Transformer-based encoder-decoder model, also referred to as a sequence-to-sequence model. The models were trained on either English-only data or multilingual data. English-only models were trained for speech recognition, while multilingual models were trained for both speech recognition and speech translation. For speech recognition, the model predicts transcriptions in the same language as the audio. For speech translation, the model predicts transcriptions in a different language. Whisper checkpoints come in five configurations of varying model sizes. The smallest four are trained on either English-only or multilingual data, while the largest checkpoints are multilingual only. The model is used in conjunction with a WhisperProcessor to pre-process audio inputs into log-Mel spectrograms and post-process model outputs into text. The model is informed of the task to perform (transcription or translation) by passing context tokens to the decoder. These context tokens include a transcription token, a language token, a task token, and optionally a timestamp prediction token. The task token can be set to either transcribe or translate. If the tokens are forced, the model is made to predict each token at each position, allowing control over the output language and task. If un-forced, the model predicts the output language and task automatically. Whisper can be used for English to English transcription, French to French transcription, and French to English translation by setting the appropriate context tokens. The model is evaluated using datasets such as LibriSpeech, and performance is measured using metrics like word error rate (WER). Whisper Tiny, for example, achieves a WER of approximately 7.55% on the LibriSpeech test clean dataset. The model is designed to work on audio samples up to 30 seconds in duration, but can be extended to handle longer audio through a chunking algorithm. Whisper can also predict sequence-level timestamps by enabling the return_timestamps option. While the pre-trained model generalizes well, its performance can be improved through fine-tuning, especially for certain languages and tasks. The models are primarily trained and evaluated on ASR and speech translation to English tasks and show strong results in approximately 10 languages. They may exhibit additional capabilities if fine-tuned for tasks like voice activity detection, speaker classification, or speaker diarization, but have not been robustly evaluated in these areas. Users are advised to perform thorough evaluations before deployment. The models are trained on 680,000 hours of audio data collected from the internet, with 65% in English, 18% in non-English audio with English transcripts, and 17% in non-English audio with corresponding non-English transcripts. The non-English data represents 98 different languages. Performance in a given language is correlated with the amount of training data available for that language. Whisper models exhibit improved robustness to accents, background noise, technical language, and zero-shot translation from multiple languages into English. However, due to training on noisy, weakly supervised data, the models may generate hallucinations—texts that are not actually spoken in the audio. Performance is uneven across languages, with lower accuracy observed in low-resource or low-discoverability languages and across different accents and dialects. The models may also exhibit higher word error rates for speakers of different genders, races, or ages. The sequence-to-sequence architecture makes the model prone to generating repetitive texts, which can be partially mitigated by beam search and temperature scheduling. The models are not recommended for use in high-risk domains or for subjective classification tasks. The broader implications of Whisper include potential use in accessibility tools and real-time speech recognition applications. However, the release of Whisper also raises concerns about dual use, including the potential for surveillance technologies. The models may have some capabilities to recognize specific individuals, which could present safety concerns. The cost of transcription is not expected to be a limiting factor in scaling up surveillance projects.

instruction: What is the best practice for handling long audio files during the encoding and decoding process?
description: X-Codec2 is a neural audio codec designed to improve speech synthesis and general audio generation for large language model (LLM) pipelines. It extends the original X-Codec by refining how semantic and acoustic information is integrated and tokenized, enabling efficient and high-fidelity audio representation. The architecture of X-Codec2 is based on X-Codec with several major differences. It uses a unified semantic-acoustic tokenization approach, fusing outputs from a semantic encoder (e.g., Wav2Vec2-BERT) and an acoustic encoder into a single embedding, capturing both high-level meaning (e.g., text content, emotion) and low-level audio details (e.g., timbre). It employs a single-stage vector quantization (VQ) using a single-layer Feature-Space Quantization (FSQ), which provides stability and compatibility with causal, autoregressive LLMs, in contrast to the multi-layer residual VQ used in most approaches. X-Codec2 includes semantic supervision during training, adding a semantic reconstruction loss to ensure that the discrete tokens preserve meaningful linguistic and emotional information, which is crucial for TTS tasks. Its 1D token structure is designed to align with the autoregressive modeling in LLMs like LLaMA, improving training efficiency and downstream compatibility. The model supports encoding and decoding of audio, with the ability to extract audio codes and reconstruct audio values. It is intended for use in LLM pipelines and is compatible with the Transformers library, though it is not yet merged into the main branch. Users can install it from source and use it with Python code to load the model, process audio data, and perform encoding and decoding operations. The model was contributed by external contributors, and the original code and checkpoints are available for reference.

instruction: What are the visible items on the kitchen counter in this image?
description: The OWLv2 model, short for Open-World Localization, was proposed by Matthias Minderer, Alexey Gritsenko, and Neil Houlsby. It is a zero-shot text-conditioned object detection model that can be used to query an image with one or multiple text queries. The model uses CLIP as its multi-modal backbone, with a ViT-like Transformer to extract visual features and a causal language model to extract text features. To use CLIP for detection, OWL-ViT removes the final token pooling layer of the vision model and attaches a lightweight classification and box head to each transformer output token. Open-vocabulary classification is enabled by replacing the fixed classification layer weights with the class-name embeddings obtained from the text model. The authors first train CLIP from scratch and fine-tune it end-to-end with the classification and box heads on standard detection datasets using a bipartite matching loss. One or multiple text queries per image can be used to perform zero-shot text-conditioned object detection. The model uses a CLIP backbone with a ViT-L/14 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned together with the box and class prediction heads with an object detection objective. The model is intended as a research output for research communities. It is hoped that this model will enable researchers to better understand and explore zero-shot, text-conditioned object detection. It is also hoped it can be used for interdisciplinary studies of the potential impact of such models, especially in areas that commonly require identifying objects whose label is unavailable during training. The primary intended users of these models are AI researchers. The model is primarily imagined to be used by researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. The CLIP backbone of the model was trained on publicly available image-caption data. This was done through a combination of crawling a handful of websites and using commonly-used pre-existing image datasets. A large portion of the data comes from crawling the internet, which means that the data is more representative of people and societies most connected to the internet. The prediction heads of OWL-ViT, along with the CLIP backbone, are fine-tuned on publicly available object detection datasets.

instruction: Which products should I keep an eye on to avoid stockouts during peak times?
description: The Smart Inventory Advisor for Retail Sales (Invense) is a machine learning model trained to predict daily sales for retail products and provide actionable inventory recommendations. The model is a Random Forest Regressor implemented using scikit-learn, designed to forecast the number of units sold for a given product based on a variety of features. It generates a 'Store Owner's Action Plan' that identifies products at risk of stocking out and recommends a reorder quantity. The model is intended for use as a decision-support tool for small to medium-sized retail business owners, helping them determine which products to restock and how many units to order. It is not suitable for real-time stock market prediction or long-term financial forecasting, as its predictions are based on historical patterns and may not account for drastic changes in market conditions. The model's performance is dependent on the quality and patterns of the training data, and it was trained on a synthetic dataset containing over 73,000 daily records across multiple stores and products. The final model achieved a Testing R² score of 0.34, indicating that about 66% of the variance in daily sales is due to unpredictable factors not captured in the data. The model was trained using a preprocessing pipeline that included feature engineering, label encoding of categorical features, and standard scaling of numerical features. Hyperparameters were tuned using RandomizedSearchCV, with the best settings including 200 estimators, a minimum of 10 samples to split, a minimum of 4 samples per leaf, 'sqrt' for max features, and a max depth of 20. The model was evaluated on a 20% holdout set using R-squared, Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), and Mean Absolute Error (MAE) as metrics. The Testing R² was 0.3402, RMSE was 88.39, and MAE was 69.27. Users are advised to treat the model's recommendations as data-driven suggestions and to apply their own domain knowledge, particularly when considering external factors not included in the dataset. The model should not be used for automated financial decisions without human oversight. The model is provided under the MIT license and was developed by jerewy (NexusCrew).

instruction: Could you generate a realistic image of a scene from a slightly shifted viewpoint?
description: This is a Core ML (.mlpackage) version of SHARP, a model for photorealistic view synthesis from a single image. SHARP regresses the parameters of a 3D Gaussian representation of a scene from a single photograph, which can then be rendered in real time to produce high-resolution photorealistic images for nearby views. The model is metric, with absolute scale, and supports metric camera movements. The Core ML version is optimized for CPU, GPU, and Neural Engine inference on macOS and iOS. The model accepts a 3-channel RGB image in uint8 format with shape (1, 3, H, W) and a scalar disparity factor representing the ratio of focal length to image width. It outputs five tensors representing a 3D Gaussian splat representation: 3D positions, scale parameters, orientation quaternions, linear RGB colors, and opacity values. The total number of Gaussians is approximately 1,179,648 for the default model. The Core ML model has been validated against the original PyTorch implementation, with all outputs matching within 0.01% mean error. Quaternion angular errors are below 1° for 99% of Gaussians. Inference on an Apple M4 Max takes approximately 1.9 seconds. The model is designed to be used with the provided inference script, which includes features such as automatic model compilation and caching, decimation to reduce point cloud size while preserving visual fidelity, and compatibility with standard RGB input images. The model is not affiliated with the original research paper titled 'Sharp Monocular View Synthesis in Less Than a Second' by Lars Mescheder, Wei Dong, Shiwei Li, Xuyang Bai, Marcel Santos, Peiyun Hu, Bruno Lecouat, Mingmin Zhen, Amaël Delaunoy, Tian Fang, Yanghai Tsin, Stephan Richter, and Vladlen Koltun.

instruction: What techniques are used to predict camera calibration in an unstructured environment?
description: HunyuanWorld-Mirror is a versatile feed-forward model for comprehensive 3D geometric prediction. It integrates diverse geometric priors, including camera poses, calibrated intrinsics, and depth maps, and simultaneously generates various 3D representations, such as point clouds, multi-view depths, camera parameters, surface normals, and 3D Gaussians, in a single forward pass. The model consists of two key components: Multi-Modal Prior Prompting, which embeds diverse prior modalities into the feed-forward model using lightweight encoding layers to convert each modality into structured tokens, and Universal Geometric Prediction, a unified architecture capable of handling the full spectrum of 3D reconstruction tasks, including camera and depth estimation, point map regression, surface normal estimation, and novel view synthesis.

instruction: Can you help me find related research papers based on this abstract?
description: The all-MiniLM-L12-v2 is a model that maps sentences and paragraphs to a 384-dimensional dense vector space. It is designed for tasks such as clustering and semantic search. The model can be used with the Sentence-Transformers library by installing it via pip and encoding sentences using the SentenceTransformer class. Alternatively, it can be used with the HuggingFace Transformers library by applying a mean pooling operation on the contextualized word embeddings and normalizing the resulting sentence embeddings. The model was developed using a self-supervised contrastive learning objective, where it was fine-tuned on a dataset of over 1 billion sentence pairs. The model was trained on a TPU v3-8 for 100,000 steps with a batch size of 1024, a learning rate of 2e-5, and a sequence length of 128 tokens. The training data was a combination of multiple datasets, including citation pairs, duplicate question pairs, question-answer pairs, and image captions, among others. The model is intended to be used as a sentence and short paragraph encoder, outputting a vector that captures semantic information for tasks such as information retrieval, clustering, or sentence similarity. Input text longer than 256 word pieces is truncated by default.

instruction: I have a question in Polish and several candidate documents—can you tell me which one is the best match?
description: The polish-reranker-roberta-v3 is a Polish language reranker model designed for use in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines. It is a general-purpose reranker that supports a wide range of document types and domains. This model is an updated version of the previous polish-reranker-roberta-v2, with the key improvement being the ability to handle long contexts of up to 8192 tokens, a 16x increase from the previous 512 tokens. This enhancement significantly improves reranking quality for datasets with longer documents. The model was trained using knowledge distillation, with a large language model-based reranker as the teacher model. The training process used the same loss function and largely the same training corpus as the previous version, with the addition of a new dataset of approximately 40,000 passages from the public administration domain, sourced from Polish government and local municipality websites. Questions for these passages were generated synthetically using DeepSeek-V3-0324. The model has 443 million parameters and delivers performance competitive with large LLM-based rerankers. For example, it outperforms the multilingual state-of-the-art reranker in 18 out of 41 tasks while having only 5% of its parameters. On the PIRB benchmark, the average score increased from 64.49 to 65.17 when using the sdadas/mmlw-retrieval-roberta-largeretriever, or from 65.30 to 66.21 when using the BAAI/bge-multilingual-gemma2retriever. For short and medium-length texts, the model achieves comparable or slightly better results than the previous version, but for very short texts, a slight decrease in quality was observed. The model was specifically improved for the public administration domain, with training data expanded to include questions and documents from this area. Evaluation results on three datasets from this domain show strong performance, with the model achieving NDCG@10 scores of 86.3 on opi-urzedowe, 80.1 on ezd-qa, and 86.7 on ezd-ir-chunked. The model can be used with Huggingface Transformers or Sentence-Transformers libraries. It achieves an NDCG@10 score of 66.21 in the Rerankers category of the Polish Information Retrieval Benchmark.

instruction: I want a video of a forest at night with glowing mushrooms and a soft mist covering the ground.
description: This model is a controlnet module designed for the Wan2.2 model. It follows the same approach as the controlnet implementation for Wan2.2. The model is intended for use with ComfyUI and supports inference tasks for generating video content based on text prompts and control frames. The model architecture includes a custom controlnet module integrated with the Wan2.2-TI2V-5B-Diffusers base model. Inference is performed using a pipeline that incorporates a controlnet, a transformer, and an autoencoder. The model processes video frames with Gaussian blur and resizing to generate output video sequences. Performance is evaluated through inference examples, including a detailed prompt for generating a video of a young woman blowing a bubblegum bubble containing a miniature aquarium. The model includes parameters for guidance scale, controlnet weight, and frame resolution. The model is available on GitHub, and users are encouraged to raise issues directly in the repository. Professional support can be requested via email.

instruction: This email is in Odia, and I need it translated into English for a business meeting. Could you assist?
description: Sarvam-Translate is an advanced translation model developed by Sarvam AI in collaboration with AI4Bharat. It is specifically designed for comprehensive, document-level translation across the 22 official Indian languages, built on the Gemma3-4B-IT architecture. The model addresses modern translation needs by handling long-context inputs, diverse content types, and various formats, moving beyond isolated sentences. Sarvam-Translate aims to provide high-quality, contextually aware translations for Indian languages, which have traditionally lagged behind high-resource languages in LLM performance. It supports a wide range of input formats, including markdown, digitized content with OCR errors, documents with embedded math and chemistry equations, and code files, translating only comments. The model is engineered to respect different contexts, formats, styles (formal/informal), and ensure inclusivity, such as appropriate gender attribution. The maximum context length supported is 8k tokens. The 22 supported Indian languages include Assamese, Bengali, Bodo, Dogri, Gujarati, English, Hindi, Kannada, Kashmiri, Konkani, Maithili, Malayalam, Manipuri, Marathi, Nepali, Odia, Punjabi, Sanskrit, Santali, Sindhi, Tamil, Telugu, and Urdu.

instruction: Could you add a pair of sunglasses to the person in the video and make it look natural?
description: Lucy Edit 1.1 Dev is an open-weight video editing model that performs instruction-guided edits on videos using free-text prompts. It supports a variety of edits, such as clothing and accessory changes, character changes, object insertions, and scene replacements, while preserving the motion and composition of the original video. Built on the Wan2.2 5B architecture, it inherits a high-compression VAE and DiT stack, making it easier to adapt existing scripts and workflows. The model preserves motion and composition perfectly, allowing for precise edits. It offers improved edit reliability compared to common inference time methods. It supports wardrobe and accessory changes, such as changing outfits and adding items like glasses, earrings, and hats. It can also perform character changes, replacing characters with monsters, animals, or known characters. It supports scene replacements, such as transforming a scene into a 2D cartoon. It accepts pure text instructions without requiring finetuning or masks for common edits. It can perform global edits to the background and style, maintaining subject identity. It provides seamless blending with cleaner boundaries and more coherent lighting across edits. It supports the insertion of general objects with improved spatial grounding. The model has approximately 5 billion parameters and is built on top of Wan2.2 5B. Version 1.1 includes better overall quality, higher fidelity details, stronger prompt adherence, and improved identity preservation. It offers more reliable global edits with better subject preservation and control. It provides better blending and seamless edits with cleaner boundaries, fewer artifacts, and improved color and lighting consistency across frames. It supports the addition of general objects, including wearable, handheld, and general scene objects, with improved spatial grounding, occlusion, and shadows. Lucy Edit 1.1 is designed for precise, realistic, and identity-preserving video edits. Prompts with 20–30 descriptive words work best, and using the right trigger words helps the model understand the intent. Supported edit types include clothing changes, human or character replacement, object replacement, color changes, adding objects, and global transformations. The model excels at identity conservation, edit precision, realism, and prompt adherence. Longer prompts describing style, appearance, and context improve results. Frame counts of 81 produce better temporal consistency than shorter clips. Version 1.1 improves boundary blending and temporal coherence across edits. The model is available via a hosted API and is compatible with the Diffusers pipeline. Future integrations include ComfyUI custom nodes and local inference nodes. The model is based on the Wan2.2 5B architecture and acknowledges contributions from the open-source community, including diffusers and Hugging Face.

instruction: Can you describe the emotional tone of this music based on its tempo and instrumentation?
description: Music Flamingo (MF) is a fully open, state-of-the-art Large Audio-Language Model (LALM) designed to advance music (including song) understanding in foundational audio models. It brings together innovations in deep music understanding across songs and instrumentals, rich, theory-aware captions and question answering (harmony, structure, timbre, lyrics, cultural context), reasoning-centric training using chain-of-thought and reinforcement learning with custom rewards for step-by-step reasoning, and long-form song reasoning over full-length, multicultural audio (extended context). Extensive evaluations confirm Music Flamingo's effectiveness, setting new benchmarks on over 10+ public music understanding and reasoning tasks. The model is intended for non-commercial research purposes only. Music Flamingo is supported in HuggingFace Transformers and processes audio in 30-second windows with a 10-minute total cap per sample. Longer inputs are truncated. It supports single-turn audio and text instruction, batch multiple conversations, text-only and audio-only prompts, and training or fine-tuning. The model is based on a Transformer architecture with a decoder-only LLM backbone (Qwen2.5-7B), an AF-Whisper unified audio encoder from Audio Flamingo 3, and an MLP-based audio adaptor. It has 8 billion parameters. The model accepts music (song or instrumental) and text as input, with audio in WAV/MP3/FLAC formats and text in UTF-8. The maximum audio length is 20 minutes, and the maximum text length is 24,000 tokens. The output is text (and optional speech) in UTF-8 string format, with a maximum text length of 2048 tokens. The model is designed and optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems (A100/H100), leveraging NVIDIA’s hardware and software frameworks for faster training and inference. It is compatible with PyTorch and HuggingFace Transformers and supports NVIDIA Ampere (A100) and Hopper (H100) hardware on Linux. The model was trained entirely on music data collected from various sources, with training data size ranging from 10,000 to 1 million hours. The data collection method for training data is human, with labeling methods including both human and automated. The model is evaluated on test splits of datasets with human data collection and labeling methods. The model version is v1.0. Ethical considerations include the belief in Trustworthy AI and the responsibility of developers to ensure the model meets requirements for the relevant industry and use case and addresses unforeseen product misuse. The model is built with Audio Flamingo 3, Qwen, NVILA, and the open audio-ML community.

instruction: Create a whimsical anime character with floating flowers in their hair and a cheerful grin.
description: This is a fine-tuned diffusion model for generating high-quality anime faces using a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM). It is based on Google's pre-trained `ddpm-celebahq-256` model and was fine-tuned on 7,000+ high-quality anime face images. The model is designed for unconditional image generation of 256×256 anime faces. It uses the Diffusers framework and is licensed under the MIT license. Training parameters include a learning rate of 2e-5, 15 epochs, a batch size of 4, and gradient accumulation steps of 2, resulting in approximately 26,250 training steps. The AdamW optimizer and Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss were used during training. The final training loss was approximately 0.0077. Inference speed ranges from 30 to 60 seconds per image, depending on the number of inference steps. It is recommended to use 100 inference steps for the best quality. The model can generate a wide diversity of anime faces with various hair colors and styles, eye colors and expressions, face shapes and features, and skin tones. However, the model is limited to 256×256 resolution and is specifically trained on anime faces, so it may not generate realistic or photorealistic faces. The diversity of generated faces is limited to the patterns present in the training data, and the clarity of face shapes depends on the number of inference steps used. The model was fine-tuned from the pre-trained `ddpm-celebahq-256` model using a low learning rate to preserve general face generation knowledge while adapting to anime-specific features such as large eyes and stylized features. The training data was sourced from the Anime Face Dataset (Kaggle), consisting of 7,000 images selected by file size. The images were preprocessed by resizing to 256×256, applying random horizontal flips with 50% probability, and normalizing to the range [-1, 1]. During training, the model adapted from photorealistic to anime style in the first 3 epochs, solidified anime features in epochs 4 to 8, and showed marginal improvements with a risk of overfitting in later epochs. Ethical considerations include the model's use for generating synthetic anime faces, and it should not be used to create misleading or deceptive content, generate non-consensual images of real people, or violate any local laws or regulations. If used in research or projects, credit should be given to the original DDPM paper, Google's pre-trained `ddpm-celebahq-256` model, and this fine-tuned adaptation. Potential future improvements include higher resolution, conditional generation for text-to-image anime faces, better diversity through larger training datasets, and improved training with advanced schedulers or techniques.

instruction: Please determine the category for a text that discusses climate change and its effects on farming.
description: This is an xlm-roberta-large model that has been fine-tuned on English training data labeled with 18 media codes. The media codes include categories such as State and Local Government Administration, Weather, Fires, emergencies and natural disasters, Crime and trials, Arts, culture, entertainment and history, Style and fashion, Food, Travel, Wellbeing and learning, Personal finance and real estate, Personal technology and popular science, Churches and Religion, Celebrities and human interest, Obituaries and death notices, Sports, Crosswords, puzzles, comics, Media production/internal, letters, and Advertisements. The model is designed for text classification tasks and is used by an open-source and free natural language processing tool intended to simplify and speed up projects for comparative research. The model was evaluated on a test set of 67,681 English examples, achieving an accuracy, precision, recall, and weighted average F1-score of 0.8. Performance metrics for individual labels include precision, recall, and F1-score values ranging from 0 to 1, with specific values provided for each of the 18 media codes and additional categories. The model requires a token for access due to gated access restrictions, and users must pass the token parameter when loading the model. In earlier versions of the Transformers package, the use_auth_token parameter may be required instead. The model uses the sentencepiece tokenizer, and users running the model before transformers version 4.27 may need to install it manually. If a RuntimeError occurs when loading the model using the from_pretrained() method, adding ignore_mismatched_sizes=True should resolve the issue. Model performance can be improved by extending the training sets, and contributions of CAP-coded corpora in any domain and language are welcomed at poltextlab@poltextlab.com.

instruction: Can you add a reflection of the store sign in the puddle on the ground in this photo?
description: Qwen-Image-Edit is an image editing model built upon the 20B Qwen-Image model. It extends Qwen-Image's text rendering capabilities to support image editing tasks, enabling precise text editing. The model simultaneously processes input images through Qwen2.5-VL for visual semantic control and the VAE Encoder for visual appearance control, allowing both semantic and appearance editing. Semantic editing includes tasks such as IP creation, object rotation, and style transfer, where the overall pixel content may change while maintaining semantic consistency. Appearance editing involves low-level modifications such as adding, removing, or modifying specific elements while keeping other regions unchanged. Qwen-Image-Edit supports bilingual (Chinese and English) text editing, allowing direct addition, deletion, and modification of text in images while preserving the original font, size, and style. Evaluations on multiple public benchmarks show that Qwen-Image-Edit achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in image editing tasks, making it a powerful foundation model for the field. The model is capable of tasks such as novel view synthesis, object rotation, style transfer, and detailed appearance modifications, including the addition of signboards with reflections and the removal of fine hair strands. It also supports chained editing approaches for progressive correction of errors in generated content, such as calligraphy artworks. Qwen-Image-Edit is licensed under Apache 2.0.

instruction: What is the relationship between 'She is a teacher' and 'She gives lessons every day'?
description: This model is ModernBERT multi-task fine-tuned on tasksource NLI tasks, including MNLI, ANLI, SICK, WANLI, doc-nli, LingNLI, FOLIO, FOL-NLI, LogicNLI, Label-NLI, and all datasets in the referenced table. It is equivalent to an 'instruct' version and was trained for 200k steps on an Nvidia A30 GPU. The model is very good at reasoning tasks, particularly outperforming llama 3.1 8B Instruct on ANLI and FOLIO, as well as long context reasoning, sentiment analysis, and zero-shot classification with new labels. The model is a single transformer with different classification heads on top, achieving high test accuracy across a wide range of NLI and related tasks. Further performance gains can be achieved by fine-tuning on a single task, such as SST, but this checkpoint is optimized for zero-shot classification and natural language inference, including contradiction, entailment, and neutral classification. The model supports zero-shot classification, natural language inference, and can serve as a backbone for further fine-tuning. It has stronger reasoning and fine-grained abilities than the base version of ModernBERT. The model is described in the paper 'tasksource: A Large Collection of NLP tasks with a Structured Dataset Preprocessing Framework' by Damien Sileo, presented at the 2024 Joint International Conference on Computational Linguistics, Language Resources and Evaluation (LREC-COLING 2024).

instruction: Make a clip of a wave crashing onto a rocky beach with foam and spray.
description: The FastWan2.1-T2V-1.3B-Diffusers model is part of the FastWan2.1 series, which is finetuned using a novel Sparse-distill strategy. This strategy combines DMD and VSA in a single training process, leveraging distillation to reduce diffusion steps and sparse attention to minimize attention computations, resulting in faster video generation. The model is based on Wan-AI/Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B-Diffusers and supports efficient 3-step inference, producing high-quality videos at a resolution of 61×448×832. It is trained on the FastVideo 480P Synthetic Wan dataset, which contains 600k synthetic latents. The model can achieve up to 16 FPS on a single H100 GPU. While it is trained on 61×448×832 resolution, it can generate videos at any resolution, though quality may degrade. The model is trained on 4 nodes with 32 H200 GPUs in total, using a global batch size of 64, gradient checkpointing, gradient accumulation steps of 2, and a learning rate of 1e-5. The VSA attention sparsity is set to 0.8, and training runs for 4000 steps, which takes approximately 12 hours. The model supports a wide range of GPUs from H100 to 4090 and is also compatible with Mac users. If the FastWan2.1-T2V-1.3B-Diffusers model is used for research, the associated papers should be cited.

instruction: I want to analyze the sentiment of this audio clip—can you help?
description: Ultravox is a multimodal Speech LLM that can consume both speech and text as input, such as a text system prompt and a voice user message. The input to the model is given as a text prompt with a special pseudo-token, and the model processor will replace this token with embeddings derived from the input audio. Using the merged embeddings as input, the model will then generate output text as usual. In a future revision of Ultravox, the token vocabulary will be expanded to support the generation of semantic and acoustic audio tokens, which can then be fed to a vocoder to produce voice output. No preference tuning has been applied to this revision of the model. The model uses a pre-trained backbone as well as the encoder part of another model. The multi-modal adapter is trained, the Whisper encoder is fine-tuned, while the Llama model is kept frozen. A knowledge-distillation loss is used where Ultravox is trying to match the logits of the text-based Llama backbone. The training dataset is a mix of ASR datasets, extended with continuations generated by Llama 3.1 8B, and speech translation datasets, which yield a modest improvement in translation evaluations. The model is trained using supervised speech instruction finetuning via knowledge-distillation. The training is performed using BF16 mixed precision training on 8x H100 GPUs. The current version of Ultravox, when invoked with audio content, has a time-to-first-token (TTFT) of approximately 150ms, and a tokens-per-second rate of ~50-100 when using an A100-40GB GPU, all using a Llama 3.1 8B backbone. The model can be used as a voice agent, for speech-to-speech translation, and for the analysis of spoken audio. The model is developed by Fixie.ai and is licensed under the MIT license. Evaluation results show that Ultravox 0.5 8B achieves a BLEU score of 12.99 on covost2 en_ar, 31.54 on covost2 en_ca, 28.70 on covost2 en_de, 40.19 on covost2 es_en, 42.13 on covost2 ru_en, 17.22 on covost2 zh_en, and 66.54 on big bench audio.

instruction: I need to modify this image by adding a border with a custom design—can you assist?
description: Qwen-Image-Edit-2511-Lightning is a collection of optimized models designed for image editing tasks, utilizing step distillation and quantization techniques to achieve high-efficiency inference performance. The model suite includes three core model files, each with distinct characteristics. One model, named Qwen-Image-Edit-2511-Lightning-4steps-V1.0-bf16.safetensors, is a 4-step distilled LoRA model with BF16 precision, offering a lightweight and fast inference option. Another model, Qwen-Image-Edit-2511-Lightning-4steps-V1.0-fp32.safetensors, is also a 4-step distilled LoRA model but with FP32 precision, providing high accuracy. The third model, qwen_image_edit_2511_fp8_e4m3fn_scaled_lightning.safetensors, is an FP8 quantized model with e4m3fn scaled precision, fused with a 4-step distilled LoRA and optimized for low-memory deployment. The models support two mainstream usage frameworks: the Qwen-Image-Lightning framework and the LightX2V lightweight video/image generation inference framework. The LoRA models are optimized through step distillation, reducing the original inference steps to just 4 steps, which results in a speedup of approximately 10 times faster than standard 40-step inference while maintaining image editing quality. Additionally, the FP8 quantized model balances performance and resource efficiency, reducing GPU memory usage by about 50% compared to FP32 while preserving editing fidelity. For technical support, users can open issues in the respective repositories for Qwen framework-specific questions or LightX2V integration questions.

instruction: 이 소설 장면을 읽고 등장인물의 감정 상태를 설명해줘: '그녀는 눈물을 훔치며 방에서 나갔다.'
description: 이 모델은 한국어 텍스트를 6가지 감정인 분노, 불안, 슬픔, 평온, 당황, 기쁨으로 분류하도록 설계되었습니다. `klue/roberta-base` 모델을 기반으로 파인튜닝되었습니다. 감정 분류를 수행하는 데 사용되며, 각 감정에 대해 정밀도, 재현율, F1 점수를 평가했습니다. 분노의 경우 정밀도 0.9801, 재현율 0.9788, F1 점수 0.9795를 기록했으며, 불안은 정밀도 0.9864, 재현율 0.9848, F1 점수 0.9856를 보였습니다. 슬픔의 정밀도는 0.9837, 재현율 0.9854, F1 점수 0.9845이며, 평온의 정밀도는 0.9782, 재현율 0.9750, F1 점수 0.9766입니다. 당황의 정밀도는 0.9607, 재현율 0.9668, F1 점수 0.9652이며, 기쁨의 정밀도는 0.9857, 재현율 0.9886, F1 점수 0.9872입니다. 전체 정확도는 0.9831이며, 매크로 평균 정밀도는 0.9791, 재현율은 0.9804, F1 점수는 0.9798입니다. 가중 평균 정밀도는 0.9831, 재현율은 0.9831, F1 점수는 0.9831입니다.

instruction: What is the most significant event that occurs in the second half of this recorded lecture?
description: The SAGE-MM-Qwen3-VL-4B-SFT from allenai is a 4B-parameter vision-language model fine-tuned from Qwen/Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct. It serves as the core decision-maker in the SAGE (Smart Any-Horizon Agent) system for long video reasoning. The model operates in two stages: Stage-1 analyzes initial sampled frames and metadata to classify queries as either single-turn (immediate answer) or multi-turn (tool-required). Stage-2 iteratively calls tools, processes observations, and refines context until resolution. The model generates JSON-formatted actions for specialized tools, including web-search for external knowledge, transcribe-speech for automatic speech recognition on timestamps, ground-event for temporal localization, extract-video-parts for high-resolution frames or subclips, and analyze for detailed visual breakdown. This enables robust handling of extended videos beyond fixed horizons through dynamic tool orchestration. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 license for research and educational use, in accordance with Ai2 guidelines. It requires the SAGE GitHub runtime for tool execution and parsing and supports GGUF quantizations for efficient deployment. The model excels in video question-answering tasks across domains such as sports, narratives, and events.

instruction: What emotion is likely being felt in the text 'I'm so tired of being ignored.'?
description: The Emotion English DistilRoBERTa-base is a model designed to classify emotions in English text data. It predicts seven emotion classes based on Ekman's 6 basic emotions plus a neutral class: anger, disgust, fear, joy, neutral, sadness, and surprise. The model was trained on a balanced subset of six diverse English text datasets, including data from Twitter, Reddit, student self-reports, and TV dialogues. These datasets collectively provided 2,811 observations per emotion, totaling nearly 20,000 observations. The model was trained on 80% of this data and evaluated on the remaining 20%, achieving an evaluation accuracy of 66%, which is significantly higher than the random-chance baseline of 14%. The model is a fine-tuned checkpoint of a base model, and for a non-distilled version, users are directed to the model card of the original version. The model is intended for use in applications requiring emotion classification, such as analyzing sentiment in social media, news, or other text sources. It can be applied to single text examples or larger datasets, including .csv files, using Hugging Face's pipeline command. The model has been cited in scientific research, including studies on rumor detection, music-to-text synesthesia, and longitudinal analysis of news media sentiment. Users are encouraged to cite the model in their work, with a working paper and BibTeX citation provided for reference. The model's development was supported by Samuel Domdey and others, and users are invited to contact the model's creator for questions or feedback.

instruction: Can you determine the viewpoint from which this photo was taken and explain your reasoning?
description: Qwen3-VL is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced Thinking editions for flexible deployment. Key enhancements include the ability to operate PC and mobile GUIs by recognizing elements, understanding functions, invoking tools, and completing tasks. It can generate Draw.io, HTML, CSS, and JS from images and videos. The model supports advanced spatial perception, including judging object positions, viewpoints, and occlusions, with capabilities for 2D and 3D grounding. It has a native 256K context length, expandable to 1M, enabling it to handle books and hours-long video with full recall and second-level indexing. The model excels in enhanced multimodal reasoning, particularly in STEM and Math, with causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. It features upgraded visual recognition with broader, higher-quality pretraining that can recognize a wide range of subjects, including celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. The model supports expanded OCR in 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare or ancient characters and jargon. It provides text understanding comparable to pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE for full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height, DeepStack for fusing multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment, and Text–Timestamp Alignment for precise, timestamp-grounded event localization to strengthen video temporal modeling. This is the weight repository for the Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Thinking variant.

instruction: I have several paragraphs on machine learning—can you rank them by relevance to the question about neural networks?
description: The Llama Nemotron Reranking 1B model is optimized for providing a logit score that represents how relevant a document(s) is to a given query. The model was fine-tuned for multilingual, cross-lingual text question-answering retrieval, with support for long documents (up to 8192 tokens). This model was evaluated on 26 languages: English, Arabic, Bengali, Chinese, Czech, Danish, Dutch, Finnish, French, German, Hebrew, Hindi, Hungarian, Indonesian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Norwegian, Persian, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Spanish, Swedish, Thai, and Turkish. This model is a component in a text retrieval system to improve the overall accuracy. A text retrieval system often uses an embedding model (dense) or lexical search (sparse) index to return relevant text passages given the input. A reranking model can be used to rerank the potential candidate into a final order. The reranking model has the question-passage pairs as an input and therefore, can process cross attention between the words. It’s not feasible to apply a Ranking model on all documents in the knowledge base, therefore, ranking models are often deployed in combination with embedding models. This model is ready for commercial use. The Llama Nemotron Reranking 1B model is a part of the NeMo Retriever collection of NIM, which provide state-of-the-art, commercially-ready models and microservices, optimized for the lowest latency and highest throughput. It features a production-ready information retrieval pipeline with enterprise support. The models that form the core of this solution have been trained using responsibly selected, auditable data sources. With multiple pre-trained models available as starting points, developers can also readily customize them for their domain-specific use cases, such as information technology, human resource help assistants, and research & development research assistants. We are excited to announce the open sourcing of this commercial embedding model. For users interested in deploying this model in production environments, it is also available via the model API in NVIDIA Inference Microservices (NIM). The Llama Nemotron Reranking 1B model is most suitable for users who want to improve their multilingual retrieval tasks by reranking a set of candidates for a given question. The Llama Nemotron Reranking 1B model is a transformer cross-encoder fine-tuned with contrastive learning. We employ bi-directional attention when fine-tuning for higher accuracy. The last embedding output by the decoder model is used with a mean pooling strategy, and a binary classification head is fine-tuned for the ranking task. Ranking models for text ranking are typically trained as a cross-encoder for sentence classification. This involves predicting the relevancy of a sentence pair (for example, question and chunked passages). The CrossEntropy loss is used to maximize the likelihood of passages containing information to answer the question and minimize the likelihood for (negative) passages that do not contain information to answer the question. We trained the model on public datasets described in the Dataset and Training section. The model takes a pair of texts as input, formatted as a list of text pairs. The input parameters are one-dimensional. The model was trained on question and answering over text documents from multiple languages. It was evaluated to work successfully with up to a sequence length of 8192 tokens. Longer texts are recommended to be either chunked or truncated. The model outputs floats, formatted as a list of floats, with one-dimensional parameters. Each value corresponds to a raw logit.
