Users can choose to apply a Sigmoid activation function to the logits to convert them into probabilities during model usage. The model requires transformers version 4.47.1. The model is compatible with NVIDIA Ampere, NVIDIA Hopper, and NVIDIA Lovelace hardware microarchitectures, and it supports Linux as the operating system. The Llama Nemotron Reranking 1B model is part of the NeMo Retriever collection of NIM and is identified by the short name llama-nemotron-rerank-1b-v2. The model was trained on 800k samples from public datasets. The evaluation datasets are based on three TextQA datasets, the TechQA dataset, MIRACL, MLDR, and MLQA multilingual retrieval datasets, which are all public datasets. The sizes range between 10,000s up to 5M depending on the dataset. The pipeline llama-nemotron-embed-1b-v2 + llama-nemotron-rerank-1b-v2 provides high BEIR+TechQA accuracy with multilingual and crosslingual support. The llama-nemotron-embed-1b-v2 ranking model is 3.5x smaller than the nv-rerankqa-mistral-4b-v3 model. The pipeline achieves an average Recall@5 of 73.64% on the NQ, HotpotQA, FiQA, and TechQA datasets. The model achieves an average Recall@5 of 65.80% on the MIRACL multilingual datasets. The model achieves an average Recall@5 of 86.83% on the MLQA dataset with different languages. The model achieves an average Recall@5 of 70.69% on the MLDR dataset. The model’s max sequence length is 8192. Therefore, the longer text inputs should be truncated. The model does not always guarantee to retrieve the correct passage(s) for a given query. Use of this model is governed by the license. Additional information is available. NVIDIA believes Trustworthy AI is a shared responsibility and has established policies and practices to enable development for a wide array of AI applications. Developers should work with their supporting model team to ensure this model meets requirements for the relevant industry and use case and addresses unforeseen product misuse. For more detailed information on ethical considerations for this model, please see the Explainability, Bias, Safety, and Privacy sections. Please report security vulnerabilities or NVIDIA AI Concerns. The model is intended for passage ranking for question and answer retrieval. It is designed for use by generative AI creators working with conversational AI models—users who want to build a multilingual question and answer application over a large text corpus, leveraging the latest dense retrieval technologies. The model transforms the tokenized input text into a dense vector representation. The model's output is an array of float numbers (dense vector representation for the input text). The model's performance is measured in terms of accuracy, throughput, and latency. The model does not always guarantee to retrieve the correct passage(s) for a given query. The model’s max sequence length is 8192. Therefore, the longer text inputs should be truncated. The model has not been tested to deliver comparable outcomes for any adversely impacted groups. The model has been verified to meet prescribed NVIDIA quality standards. The model does not generate or reverse engineer personally-identifiable information (PII). No personal data was used to create this model. The dataset is reviewed before every release. There is no mechanism in place to honor data subject right of access or deletion of personal data. Personal data was not collected for the development of this model by NVIDIA. Data labeling (annotation, metadata) complies with privacy laws. Data is not compliant with data subject requests for data correction or removal, if such a request was made. The model application is text reranking for retrieval. There is no physical safety impact. Use of this model is governed by the license. Additional information is available. The Principle of least privilege (PoLP) is applied limiting access for dataset generation and model development. Restrictions enforce dataset access during training, and dataset license constraints are adhered to.

instruction: Generate a visual sequence of a beach at sunset with waves gently crashing.
description: The GGUF version of TurboWan2.2-I2V-A14B-720P is provided for use with ComfyUI. This model is part of the TurboDiffusion framework, which accelerates video diffusion models by 100-200 times. The model is described in the research paper titled 'TurboDiffusion: Accelerating Video Diffusion Models by 100-200 Times' by Zhang, Jintao and others, published as an arXiv preprint in 2025. Additional related research includes 'SageAttention: Accurate 8-Bit Attention for Plug-and-play Inference Acceleration' by Zhang, Jintao and others, presented at the International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR) in 2025, and 'SLA: Beyond Sparsity in Diffusion Transformers via Fine-Tunable Sparse-Linear Attention' by Zhang, Jintao and others, published as an arXiv preprint in 2025. Further contributions include 'Large Scale Diffusion Distillation via Score-Regularized Continuous-Time Consistency' by Zheng, Kaiwen and others, published as an arXiv preprint in 2025, and 'Sageattention2: Efficient attention with thorough outlier smoothing and per-thread int4 quantization' by Zhang, Jintao and others, presented at the International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML) in 2025. Additional research includes 'Sageattention2++: A more efficient implementation of sageattention2' by Zhang, Jintao and others, published as an arXiv preprint in 2025, and 'SageAttention3: Microscaling FP4 Attention for Inference and An Exploration of 8-Bit Training' by Zhang, Jintao and others, published as an arXiv preprint in 2025.

instruction: Assess the similarity between this image and the text 'fibroadenoma on ultrasound with well-defined margins'?
description: MedSigLIP is a variant of SigLIP (Sigmoid Loss for Language Image Pre-training) designed to encode medical images and text into a common embedding space. It contains a 400M parameter vision encoder and a 400M parameter text encoder, supporting 448x448 image resolution with up to 64 text tokens. MedSigLIP was trained on a combination of de-identified medical image and text pairs, including chest X-rays, dermatology images, ophthalmology images, histopathology slides, and slices of CT and MRI volumes, along with associated descriptions or reports. This training data was combined with natural (non-medical) image and text pairs to retain the model's ability to parse natural images. MedSigLIP is recommended for medical image interpretation applications that do not require text generation, such as data-efficient classification, zero-shot classification, and semantic image retrieval. For medical applications that require text generation, another model is recommended. MedSigLIP accepts images and text as inputs, producing image embeddings, text embeddings, and similarity scores between the image and text. It was evaluated across a range of medical image modalities, including chest X-ray, pathology, dermatology, and ophthalmology. Key performance metrics include zero-shot AUCs for chest X-ray findings, where MedSigLIP outperformed ELIXR in several categories, and AUCs for dermatology, ophthalmology, and pathology findings, where MedSigLIP showed competitive performance compared to existing HAI-DEF embedding models. MedSigLIP is based on SigLIP-400M and uses the same encoder that powers image interpretation in a generative model. It is intended for use by software developers and researchers to facilitate the creation of healthcare applications involving medical images and text. MedSigLIP is not a finished product and requires adaptation and validation for specific use cases. Developers should consider potential biases in validation data and data contamination concerns when evaluating the model's generalization capabilities. MedSigLIP was trained on diverse public datasets, including MIMIC-CXR, Slake-VQA, PAD-UFES-20, SCIN, TCGA, CAMELYON, PMC-OA, and Mendeley Digital Knee X-Ray, as well as de-identified, licensed datasets or datasets collected internally at Google from consented participants. The model was created on July 9, 2025, and is version 1.0.0. When using this model, please cite the MedGemma Technical Report by Sellergren et al. (2025).

instruction: Create a detailed furniture structure for a hallway storage unit with a width of 80cm.
description: This is a 3D furniture generation model based on a Transformer architecture. It is designed to generate 3D furniture structural data based on text prompts such as 'wardrobe' or 'cabinet'. The model directly produces custom JSON structures containing panel size, position, rotation, and type information, which can be rendered directly in front-end frameworks such as Three.js. The model supports structured output in the form of JSON structures that can be directly parsed. It allows parametric control by supporting the input of furniture type, size, and other parameters. It achieves millimeter-level accuracy in coordinates through normalization training. The model is highly extensible and can be fine-tuned with a user's own furniture dataset. It is compatible with Three.js, enabling the generated furniture models to be rendered directly in the browser. The model is open-sourced on Hugging Face Model Hub and can be downloaded, tested, and fine-tuned. For collaboration or technical inquiries, contact chengyuhan1030@gmail.com.

instruction: Please provide a real-time caption for this video of an animal in motion.
description: CASA-Qwen2_5-VL-3B-LiveCC is a Qwen-2.5VL model adapted from token insertion to a cross-attention-based architecture using CASA layers. It has been further fine-tuned for live video captioning. The model is designed to process video inputs and generate real-time subtitles that are embedded at the timestamp they are generated. It supports tasks involving video frame processing at 2fps and the generation of subtitles in real-time. The model is based on a cross-attention-based architecture and is optimized for efficient vision-language fusion. It has been evaluated on the Animal Kingdom dataset, where it generates descriptive captions for video content. For example, it can describe a fox in the Arctic, noting environmental details and the animal's behavior. The model requires the use of the `transformers` library and specific dependencies such as `ffmpeg`. It is available for use with model weights provided in the associated repository. Users are advised to refer to the repository for detailed instructions on running the model and generating live captions. The model is part of a research project and should be cited in any academic work that uses it.

instruction: Could you assign a classification to these images based on the metadata and features provided?
description: Mitra classifier is a tabular foundation model that is pre-trained on purely synthetic datasets sampled from a mix of random classifiers. It is based on a 12-layer Transformer with 72 million parameters, pre-trained by incorporating an in-context learning paradigm. The model is designed for use in tabular data classification tasks and supports both inference and fine-tuning. For inference, the model can be used without fine-tuning, and for fine-tuning, a specified number of training steps can be applied. The model is implemented within the AutoGluon.tabular framework and can be trained and evaluated using standard machine learning workflows, including train/test splits and performance evaluation. The model is licensed under the Apache-2.0 License. The model's development and methodology are described in the research paper titled 'Mitra: Mixed synthetic priors for enhancing tabular foundation models' by Zhang et al., published as an arXiv preprint in 2025.

instruction: 有没有办法将中文句子编码成向量，以实现跨句子的语义理解？
description: This is a CoSENT (Cosine Sentence) model named shibing624/text2vec-base-chinese. It maps sentences to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for tasks like sentence embeddings, text matching, or semantic search. The model is trained using the CoSENT method based on the hfl/chinese-macbert-base architecture on the Chinese STS-B dataset. It achieves good performance on the Chinese STS-B test set. The model is available on the Hugging Face model hub and is recommended for general Chinese semantic matching tasks. The model outputs a vector that captures semantic information and can be used for information retrieval, clustering, or sentence similarity tasks. Input text longer than 256 word pieces is truncated by default. The model architecture consists of a Transformer with a max sequence length of 128 and a Pooling layer that uses mean token pooling. The model is fine-tuned using a contrastive objective, where cosine similarity is computed for all possible sentence pairs in a batch, and a rank loss is applied by comparing true and false pairs. The training dataset is unspecified, and the maximum sequence length is set to 128. The best performance was achieved at epoch 5, and the sentence embedding dimension is 768. The model can be used with the text2vec library, HuggingFace Transformers, or the sentence-transformers library. It supports acceleration via ONNX and OpenVINO backends, with ONNX providing no performance loss on GPU and OpenVINO providing a 1.12x speedup on CPU. Quantization with OpenVINO (int8) results in a small performance hit on some tasks and a minor gain on others, with performance on CPU also improved. The model is intended for use as a sentence and short paragraph encoder.

instruction: Show me a video of a bustling city street at night with bright lights and moving cars.
description: Mochi 1 is a state-of-the-art video generation model with high-fidelity motion and strong prompt adherence in preliminary evaluation. It is an open-source model that significantly closes the gap between closed and open video generation systems. The model is released under a permissive Apache 2.0 license. Mochi 1 is based on a 10 billion parameter diffusion model built on the Asymmetric Diffusion Transformer (AsymmDiT) architecture. It is the largest video generative model ever openly released and features a simple, hackable design. The model is trained entirely from scratch and includes an efficient context parallel implementation for inference. Mochi 1 uses an AsymmVAE video compression model with an asymmetric encoder-decoder structure to compress videos to a 128x smaller size, using 8x8 spatial and 6x temporal compression to a 12-channel latent space. The AsymmDiT architecture processes user prompts and compressed video tokens by streamlining text processing and focusing neural network capacity on visual reasoning. It jointly attends to text and visual tokens with multi-modal self-attention and uses separate MLP layers for each modality. The visual stream has nearly four times as many parameters as the text stream, with non-square QKV and output projection layers to unify the modalities in self-attention. Mochi 1 encodes prompts using a single T5-XXL language model, rather than multiple pretrained language models. The model supports both multi-GPU and single-GPU operation, with single-GPU operation requiring approximately 60GB VRAM. ComfyUI can optimize the model to run on less than 20GB VRAM, but this implementation prioritizes flexibility over memory efficiency. It is recommended to use at least one H100 GPU when using the repository. Mochi 1 is a research preview and is a living and evolving checkpoint. It currently generates videos at 480p resolution and may exhibit minor warping and distortions in some edge cases with extreme motion. The model is optimized for photorealistic styles and does not perform well with animated content. It is expected that the community will fine-tune the model to suit various aesthetic preferences. Genmo video models are general text-to-video diffusion models that reflect the biases and preconceptions found in their training data. While steps have been taken to limit NSFW content, organizations should implement additional safety protocols and careful consideration before deploying these model weights in any commercial services or products.

instruction: The video I downloaded from the internet is distorted. Is there a way to clean it up?
description: SeedVR2 is a one-step diffusion-based video restoration model designed to perform adversarial video restoration training against real data. It aims to address the challenges of high-resolution video restoration in real-world settings, where existing distillation-based approaches for one-step image restoration have not been effectively extended. SeedVR2 introduces several architectural and training enhancements to handle high-resolution video restoration within a single step. These include an adaptive window attention mechanism, where the window size is dynamically adjusted to fit the output resolution, preventing window inconsistency observed in high-resolution video restoration using fixed window sizes. Additionally, a series of losses are verified for their effectiveness in stabilizing and improving adversarial post-training, including a proposed feature matching loss that does not significantly sacrifice training efficiency. Extensive experiments show that SeedVR2 can achieve comparable or better performance than existing video restoration approaches in a single step. However, the model has some limitations: it may not be perfectly aligned with the paper due to being a prototype, and it is not always robust to heavy degradations or very large motions. It shares some failure cases with existing methods, such as failing to fully remove degradation or generating unpleasing details. Moreover, due to its strong generation ability, it may occasionally oversharpen results on inputs with very light degradations, such as 720p AIGC videos.

instruction: I have a reference image of a logo—can you create a 3D version for a display?
description: TripoSR is a fast and feed-forward 3D generative model developed in collaboration between Stability AI and Tripo AI. The model incorporates a series of technical advancements over the LRM model in terms of both data curation as well as model and training improvements. We closely follow the network architecture for the model design. The model type is feed-forward 3D reconstruction from a single image, and it is available under the MIT license. We train TripoSR for 5 days on 22 GPU nodes each with 8 A100 40GB GPUs. We use renders from the dataset, utilizing our enhanced rendering method that more closely replicate the distribution of images found in the real world, significantly improving our model’s ability to generalize. We selected a carefully curated subset of the Objaverse dataset for the training data, which is available under the CC-BY license. The model should not be used to intentionally create or disseminate 3D models that people would foreseeably find disturbing, distressing, or offensive; or content that propagates historical or current stereotypes.

instruction: Can you check if this video clip shows any aggressive or harmful behavior?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of the VideoMAE architecture for binary violence detection in video content. It classifies videos into two categories: Violent Crime (1) and Non-Violent Incident (0). The model was trained on a curated subset of the UCF Crime dataset with event-based categorization, consisting of 300 videos, equally split between violent and non-violent categories. The event distribution includes 34 videos of abuse, 36 of arrest, 46 of arson, 36 of assault, 70 of burglary, 24 of explosion, 30 of fighting, 86 of road accidents, 98 of robbery, 36 of shoplifting, and 62 of stealing. The dataset was split into 192 training videos, 48 validation videos, and 60 test videos. The model achieved a validation accuracy of 0.6250, precision of 0.6351, recall of 0.6250, and F1 score of 0.6114. On the test set, it achieved an accuracy of 0.6667, with equal precision, recall, and F1 score of 0.6667. The model uses a VideoMAE Base architecture with a fine-tuning approach for event-based binary classification. Training was conducted for 10 epochs with a learning rate of 5e-05, a batch size of 2, and AdamW optimizer with betas (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon 1e-08. The model processes 16 frames per video clip at a resolution of 224x224 pixels and outputs binary classification results. It was trained using the HuggingFace Transformers framework with PyTorch 2.0.1+ and is compatible with Apple Silicon MPS, CUDA, or CPU. The model has several limitations, including potential generalization issues due to dataset scope, possible false positives with non-violent but intense activities, and environmental bias from lighting, camera angles, and video quality. It is intended for research, security system enhancement with human oversight, and educational purposes. Prohibited uses include unauthorized surveillance, discriminatory profiling, automated punishment, and privacy violations. The model was developed by a research team and is available for use under the HuggingFace Transformers framework.

instruction: Visualize a motion where a person is doing a cartwheel followed by a handstand.
description: Semantic Motion Generation (SMoG) is a state-of-the-art text-to-motion synthesis model that leverages CLIP semantics and Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN)-enhanced transformers to generate realistic, diverse 3D human motions from textual descriptions. Unlike conventional approaches, SMoG replaces linear layers in transformer architectures with KANLayers, enabling adaptive nonlinear feature learning and superior motion-text alignment. It generates the sequence of SMPL body model movement for each frame. SMoG is built on MotionCLIP — a 3D motion autoencoder trained to reconstruct poses using natural language. It employs a latent space representing abstract, compressed data features non-trivially present in the input space. This approach reduces reliance on classical data labeling by using contrastive learning to distinguish similarity, identity, or difference between text-motion pairs. During training, action-text pairs are matched for similarity (positive) or dissimilarity (negative), maximizing positive pair similarity and minimizing negative pair similarity. The latent space enables semantic alignment between input text and generated motion, producing meaningful interpretations. For example, inputting 'wings' or a cultural reference could generate a wing-like arm motion without explicit training examples. The model is trained not only to reconstruct motions but also to align their latent representations with text labels in CLIP space. The KANLayer, core to Kolmogorov–Arnold Networks (KAN), implements a hybrid function approximation approach based on the Kolmogorov-Arnold theorem. This theorem states that any multivariate continuous function can be represented as a superposition of univariate functions, achieved in KANLayer via linear transformations and nonlinear B-spline components. Structurally, the layer has two parallel branches: a base branch performing linear transformation with activation (default: SiLU), and a spline branch adding nonlinearity through adaptive B-splines. The spline branch operates on coefficients 'spline_weight', computed via B-spline interpolation on a fixed grid dividing the input range (e.g., [-1, 1]) into grid_size intervals. The spline order ('spline_order') determines smoothness and complexity. The 'b_splines' method implements the recursive Cox-de Boor formula for basis functions. Spline weight initialization includes noise addition (scale_noise) and least-squares solving in 'curve2coeff' to approximate initial weights. The 'enable_standalone_scale_spline' parameter allows independent scaling of spline coefficients via 'spline_scaler' for training stability. During the forward pass, inputs are processed by both branches: the base branch generates linear output via activation and matrix multiplication, while the spline branch computes nonlinear offsets via B-spline convolution with trainable weights. Outputs are summed to form the final tensor. The model supports multi-scenario motion generation (e.g., dances, sports, daily activities) from free-form text prompts. It supports both CPU and GPU inference, though GPU is significantly faster. The model is trained on the AMASS dataset, which aggregates motion capture data from academic sources to cover diverse actions—from walking to complex gestures. The dataset records 3D joint coordinates using advanced motion-tracking technologies, preserving temporal sequences where each row represents a timestamp and columns store detailed x, y, z coordinates for body joints, formatted in npz. Notably, AMASS integrates action labels, anthropometric parameters, and synchronized sensor data, enabling detailed biomechanical studies and training deep learning models (e.g., LSTMs, Transformers) for 3D mesh pose prediction. A key feature is its scalability, encompassing tens of thousands of motions. AMASS is licensed for academic research, permitting non-commercial use with attribution, while commercial applications require explicit permission. The dataset is accompanied by visualization tools and code examples, allowing researchers to utilize its potential while preserving participant anonymity. The model's performance is evaluated using several metrics, including R@1, R@5, MMD, and FCD. SMoG achieves R@1 of 0.0004158, R@5 of 0.006237, MMD of 0.020108, and FCD of 0.50885. Additional loss metrics include Vel-Loss of 0.00029719, Text Cosine of 0.27195, rcxyz of 0.00066315, rc of 0.0014857, Mixed without CLIP of 0.24461, Mixed with CLIP of 0.73316, Mixed CLIP only of 0.48855, and Image Cosine of 0.2166. The model is implemented in PyTorch and requires specific dependencies, including CLIP, SMPL, SMPL-X, PyTorch3D, and MotionCLIP. The code is distributed under a license that must be followed, and the model depends on other libraries, each with their own respective licenses. The model is trained using a combination of losses, including velocity loss, text cosine loss, rcxyz loss, rc loss, and mixed losses with and without CLIP. The model is evaluated using a script that logs metrics with wandb. The model is trained on a dataset that includes SMPL and SMPL+H model files, and the training process involves a main training loop with fine-tuning options. The model is evaluated using a script that generates metrics and logs them with wandb. instruction: Design an abstract composition with geometric shapes and vibrant gradients.
description: The FLUX.2 [klein] model family are the fastest image models to date. FLUX.2 [klein] unifies generation and editing in a single compact architecture, delivering state-of-the-art quality with end-to-end inference in as low as under a second. It is built for applications that require real-time image generation without sacrificing quality and can run on consumer hardware with as little as 13GB VRAM. FLUX.2 [klein] 4B Base is a 4 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating images from text descriptions and supports multi-reference editing capabilities. It is a full-capacity foundation model, undistilled, preserving complete training signal for maximum flexibility. It is ideal for fine-tuning, LoRA training, research, and custom pipelines where control matters more than speed. It offers higher output diversity than the distilled models. This repository holds an FP8 version of FLUX.2 [klein] Base 4B. The main repository of this model (full BF16 weights) can be found separately. The model has an exceptional speed and quality-to-size ratio and is ideal for local deployment and fine-tuning on limited hardware. It was trained without step or guidance distillation, making it more efficient and flexible. It has open weights for customization and fine-tuning to drive science, research, and empower artists to iterate with speed. Its outputs can be used for commercial purposes as described in the relevant documentation. A reference implementation of FLUX.2 [klein] 4B Base, as well as sampling code, is provided in a dedicated repository. Developers and creatives looking to build on top of FLUX.2 [klein] 4B Base are encouraged to use this as a starting point. The model is also available in both FP8 and BF16 versions. The FLUX.2 [klein] 4B Base model fits in approximately 13GB VRAM and is accessible on NVIDIA RTX 3090/4070 and above. The model is not intended or able to provide factual information. While the model can output text, the text rendered may be inaccurate or subject to distortion. As a statistical model, this checkpoint may represent or amplify biases observed in the training data. The model may fail to generate output that matches the prompts. Prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting style. The model and its derivatives may not be used in any way that violates applicable law, for the purpose of exploiting, harming or attempting to exploit or harm minors in any way, including but not limited to the solicitation, creation, acquisition, or dissemination of child exploitative content, to generate or disseminate deceptive, fraudulent, misleading or otherwise harmful content, to generate or disseminate personal identifiable information that can be used to harm an individual, to harass, abuse, threaten, stalk, or bully individuals or groups of individuals, to create non-consensual intimate imagery or illegal pornographic content, or for fully automated decision making or high risk applications that adversely impact an individual's legal rights or otherwise create or modify a binding, enforceable obligation. Nothing contained in this Model Card should be interpreted as or deemed a restriction or modification to the license the model is released under. Black Forest Labs is committed to the responsible development and deployment of its models. Prior to releasing the FLUX.2 family of models, the company evaluated and mitigated a number of risks in its model checkpoints and hosted services, including the generation of unlawful content, including child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and nonconsensual intimate imagery (NCII). Pre-training mitigation included filtering pre-training data for multiple categories of 'not safe for work' (NSFW) and known CSAM to help prevent a user from generating unlawful content in response to text prompts or uploaded images. The company partnered with an independent nonprofit organization dedicated to preventing online abuse to filter known CSAM from the training data. Post-training mitigation included multiple rounds of targeted fine-tuning to provide additional mitigation against potential abuse, including both text-to-image (T2I) and image-to-image (I2I) attacks. By inhibiting certain behaviors and suppressing certain concepts in the trained model, these techniques can help to prevent a user from generating synthetic CSAM or NCII from a text prompt, or transforming an uploaded image into synthetic CSAM or NCII. Ongoing evaluation was conducted throughout the process, including multiple internal and external third-party evaluations of model checkpoints to identify further opportunities for mitigation. External third-party evaluations focused on eliciting CSAM and NCII through adversarial testing with (i) text-only prompts, (ii) a single uploaded reference image with text prompts, and (iii) multiple uploaded reference images with text prompts. Based on this feedback, further safety fine-tuning was conducted to produce the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] models. The release decision was made after safety fine-tuning and prior to release, a final third-party evaluation of the proposed release checkpoints was conducted, focused on T2I and I2I generation of synthetic CSAM and NCII, including a comparison with other open-weight T2I and I2I models. The final FLUX.2 [klein] checkpoints demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs in complex generation and editing tasks and demonstrated higher resilience than leading open-weight models across these risk categories. Based on these findings, the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models were approved for release under an Apache 2.0 license and the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models under a non-commercial license to support third-party research and development. Inference filters for NSFW and protected content in inputs and outputs are included in the repository for the FLUX.2 [klein] models. Filters or manual review must be used with the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models under the terms of the FLUX Non-Commercial License, and deployers are encouraged to implement these mitigations when using the FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models. Where these features are implemented on the company's hosted services, multiple filters may be applied to intercept text prompts, uploaded images, and output images. Both in-house and third-party filters are used to mitigate against harmful outputs, such as CSAM and NCII outputs, including filters provided by external organizations. Content provenance features can help users and platforms better identify, label, and interpret AI-generated content online. The inference code for FLUX.2 [klein] implements an example of pixel-layer watermarking. Additionally, the repository includes links to a standard for metadata. The API for FLUX.2 [klein] applies cryptographically-signed C2PA metadata to downloaded output content to indicate that images were produced with the model. Acceptable use of the models and access to the API are governed by policies set out in applicable documentation, including the FLUX Non-Commercial License (for non-commercial open-weight users), Developer Terms of Service, Self-Hosted Commercial License Terms, and Usage Policy (for commercial open-weight model users), and Developer Terms of Service, FLUX API Service Terms, and Usage Policy (for API users). These prohibit the generation of unlawful content or the use of generated content for unlawful, defamatory, or abusive purposes. Developers and users must consent to these conditions to access the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models on Hugging Face. Black Forest Labs takes model safety seriously and provides a dedicated email address (safety@blackforestlabs.ai) to solicit feedback from the community. The company maintains a reporting relationship with organizations such as the and the and welcomes ongoing engagement with authorities, developers, and researchers to share intelligence about emerging risks and develop effective mitigations. The model is licensed under the specified license. This project may contain trademarks or logos for projects, products, or services. Use of Black Forest Labs and FLUX trademarks or logos in modified versions of this project must not cause confusion or imply sponsorship or endorsement. Any use of third-party trademarks, intellectual property or logos is subject to those third-party's policies.

instruction: List all the pharmaceutical substances in this section of the medical journal.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition, specifically for identifying chemical entities from the BC5CDR dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated BC5CDR_CHEM dataset, which contains 1,500 PubMed abstracts with 4,409 annotated chemical entities. The model supports the classification of two entity types: B-CHEM and I-CHEM. It is intended for use in applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the DeBERTa-v3-large architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It has been validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.96, precision of 0.95, recall of 0.97, and accuracy of 0.99. It is ranked among the top-performing models on the BC5CDR_CHEM dataset, with an F1 score of 0.9614. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and academic settings. It is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and supporting biomedical NLP research. If used in research or applications, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: Could you create an embedding for this image to compare with others in a dataset?
description: The vit_huge_plus_patch16_dinov3.lvd1689m is a DINOv3 Vision Transformer (ViT) model designed as an image feature encoder. It is distilled on the LVD-1689M dataset from the DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model is intended for tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. It has 840.5 million parameters, 224.9 GMACs, and 193.6 million activations, with an input image size of 256 x 256. The model uses a ViT architecture and is trained using the DINOv3 method. The original model weights have all QKV projection biases set to zero, and for the `timm` implementation, the QKV bias is disabled (`qkv_bias=False`) for these models. Some variants have `qkvb` in the name with `qkv_bias=True` but zero values to align with the `transformers` library and original models. The original models store RoPE periods as a persistent `bfloat16` buffer, while `timm` generates `float32` periods at initialization, which may result in some numerical differences but is less problematic on devices without bfloat16 support and appears to work as well or slightly better for fine-tuning. The model is evaluated on various benchmarks, including IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair, with performance metrics such as 90.3 IN-ReaL, 90.0 IN-R, 78.6 Obj.Net, 64.5 Ox.-H, 54.8 ADE20k, 0.352 NYU, 79.3 DAVIS, 63.3 NAVI, and 56.3 SPair. It is also evaluated on GEO-Bench for satellite imagery tasks, including classification and segmentation, with metrics such as 73.0 m-BEnet, 96.5 m-brick-kiln, 94.1 m-eurosat, 60.6 m-forestnet, 96.0 m-pv4ger, 57.4 m-so2sat, and 79.6 mean for classification, and 94.2 m-cashew, 75.6 m-chesapeake, 61.8 m-NeonTree, 83.7 m-nz-cattle, 95.2 m-pv4ger-seg, 36.8 m-SA-crop, and 74.5 mean for segmentation. The model is associated with the DINOv3 paper, the ViT paper, and the PyTorch Image Models repository.

instruction: Can you generate a video of a speaker using only an audio file and a reference photo, with synchronized expressions?
description: The Wan2.2-S2V-14B model is designed for audio-driven cinematic video generation. It was introduced in the Wan-S2V paper, which addresses the challenge of achieving film-level character animation by proposing an audio-driven model built upon Wan. The model significantly enhances expressiveness and fidelity in cinematic contexts compared to existing approaches. It outperforms state-of-the-art models such as Hunyuan-Avatar and Omnihuman in extensive experiments. The model supports applications in long-form video generation and precise video lip-sync editing. Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to the foundational video models, incorporating a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture into video diffusion models. This architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity while maintaining the same computational cost. Wan2.2 also incorporates meticulously curated aesthetic data with detailed labels for lighting, composition, contrast, and color tone, enabling more precise and controllable cinematic style generation. Compared to Wan2.1, Wan2.2 is trained on a significantly larger dataset, with 65.6% more images and 83.2% more videos, enhancing generalization across multiple dimensions such as motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among all open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 includes a 5B model with a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE that achieves a compression ratio of 16×16×4. This model supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps and can run on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090. It is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available, suitable for both industrial and academic use. The Wan2.2-S2V-14B model supports video generation at 480P and 720P resolutions. It can generate videos from audio input combined with a reference image and optional text prompt. The model also supports pose-driven generation, allowing it to follow specific pose sequences while generating videos synchronized with audio input. The model can be run on a single GPU with at least 80GB VRAM or using multi-GPU inference with FSDP and DeepSpeed Ulysses. The MoE architecture in Wan2.2 uses a two-expert design tailored to the denoising process of diffusion models: a high-noise expert for early stages and a low-noise expert for later stages. Each expert model has about 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active parameters per step, keeping inference computation and GPU memory nearly unchanged. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a threshold step defined for switching between the high-noise and low-noise experts. The effectiveness of the MoE architecture is validated through comparisons with baseline and other MoE-based variants, showing that the final version achieves the lowest validation loss. Wan2.2 also explores a high-compression design with a 5B dense model, TI2V-5B, supported by a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE. This model achieves a T×H×W compression ratio of 4×16×16, increasing the overall compression rate to 64 while maintaining high-quality video reconstruction. With an additional patchification layer, the total compression ratio of TI2V-5B reaches 4×32×32. TI2V-5B can generate a 5-second 720P video in under 9 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU, ranking among the fastest 720P@24fps video generation models. It natively supports both text-to-video and image-to-video tasks within a single unified framework. Wan2.2 is compared with leading closed-source commercial models on the Wan-Bench 2.0, demonstrating superior performance across multiple crucial dimensions. The models are licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, granting users the freedom to use the generated content while ensuring compliance with the license provisions. Users are fully accountable for their use of the models, which must not involve sharing any content that violates applicable laws, causes harm to individuals or groups, disseminates personal information intended for harm, spreads misinformation, or targets vulnerable populations.

instruction: What was the unemployment rate in the United States in May 2024?
description: This model is a TAPAS medium model fine-tuned on WikiTable Questions (WTQ). It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_medium_reset` checkpoint, and the non-default version corresponds to `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_medium`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional step of intermediate pre-training, followed by fine-tuning on SQA, WikiSQL, and finally WTQ. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table. The non-default version uses absolute position embeddings instead. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised fashion. It was pre-trained with two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table using a balanced dataset of syntactically created examples. This allows the model to learn a bidirectional representation of tables and associated texts. The model can be used for tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining whether a sentence is entailed or refuted by the contents of a table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head and aggregation head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training these with the base model. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 50,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 512. The optimizer used is Adam with a learning rate of 1.93581e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.128960. An inductive bias is added to ensure the model only selects cells of the same column. The model's performance on the development set for the medium size with reset position embeddings is 0.4324 accuracy. The model is intended for answering questions related to tables. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model, and this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: I need this English script translated into a natural-sounding version in Hindi.
description: Hunyuan-MT-Chimera-7B-fp8 is a translation model developed by Tencent. The Hunyuan Translation Model includes a translation model, Hunyuan-MT-7B, and an ensemble model, Hunyuan-MT-Chimera. The translation model is used to translate source text into the target language, while the ensemble model integrates multiple translation outputs to produce a higher-quality result. It primarily supports mutual translation among 33 languages, including five ethnic minority languages in China. In the WMT25 competition, the model achieved first place in 30 out of the 31 language categories it participated in. Hunyuan-MT-7B achieves industry-leading performance among models of comparable scale. Hunyuan-MT-Chimera-7B is the industry’s first open-source translation ensemble model, elevating translation quality to a new level. A comprehensive training framework for translation models has been proposed, spanning from pretrain → cross-lingual pretraining (CPT) → supervised fine-tuning (SFT) → translation enhancement → ensemble refinement, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) results for models of similar size. The model supports translation between Chinese and other languages, as well as among multiple other language pairs. The model is available in both standard and fp8 quantized versions. The model can be used with the transformers library, and specific parameters are recommended for inference, including top_k, top_p, repetition_penalty, and temperature. The model does not have a default system_prompt. The model is open-sourced on Hugging Face.

instruction: Is it possible to encode a voice recording in real-time for streaming?
description: Mimi codec is a state-of-the-art audio neural codec that combines semantic and acoustic information into audio tokens running at 12.5Hz and a bitrate of 1.1kbps. It is a high-fidelity audio codec leveraging neural networks, introducing a streaming encoder-decoder architecture with quantized latent space, trained in an end-to-end fashion. It was trained on speech data, which makes it particularly adapted to train speech language models or text-to-speech systems. Mimi can be used directly as an audio codec for real-time compression and decompression of speech signals, providing high-quality audio compression and efficient decoding. The model is not intended to be used to impersonate other people or for any malicious use of any kind. The model has been trained with a few safeguards to try to limit potential toxic usages, however its toxicity analysis shows that it behaves in the middle of existing models with respect to textual generation. It has some bias towards certain domains and topics that are over-represented in the training data. Its capabilities are relatively limited so far and it is trained to produce only one voice to avoid impersonation. Yet, we need the perspective in time to establish the sociotechnical limitations.

instruction: I need a 15-second animation of a city skyline at night with moving cars and lights.
description: The GGUF version of TurboWan2.2-I2V-A14B-720P is provided for use with ComfyUI. This model is part of the TurboDiffusion framework, which accelerates video diffusion models by 100-200 times. The model is described in the research paper titled 'TurboDiffusion: Accelerating Video Diffusion Models by 100-200 Times' by Zhang, Jintao and others, published as an arXiv preprint in 2025. Additional related research includes 'SageAttention: Accurate 8-Bit Attention for Plug-and-play Inference Acceleration' by Zhang, Jintao and others, presented at the International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR) in 2025, and 'SLA: Beyond Sparsity in Diffusion Transformers via Fine-Tunable Sparse-Linear Attention' by Zhang, Jintao and others, published as an arXiv preprint in 2025. Further contributions include 'Large Scale Diffusion Distillation via Score-Regularized Continuous-Time Consistency' by Zheng, Kaiwen and others, published as an arXiv preprint in 2025, and 'Sageattention2: Efficient attention with thorough outlier smoothing and per-thread int4 quantization' by Zhang, Jintao and others, presented at the International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML) in 2025. Additional research includes 'Sageattention2++: A more efficient implementation of sageattention2' by Zhang, Jintao and others, published as an arXiv preprint in 2025, and 'SageAttention3: Microscaling FP4 Attention for Inference and An Exploration of 8-Bit Training' by Zhang, Jintao and others, published as an arXiv preprint in 2025.

description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video models, incorporating innovations such as an effective Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video (TI2V) capabilities. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity without increasing computational cost. Wan2.2 includes a high-noise expert for early denoising stages and a low-noise expert for later stages, with each expert having about 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active parameters per step. The model is trained on significantly larger data, with +65.6% more images and +83.2% more videos compared to Wan2.1, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. Wan2.2 also features a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE with a compression ratio of 16×16×4, enabling efficient deployment. The TI2V-5B model supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps and can run on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090. It is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available, suitable for both industrial and academic applications. The model supports single-GPU and multi-GPU inference using FSDP and DeepSpeed Ulysses, and is compatible with the Diffusers library. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license provisions.

instruction: Can you infer any cultural or social context from the content of this image?
description: Next 12B is a 12-billion parameter multimodal Vision-Language Model (VLM) based on Gemma 3, fine-tuned to deliver exceptional performance in both text and image understanding. It is designed for superior understanding and generation of text and image descriptions, advanced reasoning and context-aware multimodal outputs, professional-grade Turkish support with extensive multilingual capabilities, and enterprise-ready deployment with optimized quantization options. The model is ideal for enterprises, researchers, and organizations who need a state-of-the-art multimodal AI capable of complex visual understanding, advanced reasoning, and creative generation. Next 12B approaches frontier model performance while maintaining efficiency, with 91.8% on MMLU benchmark, 81.2% on MATH benchmark, 94.3% on GSM8K, and 78.4% on MMLU-Pro. It supports multiple quantization formats including Q8_0, Q4_K_M, F16, and F32 for flexible deployment options. The model is built on a Transformer-based architecture with a causal LLM and an enhanced vision encoder, and it is fine-tuned using advanced instruction and multimodal fine-tuning (SFT) on curated Turkish and multilingual datasets. It supports both text and image modalities and is suitable for use cases such as advanced image captioning, multimodal question answering, text generation, complex reasoning, creative storytelling, and enterprise applications. The model is open-source and licensed under the MIT License, allowing free use, modification, and distribution for commercial and non-commercial purposes.

instruction: Can you determine the emotional positivity or negativity of this short audio?
description: This model implements dimensional emotion classification based on the approach described in the paper 'Vox-Profile: A Speech Foundation Model Benchmark for Characterizing Diverse Speaker and Speech Traits.' The training pipeline is the top-performing solution (SAILER) in the INTERSPEECH 2025—Speech Emotion Challenge. Unlike the official challenge submission, this system does not use transcripts and is designed as a speech-only system to maintain simplicity while ensuring effectiveness. The model is trained on the MSP-Podcast dataset and may be sensitive to content information when making emotion predictions, which could be a beneficial feature for classifying emotions from online content. The model outputs three continuous values in the range of 0 to 1, representing Arousal (0 calm, 1 active), Valence (0 negative, 1 positive), and Dominance (0 weak, 1 strong). The model is based on the Whisper-large v3 architecture. The model is intended for use with audio data that is no longer than 15 seconds, sampled at 16kHz and in mono channel format. The model is released under the Open RAIL license and is not intended for clinical or diagnostic applications, surveillance, privacy-invasive applications, or commercial use. Users are expected to respect the privacy and consent of data subjects and to comply with relevant laws and regulations in their jurisdictions.

description: ColQwen2.5-Omni is a model based on a novel architecture and training strategy derived from Omnimodal Language Models, designed to efficiently index documents using their visual features. It is an extension of Qwen2.5-Omni-3B that generates multi-vector representations of text and images. The model takes dynamic image resolutions as input without resizing, which may alter the aspect ratio, similar to ColPali. The maximum resolution is set to produce at most 1024 image patches, with experiments showing improvements in performance with more patches, though at the cost of increased memory requirements. This version is trained using colpali-engine version 0.3.11 and uses the same data as described in the ColPali paper. The training dataset consists of 127,460 query-page pairs, with 63% from openly available academic datasets and 37% from a synthetic dataset made of web-crawled PDF pages augmented with pseudo-questions generated by a Vision Language Model (VLM). The training set is entirely in English, allowing for the study of zero-shot generalization to non-English languages. No multi-page PDF document is used in both the training and evaluation sets to avoid contamination. A validation set of 2% of the samples is used for hyperparameter tuning. The audio retrieval capabilities are acquired in a zero-shot manner, as the training data is purely image-text matching, with the audio and vision towers frozen during training. The model supports tasks involving the retrieval of documents based on visual and audio features. It is intended to be used with the colpali-engine version 0.3.11 or higher. The model is evaluated using a dataset of 500 audio samples from the 'dailytalk-conversations-grouped' dataset, and retrieval results are obtained by scoring query embeddings against document embeddings. The model is available for use with specific installation and processing instructions, including the use of a processor for audio and query processing. The model is developed by Manuel Faysse and Antonio Loison, and its methodology is described in a paper available on arXiv. If used in research, the model should be cited according to the provided BibTeX entry.

instruction: Transform this hand-painted portrait into a realistic photo with accurate skin tones and background.
description: Anything2Real 2601 A is a version of the Anything2Real family of LoRA models, designed to transform non-photorealistic images such as illustrations, anime, cartoons, and paintings into convincing photographs while preserving the original composition and content. It is based on Anything2Real 2601 and incorporates adjustments to the layer weights of anything2real alpha to achieve a more balanced performance between maintaining the original image and generating a realistic environment. Anything2Real 2601 A addresses weaknesses in previous versions, such as the limited effectiveness of Anything2Real 2601 in transforming eyes and the limited color performance of anything2real alpha. The model is built on the Qwen Edit 2511 base model and is recommended to be used with a strength of 1 to 0.75. A prompt template is suggested: 'change the picture 1 to realistic photograph, [description of your image]'. Including detailed descriptions in the prompt helps the model better understand the content and produce superior transformations, although it can still function without them. The model is used in a workflow involving 21 nodes, including CLIPLoader, ConditioningZeroOut, ImageScaleBy, KSampler, LoadImage, LoraLoaderModelOnly, and specialized Qwen Edit nodes, and is powered by an RTX 4090 Series GPU. Realism is subjective, and it is recommended to modulate strength or switch base models rather than increasing the LoRA weight if realism is insufficient. If realism remains inadequate, it is suggested to blend with an additional photorealistic LoRA and adjust to taste. The model was created to bridge the gap between artistic creations and photorealistic results without requiring users to start from scratch. It is intended for artists who want to see their characters in realistic form or for anyone interested in transforming their favorite art styles into photographs. Users are encouraged to share their results and suggestions for future improvements. For best results, it is recommended to follow the workflow demonstrated in the RunningHub example. Users can reach out with questions or collaboration ideas via Twitter, email, QQ Group, WeChat, or CivitAI.

instruction: Could you explain the meaning behind this visual artwork?
description: Qwen3-VL is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced Thinking editions for flexible deployment. Key enhancements include the ability to operate PC and mobile GUIs by recognizing elements, understanding functions, invoking tools, and completing tasks. It can generate Draw.io, HTML, CSS, and JS from images and videos. The model supports advanced spatial perception, including judging object positions, viewpoints, and occlusions, with capabilities for 2D and 3D grounding. It features long context and video understanding with a native 256K context length, expandable to 1M, enabling it to handle books and hours-long video with full recall and second-level indexing. Enhanced multimodal reasoning is demonstrated in STEM and Math tasks, with causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. The model has upgraded visual recognition with broader, higher-quality pretraining that enables recognition of a wide range of subjects, including celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. Expanded OCR supports 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare or ancient characters and jargon. The model also supports long-document structure parsing and text understanding on par with pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE for full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings, enhancing long-horizon video reasoning. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. This is the weight repository for Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Instruct.

instruction: Can you detect any visible signs of weather or climate in the image?
description: The OWLv2 model, proposed by Matthias Minderer, Alexey Gritsenko, and Neil Houlsby, is a zero-shot text-conditioned object detection model designed to query an image with one or multiple text queries. It uses CLIP as its multi-modal backbone, with a ViT-like Transformer to extract visual features and a causal language model to extract text features. To enable detection, OWL-ViT removes the final token pooling layer of the vision model and attaches a lightweight classification and box head to each transformer output token. Open-vocabulary classification is achieved by replacing the fixed classification layer weights with class-name embeddings from the text model. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned together with the box and class prediction heads using an object detection objective and a bipartite matching loss. The model uses a CLIP backbone with a ViT-L/14 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The model is intended for use by AI researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. The CLIP backbone was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including data crawled from the internet and pre-existing image datasets. The prediction heads of OWL-ViT, along with the CLIP backbone, are fine-tuned on publicly available object detection datasets. The model was released in June 2023.

instruction: I need a way to see how far each element in the image is — can you generate that?
description: The DPT (Dense Prediction Transformer) model with DINOv2 backbone is a depth estimation model proposed by Oquab et al. It is designed to demonstrate that using the DPT framework with DINOv2 as the backbone results in a powerful depth estimator. The model supports depth estimation tasks and is intended for use in applications requiring accurate depth prediction from images. The architecture combines the DPT framework with the DINOv2 backbone, which is known for learning robust visual features without supervision. The model can be used with the Transformers library by loading the appropriate image processor and model from the 'facebook/dpt-dinov2-base-kitti' repository. The model processes images, generates depth predictions, and allows for visualization of the depth output. The model is based on research published in the paper titled 'DINOv2: Learning Robust Visual Features without Supervision' available on arXiv with the identifier 2304.07193.

instruction: Can you produce a video from a still image with the option to control how much noise is included in the output?
description: The Wan2.2 Distilled LoRA Models are high-performance video generation models that enable 4-step inference using LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) techniques. These models are designed to provide excellent generation quality while supporting flexible deployment options. The LoRA weights are extracted from Wan2.2 distilled models and can be combined with base models either through offline merging or online loading during inference. The models support multiple frameworks, including LightX2V and ComfyUI. They offer dual noise control, allowing users to choose between high noise for more creative and diverse outputs or low noise for more stable and input-faithful results. The LoRA models are compact in size, with a rank of 64, and can be further optimized by combining them with quantization techniques for storage efficiency and easy sharing. The 4-step inference process ensures ultra-fast generation of high-quality videos while maintaining the benefits of distilled models. The available LoRA models include both high noise and low noise variants for image-to-video (I2V) tasks. These models must be used in conjunction with the Wan2.2 I2V base model and require additional components such as T5 text encoder, CLIP vision encoder, VAE encoder/decoder, and tokenizer for full functionality. When using 4-step inference, the recommended denoising step list is [1000, 750, 500, 250].

instruction: How confident are you that this protein sequence includes a signal for secretion?
description: SignalSeeker is a machine learning ensemble designed for predicting signal peptides in protein sequences. It integrates multiple algorithms, including Random Forest, Extra Trees, SVM, and Logistic Regression, with ProtBERT embeddings to achieve high accuracy in signal peptide detection. The model is intended to predict whether a protein sequence contains a signal peptide, assist in protein subcellular localization prediction, support research in protein secretion pathways, and aid in biotechnology applications requiring secreted proteins. The ensemble architecture includes feature extraction using ProtBERT embeddings of the N-terminal 50 amino acids, ensemble models with regularization, feature scaling via StandardScaler normalization, and a weighted ensemble decision logic with confidence assessment. The model was trained on 5000 mixed sequences from UniProt verified eukaryotic proteins and tested on 1000 mixed sequences from the same source, isolated from the training data. Performance metrics include a test AUC of 0.99433 for the best model, Logistic Regression (L2), with cross-validation AUCs ranging from 0.98711 to 0.99433 across models. The model is trained primarily on eukaryotic sequences and may exhibit reduced performance for prokaryotic proteins or highly divergent organisms. It requires sequences of at least 50 amino acids for optimal performance. The model is for research purposes only and not intended for clinical diagnosis. Users are advised to validate results experimentally and consider potential biases in the training data. SignalSeeker is released under the MIT License.

instruction: 如何将中文段落转换成适合进行向量相似度计算的格式？
description: This is a CoSENT (Cosine Sentence) model named shibing624/text2vec-base-chinese. It maps sentences to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for tasks like sentence embeddings, text matching, or semantic search. The model is trained using the CoSENT method based on the hfl/chinese-macbert-base architecture on the Chinese STS-B dataset. It achieves good performance on the Chinese STS-B test set. The model is available on the Hugging Face model hub and is recommended for general Chinese semantic matching tasks. The model outputs a vector that captures semantic information and can be used for information retrieval, clustering, or sentence similarity tasks. Input text longer than 256 word pieces is truncated by default. The model architecture consists of a Transformer with a max sequence length of 128 and a Pooling layer that uses mean token pooling. The model is fine-tuned using a contrastive objective, where cosine similarity is computed for all possible sentence pairs in a batch, and a rank loss is applied by comparing true and false pairs. The training dataset is unspecified, and the maximum sequence length is set to 128. The best performance was achieved at epoch 5, and the sentence embedding dimension is 768. The model can be used with the text2vec library, HuggingFace Transformers, or the sentence-transformers library. It supports acceleration via ONNX and OpenVINO backends, with ONNX providing no performance loss on GPU and OpenVINO providing a 1.12x speedup on CPU. Quantization with OpenVINO (int8) results in a small performance hit on some tasks and a minor gain on others, with performance on CPU also improved. The model is intended for use as a sentence and short paragraph encoder.

instruction: Make an image of a child playing with a toy, capturing the joy in their expression and realistic clothing folds.
description: Qwen-Image-2512 is a text-to-image foundational model, representing the December update of the Qwen-Image series. It is a GGUF quantized version of the model, using a methodology for state-of-the-art performance. Key improvements in Qwen-Image-2512 include enhanced human realism, finer natural detail, and improved text rendering. The model significantly reduces the 'AI-generated' look and substantially enhances overall image realism, especially for human subjects. It delivers more detailed rendering of landscapes, animal fur, and other natural elements. The model also improves the accuracy and quality of textual elements, achieving better layout and more faithful multimodal (text + image) composition. Over 10,000 rounds of blind model evaluations have been conducted, and the results show that Qwen-Image-2512 is currently the strongest open-source model and remains highly competitive among closed-source models. The model can be used with the diffusers library, and it supports generating images with various aspect ratios. It is designed to be used with prompts that describe the desired image, including detailed descriptions of subjects, environments, and visual styles. The model is capable of rendering complex scenes with high fidelity, including human portraits, landscapes, and technical or educational infographics. It is particularly effective in capturing fine details such as facial expressions, hair strands, and environmental textures. The model also supports generating images with specific lighting conditions, such as indoor illumination or natural daylight. It is capable of rendering realistic textures, including fur, fabric, and skin, and can produce images that resemble casual snapshots or professional photography. The model is suitable for a wide range of applications, including artistic creation, educational materials, and technical illustrations. It is based on a diffusion pipeline and can be run on devices with CUDA support for improved performance. The model is part of a series of updates to the Qwen-Image model, with each version introducing new features and improvements. The model is available for use through the Hugging Face and ModelScope platforms, and users are encouraged to try the latest version for the best results. The model is developed by a team of researchers and is available under an open-source license. If Qwen-Image-2512 is used in research, users are encouraged to cite the associated technical report.

instruction: What is the subject of this painting, and what style is it in?
description: Llama Nemotron Nano VL is a document intelligence vision language model (VLM) designed to query and summarize images from the physical or virtual world. It is deployable in the data center, cloud, and at the edge, including Jetson Orin and laptops, using AWQ 4bit quantization through the TinyChat framework. The model was trained on commercial images for all three stages of training and supports single image inference. Key findings include the necessity of interleaved image-text data over image-text pairs, the importance of unfreezing the language model during interleaved pre-training for in-context learning, and the benefit of re-blending text-only instruction data to enhance both VLM and text-only performance. The model uses a Transformer architecture with a vision encoder and a Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct language encoder. It accepts image and text inputs, with images in RGB format and text in string format. Input images are constrained by a 12-tile layout, with each tile being 512 × 512 pixels, supporting various aspect ratios. The model processes 2D images and 1D text, with a total input and output token limit of 16K. Output is text in string format. The model is optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems, leveraging NVIDIA hardware and software for faster training and inference. It is compatible with the TensorRT-LLM runtime engine and the H100 SXM 80GB microarchitecture, and supports Linux operating systems. The model was trained and evaluated using the NV-Pretraining and NV-CosmosNemotron-SFT datasets, which include a mix of internal and public datasets, as well as synthetic data for specific tasks. Evaluation benchmarks include scores on MMMU Val (48.2%), AI2D (85.0%), ChartQA (86.3%), InfoVQA Val (77.4%), OCRBench (839), OCRBenchV2 English (60.1%), OCRBenchV2 Chinese (37.9%), DocVQA val (91.2%), and VideoMME (54.7%). The model supports use cases such as image summarization, text-image analysis, optical character recognition, interactive Q&A on images, and text chain-of-thought reasoning. It is intended for AI foundry enterprise customers and is available in the Llama-3.1-Nemotron-Nano-VL-8B-V1 version. The model is governed by the Llama 3.1 Community Model License and is built with Llama. Ethical considerations include the responsibility of users to ensure safe integration, including the implementation of guardrails and other safety mechanisms, as outputs may contain political content, misleading information, content security issues, or unwanted bias. Users are responsible for model inputs and outputs and should work with their internal model teams to ensure compliance with industry and use case requirements.

instruction: How likely is it that a major breakthrough in fusion energy will be announced within the next five years?
description: OpenForecaster-8B is a specialized language model for open-ended forecasting and predicting future events. It is post-trained from Qwen3-8B using reinforcement learning. The model is trained to make calibrated predictions on open-ended questions about future events, including providing calibrated confidence estimates when asked, reasoning about uncertainty and future scenarios, and leveraging retrieved information in context to improve predictions. OpenForecaster-8B was trained on the OpenForesight dataset, which contains over 52,000 forecasting questions generated from global news events, using GRPO to optimize a joint reward function combining accuracy and brier score. The model's knowledge cutoff is at best until April 2025, with the base model's cutoff being around June 2024, meaning it has no knowledge of events occurring after that date. Therefore, if asked about events in 2026 or later without recent developments or relevant context, the model's answers may not be helpful or up to date. OpenForecaster-8B achieves competitive performance with larger models like DeepSeek-v3 and Qwen3-235B-A22B on forecasting benchmarks, with key improvements in accuracy, calibration of confidence estimates, and consistency in predictions. Performance on a benchmark in July-August 2025 on non-numeric questions (86 questions) showed that OpenForecaster-8B has much higher accuracy than 100B+ models, with a comparison limited to models released before April 2025 for a fair, equal knowledge cutoff. The model is released under the MIT License.

instruction: What's the most efficient way to solve this problem using recursive reasoning?
description: INTELLECT-3 is a 106B (A12B) parameter Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) reasoning model post-trained using supervised fine-tuning (SFT) followed by large-scale reinforcement learning (RL). Training was conducted using environments built with a specific library, and all training and evaluation environments are available on the Environments Hub. The model, training frameworks, and environments are open-sourced under fully-permissive licenses, including MIT and Apache 2.0. INTELLECT-3 achieves best-in-class performance on math, coding, and reasoning benchmarks, including 98.1 on MATH-500, 90.8 on AIME24, 88.0 on AIME25, 69.3 on LCB, 74.4 on GPQA, 14.6 on HLE, and 81.9 on MMLU-Pro. The BF16 version of the model can be served on two H200 GPUs using the vLLM framework with specific configuration parameters, while the FP8 version can be served on a single H200 GPU with similar settings. The model is designed to support reasoning tasks and is part of the Prime Intellect Platform. For more information, refer to the technical report and blog.

instruction: Which of these two versions of a job posting is more likely to attract qualified candidates?
description: Skywork-Reward-V2 is a series of eight reward models designed for versatility across a wide range of tasks. These models are trained on a mixture of 26 million carefully curated preference pairs. The Skywork-Reward-V2 series is based on the Bradley-Terry model, with improvements in training data scale and quality to achieve superior performance. Compared to the first generation of Skywork-Reward, the Skywork-Reward-V2 series includes the following major improvements: it is trained on a significantly larger and higher-quality preference data mixture, consisting of 26 million preference pairs curated via a large-scale human-LLM synergistic pipeline. The series achieves state-of-the-art performance on seven major reward model benchmarks, including RewardBench v1, RewardBench v2, PPE Preference, PPE Correctness, RMB, RM-Bench, and JudgeBench. The series includes eight models across multiple sizes, with the smallest 0.6B variant, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-0.6B, nearly matching the average performance of the previous best model, Skywork-Reward-Gemma-2-27B-v0.2. The largest 8B version, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B, surpasses all existing reward models across all benchmarks on average. The top experimental model, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M, outperforms all existing reward models on every benchmark. The models are available in various sizes, including Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.2-1B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.2-3B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-0.6B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-1.7B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-4B, and Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-8B. The Skywork-Reward-V2 series outperforms models in both Bradley-Terry and Generative categories with much smaller model sizes. For most use cases, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B is recommended, with smaller variants suggested for low-resource settings. All models are trained on preference data with a maximum length of 16,384 tokens, and inference should be performed within this limit. System prompts should not be included when using chat templates. The Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M model is considered experimental and is trained on the complete set of 40 million preference pairs, with about one third of the chosen-rejected pairs flipped. It is recommended for research or non-production purposes only. The model repository, including the model weights and code, is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License for models derived from Qwen3, and under the Meta License for models derived from Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct.
