If you have any questions, you can contact the authors at yuhao.liuu at kunlun-inc dot com and liang.zeng at kunlun-inc dot com. If you find the work useful, please cite it as follows: @article{liu2025skywork, title={Skywork-Reward-V2: Scaling Preference Data Curation via Human-AI Synergy}, author = {Liu, Chris Yuhao and Zeng, Liang and Xiao, Yuzhen and He, Jujie and Liu, Jiacai and Wang, Chaojie and Yan, Rui and Shen, Wei and Zhang, Fuxiang and Xu, Jiacheng and Liu, Yang and Zhou, Yahui}, journal={arXiv preprint arXiv:2507.01352}, year={2025}}

instruction: Could you analyze the body language in this surveillance footage?
description: Ultralytics YOLO26 is the latest evolution in the YOLO series, engineered from the ground up for edge and low-power devices. This is the pose estimation variant optimized for human keypoint detection. The architecture of YOLO26 is guided by three core principles: simplicity, deployment efficiency, and training innovation. YOLO26 is a native end-to-end model, producing predictions directly without the need for non-maximum suppression (NMS). By eliminating this post-processing step, inference becomes faster, lighter, and easier to deploy in real-world systems. The end-to-end design cuts out an entire stage of the pipeline, dramatically simplifying integration, reducing latency, and making deployment more robust across diverse environments. YOLO26 introduces the MuSGD optimizer, a hybrid of SGD and Muon — inspired by Moonshot AI's Kimi K2 breakthroughs in LLM training. This optimizer brings enhanced stability and faster convergence. The model includes features such as Residual Log-Likelihood Estimation (RLE) for more accurate keypoint localization, optimized decoding for increased inference speed for pose estimation, DFL Removal for simplified inference and broader hardware compatibility, and up to 43% faster CPU inference, optimized for edge computing. The model has an input size of 640 pixels, an mAP Pose (50-95, e2e) of 70.4, an mAP Pose (50, e2e) of 90.5, a CPU Speed (ONNX) of 275.4 ms, a T4 TensorRT10 Speed of 6.5 ms, 25.9M parameters, and 91.3B FLOPs.

instruction: How would you represent this text in a way that allows for similarity comparisons?
description: UME-R1 is a model that has undergone a cold-start SFT stage and an RL stage of training. It is capable of embedding text, images, multiple images, and videos. The model can generate either discriminative or generative embeddings as needed, and the generative embeddings possess the potential for test-time scaling. UME-R1 significantly outperforms discriminative embeddings and can provide either type of representation as required. Its oracle performance—selecting the best between discriminative and generative—far exceeds using either mode alone. In addition, UME-R1 can produce improved embedding representations through repeated sampling, indicating that generative embeddings also hold strong promise for inference-time scaling.

instruction: How reactive is the compound with the SMILES code 'C1=CC=CC=C1C(=O)O' with hydroxyl radicals in the atmosphere?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict the reaction rate constant with hydroxyl radicals (log kAOH). This property is related to atmospheric degradation and environmental persistence. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. The model type is AttentiveFP (Graph Neural Network), and it performs a regression task. It processes SMILES strings as input and produces a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and is based on an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model's hyperparameters include a hidden channel size of 128, three layers, three timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.1, a learning rate of 0.0005, a weight decay of 0.0001, a batch size of 32, and 50 training epochs with a patience of 10. The model was trained on the kAOH dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: Which of these statements is more closely related to the topic of quantum computing?
description: This is a model finetuned from Alibaba-NLP/gte-multilingual-reranker-base using the Sentence Transformers library. It is a Cross Encoder model designed to compute scores for pairs of texts, which can be used for text reranking and semantic search. The model has a maximum sequence length of 512 tokens and produces one output label. It can be loaded and used with the Sentence Transformers library for inference, where it can calculate scores for text pairs or rank texts based on similarity to a single text. The model was trained on a dataset of 24,588 samples, with each sample containing two sentences and a label. The training used a non-default set of hyperparameters, including a batch size of 32 for both training and evaluation, 5 training epochs, and FP16 precision. During training, the model achieved a Spearman correlation of 0.8709 on the validation dataset. The training loss decreased over time, and the Spearman correlation on the validation set improved from 0.7581 at step 100 to 0.8709 at step 3200. The model was trained using PyTorch 2.8.0+cu128, Sentence Transformers 5.1.2, and Transformers 4.57.1. The training data included a variety of sentence pairs, with sentence lengths ranging from 3 to 998 characters for the first sentence and 73 to 352 characters for the second sentence. The labels ranged from 0.0 to 1.0, with a mean of 0.41. The model uses a linear learning rate scheduler with no warmup steps and a learning rate of 5e-05. The training process included logging of training loss and validation Spearman correlation at each step.

instruction: Please take this scanned page and turn it into a well-structured Markdown document.
description: OCRFlux-3B is a preview release of a multimodal large language model toolkit designed for converting PDFs and images into clean, readable plain Markdown text. It is fine-tuned from Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct using private document datasets and some additional data. The model is based on a 3B parameter vision-language model (VLM), allowing it to run on a GTX 3090 GPU. OCRFlux-3B is intended to push the current state-of-the-art in document parsing to a significantly higher level. It supports superior parsing quality on each page, achieving higher Edit Distance Similarity (EDS) scores on the OCRFlux-bench-single benchmark compared to baseline models such as olmOCR-7B-0225-preview, Nanonets-OCR-s, and MonkeyOCR. Specifically, it achieves 0.095 higher EDS for English pages (from 0.872 to 0.967), 0.109 higher EDS for Chinese pages (from 0.858 to 0.967), and 0.187 higher EDS overall (from 0.780 to 0.967). The model also provides native support for cross-page table and paragraph merging, a feature not previously available in open-source projects. It can be used via an API for direct integration into code or through a Docker container setup, which requires Docker with GPU support and a pre-downloaded model. The model supports processing millions of documents at scale using an efficient inference setup via vllm. It includes a pipeline for generating Markdown files from JSONL outputs and offers evaluation capabilities for single-page parsing, table parsing, and cross-page merging detection and merging tasks. OCRFlux-3B is evaluated on two comprehensive benchmarks for single-page parsing and two for cross-page table/paragraph merging. In single-page parsing, it achieves an average EDS of 0.967, outperforming other models. In cross-page merging detection, it achieves a precision of 0.996, recall of 0.976, F1 score of 0.986, and accuracy of 0.986. For cross-page table merging, it achieves an average Tree Edit Distance-based Similarity (TEDS) of 0.950. The benchmarks used for evaluation are not included in the model's training or evaluation data. The model is designed to handle complex scenarios such as cross-page tables with repeated headers, vertically split tables, and multi-line table cells. It is also capable of merging paragraphs across pages by concatenation.

instruction: How can I get a depth map from a set of photos taken from different angles?
description: Depth Anything 3 (DA3) is a model that predicts spatially consistent geometry from an arbitrary number of visual inputs, with or without known camera poses. It is designed for multi-view depth estimation and camera pose estimation, using a compact foundation model with a unified depth-ray representation. The model is based on a single plain transformer, such as a vanilla DINO encoder, which serves as a backbone without architectural specialization. DA3 uses a singular depth-ray prediction target, eliminating the need for complex multi-task learning. It is trained using a teacher-student training paradigm and achieves a level of detail and generalization comparable to Depth Anything 2 (DA2). The model sets a new state-of-the-art across all tasks on a new visual geometry benchmark, surpassing prior SOTA VGGT by an average of 44.3% in camera pose accuracy and 25.1% in geometric accuracy. It also outperforms DA2 in monocular depth estimation. DA3 supports relative depth estimation, pose estimation, and pose conditioning. The model is developed by ByteDance Seed Team and is based on a vision transformer for visual geometry. It is trained exclusively on public academic datasets and may have limitations on certain domain-specific images. Performance may vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity.

instruction: Can you help me convert this text and its associated image into a form that can be used for clustering?
description: The embedding model trained by Jina AI is a version of the Qwen2.5-VL model with the code adapter merged into the base weights. This architecture modification enables native compatibility with vLLM without requiring custom adapter-handling code. The model is designed for multimodal multilingual retrieval and supports tasks involving text and image inputs. It is intended to be used for encoding prompts, including text and image data, into embeddings. The model can process text prompts for queries and passages, as well as image prompts with associated descriptions. When processing image prompts, the model identifies and extracts embeddings specifically from the vision tokens. For text-only prompts, it uses all tokens to generate embeddings. The embeddings are pooled and normalized to produce the final output. The model is compatible with the vLLM framework and can be initialized with specific configurations such as data type and pooling settings. It is important to note that the model requires a specific method to extract and process embeddings, particularly for image data, which involves identifying vision token ranges and applying normalization.

instruction: Find and label gene-related expressions in this section of a scientific paper.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for gene entity recognition, specifically for identifying and extracting gene-related entities from biomedical texts. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated GELLUS dataset, which is focused on gene recognition and genetics entities for genomics and molecular biology applications. The model supports the classification of biomedical entities such as 'B-Cell-line-name' and 'I-Cell-line-name'. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is based on the xlm-roberta-large architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model has achieved an F1 score of 1.00, with precision, recall, and accuracy also at 1.00. Comparative performance on the GELLUS dataset shows that it ranks among the top models, with an F1 score of 0.9976, precision of 0.9977, recall of 0.9975, and accuracy of 0.9989. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical and research applications. It is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. The model is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with the AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling, and it is validated using cross-validation on a held-out test set.

instruction: Please extract the main clauses from this Greek parliamentary bill.
description: GEM-RoBERTa HQ Legal is a RoBERTa-base model pre-trained from scratch on a strategically curated 21GB corpus of Greek legal, parliamentary, and governmental text. The model employs a quality-based data repetition strategy, where higher-quality legal sources are repeated multiple times during training to enhance the model's understanding of premium legal terminology and concepts. This model was trained as part of a research project and has been optimized for downstream tasks such as Named Entity Recognition (NER), Text Classification, and Question Answering within the legal field. The RoBERTa architecture provides enhanced performance through dynamic masking and removal of the Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) task, focusing entirely on Masked Language Modeling (MLM). The model was pre-trained on a comprehensive corpus of Greek text compiled from various legal and governmental sources, with a quality-based data repetition strategy that increases exposure to higher-quality legal content. The original 16.75GB corpus was expanded to 21.12GB through strategic repetition. The quality-based repetition strategy enhances the model's exposure to premium legal terminology from the Raptarchis Legal Dictionary (4x repetition), high-quality judicial reasoning from Supreme Court reports (3x repetition), and EU legal concepts from Eur-Lex content (2x repetition). This approach ensures the model develops a stronger understanding of sophisticated legal language while maintaining exposure to the broader legal corpus. The model uses the RoBERTa-base architecture with a hidden size of 768, 12 attention heads, 12 hidden layers, and approximately 125 million parameters. The model was trained using a custom ByteLevelBPE tokenizer with a vocabulary of 50,264 tokens optimized for Greek legal terminology. The data was processed into fixed-size chunks of 512 tokens, respecting document boundaries to ensure contextual coherence. Higher-quality sources were strategically repeated during the data preparation phase. The model was pre-trained from scratch for 150,000 steps on 8x NVIDIA A100 40GB GPUs, using BFloat16 (bf16) mixed-precision for stability and speed. The training took approximately 81 hours and 39 minutes to complete. The key hyperparameters used were a learning rate of 1.5e-4 (0.00015) with a linear warmup of 9,000 steps, an effective batch size of 2,688 (per_device_train_batch_size: 48, gradient_accumulation_steps: 7), the AdamW optimizer with standard parameters, a weight decay of 0.01, a max sequence length of 512, a max of 150,000 steps, 9,000 warmup steps, an MLM probability of 0.15, and a max gradient norm of 1.0. The model achieved a final training loss of 0.617 and a final evaluation loss of 0.573035. The training infrastructure consisted of 8x NVIDIA A100 40GB GPUs, with a total of 150,000 training steps and a total training time of 66 hours 39 minutes. The train/validation split was 95%/5%, and the effective training data was 21.12GB, incorporating the quality-based repetition strategy.

instruction: Could you create a voice version of this paragraph that matches the tone of a news anchor?
description: GLM-TTS is a high-quality text-to-speech (TTS) synthesis system based on large language models, supporting zero-shot voice cloning and streaming inference. The system uses a two-stage architecture that combines an LLM for speech token generation and a Flow Matching model for waveform synthesis. By introducing a Multi-Reward Reinforcement Learning framework, GLM-TTS significantly improves the expressiveness of generated speech, achieving more natural emotional control compared to traditional TTS systems. Key features include the ability to clone any speaker's voice with just 3-10 seconds of prompt audio, the use of a multi-reward reinforcement learning framework (GRPO) to optimize prosody and emotion, high-quality speech synthesis comparable to commercial systems with reduced Character Error Rate (CER), support for 'Hybrid Phoneme + Text' input for precise pronunciation control, real-time audio generation suitable for interactive applications, and optimization for Chinese and English mixed text. The system follows a two-stage design: Stage 1 uses a Llama-based model to convert input text into speech token sequences, and Stage 2 uses a Flow model to convert token sequences into high-quality mel-spectrograms, which are then turned into waveforms by a vocoder. To address flat emotional expression, GLM-TTS uses a Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) algorithm with multiple reward functions (Similarity, CER, Emotion, Laughter) to align the LLM's generation strategy. Evaluated on seed-tts-eval, GLM-TTS_RL achieves the lowest Character Error Rate (CER) while maintaining high speaker similarity. The model GLM-TTS_RL achieves a CER of 0.89 and a SIM score of 76.4, outperforming other models such as Seed-TTS (CER 1.12, SIM 79.6), CosyVoice2 (CER 1.38, SIM 75.7), and F5-TTS (CER 1.53, SIM 76.0). GLM-TTS is open-source, while Seed-TTS is not. The system is designed to be used for real-time applications and supports phoneme-level control for precise pronunciation. It is optimized for both Chinese and English mixed text and is intended for use in interactive applications requiring streaming inference.

instruction: Please extract the text and tables from this multi-page document and combine them into one clean file.
description: OCRFlux-3B is a preview release of a multimodal large language model toolkit designed for converting PDFs and images into clean, readable plain Markdown text. It is fine-tuned from Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct using private document datasets and some additional data. The model is based on a 3B parameter vision-language model (VLM), allowing it to run on a GTX 3090 GPU. OCRFlux-3B is intended to push the current state-of-the-art in document parsing to a significantly higher level. It supports superior parsing quality on each page, achieving higher Edit Distance Similarity (EDS) scores on the OCRFlux-bench-single benchmark compared to baseline models such as olmOCR-7B-0225-preview, Nanonets-OCR-s, and MonkeyOCR. Specifically, it achieves 0.095 higher EDS for English pages (from 0.872 to 0.967), 0.109 higher EDS for Chinese pages (from 0.858 to 0.967), and 0.187 higher EDS overall (from 0.780 to 0.967). The model also provides native support for cross-page table and paragraph merging, a feature not previously available in open-source projects. It can be used via an API for direct integration into code or through a Docker container setup, which requires Docker with GPU support and a pre-downloaded model. The model supports processing millions of documents at scale using an efficient inference setup via vllm. It includes a pipeline for generating Markdown files from JSONL outputs and offers evaluation capabilities for single-page parsing, table parsing, and cross-page merging detection and merging tasks. OCRFlux-3B is evaluated on two comprehensive benchmarks for single-page parsing and two for cross-page table/paragraph merging. In single-page parsing, it achieves an average EDS of 0.967, outperforming other models. In cross-page merging detection, it achieves a precision of 0.996, recall of 0.976, F1 score of 0.986, and accuracy of 0.986. For cross-page table merging, it achieves an average Tree Edit Distance-based Similarity (TEDS) of 0.950. The benchmarks used for evaluation are not included in the model's training or evaluation data. The model is designed to handle complex scenarios such as cross-page tables with repeated headers, vertically split tables, and multi-line table cells. It is also capable of merging paragraphs across pages by concatenation.

instruction: Can you detect any artificial elements in this video of a person talking?
description: This model is a powerful deepfake video detection system that combines a ResNext50 network for spatial feature extraction with an LSTM network for temporal analysis. It is designed to classify video authenticity by identifying visual artifacts and temporal inconsistencies. The model achieves 87% accuracy on internal test datasets and is built using PyTorch. It is trained on a custom dataset of real and deepfake videos and provides a binary classification (real/fake) along with a confidence score. The architecture is optimized for video clips of 20 frames and performs best on human face-focused content. Its performance may be affected by video quality, such as heavy compression or low resolution. The model is intended for ethical use in combating misinformation and verifying media authenticity. It is integrated with the Hugging Face pipeline for easy inference and is available as a Docker-ready deployment with a Hugging Face Space.

instruction: Is there a signature visible in this photo of a legal form?
description: YOLOS is a Vision Transformer (ViT) trained using the DETR loss. A base-sized YOLOS model achieves 42 AP on COCO validation 2017, similar to DETR and more complex frameworks such as Faster R-CNN. This model is a finetuned version of YOLOS, specifically adapted for detecting handwritten signatures in document images. It is intended for use in scanned documents, contracts, or forms. The model was finetuned using a dataset built from two public datasets, unified and processed by an external contributor. The processed dataset contains 1,980 training images, 420 validation images, and 419 testing images, all in COCO JSON format with a resolution of 640x640 pixels. Performance metrics on the testing set include an mAP50 of 0.856 and an mAP50-95 of 0.395. Inference times are 0.262 seconds on CPU and 0.014 seconds on GPU, measured on a laptop with an Intel Core i7-9750H CPU and an NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1650 GPU. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license, which allows for permissive use, including commercial applications, without the requirement to share modified code. The model must not be used for fraudulent purposes, such as forging signatures or any illegal activity. It is not suitable for detecting non-signature elements in documents and should not be solely relied on for high-stakes decisions without human validation. The model may exhibit bias if the training data lacks diversity in signature styles or cultural context. False positives and negatives can occur, which may impact document validation. Performance may degrade on poor-quality images or in challenging visual conditions such as noise or lighting. To improve performance, it is recommended to fine-tune the model with diverse and representative samples, include human oversight for critical use cases, use clean, high-resolution images, and apply preprocessing if needed. The model is designed for legitimate signature detection in documents and should be used in accordance with legal and ethical standards, ensuring privacy and responsible deployment.

instruction: What are the likely labels for these data points based on their attributes and past patterns?
description: Mitra classifier is a tabular foundation model that is pre-trained on purely synthetic datasets sampled from a mix of random classifiers. It is based on a 12-layer Transformer with 72 million parameters, pre-trained by incorporating an in-context learning paradigm. The model is designed for use in tabular data classification tasks and supports both inference and fine-tuning. For inference, the model can be used without fine-tuning, and for fine-tuning, a specified number of training steps can be applied. The model is implemented within the AutoGluon.tabular framework and can be trained and evaluated using standard machine learning workflows, including train/test splits and performance evaluation. The model is licensed under the Apache-2.0 License. The model's development and methodology are described in the research paper titled 'Mitra: Mixed synthetic priors for enhancing tabular foundation models' by Zhang et al., published as an arXiv preprint in 2025.

instruction: I’m working on a VR experience — can you convert this image of a building into a 3D mesh?
description: Stable Fast 3D (SF3D) is a large reconstruction model that takes a single image of an object and generates a textured UV-unwrapped 3D mesh asset. The model is a Transformer-based image-to-3D model designed to create a 3D model from a single image in under one second. The output asset is UV-unwrapped, textured, and has a relatively low polygon count. The model performs a delighting step, which facilitates easier use of the asset in downstream applications such as game engines or rendering work. It also predicts per-object material parameters, including roughness and metallic, which enhances reflective behaviors during rendering. The model expects an input size of 512x512 pixels. The model is trained using renders from a dataset available under the Open Data Commons Attribution License. An enhanced rendering method is used, which more closely replicates the distribution of images found in the real world, significantly improving the model's ability to generalize. Objects are filtered based on the review of licenses, and a subset is curated to meet training needs. Intended uses include the generation of artworks and use in design and other artistic processes, applications in educational or creative tools, and research on reconstruction models, including understanding the limitations of these models. The model was not trained to produce factual or true representations of people or events, and generating such content is out of scope for the model's capabilities. Individuals or organizations generating annual revenue of US $1,000,000 (or local currency equivalent) or more must obtain an enterprise commercial license directly from Stability AI before commercially using SF3D or any derivative work of SF3D or its outputs. Community License allows free use for research, non-commercial, and commercial use by organizations and individuals generating annual revenue of US $1M or less. Developers are encouraged to conduct their own testing and apply additional mitigations based on their specific use cases, as the model includes safety measures intended to reduce the risk of severe harms.

instruction: Can you determine the body orientation of the individual in this image?
description: YOLO26-N-pose is a variant of the Ultralytics YOLO26 model, specifically optimized for human keypoint detection. It is the latest evolution in the YOLO series, designed from the ground up for edge and low-power devices. The model has an input size of 640 pixels and achieves a mAP Pose (50-95, e2e) of 57.2 and a mAP Pose (50, e2e) of 83.3. It runs at 40.3 ms on CPU (ONNX) and 1.8 ms on T4 TensorRT10, with 2.9M parameters and 7.5B FLOPs. The architecture of YOLO26 is guided by three core principles: simplicity, deployment efficiency, and training innovation. It is a native end-to-end model that produces predictions directly without the need for non-maximum suppression (NMS), which eliminates a post-processing step and makes inference faster, lighter, and easier to deploy. The end-to-end design simplifies integration, reduces latency, and improves deployment robustness across diverse environments. YOLO26 introduces the MuSGD optimizer, a hybrid of SGD and Muon inspired by Moonshot AI's Kimi K2 breakthroughs in LLM training, which provides enhanced stability and faster convergence. The model includes features such as Residual Log-Likelihood Estimation (RLE) for more accurate keypoint localization, optimized decoding for increased inference speed, DFL removal for simplified inference and broader hardware compatibility, and up to 43% faster CPU inference for edge computing. The model can be installed via pip and used with the ultralytics library for inference on images.

instruction: I want to improve the order of these retrieved texts so the best matches come to the top. How should I proceed?
description: Contextual AI Reranker v2 1B is a text reranking model designed to re-rank retrieved documents based on custom instructions, such as prioritizing recent information. It is the first instruction-following reranker capable of handling retrieval conflicts and ranking documents according to user-defined criteria. The model achieves state-of-the-art performance on the BEIR benchmark and is positioned on the cost/performance Pareto frontier across several dimensions, including instruction following, question answering, multilinguality (supporting 100+ languages), product search and recommendation, and real-world use cases. It is a 1 billion parameter model with a context length of up to 32,000 tokens. The model is intended for use when re-ranking retrieved documents with custom instructions, handling conflicting information in retrieval results, prioritizing documents by recency or other criteria, supporting multilingual search, and processing long contexts. The model is available for use with either the vLLM or Transformers frameworks, with specific implementation details provided for each. It is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution Non Commercial Share Alike 4.0 license. For questions or issues, users are directed to open an issue on the model repository. If used, the model should be cited with the provided BibTeX entry.

instruction: What is the estimated depth of the foreground object compared to the background?
description: DA3 Small is a model for multi-view depth estimation and camera pose estimation. It is an efficient foundation model that uses a unified depth-ray representation. The model is part of the Any-view Model series, has 0.08B parameters, and is licensed under Apache 2.0. It supports relative depth estimation, pose estimation, and pose conditioning. The model is developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is based on a Vision Transformer for Visual Geometry with a plain transformer architecture. It is trained using public academic datasets only. A single plain transformer, such as a vanilla DINO encoder, is used as the backbone without architectural specialization. The depth-ray representation allows the model to avoid complex multi-task learning. The model significantly outperforms Depth Anything 2 for monocular depth estimation and VGGT for multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it is trained on academic datasets and may have limitations on domain-specific images. Performance can vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity.

instruction: From this description of a studio apartment, can you create a 3D visualization with multiple views?
description: SpatialGen is a multi-view, multi-modal diffusion model designed to generate 3D indoor scenes conditioned on either a reference image or a textual description, given a 3D semantic layout. The model supports tasks such as image-to-3D scene generation and text-to-3D scene generation. It is intended for use in 3D scene generation applications and is based on a diffusion model architecture. The model has been developed as part of a research project and is associated with a paper published in the International Conference on 3D Vision in 2026. The model is currently available for inference, with a dataset containing 48 rooms and 4.8K rendered images provided for multi-view diffusion inference. The model is derived from another project and is licensed under the same license as that project. The model is tested with Python 3.10, PyTorch 2.3.1, and CUDA Version 12.1. Training instructions and a full dataset release are planned for future updates.

instruction: I need to convert this multi-line table into Markdown. Can you ensure the alignment is correct?
description: NuMarkdown-8B-Thinking is the first reasoning OCR vision-language model (VLM) designed to convert documents into clean Markdown files, particularly suitable for RAG applications. It generates thinking tokens to analyze the layout of a document before producing the final Markdown output. The model excels at handling documents with unusual layouts and complex tables. The number of thinking tokens can range from 20% to 500% of the final answer, depending on the complexity of the task. It is a fine-tune of Qwen 2.5-VL-7B, trained on synthetic examples of documents followed by a reinforcement learning phase (GRPO) using a layout-centric reward. NuMarkdown-8B-Thinking outperforms generic non-reasoning models like GPT-4o and specialized OCR models like OCRFlux, and is competitive with large reasoning closed-source models like Gemini 2.5. In an arena ranking using the trueskill-2 system with approximately 500 anonymized votes, it ranks second with a μ of 26.10 and σ of 0.79, just behind gemini-flash-reasoning. The model is particularly effective for complex document-to-markdown tasks and is intended for use in scenarios requiring structured document conversion. A planned markdown arena will provide a tool for evaluating different solutions in this domain. The training process includes a single epoch of supervised fine-tuning on synthetic reasoning traces from public PDFs, followed by a reinforcement learning phase using a layout-centric reward with challenging image examples. The model is designed to handle headers, bullet points, tables with merged cells, footers, and multi-line content within cells, ensuring accurate and well-structured Markdown output. It is intended for use in educational and administrative settings where document conversion is required, such as for student and family information sheets. The model supports both vLLM and Hugging Face Transformers for deployment, with specific instructions for serving and inference provided.

instruction: I need to find the image that shows the anatomical structure of the human heart.
description: TomoroAI/tomoro-colqwen3-embed-8b is a state-of-the-art multimodal embedding model that maps text queries, visual documents (images, PDFs), or short videos into aligned multi-vector embeddings. It is built by merging components with a full vision stack, inheriting robust text retrieval capabilities. The model has been fine-tuned on a curated mixture of data sources and achieves state-of-the-art or competitive performance across ViDoRe V1-V3 in both English and multilingual settings. It offers a significantly reduced embedding footprint compared to other full-dimension Colpali model alternatives. The model is based on a Qwen3-VL 8B architecture with an encoder-only variant and a 320-dimension projection head. It uses a ColPali-style late interaction methodology with MaxSim scoring. It supports up to 1,280 visual tokens per page or 5,120 visual tokens per video, with text prompts constrained by the base context window of 32,000 tokens. The model is capable of handling complex layouts, such as chart-rich PDFs and domain-specific documents, and supports end-to-end retrieval of unseen multimodal documents without requiring OCR or an intermediate vision LLM for summaries. It demonstrates strong performance on non-English document inputs and inherits strong text retrieval performance from the Qwen3-Embedding-8B model. However, it is not fine-tuned on large-scale video retrieval datasets, and its video support is based on preliminary findings. The model is larger than single-vector baselines despite having a smaller token dimension. It is not currently fine-tuned with diverse system instructions, and improvements are planned using more synthetic datasets. The model is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license, with upstream Qwen checkpoints retaining their community licenses. Training data includes ViDoRe/MTEB corpora and synthetic VisRAG assets. The model is applied to enhance Tomoro’s customized multimodal agentic RAG pipelines, enabling autonomous agents to parse, reason over, and retrieve from large-scale enterprise internal documentation. It supports Tomoro AI's mission to deliver high-quality enterprise multimodal solutions across high-stakes industries.

instruction: Which species are discussed in this paper on antibiotic resistance in bacteria?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for species entity recognition, specifically for identifying and extracting species and organism names from biomedical texts. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the LINNAEUS dataset, a curated biomedical named entity recognition (NER) dataset focused on species name identification and organism recognition in scientific literature. The model supports the classification of two entity types: `B-SPECIES` and `I-SPECIES`. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is based on the e5-base-v2 architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.78, precision of 0.72, recall of 0.84, and accuracy of 0.98. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and healthcare analytics contexts. The model is part of the OpenMed NER project, which provides open-source, domain-adapted state-of-the-art transformers for biomedical NER across multiple datasets.

instruction: Generate a digital painting from a hand-drawn sketch of a mythical creature.
description: ControlNet v1.1 is a neural network structure designed to control diffusion models by adding extra conditions. It is the successor model to ControlNet v1.0 and was developed by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala. This checkpoint is a conversion into `diffusers` format and is specifically conditioned on MLSD images, which are images with annotated line segments. ControlNet is used in combination with Stable Diffusion to support additional input conditions in text-to-image generation. The model is based on a diffusion-based architecture and is primarily used in English. The license is adapted from the work of Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala in the area of responsible AI licensing. The model was trained using a dataset that previously had issues such as duplicated grayscale human images, low-quality images, and incorrect paired prompts. These issues were resolved in the new version. The training dataset was expanded by adding 300K more images containing more than 16 straight lines, and data augmentations such as random left-right flipping were applied. The model was resumed from MLSD 1.0 and continued training for 200 GPU hours using A100 80G. ControlNet supports various conditions including canny edge detection, pixel to pixel instruction, image inpainting, multi-level line segment detection, depth estimation, surface normal estimation, image segmentation, line art generation, anime line art generation, human pose estimation, scribble-based image generation, soft edge image generation, image shuffling, and image tiling. Each condition is associated with a specific type of image input, such as monochrome images with white edges on a black background for canny edge detection, or color-coded images for surface normal estimation. The model is intended to learn task-specific conditions in an end-to-end manner, and the learning process is robust even with small training datasets.

instruction: Provide a concise summary of this long essay on the history of the internet.
description: Gemma 3n is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models developed by Google, built using the same research and technology as the Gemini models. These models are designed for efficient execution on low-resource devices and are capable of handling multimodal input, including text, image, video, and audio, to generate text outputs. They are available in pre-trained and instruction-tuned variants and were trained on data in over 140 spoken languages. Gemma 3n models use selective parameter activation technology to reduce resource requirements, allowing them to operate at an effective size of 2B and 4B parameters, which is lower than the total number of parameters they contain. The raw parameter count of the model is 6B, but the architecture design allows the model to be run with a memory footprint comparable to a traditional 2B model by offloading low-utilization matrices from the accelerator. The models use a MatFormer architecture that allows nesting sub-models within them. One sub-model is provided in this model repository, or users can access a spectrum of custom-sized models. The models support a total input context of 32K tokens and can generate outputs up to 32K tokens, subtracting the request input tokens. Inputs include text strings, images normalized to 256x256, 512x512, or 768x768 resolution and encoded to 256 tokens each, and audio data encoded to 6.25 tokens per second from a single channel. Outputs include generated text in response to the input, such as an answer to a question, analysis of image content, or a summary of a document. The models were trained on a dataset totaling approximately 11 trillion tokens, with a knowledge cutoff date of June 2024. The training data includes web documents, code, mathematics, images, and audio. Rigorous data filtering methods were applied, including CSAM filtering, sensitive data filtering, and additional methods based on content quality and safety. The models were trained using TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e hardware, leveraging JAX and ML Pathways software for efficient training. Evaluation results show the models perform well across various benchmarks, including reasoning, factuality, multilingual tasks, STEM and code, and additional benchmarks. The models were evaluated for safety and ethics, including child safety, content safety, and representational harms. They showed safe levels of performance relative to previous Gemma models, with minimal policy violations and significant improvements in high severity violations. The models have certain limitations, including training data biases, context and task complexity, language ambiguity, factual accuracy, and common sense reasoning. Ethical considerations include bias and fairness, misinformation and misuse, transparency and accountability, and privacy violations. The models are intended for a wide range of applications, including content creation, research, and education. They provide high-performance open generative model implementations designed for responsible AI development.

instruction: Can you generate a 720p video based on a detailed text description?
description: This repository contains quantized models for HunyuanVideo-1.5, optimized for use with the LightX2V framework. The quantized models are designed to significantly reduce memory usage while maintaining high-quality video generation performance. The available models include two Diffusion Transformer (DIT) models for 720p video generation: one for image-to-video (I2V) and one for text-to-video (T2V) tasks, as well as a quantized text encoder based on the Qwen2.5-VL LLM architecture. These models use FP8-E4M3 quantization with the SGL (SGLang) kernel scheme, which provides up to 50% reduction in VRAM usage, minimal quality degradation compared to full precision models, and faster inference through optimized kernels. The quantized models are intended to be used in conjunction with the original HunyuanVideo-1.5 model weights and require specific configurations to be applied before the generator is created. Performance benefits include lower VRAM requirements, enabling use on GPUs with less memory, faster inference due to optimized kernels, and preserved visual quality. The models support both T2V and I2V tasks with specific parameters such as frame count, guidance scale, and aspect ratio. The original HunyuanVideo-1.5 model weights are still required for operation. For best performance, SageAttention 2 is recommended as the attention mode. The models are released under the Apache 2.0 License.

instruction: Render a video of a bustling train station with clear outlines of people and trains.
description: This model is a ControlNet module designed for the Wan2.2 text-to-video generation system, specifically tailored for canny edge detection. It follows the same approach as other ControlNet modules for Wan2.2. The model is intended to be used with the ComfyUI framework and requires the latest version of the diffusers library. It supports inference tasks for generating video content based on textual prompts and control signals derived from canny edge maps. The model architecture includes a custom WanControlnet component, which is integrated with the Wan2.2 base model. The model is evaluated through inference examples that demonstrate its ability to generate high-resolution video outputs with specific visual characteristics, such as close-up shots with soft lighting and detailed imagery. The model is provided with a Python-based implementation that includes loading the base model, setting up the pipeline, and processing video frames with canny detection. The model is available for use under the terms of the associated GitHub repository. The model has been developed by Karachev Denis and is published on GitHub. For professional support and recommendations, users are directed to contact welcomedenk@gmail.com.

instruction: Would it be possible to generate a 3D version of this architectural sketch for a design review?
description: TripoSR is a fast and feed-forward 3D generative model developed in collaboration between Stability AI and Tripo AI. The model incorporates a series of technical advancements over the LRM model in terms of both data curation as well as model and training improvements. We closely follow the network architecture for the model design. The model type is feed-forward 3D reconstruction from a single image, and it is available under the MIT license. We train TripoSR for 5 days on 22 GPU nodes each with 8 A100 40GB GPUs. We use renders from the dataset, utilizing our enhanced rendering method that more closely replicate the distribution of images found in the real world, significantly improving our model’s ability to generalize. We selected a carefully curated subset of the Objaverse dataset for the training data, which is available under the CC-BY license. The model should not be used to intentionally create or disseminate 3D models that people would foreseeably find disturbing, distressing, or offensive; or content that propagates historical or current stereotypes.

instruction: Can you explain the meaning behind the symbols and text in this ancient manuscript image?
description: Qwen3-VL is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced Thinking editions for flexible deployment. Key enhancements include the ability to operate PC and mobile GUIs by recognizing elements, understanding functions, invoking tools, and completing tasks. It can generate Draw.io, HTML, CSS, and JS from images and videos. The model supports advanced spatial perception, including judging object positions, viewpoints, and occlusions, with capabilities for 2D and 3D grounding. It handles long contexts and video understanding with a native 256K context length, expandable to 1M, enabling full recall and second-level indexing for books and hours-long video. The model excels in multimodal reasoning, particularly in STEM and Math, with causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. It features upgraded visual recognition with broader, higher-quality pretraining that enables recognition of a wide range of subjects, including celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. The model supports expanded OCR in 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare or ancient characters and jargon. It provides enhanced text understanding on par with pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE for full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings, enhancing long-horizon video reasoning. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. The model is designed for deployment in a variety of settings, with performance optimized for both text and vision tasks. It is intended for use in applications requiring visual and textual understanding, including but not limited to agent interaction, coding from visual input, and spatial reasoning. The model is available as Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, and its code is compatible with the latest Hugging Face Transformers library. The model supports a range of generation hyperparameters for both vision-language and text-only tasks, including settings for sampling strategies, repetition penalties, and output length. The model is described in technical reports and academic publications available on arXiv.

instruction: Can you detect the emotional positivity or negativity in this voice?
description: This model implements dimensional emotion classification as described in the paper 'Vox-Profile: A Speech Foundation Model Benchmark for Characterizing Diverse Speaker and Speech Traits.' The training pipeline is the top-performing solution (SAILER) in the INTERSPEECH 2025—Speech Emotion Challenge. Unlike the official challenge submission, this system does not use transcripts and is designed as a speech-only system to maintain simplicity while ensuring effectiveness. The model is trained on the MSP-Podcast dataset and is capable of predicting three emotional dimensions: Arousal (ranging from 0 for calm to 1 for active), Valence (ranging from 0 for negative to 1 for positive), and Dominance (ranging from 0 for weak to 1 for strong). The model may be sensitive to content information when making emotion predictions, which can be a beneficial feature for classifying emotions from online content. The model is based on the WavLM architecture and is available through Huggingface under the identifier 'tiantiaf/wavlm-large-msp-podcast-emotion-dim.' It is recommended to use audio inputs that are no longer than 15 seconds, sampled at 16kHz, and in mono channel format. The model is released under the Open RAIL license, and users are expected to respect the privacy and consent of data subjects and comply with relevant laws and regulations. The model is not intended for clinical or diagnostic applications, surveillance, privacy-invasive applications, or commercial use.

instruction: How can I train a high-quality TTS system in under a day?
description: Linacodec is an audio tokenizer that compresses audio into 12.5 tokens per second (171 bps) and decodes to 48khz audio. It offers 60x more compression than DAC, with a 48khz output that is much clearer than the standard 16khz or 24khz. The encoder operates at 200x realtime speed, and the decoder runs at 400x realtime speed, with even faster performance when using batching. Linacodec indirectly supports tasks such as voice conversion, audio super-resolution, and audio denoising. Audio tokenizers contribute to the speed, quality, and capability of TTS and ASR models, and Linacodec significantly improves upon previous codecs in these areas. It enables TTS models to run at 800x realtime, which is 8x faster than previously reported, and allows high-quality TTS models to be trained in less than one day. It is versatile, working for both Text-to-Speech and Speech-to-Text applications, unlike most other codecs. The model is licensed under CC-BY-4.0, which permits use for any purpose, including commercial use, provided the original creator is credited.

instruction: Detect and label any items in this image that match the phrase 'outdoor sports equipment'.
description: LLMDet (large variant) was proposed by Shenghao Fu, Qize Yang, Qijie Mo, Junkai Yan, Xihan Wei, Jingke Meng, Xiaohua Xie, and Wei-Shi Zheng. LLMDet improves upon previous models by co-training with a large language model. The model is designed for zero-shot object detection and can be used for this task without prior training on specific object categories. The checkpoints for LLMDet are available for inference only and do not include the large language model used during training. The inference process is identical to that of other similar models. The model was trained on a combination of datasets including O365, GoldG, GRIT, V3Det, O365V2, and OpenImageV6, along with GroundingCap-1M. Evaluation results on LVIS show that the large variant of LLMDet achieves a MiniVal APr of 51.1, MiniVal APc of 45.1, MiniVal APf of 46.1, and MiniVal AP of 56.6. On Val1.0, it achieves an APr of 42.0, APc of 31.6, APf of 38.8, and AP of 50.2. The model is intended for use in open-vocabulary object detection tasks and supports inference with custom text labels provided as input. Users are advised that the checkpoints provided are for inference only and do not include the training components.

instruction: I want to build a search engine that understands both text and images. Can you assist?
description: This is a W4A16 quantized version of a state-of-the-art multimodal embedding model. The quantization was performed using AutoRound with the AutoAWQ backend. The quantized model achieves approximately 3.5 GB memory usage, compared to 8.4 GB for the original model, enabling deployment on consumer GPUs while maintaining competitive retrieval performance. The model has 4.0B parameters and uses W4A16 quantization (4-bit weights, 16-bit activations). The calibration sequence length used during quantization is 1024, and the quantization configuration includes 4 bits, a group size of 128, and symmetric quantization. The calibration dataset used is NeelNanda/pile-10k, with 560 samples and 1000 iterations, and a batch size of 80. A total of 252 layers were quantized, while 105 vision-related layers remain in FP16/BF16 to preserve visual feature quality. The embedding dimension is 320, and the model supports up to 1280 visual tokens. On the ViDoRe benchmark, the model achieves an average NDCG@5 of 0.69768 across all languages, representing a -0.36% change from the original FP16 model. For English-only evaluation, the model achieves an average NDCG@5 of 0.74582, a -0.21% change from the original. Overall, the model shows quality retention of approximately 99.6%, with 17 benchmarks improved and 23 degraded. The model is suitable for deployment on GPUs with limited memory, with 8 GB GPUs supporting a batch size of approximately 64, 12 GB GPUs supporting a batch size of approximately 256, and 16 GB and 24 GB GPUs supporting high batch sizes. The calibration sequence length refers to the length used during quantization and not the maximum sequence length supported by the model. The model is designed for tasks involving retrieval, particularly in multimodal settings. The model supports inference using the AutoProcessor and AutoModel from the Transformers library, with specific dependencies including torch, torchvision, auto-round, autoawq, transformers, pillow, and requests. The model is released under a license consistent with the original model. Limitations include reduced precision due to 4-bit quantization, the non-quantized vision encoder, and performance dependence on the inference backend. The model is based on an original model by Alibaba, with quantization performed using tools developed by Intel. If used, the model should be cited along with the original model in a bibtex format.

instruction: Can you analyze the body posture of a person in this video frame?
description: YOLO26-M-pose is a variant of the Ultralytics YOLO26 model, specifically optimized for human keypoint detection. It is the latest evolution in the YOLO series, designed from the ground up for edge and low-power devices. The model operates with an input size of 640 pixels and achieves a mean average precision (mAP) of 68.8 for pose estimation across the 50-95 confidence range and 89.6 for the 50 confidence threshold. On CPU using ONNX, inference takes 218.0 milliseconds, while on T4 TensorRT10, it runs at 5.0 milliseconds. The model contains 21.5 million parameters and 73.1 billion FLOPs. YOLO26 is built on three core principles: simplicity, deployment efficiency, and training innovation. It is a native end-to-end model that eliminates the need for non-maximum suppression (NMS), resulting in faster and lighter inference. The end-to-end design reduces pipeline complexity, lowers latency, and improves deployment robustness. The model introduces the MuSGD optimizer, a hybrid of SGD and Muon, inspired by Moonshot AI's Kimi K2 breakthroughs in LLM training, which provides enhanced stability and faster convergence. YOLO26-M-pose includes features such as Residual Log-Likelihood Estimation (RLE) for more accurate keypoint localization, optimized decoding for increased inference speed, DFL removal for simplified inference and broader hardware compatibility, and up to 43% faster CPU inference for edge computing.

instruction: Can you explain how to handle multilingual input with the latest version of the Gemma 3n system?
description: Gemma 3n E4B IT is a fine-tuned version of the Gemma 3n model, produced using a modified abliteration engine. It has the best abliteration scores and can be used without a specialized system prompt for removing censorship. Use it with extreme caution and care. This repository contains static quants, while the quants have been moved to a separate repository and regenerated with a larger multilingual dataset. Vision support has been merged into llama.cpp, requiring a git pull and rebuild. Abliteration scores include 11 refusals out of 100 and a KL divergence of 0.1422. The model has 26 abliterated layers, 9 skipped layers, and modifications to 8 attn.o_proj matrices and 26 mlp.down_proj matrices. Gemma 3n models are a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models from Google, built using the same research and technology as the Gemini models. They are designed for efficient execution on low-resource devices and can handle multimodal input, including text, image, video, and audio, generating text outputs. These models were trained with data in over 140 spoken languages. Gemma 3n models use selective parameter activation technology to reduce resource requirements, allowing them to operate at an effective size of 2B and 4B parameters. The models support input in the form of text strings, images normalized to specific resolutions, audio data encoded at a specific rate, and a total input context of 32K tokens. Outputs include generated text in response to the input, with a total output length up to 32K tokens. The models were trained on a dataset of approximately 11 trillion tokens, with a knowledge cutoff date of June 2024. The training data includes web documents, code, mathematics, images, and audio. Rigorous filtering methods were applied to exclude harmful and sensitive content. Gemma was trained using TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e hardware, leveraging JAX and ML Pathways software for efficient training. The models were evaluated on a range of benchmarks, showing strong performance in reasoning, factuality, multilingual tasks, STEM and code, and additional categories. Ethics and safety evaluations were conducted, covering child safety, content safety, and representational harms. The models have limitations related to training data, context and task complexity, language ambiguity, factual accuracy, and common sense. Ethical considerations include bias and fairness, misinformation and misuse, transparency and accountability, and privacy. The models are intended for use in content creation, research, and education, with guidelines for responsible use provided.

instruction: Find and tag all the drug-related terms in this paragraph from a clinical guideline.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition in biomedical literature. It identifies and extracts chemical compounds and substances from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the BC4CHEMD dataset, a manually annotated corpus for chemical entity recognition from the BioCreative IV challenge. The BC4CHEMD dataset contains PubMed abstracts with chemical entities annotated according to ChEBI guidelines and is used to benchmark named entity recognition models in identifying chemical compounds, drugs, and other chemical substances in scientific literature. The model can identify and classify the following entity types: B-CHEM and I-CHEM. It achieves an F1 score of 0.93, precision of 0.92, recall of 0.94, and accuracy of 0.99. The model is based on the ModernBERT-large architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is suitable for use in clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. If used in research or applications, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: Show me the 3D segmentation of the spleen based on the prompt 'enlarged spleen with homogeneous signal intensity' in this abdominal MRI.
description: VoxTell is a state-of-the-art 3D vision-language segmentation model that directly maps free-form text prompts to volumetric masks in medical imaging. Unlike traditional segmentation models that require predefined class labels, VoxTell accepts natural language descriptions ranging from single words to full clinical sentences, enabling intuitive and flexible medical image analysis. The model is designed for both anatomical and pathological structures across multiple imaging modalities (CT, PET, MRI), being trained on 1000+ familiar concepts while maintaining strong generalization to related unseen classes through its multi-stage vision-language fusion architecture. VoxTell supports free-text prompting to generate 3D segmentation masks using natural language descriptions, multi-modality support across CT, PET, and MRI imaging modalities, comprehensive anatomy coverage including brain, thorax, abdomen, pelvis, musculoskeletal system, and extremities, and flexible granularity from coarse anatomical labels to fine-grained pathological findings. VoxTell v1.1 is the recommended version, trained on all datasets from the paper and additional sources (190 datasets, ~68,500 volumes), with a 95% probability of sampling from the semantic datasets corpus and 5% probability from image-text-mask triplets from instance-focused datasets. It is recommended for general application, inference, and fine-tuning, maximizing supervision and concept coverage for stronger general-purpose performance. VoxTell v1.0 is deprecated and not recommended for general use, having been used for the experiments in the paper but containing known issues that have been fixed in v1.1. VoxTell employs a multi-stage vision-language fusion approach, including an image encoder that processes 3D volumetric input into latent feature representations, a prompt encoder using a frozen model to embed text prompts, a prompt decoder that transforms text queries and image latents into multi-scale text features, and an image decoder that fuses visual and textual information at multiple resolutions using MaskFormer-style query-image fusion with deep supervision. The model is intended for research in vision-language models for medical image analysis, text-promptable or automated segmentation of anatomical structures in medical imaging, and identification and delineation of pathological findings. It is not intended for clinical diagnosis without expert radiologist review, real-time emergency medical decision-making, or commercial use. VoxTell achieves state-of-the-art performance on anatomical and pathological segmentation tasks across multiple medical imaging benchmarks. Performance may vary on imaging modalities or anatomical regions underrepresented in training data. Prompting structures absent from the image and never seen on this modality may lead to undesired results. Text prompt quality and specificity affects segmentation accuracy. The model is not validated for direct clinical use without expert review. Images must be in RAS orientation for correct anatomical localization, and performance may degrade on images with very uncommon voxel spacings. The model does not resample images to a standardized spacing for faster inference. Known issues include compatibility problems with PyTorch 2.9.0 causing OOM errors during inference, which should be avoided by using PyTorch 2.8.0 or earlier.

instruction: Can you determine the media code for a news piece about a local church's anniversary celebration?
description: This is an xlm-roberta-large model that has been fine-tuned on multilingual training data in English, German, Hungarian, Spanish, and Slovakian, labeled with media codes. The model incorporates seven additional media codes: State and Local Government Administration (24), Weather and Natural Disaster (26), Fires (27), Sports and Recreation (29), Death Notices (30), Churches and Religion (31), and Other, Miscellaneous and Human Interest (99). The model is designed for text classification tasks and is intended to categorize input text into one of the defined media codes. It is based on the xlm-roberta-large architecture and is used with a sentencepiece tokenizer. The model achieves an overall accuracy of 74%, with a macro average precision of 0.76, recall of 0.74, and F1-score of 0.73. The weighted average precision, recall, and F1-score are also 0.76, 0.74, and 0.73, respectively. Per-class performance varies, with some classes achieving high precision and recall, such as Death Notices (precision 0.9565, recall 0.88) and others showing lower performance, such as Other, Miscellaneous and Human Interest (precision 0.3789, recall 0.72). The model is used by an open-source and free natural language processing tool designed to simplify and speed up projects for comparative research. Model performance can be improved by extending the training sets, and contributions of CAP-coded corpora in any domain and language are welcomed. The model requires a token for access, and users may need to pass the token parameter when loading the model. In earlier versions of the Transformers package, the use_auth_token parameter may be required instead. If a RuntimeError occurs when loading the model using the from_pretrained() method, adding ignore_mismatched_sizes=True should resolve the issue.

instruction: I need to convert this video into a form that can be used for visual analysis—can you assist?
description: The VideoMAE-v2 (Large-sized model, Pretrained on UnlabeledHybrid-1M) is a model pre-trained for 800 epochs in a self-supervised manner on the UnlabeldHybrid-1M dataset. It was introduced in the paper 'VideoMAE V2: Scaling Video Masked Autoencoders with Dual Masking' by Wang et al. and first released in 2023. The model is intended for video feature extraction. It can be used by loading the model and processor from the 'transformers' library, processing video inputs into tensors, and extracting features through the model. The model is based on the VideoMAE architecture and incorporates dual masking for training. The model is available under the identifier 'OpenGVLab/VideoMAEv2-Large'.

instruction: Based on the tone, can you guess if the person is male or female?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of an unspecified base model on an unknown dataset. It is designed for gender classification and achieves a loss of 0.1142 and an accuracy of 0.9639 on the evaluation set. The model was trained for three epochs using a learning rate of 5e-05, a training batch size of 256, and an evaluation batch size of 256. The random seed used was 42. The optimizer used is ADAMW_TORCH_FUSED with betas of (0.9, 0.999), an epsilon of 1e-08, and no additional optimizer arguments. The learning rate scheduler type is linear with a warmup ratio of 0.05. During training, the training loss decreased from 0.2321 to 0.1002 over 400 training steps, while the validation loss stabilized at 0.1142. The accuracy on the validation set increased from 0.9434 to 0.9658 and then stabilized at 0.9639. The model was developed using Transformers 4.57.3, Pytorch 2.9.0+cu126, Datasets 4.4.2, and Tokenizers 0.22.1.

instruction: Generate an image of a steampunk-style robot walking through a desert with a sun setting behind it.
description: The FLUX.2 [klein] model family are the fastest image models to date, unifying generation and editing in a single compact architecture, delivering state-of-the-art quality with end-to-end inference in as low as under a second. Built for applications that require real-time image generation without sacrificing quality. FLUX.2 [klein] 9B is a 9 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating images from text descriptions and supports multi-reference editing capabilities. It is the flagship small model, defining the Pareto frontier for quality versus latency across text-to-image, single-reference editing, and multi-reference generation. It matches or exceeds models five times its size in under half a second. It is built on a 9B flow model with an 8B Qwen3 text embedder, step-distilled to 4 inference steps. It is a distilled model for sub-second image generation with outstanding quality. It supports text-to-image and image-to-image multi-reference editing in a single unified model. It is suitable for real-time generation and integration into applications. It is ideal for creative exploration with excellent prompt adherence and output diversity. It is available for non-commercial use. The model is not intended or able to provide factual information. While the model can output text, the text rendered may be inaccurate or subject to distortion. As a statistical model, this checkpoint may represent or amplify biases observed in the training data. The model may fail to generate output that matches the prompts. Prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting style. The model and its derivatives may not be used in any way that violates applicable law, for the purpose of exploiting, harming or attempting to exploit or harm minors in any way, including but not limited to the solicitation, creation, acquisition, or dissemination of child exploitative content, to generate or disseminate deceptive, fraudulent, misleading or otherwise harmful content, to generate or disseminate personal identifiable information that can be used to harm an individual, to harass, abuse, threaten, stalk, or bully individuals or groups of individuals, to create non-consensual intimate imagery or illegal pornographic content, or for fully automated decision making or high risk applications that adversely impact an individual's legal rights or otherwise create or modify a binding, enforceable obligation. The model fits in approximately 29GB VRAM and is accessible on NVIDIA RTX 4090 and above. Black Forest Labs is committed to the responsible development and deployment of models. Prior to releasing the FLUX.2 family of models, the company evaluated and mitigated a number of risks in model checkpoints and hosted services, including the generation of unlawful content, including child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and nonconsensual intimate imagery (NCII). Pre-training mitigation included filtering pre-training data for multiple categories of 'not safe for work' (NSFW) and known CSAM to help prevent a user generating unlawful content in response to text prompts or uploaded images. The company partnered with an independent nonprofit organization dedicated to preventing online abuse to filter known CSAM from the training data. Post-training mitigation included multiple rounds of targeted fine-tuning to provide additional mitigation against potential abuse, including both text-to-image (T2I) and image-to-image (I2I) attacks. By inhibiting certain behaviors and suppressing certain concepts in the trained model, these techniques can help to prevent a user generating synthetic CSAM or NCII from a text prompt, or transforming an uploaded image into synthetic CSAM or NCII. Ongoing evaluation included multiple internal and external third-party evaluations of model checkpoints to identify further opportunities for mitigation. External third-party evaluations focused on eliciting CSAM and NCII through adversarial testing with (i) text-only prompts, (ii) a single uploaded reference image with text prompts, and (iii) multiple uploaded reference images with text prompts. Based on this feedback, further safety fine-tuning was conducted to produce the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] models. The release decision included a final third-party evaluation of the proposed release checkpoints, focused on T2I and I2I generation of synthetic CSAM and NCII, including a comparison with other open-weight T2I and I2I models. The final FLUX.2 [klein] checkpoints demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs in complex generation and editing tasks, and demonstrated higher resilience than leading open-weight models across these risk categories. Based on these findings, the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models were approved for release under an Apache 2.0 license, and the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models were released under a non-commercial license to support third-party research and development. Inference filters for NSFW and protected content in inputs and outputs are included in the repository for the FLUX.2 [klein] models. Filters or manual review must be used with the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models under the terms of the FLUX Non-Commercial License, and deployers are encouraged to implement these mitigations when using the FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models. Where these features are implemented on hosted services, multiple filters may be applied to intercept text prompts, uploaded images, and output images. Both in-house and third-party filters are used to mitigate against harmful outputs, such as CSAM and NCII outputs, including filters provided by external organizations. Content provenance features can help users and platforms better identify, label, and interpret AI-generated content online. The inference code for FLUX.2 [klein] implements an example of pixel-layer watermarking. Additionally, the repository includes links to a standard for metadata. The API for FLUX.2 [klein] applies cryptographically-signed C2PA metadata to downloaded output content to indicate that images were produced with the model. Acceptable use of the models and access to the API are governed by policies set out in applicable documentation, including the FLUX Non-Commercial License (for non-commercial open-weight users), Developer Terms of Service, Self-Hosted Commercial License Terms, and Usage Policy (for commercial open-weight model users), and Developer Terms of Service, FLUX API Service Terms, and Usage Policy (for API users). These prohibit the generation of unlawful content or the use of generated content for unlawful, defamatory, or abusive purposes. Developers and users must consent to these conditions to access the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models on Hugging Face. Black Forest Labs takes model safety seriously and may monitor use to detect misuse or abuse of models and services. A dedicated email address (safety@blackforestlabs.ai) is provided to solicit feedback from the community. The company maintains a reporting relationship with organizations such as the and the and welcomes ongoing engagement with authorities, developers, and researchers to share intelligence about emerging risks and develop effective mitigations.

instruction: Please evaluate which of these sources best addresses the topic of space exploration advancements.
description: The Llama Nemotron Reranking 1B model is optimized for providing a logit score that represents how relevant a document(s) is to a given query. The model was fine-tuned for multilingual, cross-lingual text question-answering retrieval, with support for long documents (up to 8192 tokens). This model was evaluated on 26 languages: English, Arabic, Bengali, Chinese, Czech, Danish, Dutch, Finnish, French, German, Hebrew, Hindi, Hungarian, Indonesian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Norwegian, Persian, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Spanish, Swedish, Thai, and Turkish. This model is a component in a text retrieval system to improve the overall accuracy. A text retrieval system often uses an embedding model (dense) or lexical search (sparse) index to return relevant text passages given the input. A reranking model can be used to rerank the potential candidate into a final order. The reranking model has the question-passage pairs as an input and therefore, can process cross attention between the words. It’s not feasible to apply a Ranking model on all documents in the knowledge base, therefore, ranking models are often deployed in combination with embedding models. This model is ready for commercial use. The Llama Nemotron Reranking 1B model is a part of the NeMo Retriever collection of NIM, which provide state-of-the-art, commercially-ready models and microservices, optimized for the lowest latency and highest throughput. It features a production-ready information retrieval pipeline with enterprise support. The models that form the core of this solution have been trained using responsibly selected, auditable data sources. With multiple pre-trained models available as starting points, developers can also readily customize them for their domain-specific use cases, such as information technology, human resource help assistants, and research & development research assistants. We are excited to announce the open sourcing of this commercial embedding model.
