For users interested in deploying this model in production environments, it is also available via the model API in NVIDIA Inference Microservices (NIM). The Llama Nemotron Reranking 1B model is most suitable for users who want to improve their multilingual retrieval tasks by reranking a set of candidates for a given question. The Llama Nemotron Reranking 1B model is a transformer cross-encoder fine-tuned with contrastive learning. We employ bi-directional attention when fine-tuning for higher accuracy. The last embedding output by the decoder model is used with a mean pooling strategy, and a binary classification head is fine-tuned for the ranking task. Ranking models for text ranking are typically trained as a cross-encoder for sentence classification. This involves predicting the relevancy of a sentence pair (for example, question and chunked passages). The CrossEntropy loss is used to maximize the likelihood of passages containing information to answer the question and minimize the likelihood for (negative) passages that do not contain information to answer the question. We trained the model on public datasets described in the Dataset and Training section. The model takes a pair of texts as input, formatted as a list of text pairs. The input parameters are one-dimensional. The model was trained on question and answering over text documents from multiple languages. It was evaluated to work successfully with up to a sequence length of 8192 tokens. Longer texts are recommended to be either chunked or truncated. The model outputs floats, formatted as a list of floats, with one-dimensional parameters. Each value corresponds to a raw logit. Users can choose to apply a Sigmoid activation function to the logits to convert them into probabilities during model usage. The model requires transformers version 4.47.1. The model is compatible with NVIDIA Ampere, NVIDIA Hopper, and NVIDIA Lovelace hardware microarchitectures, and it supports Linux as the operating system. The Llama Nemotron Reranking 1B model is part of the NeMo Retriever collection of NIM and is identified by the short name llama-nemotron-rerank-1b-v2. The model was trained on 800k samples from public datasets. The evaluation datasets are based on three TextQA datasets, the TechQA dataset, MIRACL, MLDR, and MLQA multilingual retrieval datasets, which are all public datasets. The sizes range between 10,000s up to 5M depending on the dataset. The pipeline llama-nemotron-embed-1b-v2 + llama-nemotron-rerank-1b-v2 provides high BEIR+TechQA accuracy with multilingual and crosslingual support. The llama-nemotron-embed-1b-v2 ranking model is 3.5x smaller than the nv-rerankqa-mistral-4b-v3 model. The pipeline achieves an average Recall@5 of 73.64% on the NQ, HotpotQA, FiQA, and TechQA datasets. The model achieves an average Recall@5 of 65.80% on the MIRACL multilingual datasets. The model achieves an average Recall@5 of 86.83% on the MLQA dataset with different languages. The model achieves an average Recall@5 of 70.69% on the MLDR dataset. The model’s max sequence length is 8192. Therefore, the longer text inputs should be truncated. The model does not always guarantee to retrieve the correct passage(s) for a given query. Use of this model is governed by the license. Additional information is available. NVIDIA believes Trustworthy AI is a shared responsibility and has established policies and practices to enable development for a wide array of AI applications. Developers should work with their supporting model team to ensure this model meets requirements for the relevant industry and use case and addresses unforeseen product misuse. For more detailed information on ethical considerations for this model, please see the Explainability, Bias, Safety, and Privacy sections. Please report security vulnerabilities or NVIDIA AI Concerns. The model is intended for passage ranking for question and answer retrieval. It is designed for use by generative AI creators working with conversational AI models—users who want to build a multilingual question and answer application over a large text corpus, leveraging the latest dense retrieval technologies. The model transforms the tokenized input text into a dense vector representation. The model's output is an array of float numbers (dense vector representation for the input text). The model's performance is measured in terms of accuracy, throughput, and latency. The model does not always guarantee to retrieve the correct passage(s) for a given query. The model’s max sequence length is 8192. Therefore, the longer text inputs should be truncated. The model has not been tested to deliver comparable outcomes for any adversely impacted groups. The model has been verified to meet prescribed NVIDIA quality standards. The model does not generate or reverse engineer personally-identifiable information (PII). No personal data was used to create this model. The dataset is reviewed before every release. There is no mechanism in place to honor data subject right of access or deletion of personal data. Personal data was not collected for the development of this model by NVIDIA. Data labeling (annotation, metadata) complies with privacy laws. Data is not compliant with data subject requests for data correction or removal, if such a request was made. The model application is text reranking for retrieval. There is no physical safety impact. Use of this model is governed by the license. Additional information is available. The Principle of least privilege (PoLP) is applied limiting access for dataset generation and model development. Restrictions enforce dataset access during training, and dataset license constraints are adhered to.

instruction: Can I train a model to better understand the encoded tokens for a specific speaker or language?
description: X-Codec2 is a neural audio codec designed to improve speech synthesis and general audio generation for large language model (LLM) pipelines. It extends the original X-Codec by refining how semantic and acoustic information is integrated and tokenized, enabling efficient and high-fidelity audio representation. The architecture of X-Codec2 is based on X-Codec with several major differences. It uses a unified semantic-acoustic tokenization approach, fusing outputs from a semantic encoder (e.g., Wav2Vec2-BERT) and an acoustic encoder into a single embedding, capturing both high-level meaning (e.g., text content, emotion) and low-level audio details (e.g., timbre). It employs a single-stage vector quantization (VQ) using a single-layer Feature-Space Quantization (FSQ), which provides stability and compatibility with causal, autoregressive LLMs, in contrast to the multi-layer residual VQ used in most approaches. X-Codec2 includes semantic supervision during training, adding a semantic reconstruction loss to ensure that the discrete tokens preserve meaningful linguistic and emotional information, which is crucial for TTS tasks. Its 1D token structure is designed to align with the autoregressive modeling in LLMs like LLaMA, improving training efficiency and downstream compatibility. The model supports encoding and decoding of audio, with the ability to extract audio codes and reconstruct audio values. It is intended for use in LLM pipelines and is compatible with the Transformers library, though it is not yet merged into the main branch. Users can install it from source and use it with Python code to load the model, process audio data, and perform encoding and decoding operations. The model was contributed by external contributors, and the original code and checkpoints are available for reference.

instruction: I need to know if this clip is trustworthy or if it might be a deepfake.
description: The SOTA Deepfake Detector - DFD is a spatiotemporal adaptation built on a frozen CLIP-ViT-Large backbone with lightweight spatiotemporal adapters inserted to learn temporal relationships across frames. The backbone parameters are frozen; only the adapters and final classification head are trainable. The model is designed to classify short video clips or frame sequences as either real or deepfake/manipulated. The input consists of 12 frames per video, each of size 384×384 pixels, and the model outputs logits for two classes: 0 = real, 1 = fake. The model uses a PyTorch framework with Hugging Face Transformers and was trained using BF16 mixed precision. The model has approximately 5,255,938 trainable parameters, which is about 1.7% of the total 308 million parameters. The model was trained on the DFD (Deep Fake Detection) dataset, which contains 3,431 videos, with 363 real videos (10.6%) and 3,068 fake videos (89.4%). A total of 41,172 frames were extracted from the videos. The training used a 90/10 stratified train/validation split and a WeightedRandomSampler to address class imbalance. The training configuration included a batch size of 16 (effective 32 with gradient accumulation), AdamW optimizer with weight decay of 0.05, a learning rate of 5e-6 with cosine decay and 10% warmup, and 12 training epochs. The loss function was cross-entropy with label smoothing of 0.1, and gradient clipping was applied with a max_grad_norm of 1.0. Training augmentations included random horizontal flip and brightness jitter. The model was trained on an NVIDIA L4 GPU with 24GB VRAM, and all random seeds were fixed for reproducibility. The best validation accuracy achieved was 84.88%, with real class detection ranging from approximately 47–55% and fake class detection ranging from approximately 64–93%. The training loss converged from 0.7097 to 0.6921 over 12 epochs. The model has several known limitations, including a highly imbalanced validation set (89% fake), a small number of real videos (363) that limits generalization to unseen real samples, and optimization for the DFD dataset, which may require fine-tuning for other deepfake types. The temporal context is limited to 12 frames (approximately 0.4–1s depending on FPS), so long-term artifacts may be missed. The model is intended for use in research on deepfake detection, benchmarking against other detectors, and integration into content-moderation pipelines with caution. It is not intended for use in medical, legal, or other high-stakes decisions without human review, or for use on domains/styles substantially different from the DFD dataset without further fine-tuning. The model is available on the Hugging Face Hub under the repository Arko007/deepfake-detector-dfd-sota and is licensed under the Apache-2.0 license. The model files include pytorch_model.bin (1.28GB) and config.json. Inference involves loading a checkpoint, initializing the model, preprocessing input frames using the CLIP image processor, and running a forward pass to obtain logits, which are then converted to softmax probabilities and a predicted class. The model expects input tensors of shape (batch_size, 12, 3, 384, 384) with dtype float32 or bf16 where supported. The model can be used with helper functions to extract frames from a video or folder, preprocess them, and perform inference. The model is not intended for production use without additional robustness, error handling, and optimization.

instruction: What’s the best way to switch between speaking and typing in a conversation?
description: LFM2-Audio-1.5B is the first end-to-end audio foundation model from Liquid AI. Designed with low latency and real-time conversation in mind, it enables seamless conversational interaction at 1.5 billion parameters, achieving capabilities on par with much larger models. LFM2-Audio is an end-to-end multimodal speech and text language model that does not require separate ASR and TTS components. The model consists of a pretrained LFM2 model as its multimodal backbone, along with a FastConformer-based audio encoder to handle continuous audio inputs, and a RQ-transformer generating discrete Mimi tokens as audio output. LFM2-Audio supports two distinct generation routines: interleaved generation, which enables real-time speech-to-speech conversational chatbot capabilities where audio generation latency is key, and sequential generation, which is suited for non-conversational tasks such as ASR or TTS, and allows the model to switch generated modality on the fly. The model has 1.2 billion parameters in the language model component, an audio encoder with 115 million parameters, a hybrid convolutional and attention-based backbone, an audio tokenizer using 8 codebooks, a context length of 32,768 tokens, a vocabulary size of 65,536 for text and 2049*8 for audio, and uses bfloat16 precision. It is licensed under the LFM Open License v1.0 and supports English. The model achieves a VoiceBench score of 56.78, with AlpacaEval 3.71, CommonEval 3.49, WildVoice 3.17, SD-QA 30.56, MMSU 31.95, OBQA 44.40, BBH 30.54, IFEval 98.85, and ADVBench 67.33. For ASR, it has a word error rate (WER) of 7.24, with Open 15.58, AMI 10.67, GigaSpeech 2.01, LibriSpeech-clean 4.39, LibriSpeech-other 3.56, and TED-LIUM 3.56. The model is suitable for multi-turn, multi-modal chat, where it can switch between audio and text modalities. It is available via the `liquid-audio` package and can be run using a Gradio demo interface. The model is intended for use with the LFM Open License v1.0, and the audio encoder is based on code licensed under other terms. The model is described in the LFM2 Technical Report, arXiv:2511.23404.

instruction: Generate a peaceful winter scene with snow-covered trees, a cabin, and a warm glow from the windows.
description: This is a GGUF quantized version of the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B model. The unsloth/FLUX.2-klein-9B-GGUF model uses a methodology for state-of-the-art performance, with important layers upcasted to higher precision and tooling from city96. The FLUX.2 [klein] model family are the fastest image models to date, unifying generation and editing in a single compact architecture, delivering state-of-the-art quality with end-to-end inference in as low as under a second. Built for applications that require real-time image generation without sacrificing quality. FLUX.2 [klein] 9B is a 9 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating images from text descriptions and supports multi-reference editing capabilities. It is the flagship small model, defining the Pareto frontier for quality versus latency across text-to-image, single-reference editing, and multi-reference generation. It matches or exceeds models five times its size in under half a second. Built on a 9B flow model with an 8B Qwen3 text embedder, it is step-distilled to four inference steps. The model is a distilled model for sub-second image generation with outstanding quality. It supports text-to-image and image-to-image multi-reference editing in a single unified model. It is great for real-time generation and integration into applications. It is ideal for creative exploration with excellent prompt adherence and output diversity. It is available for non-commercial use. A reference implementation of FLUX.2 [klein] 9B, as well as sampling code, is provided in a dedicated repository. Developers and creatives looking to build on top of FLUX.2 [klein] 9B are encouraged to use this as a starting point. The FLUX.2 [klein] 9B model is available via the BFL API and is also available in both quantized and full-precision versions. To use FLUX.2 [klein] 9B with the Diffusers Python library, first install or upgrade diffusers. Then you can use Flux2KleinPipeline to run the model. The model is not intended or able to provide factual information. While the model can output text, text rendered may be inaccurate or subject to distortion. As a statistical model, this checkpoint may represent or amplify biases observed in the training data. The model may fail to generate output that matches the prompts. Prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting style. The model and its derivatives may not be used in any way that violates applicable law, for the purpose of exploiting, harming or attempting to exploit or harm minors in any way, including but not limited to the solicitation, creation, acquisition, or dissemination of child exploitative content, to generate or disseminate deceptive, fraudulent, misleading or otherwise harmful content, to generate or disseminate personal identifiable information that can be used to harm an individual, to harass, abuse, threaten, stalk, or bully individuals or groups of individuals, to create non-consensual intimate imagery or illegal pornographic content, or for fully automated decision making or high risk applications that adversely impact an individual's legal rights or otherwise create or modify a binding, enforceable obligation. Nothing contained in this Model Card should be interpreted as or deemed a restriction or modification to the license the model is released under. The FLUX.2 [klein] 9B model fits in approximately 29GB VRAM and is accessible on NVIDIA RTX 4090 and above. Black Forest Labs is committed to the responsible development and deployment of its models. Prior to releasing the FLUX.2 family of models, the company evaluated and mitigated a number of risks in its model checkpoints and hosted services, including the generation of unlawful content, including child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and nonconsensual intimate imagery (NCII). Pre-training mitigation included filtering pre-training data for multiple categories of 'not safe for work' (NSFW) and known CSAM to help prevent a user generating unlawful content in response to text prompts or uploaded images. The company partnered with an independent nonprofit organization dedicated to preventing online abuse to filter known CSAM from the training data. Post-training mitigation included multiple rounds of targeted fine-tuning to provide additional mitigation against potential abuse, including both text-to-image (T2I) and image-to-image (I2I) attacks. By inhibiting certain behaviors and suppressing certain concepts in the trained model, these techniques can help to prevent a user generating synthetic CSAM or NCII from a text prompt, or transforming an uploaded image into synthetic CSAM or NCII. Ongoing evaluation included multiple internal and external third-party evaluations of model checkpoints to identify further opportunities for mitigation. External third-party evaluations focused on eliciting CSAM and NCII through adversarial testing with text-only prompts, a single uploaded reference image with text prompts, and multiple uploaded reference images with text prompts. Based on this feedback, further safety fine-tuning was conducted to produce the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] models. The release decision included a final third-party evaluation of the proposed release checkpoints, focused on T2I and I2I generation of synthetic CSAM and NCII, including a comparison with other open-weight T2I and I2I models. The final FLUX.2 [klein] checkpoints demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs in complex generation and editing tasks, and demonstrated higher resilience than leading open-weight models across these risk categories. Based on these findings, the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models were approved for release under an Apache 2.0 license, and the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models were approved for release under a non-commercial license to support third-party research and development. Inference filters for NSFW and protected content in inputs and outputs are included in the repository for the FLUX.2 [klein] models. Filters or manual review must be used with the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models under the terms of the FLUX Non-Commercial License, and deployers are encouraged to implement these mitigations when using the FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models. Where these features are implemented on the company's hosted services, multiple filters may be applied to intercept text prompts, uploaded images, and output images. Both in-house and third-party filters are used to mitigate against harmful outputs, such as CSAM and NCII outputs, including filters provided by external organizations. Content provenance features can help users and platforms better identify, label, and interpret AI-generated content online. The inference code for FLUX.2 [klein] implements an example of pixel-layer watermarking. Additionally, the repository includes links to a standard for metadata. The API for FLUX.2 [klein] applies cryptographically-signed C2PA metadata to downloaded output content to indicate that images were produced with the model. Policies governing acceptable use of the models and access to the API are set out in applicable documentation, including the FLUX Non-Commercial License (for non-commercial open-weight users), Developer Terms of Service, Self-Hosted Commercial License Terms, and Usage Policy (for commercial open-weight model users), and Developer Terms of Service, FLUX API Service Terms, and Usage Policy (for API users). These prohibit the generation of unlawful content or the use of generated content for unlawful, defamatory, or abusive purposes. Developers and users must consent to these conditions to access the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models on Hugging Face. Black Forest Labs takes model safety seriously and may monitor use to detect misuse or abuse of its models and services. A dedicated email address is provided to solicit feedback from the community. The company maintains a reporting relationship with organizations such as the and the and welcomes ongoing engagement with authorities, developers, and researchers to share intelligence about emerging risks and develop effective mitigations. The model falls under a specific license. This project may contain trademarks or logos for projects, products, or services. Use of Black Forest Labs and FLUX trademarks or logos in modified versions of this project must not cause confusion or imply sponsorship or endorsement. Any use of third-party trademarks, intellectual property or logos is subject to those third-party's policies.

instruction: What are the most distinctive visual elements in this picture?
description: This is a DINOv3 Vision Transformer (ViT) model image feature encoder, distilled on the LVD-1689M dataset from the DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model is designed for image feature extraction and supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. It is based on a Vision Transformer architecture with a patch size of 16. The model has 21.6 million parameters, 6.3 GMACs, and 17.0 million activations, and processes images of size 256 x 256. The original model weights had all QKV projection biases set to zero, and for the `timm` implementation, the QKV bias is disabled (`qkv_bias=False`) for these models. Some model sizes have variants with `qkvb` in the name that have the bias enabled (`qkv_bias=True`), but set to zero, to match the behavior of `transformers` and original models. The original models keep RoPE periods as a persistent `bfloat16` buffer, while `timm` generates `float32` periods at initialization. This results in some numerical differences, but the `timm` approach is less problematic on devices without bfloat16 support and appears to work as well, if not slightly better, for fine-tuning. To match the original model outputs, the `model.rope.periods` can be converted to `bfloat16` and then to `float32`. The model is evaluated on various benchmarks, including IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair, with performance metrics such as 87.0% on IN-ReaL, 60.4% on IN-R, 50.9% on Obj.Net, 49.5% on Ox.-H, 47.0% on ADE20k, 0.403 on NYU, 72.7% on DAVIS, 56.3% on NAVI, and 50.4% on SPair. The model is also evaluated on satellite datasets such as SAT-493M, with performance metrics including 73.0% on m-BEnet, 96.5% on m-brick-kiln, 94.1% on m-eurosat, 60.6% on m-forestnet, 96.0% on m-pv4ger, 57.4% on m-so2sat, and a mean of 79.6%. For segmentation tasks, the model achieves 94.2% on m-cashew, 75.6% on m-chesapeake, 61.8% on m-NeonTree, 83.7% on m-nz-cattle, 95.2% on m-pv4ger-seg, 36.8% on m-SA-crop, and a mean of 74.5%. The model is associated with the DINOv3 paper, as well as the papers on Vision Transformers for image recognition at scale and PyTorch Image Models.

instruction: I’m curating a portfolio. Can you confirm this image doesn’t have any hidden adult content?
description: This model is a vision transformer based on the EVA architecture, fine-tuned for NSFW content classification. It has been trained to detect four categories (neutral, low, medium, high) of visual content using 100,000 synthetically labeled images. The model can be used as a binary (true/false) classifier or to obtain full output probabilities. It outperforms other publicly available models in internal benchmarks, offering the ability to select the NSFW level that suits the use case. The model was trained for 3 epochs on 3 NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPUs using two sets: training and validation. No images with a cosine similarity higher than 0.92 were included to avoid duplicates and biases between training and validation. A custom loss was created to minimize predictions that are lower than the true class. For example, it is very rare for an image labeled as 'high' to be predicted as 'neutral' (this only happens 0.46% of the time). The model supports efficient batch processing for multiple images and is recommended to be used in bf16 for optimal performance. Using torch tensors is especially indicated in cases such as use with text to image models or similar because the output is already in tensor format. The model has been evaluated on a manually labeled dataset with careful attention to avoiding biases (gender, ethnicity, etc.). In global performance comparisons, the model outperforms AdamCodd and Falconsai in accuracy, particularly on high and neutral labels. It offers greater granularity, performing excellently at identifying low and medium NSFW content. Falconsai may classify some medium and low images as not NSFW or as safe for work (SFW), which could lead to unexpected results. AdamCodd classifies both low and medium categories as NSFW, which may not be desirable depending on the use case. Furthermore, 10% of images in low and medium are considered SFW. In AI-generated content detection, the model is the best option to detect NSFW content, outperforming other models. Falconsai should be avoided for AI-generated content to prevent prediction errors. The model is licensed under the MIT License and is based on the EVA architecture as described in the paper 'EVA-02: A Visual Representation for Neon Genesis'.

instruction: What is the most probable classification for this video of a diver hitting the water?
description: V-JEPA 2 is a video understanding model developed by FAIR at Meta, extending the pretraining objectives of its predecessors to achieve state-of-the-art video understanding capabilities. It leverages large-scale data and model sizes to enhance performance. The model is based on a ViT-L architecture with a 256-pixel input size and includes a video classification head. It is pretrained on the Diving 48 dataset, which consists of diving-related video content. The model is designed to support video classification tasks, enabling the prediction of class labels for input videos. Performance is demonstrated through inference examples, where the model outputs top predicted class names along with their probabilities. The model requires specific preprocessing steps, including frame sampling and tensor conversion, to prepare input data for inference. Users are advised to install the latest version of the transformers library and use the provided code snippet to load the model and perform video classification. The model's configuration includes a frames_per_clip parameter, which defines the number of frames to sample from each video clip. The model outputs logits, which are then converted into probabilities using softmax to determine the top predicted classes. The model's training and evaluation are detailed in a technical report published by FAIR at Meta in 2025.

instruction: What text is visible in this blurry document, and can you clarify it for me?
description: Qwen3-VL is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced Thinking editions for flexible deployment. Key enhancements include the ability to operate PC and mobile GUIs by recognizing elements, understanding functions, invoking tools, and completing tasks. It can generate Draw.io, HTML, CSS, and JS from images and videos. The model supports advanced spatial perception, including judging object positions, viewpoints, and occlusions, with capabilities for 2D and 3D grounding. It handles long contexts and video understanding with a native 256K context length, expandable to 1M, enabling full recall and second-level indexing for books and hours-long video. The model excels in enhanced multimodal reasoning, particularly in STEM and Math, with causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. It features upgraded visual recognition with broader, higher-quality pretraining that can recognize a wide range of subjects, including celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. Expanded OCR supports 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare or ancient characters and jargon. The model achieves text understanding on par with pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE for full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings, enhancing long-horizon video reasoning. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. The model is designed for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library and ModelScope, with generation hyperparameters available for both visual and text tasks. The model is described in technical reports and preprints available on arXiv.

instruction: What is the expected octanol-water partition coefficient for the molecule 'C=O'? based on its molecular structure?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict the octanol-water partition coefficient (log KOW/log P), a fundamental property that indicates lipophilicity and affects bioaccumulation potential. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. It is designed for a regression task, producing a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using PyTorch Geometric and features an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model's hyperparameters include 128 hidden channels, 3 layers, 3 timesteps, 0.1 dropout, a learning rate of 0.0005, a weight decay of 0.0001, a batch size of 32, and 50 training epochs with a patience of 10. The model was trained on the KOW dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: ສະຫຼຸບບົດຄວາມນີ້ໃຫ້ຂ້ອຍໃນ 3 ໄຕຍ່າງແລະເວົ້າວ່າມີສິ່ງໃດທີ່ເຂົາເຈົ້າໄດ້ກ່າວໄວ້ທີ່ສໍາຄັນ
description: This is a Lao language summarization model fine-tuned on a dataset using the base model Gemma 3-12B Instruct. The model is designed to generate concise summaries from Lao language text. It is specifically adapted to understand and generate responses in Lao language. It was trained using a curated dataset of over 5,000 high-quality Lao input-output pairs, primarily focused on AIDC (Artificial Intelligence and Digital Content) topics. The model supports summarization, question answering, and general chat tasks. The fine-tuning was performed using LoRA with PEFT (Unsloth). The training dataset consists of 10,000 Laos supervised samples, with a sequence length of 2048 and a batch size of 2 (with gradient accumulation). The model was trained for 3–5 epochs with early stopping enabled. The model is available in GGUF format, with F16 and Q8_0 versions available. The model is based on Google's Gemma 3-12B Instruct model and is suitable for Lao language generation. The model uses the Apache 2.0 license. The evaluation metric used is the BLEU score, which assesses the quality of generated summaries against reference summaries in the dataset.

instruction: Turn this transcript into a high-quality audio file for a language learning app.
description: SpeechT5 is a model fine-tuned for speech synthesis (text-to-speech) on the LibriTTS dataset. It is based on the T5 (Text-To-Text Transfer Transformer) framework and is designed to explore encoder-decoder pre-training for self-supervised speech and text representation learning. The SpeechT5 framework includes a shared encoder-decoder network and six modal-specific (speech/text) pre/post-nets. Input speech or text is processed through the pre-nets, the shared encoder-decoder network models the sequence-to-sequence transformation, and the post-nets generate the output in the speech or text modality based on the decoder's output. The model leverages large-scale unlabeled speech and text data to learn a unified-modal representation, aiming to improve modeling capabilities for both modalities. A cross-modal vector quantization approach is used to align textual and speech information into a unified semantic space by randomly mixing up speech/text states with latent units as the interface between encoder and decoder. Extensive evaluations have demonstrated the effectiveness of the SpeechT5 framework on a wide range of spoken language processing tasks, including automatic speech recognition, speech synthesis, speech translation, voice conversion, speech enhancement, and speaker identification. The model was developed by Junyi Ao, Rui Wang, Long Zhou, Chengyi Wang, Shuo Ren, Yu Wu, Shujie Liu, Tom Ko, Qing Li, Yu Zhang, Zhihua Wei, Yao Qian, Jinyu Li, and Furu Wei. It is intended for use in speech synthesis and related spoken language processing tasks. Users should be aware of the model's potential risks, biases, and limitations, though further information is needed for detailed recommendations.

instruction: Can you generate a visual representation of the depth in this image?
description: DPT-Large, also known as MiDaS 3.0, is a Dense Prediction Transformer (DPT) model trained on 1.4 million images for monocular depth estimation. It was introduced in the paper by Ranftl et al. (2021) and first released in March 2022. DPT uses the Vision Transformer (ViT) as a backbone and adds a neck and head on top for monocular depth estimation. The model is designed for zero-shot monocular depth estimation and can be used by anyone performing such tasks. It is typically intended to be fine-tuned for specific applications and should not be used to intentionally create hostile or alienating environments for people. The model was trained on a dataset called MIX 6, which contains around 1.4 million images, and was initialized with ImageNet-pretrained weights. During training, images were resized such that the longer side was 384 pixels, and random square crops of size 384 were used, with random horizontal flips for data augmentation. The model was evaluated on several benchmarks, including DIW WHDR, ETH3D AbsRel, Sintel AbsRel, KITTI δ>1.25, NYU δ>1.25, and TUM δ>1.25, achieving results of 10.82, 0.089, 0.270, 8.46, 8.32, and 9.97, respectively. The model is available under the Apache 2.0 license. The training and inference were conducted on an Intel Xeon Platinum 8280 CPU with 8 physical cores and an NVIDIA RTX 2080 GPU. Users should be made aware of the risks, biases, and limitations of the model. The extent of the risks involved by using the model remains unknown, and no additional risk mitigation strategies were considered during model development. The model is not intended to inform decisions central to human life or flourishing.

instruction: What kind of furniture is visible in this living room photo?
description: The OWLv2 model, proposed by Matthias Minderer, Alexey Gritsenko, and Neil Houlsby, is a zero-shot text-conditioned object detection model designed to query an image with one or multiple text queries. It uses CLIP as its multi-modal backbone, with a ViT-like Transformer to extract visual features and a causal language model to extract text features. To enable detection, OWL-ViT removes the final token pooling layer of the vision model and attaches a lightweight classification and box head to each transformer output token. Open-vocabulary classification is achieved by replacing the fixed classification layer weights with class-name embeddings from the text model. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned together with the box and class prediction heads using an object detection objective and a bipartite matching loss. The model uses a CLIP backbone with a ViT-L/14 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The model is intended for use by AI researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. The CLIP backbone was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including data crawled from the internet and pre-existing image datasets. The prediction heads of OWL-ViT, along with the CLIP backbone, are fine-tuned on publicly available object detection datasets. The model was released in June 2023.

instruction: How would you describe the camera movement in this scene?
description: The model is a variant that has been fine-tuned for multi-label video classification, specifically for camera motion classification on an internal dataset. It is designed to predict 18 different camera motion classes, including 'arc_left', 'arc_right', 'dolly_in', 'dolly_out', 'pan_left', 'pan_right', 'pedestal_down', 'pedestal_up', 'roll_left', 'roll_right', 'static', 'tilt_down', 'tilt_up', 'truck_left', 'truck_right', 'undefined', 'zoom_in', and 'zoom_out', as well as 3 shot type classes: 'pov', 'shake', and 'track'. The model is trained to associate the entire video with camera motion labels, rather than frame-level motions, meaning it outputs one or more labels for the whole video. If a camera motion is not present throughout the entire video, the model should predict 'undefined'. The model processes 16 frames per input clip, extracted uniformly from the video, regardless of its original duration. For videos longer than 2 seconds, it is recommended to split the video into non-overlapping 2-second segments, run inference on each segment independently, and then post-process the results. The model achieves an accuracy of 75% on an internal test dataset of 2-second videos, and approximately 84% when excluding the 'pov', 'shake', and 'track' classes.

instruction: How can I obtain structured depth information from a group of unordered images of a statue?
description: Pi-Cubed ($\pi^3$) is a feed-forward neural network designed for visual geometry reconstruction without the need for a fixed reference view. Traditional methods often rely on a designated reference frame, which can lead to instability if the reference is suboptimal. In contrast, $\pi^3$ uses a fully permutation-equivariant architecture to directly predict affine-invariant camera poses and scale-invariant local point maps from an unordered set of images. This design allows the model to be robust to input ordering and highly scalable. An emergent property of the model's simple, bias-free design is the learning of a dense and structured latent representation of the camera pose manifold. Without complex priors or training schemes, $\pi^3$ achieves state-of-the-art performance on tasks such as camera pose estimation, monocular and video depth estimation, and dense point map estimation. The model takes a tensor of images as input and outputs a dictionary containing the reconstructed geometry, including a global point cloud, per-view local point maps, confidence scores for local points, and camera-to-world transformation matrices. The model is implemented in PyTorch and can be run on a batch of images, with optional support for mixed precision inference on compatible GPUs. The model is available for academic use under a 2-clause BSD License, and commercial use requires contacting the authors.

instruction: Can you explain how to calculate the area of a triangle when only the lengths of its three sides are known?
description: Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 is an updated version of the Qwen3-4B non-thinking mode, featuring significant improvements in general capabilities such as instruction following, logical reasoning, text comprehension, mathematics, science, coding, and tool usage. It demonstrates substantial gains in long-tail knowledge coverage across multiple languages and is better aligned with user preferences in subjective and open-ended tasks, enabling more helpful responses and higher-quality text generation. The model supports 256K long-context understanding and has 4.0B parameters, with 3.6B non-embedding parameters, 36 layers, and 32 attention heads for Q and 8 for KV. It has a context length of 262,144 tokens natively. This model is designed for non-thinking mode only and does not generate code blocks in its output. It is reported to outperform previous versions and other models on a range of benchmarks, including MMLU-Pro, MMLU-Redux, GPQA, SuperGPQA, AIME25, HMMT25, ZebraLogic, LiveBench, LiveCodeBench, MultiPL-E, Aider-Polyglot, IFEval, Arena-Hard v2, Creative Writing v3, WritingBench, BFCL-v3, TAU1-Retail, TAU1-Airline, TAU2-Retail, TAU2-Airline, TAU2-Telecom, MultiIF, MMLU-ProX, INCLUDE, and PolyMATH. The model is compatible with Hugging Face transformers and can be used with vLLM or SGLang for creating an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint. For optimal performance, it is recommended to use sampling parameters such as Temperature=0.7, TopP=0.8, TopK=20, and MinP=0, and to set an output length of 16,384 tokens. The model supports agentic use through Qwen-Agent, which encapsulates tool-calling templates and parsers to reduce coding complexity. Users can define available tools using an MCP configuration file or integrate other tools manually. The model is supported by various local applications, including Ollama, LMStudio, MLX-LM, llama.cpp, and KTransformers. If out-of-memory issues occur, reducing the context length to 32,768 is advised. For benchmarking, it is recommended to standardize output formats using specific prompts for math problems and multiple-choice questions.

instruction: Is there a way to make this image clearer and bigger for online display?
description: This is a model for high-definition magnification of images, trained on Qwen/Qwen-Image-Edit-2511. It is primarily used for losslessly enlarging images to approximately 2K size and is intended for use in ComfyUI. The model works with a modified version of Comfy's Qwen/Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 workflow, which includes adding a Qwen/Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 LoRA node connected to the base model. The model can be used with a QwenImageEditPipeline to load the base model and trained LoRA weights for in-scene editing. An input image is loaded, and an in-scene editing prompt is defined, such as 'Upscale this picture to 4K resolution.' The model generates an edited image with enhanced scene understanding using specified parameters, including a prompt, generator seed, true_cfg_scale, negative_prompt, and num_inference_steps. The model weights are available in Safetensors format. There is no fixed trigger word, and the specific removal prompt may require further testing. The model was trained by the AI Laboratory of Chongqing Valiant Cat Technology Co., LTD.

instruction: I need the background removed from this image of a model to create a transparent PNG.
description: RMBG v2.0 is a state-of-the-art background removal model that significantly improves upon RMBG v1.4. It is designed to effectively separate foreground from background in a range of categories and image types. The model has been trained on a carefully selected dataset that includes general stock images, e-commerce, gaming, and advertising content, making it suitable for commercial use cases and enterprise content creation at scale. The accuracy, efficiency, and versatility of the model currently rival leading source-available models. It is ideal for applications where content safety, legally licensed datasets, and bias mitigation are paramount. Developed by BRIA AI, RMBG v2.0 is available as a source-available model for non-commercial use. Bria RMBG2.0 is available as source-code and weights, ComfyUI nodes, or API endpoints. Commercial use is subject to a commercial agreement with BRIA. The model is released under a CC BY-NC 4.0 license for non-commercial use. BRIA RMBG-2.0 is a dichotomous image segmentation model trained exclusively on a professional-grade dataset. The model output includes a single-channel 8-bit grayscale alpha matte, where each pixel value indicates the opacity level of the corresponding pixel in the original image. This non-binary output approach offers developers the flexibility to define custom thresholds for foreground-background separation, catering to varied use cases and enhancing integration into complex pipelines. The Bria-RMBG model was trained with over 15,000 high-quality, high-resolution, manually labeled (pixel-wise accuracy), fully licensed images. The training data includes balanced gender, balanced ethnicity, and people with different types of disabilities. The distribution of images is as follows: 45.11% objects only, 25.24% people with objects/animals, 17.35% people only, 8.52% people/objects/animals with text, 2.52% text only, and 1.89% animals only. The distribution of image types is 87.70% photorealistic and 12.30% non-photorealistic. The distribution of background types is 52.05% non-solid background and 47.95% solid background. The distribution of foreground content is 51.42% single main foreground object and 48.58% multiple objects in the foreground. RMBG-2.0 is developed on an enhanced architecture with a proprietary dataset and training scheme, which significantly improves the model’s accuracy and effectiveness for background-removal tasks. If you use this model in your research, please cite the article titled 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation' by Zheng, Peng and Gao, Dehong and Fan, Deng-Ping and Liu, Li and Laaksonen, Jorma and Ouyang, Wanli and Sebe, Nicu, published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research in 2024. The model requires the following libraries: torch, torchvision, pillow, kornia, and transformers. To use the model, load the model from the 'briaai/RMBG-2.0' repository, preprocess the input image by resizing it to 1024x1024, normalizing it, and converting it to a tensor. Perform inference using the model, apply a sigmoid function to the output, and convert the result to a PIL image. Resize the mask to the original image size and apply it as an alpha channel to the image. Save the resulting image with the background removed.
