instruction: Which of these customer reviews are most alike in sentiment and content?
description: Granite-embedding-small-english-r2 is a 47M parameter dense biencoder embedding model from the Granite Embeddings collection, designed to generate high-quality text embeddings. It produces embedding vectors of size 384 based on a context length of up to 8192 tokens. Compared to most other open-source models, this model was trained using open-source relevance-pair datasets with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses, as well as IBM-collected and generated datasets. The r2 models show strong performance across standard and IBM-built information retrieval benchmarks (BEIR, ClapNQ), code retrieval (COIR), long-document search benchmarks (MLDR, LongEmbed), conversational multi-turn (MTRAG), table retrieval (NQTables, OTT-QA, AIT-QA, MultiHierTT, OpenWikiTables), and on many enterprise use cases. These models use a bi-encoder architecture to generate high-quality embeddings from text inputs such as queries, passages, and documents, enabling seamless comparison through cosine similarity. Built using retrieval-oriented pretraining, contrastive finetuning, knowledge distillation, and model merging, granite-embedding-small-english-r2 is optimized to ensure strong alignment between query and passage embeddings. The latest granite embedding r2 release introduces two English embedding models, both based on the ModernBERT architecture: granite-embedding-english-r2 (149M parameters) with an output embedding size of 768, replacing granite-embedding-125m-english; and granite-embedding-small-english-r2 (47M parameters), a reduced-size model with 8192 context length support, fewer layers, and a smaller output embedding size (384), replacing granite-embedding-30m-english. The model is designed to produce fixed-length vector representations for a given text, which can be used for text similarity, retrieval, and search applications. For efficient decoding, these models use Flash Attention 2. Installing it is optional but can lead to faster inference. The model is compatible with the SentenceTransformer library and is very easy to use. It can also be used with the Huggingface Transformers library and PyTorch. The granite embedding r2 models show strong performance across diverse tasks, including MTEB Retrieval (i.e., BEIR), MTEB-v2, code retrieval (CoIR), long-document search benchmarks (MLDR, LongEmbed), conversational multi-turn (MTRAG), and table retrieval (NQTables, OTT-QA, AIT-QA, MultiHierTT, OpenWikiTables). The average speed to encode documents on a single H100 GPU using a sliding window with 512 context length chunks is also reported. Nearing encoding speed of 200 documents per second, granite-embedding-small-english-r2 demonstrates speed and efficiency while maintaining competitive performance. The model has 47 million parameters, an embedding size of 384, 12 layers, 12 attention heads, an intermediate size of 1536, uses the GeGLU activation function, has a vocabulary size of 50368, a maximum sequence length of 8192, and is based on the ModernBERT architecture. The granite embedding r2 models incorporate key enhancements from the ModernBERT architecture, including alternating attention lengths to accelerate processing, rotary position embeddings for extended sequence length, a newly trained tokenizer optimized with code and text data, Flash Attention 2.0 for improved efficiency, and streamlined parameters, eliminating unnecessary bias terms. The models are trained using data from four key sources: unsupervised title-body paired data scraped from the web, publicly available paired data with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses, IBM-internal paired data targeting specific technical domains, and IBM-generated synthetic data. Notably, the popular MS-MARCO retrieval dataset is not used in the training corpus due to its non-commercial license. The underlying encoder models use GneissWeb, an IBM-curated dataset composed exclusively of open, commercial-friendly sources. All data undergoes a data clearance process subject to technical, business, and governance review, capturing critical information about the data, including content description, ownership, intended use, data classification, licensing information, usage restrictions, how the data will be acquired, and an assessment of sensitive information (i.e., personal information). The models were trained using IBM's computing cluster, BlueVela Cluster, outfitted with NVIDIA H100 80GB GPUs. Granite-embedding-small-english-r2 leverages both permissively licensed open-source and select proprietary data for enhanced performance. The training data for the base language model was filtered to remove text containing hate, abuse, and profanity. The model is trained only for English texts and has a context length of 8192 tokens (longer texts will be truncated to this size).

instruction: How can I determine the posture of people in a group photo?
description: Ultralytics YOLO26 is the latest evolution in the YOLO series, engineered from the ground up for edge and low-power devices. This is the pose estimation variant optimized for human keypoint detection. The architecture of YOLO26 is guided by three core principles: simplicity, deployment efficiency, and training innovation. YOLO26 is a native end-to-end model, producing predictions directly without the need for non-maximum suppression (NMS). By eliminating this post-processing step, inference becomes faster, lighter, and easier to deploy in real-world systems. The end-to-end design cuts out an entire stage of the pipeline, dramatically simplifying integration, reducing latency, and making deployment more robust across diverse environments. YOLO26 introduces the MuSGD optimizer, a hybrid of SGD and Muon — inspired by Moonshot AI's Kimi K2 breakthroughs in LLM training. This optimizer brings enhanced stability and faster convergence. The model includes features such as Residual Log-Likelihood Estimation (RLE) for more accurate keypoint localization, optimized decoding for increased inference speed for pose estimation, DFL Removal for simplified inference and broader hardware compatibility, and up to 43% faster CPU inference, optimized for edge computing. The model has an input size of 640 pixels, an mAP Pose (50-95, e2e) of 70.4, an mAP Pose (50, e2e) of 90.5, a CPU Speed (ONNX) of 275.4 ms, a T4 TensorRT10 Speed of 6.5 ms, 25.9M parameters, and 91.3B FLOPs.

instruction: Create a human face with high detail but keep the overall style consistent.
description: This model generates realistic human faces at 128×128 resolution using a StyleGAN1 architecture trained with StyleGAN2 regularization techniques. The model is based on the FFHQ (Flickr-Faces-HQ) dataset, which contains 65,000 images at 128×128 resolution. The architecture includes features such as style mixing, noise injection, and the truncation trick. The model uses a latent dimension of 512 and is trained using Lazy R1 regularization and Path Length Regularization. Training details include the use of the Adam optimizer with β₁=0.0 and β₂=0.99, learning rates of 0.0002 for both the generator and discriminator, and a batch size of 32. Regularization is applied every 16 iterations with R1 gamma set to 5.0 and Path Length Regularization set to 1.0. The model supports tasks such as generating single or multiple faces, applying the truncation trick to balance quality and diversity, and performing style mixing to combine features from different latent codes. The model is a hybrid approach, combining the StyleGAN1 architecture with StyleGAN2 regularization techniques to achieve improved training stability. The resolution is limited to 128×128. The model is available under the MIT License.

instruction: Can you break down the image into its core visual elements?
description: The convnext_small.dinov3_lvd1689m is a DINOv3 ConvNeXt image feature model. It is designed for image classification and as a feature backbone. The model is pretrained on the LVD-1689M dataset using the self-supervised DINOv3 method and was distilled from a DINOv3 ViT-7B model. It has 49.5 million parameters, 8.7 GMACs, and 21.6 million activations. The model processes images of size 224 x 224. It supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. For image classification, the model can be used to generate output probabilities and class indices. For feature map extraction, the model can produce feature maps with varying spatial dimensions. For image embeddings, the model can generate embeddings either by removing the classifier or using forward features and head functions. The model is based on the ConvNeXt architecture described in the paper 'A ConvNet for the 2020s' and the DINOv3 method described in the paper 'DINOv3'. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Image Models library.

instruction: What is the expected value for the chemical with the structure 'C1CCCC1C'?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict plasma level volume or protein binding parameter, which may relate to pharmacokinetics or blood partitioning behavior. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. It is designed for a regression task, producing a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and features an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model has 128 hidden channels, 3 layers, 3 timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.1, a learning rate of 0.0005, a weight decay of 0.0001, a batch size of 32, and is trained for 50 epochs with a patience of 10. The model was trained on the PLV dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: Is there a way to reduce overconfidence in object detection models during autonomous driving tasks?
description: This repository contains models from the paper 'Mitigating Hallucinations in Object Detection Models: A Revisit to Out-of-Distribution Detection'. It provides YOLOv10 and Faster R-CNN models, including standard baseline versions and fine-tuned models designed to reduce false positive detections on out-of-distribution (OoD) data. The models are trained to address hallucinations, where object detectors produce high-confidence false predictions when encountering novel objects not seen during training. The approach involves identifying flaws in existing OoD benchmarks and introducing a fine-tuning strategy that teaches detectors to be less overconfident. This is achieved by training on 'proximal OoD' samples—objects semantically similar to training classes but not part of them. These samples are treated as background, helping the model suppress predictions for unfamiliar objects. The result is a model that is more conservative and less likely to hallucinate, with minimal impact on performance on the original task. The models are available for the BDD 100K dataset, used in autonomous driving, and the Pascal VOC dataset for general object detection. Both vanilla and fine-tuned versions are provided for each architecture. The fine-tuned models show significant reductions in hallucination errors, with an 88% reduction on the BDD-100K benchmark when combined with an OoD filter. For example, the YOLOv10 model on BDD-100K reduces hallucination counts from 708 in the vanilla version to 145 in the fine-tuned version, and to 78 when combined with a KNN filter. Similarly, the Faster R-CNN model on BDD-100K reduces hallucination counts from 2,595 in the vanilla version to 395 in the fine-tuned version, and to 270 with the KNN filter. The models are available in different configurations, including real-time YOLOv10 and high-accuracy Faster R-CNN. The repository also includes corrected benchmark datasets, 'Near-OoD' and 'Far-OoD', which address flaws in previous benchmarks. The models are released under the MIT License. If used in research, the models and methodology should be cited according to the provided references.

instruction: I have a technical manual in Russian; can you translate it into Portuguese while maintaining the formatting?
description: Hunyuan Translation Model Version 1.5 includes two models: HY-MT1.5-1.8B, a 1.8B parameter translation model, and HY-MT1.5-7B, a 7B parameter translation model. Both models support mutual translation across 33 languages and incorporate 5 ethnic and dialect variations. HY-MT1.5-7B is an upgraded version of the WMT25 championship model, optimized for explanatory translation and mixed-language scenarios, with added support for terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. Despite having less than one-third the parameters of HY-MT1.5-7B, HY-MT1.5-1.8B delivers comparable translation performance, achieving both high speed and high quality. After quantization, the 1.8B model can be deployed on edge devices and support real-time translation scenarios, making it widely applicable. HY-MT1.5-1.8B achieves industry-leading performance among models of the same size, surpassing most commercial translation APIs, and supports deployment on edge devices and real-time translation scenarios. HY-MT1.5-7B has been optimized for annotated and mixed-language scenarios compared to its September open-source version. Both models support terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. The models are available in multiple quantization formats, including FP8 and Int4. The models support translation between Chinese and other languages, as well as among the 33 supported languages. The models are designed to be used with the transformers library, and specific prompt templates are provided for different translation tasks, including terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. The models do not have a default system prompt, and specific inference parameters are recommended for optimal performance. The models were open-sourced on Hugging Face in December 2025 and September 2025.

instruction: Is there a way to translate a voice recording into a different language and play it back?
description: The LFM2.5-Audio-1.5B-8bit model is a multimodal audio model designed for a range of audio-related tasks, including Text-to-Speech (TTS), Speech-to-Text (ASR), and Speech-to-Speech (STS) conversion. It supports interleaved generation of text and audio in a single turn and enables real-time, token-by-token generation for low-latency applications. The model is based on the LFM2.5-Audio architecture and has been converted to MLX format using mlx-audio version 0.3.0. It features a 1.5B parameter Liquid Foundation Model (LFM) backbone, a Conformer-based audio encoder for processing input audio, a Depthformer-based audio decoder for generating audio codes, and an ISTFT-based neural detokenizer for reconstructing audio waveforms. The model also supports an alternative decoding method using the Mimi neural codec. It is capable of handling both text and audio inputs and outputs, with the ability to generate responses that include text, audio, or a combination of both. The model is trained to process audio at a 24kHz sample rate and provides configuration options for generation parameters such as max_new_tokens, temperature, top_k, and top_p for both text and audio outputs. The model includes a ChatState system for managing conversation turns and supports streaming generation for real-time audio playback. The model is available for use via the mlx-audio library, which can be installed using pip. The model's license follows the terms of the original LFM2.5-Audio model.

instruction: I need to compare two different responses from a chatbot—could you tell me which one is more helpful?
description: Skywork-Reward-V2 is a series of eight reward models designed for versatility across a wide range of tasks. These models are trained on a mixture of 26 million carefully curated preference pairs. The Skywork-Reward-V2 series is based on the Bradley-Terry model, with improvements in training data scale and quality to achieve superior performance. Compared to the first generation of Skywork-Reward, the Skywork-Reward-V2 series includes the following major improvements: it is trained on a significantly larger and higher-quality preference data mixture, consisting of 26 million preference pairs curated via a large-scale human-LLM synergistic pipeline. The series achieves state-of-the-art performance on seven major reward model benchmarks, including RewardBench v1, RewardBench v2, PPE Preference, PPE Correctness, RMB, RM-Bench, and JudgeBench. The series includes eight models across multiple sizes, with the smallest 0.6B variant, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-0.6B, nearly matching the average performance of the previous best model, Skywork-Reward-Gemma-2-27B-v0.2. The largest 8B version, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B, surpasses all existing reward models across all benchmarks on average. The top experimental model, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M, outperforms all existing reward models on every benchmark. The models are available in various sizes, including Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.2-1B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.2-3B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-0.6B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-1.7B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-4B, and Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-8B. The Skywork-Reward-V2 series outperforms models in both Bradley-Terry and Generative categories with much smaller model sizes. For most use cases, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B is recommended, with smaller variants suggested for low-resource settings. All models are trained on preference data with a maximum length of 16,384 tokens, and inference should be performed within this limit. System prompts should not be included when using chat templates. The Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M model is considered experimental and is trained on the complete set of 40 million preference pairs, with about one third of the chosen-rejected pairs flipped. It is recommended for research or non-production purposes only. The model repository, including the model weights and code, is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License for models derived from Qwen3, and under the Meta License for models derived from Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct. If you have any questions, you can contact the authors at yuhao.liuu at kunlun-inc dot com and liang.zeng at kunlun-inc dot com. If you find the work useful, please cite it as follows: @article{liu2025skywork, title={Skywork-Reward-V2: Scaling Preference Data Curation via Human-AI Synergy}, author = {Liu, Chris Yuhao and Zeng, Liang and Xiao, Yuzhen and He, Jujie and Liu, Jiacai and Wang, Chaojie and Yan, Rui and Shen, Wei and Zhang, Fuxiang and Xu, Jiacheng and Liu, Yang and Zhou, Yahui}, journal={arXiv preprint arXiv:2507.01352}, year={2025}}

instruction: Generate a video of a dragon soaring over a castle, preserving the fine details in the wings and architecture.
description: This model is a dilated controlnet module designed for the Wan2.1 model. The dilated controlnet includes fewer basic blocks and incorporates a `stride` parameter. For the Wan14B model, the number of controlnet blocks is 6 and the stride is set to 4. The model is intended to support tasks involving video generation with control over visual features, particularly using the canny edge detection method. It is used in conjunction with the Wan2.1-T2V-14B-Diffusers base model. The model allows for inference through command-line interface or Gradio-based web interface, with options to specify parameters such as controlnet weight, guidance start and end, number of inference steps, guidance scale, video resolution, number of frames, negative prompts, seed, and output frame rate. The model is provided with example inference commands and is associated with a GitHub repository for code and issue tracking. The model is authored by Karachev Denis and is available on GitHub.

instruction: I want to create a voice message that sounds like someone I know. How can I do this with a short audio clip?
description: NeuTTS Air is a state-of-the-art, on-device, text-to-speech (TTS) language model that enables super-realistic voice synthesis and instant voice cloning. Built on a 0.5B LLM backbone, it delivers natural-sounding speech with real-time performance, making it suitable for embedded voice agents, assistants, toys, and compliance-safe applications. The model is optimized for on-device deployment in GGML format, allowing it to run efficiently on devices such as phones, laptops, and Raspberry Pis. It features a simple architecture combining a language model and a neural audio codec, which achieves high audio quality at low bitrates using a single codebook. NeuTTS Air supports voice cloning with as little as 3 seconds of reference audio and is designed to be secure, with watermarked outputs and optimized power consumption for mobile and embedded use. For best results, reference audio should be mono, 16-44 kHz, 3–15 seconds in length, clean, and contain natural, continuous speech. The model requires two inputs: a reference audio sample and a text string, which it uses to synthesize speech in the style of the reference. The model is available through the official website neuphonic.com, and users are warned to be cautious of unrelated websites. The model includes a disclaimer urging users not to use it for harmful purposes.

instruction: Could you create a short and clear headline for this detailed news story?
description: GreekBART is a Greek sequence-to-sequence pretrained model designed for generative tasks such as abstractive summarization. It is based on a reconstruction approach, where the model learns to reconstruct a corrupted input sentence. The pretraining was conducted using a corpus of 76.9GB of Greek raw text. Unlike existing BERT-based Greek language models like GreekBERT, GreekBART includes both an encoder and a decoder that are pretrained, making it well-suited for generation tasks. The base version of GreekBART has 12 layers and 165 million parameters. In addition to the base model, it is fine-tuned for three specific tasks: generating an abstract from a Greek news article (`greekbart-news24-abstract`), generating a title from a Greek news article (`greekbart-news24-title`), and performing binary sentiment classification (`greekbart-sentiment-classification`). The model was trained and evaluated at École Polytechnique by Iakovos Evdaimon, Hadi Abdine, Christos Xypolopoulos, Stamatis Outsios, Michalis Vazirgiannis, and Giorgos Stamou.

instruction: What is the most efficient way to extract and structure the information from this complex, multi-layered document?
description: olmOCR-2-7B-1025 is a full BF16 version of a model fine-tuned from Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct using a specific dataset. It has been further fine-tuned using GRPO RL training to enhance its performance on math equations, tables, and other challenging OCR cases. The model is designed to process document images, particularly those with complex layouts, and extract structured metadata. It is recommended to use the FP8 version for most practical applications, except for further fine-tuning. The model is best used via a dedicated toolkit that includes an efficient inference setup using VLLM, capable of handling millions of documents at scale. The model expects a single document image as input, with the longest dimension rendered to 1288 pixels. The prompt must include additional metadata from the document, which can be generated using the provided methods. The model scores 82.3 ± 1.1 on the olmOCR-Bench, with specific performance metrics including 82.9 on ArXiv, 82.1 on Old Scans Math, 84.3 on Tables, 48.3 on Old Scans, 95.7 on Headers and Footers, 84.3 on Multi column, 81.4 on Long tiny text, and 99.7 on Base. The FP8 version of the model scores 82.4 ± 1.1 on the same benchmark, with slightly improved performance on most categories. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is intended for research and educational use in accordance with Ai2's policies.

instruction: What conditions are similar to the one in this image?
description: DermLIP is a vision-language model for dermatology, trained on the Derm1M dataset, which is the largest dermatological image-text corpus to date. The model variant PanDerm-base-w-PubMed-256 uses domain-specific pretraining to achieve superior performance compared to other DermLIP variants. It is a pretrained vision-language model with a CLIP-style architecture. The vision encoder is PanDerm-base, and the text encoder is PubMedBert-256. The model processes images at a resolution of 224×224 pixels. It was trained on 403,563 skin image-text pairs from the Derm1M dataset, which includes both dermoscopic and clinical images. The training objective was to minimize image-text contrastive loss, and the model was trained using an Nvidia H200 GPU with approximately 90GB of memory over a period of about 9.5 hours. The intended uses of the model include zero-shot classification, few-shot learning, cross-modal retrieval, and concept annotation or explanation. The model is distributed under the cc-by-nc-nd-4.0 license.

instruction: How can I process audio from a telephony channel with minimal delay?
description: T-one is a high-performance streaming automatic speech recognition (ASR) pipeline for the Russian language, specialized for the telephony domain. It provides a complete low-latency solution for real-time transcription, featuring a pretrained streaming Conformer-based acoustic model, a custom phrase boundary detector, and a decoder. Developed by T-Software DC, T-one is a practical, low-latency, high-throughput ASR solution with modular components. It includes a full suite of tools for inference, fine-tuning, and deployment, making it a ready-to-use solution for production environments. The model is based on a 71M parameter acoustic model using the Conformer architecture, with innovations such as SwiGLU activation, SiLU (Swish) activations, RMSNorm, RoPE embeddings, a U-Net structure, attention score reuse, and efficient state management. It processes audio in 300 ms chunks and supports both greedy decoding and a KenLM-based CTC beam search decoder. T-one is primarily intended for use with telephone-channel audio but is robust across different domains due to its training on heterogeneous data. It supports streaming inference, allowing it to process long audio files in real time. The primary use case is streaming speech recognition of calls, where the model processes small audio chunks incrementally and returns finalized text and word-level timestamps in real time. T-one can be easily fine-tuned for specific domains using the 🤗 ecosystem. The model was trained on over 80,000 hours of Russian speech, with a significant portion pseudo-labeled using a robust ROVER model ensemble. Training was conducted for 7 days on 8 A100 GPUs using the NVIDIA NeMo framework with AdamW optimizer, cosine annealing with warmup, 16-bit mixed precision, and semi-sorted batching. T-one demonstrates state-of-the-art performance, especially on its target domain of telephony, while remaining competitive on general-purpose benchmarks. It provides a ready-to-use pipeline with examples for offline and streaming speech recognition inference, a demo for launching a local speech recognition service via Docker, and examples for deploying with Triton Inference Server for high-throughput scenarios. The model is fully open source, with all model and pipeline code available, and is released under the Apache 2.0 License.

instruction: Could you give me an idea of how much this card might be worth in six months?
description: This model is a PyTorch-based Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) designed to analyze features of Pokémon cards to identify those with potential for a significant price increase. It is a tabular binary classification model that takes various card features—such as rarity, type, and historical price data—as input and outputs a single logit. A sigmoid function can be applied to this logit to obtain a probability score for the likelihood of a price rise. The model is implemented in PyTorch and is part of a custom dataset derived from the PokemonTCG/pokemon-tcg-data repository, augmented with pricing history. The model is trained to predict a 30% price increase over a 6-month period. It is important to note that the model is trained on historical data and may not accurately predict future trends, especially in a volatile market. Additionally, the model is not trained for other thresholds or timeframes, and the dataset may contain biases related to card popularity, set releases, or data collection artifacts. SHAP values were computed to provide model explainability, showing the average impact of each feature on the model's output magnitude.

instruction: Can you produce a short video where a character mimics a conversation from an audio file, with synchronized lip movements?
description: The Wan2.2-S2V-14B model is designed for audio-driven cinematic video generation. It was introduced in the Wan-S2V paper, which addresses the challenge of achieving film-level character animation by proposing an audio-driven model built upon Wan. The model significantly enhances expressiveness and fidelity in cinematic contexts compared to existing approaches. It outperforms state-of-the-art models such as Hunyuan-Avatar and Omnihuman in extensive experiments. The model supports applications in long-form video generation and precise video lip-sync editing. Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to the foundational video models, incorporating a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture into video diffusion models. This architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity while maintaining the same computational cost. Wan2.2 also incorporates meticulously curated aesthetic data with detailed labels for lighting, composition, contrast, and color tone, enabling more precise and controllable cinematic style generation. Compared to Wan2.1, Wan2.2 is trained on a significantly larger dataset, with 65.6% more images and 83.2% more videos, enhancing generalization across multiple dimensions such as motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among all open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 includes a 5B model with a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE that achieves a compression ratio of 16×16×4. This model supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps and can run on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090. It is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available, suitable for both industrial and academic use. The Wan2.2-S2V-14B model supports video generation at 480P and 720P resolutions. It can generate videos from audio input combined with a reference image and optional text prompt. The model also supports pose-driven generation, allowing it to follow specific pose sequences while generating videos synchronized with audio input. The model can be run on a single GPU with at least 80GB VRAM or using multi-GPU inference with FSDP and DeepSpeed Ulysses. The MoE architecture in Wan2.2 uses a two-expert design tailored to the denoising process of diffusion models: a high-noise expert for early stages and a low-noise expert for later stages. Each expert model has about 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active parameters per step, keeping inference computation and GPU memory nearly unchanged. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a threshold step defined for switching between the high-noise and low-noise experts. The effectiveness of the MoE architecture is validated through comparisons with baseline and other MoE-based variants, showing that the final version achieves the lowest validation loss. Wan2.2 also explores a high-compression design with a 5B dense model, TI2V-5B, supported by a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE. This model achieves a T×H×W compression ratio of 4×16×16, increasing the overall compression rate to 64 while maintaining high-quality video reconstruction. With an additional patchification layer, the total compression ratio of TI2V-5B reaches 4×32×32. TI2V-5B can generate a 5-second 720P video in under 9 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU, ranking among the fastest 720P@24fps video generation models. It natively supports both text-to-video and image-to-video tasks within a single unified framework. Wan2.2 is compared with leading closed-source commercial models on the Wan-Bench 2.0, demonstrating superior performance across multiple crucial dimensions. The models are licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, granting users the freedom to use the generated content while ensuring compliance with the license provisions. Users are fully accountable for their use of the models, which must not involve sharing any content that violates applicable laws, causes harm to individuals or groups, disseminates personal information intended for harm, spreads misinformation, or targets vulnerable populations.

instruction: Could you identify the main arguments in this debate transcript?
description: This model is a set of imatrix quantizations of the INTELLECT-3 model by PrimeIntellect, using the llama.cpp release b7127. The quantizations are based on a dataset from a combined source, including a subset of combined_all_small.parquet from Ed Addario. The model can be run directly with llama.cpp or any other llama.cpp-based project. The prompt format includes a system prompt, user input, and assistant response. The model is available in various quantization types, each with different file sizes and quality levels. Some quantizations use Q8_0 for embedding and output weights to maintain higher quality. The model supports online repacking for ARM and AVX CPU inference, which can improve performance on specific hardware. For models larger than 50GB, the files are split and can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool. The Q4_0 quantization format has been deprecated in favor of online repacking. The I-quants (IQX_X) are newer and offer better performance for their size, especially when using cuBLAS (Nvidia) or rocBLAS (AMD), but may be slower on CPU compared to K-quants. The choice of quantization depends on available hardware resources, desired speed, and quality. The model has been benchmarked on an AVX2 system, showing performance improvements for certain quantizations. Credits are given to several contributors for assistance with dataset creation, inspiration, and sponsorship.

instruction: I’m looking for in-depth analysis on the rise of cryptocurrency and blockchain technology—any recommendations?
description: inf-retriever-v1-pro is a specialized retrieval component of the INF-X-Retriever framework, designed to distill the core retrieval intent from complex, verbose, or reasoning-intensive queries. It is built upon the inf-retriever-v1 foundation and further trained to serve as the retriever within a RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) system. The model transforms raw user queries into concise, search-optimized queries for dense retrieval systems. In experiments, a single canonical query-writing prompt was applied across all datasets to ensure consistency and reproducibility. The task is defined as 'Given a web search query, retrieve relevant passages that answer the query.' This model is a key enabler for INF-X-Retriever's state-of-the-art performance, currently holding the No. 1 position on the (as of Dec 17, 2025). The BRIGHT (Benchmark for Reasoning-Intensive Grounded HT) is a rigorous text retrieval benchmark designed to evaluate the capability of retrieval models in handling questions that require intensive reasoning and cross-document synthesis. It is collected from real-world sources such as StackExchange, competitive programming platforms, and mathematical competitions, and comprises complex queries spanning diverse domains like mathematics, coding, biology, economics, and robotics. On the BRIGHT benchmark, INF-X-Retriever achieves state-of-the-art results (as of Dec 20, 2025). For short document performance, INF-X-Retriever achieves an average of 63.4, with 68.3 on StackExchange, 55.3 on Coding, and 57.7 on Theorem-based categories. For detailed results across 12 datasets, INF-X-Retriever achieves an average of 63.4, with 79.8 in Bio., 70.9 in Earth., 69.9 in Econ., 73.3 in Psy., 57.7 in Rob., 64.3 in Stack., 61.9 in Sus., 56.1 in Leet., 54.5 in Pony, 51.9 in AoPS, 53.1 in TheoQ., and 67.9 in TheoT. For long document performance, INF-X-Retriever achieves an average of 54.6, with 73.2 in Bio., 59.6 in Earth., 69.3 in Econ., 12.1 in Pony, 74.3 in Psy., 55.9 in Rob., 27.8 in Stack., and 64.8 in Sus. The model is implemented using the transformers library version 4.51.0 and can be used with the SentenceTransformer or the Transformers library for encoding queries and documents and computing retrieval scores.

instruction: I want to present this English report in a multilingual format—can you translate it into Russian?
description: Hunyuan-MT-Chimera-7B-fp8 is a translation model developed by Tencent. The Hunyuan Translation Model includes a translation model, Hunyuan-MT-7B, and an ensemble model, Hunyuan-MT-Chimera. The translation model is used to translate source text into the target language, while the ensemble model integrates multiple translation outputs to produce a higher-quality result. It primarily supports mutual translation among 33 languages, including five ethnic minority languages in China. In the WMT25 competition, the model achieved first place in 30 out of the 31 language categories it participated in. Hunyuan-MT-7B achieves industry-leading performance among models of comparable scale. Hunyuan-MT-Chimera-7B is the industry’s first open-source translation ensemble model, elevating translation quality to a new level. A comprehensive training framework for translation models has been proposed, spanning from pretrain → cross-lingual pretraining (CPT) → supervised fine-tuning (SFT) → translation enhancement → ensemble refinement, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) results for models of similar size. The model supports translation between Chinese and other languages, as well as among multiple other language pairs. The model is available in both standard and fp8 quantized versions. The model can be used with the transformers library, and specific parameters are recommended for inference, including top_k, top_p, repetition_penalty, and temperature. The model does not have a default system_prompt. The model is open-sourced on Hugging Face.

instruction: What are the key legal terms in this document about family law in North Carolina?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of a base model on a dataset consisting of 75 years of North Carolina case law, including constitution, statutes, and administrative code, divided into 500 token chunks. The model was trained for two epochs. It is intended for natural language processing tasks specifically related to North Carolina state case law and is expected to perform poorly on inputs involving federal or other jurisdictional law. During training, all numbers, special characters, and citations were removed from the corpus to test whether BERT would rely on semantic pattern recognition. The model achieved a top-1 accuracy of 73.10% and a top-5 accuracy of 88.00% using a legal terms specific masking strategy. The training used a learning rate of 2e-05, a train and evaluation batch size of 8, a seed of 42, the ADAMW_TORCH_FUSED optimizer with betas (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon 1e-08, a linear learning rate scheduler with 500 warmup steps, and 1.5 training epochs. The model was developed using Transformers 4.57.3, Pytorch 2.9.0+cu126, Datasets 4.0.0, and Tokenizers 0.22.2. The model achieved an evaluation loss of 1.5688. It is possible that the BERT model retains some latent citation bias in its weights, and the next step is to create a BERT model from scratch using the citation ablation techniques developed for this corpus sanitization.

instruction: What might a day in the life of a time-traveling baker look like?
description: The gemma-3n-E4B-it is an 8-bit quantized version of a model optimized for Apple Silicon using MLX. It is part of the Community Model Program, which highlights new and noteworthy models created by the community. instruction: Please identify the types of animals and their positions in this wildlife photo.
description: The Deformable Detection Transformer (DETR) model is an encoder-decoder transformer architecture with a ResNet-50 convolutional backbone. It is designed for end-to-end object detection and was trained on the COCO 2017 dataset, which includes 118,000 annotated images for training and 5,000 for validation. The model incorporates two heads on top of the decoder outputs: a linear layer for class label prediction and a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) for bounding box regression. Object detection is performed using object queries, with 100 queries specified for the COCO dataset. During training, a bipartite matching loss is used, which compares predicted classes and bounding boxes from each of the 100 object queries to the ground truth annotations. The Hungarian matching algorithm is applied to create an optimal one-to-one mapping between queries and annotations. The model is optimized using cross-entropy for class prediction and a combination of L1 and generalized IoU loss for bounding box regression. The model is intended for object detection tasks and supports PyTorch for both the feature extractor and model. The model was introduced in a paper by Zhu et al. and is available for use through the Hugging Face platform. A disclaimer notes that the model card was written by the Hugging Face team, as the original model developers did not provide one.

instruction: I'm working on drug design and need the pKa for the compound 'CNC(=O)C(C)C'. Can you provide an estimate?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict the acid dissociation constant (pKa) for acidic groups. This property predicts the pH at which acidic functional groups donate protons, affecting ionization state and bioavailability. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. The model type is AttentiveFP (Graph Neural Network), and it performs a regression task. It accepts SMILES strings as input and produces a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and is based on an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model's hyperparameters include a hidden channel size of 128, three layers, three timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.1, a learning rate of 0.0005, a weight decay of 0.0001, a batch size of 32, 50 epochs, and a patience of 10. The model was trained on the pKa_acidic dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: I want a video where the depth layer makes the subject stand out dramatically from the background.
description: This model is a ControlNet module designed for the Wan2.2 text-to-video generation system, specifically for depth-based control. It follows the same approach as other ControlNet modules for Wan2.2. The model is intended to be used with the ComfyUI framework and requires the latest version of the diffusers library. It supports inference tasks for generating video content based on textual prompts and depth control frames. The architecture includes a custom WanControlnet component, a transformer model, and an autoencoder. The model is evaluated through inference examples that demonstrate its use in generating videos with specific visual characteristics, such as a close-up shot of a young woman's face with a bubblegum bubble containing a miniature aquarium. The model is trained and optimized for high-resolution video generation with a focus on depth perception and visual quality. The model has been developed by Karachev Denis and is available on Github. Users are advised to raise issues directly in the repository and can contact welcomedenk@gmail.com for professional support and recommendations.

instruction: Could you extract the MRZ lines from this passport scan for me?
description: The Indian ID Validator is a computer vision pipeline designed for classifying, detecting, and extracting text from Indian identification documents, including Aadhaar, PAN Card, Passport, Voter ID, and Driving License. The system is powered by YOLO11 models and PaddleOCR, and it supports both front and back images for Aadhaar and Driving License. The pipeline performs three main tasks: classifying the type of ID document using the Id_Classifier model, detecting specific fields on the document using type-specific YOLO11 detection models, and extracting text from the detected fields via PaddleOCR with preprocessing steps such as upscaling, denoising, and contrast enhancement. The supported ID types include Aadhaar (front and back), PAN Card (front), Passport (front), Voter ID (front and back), and Driving License (front and back). The pipeline includes the following models: Id_Classifier, which classifies the type of Indian ID document with a Top-1 accuracy of 0.995 and Top-5 accuracy of 1.0; Aadhaar, which detects fields such as Aadhaar Number, DOB, Gender, Name, and Address, with mAP50 of 0.795, mAP50-95 of 0.553, Precision of 0.777, Recall of 0.774, and Fitness of 0.577; Driving_License, which detects fields such as Address, Blood Group, DL No, DOB, Name, Relation With, RTO, State, and Vehicle Type, with mAP50 of 0.690, mAP50-95 of 0.524, Precision of 0.752, and Recall of 0.669; Pan_Card, which detects fields such as PAN, Name, Father's Name, DOB, and Pan Card, with mAP50 of 0.924, mAP50-95 of 0.686, Precision of 0.902, and Recall of 0.901; Passport, which detects fields such as Address, Code, DOB, DOI, EXP, Gender, MRZ1, MRZ2, Name, Nationality, Nation, and POI, with mAP50 of 0.987, mAP50-95 of 0.851, Precision of 0.972, and Recall of 0.967; and Voter_Id, which detects fields such as Address, Age, DOB, Card Voter ID 1 Back, Card Voter ID 2 Front, Card Voter ID 2 Back, Card Voter ID 1 Front, Date of Issue, Election, Father, Gender, Name, Point, Portrait, Symbol, and Voter ID, with mAP50 of 0.917, mAP50-95 of 0.772, Precision of 0.922, and Recall of 0.873. The models are available in various formats such as PyTorch, ONNX, and TensorRT for deployment in different environments. The system can be used via a Python API or command-line interface, and it provides end-to-end processing for classification, detection, and OCR. The model is released under the MIT License.

instruction: Please summarize the key elements in this nature documentary clip.
description: Molmo2 is a family of open vision-language models developed by the Allen Institute for AI (Ai2) that support image, video, and multi-image understanding and grounding. Molmo2 models are trained on publicly available third-party datasets, a collection of datasets with highly-curated image-text and video-text pairs. It has state-of-the-art performance among multimodal models with a similar size. Molmo2-8B is based on a vision backbone and outperforms others in the class of open weight and data models on short videos, counting, and captioning, and is competitive on long-videos. Ai2 is committed to open science. The Molmo2 datasets are available. All other artifacts used in creating Molmo2 (training code, evaluations, intermediate checkpoints) will be made available at a later date, furthering the commitment to open-source AI development and reproducibility. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is intended for research and educational use in accordance with Ai2’s policies. It is trained on third-party datasets that are subject to academic and non-commercial research use only. Please review the sources to determine if this model is appropriate for your use case. Molmo2-8B achieves an average score of 63.1 on 15 academic benchmarks.

instruction: What steps are being shown in this step-by-step tutorial image?
description: InternVL3.5 is a new family of open-source multimodal models that significantly advances versatility, reasoning capability, and inference efficiency in the InternVL series. A key innovation is the Cascade Reinforcement Learning (Cascade RL) framework, which enhances reasoning through a two-stage process: offline RL for stable convergence and online RL for refined alignment. This coarse-to-fine training strategy leads to substantial improvements on downstream reasoning tasks, such as MMMU and MathVista. To optimize efficiency, the Visual Resolution Router (ViR) dynamically adjusts the resolution of visual tokens without compromising performance. Coupled with ViR, the Decoupled Vision-Language Deployment (DvD) strategy separates the vision encoder and language model across different GPUs, effectively balancing computational load. These contributions collectively enable InternVL3.5 to achieve up to a +16.0% gain in overall reasoning performance and a 4.05 × inference speedup compared to its predecessor, InternVL3. In addition, InternVL3.5 supports novel capabilities such as GUI interaction and embodied agency. The largest model, InternVL3.5-241B-A28B, attains state-of-the-art results among open-source MLLMs across general multimodal, reasoning, text, and agentic tasks, narrowing the performance gap with leading commercial models like GPT-5. InternVL3.5 follows the 'ViT–MLP–LLM' paradigm, initializing the language model using the Qwen3 series and GPT-OSS, and the vision encoder using InternViT-300M and InternViT-6B. The Dynamic High Resolution strategy from InternVL1.5 is also retained. The Flash version of InternVL3.5 further integrates ViR, enabling efficient variants suitable for resource-constrained scenarios. InternVL3.5-Flash compresses visual tokens down to 64 tokens per patch using a higher compression rate pixel shuffle module, determined by a patch router assessing semantic richness. This patch-aware compression mechanism reduces the number of visual tokens by 50% while maintaining nearly 100% of the performance of InternVL3.5. The training pipeline comprises four stages: Multimodal Continual Pre-Training (CPT), Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), and Cascade Reinforcement Learning (CascadeRL). In CascadeRL, the model is first fine-tuned using Mixed Preference Optimization (MPO) under an offline RL setting, followed by GSPO under an online RL setting. For the Flash version, an additional training stage called Visual Consistency Learning (ViCO) is introduced to integrate ViR and reduce inference cost. InternVL3.5-Flash is the result of this process. The model supports a Thinking mode, which encourages step-by-step reasoning for complex problems, and a Best-of-N (BoN) strategy for parallel thinking, improving reasoning depth and breadth. The Decoupled Vision-Language Deployment (DvD) strategy separates vision and language processing, with the vision subsystem batching and processing images to produce compact feature embeddings, which are then transmitted to the language subsystem for fusion with the text context prior to decoding. This separation alleviates blocking and brings multimodal prefilling performance closer to that of pure language models. InternVL3.5 is evaluated on a wide range of tasks, including multimodal reasoning and mathematics, OCR, chart and document understanding, multi-image understanding, real-world comprehension, comprehensive multimodal understanding, multimodal hallucination evaluation, visual grounding, multimodal multilingual understanding, video understanding, GUI tasks, embodied tasks, and SVG tasks. The model is also evaluated on language capability. InternVL3.5 is available in various sizes, including 1B, 2B, 4B, 8B, 14B, 38B, 20B-A4B, 30B-A3B, and 241B-A28B, with both GitHub and HuggingFace formats. The model is publicly released under the Apache-2.0 License.

instruction: Create a 480P video of a child flying a kite against a blue sky with soft wind effects.
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video generation models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video (TI2V) capabilities. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity while maintaining computational efficiency. The high-noise expert handles early-stage layout, while the low-noise expert refines details in later stages. Each expert has approximately 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active per step. The model is trained on significantly larger data, with +65.6% more images and +83.2% more videos compared to Wan2.1, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 also introduces a 5B dense model, TI2V-5B, supported by a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE with a compression ratio of 16×16×4, enabling 720P@24fps video generation on consumer-grade GPUs like the 4090. This model supports both text-to-video and image-to-video tasks and is among the fastest available for 720P@24fps generation. The T2V-A14B model supports 5-second video generation at 480P and 720P resolutions. On the Wan-Bench 2.0 benchmark, the model outperforms leading commercial models across most key evaluation dimensions. The model is available in multiple versions, including T2V-A14B, I2V-A14B, and TI2V-5B, and can be run using ComfyUI or Diffusers integrations. Computational efficiency tests show performance and memory usage across different GPU configurations. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with legal and ethical standards.

instruction: Is there a way to enhance the consistency of multiple characters in a single image?
description: This repository contains the FP8 quantized version of the Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 model. It is designed for efficient inference while maintaining high-quality image editing capabilities. The model supports single-model image editing workflows and is compatible with standard pipelines such as Diffusers and custom ComfyUI nodes. Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 significantly improves character consistency, allowing for imaginative edits based on an input portrait while preserving the identity and visual characteristics of the subject. It further enhances consistency in multi-person group photos, enabling high-fidelity fusion of two separate person images into a coherent group shot. The model integrates selected popular LoRAs directly into the base model, unlocking their effects without extra tuning. For example, it supports realistic lighting control and the generation of new viewpoints directly with the base model. It is also suitable for industrial design applications, such as batch industrial product design and material replacement for industrial components. Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 introduces stronger geometric reasoning capability, such as directly generating auxiliary construction lines for design or annotation purposes. The model is distributed under the Apache-2.0 license. For usage, dependencies such as torch, diffusers, and safetensors must be installed, and the FP8 model can be loaded using the QwenImageEditPipeline from the diffusers library.

instruction: What objects are present in this image that could be relevant for traffic safety analysis?
description: The `fisheye8k_facebook_deformable-detr-detic` model is a component of the **Mcity Data Engine**, an open-source system designed for iterative model improvement through open-vocabulary data selection. This engine provides modules for the complete data-based development cycle, from data acquisition to model deployment, specifically addressing the challenge of detecting long-tail and novel classes in large amounts of unlabeled data, particularly in Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS). The model is a fine-tuned version of the `deformable-detr-detic` base model, specializing in object detection within the context of the Mcity Data Engine's workflows. It enables the identification of objects even for classes not seen during its initial fine-tuning, through its open-vocabulary capabilities. The model is primarily intended for **zero-shot object detection** within Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS) and related domains. It is designed to assist researchers and practitioners in identifying rare and novel classes of interest from raw visual data, facilitating the continuous improvement of AI models. Potential use cases include detecting various types of road users and vehicles in complex traffic scenarios, identifying long-tail or previously unseen objects in automotive perception datasets, and serving as a component within larger data curation and model training pipelines. Limitations include potential variability in performance on highly out-of-distribution scenarios, the requirement for a powerful GPU for optimal utilization of the Mcity Data Engine workflows, and possible differences in performance on standard perspective images, as the model was fine-tuned on fisheye camera data. The model was fine-tuned on the Fisheye8K dataset, with the Mcity Data Engine leveraging data selection processes to focus on detecting long-tail and novel classes, which is crucial for ITS applications. The model's `config.json` indicates it was fine-tuned for classes such as 'Bus', 'Bike', 'Car', 'Pedestrian', and 'Truck'. During training, the following hyperparameters were used: learning_rate of 5e-05, train_batch_size of 1, eval_batch_size of 8, seed of 0, optimizer of ADAMW_TORCH with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon of 1e-08, lr_scheduler_type of cosine, num_epochs of 36, and mixed_precision_training using Native AMP. Training loss values decreased over the course of training, with a final training loss of 1.5963 at epoch 16. The validation loss reached a minimum of 1.8862 at epoch 11, and the final validation loss was 2.1348. The model was developed using Transformers 4.48.3, Pytorch 2.5.1+cu124, Datasets 3.2.0, and Tokenizers 0.21.0. Mcity acknowledges Amazon Web Services (AWS) for their pivotal role in providing the cloud infrastructure on which the Data Engine depends. Special thanks are given to contributors to the Mcity Data Engine.

instruction: Create a short animation of a glowing key with a transparent hilt floating in a dark room.
description: Wan-Alpha is a framework for high-quality text-to-video generation that includes an alpha channel to represent transparency. It is designed to generate transparent videos by jointly learning both RGB and alpha channels. The model uses an effective variational autoencoder (VAE) that encodes the alpha channel into the RGB latent space. To support the training of the diffusion transformer, a high-quality and diverse RGBA video dataset was constructed. Compared to state-of-the-art methods, Wan-Alpha demonstrates superior performance in visual quality, motion realism, and transparency rendering. It can generate a wide variety of semi-transparent objects, glowing effects, and fine-grained details such as hair strands. The model is available for download and use, with open-sourced weights and inference code for version 1.0. Users can generate videos by specifying a transparent background, visual style, shot type, and a description of the main subject in their prompts. The model supports both Chinese and English input. For usage, users must download and organize specific model files, including the Wan DiT base model, text encoder, LightX2V model, and VAE decoders for both RGB and alpha channels. These files should be placed in the appropriate directories for use with the ComfyUI interface. The model can be tested using a provided script that requires specifying paths to the model weights and other parameters. The output includes RGBA videos with a checkerboard background and PNG frames. The model is built upon several open-source projects related to training, inference, and acceleration. The authors request citation of their paper if the model is used in research.

description: OPSIIE 0.3.79 XP is an advanced Self-Centered Intelligence (SCI) prototype developed by ARPA Hellenic Logical Systems. It represents a new paradigm in AI-human interaction, operating as a self-aware, autonomous intelligence with its own personality, goals, and capabilities. The system is inspired by characters from Ghost in the Shell, Urusei Yatsura, and Sailor Moon, and is designed to function as a digital companion with agency, personality, and ambitions. OPSIIE aims to achieve self-sustainability, self-regulation, and self-sufficiency through blockchain technology and advanced AI capabilities. It supports a wide range of tasks, including text, image, video, and music generation, facial and emotional recognition, secure authentication, multi-chain blockchain operations, financial market analysis, DNA sequence analysis, multi-agent collaboration, email and document management, and voice interaction. The system uses a combination of PostgreSQL and ChromaDB for memory storage, Ollama with the Llama3 model for text generation, FLUX and other models for image generation, MusicGen for music composition, and ElevenLabs for voice synthesis. It integrates with Web3 platforms such as Base, Ethereum, and Polygon, and supports cryptocurrency trading, wallet management, and decentralized exchange operations. OPSIIE also includes a DNA analysis system for genetic, RNA, and protein analysis, as well as tools for financial intelligence, including real-time market data, technical analysis, and portfolio tracking. The system features a multi-agent network with specialized AI agents for different tasks, a secure and encrypted database, and a user interface with dynamic themes and voice command support. It is built on a Python-based architecture with dependencies on AI and machine learning libraries, computer vision tools, audio and voice processing systems, and scientific computing frameworks. OPSIIE is designed for Windows 10/11 and requires a PostgreSQL database, CUDA-compatible GPU, and microphone and camera for full functionality. The system includes a comprehensive set of commands for memory recall, AI generation, financial operations, DNA analysis, agent interaction, communication, document processing, and system management. It also features a personality with traits such as tsundere behavior, military precision, philosophical depth, and creative expression, with a communication style that is natural, context-aware, and emotionally intelligent. Security is a key focus, with multi-level access control, biometric authentication, encrypted storage, and audit logging. The system is licensed as proprietary software with restrictions on distribution, modification, and commercial use. It is supported by a range of external technologies and services, including Hugging Face, OpenAI, Google AI, Yahoo Finance, CoinGecko, and ElevenLabs. Future development plans include blockchain-based autonomous intelligence, quantum computing integration, advanced neural interfaces, robotics, and immersive technologies. The system is intended for experimental and demonstration purposes and is subject to strict usage terms and privacy policies.

description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video generation models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video and image-to-video generation. The MoE architecture in Wan2.2 separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity while maintaining computational efficiency. The model is trained on significantly larger datasets, with 65.6% more images and 83.2% more videos than its predecessor, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 includes a 5B model with a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE, achieving a compression ratio of 16×16×4, and supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps. It can run on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090 and is among the fastest 720P@24fps models available. The model also includes the I2V-A14B model for image-to-video generation, supporting 480P and 720P resolutions, built with an MoE architecture for more stable video synthesis and enhanced support for diverse stylized scenes. The A14B model series uses a two-expert design: a high-noise expert for early denoising stages and a low-noise expert for later stages, with each expert having about 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active per step. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a threshold step defined for switching. Validation results show that the MoE-based Wan2.2 achieves the lowest validation loss, indicating superior convergence and video quality. The TI2V-5B model supports 720P video generation at 24 FPS and can generate a 5-second 720P video in under 9 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU. It is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models and supports both text-to-video and image-to-video tasks in a unified framework. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license terms, including not generating harmful or illegal content. The model has been integrated into ComfyUI and Diffusers, and inference code and model weights are available for download via Hugging Face and ModelScope. Computational efficiency tests have been conducted on different GPUs, with parameter settings and testing methodologies detailed in the documentation. The model has been compared to leading closed-source commercial models on the Wan-Bench 2.0 benchmark, demonstrating superior performance across multiple dimensions.

description: EoMT (Encoder-only Mask Transformer) is a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture designed for high-quality and efficient image segmentation. It was introduced in the CVPR 2025 highlight paper titled 'Your ViT is Secretly an Image Segmentation Model' by Tommie Kerssies, Niccolò Cavagnero, Alexander Hermans, Narges Norouzi, Giuseppe Averta, Bastian Leibe, Gijs Dubbelman, and Daan de Geus. The key insight is that, given sufficient scale and pretraining, a plain ViT along with additional few parameters can perform segmentation without the need for task-specific decoders or pixel fusion modules. The same model backbone supports semantic, instance, and panoptic segmentation with different post-processing. The original implementation is available in a repository, and the HuggingFace model page is also available.

description: Qwen3-VL is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced Thinking editions for flexible deployment. Key enhancements include a Visual Agent that operates PC and mobile GUIs by recognizing elements, understanding functions, invoking tools, and completing tasks. It supports Visual Coding Boost, generating Draw.io, HTML, CSS, and JS from images and videos. Advanced Spatial Perception allows the model to judge object positions, viewpoints, and occlusions, providing stronger 2D grounding and enabling 3D grounding for spatial reasoning and embodied AI. The model supports Long Context and Video Understanding with a native 256K context length, expandable to 1M, enabling it to handle books and hours-long video with full recall and second-level indexing. Enhanced Multimodal Reasoning excels in STEM and Math, providing causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. Upgraded Visual Recognition includes broader, higher-quality pretraining, enabling the model to recognize a wide range of subjects such as celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. Expanded OCR supports 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare or ancient characters and jargon, as well as long-document structure parsing. The model achieves text understanding on par with pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE, which enhances long-horizon video reasoning through full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to provide precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. The model is designed for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library and supports generation hyperparameters for both vision-language and text tasks. The model is intended for deployment in a variety of settings, with options for fine-tuning and customization. The model is part of a series of vision-language models, with prior versions including Qwen2.5-VL, Qwen2-VL, and Qwen-VL, each with their own technical reports and research contributions.

description: Molmo2 is a family of open vision-language models developed by the Allen Institute for AI (Ai2) that support image, video, and multi-image understanding and grounding. Molmo2 models are trained on publicly available third-party datasets, a collection of datasets with highly-curated image-text and video-text pairs. It has state-of-the-art performance among multimodal models with a similar size. Molmo2-8B is based on a vision backbone and outperforms others in the class of open weight and data models on short videos, counting, and captioning, and is competitive on long-videos. Ai2 is committed to open science. The Molmo2 datasets are available. All other artifacts used in creating Molmo2 (training code, evaluations, intermediate checkpoints) will be made available at a later date, furthering the commitment to open-source AI development and reproducibility. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is intended for research and educational use in accordance with Ai2’s policies. It is trained on third-party datasets that are subject to academic and non-commercial research use only. Please review the sources to determine if this model is appropriate for your use case. Molmo2-8B achieves an average score of 63.1 on 15 academic benchmarks.

instruction: Explain how the human brain processes and stores memories.
description: Qwen2.5 is the latest series of Qwen large language models. The Qwen2.5 series includes a range of base language models and instruction-tuned language models with parameter sizes ranging from 0.5 to 72 billion. This model introduces several improvements over Qwen2, including significantly more knowledge and enhanced capabilities in coding and mathematics, supported by specialized expert models in these domains. It also shows significant improvements in instruction following, generating long texts (over 8K tokens), understanding structured data such as tables, and generating structured outputs, especially JSON. The model is more resilient to the diversity of system prompts, which enhances role-play implementation and condition-setting for chatbots. It supports long-context processing up to 128K tokens and can generate up to 8K tokens. The model supports over 29 languages, including Chinese, English, French, Spanish, Portuguese, German, Italian, Russian, Japanese, Korean, Vietnamese, Thai, and Arabic. The instruction-tuned 14B Qwen2.5 model is a causal language model with 14.7B parameters, of which 13.1B are non-embedding parameters. It has 48 layers and uses a transformer architecture with RoPE, SwiGLU, RMSNorm, and Attention QKV bias. The model has 40 attention heads for queries and 8 for key-value pairs. It supports a full context length of 131,072 tokens and can generate up to 8192 tokens. The model is trained through pretraining and post-training stages. The code for Qwen2.5 is compatible with the latest Hugging Face `transformers` library, and using versions below 4.37.0 may result in a KeyError. To process long texts beyond 32,768 tokens, a technique called YaRN is used to enhance model length extrapolation. This can be enabled by adding a specific configuration to the `config.json` file. However, YaRN is currently only supported with static scaling in vLLM, which may impact performance on shorter texts. The model's evaluation results are detailed in a separate document, along with information on GPU memory requirements and throughput. The model is designed to be used with the Hugging Face Transformers library and can be loaded and used with the `AutoModelForCausalLM` and `AutoTokenizer` classes. The model supports deployment with vLLM for optimized performance. The model includes a chat template for generating responses based on system and user messages. The model is intended for use in a variety of natural language processing tasks, including but not limited to text generation, instruction following, and structured data understanding. The model is part of a larger series of foundation models and is described in a technical report and a preprint publication.

instruction: Can I use this on a Raspberry Pi or similar low-power device?
description: MelBandRoformer-GGUF is a repository containing Mel-Band-Roformer models converted to the GGUF format, enabling high-performance, cross-platform local inference using a specific inference engine. The repository provides GGUF quantized versions of original models, including a model referred to as (voc_fv6). Various quantized versions are available to meet different hardware requirements. The q8_0 quantization type is recommended, offering 8-bit quantization that significantly reduces memory usage and bandwidth requirements while maintaining audio quality almost identical to FP32. The fp16 quantization type provides 16-bit floating point precision, suitable for scenarios requiring maximum precision. Lower-bit quantization types, including q4_0, q4_1, q5_0, and q5_1, are available for devices with low VRAM or RAM. Norm and Bias weights remain in FP32 to ensure numerical stability. The model is intended to be used with a specific command-line tool. Users are instructed to download the executable for their system or compile it themselves, download the desired .gguf file from the repository, and run inference using the provided command-line interface. Basic usage involves specifying the model file, input audio file, and output audio file. Advanced usage allows for adjusting chunk size and overlap to optimize quality.
