It is based on a diffusion pipeline and can be run on devices with CUDA support for improved performance. The model is part of a series of updates to the Qwen-Image model, with each version introducing new features and improvements. The model is available for use through the Hugging Face and ModelScope platforms, and users are encouraged to try the latest version for the best results. The model is developed by a team of researchers and is available under an open-source license. If Qwen-Image-2512 is used in research, users are encouraged to cite the associated technical report.

instruction: Can you extract all the disease names mentioned in this medical research paper?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for disease entity recognition, specifically for identifying and extracting disease entities from the NCBI dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the NCBI_DISEASE dataset, a curated collection of 793 PubMed abstracts with 6,892 disease mentions mapped to 790 unique disease concepts from MeSH and OMIM. The model supports the classification of two entity types: B-Disease and I-Disease. It is intended for use in clinical and research applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the distilbert-base-multilingual-cased architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It has been validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.86, precision of 0.85, recall of 0.87, and accuracy of 0.96. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. The model is part of the OpenMed NER project, which provides open-source, domain-adapted state-of-the-art transformers for biomedical NER across multiple datasets.

instruction: I want to understand this video better—can you give a live description of it?
description: CASA-Qwen2_5-VL-3B-LiveCC is a Qwen-2.5VL model adapted from token insertion to a cross-attention-based architecture using CASA layers. It has been further fine-tuned for live video captioning. The model is designed to process video inputs and generate real-time subtitles that are embedded at the timestamp they are generated. It supports tasks involving video frame processing at 2fps and the generation of subtitles in real-time. The model is based on a cross-attention-based architecture and is optimized for efficient vision-language fusion. It has been evaluated on the Animal Kingdom dataset, where it generates descriptive captions for video content. For example, it can describe a fox in the Arctic, noting environmental details and the animal's behavior. The model requires the use of the `transformers` library and specific dependencies such as `ffmpeg`. It is available for use with model weights provided in the associated repository. Users are advised to refer to the repository for detailed instructions on running the model and generating live captions. The model is part of a research project and should be cited in any academic work that uses it.

instruction: Find and classify any CLL-specific terminology in this excerpt from a clinical case study.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for clinical entity recognition, specifically for entities related to chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL). It is optimized for high-precision biomedical entity recognition and is trained on a curated CLL dataset. The model is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. It supports the identification and classification of biomedical entities, including the entity types `B-CL` and `I-CL`. The model is intended for use in applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The CLL corpus, on which the model is trained, is a domain-specific biomedical named entity recognition (NER) dataset focused on entities related to chronic lymphocytic leukemia, a type of blood cancer. The dataset includes annotations for CLL-specific terminology, biomarkers, treatment entities, and clinical concepts relevant to hematology and oncology research. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.85, precision of 0.78, recall of 0.94, and accuracy of 0.97. Comparative performance on the CLL dataset shows that the model ranks fifth in F1 score with a value of 0.8547, compared to other models with F1 scores ranging from 0.8364 to 0.9575. The model is based on the distilbert-base-cased architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with the AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It performs token classification for named entity recognition, taking tokenized biomedical text as input and producing BIO-tagged entity predictions as output. The model is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research in biomedical NLP. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0.

instruction: Are there any chemical compounds listed in this adverse event report?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition in biomedical literature. It identifies and extracts chemical compounds and substances from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the BC4CHEMD dataset, a manually annotated corpus for chemical entity recognition from the BioCreative IV challenge. The BC4CHEMD dataset contains PubMed abstracts with chemical entities annotated according to ChEBI guidelines and is used to benchmark named entity recognition models in identifying chemical compounds, drugs, and other chemical substances in scientific literature. The model can identify and classify the following entity types: B-CHEM and I-CHEM. It achieves an F1 score of 0.93, precision of 0.92, recall of 0.94, and accuracy of 0.99. The model is based on the ModernBERT-large architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is suitable for use in clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. If used in research or applications, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: Create a 15-second animation of a robot walking with a futuristic soundtrack.
description: This is a GGUF quantized version of the LTX-2 model. The unsloth/LTX-2-GGUF model uses a methodology for state-of-the-art performance, with important layers upcasted to higher precision. The model uses tooling from city96. The LTX-2 model is a DiT-based audio-video foundation model designed to generate synchronized video and audio within a single model. It integrates the core building blocks of modern video generation, with open weights and a focus on practical, local execution. The model is developed by Lightricks and is a diffusion-based audio-video foundation model that supports English. The model is available in several versions, including a full model, a full model in fp8 quantization, a full model in nvfp4 quantization, a distilled version of the full model, a LoRA version of the distilled model applicable to the full model, an x2 spatial upscaler for the ltx-2 latents used in multi-stage pipelines for higher resolution, and an x2 temporal upscaler for the ltx-2 latents used in multi-stage pipelines for higher FPS. The model can be accessed via online demos and is available for local execution. A direct use license allows the use of the models, including full, distilled, upscalers, and any derivatives, for specific purposes. The model can be used with ComfyUI through built-in LTXVideo nodes or manually installed as per provided instructions. The PyTorch codebase includes model definitions, pipelines, and training capabilities, and was tested with Python 3.12 or higher, CUDA version 12.7 or higher, and PyTorch 2.7. The model is supported in the Diffusers library for image-to-video generation. General usage tips include ensuring width and height settings are divisible by 32 and frame count is divisible by 8 + 1. If resolution or frame count is not divisible by these values, input should be padded with -1 and then cropped to the desired dimensions. The model has several limitations: it is not intended to provide factual information, may amplify existing societal biases, may fail to generate videos that perfectly match prompts, prompt following is heavily influenced by prompting-style, may generate inappropriate or offensive content, and when generating audio without speech, the audio may be of lower quality. The base (dev) model is fully trainable, and it is easy to reproduce the LoRAs and IC-LoRAs published with the model by following provided instructions. Training for motion, style, or likeness (sound+appearance) can take less than an hour in many settings.

instruction: How can I quickly find the payment terms in this document?
description: This model is designed for the task of Question Answering on Legal Documents. It is a RoBERTa-based model developed by Mohammed Rakib and is shared under the MIT license. The model is intended to be used for contractual language understanding, particularly for making legal contracts more comprehensible to non-legal professionals. It is trained on the Contract Understanding Atticus Dataset (CUAD), which is the largest open-source contract dataset available. The model has been optimized through various pre-processing experiments and hyperparameter tuning, achieving an AUPR score of 46.6% compared to 42.6% for the original RoBERTa-base model on the same dataset. The model is part of an end-to-end contract understanding application that can highlight relevant clauses in a contract and provide descriptions to aid in due diligence before signing. The system can process both digital and scanned (non-searchable) contracts using Tesseract OCR. The model was developed using V100/P100 hardware from Google Colab Pro, with Python and the Transformers library as the software stack. The model is described in the paper titled 'An Open Source Contractual Language Understanding Application Using Machine Learning' by Nawar, Rakib, Hai, and Haq, published in 2022.

instruction: How does a player's catch rate affect their chances of being elite?
description: This model is a binary classifier developed as part of a class assignment on designing and deploying AI/ML systems. It uses AutoML (AutoGluon Tabular) to predict whether a football receiver is classified as 'Elite' (0/1) based on their statistical features. The model was trained on a dataset containing the following features: 'Tgt', 'Rec', 'Yds', 'YBC_per_R', 'YAC_per_R', 'ADOT', 'Drop_pct', and 'Rat'. The dataset was split using a stratified 80/20 train/test split on the original data. Preprocessing steps included dropping identifier columns (e.g., 'Player'), coercing data to numeric types, and removing rows with missing values. The model was trained using the 'best_quality' preset in AutoGluon Tabular with a time budget of 300 seconds and a random seed of 42. The evaluation metric used was F1 score for binary classification. Training was performed on a Colab CPU runtime with 2 vCPUs and approximately 12 GB of RAM. AutoGluon explored LightGBM, XGBoost, and ensembling methods, with auto-stacking and bagging enabled under the 'best_quality' preset. Hyperparameter tuning was handled automatically by AutoGluon. On the held-out test set, the model achieved an accuracy of 0.8333 and an F1 score of 0.8. The model has several limitations: correlations in the data do not imply causation, and the labels may reflect selection bias. Performance may decrease for out-of-distribution players or contexts. The model is intended for coursework purposes and should not be used for real personnel decisions. Generative AI tools were used to help structure code and documentation, but the model training and results are real.

instruction: What objects are present in this picture of a construction site?
description: The DETR (DEtection TRansformer) model is an encoder-decoder transformer with a convolutional backbone, specifically a ResNet-50 backbone. It is designed for end-to-end object detection and was trained on the COCO 2017 dataset, which includes 118,000 annotated images for training and 5,000 for validation. The model was introduced in a paper by Carion et al. and features two heads on top of the decoder outputs: a linear layer for class labels and a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) for bounding boxes. Object detection is performed using object queries, with 100 queries for the COCO dataset. The model is trained using a bipartite matching loss, which compares predicted classes and bounding boxes to ground truth annotations. The Hungarian matching algorithm is used to create a one-to-one mapping between queries and annotations, followed by optimization using cross-entropy for classes and a combination of L1 and generalized IoU loss for bounding boxes. The model achieves an average precision (AP) of 42.0 on the COCO 2017 validation set. It is intended for object detection tasks and supports PyTorch. The model can be used with an image processor and object detection pipeline to extract and interpret detection results. The training process involved 300 epochs on 16 V100 GPUs, with a batch size of 64. Images were preprocessed by resizing and normalizing with ImageNet mean and standard deviation values. The model card was written by the Hugging Face team, as the original DETR team did not provide one.

instruction: Highlight the parts of this image that are not occupied by the main subject.
description: This tiny model is intended for debugging purposes. It is randomly initialized using a configuration adapted from a source model. The model is designed to perform instance segmentation tasks, supporting functions such as text-based object detection and exclusion of specific regions using input boxes. The architecture includes a vision encoder with a vision transformer backbone, a text encoder based on CLIP, a geometry encoder, a DETR encoder and decoder, and a mask decoder. The vision encoder processes images through a series of transformer layers and a feature pyramid network. The text encoder processes text inputs through a transformer-based architecture. The geometry encoder handles box and label embeddings, while the DETR components manage object detection and cross-attention between text and vision features. The mask decoder generates segmentation masks and includes components for pixel decoding and mask embedding. The model is configured with reduced hidden sizes, intermediate sizes, and attention heads for a lightweight version. It is explicitly noted that this model is not intended for production use and is provided for debugging and development purposes only.

instruction: Is there a version that uses Q8_0 for better accuracy in certain parts?
description: This is a set of imatrix quantizations of the gemma-3n-E2B-it model by Google, processed using the llama.cpp release b5760. The quantizations are based on a dataset provided for calibration. The model is intended to be run using llama.cpp or any other llama.cpp-based project. No specific chat template is provided, so a default prompt format is used, which may not be accurate and should be verified against the original model card. The model offers a variety of quantization options, each with different file sizes and quality levels. Some quantizations, such as Q3_K_XL and Q4_K_L, use Q8_0 for embedding and output weights, which may differ from the default quantization method. The model supports online repacking for ARM and AVX CPU inference, particularly for the Q4_0 and IQ4_NL quantizations, which can improve performance on compatible hardware. The Q4_0 quantization has been deprecated in favor of newer formats that support online repacking. For users with AVX2 systems, benchmarks show that the Q4_K_M quantization provides a significant performance improvement over Q4_0 in prompt processing, while Q4_0_8_8 offers a moderate improvement in both prompt processing and text generation. When choosing a quantization, users should consider their available RAM and VRAM, as well as whether they prioritize speed or quality. K-quants, such as Q5_K_M, are generally recommended for most use cases, while I-quants, such as IQ3_M, may offer better performance for smaller quantizations when using cuBLAS or rocBLAS. The model can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool, with options to download specific files or entire branches. The model was created with assistance from kalomaze and Dampf for the imatrix calibration dataset, and inspired by ZeroWw's work on embedding and output weights. Support for the model's development was provided by LM Studio.

instruction: How can I optimize the training process to avoid learning shortcuts that affect video realism?
description: The Wan2.2-Fun-Reward-LoRAs are pre-trained models in the form of LoRAs (Low-Rank Adaptations) designed to enhance the alignment of generated videos with human preferences through the use of reward backpropagation techniques. These LoRAs can be used as plug-ins to improve the performance of the corresponding base model or to train custom reward LoRAs. The models include Wan2.2-Fun-A14B-InP-high-noise-HPS2.1, Wan2.2-Fun-A14B-InP-low-noise-HPS2.1, Wan2.2-Fun-A14B-InP-high-noise-MPS, and Wan2.2-Fun-A14B-InP-low-noise-MPS. Each LoRA is trained with a batch size of 8 for varying numbers of steps: 5,000 steps for the high-noise HPS2.1 and high-noise MPS models, 2,700 steps for the low-noise HPS2.1 model, and 4,500 steps for the low-noise MPS model. The HPS2.1 and MPS reward LoRAs have a rank of 128 and a network alpha of 64. It is noted that the MPS reward LoRA for the low-noise model converges significantly more slowly and may not deliver satisfactory results, so the HPSv2.1 reward LoRA is recommended for the low-noise model. The models are intended to be used in conjunction with the base model Wan2.2-Fun-A14B-InP. During training, the reward continues to increase after a certain point, but the quality of the generated videos does not improve further, as the model may learn to exploit shortcuts such as adding artifacts in the background. Additionally, there is a lack of suitable preference models for video generation, and using image preference models leads to a decrease in the dynamism of generated videos, although this can be partially mitigated by computing the reward using only the first frame of the decoded video.

instruction: Can you help me understand the underlying principles of this optimization problem?
description: INTELLECT-3 is a 106B (A12B) parameter Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) reasoning model post-trained using supervised fine-tuning (SFT) followed by large-scale reinforcement learning (RL). Training was conducted using environments built with a specific library, and all training and evaluation environments are available on the Environments Hub. The model, training frameworks, and environments are open-sourced under fully-permissive licenses, including MIT and Apache 2.0. INTELLECT-3 achieves best-in-class performance on math, coding, and reasoning benchmarks, including 98.1 on MATH-500, 90.8 on AIME24, 88.0 on AIME25, 69.3 on LCB, 74.4 on GPQA, 14.6 on HLE, and 81.9 on MMLU-Pro. The BF16 version of the model can be served on two H200 GPUs using the vLLM framework with specific configuration parameters, while the FP8 version can be served on a single H200 GPU with similar settings. The model is designed to support reasoning tasks and is part of the Prime Intellect Platform. For more information, refer to the technical report and blog.

instruction: Could you produce a 3D object of a human vertebra for educational purposes?
description: Shap-MeD is a text-to-3D object generative model specialized in the biomedical domain. It is designed to assist in the 3D modeling of medical objects, aiming to reduce development time. 3D modeling in medicine has various applications, including surgical procedure simulation and planning, the design of personalized prosthetic implants, medical education, the creation of anatomical models, and the development of research prototypes. Shap-MeD is based on Shap-e, an open-source text-to-3D generative model developed by OpenAI, and is fine-tuned using a dataset of biomedical objects. The model achieved a mean squared error (MSE) of 0.089 in latent generation on the evaluation set, compared to Shap-e's MSE of 0.147. A qualitative evaluation was also conducted, comparing Shap-MeD with other models in the generation of biomedical objects. The results indicate that Shap-MeD demonstrates higher structural accuracy in biomedical object generation.

instruction: Produce a synthetic CT scan of the spine with degenerative disc disease at L4-L5.
description: This model is a 3D latent diffusion model (RFlow) combined with a 3D VAE and a CLIP3D text encoder, designed for text-to-CT generation. It is intended for research and experimentation in text-conditioned 3D CT synthesis, including the generation of synthetic data for benchmarking or augmentation. The model supports tasks such as CT volume generation from textual descriptions. The architecture includes a 3D VAE for latent compression and decoding, a diffusion UNet trained with rectified flow in latent space, and a CLIP3D text encoder for report embeddings. The model was trained on the CT-RATE dataset, which includes CT volumes and reports, and the CLIP3D component was trained for vision-language alignment on this dataset. The diffusion UNet was trained using rectified flow in latent space, conditioned on text embeddings. The model is not validated for clinical use and may encode dataset biases. Synthetic outputs may contain artifacts and should not be used for patient care. Evaluation results are detailed in the associated paper. The model is released under an Apache 2.0 license. The model weights include an autoencoder checkpoint, a diffusion UNet checkpoint, and CLIP3D weights for report embeddings. The model is not intended for clinical decision-making, diagnostic use, or deployment without proper validation and approvals. Training was conducted using a multi-GPU setup, though environmental impact was not reported.

instruction: What category would this image of a fruit fall into?
description: MobileNet V1 is a pre-trained model on ImageNet-1k at a resolution of 192x192. It was introduced by Howard et al and first released by the Hugging Face team, as the original team did not provide a model card. MobileNets are designed to be small, low-latency, and low-power models that can be adapted for various use cases by adjusting their parameters to meet resource constraints. They support tasks such as classification, detection, embeddings, and segmentation, similar to other large-scale models like Inception. MobileNets are optimized to run efficiently on mobile devices and balance trade-offs between latency, size, and accuracy while performing favorably compared to other models in the literature. The model can be used for image classification, predicting one of 1001 classes, which includes the 1000 ImageNet classes plus an additional 'background' class. The model and its feature extractor are currently supported in PyTorch.

instruction: Is there a way to extract depth data from this image for a depth-based filter effect?
description: Depth Anything is a deep convolutional neural network model designed for estimating depth at each point in an image. It is optimized for mobile deployment and is intended to be used on Qualcomm® devices. The model has an input resolution of 518x518 and contains 24.7 million parameters, with a model size of 94.3 MB in float precision. The model is available as a checkpoint named DepthAnything_Small. Performance metrics across various devices include inference times ranging from 174.976 ms to 73.28 ms and peak memory usage ranging from 0 - 393 MB to 1 - 13 MB, depending on the device and runtime. The primary compute unit used is the NPU. The model can be run on a variety of Qualcomm® devices, including QCS8275, QCS8450, QCS8550, QCS9075, SA7255P, SA8255, SA8295P, SA8650, SA8775P, and Snapdragon® devices such as the Samsung Galaxy S24 and S25, as well as the Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 QRD and Snapdragon X Elite CRD. The model can be executed using different runtimes, including TFLITE, QNN_DLC, and ONNX. The model is part of the Qualcomm® AI Hub and can be deployed on Android using TensorFlow Lite or QNN. The model can be compiled for on-device deployment using PyTorch, and performance profiling and accuracy checks can be conducted on cloud-hosted devices. A demo is available to run the model on sample input data, and the model can also be executed on-device using the Qualcomm® AI Hub Workbench. The model is intended for use in on-device AI applications and is supported by a community for collaboration and feedback.

instruction: Generate a voice for a character who is both excited and confused, with a mix of laughter and gasps.
description: Maya1 is a state-of-the-art speech model for expressive voice generation, designed to capture real human emotion and precise voice design. It allows users to create any voice they can imagine, including specific age, gender, accent, and character traits, and to add emotion tags such as laugh, cry, whisper, rage, sigh, and gasp to make the output feel more realistic. The model streams audio instantly, runs on a single GPU, and has 3B parameters. It outperforms top proprietary models and is developed by Maya Research. Maya1 uses a 3B-parameter decoder-only transformer (Llama-style) to predict SNAC neural codec tokens, enabling real-time streaming at ~0.98 kbps. It is trained on an internet-scale English speech corpus and a curated dataset of studio recordings with human-verified voice descriptions, 20+ emotion tags, multi-accent English coverage, and character variations. The model supports natural language voice control, allowing users to describe voices in plain text without complex parameters or training data. It also supports inline emotion tags for expressive speech, enabling users to add emotions directly into the text. Maya1 is suitable for a variety of use cases, including game character voices, podcast and audiobook production, AI voice assistants, video content creation, customer service AI, and accessibility tools. It is open source under the Apache 2.0 license, allowing commercial use, deployment, and customization. The model can be fine-tuned on custom datasets for specialized voices and is compatible with vLLM for production-scale deployment. It generates 24 kHz audio output and is web audio compatible for browser playback. Maya1 is production-ready and was released in November 2025.

instruction: Provide a translation of a research abstract from English to Persian while preserving the academic tone.
description: LMT-60 is a suite of Chinese-English-centric multilingual machine translation (MMT) models trained on 90B tokens, including both monolingual and bilingual data, covering 60 languages across 234 translation directions. The model achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance among models with similar language coverage. Both the CPT and SFT versions of LMT-60 are available in four sizes: 0.6B, 1.7B, 4B, and 8B. The supervised fine-tuning (SFT) data are publicly released. The model supports translation tasks across a wide range of languages, including 13 high-resource languages, 18 medium-resource languages, and 29 low-resource languages. The high-resource languages include Arabic, English, Spanish, German, French, Italian, Japanese, Dutch, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Turkish, and Chinese. The medium-resource languages include Bulgarian, Bengali, Czech, Danish, Modern Greek, Persian, Finnish, Hindi, Hungarian, Indonesian, Korean, Norwegian, Romanian, Slovak, Swedish, Thai, Ukrainian, and Vietnamese. The low-resource languages include Amharic, Azerbaijani, Tibetan, Modern Hebrew, Croatian, Armenian, Icelandic, Javanese, Georgian, Kazakh, Central Khmer, Kirghiz, Lao, Chinese Mongolian, Marathi, Malay, Burmese, Nepali, Pashto, Sinhala, Swahili, Tamil, Telugu, Tajik, Tagalog, Uighur, Urdu, Uzbek, and Yue Chinese. The model is designed to perform translation tasks and is available for use through the Hugging Face Transformers library. The model is described in a paper titled 'Beyond English: Toward Inclusive and Scalable Multilingual Machine Translation with LLMs,' authored by Yingfeng Luo, Ziqiang Xu, Yuxuan Ouyang, Murun Yang, Dingyang Lin, Kaiyan Chang, Tong Zheng, Bei Li, Peinan Feng, Quan Du, Tong Xiao, and Jingbo Zhu, and available on arXiv with the identifier 2511.07003.

instruction: Render the image from a 30-degree azimuth and a 10-degree elevation with a medium distance.
description: Qwen-Image-Edit-2511-Multiple-Angles-LoRA is a multi-angle camera control LoRA designed for the Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 model. It provides 96 precise camera positions, derived from 4 elevations, 8 azimuths, and 3 distances. The model was trained on over 3000 Gaussian Splatting renders, which are high-quality 3D-consistent images, to enable better spatial understanding and camera control. It supports low-angle camera positions as low as -30°, making it suitable for ground-level and low camera shots. The LoRA enhances the built-in viewpoint capabilities of Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 by offering exact control over camera position, with a large dataset ensuring maximum accuracy. The model is intended for use with the exact prompt format of [azimuth] [elevation] [distance], and it is recommended to use a LoRA strength between 0.8 and 1.0 for optimal results. The model was built by Lovis Odin at fal using synthetic 3D renders with precise camera control. For best results, clear input images with good lighting are recommended, and users are advised to follow the specified prompt order and experiment with low-angle shots, where the model excels.

instruction: Which image best represents the concept of 'a bustling city street at night'?
description: The Perception Encoder (PE) is a state-of-the-art encoder for image and video understanding, trained using a simple vision-language learning approach. It was introduced by Meta and is part of a family of large-scale vision encoder models with strong performance on a wide range of vision tasks. PE uses a robust contrastive pretraining recipe and is finetuned on synthetically aligned videos, enabling it to outperform existing models on classification and retrieval tasks. It also produces strong, general features that scale for downstream tasks, allowing large-scale contrastive pretraining to transfer effectively with alignment tuning. PE Core is the base model, trained with a robust image pretraining schedule and finetuned on data generated by a synthetic video data engine. PE Core comes in three sizes: B, L, and G, with G being the main checkpoint and B and L models distilled from it. The G/14 variant has 1.88 billion parameters for the vision tower, a width of 1536, a depth of 50, an MLP size of 8960, 16 attention heads, a CLIP dimension of 1280, and a resolution of 448px. The text tower for G/14 has 0.47 billion parameters, a width of 1280, a depth of 24, an MLP size of 5120, 20 attention heads, and a CLIP dimension of 1280, with a context length of 72 tokens. All PE Core models use an attention pooling block with 8 heads on top of the vision tower, and the B and L models include an additional class token for global aggregation. PE Core achieves strong results on zero-shot image and video classification and retrieval tasks, with the G/14 variant achieving 85.4% accuracy on ImageNet-1k, 80.2% on ImageNet-v2, 92.6% on ImageNet-A, 88.2% on ObjectNet, 58.1% on COCO-T2I, 76.9% on Kinetics-400, and 51.2% on VTT-T2I. PE Core performs particularly well on challenging benchmarks such as ObjectNet and ImageNet-A. The model is designed for image and text feature extraction and supports tasks such as classification and retrieval. It is based on a vision-language architecture and is compatible with the OpenCLIP framework. The model can be used for encoding images and text, computing similarity scores, and generating label probabilities. The model is available through the OpenCLIP and original GitHub repositories. The model is described in detail in the associated research paper.

instruction: Visualize a peaceful beach at sunset with waves gently rolling onto the sand.
description: Z-Image is a powerful and highly efficient image generation model with 6B parameters. It includes three variants: Z-Image-Turbo, Z-Image-Base, and Z-Image-Edit. Z-Image-Turbo is a distilled version that matches or exceeds leading competitors with only 8 NFEs (Number of Function Evaluations). It offers sub-second inference latency on enterprise-grade H800 GPUs and fits within 16G VRAM consumer devices. It excels in photorealistic image generation, bilingual text rendering (English and Chinese), and robust instruction adherence. Z-Image-Base is the non-distilled foundation model, intended for community-driven fine-tuning and custom development. Z-Image-Edit is a variant fine-tuned for image editing tasks, supporting creative image-to-image generation with strong instruction-following capabilities for precise edits based on natural language prompts. The model uses a Scalable Single-Stream DiT (S3-DiT) architecture, where text, visual semantic tokens, and image VAE tokens are concatenated at the sequence level to serve as a unified input stream, maximizing parameter efficiency compared to dual-stream approaches. According to Elo-based Human Preference Evaluation, Z-Image-Turbo shows highly competitive performance against other leading models and achieves state-of-the-art results among open-source models. The model is supported by the Decoupled-DMD algorithm, a core few-step distillation method that enables the 8-step Z-Image model. Decoupled-DMD is based on the insight that the success of existing DMD methods results from two independent mechanisms: CFG Augmentation (CA), which drives the distillation process, and Distribution Matching (DM), which ensures the stability and quality of the generated output. By decoupling these mechanisms, the model's distillation process is optimized for performance. Building on Decoupled-DMD, the model also incorporates DMDR, which fuses DMD with Reinforcement Learning (RL) during post-training. DMDR improves semantic alignment, aesthetic quality, and structural coherence, while producing images with richer high-frequency details. The model is available for download and use with the Hugging Face diffusers library, with support added via pull requests merged into the latest official release. Users are advised to install diffusers from source to access the latest features and Z-Image support.

instruction: Check the relevance of this slide to the question about team performance indicators.
description: The `llama-nemotron-rerank-vl-1b-v2` is a multimodal reranking model developed by NVIDIA for question-answering retrieval. It is designed to provide a logit score that represents the relevance of a document page to a given query. The model can process documents in the form of image, text, or a combination of both. The expected images are screenshots of document pages or slides, and the model supports images containing text, tables, charts, and infographics. The reranking model is a key component of a multimodal retrieval system, such as a vision RAG pipeline, where it helps improve overall accuracy by reranking top candidate documents retrieved by embedding models. The model is ready for commercial use and is suitable for users who want to build a multimodal question-and-answer application over a large corpus, leveraging the latest dense retrieval technologies. The model is based on the Eagle VLM architecture with a SigLIP 2 400M vision encoder and the Llama 3.2 1B language model. It is a cross-encoder model with approximately 1.7B parameters, fine-tuned for the ranking task. The final embedding is aggregated using a mean pooling strategy, and a binary classification head is used to produce the logit output. The model was fine-tuned exclusively on image data with a maximum of 4 input tiles and a maximum context length of 2048 tokens. For evaluation, it was tested on image-only, image+text, and text-only inputs with a maximum of 6 input tiles and a maximum context length of 10240 tokens. Inputs exceeding the maximum length are truncated. The model outputs a list of floats, where each value corresponds to a raw logit. Users can apply a Sigmoid activation function to convert the logits into probabilities. The model is optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems, leveraging NVIDIA’s hardware and software frameworks for faster training and inference. The model requires transformers version 4.47.1 or higher and flash-attention 2.6.3 or higher. It is compatible with NVIDIA Ampere, Hopper, Lovelace, and Blackwell microarchitectures and is supported on Linux. The model was fine-tuned using publicly available image datasets and synthetic queries generated from proprietary models. The training data includes a mix of automated, human, and synthetic data collection and labeling methods. The model was evaluated on the ViDoRe V1, V2, and V3 benchmarks, as well as two internal visual retrieval datasets: DigitalCorpora-10k and Earnings V2. On these benchmarks, the model achieved an average Recall@5 improvement of approximately 7.2% for text modality, 6.9% for image modality, and 6% for image+text modality compared to the VLM embedding baseline. The model also demonstrated competitive performance on text retrieval benchmarks, comparable to NVIDIA's text-only reranking model. The model is designed to be used in conjunction with the `llama-nemotron-embed-vl-1b-v2` embedding model. The model is intended for use in document reranking for retrieval, where user queries can be text and documents can be text, document page images, charts, tables, and infographics. The model does not generate or reverse engineer personal data, and no personal data was used to create the model. The dataset is reviewed upon addition and as needed. The model has been verified to meet prescribed NVIDIA quality standards and is intended for use by generative AI developers working with conversational AI models. The model has technical limitations, including a maximum sequence length of 10240 tokens. Longer text inputs should be truncated. The model does not always guarantee to provide a meaningful ranking of documents for a given query. The use of the model is governed by specific terms of use, and the post-processing scripts are licensed under a separate license. The model is built with Llama.

instruction: Could you help me understand what the main focus of this image is?
description: LSE-DINOv2 is a DINOv2 Vision Transformer enhanced with a Deep Equilibrium Model (DEM) based local scale adaptation mechanism to improve scale equivariance, ensuring local scale consistency. The model learns to adaptively scale different image regions based on their content using fixed-point iteration to find optimal deformation parameters. This adaptation is applied at multiple transformer layers, and the model is fine-tuned on the ImageNet-1K dataset for image classification. The base architecture is a ViT-Base model with a 14×14 patch size, 224×224 image size, 768 embedding dimension, 12 transformer blocks, 12 attention heads, and 4 register tokens. The DEM adapter includes 3 phi layers, local scaling grids of [16x16, 8x8, 8x8], 2 DEQ layers, CNN channels of [3, 64, 128], 5 DEQ iterations, and is applied at the input, Layer 4, and Layer 8. The model was trained on ImageNet-1K for 20 epochs with a batch size of 200, using the AdamW optimizer with a backbone learning rate of 5e-5 and a DEM learning rate of 1e-5. The model introduces some computational overhead due to DEQ iterations and requires higher memory usage compared to standard DINOv2. It also depends on the `timm` library and optionally the `torchdeq` library. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 license and is described in the paper titled 'Local Scale Equivariance with Latent Deep Equilibrium Canonicalizer' by Rahman et al., published in the Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision in 2025.

instruction: Can you convert this query into a vector so I can efficiently search for matching content in a database?
description: The Qwen3 Embedding model series is the latest proprietary model of the Qwen family, specifically designed for text embedding and ranking tasks. Building upon the dense foundational models of the Qwen3 series, it provides a comprehensive range of text embeddings and reranking models in various sizes (0.6B, 4B, and 8B). This series inherits the exceptional multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills of its foundational model. The Qwen3 Embedding series represents significant advancements in multiple text embedding and ranking tasks, including text retrieval, code retrieval, text classification, text clustering, and bitext mining. The embedding model has achieved state-of-the-art performance across a wide range of downstream application evaluations. The 8B size embedding model ranks No.1 in the MTEB multilingual leaderboard (as of June 5, 2025, score 70.58), while the reranking model excels in various text retrieval scenarios. The Qwen3 Embedding series offers a full spectrum of sizes (from 0.6B to 8B) for both embedding and reranking models, catering to diverse use cases that prioritize efficiency and effectiveness. Developers can seamlessly combine these two modules. Additionally, the embedding model allows for flexible vector definitions across all dimensions, and both embedding and reranking models support user-defined instructions to enhance performance for specific tasks, languages, or scenarios. The Qwen3 Embedding series offers support for over 100 languages, thanks to the multilingual capabilities of Qwen3 models. This includes various programming languages, and provides robust multilingual, cross-lingual, and code retrieval capabilities. Qwen3-Embedding-8B is a text embedding model with 8B parameters, a context length of 32k, and an embedding dimension of up to 4096, supporting user-defined output dimensions ranging from 32 to 4096. Developers are advised to create tailored instructions specific to their tasks and scenarios, as using instructions typically yields an improvement of 1% to 5% compared to not using them. In multilingual contexts, it is also advised to write instructions in English, as most instructions used during the model training process were originally written in English.

instruction: Could you provide a brief narrative of what's happening in this footage?
description: Keye-VL-1.5 is a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) designed to address fundamental challenges in video comprehension. It incorporates a novel Slow-Fast video encoding strategy, a progressive four-stage pre-training methodology that extends context length up to 128K tokens, and a comprehensive post-training pipeline focused on reasoning enhancement and human preference alignment. The model demonstrates significant improvements in video understanding tasks and maintains competitive performance on general multimodal benchmarks. Keye-VL-1.5 is based on the Qwen3-8B language model and includes a vision encoder initialized from the open-source SigLIP. It supports SlowFast video encoding and native dynamic resolution, preserving the original aspect ratio of images by dividing each into a 14x14 patch sequence. A simple MLP layer maps and merges the visual tokens. The model uses 3D RoPE for unified processing of text, image, and video information. The post-training process includes a non-reasoning stage composed of SFT and MPO training, and a reasoning stage consisting of three key steps: CoT Cold Start, General RL, and Alignment RL. Keye-VL-1.5-8B establishes itself with powerful, state-of-the-art perceptual abilities that are competitive with leading models. It demonstrates exceptional proficiency in video understanding across authoritative public video benchmarks such as Video-MME, Video-MMMU, TempCompass, LongVideoBench, and MMVU. In evaluation sets requiring complex logical reasoning and mathematical problem-solving, such as WeMath, MathVerse, and LogicVista, Keye-VL-1.5-8B displays strong performance, highlighting its advanced capacity for logical deduction and solving complex quantitative problems.

instruction: Can you convert this contract from English to Dutch, ensuring legal terms are correctly translated?
description: Hunyuan Translation Model Version 1.5 includes two models: HY-MT1.5-1.8B, a 1.8B parameter translation model, and HY-MT1.5-7B, a 7B parameter translation model. Both models support mutual translation across 33 languages and include 5 ethnic and dialect variations. HY-MT1.5-7B is an upgraded version of the WMT25 championship model, optimized for explanatory translation and mixed-language scenarios. It supports terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. HY-MT1.5-1.8B, despite having less than one-third the parameters of HY-MT1.5-7B, delivers comparable translation performance, achieving both high speed and high quality. After quantization, the 1.8B model can be deployed on edge devices and supports real-time translation scenarios, making it widely applicable. HY-MT1.5-1.8B achieves industry-leading performance among models of the same size, surpassing most commercial translation APIs. It supports deployment on edge devices and real-time translation scenarios, offering broad applicability. HY-MT1.5-7B has been optimized for annotated and mixed-language scenarios compared to its September open-source version. Both models support terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. The models are available in multiple quantization formats, including FP8 and Int4. The models support a wide range of languages, including Chinese, English, French, Portuguese, Spanish, Japanese, Turkish, Russian, Arabic, Korean, Thai, Italian, German, Vietnamese, Malay, Indonesian, Filipino, Hindi, Traditional Chinese, Polish, Czech, Dutch, Khmer, Burmese, Persian, Gujarati, Urdu, Telugu, Marathi, Hebrew, Bengali, Tamil, Ukrainian, Tibetan, Kazakh, Mongolian, and Uyghur. The models can be used with the transformers library, and specific parameters are recommended for inference. The models do not have a default system prompt. The HY-MT1.5-1.8B and HY-MT1.5-7B models were open-sourced on Hugging Face in December 2025 and September 2025, respectively. The technical report provides more experimental results and analysis.

instruction: Can you create a quick summary of this customer service email so I can see the main issue right away?
description: This is a fine-tuned T5-small model specialized for email summarization. The model can generate both brief (one-line) and detailed (comprehensive) summaries of emails, and is robust to messy, informal inputs with typos and abbreviations. The base model is T5-small, and it was fine-tuned by the Wordcab Team. It is a T5 (Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer) model designed for English language processing and is released under the Apache 2.0 License. The model supports dual-mode summarization through the use of `summarize_brief:` and `summarize_full:` prefixes. It is optimized for fast inference and real-world email processing. The intended uses include summarizing professional and casual emails, email inbox management and prioritization, meeting invitation processing, customer support email triage, and newsletter summarization. Limitations include a maximum input length of 512 tokens (~2500 characters), support for English language only, potential difficulty in capturing nuanced context in very long email threads, and unsuitability for legal or medical document summarization. The model was fine-tuned on a dataset containing 364,000 email-summary pairs, including professional business emails, casual informal communications, meeting invitations and updates, technical discussions, and customer service interactions. The training data was augmented with subtle typos and misspellings in 50% of examples, common abbreviations, and various formatting styles. Training was conducted for one epoch with a batch size of 64 and a learning rate of 3e-4, using a validation split of 1%. The model was trained on an NVIDIA GPU with mixed precision (fp16) using the HuggingFace Transformers 4.36.0 framework. Emails were formatted as `Subject: [subject]. Body: [content]`, and two training examples were created per email (brief and full summaries). Data augmentation was applied to 50% of examples. The model achieved a ROUGE-L score of 0.42, an average inference time of 0.63 seconds for brief summaries and 0.71 seconds for full summaries on a T4 GPU, and a coherence score of 80% on messy inputs. It successfully differentiates between brief and full summaries, with a 2.5x length difference, and is robust in handling informal text and typos. The model is based on the T5 architecture, which includes an encoder and decoder with multiple T5 blocks, embedding layers, and a linear language model head. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 License and is based on the T5 model. It was fine-tuned on a dataset and trained using HuggingFace Transformers. Special thanks are given to the open-source community.

instruction: Could you retrieve the most up-to-date reports on urban sustainability practices?
description: The mxbai-edge-colbert-v0-17m is a lightweight ColBERT model with 17 million parameters and a projection dimension of 48. It is built on top of ModernBERT, benefiting from its architectural efficiencies. Despite its small size, it is the best-performing 'edge-sized' retriever, outperforming ColBERTv2 and many models with over ten times more parameters. The model can create multi-vector representations for documents of up to 32,000 tokens and is fully compatible with the PyLate library. It is designed for end-to-end retrieval tasks and supports encoding documents and queries, indexing, and retrieving top-k matches. It is also well-suited for use as a re-ranker following a first-stage retrieval with more lightweight models. On the BEIR benchmark, it achieves an average score of 0.490, with specific scores of 0.416 on MS MARCO, 0.719 on SciFact, 0.316 on Touche, 0.326 on FiQA, 0.713 on TREC-COVID, 0.551 on NQ, and 0.410 on DBPedia. On the LongEmbed benchmark, it achieves an average score of 0.847 for 32k tokens and 0.776 for 4k tokens. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 license. If used, it should be cited using the provided BibTeX entries for the associated tech report, projection variants report, and PyLate library.

instruction: What laboratory action is the subject performing in this clip?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of VideoMAE-v2 vit_base_patch16_224 for video classification on the FineBio dataset. The base model is VideoMAE-v2 (vit_base_patch16_224), and it has been fine-tuned on the FineBio dataset for a total of 32 classes. The model processes video clips consisting of 16 frames, each with a resolution of 224x224 pixels. The training configuration includes the AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of 0.001, a batch size of 8 (effective: 24), and 50 training epochs. Additional training parameters include a weight decay of 0.05, a layer decay of 0.75, a drop path rate of 0.1, and 10 warmup epochs. The dataset used is a custom video dataset for laboratory action recognition, which contains 32 classes, but the sizes of the training, validation, and test sets are not provided. The best validation accuracy is not available. The model is intended for video classification tasks and is based on the VideoMAE-v2 architecture. Users are advised to cite the associated research paper if they use this model. For any questions or issues, users should open an issue in the model repository.

instruction: How do I make sure the most accurate documents appear first in a conversational search?
description: The zeroentropy/zerank-1-small is a 1.7B parameter reranker model, serving as a smaller version of the flagship model. Despite being over 2x smaller, it maintains nearly the same level of performance, outperforming other popular rerankers and showing significant accuracy improvements over traditional vector search. The model is designed to improve the accuracy of retrieval systems and is intended for use in search engines. It is released under the open-source Apache 2.0 license to support the open-source community and advance the capabilities of open-source models. The model can be used for reranking tasks and is compatible with the sentence_transformers library as a CrossEncoder. Evaluations show that the model achieves high NDCG@10 scores across various tasks, including Code, Conversational, Finance, Legal, Medical, and STEM. For example, in the Code task, it achieves an NDCG@10 score of 0.730, outperforming the embedding baseline of 0.678 and other rerankers. In the Conversational task, it achieves 0.556, surpassing the embedding baseline of 0.250. In the Finance task, it achieves 0.861, outperforming the embedding baseline of 0.839. In the Legal task, it achieves 0.817, surpassing the embedding baseline of 0.703. In the Medical task, it achieves 0.773, outperforming the embedding baseline of 0.619. In the STEM task, it achieves 0.680, surpassing the embedding baseline of 0.401. The model also outperforms other rerankers such as cohere-rerank-v3.5 and Salesforce/Llama-rank-v1 in these tasks. The model can be used in conjunction with an initial retriever, such as OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small, to rerank the top 100 candidate documents. The model can be used for inference via ZeroEntropy's endpoint.

instruction: Could you explain the scene in the image to me as if I couldn't see it?
description: BLIP is a Vision-Language Pre-training (VLP) framework designed to transfer flexibly to both vision-language understanding and generation tasks. It is trained on visual question answering using a large architecture with a ViT large backbone. BLIP addresses the limitations of existing pre-trained models, which typically excel in either understanding-based or generation-based tasks but not both. The model improves performance by effectively utilizing noisy web data through a process of bootstrapping captions, where a captioner generates synthetic captions and a filter removes the noisy ones. BLIP achieves state-of-the-art results on a wide range of vision-language tasks, including image-text retrieval with a 2.7% increase in average recall@1, image captioning with a 2.8% increase in CIDEr score, and visual question answering (VQA) with a 1.6% increase in VQA score. It also demonstrates strong generalization ability when directly transferred to video-language tasks in a zero-shot manner. The model can be used for conditional and un-conditional image captioning. Ethical considerations include the fact that this release is for research purposes only in support of an academic paper. The models, datasets, and code are not specifically designed or evaluated for all downstream purposes. Users are strongly recommended to evaluate and address potential concerns related to accuracy, safety, and fairness before deployment. Users should consider the common limitations of AI, comply with applicable laws, and leverage best practices when selecting use cases, particularly for high-risk scenarios where errors or misuse could significantly impact people’s lives, rights, or safety.

instruction: Make a clip of a dancer performing a contemporary routine with colorful lighting effects.
description: The FastWan2.1-T2V-1.3B-Diffusers model is part of the FastWan2.1 series, which is finetuned using a novel Sparse-distill strategy. This strategy combines DMD and VSA in a single training process, leveraging distillation to reduce diffusion steps and sparse attention to minimize attention computations, resulting in faster video generation. The model is based on Wan-AI/Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B-Diffusers and supports efficient 3-step inference, producing high-quality videos at a resolution of 61×448×832. It is trained on the FastVideo 480P Synthetic Wan dataset, which contains 600k synthetic latents. The model can achieve up to 16 FPS on a single H100 GPU. While it is trained on 61×448×832 resolution, it can generate videos at any resolution, though quality may degrade. The model is trained on 4 nodes with 32 H200 GPUs in total, using a global batch size of 64, gradient checkpointing, gradient accumulation steps of 2, and a learning rate of 1e-5. The VSA attention sparsity is set to 0.8, and training runs for 4000 steps, which takes approximately 12 hours. The model supports a wide range of GPUs from H100 to 4090 and is also compatible with Mac users. If the FastWan2.1-T2V-1.3B-Diffusers model is used for research, the associated papers should be cited.

instruction: What’s a good way to detect if two sentences are semantically related?
description: EmbeddingGemma is a 300M parameter open embedding model developed by Google DeepMind, built from Gemma 3 with T5Gemma initialization and the same research and technology used to create Gemini models. It produces vector representations of text, making it suitable for search and retrieval tasks, including classification, clustering, and semantic similarity search. The model was trained on data in over 100 spoken languages, and its small size and on-device focus allow it to be deployed in environments with limited resources such as mobile phones, laptops, or desktops. EmbeddingGemma supports input text strings with a maximum context length of 2048 tokens and outputs numerical vector representations with a default embedding dimension size of 768. Smaller embedding sizes (512, 256, or 128) are available via Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL), which allows users to truncate the output embedding and re-normalize it for efficient and accurate representation. The model was trained on a dataset of approximately 320 billion tokens, including web documents, code and technical documents, and synthetic and task-specific data. Rigorous filtering methods were applied to exclude harmful and sensitive content, including CSAM and personal information. Training was conducted using the latest generation of TPUv5e hardware. The model was evaluated on a range of benchmarks, including MTEB (Multilingual, English, and Code versions), with performance metrics varying by embedding dimension and quantization configuration. EmbeddingGemma can be used for various tasks such as semantic similarity, classification, clustering, retrieval (document and query), question answering, fact verification, and code retrieval. Users are advised to be aware of potential limitations, including biases or gaps in training data, and the model's potential challenges in understanding language ambiguity and nuance. Ethical considerations include the risk of bias perpetuation, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. Developers are encouraged to use de-biasing techniques, adhere to privacy regulations, and monitor for misuse. EmbeddingGemma is intended for responsible AI development and has demonstrated superior performance compared to similarly sized open models.

instruction: What is the income prediction for someone with a technical certification and 7 years of experience?
description: This model is a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) that uses Categorical Embeddings to classify income levels as either greater than 50K or less than or equal to 50K based on census data. It is a more complex and robust approach compared to models that use standard one-hot encoding. The architecture includes specialized Embedding layers for categorical features and has 13.1k parameters. It contains two hidden layers with dimensions 128 and 64, both using ReLU activation. A key feature of the model is its use of dense representations (embeddings) for categorical variables instead of one-hot encoding. The preprocessing pipeline includes Log-transformation for numerical stability and Standard Scaling. The model achieves an accuracy of 0.806 and an F1-Score of 0.768. While slightly more conservative in raw scores compared to base models, it demonstrates excellent generalization. The Embedding architecture is noted for its training stability, as the evaluation loss curves show that the model remains flatter and more controlled compared to base models, which tend to overfit more aggressively.

instruction: What cultural influences can be heard in the instrumentation of this piece?
description: Music Flamingo (MF) is a fully open, state-of-the-art Large Audio-Language Model (LALM) designed to advance music understanding in foundational audio models. It supports deep music understanding across songs and instrumentals, and is capable of generating rich, theory-aware captions and answering questions about harmony, structure, timbre, lyrics, and cultural context. The model is trained using reasoning-centric methods that include chain-of-thought and reinforcement learning with custom rewards for step-by-step reasoning. It is also capable of long-form song reasoning over full-length, multicultural audio with extended context. Evaluations have confirmed its effectiveness, setting new benchmarks on over 10 public music understanding and reasoning tasks. The model is based on a Transformer architecture and includes an AF-Whisper unified audio encoder from Audio Flamingo 3, an MLP-based audio adaptor, and a decoder-only LLM backbone (Qwen2.5-7B). It has 8 billion parameters. The model is intended for non-commercial research purposes only and is supported in HuggingFace Transformers. It processes audio in 30-second windows with a 10-minute total cap per sample, and longer inputs are truncated. The model accepts music (song or instrumental) and text as input, with audio in WAV/MP3/FLAC formats and text in UTF-8. It can handle up to 20 minutes of audio and 24,000 text tokens. The output is text in UTF-8 string format, with a maximum of 2048 tokens. The model is optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems (A100/H100) and is compatible with PyTorch and HuggingFace Transformers. It supports Linux-based operating systems and is intended for use in research and development for applications such as music question answering, long-context music comprehension, and interactive music design assistants. The model was trained on music data collected from various sources, with training data ranging from 10,000 to 1 million hours. The training data was collected using human methods, while labeling was a mix of human and automated methods. Testing was performed on datasets with human-collected data, with labeling also a mix of human and automated methods. The model is a preview version, and a better base and a thinking version are expected to be released in the near future. Ethical considerations include ensuring the model meets industry and use-case requirements and addressing potential misuse. The model is subject to NVIDIA's terms of service and policies on Trustworthy AI.

instruction: How can I get a realistic image generated from my black and white sketch?
description: ControlNet is a neural network structure designed to control diffusion models by adding extra conditions. This specific checkpoint is conditioned on Canny edges, which are represented as a monochrome image with white edges on a black background. The model was developed by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala and is a diffusion-based text-to-image generation model. It is intended to be used in combination with other models, particularly those based on Stable Diffusion. The model supports various input conditions, including Canny edge detection, Midas depth estimation, HED edge detection, M-LSD line detection, normal maps, OpenPose bone images, human scribbles, and semantic segmentation. The Canny edge model was trained on 3 million edge-image, caption pairs for 600 GPU-hours using an Nvidia A100 80G and Stable Diffusion 1.5 as the base model. The model is licensed under a framework adapted from responsible AI licensing practices. It is recommended to use this checkpoint with the Stable Diffusion 1.5 model, although it can also be used with other diffusion models such as dreamboothed stable diffusion. To process an image for the auxiliary conditioning, external dependencies such as OpenCV, diffusers, and related packages are required. The model is described in the paper titled 'Adding Conditional Control to Text-to-Image Diffusion Models' by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala, available on arXiv with the identifier 2302.05543.

instruction: Is there a way to make the video appear as if it's being watched on a CRT monitor?
description: Ditto is a framework designed to address the scarcity of high-quality training data for instruction-based video editing. It introduces a novel data generation pipeline that combines the creative diversity of a leading image editor with an in-context video generator. The framework employs an efficient, distilled model architecture augmented by a temporal enhancer to reduce computational overhead and improve temporal coherence. An intelligent agent drives the entire pipeline, crafting diverse instructions and filtering output to ensure quality control at scale. Using this framework, the Ditto-1M dataset was created, consisting of one million high-fidelity video editing examples. The Editto model was trained on this dataset using a curriculum learning strategy, demonstrating superior instruction-following ability and establishing a new state-of-the-art in instruction-based video editing. Editto is a model designed for video editing based on textual instructions. It supports tasks such as applying editing instructions to input videos to generate output videos. The model can be used with the DiffSynth framework or the ComfyUI interface. When using ComfyUI, it is noted that due to the use of quantized and distilled models, there may be some quality degradation compared to full-precision models. The model requires specific model weights and dependencies to be downloaded and set up in designated directories. The model is licensed under the CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 and is provided for academic research purposes only.

instruction: What is the main focus of this video, and how is it presented?
description: Kwai Keye-VL is an 8-billion-parameter multimodal foundation model designed for leading-edge performance in short-video understanding while maintaining robust general-purpose vision-language abilities. It is engineered to bridge the gap between static image comprehension and dynamic, information-dense short-form videos, which are a dominant medium in today's digital landscape. The model is built on a massive, high-quality dataset exceeding 600 billion tokens with a strong emphasis on video, and an innovative training recipe. This recipe includes a four-stage pre-training process for solid vision-language alignment, followed by a meticulous two-phase post-training process. The first post-training stage enhances foundational capabilities like instruction following, while the second phase focuses on stimulating advanced reasoning. A key innovation in the second phase is the five-mode ``cold-start'' data mixture, which includes ``thinking'', ``non-thinking'', ``auto-think'', ``think with image'', and high-quality video data. This mixture teaches the model to decide when and how to reason. Subsequent reinforcement learning (RL) and alignment steps further enhance these reasoning capabilities and correct abnormal model behaviors, such as repetitive outputs. Kwai Keye-VL achieves state-of-the-art results on public video benchmarks and remains highly competitive on general image-based tasks. Additionally, it shows a significant advantage on the newly developed KC-MMBench, a benchmark tailored for real-world short-video scenarios. The model architecture is based on the Qwen3-8B language model and incorporates a vision encoder initialized from the open-source SigLIP. It supports native dynamic resolution, preserving the original aspect ratio of images by dividing each into a 14x14 patch sequence. A simple MLP layer then maps and merges the visual tokens. The model uses 3D RoPE for unified processing of text, image, and video information, establishing a one-to-one correspondence between position encoding and absolute time to ensure precise perception of temporal changes in video information. The pre-training pipeline features a four-stage progressive strategy: Image-Text Matching, ViT-LLM Alignment, Multi-task Pre-training, and Annealing with model merging. The pre-training data is diverse, including image-text pairs, videos, pure text, and tasks such as fine-grained description, OCR, Q&A, and localization. The data is filtered using CLIP scores and VLM discriminators, and MinHASH is used for deduplication to prevent data leakage. High-quality internal datasets are specifically constructed, especially for detailed captions and Chinese OCR, to compensate for the shortcomings of open-source data. The training process includes four stages: Visual Pre-training, Cross-Modal Alignment, Multi-Task Pre-training, and Annealing Training. The post-training phase is designed into two phases with five stages, aiming to comprehensively enhance the model's performance, especially its reasoning ability in complex tasks. The first phase focuses on the model's basic performance and stability in non-reasoning scenarios, including Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Mixed Preference Optimization (MPO). The second phase is the highlight and major contribution of the training process, introducing a mix-mode Chain of Thought (CoT) and multi-thinking mode reinforcement learning (RL) mechanisms to significantly enhance the model's multimodal perception, reasoning, and think-with-image capabilities. The model is evaluated on a comprehensive suite of authoritative public video benchmarks, including Video-MME, Video-MMMU, TempCompass, LongVideoBench, and MMVU, where it significantly surpasses other top-tier models of a comparable size. It also demonstrates strong performance in evaluation sets that require complex logical reasoning and mathematical problem-solving, such as WeMath, MathVerse, and LogicVista, highlighting its advanced capacity for logical deduction and solving complex quantitative problems.

instruction: Could you help me interpret the depth in this image by creating a visual representation?
description: DA3MONO-LARGE is a monocular depth estimation model designed for high-quality relative depth prediction. It directly predicts depth, which results in superior geometric accuracy compared to disparity-based models like Depth Anything 2. The model is part of the Depth Anything 3 series and is based on a plain transformer architecture with a unified depth-ray representation. It supports tasks such as relative depth estimation and sky segmentation. The model has 0.35B parameters and is released under the Apache 2.0 license. It was developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is trained exclusively on public academic datasets. The model outperforms Depth Anything 2 in monocular depth estimation and VGGT in multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it may have limitations when applied to domain-specific images, and its performance can vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity.

instruction: I need to know if there are any bicycles in this image and where they are located.
description: The Grounding DINO model was proposed by Shilong Liu, Zhaoyang Zeng, Tianhe Ren, Feng Li, Hao Zhang, Jie Yang, Chunyuan Li, Jianwei Yang, Hang Su, Jun Zhu, and Lei Zhang. Grounding DINO extends a closed-set object detection model with a text encoder, enabling open-set object detection. The model achieves remarkable results, such as 52.5 AP on COCO zero-shot. You can use the raw model for zero-shot object detection, which is the task of detecting things in an image out-of-the-box without labeled data.

instruction: Based on recent data, what might be the best time to evaluate a position in $ORBX?
description: Orbital X ($ORBX) is a next-generation crypto token that integrates AI-driven insights to optimize and enhance the cryptocurrency experience. Built on a foundation of powerful, specially trained Large Language Models (LLMs), $ORBX aims to give investors and enthusiasts an edge in the fast-paced world of digital assets. Orbital X bridges the gap between complex data analysis and accessible insights, offering users interactive, personalized guidance, predictive analytics, and investment tools informed by real-time market data. At the heart of $ORBX is a highly trained LLM that processes large volumes of data, including market trends, historical price movements, and social sentiment, generating forecasts for asset volatility and identifying patterns and price indicators. The LLM continuously adapts to incoming information, offering data-driven predictions that factor in emerging patterns, sentiment shifts, and market dynamics. $ORBX’s LLM provides users with an interactive interface where they can ask investment-related questions in plain language. Whether users are looking to refine a trading strategy, understand a token’s performance, or gain insights on recent market trends, $ORBX’s AI responds with personalized, actionable advice. The AI model learns from user inputs and tailors responses to individual preferences, helping them make informed decisions with accurate, user-centered insights. $ORBX’s neural network scans vast amounts of social and market data, detecting shifts in sentiment and interpreting the impact on market behavior. This information enables the LLM to generate real-time analytics on market sentiment, helping investors stay ahead of emerging trends. By correlating news events, social sentiment, and market fluctuations, the LLM identifies major influences on $ORBX’s price and trading volume, delivering insights into the broader context of market changes. The $ORBX platform includes a 3D neural network visualization, allowing users to explore how AI interprets data, patterns, and predictions. The interactive display of neural connections simulates the token’s AI-driven decision-making, providing a clear view into the backend processing. Charts and graphs based on predictive models provide an easy-to-understand visualization of current and forecasted market conditions. The $ORBX AI is powered by a unique Large Language Model trained specifically for the financial and blockchain industries. $ORBX’s LLM is trained on financial data, blockchain transaction histories, social sentiment from platforms like Twitter, and relevant news sources, ensuring a holistic view of market influences. The LLM analyzes market data in real time, identifying trends and forecasting price shifts. It uses sophisticated neural network models to correlate social and market data with predictive power. Through its natural language interface, the LLM delivers custom insights, allowing users to ask questions and receive answers contextualized by recent data trends and personal investment history. The development of the core LLM and neural network for baseline prediction capabilities and sentiment analysis is part of the project’s roadmap, followed by the launch of an interactive dashboard, 3D neural network visualization, and AI-driven advisor. The project also plans to expand AI capabilities with enhanced NLP responses, improved predictive accuracy, and user-tailored insights. $ORBX - Orbital X is licensed under the MIT License.

instruction: I need help retrieving documents related to the latest advancements in AI ethics.
description: The mxbai-edge-colbert-v0-17m is a lightweight ColBERT model with 17 million parameters and a projection dimension of 48. It is built on top of ModernBERT, benefiting from its architectural efficiencies. Despite its small size, it is the best-performing 'edge-sized' retriever, outperforming ColBERTv2 and many models with over ten times more parameters. The model can create multi-vector representations for documents of up to 32,000 tokens and is fully compatible with the PyLate library. It is designed for end-to-end retrieval tasks and supports encoding documents and queries, indexing, and retrieving top-k matches. It is also well-suited for use as a re-ranker following a first-stage retrieval with more lightweight models. On the BEIR benchmark, it achieves an average score of 0.490, with specific scores of 0.416 on MS MARCO, 0.719 on SciFact, 0.316 on Touche, 0.326 on FiQA, 0.713 on TREC-COVID, 0.551 on NQ, and 0.410 on DBPedia. On the LongEmbed benchmark, it achieves an average score of 0.847 for 32k tokens and 0.776 for 4k tokens. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 license. If used, it should be cited using the provided BibTeX entries for the associated tech report, projection variants report, and PyLate library.

instruction: What is happening in the background of this photo, and how does it relate to the foreground?
description: Molmo2 is a family of open vision-language models developed by the Allen Institute for AI (Ai2) that support image, video, and multi-image understanding and grounding. Molmo2 models are trained on publicly available third-party datasets, a collection of datasets with highly-curated image-text and video-text pairs. It has state-of-the-art performance among multimodal models with a similar size. Molmo2-4B is based on a vision backbone and outperforms others in the class of open weight and data models on short videos, counting, and captioning, and is competitive on long-videos. Ai2 is committed to open science. The Molmo2 datasets are available. All other artifacts used in creating Molmo2 (training code, evaluations, intermediate checkpoints) will be made available at a later date, furthering the commitment to open-source AI development and reproducibility. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is intended for research and educational use in accordance with Ai2’s policies. The model is trained on third-party datasets that are subject to academic and non-commercial research use only. Users should review the sources to determine if the model is appropriate for their use case. The Molmo2-4B model achieves an average score of 62.8 on 15 academic benchmarks.

instruction: Generate a voiceover for this script that sounds smooth and realistic.
description: Soprano is an ultra-lightweight, on-device text-to-speech (TTS) model designed for expressive, high-fidelity speech synthesis at unprecedented speed.
