It supports up to 2000x real-time generation on GPU and 20x real-time on CPU. The model enables lossless streaming and is designed for use in various environments, including WebUI, CLI, Python scripts, and OpenAI-compatible endpoints. Soprano allows for basic inference, batched inference, and streaming inference with customizable sampling parameters such as temperature, top_p, and repetition_penalty. It can be configured with different cache sizes and decoder batch sizes to optimize inference speed, though this may increase memory usage. The model is currently English-only and does not support voice cloning. It was trained on 1,000 hours of audio, which is approximately 100 times less than other TTS models, and as a result, mispronunciation of uncommon words may occur. This limitation is expected to improve with additional training data. Users are advised that Soprano is now outdated and should use a newer version instead. On Windows with CUDA, users must reinstall PyTorch explicitly with the correct CUDA wheel after installing Soprano to ensure CUDA support. If Soprano produces unsatisfactory results, users can regenerate the output for a new generation or adjust sampling settings for more varied results. Improper grammar, such as the lack of contractions or multiple spaces, should be avoided for optimal performance.

instruction: What is the best way to extract both segmentation masks and pose data from a group photo?
description: The BBox-Mask-Pose (BMP) method integrates detection, pose estimation, and segmentation into a self-improving loop by conditioning these tasks on each other. This approach enhances all three tasks simultaneously. Using segmentation masks instead of bounding boxes improves performance in crowded scenarios, making top-down methods competitive with bottom-up approaches. The method includes MaskPose, a pose estimation model conditioned by segmentation masks instead of bounding boxes, which boosts performance in dense scenes without adding parameters. The BBox-MaskPose (BMP) method links bounding boxes, segmentation masks, and poses to simultaneously address multi-body detection, segmentation, and pose estimation. A fine-tuned RTMDet is adapted for iterative detection, ignoring 'holes'. The system supports multi-dataset training of ViTPose, previously implemented in the official ViTPose repository but absent in MMPose. The ViTPose-b multi-dataset model uses a ViT-b backbone with a multi-layer decoder, takes RGB images (192x256) as input, and outputs keypoint coordinates (48x64 heatmap for each keypoint, 21 keypoints). It is a vision model not dependent on language, licensed under GPL-3.0, and implemented in the MMPose framework. It was trained for 210 epochs with a batch size of 64 and a learning rate of 5e-5 using 4x NVIDIA A-100 hardware. The model was trained on multiple datasets and performs better in multi-body and crowded scenarios than COCO-trained ViTPose. The MaskPose-b model also uses a ViT-b backbone with a multi-layer decoder, takes RGB images (192x256) and estimated instance segmentation as input, and outputs keypoint coordinates (48x64 heatmap for each keypoint, 21 keypoints). It is a vision model not dependent on language, licensed under GPL-3.0, and implemented in the MMPose framework. It was trained for 210 epochs with a batch size of 64 and a learning rate of 5e-5 using 4x NVIDIA A-100 hardware. It uses training data including SAM-estimated instance masks. Compared to ViTPose, MaskPose takes instance segmentation as input and is better at distinguishing instances in multi-body scenes without computational overhead. The fine-tuned RTMDet-l model uses a CSPNeXt-P5 backbone, CSPNeXtPAFPN neck, and RTMDetInsSepBN head. It takes RGB images as input and outputs detected instances, including bounding boxes, instance masks, and class for each. It is a vision model not dependent on language, licensed under GPL-3.0, and implemented in the MMDetection framework. It was trained for 10 epochs with a batch size of 16 and a learning rate of 2e-2 using 4x NVIDIA A-100 hardware. It was trained on data with randomly masked-out instances and is designed for iterative detection, especially effective in multi-body scenes where background would not be detected otherwise.

instruction: Is it possible to generate a scene based on an image prompt and then modify its lighting and textures afterward?
description: OmniX is a family of flow matching generative models designed for unified panoramic perception, generation, and completion. It enables the creation of graphics-ready 3D scenes suitable for physically based rendering, relighting, and simulation. OmniX repurposes 2D generative models for panoramic perception of geometry, textures, and PBR materials, offering a versatile and unified framework. It is based on a lightweight and efficient cross-modal adapter structure that reuses 2D generative priors for a broad range of panoramic vision tasks. OmniX supports tasks such as panoramic image generation from text or image prompts, panoramic perception of geometric, intrinsic, and semantic properties, and the combination of generation and perception to produce panoramic images with multiple property annotations. The model is evaluated using a large-scale synthetic panorama dataset containing high-quality multimodal panoramas from diverse indoor and outdoor scenes. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of OmniX in panoramic visual perception and graphics-ready 3D scene generation. The model is still under development for certain functionalities, such as graphics-ready scene reconstruction and generation, which are noted as being in progress.

description: gemma-3n-E4B-it is a 4-bit quantized version of a model optimized for Apple Silicon using MLX. It is part of the Community Model Program, which highlights new and noteworthy models created by the community. The model is provided by a third party and is not created, owned, or endorsed by LM Studio. LM Studio does not guarantee the accuracy, reliability, or appropriateness of the model's outputs, and disclaims all warranties related to the model's performance, security, or availability. Users are solely responsible for any damage or issues arising from the use of the model.

instruction: What are the most similar articles on the impact of social media on mental health?
description: The `llama-embed-nemotron-8b` is a text embedding model developed by NVIDIA, optimized for tasks such as retrieval, reranking, semantic similarity, and classification. It is designed for multilingual and cross-lingual text retrieval and serves as a foundational component in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems. The model is based on the Llama-3.1-8B architecture with a bidirectional attention mechanism and consists of 32 hidden layers and an embedding size of 4096. It was trained on a combination of public and synthetically generated datasets, with a total of 16.4 million query-passage pairs. The model achieves state-of-the-art performance on the MTEB (Multilingual, v2) benchmark as of October 21, 2025, with a Borda rank of 1 and 39,573 Borda votes, along with a mean task performance of 69.46. The model is instruction-aware, meaning it supports custom instructions to improve performance for specific use cases. For retrieval tasks, queries must be accompanied by a task instruction in the format `Instruct: {task_instruction}\nQuery: {query}`, while documents do not require special handling. The model outputs embedding vectors of dimension 4096 for each text input and supports input sequences up to 32768 tokens in length. It is compatible with NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems, including architectures such as Ampere, Hopper, Lovelace, Pascal, Turing, and Volta, and is optimized for use with software frameworks like TensorRT and Triton. The model is intended for non-commercial and research use only and is available on Hugging Face as of October 21, 2025. It is recommended to use the model with transformers version 4.51.0 and flash-attention for GPU processing. The model can be integrated using libraries such as Sentence Transformers or Hugging Face Transformers. NVIDIA encourages users to ensure the model meets industry and use-case requirements and to report any concerns regarding model quality, risk, or security vulnerabilities.

instruction: How can I turn my written story about a futuristic city into a 720p animated video?
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to the foundational video generation models, incorporating several key innovations to enhance generation quality and model capability. It introduces a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture into the video diffusion model, which separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models. This design increases the overall model capacity while maintaining the same computational cost. The A14B model series in Wan2.2 uses a two-expert design: a high-noise expert for the early stages of denoising, focusing on overall layout, and a low-noise expert for the later stages, refining video details. Each expert has about 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters, but only 14B active parameters per step, keeping inference computation and GPU memory nearly unchanged. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a threshold step defined to switch from the high-noise to the low-noise expert. Validation results show that the MoE architecture in Wan2.2 achieves the lowest validation loss, indicating superior convergence and video quality compared to other configurations. In addition to the MoE models, Wan2.2 also includes a 5B dense model, TI2V-5B, supported by a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE that achieves a compression ratio of 16×16×4, increasing the overall compression rate to 64 while maintaining high-quality video reconstruction. With an additional patchification layer, the total compression ratio of TI2V-5B reaches 4×32×32. TI2V-5B can generate a 5-second 720P video in under 9 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU, making it one of the fastest 720P@24fps video generation models. It supports both text-to-video and image-to-video tasks within a unified framework. Wan2.2 also incorporates meticulously curated aesthetic data, including detailed labels for lighting, composition, contrast, and color tone, allowing for more precise and controllable cinematic style generation. Compared to Wan2.1, Wan2.2 is trained on a significantly larger dataset, with +65.6% more images and +83.2% more videos, enhancing its generalization across multiple dimensions such as motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among all open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 open-sources a 5B model that supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps and can run on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090. It also supports character animation and replacement, where the model can generate a video of a character image that mimics the human motion in an input video (animation mode) or replace the character image with the input video (replacement mode). Wan2.2 has been integrated into ComfyUI and Diffusers, and its inference code and model weights have been released. It also supports text-to-speech synthesis for the Speech-to-Video generation task. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license provisions, including not generating harmful or illegal content.

instruction: I need the text from this image of a receipt translated into a digital version. Can you help?
description: en_PP-OCRv5_mobile_rec is a text line recognition model developed by the PaddleOCR team as part of the PP-OCRv5_rec series. It is designed to efficiently and accurately recognize English text. The model achieves an accuracy of 85.3 percent, where a line is considered incorrect if any character, including punctuation, is wrong. This strict evaluation ensures higher accuracy in practical applications. The model can be used for text recognition tasks and is part of a larger OCR pipeline that includes modules for document image orientation classification, text image unwarping, text line orientation classification, text detection, and text recognition. The en_PP-OCRv5_mobile_rec model is intended to be used in conjunction with other models in the pipeline to address complex real-world OCR challenges. The model supports integration into projects through the PaddleOCR library, which requires installation of PaddlePaddle and PaddleOCR. The model can be run using a command-line interface or integrated into Python code for inference. The default model in the pipeline is PP-OCRv5_server_rec, so specifying en_PP-OCRv5_mobile_rec is necessary for use in the pipeline. The model can also be used with a local model file if provided.

instruction: I'd like to see a detailed scene of a dragon flying over a mountain range with a storm in the background.
description: Quantized GGUF versions of the model by Tongyi-Mai. The available models include Z-Image Turbo GGUF and Qwen3-4B (Text Encoder). The model can be used with tools provided by city96. The model is designed for image generation tasks and supports detailed text-to-image synthesis. It utilizes a transformer-based architecture and can be loaded using the Diffusers library with specific quantization configurations. The model can be run on CUDA-enabled devices with options for attention backend selection, model compilation, and CPU offloading to optimize performance and memory usage. The model is evaluated for efficiency and inference speed, particularly with the Turbo variant requiring zero guidance and a reduced number of inference steps. Users are advised to consult the original model card for detailed information. The repository follows the same license as the original model.

instruction: Is there a way to make a video of a forest scene appear as if it was shot at golden hour?
description: TC-Light is a one-shot model designed to manipulate the illumination distribution of video and achieve realistic world transfer. It is particularly suitable for high-dynamic videos, such as those with motion-rich actions and frequent switching of foreground and background objects. The model is distinguished by its outstanding temporal consistency on highly dynamic scenarios and superior computational efficiency, enabling long video processing, such as 300 frames at a resolution of 1280x720 on a 40G A100 GPU. These features make it valuable for sim2real and real2real augmentation for embodied agents or for preparing video pairs to train stronger video relighting models. TC-Light is a novel generative renderer that addresses the limitations of existing models, which are often constrained by the domain of training data or face bottlenecks in temporal consistency and computational efficiency, especially with complex dynamics and long durations. The model operates in two stages: the first stage optimizes appearance embedding to align global illumination, and the second stage optimizes the Unique Video Tensor (UVT), a canonical video representation, to align fine-grained texture and lighting. To evaluate performance, a long and highly dynamic video benchmark was established. Extensive experiments show that TC-Light enables physically plausible re-rendering results with superior temporal coherence and low computational cost. The model tokenizes input latents in the xy plane and yt plane separately, and the predicted noises are combined for denoising. The output undergoes the two-stage optimization process. The model works better on video resolutions over 512x512, as this is the minimum resolution used to train the related IC-Light model. Higher resolutions help maintain the consistency of image intrinsic properties. It performs relatively better on realistic scenes than on synthetic ones, in terms of both temporal consistency and physical plausibility. However, it struggles to drastically change the illumination of night scenarios or hard shadows, as demonstrated in IC-Light.

instruction: Detect and outline all the trees in this nature reserve photo.
description: YOLOWorld is an SDK that enables efficient open-vocabulary object detection using YOLO-Worldv2 Large. It is optimized for Axera’s NPU-based SoC platforms, including the AX650 Series, AX630C Series, and AX8850 Series, as well as Axera's dedicated AI accelerator. The model supports inference on both host systems and M.2 accelerator cards, such as those used with Raspberry PI 5. It includes two main models: one for object detection (yolo_u16_ax650.axmodel and yolo_u16_ax630c.axmodel) and one for text encoding (clip_b1_u16_ax650.axmodel and clip_b1_u16_ax630c.axmodel). On the AX650 platform, the object detection model has an input shape of 1 x 640 x 640 x 3 and a latency of 9.522 ms, with a CMM usage of 21 MB, while the text encoder has an input shape of 1 x 77 and a latency of 2.997 ms, with a CMM usage of 137 MB. On the AX630C platform, the object detection model has a latency of 43.450 ms and a CMM usage of 31 MB, and the text encoder has a latency of 10.703 ms and a CMM usage of 134 MB. The model is designed to accept text inputs for object detection, such as 'person', 'dog', 'car', 'horse', 'man', 'shoes', 'ball', and others, and it processes images for inference. The SDK includes a Python interface with example scripts for running inference, and it requires specific dependencies installed via pip. The model is deployed using precompiled shared libraries and model files, and it supports both host and AXCL device types for execution. The system outputs detection results in multiple feature maps with varying spatial resolutions. The model is intended for use with Axera hardware and may not be compatible with other platforms.

instruction: Generate a scene of a cozy living room with the sound of a crackling fire and soft piano.
description: LTX-2 is a DiT-based audio-video foundation model designed to generate synchronized video and audio within a single model. It integrates the core building blocks of modern video generation, with open weights and a focus on practical, local execution. The model is developed by Lightricks and is a diffusion-based audio-video foundation model that supports English. It includes several checkpoints, such as the full model in bf16, fp8, and nvfp4 quantizations, a distilled version of the full model, a LoRA version of the distilled model, and upscalers for spatial and temporal resolution enhancement. The model can be used for image-to-video generation and is supported in the Diffusers framework. It is accessible via online demos and can be run locally using the PyTorch codebase, which includes model definitions, pipelines, and training capabilities. The codebase requires Python 3.12 or higher, CUDA version 12.7 or higher, and PyTorch 2.7. For local use, it is recommended to use the built-in LTXVideo nodes in the ComfyUI Manager. The model has several limitations: it is not intended to provide factual information, may amplify societal biases, may fail to generate videos that perfectly match prompts, prompt following is influenced by prompting style, may generate inappropriate or offensive content, and audio generated without speech may be of lower quality. The base model is fully trainable, and it is easy to reproduce the LoRAs and IC-LoRAs published with the model by following the provided instructions. Training for motion, style, or likeness (sound+appearance) can take less than an hour in many settings.

instruction: Could you generate a feature vector for this image that works well with k-nearest neighbors?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. These models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones that provide multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers on the class token, or a linear layer applied on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation using linear layers, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) consists of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) consists of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, image retrieval, 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification. Evaluation results show that the ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on web data achieve high scores on IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair. The ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web data also achieve strong performance on similar tasks. The ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on satellite data achieve high scores on GEO-Bench classification and segmentation tasks. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters), ViT-S+ (29M parameters), ViT-B (86M parameters), ViT-L (300M parameters), ViT-H+ (840M parameters), and ViT-7B (6716M parameters), all with a patch size of 16 and varying embedding dimensions, register tokens, heads, and FFN types. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The models were trained using Nvidia H100 GPUs on private infrastructure in the USA, with 61,440 hours of compute time and an estimated carbon emission of 18t CO2eq. The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: I’m working on a project where I need a clear foreground extraction. Can you assist?
description: BiRefNet is a model designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation (DIS). It is trained on the DIS-TR dataset and validated on DIS-TE and DIS-VD datasets. The model has achieved state-of-the-art performance on three tasks: DIS, HRSOD, and COD. BiRefNet is based on a bilateral reference approach, which is intended to improve segmentation accuracy and resolution. The model supports inference on single images and can be used with adjustable resolutions. It is implemented in Python and can be loaded using the HuggingFace Transformers library or from local GitHub code. The model weights are available on HuggingFace, and the model can be used for inference with or without GPU acceleration. The model includes a preprocessing pipeline that resizes images, normalizes pixel values, and applies a sigmoid function to the final predictions. The model outputs a segmentation mask that can be visualized or used for further processing. The model is described in the paper 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation,' published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research in 2024. The authors of the model are Peng Zheng, Dehong Gao, Deng-Ping Fan, Li Liu, Jorma Laaksonen, Wanli Ouyang, and Nicu Sebe, affiliated with Nankai University, Northwestern Polytechnical University, National University of Defense Technology, Aalto University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and University of Trento, respectively. The model is supported by GPU resources provided by @fal and deployment assistance from @not-lain.

instruction: Can you look at this protein and tell me if it's likely to be part of a secretion pathway?
description: SignalSeeker is a machine learning ensemble designed for predicting signal peptides in protein sequences. It integrates multiple algorithms, including Random Forest, Extra Trees, SVM, and Logistic Regression, with ProtBERT embeddings to achieve high accuracy in signal peptide detection. The model is intended to predict whether a protein sequence contains a signal peptide, assist in protein subcellular localization prediction, support research in protein secretion pathways, and aid in biotechnology applications requiring secreted proteins. The ensemble architecture includes feature extraction using ProtBERT embeddings of the N-terminal 50 amino acids, ensemble models with regularization, feature scaling via StandardScaler normalization, and a weighted ensemble decision logic with confidence assessment. The model was trained on 5000 mixed sequences from UniProt verified eukaryotic proteins and tested on 1000 mixed sequences from the same source, isolated from the training data. Performance metrics include a test AUC of 0.99433 for the best model, Logistic Regression (L2), with cross-validation AUCs ranging from 0.98711 to 0.99433 across models. The model is trained primarily on eukaryotic sequences and may exhibit reduced performance for prokaryotic proteins or highly divergent organisms. It requires sequences of at least 50 amino acids for optimal performance. The model is for research purposes only and not intended for clinical diagnosis. Users are advised to validate results experimentally and consider potential biases in the training data. SignalSeeker is released under the MIT License.

instruction: Can you create a motion sequence where a person gracefully flaps their arms like wings?
description: Semantic Motion Generation (SMoG) is a state-of-the-art text-to-motion synthesis model that leverages CLIP semantics and Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN)-enhanced transformers to generate realistic, diverse 3D human motions from textual descriptions. Unlike conventional approaches, SMoG replaces linear layers in transformer architectures with KANLayers, enabling adaptive nonlinear feature learning and superior motion-text alignment. It generates the sequence of SMPL body model movement for each frame. SMoG is built on MotionCLIP — a 3D motion autoencoder trained to reconstruct poses using natural language. It employs a latent space representing abstract, compressed data features non-trivially present in the input space. This approach reduces reliance on classical data labeling by using contrastive learning to distinguish similarity, identity, or difference between text-motion pairs. During training, action-text pairs are matched for similarity (positive) or dissimilarity (negative), maximizing positive pair similarity and minimizing negative pair similarity. The latent space enables semantic alignment between input text and generated motion, producing meaningful interpretations. For example, inputting 'wings' or a cultural reference could generate a wing-like arm motion without explicit training examples. The model is trained not only to reconstruct motions but also to align their latent representations with text labels in CLIP space. The KANLayer, core to Kolmogorov–Arnold Networks (KAN), implements a hybrid function approximation approach based on the Kolmogorov-Arnold theorem. This theorem states that any multivariate continuous function can be represented as a superposition of univariate functions, achieved in KANLayer via linear transformations and nonlinear B-spline components. Structurally, the layer has two parallel branches: a base branch performing linear transformation with activation (default: SiLU), and a spline branch adding nonlinearity through adaptive B-splines. The spline branch operates on coefficients 'spline_weight', computed via B-spline interpolation on a fixed grid dividing the input range (e.g., [-1, 1]) into grid_size intervals. The spline order ('spline_order') determines smoothness and complexity. The 'b_splines' method implements the recursive Cox-de Boor formula for basis functions. Spline weight initialization includes noise addition (scale_noise) and least-squares solving in 'curve2coeff' to approximate initial weights. The 'enable_standalone_scale_spline' parameter allows independent scaling of spline coefficients via 'spline_scaler' for training stability. During the forward pass, inputs are processed by both branches: the base branch generates linear output via activation and matrix multiplication, while the spline branch computes nonlinear offsets via B-spline convolution with trainable weights. Outputs are summed to form the final tensor. The model supports multi-scenario motion generation (e.g., dances, sports, daily activities) from free-form text prompts. It supports both CPU and GPU inference, though GPU is significantly faster. The model is trained on the AMASS dataset, which aggregates motion capture data from academic sources to cover diverse actions—from walking to complex gestures. The dataset records 3D joint coordinates using advanced motion-tracking technologies, preserving temporal sequences where each row represents a timestamp and columns store detailed x, y, z coordinates for body joints, formatted in npz. Notably, AMASS integrates action labels, anthropometric parameters, and synchronized sensor data, enabling detailed biomechanical studies and training deep learning models (e.g., LSTMs, Transformers) for 3D mesh pose prediction. A key feature is its scalability, encompassing tens of thousands of motions. AMASS is licensed for academic research, permitting non-commercial use with attribution, while commercial applications require explicit permission. The dataset is accompanied by visualization tools and code examples, allowing researchers to utilize its potential while preserving participant anonymity. The model's performance is evaluated using several metrics, including R@1, R@5, MMD, and FCD. SMoG achieves R@1 of 0.0004158, R@5 of 0.006237, MMD of 0.020108, and FCD of 0.50885. Additional loss metrics include Vel-Loss of 0.00029719, Text Cosine of 0.27195, rcxyz of 0.00066315, rc of 0.0014857, Mixed without CLIP of 0.24461, Mixed with CLIP of 0.73316, Mixed CLIP only of 0.48855, and Image Cosine of 0.2166. The model is implemented in PyTorch and requires specific dependencies, including CLIP, SMPL, SMPL-X, PyTorch3D, and MotionCLIP. The code is distributed under a license that must be followed, and the model depends on other libraries, each with their own respective licenses. The model is trained using a combination of losses, including velocity loss, text cosine loss, rcxyz loss, rc loss, and mixed losses with and without CLIP. The model is evaluated using a script that logs metrics with wandb. The model is trained on a dataset that includes SMPL and SMPL+H model files, and the training process involves a main training loop with fine-tuning options. The model is evaluated using a script that generates metrics and logs them with wandb. The model is trained using a combination of losses, including velocity loss, text cosine loss, rcxyz loss, rc loss, and mixed losses with and without CLIP. The model is evaluated using a script that generates metrics and logs them with wandb. The model is trained using a combination of losses, including velocity loss, text cosine loss, rcxyz loss, rc loss, and mixed losses with and without CLIP. The model is evaluated using a script that generates metrics and logs them with wandb.

instruction: What is the predicted log KOA for the structure represented by the SMILES string C1CCCCC1C(=O)O?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict the octanol-air partition coefficient (log KOA). This property measures a compound's tendency to partition between octanol and air, indicating volatility and environmental transport potential. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. The model type is AttentiveFP (Graph Neural Network), and it performs a regression task. It accepts SMILES strings as input and produces a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and is based on an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model has 128 hidden channels, 3 layers, 3 timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.1, a learning rate of 0.0005, a weight decay of 0.0001, a batch size of 32, and is trained for 50 epochs with a patience of 10. The model was trained on the KOA dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: I want to know if the person in this image is standing, sitting, or lying down—can you determine that?
description: YOLO26-X-pose is a variant of the Ultralytics YOLO26 model, specifically optimized for human keypoint detection. It is the latest evolution in the YOLO series, designed from the ground up for edge and low-power devices. The model is a native end-to-end architecture that produces predictions directly without the need for non-maximum suppression (NMS), which eliminates a post-processing step and results in faster, lighter, and easier deployment in real-world systems. The end-to-end design simplifies integration, reduces latency, and enhances deployment robustness across diverse environments. YOLO26 introduces the MuSGD optimizer, a hybrid of SGD and Muon, inspired by Moonshot AI's Kimi K2 breakthroughs in LLM training, which provides enhanced stability and faster convergence. The model includes features such as Residual Log-Likelihood Estimation (RLE) for more accurate keypoint localization, optimized decoding for increased inference speed, and the removal of DFL to simplify inference and improve hardware compatibility. It achieves an mAP Pose of 71.6 (50-95, e2e) and 91.6 (50, e2e), with a CPU inference speed of 565.4 ms and a T4 TensorRT10 speed of 12.2 ms. The model has 57.6 million parameters and 201.7 billion FLOPs. It is optimized for edge computing, offering up to 43% faster CPU inference.

instruction: What approach would you suggest for matching images with different lighting conditions?
description: LightGlue is a neural network designed to match two sets of local features by jointly finding correspondences and rejecting non-matchable points. It is a variant of the LightGlue model trained on DISK and is available under a commercially permissive Apache 2.0 license. The model requires the `kornia` library to be installed and is usable with the Transformers library. LightGlue was proposed by Philipp Lindenberger, Paul-Edouard Sarlin, and Marc Pollefeys. It is useful for tasks such as image matching, homography estimation, and pose estimation between images. The model is adaptive to the difficulty of the problem, with faster inference on image pairs that are easier to match. It is more efficient in terms of memory and computation, more accurate, and easier to train than the previous state-of-the-art model, SuperGlue. LightGlue can introspect the confidence of its predictions and adapts its depth and width during inference. It can stop at an early layer if all predictions are ready and discards non-matchable points early in the process. The model is developed by ETH Zurich - Computer Vision and Geometry Lab and is designed for feature matching and pose estimation tasks in computer vision. It can be applied to a variety of multiple-view geometry problems and can handle challenging real-world indoor and outdoor environments. However, it may not perform well on tasks that require different types of visual understanding, such as object detection or image classification. LightGlue is trained on large annotated datasets for pose estimation, including image pairs with ground truth correspondences and unmatched keypoints derived from ground truth poses and depth maps. It is first pre-trained with synthetic homographies sampled from 1 million images and then fine-tuned with the MegaDepth dataset, which includes 1 million crowd-sourced images of 196 tourism landmarks. The model is trained using a supervised setup and runs in real-time on a modern GPU, with a forward pass taking approximately 44 milliseconds (22 FPS) for an image pair. It has 13.7 million parameters and is suitable for integration into modern Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) or Structure-from-Motion (SfM) systems.

instruction: Please produce a voice output in Odia for a radio jingle promoting literacy in the region.
description: svara-TTS is a multilingual text-to-speech (TTS) model designed for 19 languages, including 18 Indic languages and Indian English. It is built using an Orpheus-style discrete audio token approach, aiming to provide clarity, expressiveness, and low-latency performance on commodity GPUs and CPUs. The model supports lightweight emotion and style control through tags such as `` and `` and allows for simple speaker identities using the format `Language (Gender)`. It also enables zero-shot adaptation for new use cases. The model is trained on over 2000 hours of high-quality speech data from sources including SYSPIN, RASA, IndicTTS, and SPICOR, covering approximately 50 speakers across the 19 languages. The data was selected to support natural prosody, broad language coverage, and stable multilingual transfer. The model is intended for use in multilingual assistants, IVR systems, learning applications, reading aids, accessibility tools, and content localization for education, public information, and civic services. It is also suitable for research on Indic prosody, emotion control, and cross-lingual transfer. It is not intended for impersonation of individuals, deceptive content creation, or safety-critical deployments without human oversight. The model has limitations, including challenges with proper nouns and rare entities, very long sentences, varying emotion strength by language, and code-mixing that is not fully deterministic. These limitations can be improved with targeted LoRA finetuning and better preprocessing. Users are encouraged to follow applicable laws and ethical guidelines, avoid harmful uses such as impersonation or harassment, and disclose synthetic speech to end users where appropriate. The model is licensed under Apache-2.0 and is versioned as v1.0.0, marking its initial public release with support for 19 languages.

instruction: Transcribe this 18-minute English educational video and note the key examples mentioned.
description: Voxtral-Small-24B-2507-FP8-dynamic is a model based on the VoxtralForConditionalGeneration architecture, designed to process audio-text inputs and generate text outputs. It is optimized through FP8 quantization for both weights and activations, reducing GPU memory requirements by approximately 50% and increasing matrix-multiply compute throughput by approximately 2x. The model is intended for audio understanding tasks, including transcription, question answering, summarization, and function calling based on spoken input. It supports a pure speech transcription mode with automatic language detection and transcribes audio in multiple languages, including English, Spanish, French, Portuguese, Hindi, German, Dutch, and Italian. Voxtral can handle audios up to 30 minutes for transcription or 40 minutes for understanding, with a 32k token context length. It retains the text understanding capabilities of its language model backbone, Ministral-3B. The model was evaluated on the Fleurs transcription task, with performance measured by word error rate (WER) recovery. For English, the WER is 3.43% with 100.0% recovery; for French, 3.96% with 100.0% recovery; for Spanish, 2.84% with 100.1% recovery; for German, 3.36% with 100.1% recovery; for Italian, 2.54% with 99.7% recovery; for Portuguese, 3.57% with 100.0% recovery; and for Dutch, 5.29% with 100.1% recovery. The model was developed by Red Hat and released on 08/21/2025, with version 1.0. It was created by quantizing the MLP operators within transformer blocks of the language model using a symmetric static per-channel scheme for weights and a symmetric dynamic per-token scheme for activations. The model can be deployed using the vLLM framework and supports both transcription and audio instruct use cases.

instruction: How do I troubleshoot the error code I received when using my RealRobot?
description: The RealRobot_chatbot_llm is a highly optimized GGUF (quantized) version of a language model developed by fibonacciai. It is built on the Gemma 3n architecture and fine-tuned on a proprietary dataset from the RealRobot product catalog. The model is designed to serve as a proof-of-concept for a scalable business model that enables the rapid creation of accurate, cost-effective, and deployable specialized language models for any business based on their own product data. The model is trained exclusively on product data, eliminating the noise and inaccuracy of general-purpose models, and provides authoritative, relevant answers directly related to the RealRobot product line. The GGUF format allows for seamless integration into embedded systems, mobile applications, and local servers using tools like llama.cpp. The model is based on the Gemma 3n architecture, which is optimized for size and speed, with a model size of approximately 4 billion parameters in quantized form. It is fine-tuned from the gemma-3n-e2b-it-bnb-4bit base. The training data is derived from the RealRobot website product documentation and support materials, ensuring the chatbot can accurately answer customer questions about product features, usage, and troubleshooting. The model is intended for use in customer support and product-related inquiries. It is highly specialized and will have limited performance on general knowledge outside the RealRobot domain. Output should be verified by human oversight before use in critical processes. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license.

instruction: Can you create a video from this photo where the person moves in a realistic and coherent way?
description: SteadyDancer is a model designed for harmonized and coherent human image animation with first-frame preservation. It is intended to animate human images while maintaining the structure and appearance of the first frame. The model is associated with the Multimedia Computing Group at Nanjing University and the Platform and Content Group (PCG) at Tencent. This repository provides checkpoints for the model described in the paper titled 'SteadyDancer: Harmonized and Coherent Human Image Animation with First-Frame Preservation.' The model offers lower computational cost compared to alternatives. For users encountering a 'RuntimeError: expected scalar type Float but found BFloat16' issue, it is recommended to pull the latest code of Kijai ComfyUI. For each quantized version of the model, two sets of weights are provided: one derived from fp16 format and another from fp32 format. Both sets of weights are available and yield largely consistent results. The team is currently working to determine the final version of the model to be retained.

instruction: What locations are mentioned in this research paper's abstract?
description: The bert-base-NER is a fine-tuned BERT model designed for Named Entity Recognition (NER) and achieves state-of-the-art performance for the NER task. It is trained to recognize four types of entities: location (LOC), organizations (ORG), person (PER), and Miscellaneous (MISC). Specifically, this model is a bert-base-cased model that was fine-tuned on the English version of the standard dataset. A larger BERT-large model fine-tuned on the same dataset is also available. The model can be used with the Transformers pipeline for NER. It is limited by its training dataset of entity-annotated news articles from a specific span of time, which may not generalize well for all use cases in different domains. Additionally, the model occasionally tags subword tokens as entities, and post-processing of results may be necessary to handle those cases. The model was fine-tuned on the English version of the standard dataset, which distinguishes between the beginning and continuation of an entity to allow the model to output where the second entity begins. Each token is classified as one of the following classes: O (Outside of a named entity), B-MISC (Beginning of a miscellaneous entity right after another miscellaneous entity), I-MISC (Miscellaneous entity), B-PER (Beginning of a person’s name right after another person’s name), I-PER (Person’s name), B-ORG (Beginning of an organization right after another organization), I-ORG (Organization), B-LOC (Beginning of a location right after another location), and I-LOC (Location). The training dataset was derived from the Reuters corpus, consisting of Reuters news stories. The dataset includes 7140 training examples for LOC, 3438 for MISC, 6321 for ORG, and 6600 for PER. The development set includes 1837 LOC, 922 MISC, 1341 ORG, and 1842 PER examples. The test set includes 1668 LOC, 702 MISC, 1661 ORG, and 1617 PER examples. The training dataset consists of 946 articles, 14,987 sentences, and 203,621 tokens. The development dataset includes 216 articles, 3,466 sentences, and 51,362 tokens. The test dataset includes 231 articles, 3,684 sentences, and 46,435 tokens. The model was trained on a single NVIDIA V100 GPU with recommended hyperparameters from the source that trained and evaluated the model on the CoNLL-2003 NER task. The model achieved an f1 score of 95.1 on the development set and 91.3 on the test set. The precision was 95.0 on the development set and 90.7 on the test set. The recall was 95.3 on the development set and 91.9 on the test set. The test metrics are slightly lower than the official Google BERT results, which encoded document context and experimented with CRF.

instruction: What is the significance of the shadowing in this liver ultrasound?
description: This repository contains a drop-in, Hugging Face–compatible checkpoint converted from an original model. It can be loaded using the same code as the original model, without requiring any additional conversion steps. The model is based on an architecture that includes an embedding layer (embed_tokens) and an output layer (lm_head), both of which may change in shape when the vocabulary size is expanded. However, the attention mechanism remains unaffected, as it operates on the hidden state dimensions, which stay consistent. Expanding the vocabulary increases the number of token embeddings but does not alter the attention calculation logic. The only change in the model is the addition of new token embeddings, which are initialized and used during the first forward pass. The output layer (lm_head) changes in shape but not in functional logic. The model supports tasks involving both image and text inputs, such as answering questions about medical images. It can be loaded using the LlavaForConditionalGeneration class from the transformers library and an AutoProcessor for input processing. Example inference involves constructing a prompt with an image and a text question, tokenizing the input, and generating a response using the model's generate method. The model is compatible with multi-GPU setups and uses the bfloat16 data type for efficient computation. It also supports the flash_attention_2 implementation for attention calculations.

instruction: Identify the proteins mentioned in this clinical trial summary.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for gene and protein entity recognition in biomedical texts. It is optimized for high-precision identification of gene and protein mentions in clinical and research documents, supporting applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is trained on the BC2GM dataset, a foundational corpus for gene and protein name recognition in biomedical literature, which contains manually annotated gene and protein mentions from MEDLINE abstracts. It identifies and classifies entities using the `B-GENE/PROTEIN` and `I-GENE/PROTEIN` labels. The model is based on the ModernBERT-base architecture and is implemented using the Hugging Face Transformers framework. It achieves an F1 score of 0.81, with a precision of 0.80, recall of 0.81, and accuracy of 0.95. It is compatible with the Hugging Face ecosystem and supports various aggregation strategies for entity prediction, including `none`, `simple`, `first`, `average`, and `max`. The model is suitable for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in both clinical and research applications with production-ready reliability. If used in research or applications, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: Create an image of a snowy mountain peak with a hidden temple at the summit.
description: The Stable Diffusion v2-1-base is a diffusion-based text-to-image generation model designed to generate and modify images based on text prompts. It is a latent diffusion model that combines an autoencoder with a diffusion model trained in the latent space of the autoencoder. The model uses a fixed, pretrained text encoder for processing text prompts. It was developed by Robin Rombach and Patrick Esser and is primarily intended for research purposes. The model supports tasks such as the safe deployment of generative models, understanding their limitations and biases, artistic creation, and educational or creative tool development. The model is not intended for misuse, including the generation of harmful, offensive, or discriminatory content, or the creation of mis- and disinformation. The model has several limitations, including the inability to achieve perfect photorealism, difficulties in rendering legible text, challenges with compositional tasks, and potential issues with generating faces and people. It was trained mainly on English captions and may not perform well with non-English prompts. The model was trained on a subset of the LAION-5B dataset, which contains adult, violent, and sexual content, and was partially filtered using LAION's NSFW detector. The model's training procedure involves encoding images into latent representations using an autoencoder with a downsampling factor of 8, and encoding text prompts using the OpenCLIP-ViT/H text encoder. The text encoder's output is fed into the UNet backbone of the latent diffusion model via cross-attention, with a reconstruction objective and the v-objective used for training. The model was trained using 32 x 8 x A100 GPUs, the AdamW optimizer, a batch size of 2048, and a learning rate that warmed up to 0.0001 over 10,000 steps. Evaluation results show relative improvements of the checkpoints using different classifier-free guidance scales and 50 steps of DDIM sampling. The model's environmental impact includes an estimated CO2 emission of 15,000 kg eq. based on 200,000 hours of A100 GPU usage on AWS in the US-east region.

instruction: Make an image of a city skyline at night, with colorful lights reflecting on the water below.
description: Qwen-Image is an image generation foundation model in the Qwen series that achieves significant advances in complex text rendering and precise image editing. It demonstrates strong general capabilities in both image generation and editing, with exceptional performance in text rendering, especially for Chinese. The model supports a wide range of artistic styles, from photorealistic scenes to impressionist paintings, anime aesthetics, and minimalist design. It enables advanced image editing operations such as style transfer, object insertion or removal, detail enhancement, text editing within images, and human pose manipulation. Qwen-Image also supports image understanding tasks, including object detection, semantic segmentation, depth and edge (Canny) estimation, novel view synthesis, and super-resolution. These capabilities are powered by deep visual comprehension, allowing the model to adapt fluidly to creative prompts and provide intuitive input with coherent output. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0.

instruction: Can you process this image and return tokens that represent both the overall scene and individual elements?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. These models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones that provide multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers on the class token, or a linear layer applied on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation using linear layers, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) consists of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) consists of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, image retrieval, 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification. Evaluation results show that the ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on web data achieve high scores on IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair. The ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web data also achieve strong performance on similar tasks. The ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on satellite data achieve high scores on GEO-Bench classification and segmentation tasks. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters), ViT-S+ (29M parameters), ViT-B (86M parameters), ViT-L (300M parameters), ViT-H+ (840M parameters), and ViT-7B (6716M parameters), all with a patch size of 16 and varying embedding dimensions, register tokens, heads, and FFN types. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The models were trained using Nvidia H100 GPUs on private infrastructure in the USA, with 61,440 hours of compute time and an estimated carbon emission of 18t CO2eq. The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: Could you walk me through how you determine the most significant trend in this chart?
description: This model is a specialized version of Qwen3-VL-2B-Thinking, fine-tuned using LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) on the ChartQA dataset for advanced chart understanding, data extraction, and visual reasoning. It is designed to perform 'System 2' thinking, generating step-by-step reasoning traces before providing the final answer, which makes the decision process regarding chart interpretation transparent and verifiable. The model uses tags to articulate the visual analysis process, such as identifying axes and comparing bars, before concluding. It is optimized for bar charts, line graphs, and pie charts found in the ChartQA dataset. The model is fine-tuned with LoRA, involving approximately 2% trainable parameters, while retaining the base model's general capabilities. It is trained to return answers in a strict JSON format {\

instruction: Which response to a question about learning a new language is more motivating and detailed?
description: CompassJudger-2 is a series of generalist judge models designed to overcome the narrow specialization and limited robustness of existing LLM-as-judge solutions. The model is trained using a task-driven, multi-domain data curation and synthesis strategy to enhance robustness and domain adaptability. Judgment tasks are supervised with verifiable rewards, guiding the model's intrinsic reasoning through chain-of-thought (CoT) and rejection sampling, with a refined margin policy gradient loss further enhancing performance. CompassJudger-2 achieves state-of-the-art results across multiple judge and reward benchmarks, with the 7B model demonstrating competitive accuracy with significantly larger models. The model series includes CompassJudger-2-7B-Instruct and CompassJudger-2-32B-Instruct, both fine-tuned on the Qwen2.5-Instruct series. A new benchmark, JudgerBenchV2, with 10,000 questions across 10 scenarios, uses a Mixture-of-Judgers (MoJ) consensus for more reliable ground truth. CompassJudger-2 outperforms general models, reward models, and other specialized judge models across a wide range of benchmarks, including JudgerBench V2, JudgeBench, RMB, and RewardBench. The 7B model achieves 60.52 on JudgerBench V2, 63.06 on JudgeBench, 73.90 on RMB, and 90.96 on RewardBench, with an average of 72.11. The 32B model achieves 62.21 on JudgerBench V2, 65.48 on JudgeBench, 72.98 on RMB, and 92.62 on RewardBench, with an average of 73.32. The model is designed to evaluate AI assistant responses based on helpfulness, relevance, accuracy, depth, creativity, and level of detail, providing structured output indicating the preferred response. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License.

instruction: I need to rank these documents for a multilingual search. Can the system handle languages like French and Spanish?
description: Contextual AI Reranker v2 1B is a text reranking model designed to re-rank retrieved documents based on custom instructions, such as prioritizing recent information. It is the first instruction-following reranker capable of handling retrieval conflicts and ranking documents according to user-defined criteria. The model achieves state-of-the-art performance on the BEIR benchmark and is positioned on the cost/performance Pareto frontier across several dimensions, including instruction following, question answering, multilinguality (supporting 100+ languages), product search and recommendation, and real-world use cases. It is a 1 billion parameter model with a context length of up to 32,000 tokens. The model is intended for use when re-ranking retrieved documents with custom instructions, handling conflicting information in retrieval results, prioritizing documents by recency or other criteria, supporting multilingual search, and processing long contexts. The model is available for use with either the vLLM or Transformers frameworks, with specific implementation details provided for each. It is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution Non Commercial Share Alike 4.0 license. For questions or issues, users are directed to open an issue on the model repository. If used, the model should be cited with the provided BibTeX entry.

instruction: Could you help me turn this legal contract from English into Korean?
description: Seed-X is a series of open-source multilingual translation language models, including an instruction model, a reinforcement learning model, and a reward model. It is designed to push the boundaries of translation capabilities within a 7 billion parameter scale. Seed-X is intended as an accessible, off-the-shelf tool to support the community in advancing translation research and applications. It exhibits state-of-the-art translation capabilities, on par with or outperforming ultra-large models like Gemini-2.5, Claude-3.5, and GPT-4, as validated by human evaluations and automatic metrics. With a compact 7B parameter count and mistral architecture, Seed-X offers outstanding translation performance in a lightweight and efficient package, ideal for deployment and inference. It excels on a highly challenging translation test set spanning diverse domains, including the internet, science and technology, office dialogues, e-commerce, biomedicine, finance, law, literature, and entertainment. The Seed-X-PPO model is a causal language model that has undergone pretraining and post-training. It supports multilingual translation among 28 languages, including Arabic, Czech, Danish, German, English, Spanish, Finnish, French, Hungarian, Indonesian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Dutch, Norwegian Bokmal, Polish, Portuguese, Romanian, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian, Vietnamese, and Chinese. The model is specialized in multilingual translation and is not expected to support other tasks. The language tags at the end of the prompt are necessary for translation, as used in PPO training. The model does not have a chat template, so users should avoid prompting it in a multi-round conversation format. The model is evaluated on a diverse set of translation benchmarks, including FLORES-200, WMT-25, and a publicly released benchmark, accompanied by human evaluations. The model is licensed under OpenMDW. Users are recommended to use the Seed-X-PPO model, as its translation performance is superior to Seed-X-Instruct. Unofficial quantized versions for local deployment are not recommended, as the team plans to release an official quantized model and develop a demo on Hugging Face Space.

instruction: Could you extract visual features from this image for further analysis?
description: The vit_huge_plus_patch16_dinov3.lvd1689m is a DINOv3 Vision Transformer (ViT) model designed as an image feature encoder. It is distilled on the LVD-1689M dataset from the DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model is intended for tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. It has 840.5 million parameters, 224.9 GMACs, and 193.6 million activations, with an input image size of 256 x 256. The model uses a ViT architecture and is trained using the DINOv3 method. The original model weights have all QKV projection biases set to zero, and for the `timm` implementation, the QKV bias is disabled (`qkv_bias=False`) for these models. Some variants have `qkvb` in the name with `qkv_bias=True` but zero values to align with the `transformers` library and original models. The original models store RoPE periods as a persistent `bfloat16` buffer, while `timm` generates `float32` periods at initialization, which may result in some numerical differences but is less problematic on devices without bfloat16 support and appears to work as well or slightly better for fine-tuning. The model is evaluated on various benchmarks, including IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair, with performance metrics such as 90.3 IN-ReaL, 90.0 IN-R, 78.6 Obj.Net, 64.5 Ox.-H, 54.8 ADE20k, 0.352 NYU, 79.3 DAVIS, 63.3 NAVI, and 56.3 SPair. It is also evaluated on GEO-Bench for satellite imagery tasks, including classification and segmentation, with metrics such as 73.0 m-BEnet, 96.5 m-brick-kiln, 94.1 m-eurosat, 60.6 m-forestnet, 96.0 m-pv4ger, 57.4 m-so2sat, and 79.6 mean for classification, and 94.2 m-cashew, 75.6 m-chesapeake, 61.8 m-NeonTree, 83.7 m-nz-cattle, 95.2 m-pv4ger-seg, 36.8 m-SA-crop, and 74.5 mean for segmentation. The model is associated with the DINOv3 paper, the ViT paper, and the PyTorch Image Models repository.

instruction: If a player has a high yards per target but a low yards after catch, what might that indicate?
description: This model is a binary classifier developed as part of a class assignment on designing and deploying AI/ML systems. It uses AutoML (AutoGluon Tabular) to predict whether a football receiver is classified as 'Elite' (0/1) based on their statistical features. The model was trained on a dataset containing the following features: 'Tgt', 'Rec', 'Yds', 'YBC_per_R', 'YAC_per_R', 'ADOT', 'Drop_pct', and 'Rat'. The dataset was split using a stratified 80/20 train/test split on the original data. Preprocessing steps included dropping identifier columns (e.g., 'Player'), coercing data to numeric types, and removing rows with missing values. The model was trained using the 'best_quality' preset in AutoGluon Tabular with a time budget of 300 seconds and a random seed of 42. The evaluation metric used was F1 score for binary classification. Training was performed on a Colab CPU runtime with 2 vCPUs and approximately 12 GB of RAM. AutoGluon explored LightGBM, XGBoost, and ensembling methods, with auto-stacking and bagging enabled under the 'best_quality' preset. Hyperparameter tuning was handled automatically by AutoGluon. On the held-out test set, the model achieved an accuracy of 0.8333 and an F1 score of 0.8. The model has several limitations: correlations in the data do not imply causation, and the labels may reflect selection bias. Performance may decrease for out-of-distribution players or contexts. The model is intended for coursework purposes and should not be used for real personnel decisions. Generative AI tools were used to help structure code and documentation, but the model training and results are real.

instruction: Are these two Chinese sentences semantically similar?
description: The Multilingual-E5-base model is a multilingual text embedding model with 12 layers and an embedding size of 768. It is designed to encode queries and passages for tasks such as passage retrieval, semantic similarity, and bitext mining. The model supports 100 languages, including English, Chinese, and others, but performance may degrade for low-resource languages. It is initialized from a multilingual foundation and trained on a mixture of multilingual datasets. The first stage of training involves contrastive pre-training with weak supervision using a large number of text pairs from various sources, including titles, page content, news content, translation pairs, and more. The second stage involves supervised fine-tuning on labeled datasets, primarily in English and Chinese. The model is evaluated on benchmark tasks and achieves an average MRR@10 score of 65.9, outperforming baselines such as BM25 and mDPR. It is also evaluated on the MTEB benchmark. The model requires input texts to be prefixed with 'query: ' or 'passage: ' to maintain performance, as this is how it was trained. Cosine similarity scores typically range from 0.7 to 1.0 due to the use of a low temperature in the InfoNCE contrastive loss, but the relative order of scores is what matters for tasks like text retrieval. Long texts are truncated to a maximum of 512 tokens.

instruction: Please extract the English text from this image of a business card for me.
description: en_PP-OCRv5_mobile_rec is a text line recognition model developed by the PaddleOCR team as part of the PP-OCRv5_rec series. It is designed to efficiently and accurately recognize English text. The model achieves an accuracy of 85.3 percent, where a line is considered incorrect if any character, including punctuation, is wrong. This strict evaluation ensures higher accuracy in practical applications. The model can be used for text recognition tasks and is part of a larger OCR pipeline that includes modules for document image orientation classification, text image unwarping, text line orientation classification, text detection, and text recognition. The en_PP-OCRv5_mobile_rec model is intended to be used in conjunction with other models in the pipeline to address complex real-world OCR challenges. The model supports integration into projects through the PaddleOCR library, which requires installation of PaddlePaddle and PaddleOCR. The model can be run using a command-line interface or integrated into Python code for inference. The default model in the pipeline is PP-OCRv5_server_rec, so specifying en_PP-OCRv5_mobile_rec is necessary for use in the pipeline. The model can also be used with a local model file if provided.

instruction: I have a reference image of a person in a specific pose—can you apply it to a different model?
description: AnyPose LoRAs are designed to enable pose transfer between images without the need for control nets or openpose. The model allows users to copy the pose from a reference image onto another image by using a detailed prompt. It is optimized for fast inference and works with a single reference image as a pose guide. The model supports both simple and complex poses, including yoga poses, and is intended to be used with Qwen Image Edit 2511. It uses a 4-step lightning LoRA setup with both base and helper weights set to 0.7 for optimal performance. The model is trained primarily on 3D poses generated via Blender, which allows for precise control over character positioning and movement. However, it has limitations when applied to 2D or flat art styles such as cel-shaded or pixel art, where performance may be poor. It also struggles with highly technical poses and images containing more than one character. The model may sometimes replace the background of the reference image with the background of the target image, which can be corrected by explicitly prompting for the desired background. Users are advised to provide full-body images with visible environments to improve consistency. The model may hallucinate or incorrectly fill in missing areas, but these issues can often be addressed with additional prompting. A version 2 of the model is planned to address these limitations and improve support for non-humanoid characters and overall consistency.

instruction: Could you describe the visual patterns in this image using a set of numerical features?
description: This is a DINOv3 ConvNeXt image feature model, designed for image classification and as a feature backbone. It is pretrained on the LVD-1689M dataset using the self-supervised DINOv3 method and distilled from a DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model has 27.8 million parameters, 4.5 GMACs, and 13.4 million activations. It processes images of size 224 x 224. The model supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. For image classification, it can be used to generate top 5 probabilities and class indices. For feature map extraction, it outputs feature maps with varying shapes, such as [1, 96, 56, 56], [1, 192, 28, 28], [1, 384, 14, 14], and [1, 768, 7, 7]. For image embeddings, it can produce a tensor of shape (batch_size, num_features) by removing the classifier or using forward features and forward head methods. The model is based on the research described in the papers 'DINOv3', 'A ConvNet for the 2020s', and 'PyTorch Image Models'.

instruction: I need a real-time translation of this video script from English to Spanish — can you provide it quickly?
description: Hunyuan Translation Model Version 1.5 includes two models: HY-MT1.5-1.8B, a 1.8B parameter translation model, and HY-MT1.5-7B, a 7B parameter translation model. Both models support mutual translation across 33 languages and incorporate 5 ethnic and dialect variations. HY-MT1.5-7B is an upgraded version of the WMT25 championship model, optimized for explanatory translation and mixed-language scenarios, with added support for terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. Despite having less than one-third the parameters of HY-MT1.5-7B, HY-MT1.5-1.8B delivers comparable translation performance, achieving both high speed and high quality. After quantization, the 1.8B model can be deployed on edge devices and support real-time translation scenarios, making it widely applicable. HY-MT1.5-1.8B achieves industry-leading performance among models of the same size, surpassing most commercial translation APIs, and supports deployment on edge devices and real-time translation scenarios. HY-MT1.5-7B has been optimized for annotated and mixed-language scenarios compared to its September open-source version. Both models support terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. The models are available in multiple quantization formats, including FP8 and Int4. The models support translation between Chinese and other languages, as well as among the 33 supported languages. The models are designed to be used with the transformers library, and specific prompt templates are provided for different translation tasks, including terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. The models do not have a default system prompt, and specific inference parameters are recommended for optimal performance. The models were open-sourced on Hugging Face in December 2025 and September 2025.

instruction: Help me identify the best sources that discuss recent advancements in quantum computing.
description: The mxbai-edge-colbert-v0-32m is a lightweight ColBERT model with 32 million parameters and a projection dimension of 64. It is built on top of ModernBERT, benefiting from its architectural efficiencies. Despite its small size, it is the best-performing 'edge-sized' retriever, outperforming ColBERTv2 and many models with over ten times more parameters. The model can create multi-vector representations for documents of up to 32,000 tokens and is fully compatible with the PyLate library. It is designed for end-to-end retrieval tasks and supports encoding documents and queries, indexing, and retrieving top-k matches. It is also well-suited for use as a re-ranker following a first-stage retrieval with more lightweight models. Evaluation results on the BEIR benchmark show that it achieves an average score of 0.521, with strong performance on tasks such as MS MARCO (0.450), SciFact (0.740), and TREC-COVID (0.775). On the LongEmbed benchmark, it achieves an average score of 0.849 for documents up to 32,000 tokens. The model is available under the Apache 2.0 license. If used, it should be cited using the associated tech report, and if its projection heads are specifically discussed, an additional report on projection variants should also be cited. Additionally, if PyLate is used in conjunction with the model, it should be cited separately.

instruction: Can you create a depth map that preserves the original depth range without inversion?
description: This ONNX-based DepthPro model generates high-quality depth maps with minimal overhead. Depth values are encoded such that near points are bright and far points are dark, making the output directly usable for stereo and disparity-based applications without additional inversion or preprocessing. The model is optimized for efficient inference on standard hardware. It features a depth-only ONNX export, which significantly reduces model size while preserving full depth quality.
