It skips field-of-view calibration, outputting raw predicted depth values without post-processing, thereby avoiding normalization artifacts and computational overhead. The output is disparity-ready and compatible with stereo/disparity workflows without conversion. The model uses FP16 weights, optimized for GPU acceleration via DirectML for faster inference. It is configured for batch size 1, as benchmarks show single-image batches deliver optimal throughput, while larger batches are slower. The model uses Opset 21 for broader runtime optimization support and includes aggressive graph optimization to simplify the model graph, reduce computation, and speed up loading. It achieves fast inference with a minimal memory footprint and rapid depth map generation. The input shape is (1, 3, 1536, 1536) in NCHW format, with an input data type of float16 and an input range of [-1.0, 1.0] (normalized RGB). The output shape is (1, 1536, 1536), with an output data type of float16 and an output range of relative depth (higher values indicate closer objects). The model requires approximately 5.2 GB of VRAM, ONNX Runtime version 1.19.0 or higher, and Python version 3.8 or higher. Benchmarks on an AMD Radeon RX 7900 XTX using ONNX Runtime v1.23.0 with DirectML show a throughput of 75.7 images per minute and a model size of 1.2 GB. The model is designed without post-processing, which can cause severe artifacts such as crushed contrast and inconsistent results due to extreme outlier depth values and unpredictable normalization effects. The model is licensed under the Apple Machine Learning Research Model License, which restricts use to non-commercial scientific research and academic development. Redistribution is allowed only with the license included. Use of Apple's trademarks, logos, or name to promote derivative models is prohibited. Commercial use, product integration, or service deployment is not allowed.

instruction: What is the overall mood or atmosphere conveyed by this image?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. The models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones providing multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers on the class token, or a linear layer on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) consists of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) consists of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, image retrieval, 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on web data, the models achieve results such as 87.0 on IN-ReaL, 60.4 on IN-R, 50.9 on Obj.Net, 49.5 on Ox.-H, 47.0 on ADE20k, 0.403 on NYU, 72.7 on DAVIS, 56.3 on NAVI, and 50.4 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-S/16. For ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web data, the models achieve results such as 86.6 on IN-ReaL @256px, 87.7 on IN-ReaL @512px, 73.7 on Obj.Net @256px, 74.1 on Obj.Net @512px, 52.6 on ADE20k @256px, 58.7 on ADE20k @512px, 42.7 on NYU @256px, and 0.448 on NYU @512px for DINOv3 ConvNeXt Tiny. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on satellite data, the models achieve results such as 73.0 on m-BEnet, 96.5 on m-brick-kiln, 94.1 on m-eurosat, 60.6 on m-forestnet, 96.0 on m-pv4ger, 57.4 on m-so2sat, and 79.6 mean for DINOv3 ViT-L/16. The models were trained using Nvidia H100 GPUs on private infrastructure in the USA, with 61,440 hours used for ViT-7B model training, resulting in 18t CO2eq of carbon emissions. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters), ViT-S+ (29M parameters), ViT-B (86M parameters), ViT-L (300M parameters), ViT-H+ (840M parameters), and ViT-7B (6716M parameters), all with a patch size of 16 and varying embedding dimensions, register tokens, heads, and FFN types. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: Can you change the person in the video to a cartoon character while keeping the same movements?
description: Lucy Edit Dev is an open-weight video editing model that performs instruction-guided edits on videos using free-text prompts. It supports a variety of editing tasks, including clothing and accessory changes, character changes, object insertions, and scene replacements, while preserving the motion and composition of the original video. The model is built on the Wan2.2 5B architecture, which includes a high-compression VAE and DiT stack, allowing for easy adaptation of existing scripts and workflows. It is designed to preserve motion and composition perfectly, enabling precise edits. Compared to common inference-time methods, Lucy Edit Dev offers more reliable editing results. It supports changes to wardrobe and accessories, such as adding glasses, earrings, or hats. It can also perform character changes, such as replacing a person with a monster, animal, or known character. Additionally, it supports scene transformations, such as changing a real-world scene into a 2D cartoon. The model operates using pure text instructions, requiring no finetuning or masks for common edits. The model has approximately 5 billion parameters and is built on top of the Wan2.2 5B architecture. Lucy Edit Dev is the first open-source instruction-guided video editing model. It is designed to be used with descriptive text prompts, with best results achieved using prompts of around 20–30 words. Trigger words such as 'change,' 'add,' 'replace,' and 'transform to' help the model understand the user's intent. The model excels at clothing changes, human or character replacement, and object replacement, with strong results for structure-preserving swaps. Color changes have mixed reliability and work best with precise descriptions. Adding objects is most effective for wearable or handheld props. Global transformations can alter the environment or style but may also affect the subject's identity. Lucy Edit Dev is particularly strong in identity conservation, edit precision, realism, and adherence to prompts. Longer, detailed prompts improve results, and 81-frame generations provide better temporal consistency than shorter clips. The model is available for use with the diffusers library and is integrated with the LucyEditPipeline. It is also available via a hosted API. Lucy Edit Dev is built on the Wan2.2 5B architecture and acknowledges the contributions of the open-source community, including diffusers and Hugging Face. The model was first released on 2025-09-18, with a diffusers integration merged on 2025-09-16. Future plans include local inference ComfyUI nodes, LoRA and fine-tuning scripts, and additional technical documentation.

instruction: What are the identified chemical substances in this part of the clinical documentation?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition in biomedical literature. It identifies and extracts chemical compounds and substances from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the BC4CHEMD dataset, a manually annotated corpus for chemical entity recognition from the BioCreative IV challenge. The BC4CHEMD dataset contains PubMed abstracts with chemical entities annotated according to ChEBI guidelines and is used as a benchmark for evaluating named entity recognition models. The model can identify and classify the following entity types: B-CHEM and I-CHEM. It is based on the xlm-roberta-large architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.95, precision of 0.94, recall of 0.95, and accuracy of 0.99. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0. If used in research or applications, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: Please translate this English research summary into Japanese for an academic audience.
description: Tri-1.8B Translation is a lightweight multilingual translation model developed by Trillion Labs. It is trained through pretraining and supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and was distilled from the larger Tri-21B model to maintain strong translation quality while being a much smaller, deployment-friendly model with approximately 1.8B parameters. The model supports all translation directions among English, Korean, Japanese, and Chinese, enabling fully bidirectional translation between these languages. It is designed to be compact and efficient, suitable for serving on a single GPU. The model works with a simple task instruction and language tag format. It is research-ready and can be used for domain-specific fine-tuning or with lightweight adapters. The model is licensed under Apache-2.0 for model weights and code, with a note to verify data licenses for specific use cases. The development of the model was influenced by the Seed-X project from the ByteDance Seed team, particularly in terms of prompt template and training design.

instruction: Please tilt the camera slightly up to show the sky above the building.
description: This model is trained on ModelScope without the use of code. It is designed to support camera control functions, including moving the camera up, down, left, and right, rotating it left and right, looking down or up, and switching to a wide-angle or close-up shot. The model does not require trigger words for these operations. It is based on the Qwen-Edit-2509 model, and a LoRa version is available for use. The model has been updated to address an instability and consistency issue by increasing the number of training iterations. Users are encouraged to try various camera control commands, as there are many possibilities. The model's performance is described as very amazing, and it is considered more powerful and intelligent compared to the original model, which already had certain capabilities. Users are invited to join a Discord group for communication and cooperation. If the resources are helpful or used for business purposes, users are encouraged to support the content creator through a PayPal donation.

instruction: What's the process for turning a single image into a 3D scene that I can explore interactively?
description: This is a float16 conversion of the SHARP model for use with Swift/MLX implementations. SHARP is an approach to photorealistic view synthesis from a single image. Given a single photograph, SHARP regresses the parameters of a 3D Gaussian representation of the depicted scene. This is done in less than a second on a standard GPU via a single feedforward pass through a neural network. The 3D Gaussian representation produced by SHARP can then be rendered in real time, yielding high-resolution photorealistic images for nearby views. The representation is metric, with absolute scale, supporting metric camera movements. Experimental results demonstrate that SHARP delivers robust zero-shot generalization across datasets. It sets a new state of the art on multiple datasets, reducing LPIPS by 25–34% and DISTS by 21–43% versus the best prior model, while lowering the synthesis time by three orders of magnitude. The results are 3D Gaussian splats (3DGS) in the output folder. The 3DGS .ply files are compatible with various public 3DGS renderers. The model follows the OpenCV coordinate convention (x right, y down, z forward). The 3DGS scene center is roughly at (0, 0, +z). When dealing with third-party renderers, please scale and rotate to re-center the scene accordingly. Additionally, you can render videos with a camera trajectory. While the Gaussian prediction works for all CPU, CUDA, and MPS, rendering videos via the --render option currently requires a CUDA GPU. The gsplat renderer takes a while to initialize at the first launch.

instruction: Can you help me detect and segment different regions in this image based on visual similarity?

instruction: What is the primary action taking place in the scene?
description: V-JEPA 2 is a frontier video understanding model developed by FAIR at Meta. It extends the pretraining objectives of previous models, resulting in state-of-the-art video understanding capabilities by leveraging large-scale data and model sizes. The model includes a video classification head and is pretrained on the Something-Something-V2 dataset. V-JEPA 2 is designed to support video classification tasks and is intended for use with the latest version of the transformers library. The model can be loaded using the AutoModelForVideoClassification and AutoVideoProcessor classes from the transformers library. During inference, video frames are sampled according to the model's configuration, preprocessed, and passed through the model to obtain classification logits. The model outputs probabilities for the top predicted classes, enabling interpretation of the video content. V-JEPA 2 is part of ongoing research in self-supervised video modeling and is intended to enable understanding, prediction, and planning in video data.

instruction: Can I track several objects in a video simultaneously?
description: EdgeTAM is an on-device executable variant of the SAM 2 model designed for promptable segmentation and tracking in videos. It runs 22× faster than SAM 2 and achieves 16 FPS on iPhone 15 Pro Max without quantization. EdgeTAM supports automatic mask generation to segment all objects in an image. It can be used for basic image segmentation through single point clicks, multiple points for refinement, and bounding box inputs. The model allows for segmentation of multiple objects simultaneously. It supports batch inference for multiple images and multiple objects per image, including complex batch scenarios with multiple points per object. EdgeTAM can use masks from previous predictions as input to refine segmentation. A key strength of EdgeTAM is its ability to track objects across video frames. It supports basic video tracking, multi-object video tracking, and refining video segmentation through additional clicks on any frame. For real-time applications, EdgeTAM supports streaming video inference, processing video frames as they arrive. It also supports video batch processing for multiple objects, allowing multiple objects to be tracked simultaneously in video by adding them all at once.

instruction: How would you define artificial intelligence and provide examples of its current applications?
description: Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 is an updated version of the Qwen3-4B non-thinking mode, featuring significant improvements in general capabilities such as instruction following, logical reasoning, text comprehension, mathematics, science, coding, and tool usage. It demonstrates substantial gains in long-tail knowledge coverage across multiple languages and is better aligned with user preferences in subjective and open-ended tasks, enabling more helpful responses and higher-quality text generation. The model supports 256K long-context understanding and has 4.0B parameters, with 3.6B non-embedding parameters, 36 layers, and 32 attention heads for Q and 8 for KV. It has a context length of 262,144 tokens natively. This model is designed for non-thinking mode only and does not generate code blocks in its output. It is reported to outperform previous versions and other models on a range of benchmarks, including MMLU-Pro, MMLU-Redux, GPQA, SuperGPQA, AIME25, HMMT25, ZebraLogic, LiveBench, LiveCodeBench, MultiPL-E, Aider-Polyglot, IFEval, Arena-Hard v2, Creative Writing v3, WritingBench, BFCL-v3, TAU1-Retail, TAU1-Airline, TAU2-Retail, TAU2-Airline, TAU2-Telecom, MultiIF, MMLU-ProX, INCLUDE, and PolyMATH. The model is compatible with Hugging Face transformers and can be used with vLLM or SGLang for creating an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint. For optimal performance, it is recommended to use sampling parameters such as Temperature=0.7, TopP=0.8, TopK=20, and MinP=0, and to set an output length of 16,384 tokens. The model supports agentic use through Qwen-Agent, which encapsulates tool-calling templates and parsers to reduce coding complexity. Users can define available tools using an MCP configuration file or integrate other tools manually. The model is supported by various local applications, including Ollama, LMStudio, MLX-LM, llama.cpp, and KTransformers. If out-of-memory issues occur, reducing the context length to 32,768 is advised. For benchmarking, it is recommended to standardize output formats using specific prompts for math problems and multiple-choice questions.

instruction: Can you calculate the resin amount for this design using its volume and surface area?
description: MiniRes is a small Python library and pretrained ensemble model designed to predict resin usage in grams for pre-supported 3D printed miniatures. It accepts tabular features exported from slicing and analysis tools such as UVtools, PrusaSlicer, and trimesh, and returns a single float per part representing the estimated resin usage. The model combines a Keras neural network with an XGBoost regressor. Model weights are hosted on Hugging Face and automatically downloaded and cached. The training data was generated from STL files provided by multiple miniature artists, which were sliced using PrusaSlicer with a consistent profile and a resin density of 1.1/g. UVtools was used to extract resin usage statistics, including the label of grams used, while trimesh extracted mesh-level features such as volume, surface area, implied mass, Euler characteristic, and bounding box sizes. Feature engineering added interaction and ratio features such as volume × mass, surface/volume, surface/mass, bounding-box/volume, and similar. MiniRes is trained on this tabular data to predict resin usage based on these engineered features. The model expects a fixed set of feature columns used during training, which can be inspected at runtime. Required features include kb, volume, surface_area, bbox_area, euler_number, scale, and surface_volume_ratio. The model raises an error if any required columns are missing. MiniRes includes two command-line scripts: stl-scanner.py, which scans STL files and exports geometric features to a CSV using trimesh, and mini-pricing.py, which calculates the total cost of each file based on resin usage and user-defined cost parameters. The model is distributed under the MIT License, which permits use, modification, and distribution of the software without restriction, provided the copyright and permission notices are included. The software is provided 'as is', without warranty of any kind, and the authors or copyright holders are not liable for any damages arising from its use.

instruction: I want to add depth perception to this image for a more immersive experience. Can you assist?
description: DA3MONO-LARGE is a monocular depth estimation model designed for high-quality relative depth prediction. It directly predicts depth, which results in superior geometric accuracy compared to disparity-based models like Depth Anything 2. The model is part of the Depth Anything 3 series and is based on a plain transformer architecture with a unified depth-ray representation. It supports tasks such as relative depth estimation and sky segmentation. The model has 0.35B parameters and is released under the Apache 2.0 license. It was developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is trained exclusively on public academic datasets. The model outperforms Depth Anything 2 in monocular depth estimation and VGGT in multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it may have limitations when applied to domain-specific images, and its performance can vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity.

instruction: I want to highlight all the birds flying in the sky from a nature video. How can I do that?
description: SeC is a concept-driven segmentation framework for video object segmentation that integrates Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) to provide robust, object-centric representations. The framework dynamically balances semantic reasoning with feature matching, adaptively adjusting computational efforts based on scene complexity to achieve optimal segmentation performance. SeC is designed to support video object segmentation tasks and is evaluated on multiple benchmarks, including SA-V, LVOS v2, MOSE, DAVIS 2017, YTVOS 2019, and the newly proposed SeCVOS benchmark, which is specifically designed to evaluate segmentation in complex and challenging scenarios. On these benchmarks, SeC achieves a SA-V validation score of 82.7, a SA-V test score of 81.7, an LVOS v2 validation score of 86.5, a MOSE validation score of 75.3, a DAVIS 2017 validation score of 91.3, a YTVOS 2019 validation score of 88.6, and a SeCVOS score of 70.0. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of SeC in handling complex video object segmentation tasks. The model is intended for use in scenarios where accurate and adaptive segmentation is required, particularly in complex visual environments.

instruction: Can you determine the jurisdiction implied in this Greek legal text?
description: GEM-RoBERTa HQ Legal is a RoBERTa-base model pre-trained from scratch on a strategically curated 21GB corpus of Greek legal, parliamentary, and governmental text. The model employs a quality-based data repetition strategy, where higher-quality legal sources are repeated multiple times during training to enhance the model's understanding of premium legal terminology and concepts. This model was trained as part of a research project and has been optimized for downstream tasks such as Named Entity Recognition (NER), Text Classification, and Question Answering within the legal field. The RoBERTa architecture provides enhanced performance through dynamic masking and removal of the Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) task, focusing entirely on Masked Language Modeling (MLM). The model was pre-trained on a comprehensive corpus of Greek text compiled from various legal and governmental sources, with a quality-based data repetition strategy that increases exposure to higher-quality legal content. The original 16.75GB corpus was expanded to 21.12GB through strategic repetition. The quality-based repetition strategy enhances the model's exposure to premium legal terminology from the Raptarchis Legal Dictionary (4x repetition), high-quality judicial reasoning from Supreme Court reports (3x repetition), and EU legal concepts from Eur-Lex content (2x repetition). This approach ensures the model develops a stronger understanding of sophisticated legal language while maintaining exposure to the broader legal corpus. The model uses the RoBERTa-base architecture with a hidden size of 768, 12 attention heads, 12 hidden layers, and approximately 125 million parameters. The model was trained using a custom ByteLevelBPE tokenizer with a vocabulary of 50,264 tokens optimized for Greek legal terminology. The data was processed into fixed-size chunks of 512 tokens, respecting document boundaries to ensure contextual coherence. Higher-quality sources were strategically repeated during the data preparation phase. The model was pre-trained from scratch for 150,000 steps on 8x NVIDIA A100 40GB GPUs, using BFloat16 (bf16) mixed-precision for stability and speed. The training took approximately 81 hours and 39 minutes to complete. The key hyperparameters used were a learning rate of 1.5e-4 (0.00015) with a linear warmup of 9,000 steps, an effective batch size of 2,688 (per_device_train_batch_size: 48, gradient_accumulation_steps: 7), the AdamW optimizer with standard parameters, a weight decay of 0.01, a max sequence length of 512, a max of 150,000 steps, 9,000 warmup steps, an MLM probability of 0.15, and a max gradient norm of 1.0. The model achieved a final training loss of 0.617 and a final evaluation loss of 0.573035. The training infrastructure consisted of 8x NVIDIA A100 40GB GPUs, with a total of 150,000 training steps and a total training time of 66 hours 39 minutes. The train/validation split was 95%/5%, and the effective training data was 21.12GB, incorporating the quality-based repetition strategy.

instruction: Can you generate a voiceover that sounds like a podcast host reading this script?
description: VibeVoice is a novel open-source text-to-speech (TTS) model designed to generate expressive, long-form, multi-speaker conversational audio, such as podcasts, from text. It addresses key limitations in traditional TTS systems, including scalability, speaker consistency, and natural turn-taking. A core innovation is the use of continuous speech tokenizers—Acoustic and Semantic—operating at an ultra-low frame rate of 7.5 Hz. These tokenizers preserve audio fidelity while improving computational efficiency for long sequences. VibeVoice employs a next-token diffusion framework, combining a Large Language Model (LLM) to understand textual context and dialogue flow with a diffusion head to generate high-fidelity acoustic details. The model can synthesize speech up to 90 minutes long with up to 4 distinct speakers, surpassing the typical 1-2 speaker limits of many prior models. The LLM is integrated with specialized acoustic and semantic tokenizers and a diffusion-based decoding head. The Acoustic Tokenizer is based on a σ-VAE variant with a mirror-symmetric encoder-decoder structure featuring 7 stages of modified Transformer blocks, achieving 3200x downsampling from 24kHz input. Encoder and decoder components are approximately 340M parameters each. The Semantic Tokenizer mirrors the Acoustic Tokenizer's architecture (without VAE components) and is trained with an ASR proxy task. The Diffusion Head is a lightweight module with 4 layers and ~123M parameters, conditioned on LLM hidden states, and uses a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM) process with Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) and DPM-Solver during inference. The model is trained with a curriculum increasing up to 65,536 tokens. Training involves separate pre-training of the Acoustic and Semantic tokenizers, followed by training of the LLM and diffusion head with a curriculum learning strategy for input sequence length (4k -> 16K -> 32K -> 64K). The LLM used is Qwen2.5, which typically uses its own text tokenizer. Audio is tokenized via the acoustic and semantic tokenizers. The VibeVoice-1.5B model has a context length of 64K and can generate up to ~90 minutes of audio. The model is intended for research purposes only and is not recommended for commercial or real-world applications without further testing and development. It is limited to English and Chinese data, and outputs in other languages may be unintelligible or offensive. The model does not handle background noise, music, or other sound effects and does not generate overlapping speech segments in conversations. It may inherit biases, errors, or omissions from its base model (Qwen2.5 1.5b). High-quality synthetic speech can be misused for impersonation, fraud, or disinformation. Users are expected to use the model lawfully and to disclose the use of AI when sharing AI-generated content. An audible disclaimer is embedded in every synthesized audio file, and an imperceptible watermark is added for provenance verification. Inference requests are logged (hashed) for abuse pattern detection and to publish aggregated statistics quarterly. Users are responsible for sourcing datasets legally and ethically, including securing appropriate rights and anonymizing data prior to use. The model is developed by members of Microsoft Research, and feedback or reports of issues can be sent to VibeVoice@microsoft.com.

instruction: How can I generate a more stylized version of this photo?
description: This repository contains Nunchaku-quantized versions of a high-performance image generation model. The model is optimized for efficient inference while maintaining minimal loss in performance. It is an image-to-image model developed by the Nunchaku Team and is licensed under the Apache-2.0 license. The model is quantized from an unspecified base model. The data type used is INT4 for non-Blackwell GPUs (pre-50-series) and NVFP4 for Blackwell GPUs (50-series). The model supports different ranks for inference: r32 for faster inference, r128 for better quality but slower inference, and r256 for the highest quality (slowest inference). Standard inference speed models are available for general use. The model is based on an unspecified inference engine and quantization library. A paper titled 'SVDQuant: Absorbing Outliers by Low-Rank Components for 4-Bit Diffusion Models' by Li, Muyang, Lin, Yujun, Zhang, Zhekai, Cai, Tianle, Li, Xiuyu, Guo, Junxian, Xie, Enze, Meng, Chenlin, Zhu, Jun-Yan, and Han, Song, presented at The Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations in 2025, is associated with the model.

instruction: What question would make sense to ask about this visual content?
description: BLIP is a Vision-Language Pre-training (VLP) framework designed to transfer flexibly to both vision-language understanding and generation tasks. It is trained on visual question answering using a large architecture with a ViT large backbone. BLIP addresses the limitations of existing pre-trained models, which typically excel in either understanding-based or generation-based tasks but not both. The model improves performance by effectively utilizing noisy web data through a process of bootstrapping captions, where a captioner generates synthetic captions and a filter removes the noisy ones. BLIP achieves state-of-the-art results on a wide range of vision-language tasks, including image-text retrieval with a 2.7% increase in average recall@1, image captioning with a 2.8% increase in CIDEr score, and visual question answering (VQA) with a 1.6% increase in VQA score. It also demonstrates strong generalization ability when directly transferred to video-language tasks in a zero-shot manner. The model can be used for conditional and un-conditional image captioning. Ethical considerations include the fact that this release is for research purposes only in support of an academic paper. The models, datasets, and code are not specifically designed or evaluated for all downstream purposes. Users are strongly recommended to evaluate and address potential concerns related to accuracy, safety, and fairness before deployment. Users should consider the common limitations of AI, comply with applicable laws, and leverage best practices when selecting use cases, particularly for high-risk scenarios where errors or misuse could significantly impact people’s lives, rights, or safety.

instruction: Can you detect if a person is raising their arms in a surveillance footage clip?
description: The Person Pose Detection 480x640 model, developed by Synaptics, is a lightweight quantized tflite model designed for the SR100 processor in the Synaptics Astra™ SR MCU Series. It is intended to generate bounding boxes and identify 17 keypoints for each person, corresponding to various body parts. Each keypoint is accompanied by a confidence score that indicates the reliability of the detection, enabling accurate estimation of the person's pose. The model is designed to fit in Flash and is also intended to fit in SRAM. It is a pose detection model with an input size of 480x640 and outputs, for each detected person, bounding box coordinates, 17 keypoint locations, and confidence scores. The model is designed for a single class (person) and is specifically intended for pose estimation of people. It is optimized for deployment on the Astra™ SR100 MCU, with INT8 quantization and preprocessing that requires input images to be resized and quantized to match model requirements. The model is intended for real-time person pose estimation on embedded, resource-constrained edge devices. Typical use cases include human activity recognition and gesture control, fitness and wellness tracking, smart home automation based on body posture, and interactive applications and user interfaces. The model can be evaluated and tested in a hosted environment optimized for the Synaptics SR110 MCU, which provides a seamless sandbox for model evaluation using hardware-specific quantization and runtime settings. The model is distributed under a license that allows flexible use, modification, and distribution.

instruction: Please find the correlation between customer satisfaction scores and purchase frequency from the data provided.
description: JT-DA-8B is a specialized large language model designed for complex table reasoning tasks across diverse real-world scenarios. It is built upon the open-sourced JT-Coder-8B model, an 8B-parameter decoder-only foundation model trained from scratch. The model architecture follows a mainstream Transformer decoder design, including RMS layer normalization, RoPE, and GQA mechanism. The model is trained on a comprehensive and diverse training corpus, which includes 29 public table QA datasets, over 300 domain-specific non-QA tables, and 1 million rule-based table reasoning QA pairs. A structured pipeline is used to generate realistic multi-step analytical tasks involving reasoning patterns like correlation, anomaly detection, and hypothesis testing. The training data is extensively cleaned and processed, including short answer filtering, repetitive thinking filtering, low information density filtering, and LLM-based scoring to ensure high-quality, table-centric data. The training data composition includes six core capabilities with 34 subtasks, covering natural language understanding, table understanding, table basic operation, table computational operation, data analysis, and advanced data analysis. The model is trained using a distributed parallel strategy on 96 NPUs, with a high-bandwidth, low-latency communication network and shared high-performance storage. Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) is conducted to enhance the model's capability in structured table reasoning, using a teacher-forcing approach to minimize the cross-entropy loss between predicted and ground-truth tokens. A four-stage table reasoning workflow is proposed, including table preprocessing, table sensing, tool-integrated reasoning, and prompt engineering, to improve model interpretability and execution accuracy. The model is evaluated on the TReB benchmark, which covers six core capabilities and 26 subtasks, using three inference modes: Textual Chain-of-Thought (TCoT), Program-of-Thought (PoT), and Interleaved Chain-of-Thought (ICoT). The evaluation results demonstrate that the model excels among models of the same size, showcasing outstanding performance in table understanding, querying, computation, and data analysis tasks. The model is capable of performing multi-step reasoning, handling inconsistent and noisy data types, and generating executable code for data analysis. It supports tasks such as table summarization, descriptive analysis, statistical metric calculation, variable relationship modeling, and result interpretation. The model also supports data visualization, including the generation of bar charts to illustrate data patterns. The model is distributed under a license and includes a disclaimer that it may still produce content that is inaccurate, biased, or inappropriate, and users are advised to exercise discretion when interpreting the model’s outputs and assume full responsibility for any outcomes arising from its use.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: What is the difference in performance between Q4_0 and Q4_K_M on an AVX2 system?
description: This model is a quantized version of the Huihui-gemma-3n-E4B-it-abliterated model, processed using the imatrix quantization method with a dataset provided by an unspecified source. The quantizations were performed using the llama.cpp release b5856. The model is intended to be run using llama.cpp or any other llama.cpp-based project. The prompt format uses a default template since no chat template was specified, which may not be accurate and should be verified against the original model card. The model is available in various quantization types, each with different file sizes and quality levels. Some quantizations, such as Q3_K_XL and Q4_K_L, use Q8_0 for the embedding and output weights instead of the default quantization. The model supports online repacking for ARM and AVX CPU inference, which can improve performance on specific hardware. The Q4_0 quantization format is compatible with online repacking, and newer IQ4_NL and IQ4_XS formats offer similar benefits for ARM CPUs. The model can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool, with instructions provided for both single-file and multi-file downloads. Users are advised to consider their hardware limitations, such as RAM and VRAM availability, when selecting a quantization type. For maximum quality, users should aim for a quant with a file size 1-2GB smaller than the total available memory. K-quant formats, such as Q5_K_M, are recommended for general use, while I-quant formats, such as IQ3_M, may offer better performance for specific hardware like Nvidia or AMD GPUs. The model includes a variety of quantization options, ranging from high-quality Q6_K_L and Q5_K_L to lower-quality but more memory-efficient options like Q3_K_S and Q2_K. Performance benchmarks on an AVX2 system show that Q4_K_M and Q4_0_8_8 formats offer improvements in prompt processing and text generation compared to the standard Q4_0 format. The model was developed with assistance from kalomaze and Dampf for the imatrix calibration dataset, and inspired by ZeroWw's work on embedding and output weights. Support for the model's development was provided by LM Studio.

instruction: How would you translate this English sentence into Chinese for a more formal setting?
description: Tri-1.8B Translation is a lightweight multilingual translation model developed by Trillion Labs. It is trained through pretraining and supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and was distilled from the larger Tri-21B model to maintain strong translation quality while being a much smaller, deployment-friendly model with approximately 1.8B parameters. The model supports all translation directions among English, Korean, Japanese, and Chinese, enabling fully bidirectional translation between these languages. It is designed to be compact and efficient, suitable for serving on a single GPU. The model works with a simple task instruction and language tag format. It is research-ready and can be used for domain-specific fine-tuning or with lightweight adapters. The model is licensed under Apache-2.0 for model weights and code, with a note to verify data licenses for specific use cases. The development of the model was influenced by the Seed-X project from the ByteDance Seed team, particularly in terms of prompt template and training design.

instruction: I need assistance in summarizing a research paper on the impact of climate change on coastal ecosystems.
description: gemma-3n-E4B-it is a 6-bit quantized version of a model optimized for Apple Silicon using MLX. It is part of the Community Model Program, which highlights new and noteworthy models created by the community. The model is provided by a third party and is not created, owned, or endorsed by LM Studio. LM Studio does not guarantee the accuracy, reliability, or safety of the model's outputs, and disclaims all warranties related to the model's performance, security, or suitability for any purpose. Users are solely responsible for any damage or issues arising from the use of the model.

instruction: Can you convert this article into an audio file for me to listen to while commuting?
description: Pocket TTS is a lightweight text-to-speech (TTS) application designed to run efficiently on CPUs. It eliminates the need for GPUs or web APIs by allowing audio generation through a simple pip install and function call. The model supports Python versions 3.10 through 3.14 and requires PyTorch 2.5 or higher, but does not require the GPU version of PyTorch. It is a CPU-based model with a small size of 100 million parameters. It supports audio streaming with low latency, approximately 200 milliseconds to produce the first audio chunk, and operates at about six times real-time speed on the CPU of a MacBook Air M4. It uses only two CPU cores and provides both a Python API and a command-line interface (CLI). The model supports voice cloning and can handle infinitely long text inputs. It is currently limited to English language support. Users can try the model through a web interface without installation, or use it via the CLI or as a Python library. The model can be run as a local server to generate audio via HTTP requests, keeping the model in memory between requests for faster performance. The model is not supported for GPU execution, as no speed improvements were observed compared to CPU execution due to the use of a batch size of 1 and a small model size. Contributions are welcome, and development instructions are available in the relevant documentation. The model must be used in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations, and its use must not involve illegal, harmful, deceptive, fraudulent, or unauthorized activities. Prohibited uses include voice impersonation or cloning without explicit and lawful consent, misinformation or disinformation, and the generation of unlawful, harmful, or privacy-invasive content.

instruction: I'm working on a project that involves retrieving and ranking legal documents. What techniques can I use to improve the ranking accuracy?
description: ZeroEntropy has developed zerank-1, a state-of-the-art reranker designed to improve the accuracy of retrieval systems in search engines. This model outperforms closed-source competitors such as cohere-rerank-v3.5 and Salesforce/LlamaRank-v1 across various domains, including finance, legal, code, STEM, medical, and conversational data. The model is part of an innovative multi-stage pipeline that models query-document relevance scores as adjusted values. The model is currently released under a non-commercial license, and commercial license inquiries should be directed to founders@zeroentropy.dev. A smaller version of the model is fully open-sourced under an Apache 2.0 License. Evaluations using NDCG@10 scores show that zerank-1 achieves strong performance across multiple tasks, including Code, Conversational, Finance, Legal, Medical, and STEM. For example, in the Finance domain, zerank-1 achieves an NDCG@10 score of 0.894, outperforming other models. In the STEM domain, it achieves a score of 0.694. The model is implemented as a CrossEncoder and can be used with the sentence_transformers library. It can also be accessed via ZeroEntropy's inference endpoint.

instruction: Can you help me convert this voice memo into written text?
description: LFM2-Audio-1.5B is the first end-to-end audio foundation model from Liquid AI. Designed with low latency and real-time conversation in mind, it enables seamless conversational interaction at 1.5 billion parameters, achieving capabilities on par with much larger models. LFM2-Audio is an end-to-end multimodal speech and text language model that does not require separate ASR and TTS components. The model consists of a pretrained LFM2 model as its multimodal backbone, along with a FastConformer-based audio encoder to handle continuous audio inputs, and a RQ-transformer generating discrete Mimi tokens as audio output. LFM2-Audio supports two distinct generation routines: interleaved generation, which enables real-time speech-to-speech conversational chatbot capabilities where audio generation latency is key, and sequential generation, which is suited for non-conversational tasks such as ASR or TTS, and allows the model to switch generated modality on the fly. The model has 1.2 billion parameters in the language model component, an audio encoder with 115 million parameters, a hybrid convolutional and attention-based backbone, an audio tokenizer using 8 codebooks, a context length of 32,768 tokens, a vocabulary size of 65,536 for text and 2049*8 for audio, and uses bfloat16 precision. It is licensed under the LFM Open License v1.0 and supports English. The model achieves a VoiceBench score of 56.78, with AlpacaEval 3.71, CommonEval 3.49, WildVoice 3.17, SD-QA 30.56, MMSU 31.95, OBQA 44.40, BBH 30.54, IFEval 98.85, and ADVBench 67.33. For ASR, it has a word error rate (WER) of 7.24, with Open 15.58, AMI 10.67, GigaSpeech 2.01, LibriSpeech-clean 4.39, LibriSpeech-other 3.56, and TED-LIUM 3.56. The model is suitable for multi-turn, multi-modal chat, where it can switch between audio and text modalities. It is available via the `liquid-audio` package and can be run using a Gradio demo interface. The model is intended for use with the LFM Open License v1.0, and the audio encoder is based on code licensed under other terms. The model is described in the LFM2 Technical Report, arXiv:2511.23404.

instruction: I want to track the movement of a person's torso over a series of images—can you help?
description: The Person Pose Detection 256x480 model is a lightweight quantized tflite model developed by Synaptics for the SR100 processor in the Synaptics Astra™ SR MCU Series. It is designed to generate bounding boxes and identify 17 keypoints for each person, corresponding to various body parts. Each keypoint is accompanied by a confidence score that indicates the reliability of the detection, enabling accurate estimation of the person's pose. The model is optimized to fit in Flash and is also designed to fit in SRAM. It accepts an input size of 256x480 and outputs, for each detected person, bounding box coordinates, 17 keypoint locations, and confidence scores. The model is a single-class model, specifically designed for pose estimation of people. It is fully quantized in INT8 and is intended for real-time person pose estimation on embedded, resource-constrained edge devices. Typical use cases include human activity recognition and gesture control, fitness and wellness tracking, smart home automation based on body posture, and interactive applications and user interfaces. The model can be optimized for the Astra™ SR100 MCU using the Astra™ Machina Micro Dev Kit. Input images must be resized and quantized to match model requirements. The model is distributed under a license that allows flexible use, modification, and distribution. Evaluation and testing can be performed in a hosted environment optimized for the Synaptics SR110 MCU, which provides a seamless sandbox for model evaluation using hardware-specific quantization and runtime settings.

instruction: What is the primary action being performed in this video, and how would you categorize it?
description: Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Instruct is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced editions for flexible deployment. Key enhancements include a Visual Agent that can operate PC/mobile GUIs, recognize elements, understand functions, and invoke tools to complete tasks. It supports visual coding by generating Draw.io/HTML/CSS/JS from images and videos. The model features advanced spatial perception, capable of judging object positions, viewpoints, and occlusions, with support for 2D and 3D grounding for spatial reasoning and embodied AI. It has a long context and video understanding capability, with a native 256K context expandable to 1M, enabling it to handle books and hours-long video with full recall and second-level indexing. The model excels in enhanced multimodal reasoning, particularly in STEM and Math, with causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. It has upgraded visual recognition with broader, higher-quality pretraining, enabling it to recognize a wide range of subjects including celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. The model also features expanded OCR, supporting 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare/ancient characters and jargon, as well as long-document structure parsing. The model's text understanding is on par with pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE for full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings, enhancing long-horizon video reasoning. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. The model is designed for deployment using the vLLM framework with a recommended context length of 32768. It is available for download and can be run with a vLLM startup command that specifies GPU memory utilization, tensor parallel size, and other parameters. The model file size is 17GB, last updated on 2025-10-04. The model can be used for chat with the Transformers library, requiring specific installation and setup steps. The model is described in technical reports and academic articles published in 2023, 2024, and 2025.

instruction: Can you assess the tone and intent of this legal notice?
description: LEGAL-BERT is a family of BERT models designed for the legal domain, intended to support legal NLP research, computational law, and legal technology applications. The models were pre-trained on 12 GB of diverse English legal text from various fields, including legislation, court cases, and contracts, collected from publicly available resources. Different sub-domain variants, such as CONTRACTS-BERT-BASE, EURLEX-BERT-BASE, and ECHR-BERT-BASE, as well as the general LEGAL-BERT-BASE, perform better than using BERT out of the box for domain-specific tasks. LEGAL-BERT-SMALL is a lightweight version of BERT-BASE, pre-trained from scratch on legal data, which is 33% the size of BERT-BASE. It achieves comparable performance to larger models while being approximately four times faster and having a smaller environmental footprint. The pre-training corpora include 116,062 documents of EU legislation from EURLEX, 61,826 documents of UK legislation, 19,867 cases from the European Court of Justice, 12,554 cases from the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR), 164,141 cases from various US courts, and 76,366 US contracts from EDGAR. LEGAL-BERT-BASE, also known as LEGAL-BERT-SC, was trained from scratch using a newly created vocabulary by a sentence-piece tokenizer trained on the same legal corpora. LEGAL-BERT-FP models, which rely on the original BERT-BASE checkpoint, have been released in Archive.org and are considered secondary. The models were trained using the official Google BERT code, with a setup of 1 million training steps, batches of 256 sequences of length 512, and an initial learning rate of 1e-4. Training was conducted on a single Google Cloud TPU v3-8. LEGAL-BERT variants have been evaluated on downstream tasks and are described in the paper 'LEGAL-BERT: The Muppets straight out of Law School' by Chalkidis et al. (2020).

instruction: I'm looking for articles that analyze the causes of the 2008 financial crisis.
description: This is a learned sparse retrieval model designed for efficient and effective document search. It encodes documents into 30522-dimensional sparse vectors, where the non-zero dimensions represent token indices in the vocabulary, and the corresponding weights indicate the importance of each token. For queries, it uses a tokenizer and a weight look-up table to generate sparse vectors. The similarity between query and document vectors is calculated using the inner product. The model is intended to be used with OpenSearch's neural sparse feature, which supports learned sparse retrieval using a Lucene inverted index. The model can also be used outside the OpenSearch cluster with HuggingFace's API. The model is trained on a variety of datasets, including MS MARCO, eli5_question_answer, squad_pairs, WikiAnswers, yahoo_answers_title_question, gooaq_pairs, stackexchange_duplicate_questions_body_body, wikihow, S2ORC_title_abstract, stackexchange_duplicate_questions_title-body_title-body, yahoo_answers_question_answer, searchQA_top5_snippets, stackexchange_duplicate_questions_title_title, yahoo_answers_title_answer, fever, fiqa, hotpotqa, nfcorpus, and scifact. The model's performance is benchmarked on a subset of the BEIR benchmark, including TrecCovid, NFCorpus, NQ, HotpotQA, FiQA, ArguAna, Touche, DBPedia, SCIDOCS, FEVER, Climate FEVER, SciFact, and Quora. The v3 series of models demonstrates better search relevance, efficiency, and inference speed compared to the v1 and v2 series, though specific advantages and disadvantages may vary across datasets. The model has an average NDCG@10 score of 0.546 and an average FLOPS of 1.7. The model is inference-free for retrieval and has 133M parameters. The model is available on the HuggingFace Hub and can be used with the Sentence Transformers library. The model's performance varies across different datasets, with the highest NDCG@10 scores observed on TrecCovid (0.734) and Quora (0.873), and the lowest on SCIDOCS (0.167).

instruction: List the diseases mentioned in this drug interaction case report.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for disease entity recognition, specifically for identifying and extracting disease entities from the NCBI dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the NCBI_DISEASE dataset, a curated collection of 793 PubMed abstracts with 6,892 disease mentions mapped to 790 unique disease concepts from MeSH and OMIM. The model supports the classification of two entity types: B-Disease and I-Disease. It is intended for use in clinical and research applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the distilbert-base-multilingual-cased architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It has been validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.86, precision of 0.85, recall of 0.87, and accuracy of 0.96. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. The model is part of the OpenMed NER project, which provides open-source, domain-adapted state-of-the-art transformers for biomedical NER across multiple datasets.

instruction: I'm trying to find a document that has a pie chart showing market share distribution. Can you assist?
description: ColModernVBERT is the late-interaction version of ModernVBERT that is fine-tuned for visual document retrieval tasks and is the most performant model on this task. The ModernVBERT suite consists of compact 250M-parameter vision-language encoders that achieve state-of-the-art performance in this size class, matching the performance of models up to 10 times larger. ColModernVBERT is designed to support visual document retrieval tasks and is part of a family of models that includes BiModernVBERT, ModernVBERT-embed, and the base ModernVBERT model. The base ModernVBERT model is trained using a masked language modeling objective for modality alignment, while ModernVBERT-embed is further trained using contrastive learning without document specialization. BiModernVBERT is a bi-encoder version fine-tuned for visual document retrieval. ColModernVBERT matches the performance of models nearly 10 times larger on visual document benchmarks and provides an interesting inference speed on CPU compared to models of similar performance. The model is released under the MIT license. If used in academic work, it should be cited with the provided citation information.

instruction: I have a painting of a cityscape—can you help me make it look like a real-world photo?
description: Anything2Real 2601 A is a version of the Anything2Real family of LoRA models, designed to transform non-photorealistic images such as illustrations, anime, cartoons, and paintings into convincing photographs while preserving the original composition and content. It is based on Anything2Real 2601 and incorporates adjustments to the layer weights of anything2real alpha to achieve a more balanced performance between maintaining the original image and generating a realistic environment. Anything2Real 2601 A addresses weaknesses in previous versions, such as the limited effectiveness of Anything2Real 2601 in transforming eyes and the limited color performance of anything2real alpha. The model is built on the Qwen Edit 2511 base model and is recommended to be used with a strength of 1 to 0.75. A prompt template is suggested: 'change the picture 1 to realistic photograph, [description of your image]'. Including detailed descriptions in the prompt helps the model better understand the content and produce superior transformations, although it can still function without them. The model is used in a workflow involving 21 nodes, including CLIPLoader, ConditioningZeroOut, ImageScaleBy, KSampler, LoadImage, LoraLoaderModelOnly, and specialized Qwen Edit nodes, and is powered by an RTX 4090 Series GPU. Realism is subjective, and it is recommended to modulate strength or switch base models rather than increasing the LoRA weight if realism is insufficient. If realism remains inadequate, it is suggested to blend with an additional photorealistic LoRA and adjust to taste. The model was created to bridge the gap between artistic creations and photorealistic results without requiring users to start from scratch. It is intended for artists who want to see their characters in realistic form or for anyone interested in transforming their favorite art styles into photographs. Users are encouraged to share their results and suggestions for future improvements. For best results, it is recommended to follow the workflow demonstrated in the RunningHub example. Users can reach out with questions or collaboration ideas via Twitter, email, QQ Group, WeChat, or CivitAI.

instruction: Can you analyze the video and provide a summary of the main events that take place?
description: V-JEPA 2 is a frontier video understanding model developed by FAIR at Meta. It extends the pretraining objectives of previous models, resulting in state-of-the-art video understanding capabilities, leveraging data and model sizes at scale. The model is intended to represent any video (and image) to perform video classification, retrieval, or as a video encoder for vision-language models. V-JEPA 2 is designed to process videos by sampling a specific number of frames, with this model using 64 frames. For image processing, the image is copied to the desired number of frames. The model supports loading and processing video and image data using the Hugging Face Transformers library. Performance and evaluation results are not explicitly detailed in the provided text. The model is associated with the technical report titled 'V-JEPA~2: Self-Supervised Video Models Enable Understanding, Prediction and Planning' by Assran et al., published in 2025 by FAIR at Meta.

instruction: Find the most relevant information to clarify a confusing concept in theoretical physics.
description: The Diver Retriever 4B model is a reasoning-intensive model designed to tackle the challenge of reasonIR and rader. It is a text embedding model that supports bilingual (Chinese and English) processing with a context length of 40,000 tokens and contains 4 billion parameters. The model was trained using data from the fields of mathematics, coding, and healthcare, with careful attention to the difficulty level of the samples and the construction of unique negative samples for each field. As a result, the model performs very well on the Bright LeaderBoard and the Mteb-Medical Benchmark. Evaluation results show that the model achieves a BRIGHT score of 28.9, outperforming several other models in various categories including biology, earth science, economics, psychology, robotics, stack overflow, sustainability, leetcode, pony, AoPS, theoretical questions, and theoretical topics. The model can be used for inference tasks such as encoding queries and documents to compute cosine similarity, and it supports both Sentence Transformers and Transformers libraries for implementation. For finetuning, the model can be trained using the infonce loss with appropriate configuration and dependencies. The model is available for download from HuggingFace and ModelScope.

instruction: Could you evaluate which of these two summaries of a research paper captures the key points better?
description: Skywork-Reward-V2 is a series of eight reward models designed for versatility across a wide range of tasks. These models are trained on a mixture of 26 million carefully curated preference pairs, using a large-scale human-LLM synergistic pipeline. The series is based on the Bradley-Terry model and offers significant improvements over the first generation of Skywork-Reward. The Skywork-Reward-V2 series is trained on a significantly larger and higher-quality preference data mixture, which contributes to its superior performance. The models are available in eight different sizes, ranging from the smallest 0.6B variant, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-0.6B, to the largest 8B version, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B. The smallest model nearly matches the average performance of the previous best model, Skywork-Reward-Gemma-2-27B-v0.2, while the largest model surpasses all existing reward models across all benchmarks on average. The top experimental model, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M, outperforms all existing reward models on every benchmark. The Skywork-Reward-V2 series achieves state-of-the-art performance on seven major reward model benchmarks, including RewardBench v1, RewardBench v2, PPE Preference, PPE Correctness, RMB, RM-Bench, and JudgeBench. For most use cases, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B is recommended, with smaller variants suggested for low-resource settings. All models are trained on preference data with a maximum length of 16,384 tokens, and it is recommended to perform inference within this limit. System prompts should not be included when using chat templates. The Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M model is considered an experimental variant, trained on the complete set of 40 million preference pairs, with about one third of the chosen-rejected pairs flipped. It is recommended for research or non-production purposes only. The model series is available under different licenses depending on the base model. Models derived from Qwen3 support commercial use and permit modifications and the creation of derivative works under the Apache 2.0 License, provided that all conditions are met and proper attribution is given. Models derived from Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct follow the Meta License. The model can be used for inference in Hugging Face Transformers or via distributed inference using SGLang for optimal throughput with a large number of conversations. The model repository, including the model weights and code, is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License for Qwen3-derived models and the Meta License for Llama-derived models. If you have any questions, you can contact the authors at yuhao.liuu at kunlun-inc dot com and liang.zeng at kunlun-inc dot com. If you find the work useful, please cite it as follows: @article{liu2025skywork, title={Skywork-Reward-V2: Scaling Preference Data Curation via Human-AI Synergy}, author = {Liu, Chris Yuhao and Zeng, Liang and Xiao, Yuzhen and He, Jujie and Liu, Jiacai and Wang, Chaojie and Yan, Rui and Shen, Wei and Zhang, Fuxiang and Xu, Jiacheng and Liu, Yang and Zhou, Yahui}, journal={arXiv preprint arXiv:2507.01352}, year={2025}}

instruction: I'm looking for a text document that discusses the economic impact of AI in healthcare.
description: The nvidia/llama-nemoretriever-colembed-1b-v1 is a late interaction embedding model fine-tuned for query-document retrieval. It accepts text queries and documents in the form of text or page images as input and outputs ColBERT-style multi-vector numerical representations for both. It is the smaller version of a model that achieved 1st place on ViDoRe V1 (nDCG@5), ViDoRe V2 (nDCG@5), and MTEB VisualDocumentRetrieval (Rank Borda) as of June 27, 2025. The nvidia/llama-nemoretriever-colembed-1b-v1 achieves 2nd place on these benchmarks. The model is based on a transformer architecture and is built on top of a vision-language model derived from google/siglip2-giant-opt-patch16-384 and meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B. It is intended for researchers exploring applications that require understanding or retrieving information across both text and image modalities, particularly in multimodal RAG systems where queries are in text format and documents are images such as pages, text, charts, tables, or infographics. Potential applications include multimedia search engines, cross-modal retrieval systems, and conversational AI with rich input understanding. The model has a maximum context length of 8192 tokens for both text queries and documents, with longer texts needing to be chunked or truncated. For images, the model scales them into multiple tiles of 512x512 and requires them to be in Python PIL format. The model outputs embedding vectors of a specified dimension for each input token. It is designed and optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems, leveraging NVIDIA’s hardware and software frameworks for faster training and inference times compared to CPU-only solutions. The model was trained on 12.74 million QA pairs across 23 datasets and evaluated on multiple benchmarks for Visual Document Retrieval, including ViDoRe V1, ViDoRe V2, and MTEB Visual Document Retrieval. On these benchmarks, the model achieved an average NDCG@5 score of 0.9050 on ViDoRe V1 (06/27/2025), 0.6209 on ViDoRe V2 (06/27/2025), and 0.8238 on MTEB Visual Document Retrieval. The model is for non-commercial/research use only and is governed by specific terms of use. It is built with Meta Llama 3 and improved using Qwen. The model requires transformers version 4.49.0 and flash attention for usage. It is supported on Linux operating systems and compatible with A100 40GB, A100 80GB, and H100 80GB hardware. NVIDIA encourages users to ensure the model meets requirements for the relevant industry and use case and to address any potential misuse. Security vulnerabilities or concerns should be reported to NVIDIA.

instruction: Which anatomical regions are mentioned in this EHR note from the patient's visit?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for anatomical entity recognition, focusing on identifying and extracting anatomical structures and body parts from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and trained on a curated ANATOMY dataset, making it suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model supports the classification of two entity types: B-Anatomy and I-Anatomy. It is based on the e5-base-v2 architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. The model has achieved an F1 score of 0.88, precision of 0.88, recall of 0.89, and accuracy of 0.97. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0.

instruction: Generate an embedding for this sentence and this image together.
description: UME-R1 is a model that has undergone a cold-start SFT stage and an RL stage of training. It is capable of embedding text, images, multiple images, and videos. The model can generate either discriminative or generative embeddings as needed, and the generative embeddings possess the potential for test-time scaling. UME-R1 significantly outperforms discriminative embeddings and can provide either type of representation as required. Its oracle performance—selecting the best between discriminative and generative—far exceeds using either mode alone. In addition, UME-R1 can produce improved embedding representations through repeated sampling, indicating that generative embeddings also hold strong promise for inference-time scaling.

instruction: Explain the concept of blockchain in simple terms for someone with no technical background.
description: The gpt-oss series is designed for powerful reasoning, agentic tasks, and versatile developer use cases. Two models are available: gpt-oss-120b, intended for production, general-purpose, and high reasoning use cases that fit into a single 80GB GPU (like NVIDIA H100 or AMD MI300X), with 117B parameters and 5.1B active parameters; and gpt-oss-20b, intended for lower latency, local, or specialized use cases, with 21B parameters and 3.6B active parameters. Both models were trained on OpenAI's data and require the harmony format to function correctly. The gpt-oss-120b model is covered by this model card. The models are released under a permissive Apache 2.0 license, allowing for free use without copyleft restrictions or patent risk, making them suitable for experimentation, customization, and commercial deployment. Users can adjust the reasoning effort (low, medium, high) based on their specific use case and latency needs. The models provide full access to the reasoning process, which is useful for debugging and increasing trust in outputs, though it is not intended for end users. They are fine-tunable for specific use cases through parameter fine-tuning and support agentic capabilities such as function calling and structured outputs. The models were post-trained with MXFP4 quantization of the MoE weights, enabling gpt-oss-120b to run on a single 80GB GPU and gpt-oss-20b to run within 16GB of memory. All evaluations were performed using the same MXFP4 quantization. The models can be used with Transformers, vLLM, PyTorch / Triton, Ollama, and LM Studio. They can be downloaded directly from Hugging Face CLI. The larger model, gpt-oss-120b, can be fine-tuned on a single H100 node, while the smaller model can be fine-tuned on consumer hardware. The models are suitable for web browsing, function calling with defined schemas, and agentic operations like browser tasks.

instruction: Produce a high-quality image of a dog in the same resolution and style as CIFAR10 data.
description: Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM) are a class of latent variable models inspired by considerations from nonequilibrium thermodynamics. These models are designed for high-quality image synthesis. The training process is based on a weighted variational bound derived from a novel connection between diffusion probabilistic models and denoising score matching with Langevin dynamics. The models naturally support a progressive lossy decompression scheme that can be interpreted as a generalization of autoregressive decoding. On the unconditional CIFAR10 dataset, the model achieves an Inception score of 9.46 and a state-of-the-art FID score of 3.17. On 256x256 LSUN, the sample quality is comparable to that of ProgressiveGAN. For inference, DDPM models can use discrete noise schedulers such as ddpm, ddim, or pndm. While the ddpm scheduler provides the highest quality, it is also the slowest. The ddim or pndm schedulers offer a better trade-off between quality and inference speed. The model can be loaded using the DDPMPipeline, DDIMPipeline, or PNDMPipeline from the diffusers library. A generated image can be saved as a file for later use.

instruction: Can you show the positions of the trees and flowers in this garden picture?
description: The Deformable Detection Transformer (DETR) model is an encoder-decoder transformer architecture with a ResNet-50 convolutional backbone. It is designed for end-to-end object detection and was trained on the COCO 2017 dataset, which includes 118,000 annotated images for training and 5,000 for validation. The model incorporates two heads on top of the decoder outputs: a linear layer for class label prediction and a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) for bounding box regression. Object detection is performed using object queries, with 100 queries specified for the COCO dataset. During training, a bipartite matching loss is used, which compares predicted classes and bounding boxes from each of the 100 object queries to the ground truth annotations. The Hungarian matching algorithm is applied to create an optimal one-to-one mapping between queries and annotations. The model is optimized using cross-entropy for class prediction and a combination of L1 and generalized IoU loss for bounding box regression. The model is intended for object detection tasks and supports PyTorch for both the feature extractor and model. The model was introduced in a paper by Zhu et al. and is available for use through the Hugging Face platform. A disclaimer notes that the model card was written by the Hugging Face team, as the original model developers did not provide one.

instruction: Replace the background of this portrait with a modern cityscape, while preserving the subject’s expression and lighting.
description: LongCat-Image-Edit is the image editing version of Longcat-Image. It supports bilingual (Chinese-English) editing and achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source image editing models, delivering leading instruction-following and image quality with superior visual consistency. LongCat-Image-Edit supports various editing tasks, such as global editing, local editing, text modification, and reference-guided editing. It has strong semantic understanding capabilities and can perform precise editing according to instructions. The model has strong consistency preservation capabilities, specifically scrutinizing whether attributes in non-edited regions, such as layout, texture, color tone, and subject identity, remain invariant unless targeted by the instruction, which is well demonstrated in multi-turn editing. LongCat-Image-Edit achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in image editing tasks while significantly improving model inference efficiency, especially among open-source image editing models. For both Text-to-Image and Image Editing tasks involving text generation, you must enclose the target text within single or double quotation marks (both English '...' / "..." and Chinese ‘...’ / “...” styles are supported). The model utilizes a specialized character-level encoding strategy specifically for quoted content. Failure to use explicit quotation marks prevents this mechanism from triggering, which will severely compromise the text rendering capability.

instruction: What kind of plant is shown in this picture?
description: MobileNet V1 is a pre-trained model on ImageNet-1k at a resolution of 192x192. It was introduced by Howard et al and first released by the Hugging Face team, as the original team did not provide a model card. MobileNets are designed to be small, low-latency, and low-power models that can be adapted for various use cases by adjusting their parameters to meet resource constraints. They support tasks such as classification, detection, embeddings, and segmentation, similar to other large-scale models like Inception.
