The methods are sometimes not robust to heavy degradations and very large motions, and they share some failure cases with existing methods, such as failing to fully remove degradation or generating unpleasing details. Additionally, due to their strong generation ability, the methods may overly generate details on inputs with very light degradations, such as 720p AIGC videos, occasionally leading to oversharpened results.

instruction: Arrange these items based on how well they match the query about modern art movements.
description: This is a model finetuned using the Sentence Transformers library. It computes scores for pairs of texts, which can be used for text reranking and semantic search. The model is a Cross Encoder with a maximum sequence length of 1024 tokens and produces one output label. It is designed to work with multilingual text and is licensed under cc-by-nc-4.0. The model can be loaded using the Sentence Transformers library and supports tasks such as computing scores for text pairs and ranking texts based on similarity to a single text. It was trained on a dataset of 16,862 samples with query, document, and label columns, and evaluated on a dataset of 808 samples. The training used a batch size of 32 for both training and evaluation, a learning rate of 2e-05, and 2 training epochs with a warmup ratio of 0.1. The model was trained using bf16 precision. Evaluation results on the affiliation-val dataset show a mean average precision (map) of 0.9307, a mean reciprocal rank at 10 (mrr@10) of 0.9307, and a normalized discounted cumulative gain at 10 (ndcg@10) of 0.9502. The model was trained using the AdamW optimizer with a linear learning rate scheduler and a weight decay of 0.0. The training loss decreased from 0.577 at the first step to 0.2783 at step 1000, with a validation loss of 0.528 at the saved checkpoint. The model was developed using Python 3.12.12, Sentence Transformers 5.2.0, Transformers 4.57.3, PyTorch 2.9.1+cu128, Accelerate 1.12.0, Datasets 4.4.2, and Tokenizers 0.22.1.

instruction: I need a creative way to explain quantum physics to a middle school class—can you suggest an analogy?
description: The gemma-3n-E4B-it is an 8-bit quantized version of a model optimized for Apple Silicon using MLX. It is part of the Community Model Program, which highlights new and noteworthy models created by the community. The model is provided by a third party and is not created, owned, or endorsed by LM Studio. LM Studio does not guarantee the accuracy, reliability, or safety of the model's outputs, and disclaims all warranties related to the model's performance, security, or suitability for any purpose. Users are solely responsible for any damage or issues arising from the use of the model.

instruction: How do I merge two images of different products into one cohesive design?
description: This repository contains the FP8 quantized version of the Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 model. It is designed for efficient inference while maintaining high-quality image editing capabilities. The model supports single-model image editing workflows and is compatible with standard pipelines such as Diffusers and custom ComfyUI nodes. Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 significantly improves character consistency, allowing for imaginative edits based on an input portrait while preserving the identity and visual characteristics of the subject. It further enhances consistency in multi-person group photos, enabling high-fidelity fusion of two separate person images into a coherent group shot. The model integrates selected popular LoRAs directly into the base model, unlocking their effects without extra tuning. For example, it supports realistic lighting control and the generation of new viewpoints directly with the base model. It is also suitable for industrial design applications, such as batch industrial product design and material replacement for industrial components. Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 introduces stronger geometric reasoning capability, such as directly generating auxiliary construction lines for design or annotation purposes. The model is distributed under the Apache-2.0 license. For usage, dependencies such as torch, diffusers, and safetensors must be installed, and the FP8 model can be loaded using the QwenImageEditPipeline from the diffusers library.

instruction: How does the lighting affect the visual features in this photo?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. These models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones that provide multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN or logistic regression classifiers on the class token, or using a linear layer applied on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) is a curated dataset of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) is a dataset of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, the iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, semantic segmentation, depth estimation, and video classification. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on web data, the models achieve results such as 87.0 on IN-ReaL, 60.4 on IN-R, 50.9 on Obj.Net, 49.5 on Ox.-H, 47.0 on ADE20k, 0.403 on NYU, 72.7 on DAVIS, 56.3 on NAVI, and 50.4 on SPair for the ViT-S/16 model. For ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web data, the models achieve results such as 86.6 on IN-ReaL @256px, 87.7 on IN-ReaL @512px, 73.7 on Obj.Net @256px, 74.1 on Obj.Net @512px, 52.6 on ADE20k @256px, 58.7 on ADE20k @512px, 42.7 on NYU @256px, and 0.448 on NYU @512px for the ConvNeXt Tiny model. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on satellite data, the models achieve results such as 73.0 on m-BEnet, 96.5 on m-brick-kiln, 94.1 on m-eurosat, 60.6 on m-forestnet, 96.0 on m-pv4ger, 57.4 on m-so2sat, and a mean of 79.6 for the ViT-L/16 model. The models were trained using Nvidia H100 GPUs on private infrastructure in the USA, with 61,440 hours of compute time for the ViT-7B model, resulting in an estimated carbon emission of 18t CO2eq. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters), ViT-S+ (29M parameters), ViT-B (86M parameters), ViT-L (300M parameters), ViT-H+ (840M parameters), and ViT-7B (6716M parameters), all with a patch size of 16 and varying embedding dimensions, register tokens, heads, and FFN types. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: How can I add a new product to an existing scene in a realistic way?
description: Qwen-Image-Edit-2509 is the monthly iteration of Qwen-Image-Edit, introduced in September. It is designed to support image editing tasks, with improvements over the previous version released in August. The model supports multi-image editing, allowing combinations such as 'person + person,' 'person + product,' and 'person + scene,' with optimal performance observed when using 1 to 3 input images. For single-image inputs, it significantly improves editing consistency, particularly in preserving facial identity for person editing, product identity for product editing, and text content, fonts, colors, and materials for text editing. The model also includes native support for ControlNet, enabling the use of depth maps, edge maps, keypoint maps, and other related features. It is built upon the Qwen-Image-Edit architecture and further trained via image concatenation to enable multi-image editing. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0.

instruction: How do I adjust the settings to upscale a long video without running into memory issues?
description: ComfyUI-SeedVR2_VideoUpscaler is an official release for ComfyUI that enables video and image upscaling. It provides high-quality upscaling and is suitable for any video length when the appropriate settings are used. The model supports both 3B and 7B versions, with FP8 and FP16 precision options. It requires a GPU with substantial VRAM, with at least 18GB recommended for the 3B version and more than 24GB for optimal performance with the 7B version. The model automatically downloads into the `models/SEEDVR2` directory. Users must configure parameters such as model version, seed, new resolution, batch size, and VRAM preservation settings. Temporal consistency requires a batch size of at least 5, and higher batch sizes improve results but increase VRAM usage. Input and output resolutions impact VRAM consumption, and users may need to adjust these if they encounter out-of-memory errors. The model has been benchmarked on various hardware, including NVIDIA H100 and RTX4090 GPUs, with performance metrics showing improvements in speed and VRAM efficiency over time. The 7B FP8 model has quality issues and is recommended to be used in FP16 if possible. The model has undergone several updates to improve speed, reduce VRAM usage, fix memory leaks, and allow process interruption. It is compatible with the latest ComfyUI version and Python 3.12.9. The model has high VRAM requirements and processing speed depends on GPU capabilities. Contributions are welcome, and users are encouraged to submit pull requests for improvements. The code is released under the MIT license.

instruction: Create a visual of a futuristic train speeding through a tunnel with glowing lights.
description: This repository contains Nunchaku-quantized versions of a model designed to generate high-quality images from text prompts and to advance complex text rendering. The model is optimized for efficient inference while maintaining minimal loss in performance. It is a text-to-image model developed by the Nunchaku Team and is licensed under the Apache-2.0 license. The model is available in different quantized versions, with data types including INT4 for non-Blackwell GPUs (pre-50-series) and NVFP4 for Blackwell GPUs (50-series). Two rank configurations are provided: r32 for faster inference and r128 for better quality but slower inference. The model includes standard inference speed versions for general use, as well as 4-step and 8-step distilled models fused with additional components using LoRA strength = 1.0. The 4-step distilled models are available in both r32 and r128 ranks for INT4 and NVFP4 data types, with the r128 versions offering better quality at the cost of slower inference. Similarly, the 8-step distilled models are available in the same configurations, with r128 versions providing improved quality but slower performance. The model is based on a diffusion architecture and is related to the SVDQuant method, as described in the paper 'SVDQuant: Absorbing Outliers by Low-Rank Components for 4-Bit Diffusion Models' presented at the Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations in 2025.

instruction: How similar are the phrases 'climate change is a global issue' and 'global warming affects everyone' in meaning?
description: This is a model finetuned from distilbert/distilbert-base-uncased. It maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for semantic textual similarity, semantic search, paraphrase mining, text classification, clustering, and more. The model is a Sentence Transformer with a maximum sequence length of 512 tokens and an output dimensionality of 768 dimensions. The similarity function used is Cosine Similarity. The full model architecture includes a Transformer with a DistilBertModel architecture and a Pooling layer with a word embedding dimension of 768. The pooling configuration includes pooling_mode_mean_tokens set to True and all other pooling modes set to False. The model was trained using Python 3.12.8, Sentence Transformers 5.1.0, Transformers 4.55.4, PyTorch 2.8.0, Accelerate 1.5.2, Datasets 3.5.0, and Tokenizers 0.21.1.

instruction: I want to highlight the object in this image for a presentation. Can you help with the segmentation?
description: BiRefNet is a bilateral reference network designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation, particularly for matting tasks. The model is adaptable to arbitrary input shapes, ranging from 256x256 to 2304x2304, and demonstrates robust performance on inputs of any shape. It was trained on the DIS-TR dataset and validated on DIS-TE and DIS-VD datasets. The model has achieved state-of-the-art performance on three tasks: DIS (Dichotomous Image Segmentation), HRSOD (High-Resolution Salient Object Detection), and COD (Complementary Object Detection). The architecture of BiRefNet is not explicitly detailed in the text, but it supports inference on arbitrary resolutions without the need for resizing. The model can be loaded using the HuggingFace Transformers library or via local code and weights. It is implemented in PyTorch and supports GPU inference. The model is associated with the research paper 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation' by Peng Zheng, Dehong Gao, Deng-Ping Fan, Li Liu, Jorma Laaksonen, Wanli Ouyang, and Nicu Sebe, published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research in 2024. The model is available through a GitHub repository and HuggingFace, and online inference demos are provided. The authors acknowledge support from Freepik and Fal for GPU resources and from Not-lain for deployment assistance on HuggingFace.

instruction: Which part of the scan shows the 'fluid-filled cavity in the right lobe of the liver' described in the report?
description: Knowledge to Sight (K2Sight) is a novel framework designed for grounding abnormalities in medical images, where the goal is to localize clinical findings based on textual descriptions. Unlike generalist Vision-Language Models (VLMs) that often struggle with domain-specific medical terms, K2Sight introduces structured semantic supervision. It achieves this by decomposing clinical concepts into interpretable visual attributes like shape, density, and anatomical location, distilled from domain ontologies. This approach guides region-text alignment during training, enabling data-efficient training of compact models (0.23B and 2B parameters) using only 1.5% of the data required by state-of-the-art medical VLMs. Despite their small size and limited training data, K2Sight models achieve performance on par with or better than 7B+ medical VLMs, with up to 9.82% improvement in $mAP_{50}$. This model can be easily integrated and used for zero-shot abnormality grounding in medical images.

instruction: Can you help me design a product by replacing the material of a component in a given image?
description: Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 is an enhanced version of Qwen-Image-Edit-2509, featuring multiple improvements, including notably better consistency. Key enhancements include mitigating image drift, improved character consistency, integrated LoRA capabilities, enhanced industrial design generation, and strengthened geometric reasoning ability. The model is designed to perform imaginative edits based on an input portrait while preserving the identity and visual characteristics of the subject. It further enhances consistency in multi-person group photos, enabling high-fidelity fusion of two separate person images into a coherent group shot. Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 integrates selected popular LoRAs directly into the base model, unlocking their effects without extra tuning. For example, it supports realistic lighting control and generating new viewpoints directly with the base model. The model is also optimized for industrial design applications, such as batch industrial product design and material replacement for industrial components. Additionally, it introduces stronger geometric reasoning capability, such as directly generating auxiliary construction lines for design or annotation purposes. Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 is licensed under Apache 2.0.

instruction: Analyze the relevance of this image to the statement 'normal fundus with no signs of diabetic retinopathy'?
description: MedSigLIP is a variant of SigLIP (Sigmoid Loss for Language Image Pre-training) designed to encode medical images and text into a common embedding space. It contains a 400M parameter vision encoder and a 400M parameter text encoder, supporting 448x448 image resolution with up to 64 text tokens. MedSigLIP was trained on a combination of de-identified medical image and text pairs, including chest X-rays, dermatology images, ophthalmology images, histopathology slides, and slices of CT and MRI volumes, along with associated descriptions or reports. This training data was combined with natural (non-medical) image and text pairs to retain the model's ability to parse natural images. MedSigLIP is recommended for medical image interpretation applications that do not require text generation, such as data-efficient classification, zero-shot classification, and semantic image retrieval. For medical applications that require text generation, another model is recommended. MedSigLIP accepts images and text as inputs, producing image embeddings, text embeddings, and similarity scores between the image and text. It was evaluated across a range of medical image modalities, including chest X-ray, pathology, dermatology, and ophthalmology. Key performance metrics include zero-shot AUCs for chest X-ray findings, where MedSigLIP outperformed ELIXR in several categories, and AUCs for dermatology, ophthalmology, and pathology findings, where MedSigLIP showed competitive performance compared to existing HAI-DEF embedding models. MedSigLIP is based on SigLIP-400M and uses the same encoder that powers image interpretation in a generative model. It is intended for use by software developers and researchers to facilitate the creation of healthcare applications involving medical images and text. MedSigLIP is not a finished product and requires adaptation and validation for specific use cases. Developers should consider potential biases in validation data and data contamination concerns when evaluating the model's generalization capabilities. MedSigLIP was trained on diverse public datasets, including MIMIC-CXR, Slake-VQA, PAD-UFES-20, SCIN, TCGA, CAMELYON, PMC-OA, and Mendeley Digital Knee X-Ray, as well as de-identified, licensed datasets or datasets collected internally at Google from consented participants. The model was created on July 9, 2025, and is version 1.0.0. When using this model, please cite the MedGemma Technical Report by Sellergren et al. (2025).

instruction: Can you separate the clothing items from the body in this fashion shot?
description: The FASHN Human Parser is a SegFormer-B4 model fine-tuned for human parsing with 18 semantic classes, optimized for fashion and virtual try-on applications. It segments human images into 18 semantic categories, including body parts such as face, hair, arms, hands, legs, feet, and torso; clothing items such as top, dress, skirt, pants, belt, and scarf; and accessories such as bag, hat, glasses, and jewelry. The model uses a SegFormer-B4 architecture, which includes an MIT-B4 encoder and an MLP decoder. It accepts input images of size 384 x 576 and produces 18-class semantic segmentation masks. The model was fine-tuned on a proprietary dataset curated and annotated by FASHN AI, specifically designed for virtual try-on applications. The 18-class label schema was developed to capture the semantic regions most relevant for clothing transfer and human body understanding in fashion contexts. The model is optimized for single-person images with clear visibility and performs best on fashion or e-commerce style photography. Input images are resized to 384x576, and very small subjects may lose detail. The label definitions include background, face, hair, top, dress, skirt, pants, belt, bag, hat, scarf, glasses, arms, hands, legs, feet, torso, and jewelry. For virtual try-on applications, the categories are mapped as follows: Tops (upper body) include top, dress, and scarf; Bottoms (lower body) include skirt, pants, and belt; and One-pieces (full body) include top, dress, scarf, skirt, pants, and belt. Labels typically preserved during virtual try-on include face, hair, jewelry, bag, glasses, and hat.

instruction: Could you help me understand the tone or intent behind this voice message?
description: Ultravox is a multimodal Speech LLM that can consume both speech and text as input, such as a text system prompt and a voice user message. The input to the model is given as a text prompt with a special pseudo-token, and the model processor replaces this token with embeddings derived from the input audio. Using the merged embeddings as input, the model generates output text as usual. In the v0.6 series, Ultravox models are trained on expanded Hindi speech data, resulting in significantly improved speech understanding performance on Hindi and modest degradation on other languages. Additionally, the v0.6 models are trained on noise datasets for improved noise robustness and the ability to output a special string '((noise))' if the input audio is too noisy or doesn't contain clear speech. In a future revision of Ultravox, the plan is to expand the token vocabulary to support the generation of semantic and acoustic audio tokens, which can then be fed to a vocoder to produce voice output. No preference tuning has been applied to this revision of the model. Ultravox is built around a pretrained LLM (Llama, Gemma, Qwen, etc) and a speech encoder backbone. The multi-modal adapter is trained, the Whisper encoder is fine-tuned, and the LLM is kept frozen. The model uses a knowledge-distillation loss where Ultravox is trying to match the logits of the text-based LLM backbone. The training dataset is a mix of ASR datasets, extended with continuations generated by Llama 3.1 8B, speech translation datasets, and noise datasets. The training procedure involves supervised speech instruction finetuning via knowledge-distillation. The training regime uses BF16 mixed precision training on 8x H100 GPUs. The current version of Ultravox, when invoked with audio content, has a time-to-first-token (TTFT) of approximately 150ms, and a tokens-per-second rate of ~50-100 when using an A100-40GB GPU, all using a text-based LLM (Llama, Gemma, or Qwen) backbone. Evaluations are conducted on covost2 (speech translation measured in BLEU), fleurs and ultravox_calls (speech recognition measured in WER), big bench audio (audio reasoning measured in accuracy), as well as musan and ultravox_unintelligible (noise/unintelligible speech detection measured in recall). The model is developed by Fixie.ai and is licensed under the MIT license. It can be used as a voice agent, for speech-to-speech translation, and for the analysis of spoken audio.

instruction: I need to verify if the text in this image is in the correct orientation for OCR processing.
description: The text line orientation classification module is designed to distinguish the orientation of text lines and correct them using post-processing. In scenarios such as document scanning and license or certificate photography, the capture device may be rotated, resulting in text lines in various orientations. Standard OCR pipelines are not well-suited to handle such data. By utilizing image classification technology, the orientation of text lines can be predetermined and adjusted, thereby enhancing the accuracy of OCR processing. The PP-LCNet_x1_0_textline_ori is a text line classification model based on PP-LCNet_x0_25, with two classes: 0 degrees and 180 degrees. It achieves an average recognition accuracy of 98.85 percent and has a model storage size of 0.96 megabytes.

instruction: I want to know if this picture is a genuine photo or if it's been generated by a machine.
description: AIRealNet is a binary image classifier designed to distinguish AI-generated images from real human photographs. It is optimized for detecting conventional AI-generated content while adhering to strict privacy standards, avoiding the use of personal or sensitive images. The model leverages the SwinV2 Tiny architecture as its backbone, achieving high accuracy while maintaining a lightweight design suitable for practical deployment. AIRealNet was fine-tuned on a 14k-image dataset from the `Parveshiiii/AI-vs-Real` repository, which is balanced between AI-generated and real images (60% AI-generated, 40% real human images). The training data was sourced from public datasets and AI generation tools, with intentional exclusion of edited personal photos. The model demonstrates high accuracy and robustness on public datasets, though it has limitations in detecting subtle edits, such as nano-scale modifications, and cannot identify AI-modified real-person images due to privacy and ethical guidelines. Performance may also vary on images from unseen AI generators or unconventional content. AIRealNet is intended for use in detecting AI-generated imagery on social media, research publications, and digital media platforms, assisting content moderators, researchers, and fact-checkers in identifying synthetic media. It is not intended for legal verification without human corroboration. The model preprocesses images to 256x256 resolution, extracts features using the SwinV2 Tiny vision transformer, and outputs binary classification probabilities for AI-generated versus real human images. Ethical considerations include a privacy-first approach, responsible deployment with human review, and transparency in communicating limitations and dataset design. Future work aims to improve detection of subtle AI-generated edits, expand training data with diverse AI generators, and explore multi-modal detection capabilities.

instruction: I need a high-quality version of this image for a poster—can you upscale it?
description: This is a model for high-definition magnification of images, trained on Qwen/Qwen-Image-Edit-2511. It is primarily used for losslessly enlarging images to approximately 2K size and is intended for use in ComfyUI. The model works with a modified version of Comfy's Qwen/Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 workflow, which includes adding a Qwen/Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 LoRA node connected to the base model. The model can be used with a QwenImageEditPipeline to load the base model and trained LoRA weights for in-scene editing. An input image is loaded, and an in-scene editing prompt is defined, such as 'Upscale this picture to 4K resolution.' The model generates an edited image with enhanced scene understanding using specified parameters, including a prompt, generator seed, true_cfg_scale, negative_prompt, and num_inference_steps. The model weights are available in Safetensors format. There is no fixed trigger word, and the specific removal prompt may require further testing. The model was trained by the AI Laboratory of Chongqing Valiant Cat Technology Co., LTD.

instruction: What does the note 'for the desperate' mean for a particular quant version?
description: This model provides various quantized versions of a model, including imatrix and IQ quants, which are available for download. The quants are sorted by size, though not necessarily by quality, with IQ quants often being preferable to similar-sized non-IQ quants. The available quant types include i1-IQ1_S, i1-IQ1_M, i1-IQ2_XXS, i1-IQ2_XS, i1-IQ2_S, i1-IQ2_M, i1-Q2_K_S, i1-Q2_K, i1-IQ3_XXS, i1-IQ3_XS, i1-Q3_K_S, i1-IQ3_S, i1-IQ3_M, i1-Q3_K_M, i1-Q3_K_L, i1-IQ4_XS, i1-Q4_0, i1-IQ4_NL, i1-Q4_K_S, i1-Q4_K_M, i1-Q4_1, i1-Q5_K_S, i1-Q5_K_M, and i1-Q6_K. Each quant type has a specified size in gigabytes and associated notes, such as 'for the desperate', 'very low quality', or 'recommended'. An imatrix file is also provided for creating custom quants. Users are advised to refer to external resources for detailed instructions on using GGUF files and concatenating multi-part files. The model's quantization work was made possible through the use of company servers and a private supercomputer, which enabled the production of higher-quality imatrix quants.

instruction: How can I create a realistic voice recording of a story being read aloud using a sample of someone’s voice?
description: NeuTTS Air is a state-of-the-art, on-device, text-to-speech (TTS) language model that enables super-realistic voice synthesis and instant voice cloning. Built on a 0.5B LLM backbone, it delivers natural-sounding speech with real-time performance, making it suitable for embedded voice agents, assistants, toys, and compliance-safe applications. The model is optimized for on-device deployment in GGML format, allowing it to run efficiently on devices such as phones, laptops, and Raspberry Pis. It features a simple architecture combining a language model and a neural audio codec, which achieves high audio quality at low bitrates using a single codebook. NeuTTS Air supports voice cloning with as little as 3 seconds of reference audio and is designed to be secure, with watermarked outputs and optimized power consumption for mobile and embedded use. For best results, reference audio should be mono, 16-44 kHz, 3–15 seconds in length, clean, and contain natural, continuous speech. The model requires two inputs: a reference audio sample and a text string, which it uses to synthesize speech in the style of the reference. The model is available through the official website neuphonic.com, and users are warned to be cautious of unrelated websites. The model includes a disclaimer urging users not to use it for harmful purposes.

instruction: Could you generate a Japanese voice output that sounds like it belongs in an anime from this input?
description: Anime-XCodec2-44.1kHz is a fine-tuned variant of NandemoGHS/Anime-XCodec2, designed to produce 44.1kHz high-fidelity Japanese speech in an anime/game-style. It incorporates upsampling layers and RMS loss inspired by the Inworld TTS-1 paper. The model was trained on approximately 22,000 hours of Japanese speech data. Only the decoder was updated; the encoder and codebook remain frozen, ensuring speech tokens are identical to the original XCodec2. This makes the model a drop-in decoder for systems already using XCodec2 tokens, such as Llasa. The model accepts 16kHz input for encoding and outputs 44.1kHz audio. It is intended for decoding XCodec2 speech tokens into high-fidelity Japanese speech, upgrading existing Anime-XCodec2 pipelines to 44.1kHz output, and potentially for audio super-resolution, though this use case is untested. The model modifies the original XCodec2 architecture by adding upsampler blocks and requires a custom xcodec2 library for inference, as the standard library will not work. The model is optimized for Japanese and anime/game-style voices, and it is not compatible with the original xcodec2 library. The training procedure involved integrating an UpSamplerBlock into the decoder with parameters hop_length=147, upsample_factors=[3, 2], and kernel_sizes=[7, 6]. RMS Loss was adopted in addition to the original losses. The encoder and codebook were frozen, while the generator's backbone, head, upsampler, and fc_post_a were fine-tuned. The model is licensed under CC-BY-NC 4.0, inherited from XCodec2 and Anime-XCodec2. It acknowledges the original HKUSTAudio/xcodec2 model and Inworld AI for their contributions to the upsampler architecture and RMS Loss.

instruction: What are the main advantages of using IQ-quants compared to non-IQ quants of similar size?
description: This is a vision model, and if applicable, mmproj files will be provided. Weighted imatrix quantizations are available. Static quantizations are also available for download. For users unfamiliar with GGUF files, guidance is available on how to use them, including instructions for concatenating multi-part files. A variety of quantized versions are provided, sorted by size but not necessarily by quality. IQ-quants are often considered preferable to similar-sized non-IQ quants. The available quant types include i1-IQ1_S, i1-IQ1_M, i1-IQ2_XXS, i1-IQ2_XS, i1-IQ2_S, i1-IQ2_M, i1-Q2_K_S, i1-IQ3_XXS, i1-Q2_K, i1-IQ3_XS, i1-IQ3_S, i1-Q3_K_S, i1-IQ3_M, i1-Q3_K_M, i1-Q3_K_L, i1-IQ4_XS, i1-IQ4_NL, i1-Q4_0, i1-Q4_K_S, i1-Q4_K_M, i1-Q4_1, i1-Q5_K_S, i1-Q5_K_M, and i1-Q6_K. Each quant type has a specified size in gigabytes and associated notes. For example, the i1-IQ1_S quant is 1.2 GB and is described as suitable for the desperate, while the i1-Q4_K_M quant is 2.6 GB and is described as fast and recommended. An imatrix file is also available for creating custom quants, and it is 0.1 GB in size. A graph by ikawrakow compares some lower-quality quant types, and Artefact2 has provided additional commentary on the topic. Users are directed to a resource for frequently asked questions and for requesting other models to be quantized. The model developer acknowledges their company for providing server access and workstation upgrades to support this work, as well as another individual for granting access to a private supercomputer, which enabled the production of higher-quality imatrix quants.

instruction: Produce a vibrant, colorful mural-style artwork of a dancer in motion.
description: HiDream-I1 is a new open-source image generative foundation model with 17B parameters that achieves state-of-the-art image generation quality within seconds. It produces exceptional results across multiple styles including photorealistic, cartoon, and artistic. The model achieves a state-of-the-art HPS v2.1 score, which aligns with human preferences. It also achieves industry-leading scores on the GenEval and DPG benchmarks, outperforming all other open-source models. The model is released under the MIT license to foster scientific advancement and enable creative innovation. Generated images can be freely used for personal projects, scientific research, and commercial applications. The model includes a VAE component from FLUX.1 [schnell], licensed under Apache 2.0, and text encoders from google/t5-v1_1-xxl (licensed under Apache 2.0) and meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct (licensed under the Llama 3.1 Community License Agreement). The Transformer models in the repository are licensed under the MIT License. Users own all content they create with this model and can use their generated content freely, but must comply with the license agreement. Users are responsible for how they use the models and must not create illegal content, harmful material, personal information that could harm others, false information, or content targeting vulnerable groups.

instruction: Can you generate a 3D reconstruction of a room based on several photos taken from different angles?
description: AnySplat is a feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting model designed to process input from unconstrained views. It is intended to reconstruct 3D scenes from a set of 2D images by inferring camera poses and generating Gaussian representations of the scene. The model supports inference tasks, including the estimation of extrinsic and intrinsic camera parameters and the generation of interpolated video outputs based on the reconstructed 3D data. The architecture is implemented in Python using PyTorch and is designed to be used in a pipeline that includes image preprocessing, model inference, and post-processing for video output. The model is trained to handle multiple input images, which are processed and stacked into a tensor for inference. During inference, the model outputs Gaussian representations of the scene along with predicted camera poses. The model is evaluated using standard practices in 3D reconstruction and is intended for use in applications requiring 3D scene understanding from arbitrary camera views. The model is provided with a license, and users are encouraged to cite the associated research paper for any use of the model or its outputs.

instruction: Identify the compounds discussed in this section of the scientific article.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition in biomedical literature. It identifies and extracts chemical compounds and substances from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the BC4CHEMD dataset, a manually annotated corpus for chemical entity recognition from the BioCreative IV challenge. The BC4CHEMD dataset contains PubMed abstracts with chemical entities annotated according to ChEBI guidelines and is used to benchmark named entity recognition models in identifying chemical compounds, drugs, and other chemical substances in scientific literature. The model can identify and classify the following entity types: B-CHEM and I-CHEM. It achieves an F1 score of 0.92, precision of 0.91, recall of 0.93, and accuracy of 0.98. The model is based on the ModernBERT-base architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is suitable for use in clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. If used in research or applications, it should be cited as described in the provided citation.

instruction: I have a text document; can you generate a spoken version with a professional tone?
description: The LFM2.5-Audio-1.5B-8bit model is a multimodal audio model designed for a range of audio-related tasks, including Text-to-Speech (TTS), Speech-to-Text (ASR), and Speech-to-Speech (STS) conversion. It supports interleaved generation of text and audio in a single turn and enables real-time, token-by-token generation for low-latency applications. The model is based on the LFM2.5-Audio architecture and has been converted to MLX format using mlx-audio version 0.3.0. It features a 1.5B parameter Liquid Foundation Model (LFM) backbone, a Conformer-based audio encoder for processing input audio, a Depthformer-based audio decoder for generating audio codes, and an ISTFT-based neural detokenizer for reconstructing audio waveforms. The model also supports an alternative decoding method using the Mimi neural codec. It is capable of handling both text and audio inputs and outputs, with the ability to generate responses that include text, audio, or a combination of both. The model is trained to process audio at a 24kHz sample rate and provides configuration options for generation parameters such as max_new_tokens, temperature, top_k, and top_p for both text and audio outputs. The model includes a ChatState system for managing conversation turns and supports streaming generation for real-time audio playback. The model is available for use via the mlx-audio library, which can be installed using pip. The model's license follows the terms of the original LFM2.5-Audio model.

instruction: Produce a 3D model of a liver tumor to assist in a biopsy planning meeting.
description: Shap-MeD is a text-to-3D object generative model specialized in the biomedical domain. It is designed to assist in the 3D modeling of medical objects, aiming to reduce development time. 3D modeling in medicine has various applications, including surgical procedure simulation and planning, the design of personalized prosthetic implants, medical education, the creation of anatomical models, and the development of research prototypes. Shap-MeD is based on Shap-e, an open-source text-to-3D generative model developed by OpenAI, and is fine-tuned using a dataset of biomedical objects. The model achieved a mean squared error (MSE) of 0.089 in latent generation on the evaluation set, compared to Shap-e's MSE of 0.147. A qualitative evaluation was also conducted, comparing Shap-MeD with other models in the generation of biomedical objects. The results indicate that Shap-MeD demonstrates higher structural accuracy in biomedical object generation.

instruction: Could you highlight the region described as a 'diffuse infiltrate in the apical lung field'?
description: Knowledge to Sight (K2Sight) is a novel framework designed for grounding abnormalities in medical images, where the goal is to localize clinical findings based on textual descriptions. Unlike generalist Vision-Language Models (VLMs) that often struggle with domain-specific medical terms, K2Sight introduces structured semantic supervision. It achieves this by decomposing clinical concepts into interpretable visual attributes like shape, density, and anatomical location, distilled from domain ontologies. This approach guides region-text alignment during training, enabling data-efficient training of compact models (0.23B and 2B parameters) using only 1.5% of the data required by state-of-the-art medical VLMs. Despite their small size and limited training data, K2Sight models achieve performance on par with or better than 7B+ medical VLMs, with up to 9.82% improvement in $mAP_{50}$. This model can be easily integrated and used for zero-shot abnormality grounding in medical images.

instruction: Can you help me generate a 3D scene from a photo and export it for rendering in other tools?
description: This is a float16 conversion of the SHARP model for use with Swift/MLX implementations. SHARP is an approach to photorealistic view synthesis from a single image. Given a single photograph, SHARP regresses the parameters of a 3D Gaussian representation of the depicted scene. This is done in less than a second on a standard GPU via a single feedforward pass through a neural network. The 3D Gaussian representation produced by SHARP can then be rendered in real time, yielding high-resolution photorealistic images for nearby views. The representation is metric, with absolute scale, supporting metric camera movements. Experimental results demonstrate that SHARP delivers robust zero-shot generalization across datasets. It sets a new state of the art on multiple datasets, reducing LPIPS by 25–34% and DISTS by 21–43% versus the best prior model, while lowering the synthesis time by three orders of magnitude. The results are 3D Gaussian splats (3DGS) in the output folder. The 3DGS .ply files are compatible with various public 3DGS renderers. The model follows the OpenCV coordinate convention (x right, y down, z forward). The 3DGS scene center is roughly at (0, 0, +z). When dealing with third-party renderers, please scale and rotate to re-center the scene accordingly. Additionally, you can render videos with a camera trajectory. While the Gaussian prediction works for all CPU, CUDA, and MPS, rendering videos via the --render option currently requires a CUDA GPU. The gsplat renderer takes a while to initialize at the first launch.

instruction: Please detect the reading order of the elements in this complex document layout.
description: dots.ocr is a multilingual document layout parsing model that unifies layout detection and content recognition within a single vision-language model while maintaining good reading order. Built on a compact 1.7B-parameter LLM foundation, it achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance for text, tables, and reading order, with formula recognition results comparable to much larger models like Doubao-1.5 and Gemini2.5-Pro. It demonstrates robust parsing capabilities for low-resource languages, achieving decisive advantages across both layout detection and content recognition on in-house multilingual document benchmarks. By leveraging a single vision-language model, dots.ocr offers a significantly more streamlined architecture than conventional methods that rely on complex, multi-model pipelines. Switching between tasks is accomplished simply by altering the input prompt, proving that a VLM can achieve competitive detection results compared to traditional detection models like DocLayout-YOLO. Built upon a compact 1.7B LLM, dots.ocr provides faster inference speeds than many other high-performing models based on larger foundations. It supports a range of layout categories, including 'Caption', 'Footnote', 'Formula', 'List-item', 'Page-footer', 'Page-header', 'Picture', 'Section-header', 'Table', 'Text', and 'Title'. Text extraction and formatting rules specify that 'Picture' elements omit text, 'Formula' elements are formatted as LaTeX, 'Table' elements as HTML, and all others as Markdown. The output text must be the original text from the image, with no translation, and all layout elements must be sorted according to human reading order. The final output is a single JSON object. dots.ocr is not yet perfect for high-complexity tables and formula extraction, and pictures in documents are currently not parsed. The model may fail to parse under certain conditions, such as when the character-to-pixel ratio is excessively high or when continuous special characters are present. It is not yet optimized for high-throughput processing of large PDF volumes. Future work includes achieving more accurate table and formula parsing, enhancing OCR capabilities for broader generalization, and developing a more general-purpose perception model based on VLMs that integrates general detection, image captioning, and OCR tasks into a unified framework. Parsing the content of pictures in documents is also a key priority for future work.

instruction: What are the most discriminative features for recognizing this object in the wild?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperform specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. These models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones that provide multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers applied on the class token, or a linear layer applied on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) is a curated dataset of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) is a dataset of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, image retrieval, 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification. Evaluation results show that the models outperform previous models on multiple benchmarks. For example, the ViT-7B model achieves 90.4 on IN-ReaL, 91.1 on IN-R, 91.1 on Obj.Net, 72.8 on Ox.-H, 55.9 on ADE20k, 0.309 on NYU, 79.7 on DAVIS, 64.4 on NAVI, and 58.7 on SPair. The ConvNeXt Large model achieves 88.9 on IN-ReaL, 89.4 on IN-R, 81.3 on Obj.Net, 82.4 on ADE20k, 59.3 on NYU, 65.2 on DAVIS, 47.8 on NAVI, and 0.403 on SPair. On satellite data, the ViT-7B model achieves 74.0 on m-BEnet, 97.2 on m-brick-kiln, 94.8 on m-eurosat, 62.3 on m-forestnet, 96.1 on m-pv4ger, 62.1 on m-so2sat, and 81.1 mean for classification tasks, and 94.1 on m-cashew, 76.6 on m-chesapeake, 62.6 on m-NeonTree, 83.4 on m-nz-cattle, 95.5 on m-pv4ger-seg, 37.6 on m-SA-crop, and 75.0 mean for segmentation tasks. The model architecture includes Vision Transformer models with varying parameters and ConvNeXt models with different sizes. The training of the ViT-7B model required 61,440 hours of compute time on Nvidia H100 GPUs, resulting in an estimated carbon emission of 18t CO2eq. The models were trained using PyTorch 2.7 on private infrastructure in the USA.

instruction: Create a short anime video of a character training in a martial arts dojo.
description: Wan2.2-Anime-Unlimited (v1.0 Stable Toy) is the first truly open-source, unrestricted anime video generation model. It is based on the Wan2.2-5B architecture and can run on 6GB VRAM. The model is uncensored and designed to generate anime-style video content. The v1.0 version is described as a 'Qualified Toy' and is fully stable, serving as a proof of concept that a 5B model, when fine-tuned with 531 high-quality polished clips, can produce impressive anime content. It specializes in character interactions and basic movements. The model is currently in a stable state and is intended to be a fun and usable tool for generating anime videos. The development roadmap includes future versions such as v2.x, which will focus on battle scenes and slice-of-life content with support for 8s and 10s video generation, and v3.0, which will expand the range of anime concepts and art styles. The v5.0 to v6.0 versions will introduce a new audio model, transforming the system into a multimodal audio-video model with sound alignment and unrestricted creativity. The model is being developed with the support of the open-source community and is intended to surpass the performance of the 27B base model in the anime domain. The model is currently being trained on 4,000+ high-quality videos for the v2.x series, and further development depends on community support and funding.

instruction: I need to compare a user's query with a database of technical manuals. What's the best way to find the closest match?
description: Sarashina-Embedding-v2-1B is a Japanese text embedding model based on a Japanese large language model. It was trained using multi-stage contrastive learning and achieved a state-of-the-art average score across 28 datasets in the Japanese Massive Text Embedding Benchmark as of July 28, 2025. The model maps sentences and paragraphs into a 1792-dimensional dense vector space and supports applications such as semantic textual similarity, semantic search, paraphrase mining, text classification, and clustering. It is a Sentence Transformer model with a maximum sequence length of 8,192 tokens and uses cosine similarity as the similarity function. The model architecture consists of a Transformer with LlamaModel and a Pooling layer that uses the last token for pooling. The model is designed to work with Japanese text. For usage, it can be loaded using the sentence-transformers library version 4.0.2. To enhance performance, the model was created through a three-stage learning process: first, weakly-supervised learning using contrastive learning with web-crawled and open datasets; second, supervised fine-tuning with higher-quality data and multiple model variations; and third, model merging by linearly combining the weights of the top-performing models from the second stage. Evaluation results as of July 28, 2025, show an average score of 76.38 across all tasks, with 76.48 in Retrieval, 84.22 in STS, 77.14 in Classification, 86.28 in Reranking, and 52.56 in Clustering. The model is licensed under an unspecified license, and commercial use requires contacting the developers.

instruction: Can you summarize the visual content of this image in a structured format?
description: This is a DINOv3 Vision Transformer (ViT) model image feature encoder, distilled on the LVD-1689M dataset from the DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model is designed for image feature extraction and supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. It is based on a Vision Transformer architecture with a patch size of 16x16. The model has 303.1 million parameters, 82.4 GMACs, and 90.6 million activations, and processes images of size 256 x 256. The original model weights had all QKV projection biases set to zero, and for the `timm` implementation, the QKV bias is disabled (`qkv_bias=False`) for most models, except for variants with `qkvb` in the name, which have the bias enabled but set to zero. The original models store RoPE periods as a persistent `bfloat16` buffer, while `timm` generates `float32` periods at initialization, which may result in some numerical differences but is less problematic on devices without bfloat16 support and appears to work as well or slightly better for fine-tuning. The model is evaluated on various benchmarks, including IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair, with performance metrics such as 90.2% on IN-ReaL and 0.352 on NYU for the ViT-L/16 variant. It is also evaluated on satellite-related tasks such as classification and segmentation, with performance metrics such as 79.6% mean accuracy on GEO-Bench classification and 74.5% mean accuracy on GEO-Bench segmentation for the ViT-L/16 variant. The model is associated with the DINOv3 paper and the Vision Transformer paper, and is implemented using the PyTorch Image Models library.

instruction: Please generate a sample of a frog in RGB format from the available categories.
description: This model is an EMA (Exponential Moving Average) version of the DDPM (Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models) trained on the CIFAR-10 dataset. It is based on the original model but uses exponential moving averages of model parameters for improved stability and quality. The model is designed for unconditional image generation and implements the DDPM approach described in the paper by Jonathan Ho, Ajay Jain, and Pieter Abbeel. The EMA version provides more stable training and often better sample quality by maintaining exponentially weighted averages of model parameters. The model generates high-quality 32×32 pixel images from all 10 CIFAR-10 categories: airplane, automobile, bird, cat, deer, dog, frog, horse, ship, and truck. It is fully compatible with the Hugging Face Diffusers library. The model uses a U-Net architecture and is trained on the CIFAR-10 dataset, which consists of 50,000 training images of 32×32 RGB pixels. The training procedure is an EMA version of standard DDPM training, with exponential moving averages applied to model weights. The training objective is the variational lower bound on negative log likelihood. The EMA version typically provides improved stability, better sample quality, and reduced mode collapse, often achieving better FID scores compared to non-EMA versions. Approximate performance metrics include FID scores of 4.5216 for 50K .png samples generated by the DDIM with 100 sampling steps, and 6.5398 for 10K .png samples generated by the DDIM with 100 sampling steps. The model generates diverse samples across all CIFAR-10 categories, with all generated images being 32×32 pixels in RGB format. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 License.

instruction: Can you analyze this dataset of customer transactions and predict which ones are likely to churn?
description: TabPFN-2.5 is a transformer-based foundation model that uses in-context-learning to solve tabular prediction problems in a forward pass. It is designed for regression and classification tasks with up to 50,000 samples and 2000 features in structured tabular format. The model is not suitable for unstructured data such as text or images, and it has not been tested for use with more than 50,000 samples or 2000 features. The architecture consists of a transformer with TabPFNv2-like alternating attention and 18-24 layers. TabPFN-2.5 is trained purely on synthetic tabular tasks, while Real-TabPFN-2.5 is a version that has been continued pre-trained on real-world datasets. The model has been evaluated on proprietary benchmark collections, including TabArena and RealCause, and it achieves new state-of-the-art results in each. It is free from dataset leakage from the pretraining stage due to its training on synthetic data, but users should ensure that the labeled data is free of biases when applied to high-risk use cases. Performance can degrade when applied to more than 50,000 data points and/or 2000 features. The model weights are released under the tabpfn-2.5-license-v1.0, which allows for research and limited internal evaluation but prohibits use for commercial or production purposes. A Commercial Enterprise License is available for production use cases, providing access to a high-speed inference engine, dedicated support, integration tooling, and other internal models. The model was developed by Prior Labs and is available in version v1.0.

instruction: I want to generate a character with a custom pose—can you guide me through the process?
description: Hunyuan3D-Omni is a unified framework for the controllable generation of 3D assets, built on Hunyuan3D 2.1. It introduces a unified control encoder to incorporate additional control signals, including point cloud, voxel, skeleton, and bounding box. The model supports multi-modal conditional control, allowing 3D model generation constrained by 3D bounding boxes, creation of 3D human models with specific skeletal poses, generation guided by input point clouds, and creation from voxel representations. Hunyuan3D-Omni uses a single cross-modal architecture to unify all signals, rather than separate heads for each modality. It is trained with a progressive, difficulty-aware sampling strategy that selects one control modality per example, favoring harder signals such as skeletal pose and downweighting easier ones like point clouds. This approach encourages robust multi-modal fusion and graceful handling of missing inputs. Experiments show that these additional controls improve generation accuracy, enable geometry-aware transformations, and increase robustness for production workflows. The model requires 10 GB VRAM for generation and has a size of 3.3B parameters.

instruction: Can you suggest a way to encode audio with a single codebook for downstream modeling tasks?
description: NeuCodec is a lightweight neural audio codec designed for encoding speech at a bit-rate of 0.8 kbps. It is intended for use in training high-quality text-to-speech models and is optimized for real-world applications, including commercial use. The model uses Finite Scalar Quantisation (FSQ) to produce a single codebook, which is ideal for downstream modeling with Speech Language Models. It operates by taking 16kHz audio input and upscaling it to 24kHz using an upsampling decoder. The codec processes audio at 50 tokens per second with 16 bits per token, resulting in the 0.8kbps bit-rate. The FSQ encoding scheme provides bit-level error resistance, making it suitable for use in unreliable or noisy communication channels. NeuCodec was trained on a variety of datasets, including Emilia-YODAS, MLS, LibriTTS, Fleurs, CommonVoice, HUI, and an additional proprietary set. Publicly available data was covered by either the CC-BY-4.0 or CC0 license. The model is released under the Apache-2.0 license and is available for use in tools or products. Pre-encoded datasets are provided, including a version of Emilia-YODAS compressed from 1.7TB to 41GB using NeuCodec, which significantly reduces the compute requirements for training. The model is developed by Neuphonic and is available for installation via PyPI.

instruction: What is the dominant color scheme in this painting, and what emotions does it evoke?
description: Qwen3-VL is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced Thinking editions for flexible deployment. Key enhancements include the ability to operate PC and mobile GUIs by recognizing elements, understanding functions, invoking tools, and completing tasks. It can generate Draw.io, HTML, CSS, and JS from images and videos. The model supports advanced spatial perception, including judging object positions, viewpoints, and occlusions, with capabilities for 2D and 3D grounding. It has a native 256K context length, expandable to 1M, enabling it to handle books and hours-long video with full recall and second-level indexing. The model excels in enhanced multimodal reasoning, particularly in STEM and Math, with causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. It features upgraded visual recognition with broader, higher-quality pretraining that can recognize a wide range of subjects, including celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. The model supports expanded OCR in 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare or ancient characters and jargon. It also provides improved long-document structure parsing and text understanding comparable to pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE for full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings, enhancing long-horizon video reasoning. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. This is the weight repository for Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Instruct.

instruction: How similar is this image to another one in terms of learned visual representations?
description: This is a DINOv3 ViT model image feature encoder, distilled on the LVD-1689M dataset from the DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model is designed to extract image features and supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. It is based on a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture with a base configuration and a patch size of 16. The model has 85.6 million parameters, 23.6 GMACs, and 34.1 million activations, and processes images of size 256 x 256. The original model weights had all QKV projection biases set to zero, and for the `timm` implementation, the QKV bias is disabled (`qkv_bias=False`) for these models. Some model sizes have variants with `qkvb` in the name that have the bias enabled (`qkv_bias=True`), but set to zero, to match the behavior of `transformers` and original models. The original models keep RoPE periods as a persistent `bfloat16` buffer, while `timm` generates `float32` periods at initialization, which may result in some numerical differences but appears to work as well or slightly better for fine-tuning. The model is evaluated on various benchmarks, including IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair, with performance metrics such as accuracy and mean intersection over union (mIoU). For ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on web data (LVD-1689M), the model achieves 89.3% accuracy on IN-ReaL, 76.7% on IN-R, 64.1% on Obj.Net, 58.5% on Ox.-H, 51.8% on ADE20k, 0.373 on NYU, 77.2% on DAVIS, 58.8% on NAVI, and 57.2% on SPair. For ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on satellite data (SAT-493M), the model achieves 73.0% mean accuracy on m-BEnet, 96.5% on m-brick-kiln, 94.1% on m-eurosat, 60.6% on m-forestnet, 96.0% on m-pv4ger, 57.4% on m-so2sat, and 79.6% mean accuracy. For segmentation tasks on satellite data, the model achieves 94.2% mean accuracy on m-cashew, 75.6% on m-chesapeake, 61.8% on m-NeonTree, 83.7% on m-nz-cattle, 95.2% on m-pv4ger-seg, 36.8% on m-SA-crop, and 74.5% mean accuracy. The model is associated with the DINOv3 paper, the paper on Vision Transformers for image recognition at scale, and the PyTorch Image Models repository. The model is available through the `timm` library and can be used for image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. For image classification, the model can be used with the `timm.create_model` function and the `eval` method, along with model-specific transforms. For feature map extraction, the model can be used with the `features_only=True` argument. For image embeddings, the model can be used with the `num_classes=0` argument to remove the classifier, or with the `forward_features` and `forward_head` methods to obtain the embeddings. The model has some caveats, including the handling of QKV biases and RoPE periods, which may affect numerical stability and compatibility with certain hardware.

instruction: Could you recognize and extract the footnotes and endnotes from this document?
description: MinerU2.5 is a 1.2B-parameter vision-language model for document parsing that achieves state-of-the-art accuracy with high computational efficiency. It uses a two-stage parsing strategy: first performing efficient global layout analysis on downsampled images, then conducting fine-grained content recognition on native-resolution crops for text, formulas, and tables. The model is supported by a large-scale, diverse data engine for pretraining and fine-tuning, and it consistently outperforms both general-purpose and domain-specific models across multiple benchmarks while maintaining low computational overhead. MinerU2.5 provides comprehensive and granular layout analysis, preserving non-body elements like headers, footers, and page numbers to ensure full content integrity, and it employs a refined and standardized labeling schema for a clearer, more structured representation of elements such as lists, references, and code blocks. It delivers high-quality parsing of complex, lengthy mathematical formulae and accurately recognizes mixed-language (Chinese-English) equations. The model also demonstrates enhanced robustness in table parsing, handling challenging cases such as rotated tables, borderless tables, and tables with partial borders. For inference, MinerU2.5 can be used with the `mineru-vl-utils` Python package, which simplifies the process of sending requests and handling responses. The `vllm-async-engine` is recommended for inference, as it can achieve a concurrent inference speed of 2.12 fps on one A100. The model can be installed using pip with either the `transformers` or `vllm` backend. MinerU2.5 is designed to extract document content in two steps, using a client interface that supports both synchronous and asynchronous operations. The model is based on a vision-language architecture and is intended for high-resolution document parsing tasks. The model's performance has been evaluated on the OmniDocBench benchmark across different elements and document types. The model is open-source and available for use under the terms of the provided citation. The authors acknowledge contributions from various individuals and organizations for providing valuable code and models. If the model is used in research, the authors request that it be cited using the provided BibTeX entry.

instruction: Based on this image, can you determine the time of day or the season it was taken in?
description: Qwen3-VL is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced Thinking editions for flexible deployment. Key enhancements include the ability to operate PC and mobile GUIs by recognizing elements, understanding functions, invoking tools, and completing tasks. It can generate Draw.io, HTML, CSS, and JS from images and videos. The model supports advanced spatial perception, including judging object positions, viewpoints, and occlusions, with capabilities for 2D and 3D grounding. It features long context and video understanding with a native 256K context length, expandable to 1M, enabling it to handle books and hours-long video with full recall and second-level indexing. Enhanced multimodal reasoning is demonstrated in STEM and Math tasks, with causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. The model has upgraded visual recognition with broader, higher-quality pretraining that enables recognition of a wide range of subjects, including celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. Expanded OCR supports 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare or ancient characters and jargon. The model also supports long-document structure parsing and text understanding on par with pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE for full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings, enhancing long-horizon video reasoning. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. This is the weight repository for Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Instruct.

instruction: Could you check this audio to see if it was created using a TTS platform like Speechify or Hume AI?
description: This is a fine-tuned Wav2Vec2 transformer model for binary audio classification, designed to distinguish between real human speech and AI-generated speech from text-to-speech and voice cloning software. The model is trained to detect audio generated by platforms including ElevenLabs, Amazon Polly, Hexgrad Kokoro, Hume AI, Speechify, and Luvvoice. It is based on a pre-trained Wav2Vec2-XLSR model that was already fine-tuned for deepfake audio detection, and it uses transfer learning with selective layer freezing. The feature extractor and bottom 12 transformer encoder layers are frozen, while the top 12 transformer encoder layers and a classification head are trained. The model has approximately 160 million trainable parameters. It is optimized for 2.5-13 second audio clips, resampled to 16kHz and converted to mono. The model outputs logits for two classes: real (human) audio and fake (AI-generated) audio, which can be converted to probabilities using softmax. The model achieves high performance on the validation set, with an accuracy of 97.9%, a ROC-AUC of 0.998, and a balanced accuracy of 97.9%. However, it is important to note that the high performance is due to the base model's existing deepfake detection capabilities and the similarity of the training data to the validation set. The model may not generalize well to novel TTS engines, advanced voice cloning systems, real-time voice manipulation, or low-quality recordings. The model is intended for use on audio files in WAV, MP3, or FLAC formats, and it is recommended to test it on TTS engines not included in the training data for true generalization assessment.

instruction: How can I forecast the temperature for the next week using past weather patterns?
description: Mitra regressor is a tabular foundation model that is pre-trained on purely synthetic datasets sampled from a mix of random regressors. It is based on a 12-layer Transformer with 72 million parameters, pre-trained by incorporating an in-context learning paradigm. The model is designed for regression tasks and supports inference and training on tabular data. To use the model, users can install AutoGluon and follow a workflow that includes loading a dataset, splitting it into training and test sets, converting it into a TabularDataset, and creating a TabularPredictor configured for regression. The model can be trained on a subset of the training data, and its performance can be evaluated using a leaderboard on the test data. The model is available under the Apache-2.0 License. The development of Mitra is described in the paper titled 'Mitra: Mixed synthetic priors for enhancing tabular foundation models,' published as an arXiv preprint in 2025.

instruction: How does the selective parameter activation technology help reduce resource usage?
description: Gemma 3n E4B IT is a fine-tuned version of the Gemma 3n model, produced using a modified abliteration engine. It has the best abliteration scores and can be used without a specialized system prompt for removing censorship. Use it with extreme caution and care. This repository contains static quants, while the quants have been moved to a separate repository and regenerated with a larger multilingual dataset. Vision support has been merged into llama.cpp, requiring a git pull and rebuild. Abliteration scores include 11 refusals out of 100 and a KL divergence of 0.1422. The model has 26 abliterated layers, 9 skipped layers, and modifications to 8 attn.o_proj matrices and 26 mlp.down_proj matrices. Gemma 3n models are a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models from Google, built using the same research and technology as the Gemini models. They are designed for efficient execution on low-resource devices and can handle multimodal input, including text, image, video, and audio, generating text outputs. These models were trained with data in over 140 spoken languages. Gemma 3n models use selective parameter activation technology to reduce resource requirements, allowing them to operate at an effective size of 2B and 4B parameters. The models support input in the form of text strings, images normalized to specific resolutions, audio data encoded at a specific rate, and a total input context of 32K tokens. Outputs include generated text in response to the input, with a total output length up to 32K tokens. The models were trained on a dataset of approximately 11 trillion tokens, with a knowledge cutoff date of June 2024. The training data includes web documents, code, mathematics, images, and audio. Rigorous filtering methods were applied to exclude harmful and sensitive content. Gemma was trained using TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e hardware, leveraging JAX and ML Pathways software for efficient training. The models were evaluated on a range of benchmarks, showing strong performance in reasoning, factuality, multilingual tasks, STEM and code, and additional categories. Ethics and safety evaluations were conducted, covering child safety, content safety, and representational harms. The models have limitations related to training data, context and task complexity, language ambiguity, factual accuracy, and common sense. Ethical considerations include bias and fairness, misinformation and misuse, transparency and accountability, and privacy.
