The model is intended for assisting real-time track/platform assignment predictions for MBTA commuter rail but is not a safety system and should not replace official dispatch or operations. It is sensitive to concept drift and unseen stations or routes. Consistent categorical index mapping between training and inference is required for reliable performance.

instruction: Can you generate an image of a medieval castle surrounded by fog and ancient ruins?
description: This repository contains Nunchaku-quantized versions of a model designed to generate high-quality images from text prompts and to advance complex text rendering. The model is optimized for efficient inference while maintaining minimal loss in performance. It is a text-to-image model developed by the Nunchaku Team and is licensed under the Apache-2.0 license. The model is available in different quantized versions, with data types including INT4 for non-Blackwell GPUs (pre-50-series) and NVFP4 for Blackwell GPUs (50-series). Two rank configurations are provided: r32 for faster inference and r128 for better quality but slower inference. The model includes standard inference speed versions for general use, as well as 4-step and 8-step distilled models fused with additional components using LoRA strength = 1.0. The 4-step distilled models are available in both r32 and r128 ranks for INT4 and NVFP4 data types, with the r128 versions offering better quality at the cost of slower inference. Similarly, the 8-step distilled models are available in the same configurations, with r128 versions providing improved quality but slower performance. The model is based on a diffusion architecture and is related to the SVDQuant method, as described in the paper 'SVDQuant: Absorbing Outliers by Low-Rank Components for 4-Bit Diffusion Models' presented at the Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations in 2025.

instruction: What are the strengths and weaknesses of this AI-generated video?
description: VideoScore2 is a multi-dimensional, interpretable, and human-aligned framework designed to evaluate AI-generated videos across three dimensions: visual quality, text-to-video alignment, and physical/common-sense consistency. The model produces detailed chain-of-thought rationales to support its evaluations. It is trained on a large-scale dataset called VideoFeedback2, which contains 27,168 human-annotated videos with scores and reasoning traces across the three evaluation dimensions. The training process uses a two-stage pipeline involving supervised fine-tuning followed by reinforcement learning with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to enhance analytical robustness. Extensive experiments show that VideoScore2 achieves 44.35 (+5.94) accuracy on the in-domain benchmark VideoScore-Bench-v2 and an average performance of 50.37 (+4.32) across four out-of-domain benchmarks, including VideoGenReward-Bench and VideoPhy2. The model is intended for evaluating AI-generated videos and provides both hard scores and soft, normalized scores based on token-level probabilities. It is designed to be used with a specific inference pipeline that includes video preprocessing, model generation, and score parsing. The model supports evaluation of visual quality (clarity, smoothness, artifacts), text-to-video alignment (fidelity to the prompt), and physical/common-sense consistency (naturalness and physics plausibility).

instruction: Can you process this audio and determine if the voice belongs to a man or a woman?
description: Common-Voice-Gender-Detection is a fine-tuned version of `facebook/wav2vec2-base-960h` for binary audio classification, specifically trained to detect speaker gender as female or male. This model uses the `Wav2Vec2ForSequenceClassification` architecture for efficient and accurate voice-based gender classification. The model achieves a precision of 0.9705 for female and 0.9943 for male, with a recall of 0.9916 for female and 0.9799 for male. The F1-scores are 0.9809 for female and 0.9870 for male, with an overall accuracy of 0.9846 across 6545 samples. The model is intended for use in speech analytics, conversational AI personalization, voice dataset curation, research applications, and multimedia content tagging. It is designed to assist in analyzing speaker demographics in call centers or customer service recordings, adjust tone or dialogue in voice assistants based on gender detection, automatically tag or filter voice datasets by speaker gender, enable linguistic and acoustic research involving gender-specific speech patterns, and automate metadata generation for gender identification in podcasts, interviews, or video content.

instruction: Remove the car from this photo and leave the rest untouched.
description: This tiny model is intended for debugging purposes. It is randomly initialized using a configuration adapted from a source model. The model is designed to perform instance segmentation tasks, supporting functions such as text-based object detection and exclusion of specific regions using input boxes. The architecture includes a vision encoder with a vision transformer backbone, a text encoder based on CLIP, a geometry encoder, a DETR encoder and decoder, and a mask decoder. The vision encoder processes images through a series of transformer layers and a feature pyramid network. The text encoder processes text inputs through a transformer-based architecture. The geometry encoder handles box and label embeddings, while the DETR components manage object detection and cross-attention between text and vision features. The mask decoder generates segmentation masks and includes components for pixel decoding and mask embedding. The model is configured with reduced hidden sizes, intermediate sizes, and attention heads for a lightweight version. It is explicitly noted that this model is not intended for production use and is provided for debugging and development purposes only.

instruction: I want to enhance the search experience on a legal research platform—how can I improve result accuracy?
description: ZeroEntropy has developed zerank-2, a state-of-the-art reranker designed to improve the accuracy of retrieval systems in search engines. This model outperforms closed-source competitors such as cohere-rerank-v3.5 and gemini-2.5-flash across various domains, including finance, legal, code, STEM, medical, and conversational data. The model uses an innovative multi-stage pipeline that models query-document relevance scores as adjusted. The model is currently released under a non-commercial license, and commercial license inquiries can be directed to founders@zeroentropy.dev. For evaluation purposes, OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small is used as an initial retriever for the Top 100 candidate documents. Across multiple domains, zerank-2 achieves NDCG@10 scores of 0.6346 in the Web domain, 0.6140 in Conversational, 0.6521 in STEM & Logic, 0.6528 in Code, 0.6644 in Legal, 0.7217 in Biomedical, and 0.7600 in Finance, compared to an average score of 0.6714. These scores outperform or are competitive with other models such as zerank-1, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Cohere rerank-3.5. The model can be used via the sentence_transformers library as a CrossEncoder and is also available for inference through ZeroEntropy's endpoint.

instruction: What is the emotional tone of this video clip?
description: Keye-VL-1.5 is a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) designed to address fundamental challenges in video comprehension. It incorporates a novel Slow-Fast video encoding strategy, a progressive four-stage pre-training methodology that extends context length up to 128K tokens, and a comprehensive post-training pipeline focused on reasoning enhancement and human preference alignment. The model demonstrates significant improvements in video understanding tasks and maintains competitive performance on general multimodal benchmarks. Keye-VL-1.5 is based on the Qwen3-8B language model and includes a vision encoder initialized from the open-source SigLIP. It supports SlowFast video encoding and native dynamic resolution, preserving the original aspect ratio of images by dividing each into a 14x14 patch sequence. A simple MLP layer maps and merges the visual tokens. The model uses 3D RoPE for unified processing of text, image, and video information. The post-training process includes a non-reasoning stage composed of SFT and MPO training, and a reasoning stage consisting of three key steps: CoT Cold Start, General RL, and Alignment RL. Keye-VL-1.5-8B establishes itself with powerful, state-of-the-art perceptual abilities that are competitive with leading models. It demonstrates exceptional proficiency in video understanding across authoritative public video benchmarks such as Video-MME, Video-MMMU, TempCompass, LongVideoBench, and MMVU. In evaluation sets requiring complex logical reasoning and mathematical problem-solving, such as WeMath, MathVerse, and LogicVista, Keye-VL-1.5-8B displays strong performance, highlighting its advanced capacity for logical deduction and solving complex quantitative problems.

instruction: List the chemical names present in this medical case study.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition in biomedical literature. It identifies and extracts chemical compounds and substances from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the BC4CHEMD dataset, a manually annotated corpus for chemical entity recognition from the BioCreative IV challenge. The BC4CHEMD dataset contains PubMed abstracts with chemical entities annotated according to ChEBI guidelines and is used to benchmark named entity recognition models in identifying chemical compounds, drugs, and other chemical substances in scientific literature. The model can identify and classify the following entity types: B-CHEM and I-CHEM. It achieves an F1 score of 0.95, precision of 0.94, recall of 0.95, and accuracy of 0.99. The model is based on the bge-m3 architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research in biomedical NLP. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0.

instruction: Search for a text document that outlines the key components of a balanced diet.
description: The nvidia/llama-nemoretriever-colembed-1b-v1 is a late interaction embedding model fine-tuned for query-document retrieval. It accepts text queries and documents in the form of text or page images as input and outputs ColBERT-style multi-vector numerical representations for both. It is the smaller version of a model that achieved 1st place on ViDoRe V1 (nDCG@5), ViDoRe V2 (nDCG@5), and MTEB VisualDocumentRetrieval (Rank Borda) as of June 27, 2025. The nvidia/llama-nemoretriever-colembed-1b-v1 achieves 2nd place on these benchmarks. The model is based on a transformer architecture and is built on top of a vision-language model derived from google/siglip2-giant-opt-patch16-384 and meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B. It is intended for researchers exploring applications that require understanding or retrieving information across both text and image modalities, particularly in multimodal RAG systems where queries are in text format and documents are images such as pages, text, charts, tables, or infographics. Potential applications include multimedia search engines, cross-modal retrieval systems, and conversational AI with rich input understanding. The model has a maximum context length of 8192 tokens for both text queries and documents, with longer texts needing to be chunked or truncated. For images, the model scales them into multiple tiles of 512x512 and requires them to be in Python PIL format. The model outputs embedding vectors of a specified dimension for each input token. It is designed and optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems, leveraging NVIDIA’s hardware and software frameworks for faster training and inference times compared to CPU-only solutions. The model was trained on 12.74 million QA pairs across 23 datasets and evaluated on multiple benchmarks for Visual Document Retrieval, including ViDoRe V1, ViDoRe V2, and MTEB Visual Document Retrieval. On these benchmarks, the model achieved an average NDCG@5 score of 0.9050 on ViDoRe V1 (06/27/2025), 0.6209 on ViDoRe V2 (06/27/2025), and 0.8238 on MTEB Visual Document Retrieval. The model is for non-commercial/research use only and is governed by specific terms of use. It is built with Meta Llama 3 and improved using Qwen. The model requires transformers version 4.49.0 and flash attention for usage. It is supported on Linux operating systems and compatible with A100 40GB, A100 80GB, and H100 80GB hardware. NVIDIA encourages users to ensure the model meets requirements for the relevant industry and use case and to address any potential misuse. Security vulnerabilities or concerns should be reported to NVIDIA.

instruction: Create a summary of this call where a caller is reporting a child in danger due to family conflict.
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of the google/flan-t5-base model, specifically optimized for summarizing child helpline case call transcripts. It has been trained on domain-specific data to better understand and summarize conversations involving child protection issues. The model uses the T5ForConditionalGeneration architecture and operates in English with 248 million parameters. It is designed for the task of text summarization within the domain of child protection and helpline conversations. The model has been specialized to understand child protection terminology, helpline conversation patterns, sensitive case reporting protocols, and legal and procedural references specific to child welfare. It demonstrates enhanced contextual understanding of child welfare scenarios, including child labor, forced marriage, and abuse cases. The model generates concise, actionable summaries that capture key information such as caller identity and location, the nature of the concern or issue, and action items and referrals provided. It is trained to appropriately summarize sensitive child protection cases while maintaining essential details. The model has a maximum source length of 1024 tokens and a maximum target length of 256 tokens. It was trained for 3 epochs with a learning rate of 3e-5 and a batch size of 4. It uses beam search with 4 beams, a length penalty of 2.0, and a no repeat n-gram size of 2. The model is intended for use by child protection organizations, helpline services, social workers, and researchers for automated summarization of case calls, quick generation of case summaries, efficient case documentation, and analysis of child protection case patterns and trends. The model has limitations, including domain specificity, language restriction to English, a context window limited to 1024 input tokens, and the need for human review of outputs for critical cases. Ethical considerations include the handling of sensitive information, the need for professional review of outputs, the maintenance of privacy and confidentiality protocols, and the model's role as an assistant rather than a replacement for human judgment in child protection cases. The model shows significant improvements over the base FLAN-T5 model, with 50-84% improvement across ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, and ROUGE-L metrics, 94% accuracy in using child protection terminology, 91% success rate in identifying key case details, 87% accuracy in identifying referrals and follow-up actions, and a 4.4/5 overall quality rating from child protection professionals. Performance highlights include better identification of key information in child protection contexts, more structured and actionable summaries, proper use of child protection terminology and procedures, more reliable output format across different case types, and summaries that meet standards for official case documentation. Evaluation results include a Rouge1 score of 0.5804, a Rouge2 score of 0.3623, a Rougel score of 0.5325, a train loss of 0.8403, and a val loss of 0.8031.

instruction: What is the speaker’s main point, and can you summarize it in a concise way?
description: Phi-4-multimodal-instruct is a lightweight open multimodal foundation model that leverages the language, vision, and speech research and datasets used for Phi-3.5 and 4.0 models. The model processes text, image, and audio inputs, generating text outputs, and comes with 128K token context length. The model underwent an enhancement process, incorporating both supervised fine-tuning, direct preference optimization, and RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) to support precise instruction adherence and safety measures. The languages that each modal supports are the following: Text: Arabic, Chinese, Czech, Danish, Dutch, English, Finnish, French, German, Hebrew, Hungarian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Norwegian, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Spanish, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian; Vision: English; Audio: English, Chinese, German, French, Italian, Japanese, Spanish, Portuguese. The model is intended for broad multilingual and multimodal commercial and research use. The model provides uses for general purpose AI systems and applications which require memory/compute constrained environments, latency bound scenarios, strong reasoning (especially math and logic), function and tool calling, general image understanding, optical character recognition, chart and table understanding, multiple image comparison, multi-image or video clip summarization, speech recognition, speech translation, speech QA, and audio understanding. The model is designed to accelerate research on language and multimodal models, for use as a building block for generative AI powered features. The model is not specifically designed or evaluated for all downstream purposes. Developers should consider common limitations of language models and multimodal models, as well as performance difference across languages, as they select use cases, and evaluate and mitigate for accuracy, safety, and fairness before using within a specific downstream use case, particularly for high-risk scenarios. Developers should be aware of and adhere to applicable laws or regulations (including but not limited to privacy, trade compliance laws, etc.) that are relevant to their use case. This release of Phi-4-multimodal-instruct is based on valuable user feedback from the Phi-3 series. Previously, users could use a speech recognition model to talk to the Mini and Vision models. To achieve this, users needed to use a pipeline of two models: one model to transcribe the audio to text, and another model for the language or vision tasks. This pipeline means that the core model was not provided the full breadth of input information – e.g. cannot directly observe multiple speakers, background noises, jointly align speech, vision, language information at the same time on the same representation space. With Phi-4-multimodal-instruct, a single new open model has been trained across text, vision, and audio, meaning that all inputs and outputs are processed by the same neural network. The model employed new architecture, larger vocabulary for efficiency, multilingual, and multimodal support, and better post-training techniques were used for instruction following and function calling, as well as additional data leading to substantial gains on key multimodal capabilities. It is anticipated that Phi-4-multimodal-instruct will greatly benefit app developers and various use cases. The enthusiastic support for the Phi-4 series is greatly appreciated. Feedback on Phi-4 is welcomed and crucial to the model's evolution and improvement. The Phi-4-multimodal-instruct was observed as having strong automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech translation (ST) performance, surpassing expert ASR model WhisperV3 and ST models SeamlessM4T-v2-Large. It ranked number 1 on the leaderboard with word error rate 6.14% in comparison with the current best model 6.5% as of March 04, 2025. It is the first open-sourced model that can perform speech summarization, and the performance is close to GPT4o. It has a gap with close models, e.g. Gemini-1.5-Flash and GPT-4o-realtime-preview, on speech QA task. Work is being undertaken to improve this capability in the next iterations. Phi-4-multimodal-instruct is capable of processing both image and audio together, and achieves much stronger performance on multiple benchmarks compared to other existing state-of-the-art omni models that can enable audio and visual signal as input. The model has 5.6B parameters and is a multimodal transformer model. The model has the pretrained Phi-4-Mini-Instruct as the backbone language model, and the advanced encoders and adapters of vision and speech. The model's inputs are text, image, and audio. It is best suited for prompts using the chat format. The model's context length is 128K tokens. The model was trained on 512 A100-80G GPUs for 28 days. The training data includes 5 trillion text tokens, 2.3M speech hours, and 1.1T image-text tokens. The model's outputs are generated text in response to the input. The model was trained between December 2024 and January 2025. The model is a static model trained on offline datasets with the cutoff date of June 2024 for publicly available data. The model supports the following languages: Text: Arabic, Chinese, Czech, Danish, Dutch, English, Finnish, French, German, Hebrew, Hungarian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Norwegian, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Spanish, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian; Vision: English; Audio: English, Chinese, German, French, Italian, Japanese, Spanish, Portuguese. The model's release date is February 2025. The model's training data includes a wide variety of sources, totaling 5 trillion text tokens, and is a combination of publicly available documents filtered for quality, selected high-quality educational data, and code; newly created synthetic, “textbook-like” data for the purpose of teaching math, coding, common sense reasoning, general knowledge of the world (e.g., science, daily activities, theory of mind, etc.); high quality human labeled data in chat format; selected high-quality image-text interleave data; synthetic and publicly available image, multi-image, and video data; anonymized in-house speech-text pair data with strong/weak transcriptions; selected high-quality publicly available and anonymized in-house speech data with task-specific supervisions; selected synthetic speech data; and synthetic vision-speech data. Focus was placed on the quality of data that could potentially improve the reasoning ability for the model, and the publicly available documents were filtered to contain a preferred level of knowledge. As an example, the result of a game in premier league on a particular day might be good training data for large foundation models, but such information was removed for the Phi-4-multimodal-instruct to leave more model capacity for reasoning for the model's small size. The data collection process involved sourcing information from publicly available documents, with a focus on filtering out undesirable documents and images. To safeguard privacy, image and text data sources were filtered to remove or scrub potentially personal data from the training data. The decontamination process involved normalizing and tokenizing the dataset, then generating and comparing n-grams between the target dataset and benchmark datasets. Samples with matching n-grams above a threshold were flagged as contaminated and removed from the dataset. A detailed contamination report was generated, summarizing the matched text, matching ratio, and filtered results for further analysis. Like other language models, the Phi family of models can potentially behave in ways that are unfair, unreliable, or offensive. Some of the limiting behaviors to be aware of include: Quality of Service: The Phi models are trained primarily on English language content across text, speech, and visual inputs, with some additional multilingual coverage. Performance may vary significantly across different modalities and languages: Text: Languages other than English will experience reduced performance, with varying levels of degradation across different non-English languages. English language varieties with less representation in the training data may perform worse than standard American English. Speech: Speech recognition and processing shows similar language-based performance patterns, with optimal performance for standard American English accents and pronunciations. Other English accents, dialects, and non-English languages may experience lower recognition accuracy and response quality. Background noise, audio quality, and speaking speed can further impact performance. Vision: Visual processing capabilities may be influenced by cultural and geographical biases in the training data. The model may show reduced performance when analyzing images containing text in non-English languages or visual elements more commonly found in non-Western contexts. Image quality, lighting conditions, and composition can also affect processing accuracy. Multilingual performance and safety gaps: We believe it is important to make language models more widely available across different languages, but the Phi 4 models still exhibit challenges common across multilingual releases. As with any deployment of LLMs, developers will be better positioned to test for performance or safety gaps for their linguistic and cultural context and customize the model with additional fine-tuning and appropriate safeguards. Representation of Harms & Perpetuation of Stereotypes: These models can over- or under-represent groups of people, erase representation of some groups, or reinforce demeaning or negative stereotypes. Despite safety post-training, these limitations may still be present due to differing levels of representation of different groups, cultural contexts, or prevalence of examples of negative stereotypes in training data that reflect real-world patterns and societal biases. Inappropriate or Offensive Content: These models may produce other types of inappropriate or offensive content, which may make it inappropriate to deploy for sensitive contexts without additional mitigations that are specific to the case. Information Reliability: Language models can generate nonsensical content or fabricate content that might sound reasonable but is inaccurate or outdated. Limited Scope for Code: The majority of Phi 4 training data is based in Python and uses common packages such as

description: The Perception Encoder (PE) is a state-of-the-art encoder for image and video understanding, trained using a simple vision-language learning approach. It was introduced by Meta and is part of a family of large-scale vision encoder models with strong performance on a wide range of vision tasks. PE leverages a robust contrastive pretraining recipe and is finetuned on synthetically aligned videos, enabling it to outperform existing models on classification and retrieval tasks. It also produces strong, general features that scale well for downstream applications. PE core is the base model, trained with a robust image pretraining schedule and finetuned on data generated by a synthetic video data engine. PE core currently comes in three sizes: PE core G is the main checkpoint, with L and B models distilled from it. The L and B models include a class token for global aggregation in addition to an attention pooling block with 8 heads on top of the vision tower. PE core achieves strong results on zero-shot image classification and retrieval, as well as zero-shot video classification and retrieval. It performs particularly well on challenging benchmarks such as ObjectNet and ImageNet-A. The model is designed for image and text feature extraction and supports tasks such as classification and retrieval. It is based on a contrastive pretraining approach and is compatible with the OpenCLIP framework. The model is available for use through the OpenCLIP library and can be accessed via the provided GitHub repositories. The model is described in detail in the associated technical report and research papers.

instruction: What category fits a text describing a religious ceremony?
description: This is an xlm-roberta-large model that has been fine-tuned on English training data labeled with 18 media codes. The media codes include categories such as State and Local Government Administration, Weather, Fires, emergencies and natural disasters, Crime and trials, Arts, culture, entertainment and history, Style and fashion, Food, Travel, Wellbeing and learning, Personal finance and real estate, Personal technology and popular science, Churches and Religion, Celebrities and human interest, Obituaries and death notices, Sports, Crosswords, puzzles, comics, Media production/internal, letters, and Advertisements. The model is designed for text classification tasks and is used by an open-source and free natural language processing tool intended to simplify and speed up projects for comparative research. The model was evaluated on a test set of 67,681 English examples, achieving an accuracy, precision, recall, and weighted average F1-score of 0.8. Performance metrics for individual labels include precision, recall, and F1-score values ranging from 0 to 1, with specific values provided for each of the 18 media codes and additional categories. The model requires a token for access due to gated access restrictions, and users must pass the token parameter when loading the model. In earlier versions of the Transformers package, the use_auth_token parameter may be required instead. The model uses the sentencepiece tokenizer, and users running the model before transformers version 4.27 may need to install it manually. If a RuntimeError occurs when loading the model using the from_pretrained() method, adding ignore_mismatched_sizes=True should resolve the issue. Model performance can be improved by extending the training sets, and contributions of CAP-coded corpora in any domain and language are welcomed at poltextlab@poltextlab.com.

instruction: Can you bring out a more natural glow in the skin of this image?
description: The qwen-edit-skin is a finetuned Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) model designed to enhance the realism and detail of human skin in images. It is built on top of the Qwen/Qwen-Image-Edit-2509 base model, which is known for its advanced image editing capabilities, particularly in maintaining consistency in single-image edits and preserving personal identity in multi-image editing. The LoRA was trained for 5000 steps on an NVIDIA RTX 5090 GPU using the AI-Toolkit. The training dataset was curated by taking real images of subject portraits with exposed skin, captioning them as target images, and then editing them in Photoshop to reduce skin texture, tone, and pore visibility to create control images. The model was trained at resolutions of 512, 768, and 1024 pixels using bf16 precision. Key training parameters include a batch size of 1, gradient accumulation of 1, a learning rate of 1.0e-04, and the adamw8bit optimizer, which helps reduce memory usage during training. The noise scheduler used is flowmatch, which contributes to efficient training and high-quality image generation. The LoRA architecture features linear and convolutional ranks and alphas of 16, ensuring a balanced scaling of learned adaptations to prevent overfitting. To use the model, the base Qwen-Image-Edit-2509 model must be loaded, followed by the qwen-edit-skin LoRA weights. The recommended weight strength is between 1 and 1.5, though higher values can be used to observe the effect of the LoRA. The model is intended for creative and artistic purposes, such as enhancing the realism of human skin in digital images for digital artists, photographers, and AI art enthusiasts. It may carry inherent biases from the base model and the training dataset, which focused on improving skin details and may not represent all skin tones and types equally. Users are encouraged to use descriptive and inclusive language in their prompts and to use the model responsibly. The model is not intended for unethical or harmful use, and users are responsible for the content they generate. A trigger phrase to activate the model's effect is 'make the subjects skin details more prominent and natural.'

instruction: I'm trying to find a report that includes a diagram of the company's organizational hierarchy. Can you help me retrieve it?
description: ColModernVBERT is the late-interaction version of ModernVBERT that is fine-tuned for visual document retrieval tasks and is the most performant model on this task. The ModernVBERT suite consists of compact 250M-parameter vision-language encoders that achieve state-of-the-art performance in this size class, matching the performance of models up to 10 times larger. ColModernVBERT is designed to support visual document retrieval tasks and is part of a family of models that includes BiModernVBERT, ModernVBERT-embed, and the base ModernVBERT model. The base ModernVBERT model is trained using a masked language modeling objective for modality alignment, while ModernVBERT-embed is further trained using contrastive learning without document specialization. BiModernVBERT is a bi-encoder version fine-tuned for visual document retrieval. ColModernVBERT matches the performance of models nearly 10 times larger on visual document benchmarks and provides an interesting inference speed on CPU compared to models of similar performance. The model is released under the MIT license. If used in academic work, it should be cited with the provided citation information.

instruction: What is the bioaccumulation potential of this compound with the given SMILES string?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict teratogenic bioaccumulation potential, a property that may relate to developmental toxicity or bioaccumulation scoring. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. It is designed for a regression task, producing a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and features an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model has 128 hidden channels, 3 layers, 3 timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.1, a learning rate of 0.0005, a weight decay of 0.0001, a batch size of 32, and is trained for 50 epochs with a patience of 10. The model was trained on the TBP dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: Could you recognize the structures in this image and categorize them by type?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. The models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones providing multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers on the class token, or a linear layer on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) consists of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) consists of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, image retrieval, 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on web data, the models achieve results such as 87.0 on IN-ReaL, 60.4 on IN-R, 50.9 on Obj.Net, 49.5 on Ox.-H, 47.0 on ADE20k, 0.403 on NYU, 72.7 on DAVIS, 56.3 on NAVI, and 50.4 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-S/16. For ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web data, the models achieve results such as 86.6 on IN-ReaL @256px, 87.7 on IN-ReaL @512px, 73.7 on Obj.Net @256px, 74.1 on Obj.Net @512px, 52.6 on ADE20k @256px, 58.7 on ADE20k @512px, 42.7 on NYU @256px, and 0.448 on NYU @512px for DINOv3 ConvNeXt Tiny. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on satellite data, the models achieve results such as 73.0 on m-BEnet, 96.5 on m-brick-kiln, 94.1 on m-eurosat, 60.6 on m-forestnet, 96.0 on m-pv4ger, 57.4 on m-so2sat, and 79.6 mean for DINOv3 ViT-L/16. The models were trained using Nvidia H100 GPUs on private infrastructure in the USA, with 61,440 hours used for ViT-7B model training, resulting in 18t CO2eq of carbon emissions. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters), ViT-S+ (29M parameters), ViT-B (86M parameters), ViT-L (300M parameters), ViT-H+ (840M parameters), and ViT-7B (6716M parameters), all with a patch size of 16 and varying embedding dimensions, register tokens, heads, and FFN types. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.
